\newcommand*\pandocbounded[1]{% scales image to fit in text height/width
  \sbox\pandoc@box{#1}%
  \Gscale@div\@tempa{\textheight}{\dimexpr\ht\pandoc@box+\dp\pandoc@box\relax}%
  \Gscale@div\@tempb{\linewidth}{\wd\pandoc@box}%
  \ifdim\@tempb\p@<\@tempa\p@\let\@tempa\@tempb\fi% select the smaller of both
  \ifdim\@tempa\p@<\p@\scalebox{\@tempa}{\usebox\pandoc@box}%
  \else\usebox{\pandoc@box}%
  \fi%
}

% Java language definition
\lstdefinelanguage{Java21}{
    morekeywords={abstract,assert,boolean,break,byte,case,catch,char,class,
        const,continue,default,do,double,else,enum,extends,final,finally,
        float,for,goto,if,implements,import,instanceof,int,interface,long,
        native,new,package,private,protected,public,return,short,static,
        strictfp,super,switch,synchronized,this,throw,throws,transient,
        try,var,void,volatile,while,yield,record,sealed,permits,when},
    sensitive=true,
    morecomment=[l]{//},
    morecomment=[s]{/*}{*/},
    morestring=[b]",
    morestring=[b]',
}

\lstdefinelanguage{ASN1}{
    morekeywords={SEQUENCE,SET,CHOICE,OPTIONAL,IMPLICIT,EXPLICIT,
        OF,INTEGER,BOOLEAN,OCTET,STRING,BIT,NULL,OBJECT,IDENTIFIER,
        ANY,DEFINED,BY},
    sensitive=true,
    morecomment=[l]{--},
    morestring=[b]",
}

\title{PDF Digital Signatures --- A Technical Reference}
\author{PDFalyzer Studio Contributors}
\date{2026-03-06}

\begin{document}
\frontmatter
\maketitle

{
\setcounter{tocdepth}{2}
\tableofcontents
}
\mainmatter
\section{Preface}\label{_preface}

PDF digital signatures are one of those technologies that seem
straightforward until you actually have to implement them. You read the
spec, write some code, run a test --- and the signature validates in
your tool but fails in Adobe Acrobat. Or it works in Acrobat but fails
the EU's validation service. Or it works everywhere on Monday and breaks
on Friday because a timestamp authority renewed its certificate.

If you've been there, this book is for you.

This is a technical reference for engineers, developers, and security
researchers who need to understand PDF digital signatures at the deepest
level --- from the raw bytes in the file to the legal framework that
gives those bytes legal standing across 27 EU member states. Every claim
is backed by precise references to ISO 32000-2:2020, the ETSI PAdES
standards, relevant RFCs, and the eIDAS regulation. Code examples use
Apache PDFBox 3.0.0 and BouncyCastle 1.80 on Java 21, because that's
what most of us actually use in production.

\subsection{Who This Book Is For}\label{_who_this_book_is_for}

You are a software engineer, a security researcher, or a technically
curious person who needs to \emph{understand} PDF signatures, not just
use them. Maybe you're building a signing service. Maybe you're
debugging why a customer's signature shows "invalid" in one viewer but
"valid" in another. Maybe you're the person who got handed a government
compliance checklist that says "PAdES-B-LTA" and nobody in your
organization knows what that means.

We assume you know how to program, that you've encountered public-key
cryptography before (even if the details are fuzzy), and that you're
comfortable reading data structures. We do \emph{not} assume you know
anything about PDF internals, CMS, or the European regulatory landscape.
Parts I and II build that foundation from scratch.

\subsection{What This Book Is Not}\label{_what_this_book_is_not}

This is not a tutorial. There are no "Hello World" signing examples in
Chapter 1. If you need to sign a PDF in the next 20 minutes, copy the
code from Chapter 28 and come back later to understand why it works.

This is not a product manual. We use PDFBox and BouncyCastle because
they're open-source, widely deployed, and well-documented. The concepts
apply equally to iText, OpenPDF, or any other library --- the PDF
specification doesn't care what language you write in.

This is not a legal guide. We explain the eIDAS regulation and its
technical requirements in detail, but we are engineers, not lawyers.
When legal precision matters --- and with qualified electronic
signatures, it always does --- consult someone with a law degree.

\subsection{How This Book Is
Organized}\label{_how_this_book_is_organized}

The book is structured in six parts, each building on the previous:

\textbf{Part I: Cryptographic Foundations} (Chapters 1--6) starts from
first principles. If you can explain the difference between RSA and
ECDSA, between a certificate and a key pair, between CMS and PKCS\#7 ---
skip ahead. If any of those distinctions are hazy, start here. We cover
just enough cryptography to make the rest of the book make sense, with a
focus on the specific algorithms and structures you'll encounter in PDF
signatures.

\textbf{Part II: PDF Internals} (Chapters 7--11) dissects the PDF file
format itself. You'll learn how objects, cross-reference tables, and
incremental updates work --- because you cannot understand PDF
signatures without understanding incremental saves. You'll also learn
about AcroForm fields, annotations, and coordinate systems, because
signature fields are form fields, signature appearances are annotations,
and the coordinate system \emph{will} trip you up.

\textbf{Part III: PDF Digital Signatures} (Chapters 12--18) is the heart
of the book. This is where we take apart the \texttt{/Sig} dictionary
key by key, trace the byte range byte by byte, and walk through the CMS
structure field by field. We cover approval and certification
signatures, visual appearances, validation logic, PAdES profiles, and
long-term validation. If you only read one part of this book, read this
one.

\textbf{Part IV: eIDAS \& Qualified Signatures} (Chapters 19--23) enters
the regulatory world. The European eIDAS regulation created a legal
framework where a digital signature can have the same legal standing as
a handwritten one --- but only if it meets very specific technical
requirements. We explain what those requirements are, how the EU Trusted
List works, what a Qualified Trust Service Provider does, and how remote
signing via the Cloud Signature Consortium API fits into the picture.

\textbf{Part V: Security, Incidents \& Outlook} (Chapters 24--27) covers
what can go wrong --- and what has gone wrong. The Ruhr University
Bochum research team published devastating attacks against PDF signature
validation in 2019--2021. We analyze every attack technically, explain
why it worked, and show how to defend against it. We also cover
certificate authority breaches, industry oddities, and the emerging EUDI
wallet.

\textbf{Part VI: Code \& Implementation} (Chapters 28--31) is where
theory meets production. Complete, tested Java code for signing, CMS
construction, verification, and analysis --- not snippets, but full
classes you can drop into a project. We also provide test cases and
reference PDFs for every scenario discussed in the book.

\subsection{Conventions Used in This
Book}\label{_conventions_used_in_this_book}

PDF dictionary keys are written in monospace with a leading slash:
\texttt{/SubFilter}, \texttt{/ByteRange}, \texttt{/Contents}. When we
show a full dictionary, it's presented as a code listing with callout
annotations explaining each key.

Spec references follow this pattern: "ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8.1" means
ISO 32000-2:2020, section 12.8.1. RFC references: "RFC 5652 ---
Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) §5" means RFC 5652, section 5. We use
these abbreviations consistently throughout.

Code examples are Java 21 using PDFBox 3.0.0 and BouncyCastle 1.80.
Every code listing compiles and runs --- the source is in the book's
repository alongside test cases that verify the examples.

Admonition blocks carry specific meaning:

Additional context, background, or alternative approaches.

Practical advice from experience --- the kind of thing a senior
colleague would tell you over coffee.

Something you must understand to avoid a specific class of bugs.

A common mistake or misunderstanding that leads to broken signatures.

A security-relevant issue that could lead to signature bypasses or
compliance failures.

\subsection{A Note on the PDF
Specification}\label{_a_note_on_the_pdf_specification}

The PDF specification has a complicated history. Adobe published it as a
proprietary format in 1993, released it as ISO 32000-1 in 2008, and then
ISO 32000-2:2020 superseded it in 2020 with significant changes to the
digital signature sections.

Throughout this book, we reference ISO 32000-2:2020 as the authoritative
source. Where the behavior of older PDF versions matters (and for
digital signatures, it often does --- plenty of documents in the wild
still use PDF 1.7 features), we note the differences explicitly.

The PAdES standards (ETSI EN 319 142) add an additional layer of
requirements on top of ISO 32000-2. They don't replace the PDF spec ---
they constrain it. A PAdES-compliant signature is always a valid ISO
32000-2 signature, but not every valid ISO 32000-2 signature is
PAdES-compliant. This distinction matters enormously in the EU, and
increasingly, everywhere else.

\subsection{Three Decades of PDF: A
Timeline}\label{_three_decades_of_pdf_a_timeline}

PDF was born as a page description language for printing. Digital
signatures were an afterthought --- bolted on six years later in PDF
1.3, using a cryptographic framework (PKCS\#7) that predated PDF itself.
Understanding why PDF signatures work the way they do --- and why they
break the way they break --- requires understanding the history that
shaped them.

The following table traces every major milestone in the evolution of PDF
and, specifically, the digital signature infrastructure built on top of
it. Some of these are format changes. Some are regulatory events. Some
are security incidents that forced the entire industry to rethink
assumptions it had held for years. All of them left marks on the code
you will write or debug.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.6250}}@{}}
\caption{The evolution of PDF and PDF digital signatures,
1993--2026}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Year
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Event
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What happened and why it matters
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Year
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Event
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What happened and why it matters
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
1993 & \textbf{PDF 1.0} released (Acrobat 1.0) & Adobe published the
Portable Document Format as a proprietary specification for reliable
cross-platform document rendering. No forms, no interactivity, no
signatures --- just pages, text, images, and fonts. The file structure
established here (header, body, cross-reference table, trailer) survives
unchanged to this day. Every PDF you sign in 2026 still starts with
\texttt{\%PDF-1.x} or \texttt{\%PDF-2.0} and ends with
\texttt{\%\%EOF}. \\
1996 & \textbf{PDF 1.2} (Acrobat 3.0) & Introduced \textbf{AcroForm} ---
interactive form fields that users could fill in. This matters for
signatures because signature fields \emph{are} form fields. The
\texttt{/AcroForm} dictionary, the field tree, widget annotations ---
all of the infrastructure that Chapter 9 dissects --- was born here. PDF
1.2 also added Unicode support and Flate compression, both of which are
ubiquitous in modern PDFs. \\
1999 & \textbf{PDF 1.3} --- digital signatures introduced (Acrobat 4.0)
& The moment PDF gained the ability to carry cryptographic signatures.
Adobe defined the \texttt{/Sig} dictionary type, the \texttt{/ByteRange}
array, the \texttt{/Contents} hex string for the signature value, and
the \texttt{/SubFilter} key with its initial value
\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached}. The design decision to embed the CMS
signature \emph{inside} the PDF file (rather than as a detached sidecar)
and to define the signed region as a byte range with a gap for the
signature value itself --- these were architectural choices made in 1999
that we still live with. Every quirk in Chapter 12 traces back to this
release. \\
2001 & \textbf{PDF 1.4} (Acrobat 5.0) & Added \textbf{transparency} (the
imaging model's biggest change ever), 128-bit RC4 encryption, and
\textbf{tagged PDF} for accessibility. For signatures: PDF 1.4
formalized the \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.sha1} SubFilter (where the SHA-1 hash
of the entire document is embedded as the CMS content, rather than using
a detached signature). This SubFilter is now deprecated but still
appears in legacy documents. Acrobat 5 also introduced
\textbf{certification signatures} (\texttt{/DocMDP} transform) --- the
distinction between "I approve this document" and "I certify this is the
authoritative version" starts here. \\
2003 & \textbf{PDF 1.5} (Acrobat 6.0) & Introduced \textbf{object
streams} and \textbf{cross-reference streams} --- a complete reworking
of how PDF files store their internal structure. Instead of the classic
ASCII cross-reference table, PDF 1.5 allows binary cross-reference data
compressed inside stream objects. This matters for signatures because
the byte range must cover the \emph{entire} file structure, and
cross-reference streams change where objects physically live in the
file. PDF 1.5 also added \textbf{XFA forms} (Adobe's XML-based form
format), which would become a persistent source of interoperability
problems for signatures on XFA documents --- a problem so annoying that
ISO 32000-2:2020 deprecated XFA entirely. \\
2005 & \textbf{PDF 1.6} (Acrobat 7.0) & \textbf{AES-128 encryption}
replaced the weak RC4-based encryption from earlier versions. Embedded
file streams (attachments) and 3D content were added. For signatures:
PDF 1.6 refined the interaction between encryption and signing --- a
signed, encrypted PDF requires careful handling to ensure the signature
covers the encrypted content correctly. The \texttt{/NeedAppearances}
flag behavior was also clarified, which matters because a missing
appearance stream on a signature field causes some viewers to show a
blank signature block. \\
2006 & \textbf{PDF 1.7} (Acrobat 8.0) & The last version of PDF that
Adobe defined unilaterally before handing the specification to ISO. PDF
1.7 solidified the \textbf{AES-256 encryption} scheme and finalized the
AcroForm/XFA coexistence model. The vast majority of signed PDFs in the
wild today are PDF 1.7 documents --- not because of any technical
limitation of PDF 2.0, but because template authors, form generators,
and enterprise software have not yet migrated. When you debug a
signature failure in production, you are almost certainly looking at a
PDF 1.7 file. \\
2008 & \textbf{ISO 32000-1:2008} --- PDF becomes an ISO standard & Adobe
donated the PDF 1.7 specification to ISO, making it an open
international standard. The technical content was essentially identical
to Adobe's PDF Reference 1.7, but the governance changed: future changes
would go through the ISO committee process, not Adobe's product team.
For signatures, this was the moment PDF digital signatures gained the
credibility of an ISO-backed specification --- a prerequisite for
government and regulated-industry adoption. \\
2009 & \textbf{PAdES} --- ETSI TS 102 778 (Parts 1--5) & The European
Telecommunications Standards Institute published the first \textbf{PDF
Advanced Electronic Signatures} standards. PAdES defined profiles for
how CMS signatures should be embedded in PDF to meet European legal
requirements: which signed attributes are mandatory (ESS
signing-certificate-v2), which SubFilter values to use
(\texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached}), and how to embed timestamps, revocation
data, and long-term validation information. PAdES didn't replace ISO
32000 --- it constrained it. A PAdES-compliant signature is always a
valid ISO 32000 signature, but the reverse is not true. This distinction
--- and the interoperability problems it causes when a PDF viewer
validates against ISO 32000 rules but not PAdES rules --- is a recurring
theme in this book (Chapters 17 and 26). \\
2011 & \textbf{DigiNotar breach} --- CA infrastructure crisis & A Dutch
certificate authority, DigiNotar, was completely compromised by an
attacker who issued over 500 fraudulent certificates --- including
certificates for google.com, mozilla.org, and intelligence agencies. The
certificates were used in man-in-the-middle attacks against Iranian
dissidents. Every major browser vendor revoked trust in DigiNotar within
weeks; the company went bankrupt within months. For PDF signatures:
DigiNotar was a wake-up call about the fragility of the certificate
trust model that all digital signatures depend on. If a CA can be
compromised, any signature issued by that CA's certificates becomes
suspect. This incident accelerated the adoption of Certificate
Transparency logs and tighter CA audit requirements --- both of which
affected the trust infrastructure behind PDF signatures. Chapter 25
covers this and other CA breaches in detail. \\
2012 & \textbf{Adobe Approved Trust List (AATL)} formalized & Adobe
formalized its \textbf{AATL} program --- a curated list of root
certificates that Acrobat trusts for signature validation. If your
signing certificate chains up to an AATL root, Acrobat shows a green
checkmark. If it doesn't --- even if the certificate is perfectly valid,
issued by a reputable CA, and chains to a trust anchor in your OS trust
store --- Acrobat shows a warning. This created a de facto monopoly on
"valid" PDF signatures: to get a green checkmark in the world's dominant
PDF viewer, you need an AATL-listed certificate, which costs
significantly more than a standard code-signing certificate. The AATL
remains one of the most contentious aspects of the PDF signature
ecosystem (Chapter 26). \\
2014 & \textbf{eIDAS Regulation} (EU 910/2014) enacted & The European
Union passed the \textbf{Electronic Identification, Authentication and
Trust Services} regulation, creating a legal framework where digital
signatures can have the same legal standing as handwritten signatures
--- but only if they meet strict technical requirements. eIDAS defined
three tiers: \textbf{electronic signatures} (any electronic indication
of signing intent), \textbf{advanced electronic signatures} (uniquely
linked to the signatory, capable of identifying them, under their sole
control, linked to the data so any change is detectable), and
\textbf{qualified electronic signatures} (advanced + created by a
qualified device + based on a qualified certificate issued by a
qualified trust service provider). Only QES has automatic legal
equivalence to a handwritten signature across all EU member states.
PAdES became the primary technical mechanism for creating
eIDAS-compliant signatures in PDF. Chapters 19--23 cover the entire
eIDAS ecosystem. \\
2016 & \textbf{eIDAS fully effective} (July 1, 2016) & The eIDAS
regulation became fully effective across all EU member states, with the
\textbf{EU Trusted List (EUTL)} operational. The EUTL is a
machine-readable, cryptographically signed list of every Qualified Trust
Service Provider in the EU --- the authoritative source for determining
whether a signing certificate qualifies for the highest legal tier. For
developers: if your application needs to validate whether a PDF
signature is a qualified electronic signature, you must check the
signing certificate against the EUTL, not just against a generic trust
store. The EUTL XML schema, its signature validation, and the LOT (List
of Trusted Lists) hierarchy are covered in Chapter 20. \\
2017 & \textbf{ISO 32000-2:2017} --- PDF 2.0, first edition & The first
major revision of the PDF specification under ISO governance. PDF 2.0
deprecated XFA forms, removed several legacy features, and made
significant changes to the digital signature sections. Notably: the
\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.sha1} SubFilter was deprecated (SHA-1 is
cryptographically broken for collision resistance since the SHAttered
attack of February 2017), and the specification added the
\texttt{ETSI.RFC3161} SubFilter for document-level timestamps. PDF 2.0
also clarified the byte range coverage rules and tightened the
requirements around incremental updates after signing --- changes that
were partly motivated by early reports of the signature validation
problems that Bochum would systematically expose two years later. \\
2019 & \textbf{"1 Trillion Dollar Refund"} --- Bochum attacks (CCS 2019)
& The Ruhr University Bochum team published their landmark paper at ACM
CCS 2019, systematically breaking PDF signature validation in every
major viewer. They identified three attack classes: \textbf{Universal
Signature Forgery (USF)} --- crafting a signed PDF with a garbage or
empty signature value that viewers accepted as valid;
\textbf{Incremental Saving Attack (ISA)} --- appending unsigned changes
to a signed PDF that viewers failed to flag; and \textbf{Signature
Wrapping Attack (SWA)} --- relocating the signature's byte range to
point at attacker-controlled data. Of 22 desktop viewers tested, all
were vulnerable to at least one attack. The paper title referenced the
fact that signed PDFs are used for tax refund claims, insurance payouts,
and financial contracts --- if signatures could be forged, the financial
exposure was enormous. This research is the subject of Chapter 24. \\
2020 & \textbf{ISO 32000-2:2020} --- PDF 2.0, second edition & The
second edition of PDF 2.0 incorporated corrections and clarifications,
including tightened language around signature validation motivated by
the Bochum attacks. In the same year, Adobe issued
\textbf{CVE-2020-9592} and \textbf{CVE-2020-9596} and patched Acrobat
against USF, ISA, and SWA. Foxit, Nitro, LibreOffice, Okular, and every
other major viewer released their own patches. The PAdES standards were
also updated (ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1) to align with the second edition
of ISO 32000-2. \\
2021 & \textbf{Shadow Attacks} (NDSS 2021) & The Bochum team published a
follow-up: \textbf{Shadow Attacks}. Unlike the 2019 attacks (which
modified a PDF \emph{after} signing), Shadow Attacks prepared malicious
content \emph{before} the document was signed. The attacker created a
PDF with hidden content (invisible text, overlapping images, replaced
form fields) that the signer could not see during signing --- but that
became visible when the recipient opened the document. The signature
validated correctly because the signed bytes were genuinely signed by
the legitimate signer. The attack exploited the gap between what the
signer \emph{saw} and what the bytes \emph{contained} --- a fundamental
problem in any visual-document signing model. Three variants were
demonstrated: \textbf{Hide} (hiding content behind overlapping
elements), \textbf{Replace} (swapping visible content via interactive
form features), and \textbf{Hide-and-Replace} (combining both). Again,
all major viewers were vulnerable. \\
2022 & \textbf{ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.2.1} --- PAdES update & ETSI updated
the PAdES standard to align with ISO 32000-2:2020 and to incorporate
lessons from the Bochum attacks. The update clarified requirements
around signature scope validation, incremental update handling, and the
relationship between the byte range and the visible document content. It
also formalized the PAdES-B-LTA profile's requirements for archival
timestamps in the DSS (Document Security Store) --- the mechanism that
enables long-term validation decades after the signing certificate has
expired. \\
2024 & \textbf{eIDAS 2.0} (EU 2024/1183) enacted & The EU adopted the
revised eIDAS regulation, which introduced the \textbf{European Digital
Identity (EUDI) Wallet} --- a smartphone-based identity wallet that
every EU member state must offer to its citizens by 2026. The EUDI
wallet will carry government-issued identity attestations (PID ---
Person Identification Data) and qualified electronic attestations of
attributes (QEAA), enabling citizens to sign documents with qualified
electronic signatures from their phone. This represents the most
ambitious attempt to make QES mainstream --- moving it from specialized
HSM-backed signing services to consumer smartphones. The Architecture
Reference Framework (ARF) defines the technical specifications. Chapter
27 covers the EUDI wallet and its impact on PDF signing. \\
2025--26 & \textbf{EUDI wallet pilots \& deployment} & EU member states
are conducting large-scale pilot programs for the EUDI wallet across
sectors: banking (identity verification), travel (digital travel
credentials), education (diplomas), healthcare (prescriptions), and
government services. The pilots test the wallet's signing capabilities,
including PAdES-B-LTA signature creation from mobile devices, remote
QSCD integration, and cross-border signature validation. The Cloud
Signature Consortium (CSC) API --- the standard interface for remote
signing --- is being adapted for wallet integration. The outcome of
these pilots will determine whether QES becomes a mainstream consumer
technology or remains a specialized enterprise tool. \\
\end{longtable}

The table omits minor point releases and focuses on the events that
changed what developers need to know. If you are working with PDF
signatures today, every row in that table has left its fingerprint on
the code you are writing --- whether it's the byte range gap from 1999,
the PAdES constraints from 2009, the trust model questions raised by
DigiNotar in 2011, or the validation rewrites forced by Bochum in
2019--2021.

\subsection{Acknowledgments}\label{_acknowledgments}

This book would not exist without the open-source communities that make
PDF signature work accessible to everyone. The \textbf{Apache PDFBox}
team --- particularly Andreas Lehmkühler, Tilman Hausherr, and the many
contributors who maintain the signing infrastructure --- have built the
most widely used open-source PDF library in the Java ecosystem. The
\textbf{Legion of the Bouncy Castle} (yes, that's their actual name)
gave us BouncyCastle, which provides the CMS and X.509 implementations
that most Java signing code depends on, whether it knows it or not.

Special acknowledgment goes to the security researchers at \textbf{Ruhr
University Bochum} --- Vladislav Mladenov, Christian Mainka, Karsten
Meyer zu Selhausen, Martin Grothe, and Jörg Schwenk --- whose systematic
analysis of PDF signature validation between 2019 and 2021 exposed
fundamental flaws in how every major PDF viewer handled signatures.
Their papers --- \emph{"1 Trillion Dollar Refund: How To Spoof PDF
Signatures"} (CCS 2019), \emph{"Shadow Attacks: Hiding and Replacing
Content in Signed PDFs"} (NDSS 2021), and several follow-ups ---
demonstrated that attackers could forge signatures, hide content changes
behind valid signatures, and bypass validation in Adobe Acrobat, Foxit
Reader, and dozens of other viewers. The industry patched, but the
research fundamentally changed how we think about PDF signature
security. Their work is cited throughout this book, and Chapter 24 is
dedicated to a technical analysis of their attacks.

What they found was this: PDF viewers did not actually verify what they
claimed to verify. The Bochum team identified three major attack
classes. \textbf{Universal Signature Forgery (USF)} showed that some
viewers only checked whether a signature \emph{existed} --- not whether
its cryptographic value was correct. An attacker could craft a PDF with
an empty or garbage \texttt{/Contents} value, and viewers like Adobe
Acrobat DC (before the patch) would display it as "signed and valid."
\textbf{Incremental Saving Attack (ISA)} exploited the incremental
update mechanism that PDF signatures depend on: an attacker could append
a new revision to a signed PDF --- changing text, adding pages,
replacing content entirely --- and because viewers did not properly
check whether the appended changes fell outside the signed byte range,
the signature still showed as valid. \textbf{Signature Wrapping Attack
(SWA)} relocated the signed content within the file so that the byte
range pointed at attacker-controlled data while the original signature
remained structurally present. The follow-up \textbf{Shadow Attacks}
paper (NDSS 2021) went further: an attacker could prepare a PDF
\emph{before} it was signed, embedding hidden content that would only
become visible after signing --- the signer would see one document, but
the recipient would see another, and the signature would validate on
both views.

The team tested 22 desktop PDF viewers and 7 online validation services.
Every single one was vulnerable to at least one attack class. Adobe
issued CVE-2020-9592 and CVE-2020-9596 and patched Acrobat in May 2020.
Foxit, Nitro, LibreOffice, and every other major viewer followed with
their own patches. The research forced a fundamental rethinking of PDF
signature validation --- in particular, how viewers handle incremental
updates after signing, how byte range coverage is verified, and how the
relationship between the CMS signature and the actual document bytes is
validated. Before Bochum, most viewers treated signature validation as a
checkbox. After Bochum, it became a security-critical code path that
required adversarial thinking.

The \textbf{European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI)}
deserves credit for the PAdES standards that brought PDF signatures into
the regulated world. Whatever one may think about the complexity of ETSI
EN 319 142, it solved the real problem of making PDF signatures legally
interoperable across borders.

And the PDF specification itself is the product of hundreds of engineers
at Adobe, ISO, and ETSI who somehow managed to build a digital signature
framework into a page description language originally designed for
printing. That it works as well as it does is a testament to their
effort. That it has the quirks it has is a testament to the fact that
three decades of backward compatibility leaves marks.

\chapter{Cryptographic Foundations}\label{_cryptographic_foundations}

\chapter{Asymmetric Cryptography Primer}\label{ch01}

This chapter builds the cryptographic foundation that everything else in
this book depends on. We start with the key distribution problem that
asymmetric cryptography was invented to solve, work through RSA and
elliptic curve algorithms at a conceptual level, and end with practical
Java code that generates the key pairs you will use to sign PDFs.

If you can already explain why RSA-2048 and ECDSA with P-256 provide
roughly equivalent security, and you know why BouncyCastle exists
alongside the JDK's built-in providers, skip to Chapter 2. Everyone
else: this is where your journey into PDF signatures begins.

\section{The Key Distribution
Problem}\label{_the_key_distribution_problem}

Before we talk about RSA, elliptic curves, or any of the algorithms that
make PDF signatures possible, we need to understand the problem they
were invented to solve.

Symmetric cryptography --- where the same key encrypts and
decrypts --- has been around for millennia. Caesar ciphers, Enigma
machines, AES-256: they all share a single fundamental weakness. Both
parties must possess the same secret key. If Alice wants to send an
encrypted message to Bob, she and Bob must first agree on a shared
secret. In person, this is trivial --- write the key on a piece of paper
and hand it over. Over a network, it is a nightmare.

This is the \textbf{key distribution problem}: how do you securely share
a secret with someone you have never met, over a channel that an
adversary controls?

For most of human history, the answer was "you don't --- you use a
trusted courier." Governments ran key distribution networks. Military
organizations employed dedicated personnel whose sole job was
transporting codebooks. Banks used armored vehicles. None of this scales
to the internet, where you need to establish secure communications with
millions of strangers, instantly, without any prior arrangement.

In 1976, Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman published "New Directions
in Cryptography," describing a system where two parties could agree on a
shared secret over a public channel without ever having communicated
before. A year later, Ronald Rivest, Adi Shamir, and Leonard Adleman
published the RSA algorithm --- the first practical public-key
cryptosystem. (Clifford Cocks at GCHQ had independently discovered an
equivalent system in 1973, but it was classified until 1997.
Cryptography has a habit of being invented multiple times.)

The insight behind all of these systems is the same:
\textbf{mathematical one-way functions}. Operations that are easy to
perform in one direction but computationally infeasible to reverse.
Multiplying two large primes takes microseconds; factoring their product
takes longer than the age of the universe. This asymmetry --- easy
forward, hard backward --- is the foundation of everything that follows.

\section{Key Pairs: The Fundamental
Asymmetry}\label{_key_pairs_the_fundamental_asymmetry}

An asymmetric key pair consists of two mathematically related keys:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  A \textbf{private key} --- kept secret, never shared, never
  transmitted
\item
  A \textbf{public key} --- derived from the private key, freely
  distributable
\end{itemize}

The relationship between them has two critical properties:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{It is computationally infeasible to derive the private key
  from the public key.} Given a public key, there is no known efficient
  algorithm to compute the corresponding private key. The security of
  the entire system rests on this.
\item
  \textbf{Operations performed with one key can only be reversed with
  the other.} Data encrypted with the public key can only be decrypted
  with the private key. Data signed with the private key can only be
  verified with the public key.
\end{enumerate}

This second property is what makes digital signatures possible. When you
sign a PDF, your private key produces a cryptographic value that is
mathematically bound to both your key and the document content. Anyone
who has your public key --- which is embedded in your certificate, which
is embedded in the PDF itself --- can verify that the signature was
produced by someone holding the corresponding private key.

They cannot forge a new signature. They cannot modify the document
without invalidating the signature. They cannot extract your private key
from the signature or the public key.

This is the fundamental asymmetry that gives asymmetric cryptography its
name, and it is the reason PDF digital signatures work at all.

The terms "encrypt" and "decrypt" are technically imprecise when applied
to digital signatures. Signing is not encryption in the classical
sense --- you are not hiding data; you are producing a proof. The
distinction matters in formal cryptographic contexts and in legal
frameworks like eIDAS (Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS)).

Throughout this book, we use "sign" and "verify" for signature
operations, reserving "encrypt" and "decrypt" for actual confidentiality
operations. Many older references (and some library APIs, including
parts of the JCA) conflate the two. Do not let the terminology confuse
you.

\section{RSA: The Workhorse}\label{_rsa_the_workhorse}

RSA, named after Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman, remains the most widely
deployed asymmetric algorithm in the world. Every PDF viewer supports
it. Every certificate authority issues RSA certificates. Every signing
library implements it. If you encounter a signed PDF in production,
there is an overwhelming probability that it was signed with RSA.

\subsection{The Mathematical Basis}\label{_the_mathematical_basis}

The security of RSA rests on the \textbf{integer factorization problem}:
given the product of two large primes, recover the original primes.
Multiplication is trivial; factoring is believed to be computationally
infeasible for sufficiently large numbers. Here is how this translates
into a key pair.

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Choose two large primes, \emph{p} and \emph{q}.} These primes
  are generated independently using a cryptographically secure random
  number generator. For RSA-2048, each prime is approximately 1024 bits
  long. Finding primes of this size is itself nontrivial --- it requires
  probabilistic primality tests (Miller-Rabin is the standard), and
  several candidates may be tested before a probable prime is found.
  This is why RSA key generation is noticeably slower than ECDSA key
  generation.
\item
  \textbf{Compute the modulus \emph{n = p * q}.} This product \emph{n}
  appears in both the public and private keys. For RSA-2048, \emph{n} is
  a 2048-bit number. Computing \emph{n} from \emph{p} and \emph{q} takes
  microseconds. Recovering \emph{p} and \emph{q} from \emph{n} is the
  hard problem.
\item
  \textbf{Compute the totient.} Euler's totient function for \emph{n} is
  \emph{phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)}. This value is used to derive the
  private exponent and is then discarded. It must never be stored or
  revealed --- knowing \emph{phi(n)} is equivalent to knowing the
  factorization of \emph{n}.
\item
  \textbf{Choose the public exponent \emph{e}.} In practice, \emph{e} is
  almost always 65537 (0x10001). This value was chosen because it is
  prime, it has a low Hamming weight (only two bits set), and it makes
  modular exponentiation during verification fast. You will see 65537 in
  virtually every RSA certificate you examine. Some ancient systems used
  \emph{e} = 3, which is faster but has known vulnerabilities when
  combined with naive padding.
\item
  \textbf{Compute the private exponent \emph{d}.} \emph{d} is the
  modular inverse of \emph{e} modulo \emph{phi(n)}: \emph{d * e = 1 mod
  phi(n)}. This is computed using the extended Euclidean algorithm.
  Knowing \emph{d} requires knowing \emph{phi(n)}, which requires
  knowing \emph{p} and \emph{q}.
\item
  \textbf{The public key} is the pair (\emph{n}, \emph{e}).
\item
  \textbf{The private key} is the pair (\emph{n}, \emph{d}). In
  practice, the private key also stores \emph{p}, \emph{q}, and several
  precomputed values (the CRT --- Chinese Remainder
  Theorem --- parameters) that make signing four times faster.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Why Large Primes Matter}\label{_why_large_primes_matter}

The security of RSA depends on the inability of an attacker to factor
\emph{n} and recover \emph{p} and \emph{q}. The difficulty of factoring
grows super-polynomially with the size of \emph{n}. The best known
classical factoring algorithm is the General Number Field Sieve (GNFS),
whose complexity is roughly:

\begin{verbatim}
exp( (1.923 + o(1)) * (ln n)^(1/3) * (ln ln n)^(2/3) )
\end{verbatim}

This means that doubling the key size does \emph{not} merely double the
work --- it increases it by many orders of magnitude. Factoring a
768-bit RSA modulus was achieved in 2009 by a team of researchers using
hundreds of machines over two years. Factoring a 1024-bit modulus is
estimated to be within the reach of a well-funded nation-state.
Factoring a 2048-bit modulus is estimated to require more computation
than humanity can currently muster.

\subsection{RSA Key Sizes vs Security
Levels}\label{_rsa_key_sizes_vs_security_levels}

The size of an RSA key is the bit length of the modulus \emph{n}. Larger
modulus means harder factoring means more security --- but also slower
operations.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1111}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1111}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}

Key Size
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright

\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Sign Time (relative)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Signature Size
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Status
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
1024-bit & \textasciitilde80-bit & 1x (baseline) & 128 bytes &
\textbf{Broken.} NIST disallowed after 2013. Do not use for any purpose.
You will still encounter these in legacy PDFs --- they were common
before 2010. \\
2048-bit & \textasciitilde112-bit & \textasciitilde4x & 256 bytes &
\textbf{Current minimum.} NIST SP 800-57 minimum through 2030. This is
what most CAs issue today. Adobe AATL requires at least 2048-bit RSA. \\
3072-bit & \textasciitilde128-bit & \textasciitilde9x & 384 bytes &
\textbf{Recommended for new deployments.} Provides 128-bit
security --- the level NIST considers adequate through at least 2030 and
likely well beyond. \\
4096-bit & \textasciitilde140-bit & \textasciitilde16x & 512 bytes &
\textbf{Conservative choice.} Noticeable performance impact for
high-volume signing. Some government requirements mandate this. The
additional security margin over 3072 is modest relative to the
performance cost. \\
\end{longtable}

RSA-1024 is not merely "weak" --- it is \textbf{factored}. In 2010, a
team factored a 768-bit RSA modulus. The computational cost of factoring
1024-bit moduli is within reach of well-funded adversaries. If you
encounter a signed PDF with an RSA-1024 certificate, the signature
provides no meaningful security guarantee, even if the viewer shows it
as "valid." Many viewers do not check key size during validation.

\subsection{Performance Impact of Key
Size}\label{_performance_impact_of_key_size}

RSA has an unusual asymmetry in its operation costs:
\textbf{verification is fast; signing is slow}.

This is because the public exponent \emph{e} is small (65537), making
the modular exponentiation during verification much cheaper than the
exponentiation with the large private exponent \emph{d} during signing.
For PDF signatures, this works in our favor: documents are signed once
but verified many times.

However, RSA's performance degrades sharply as key sizes increase. The
signing operation scales roughly as O(k\textsuperscript{3}) where
\emph{k} is the key size in bits. Doubling the key size makes signing
approximately eight times slower. For a desktop application signing a
single document, this is irrelevant. For a server processing thousands
of signing requests per second, the difference between 2048-bit and
4096-bit RSA is significant.

\subsection{Why RSA-1024 Persists in the
Wild}\label{_why_rsa_1024_persists_in_the_wild}

If RSA-1024 is broken, why do you still encounter it in signed PDFs? The
answer is a combination of institutional inertia, cost, and the peculiar
nature of long-lived documents.

Government agencies issued RSA-1024 certificates throughout the 2000s
for document signing infrastructure. Many of those signed documents
still exist, still circulate, and still need to be validated. Some
organizations operate signing infrastructure on hardware security
modules (HSMs) that cannot be easily re-keyed --- the cost of replacing
an HSM fleet across dozens of offices can be enormous. Other systems
operate in air-gapped environments where updating cryptographic
infrastructure requires physical site visits.

The practical consequence: your validation code must \emph{handle}
RSA-1024 gracefully, even if it correctly flags it as insecure. Refusing
to parse a legacy document because the key size is too small is
unhelpful. Displaying it as "valid" without qualification is
irresponsible. The right approach is what PDFalyzer does: show the
signature, show the key size, and let the user draw the appropriate
conclusion.

\textbf{How to check the key size of a certificate in a signed PDF:}

In PDFalyzer, open the Signatures tab and look at the "Public Key"
section of any signature. The key size is displayed directly.

From the command line, extract the certificate and use OpenSSL:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ExtensionTok{openssl}\NormalTok{ x509 }\AttributeTok{{-}in}\NormalTok{ cert.pem }\AttributeTok{{-}text} \AttributeTok{{-}noout} \KeywordTok{|} \FunctionTok{grep} \StringTok{"Public{-}Key"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

In Java with BouncyCastle, it is one line:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ keySize }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{((}\BuiltInTok{RSAPublicKey}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPublicKey}\OperatorTok{()).}\FunctionTok{getModulus}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{bitLength}\OperatorTok{();}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

If you see 1024 or less, the certificate was issued before modern
standards were enforced, and the signature's cryptographic value is nil.

\section{Elliptic Curve Cryptography: Smaller Keys, Same
Security}\label{_elliptic_curve_cryptography_smaller_keys_same_security}

In the mid-1980s, Neal Koblitz and Victor Miller independently proposed
using elliptic curves over finite fields as the basis for public-key
cryptography. The core idea: the \textbf{elliptic curve discrete
logarithm problem (ECDLP)} is believed to be harder than integer
factorization for equivalent key sizes. This means we can achieve the
same security level with dramatically smaller keys.

\subsection{Elliptic Curve Basics}\label{_elliptic_curve_basics}

An elliptic curve, for cryptographic purposes, is the set of points
\emph{(x, y)} satisfying an equation of the form
\emph{y\textsuperscript{2} = x\textsuperscript{3} + ax + b} over a
finite field. The "finite field" part is important: we are not working
with real numbers and smooth curves. We are working with integers modulo
a large prime \emph{p}, and the curve is a discrete set of points.

The cryptographic operation on these points is \textbf{point addition}:
given two points on the curve, there is a geometric rule for computing a
third point. Repeated point addition (scalar multiplication) gives us
the one-way function: given a point \emph{G} and a scalar \emph{d},
computing \emph{Q = d * G} is efficient. Recovering \emph{d} from
\emph{Q} and \emph{G} (the ECDLP) is computationally infeasible.

You do not need to understand the geometry to use ECDSA effectively.
What matters is the practical consequence: the ECDLP is harder than
integer factorization for the same key size, which is why a 256-bit
elliptic curve key provides the same security as a 3072-bit RSA key.

\subsection{The NIST Curves: P-256, P-384,
P-521}\label{_the_nist_curves_p_256_p_384_p_521}

The standard curves for ECDSA, defined by NIST in FIPS 186-4:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}@{}}
\caption{NIST standard curves for ECDSA}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Curve
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Security Level
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Signature Size
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Notes
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Curve
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Security Level
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Signature Size
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Notes
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
P-256 (secp256r1) & 128-bit & 64 bytes & The workhorse. Universally
supported. Adobe Acrobat, PDFBox, iText, BouncyCastle --- everyone
supports P-256. If you are choosing an ECDSA curve for PDF signing, this
is the default choice. \\
P-384 (secp384r1) & 192-bit & 96 bytes & Required by certain government
profiles (e.g., NSA Suite B, CNSS Policy 15). Broader support than P-521
but less than P-256. \\
P-521 (secp521r1) & 256-bit & 132 bytes & Maximum security. The name is
not a typo --- it is 521 bits, not 512. Rarely used in practice for PDF
signatures because the security margin over P-384 is excessive for most
threat models, and some tools have spotty support. \\
\end{longtable}

The curve names are a source of endless confusion. "P-256" is the NIST
name. "secp256r1" is the SECG (Standards for Efficient Cryptography
Group) name. "prime256v1" is the OpenSSL name. They are all the same
curve. You will encounter all three names in certificate metadata,
library APIs, and documentation. When PDFBox reports a signature
algorithm, it uses the JCA name; when BouncyCastle constructs the CMS
structure, it uses OIDs; when you read the certificate in OpenSSL, it
says "prime256v1." They are all P-256.

\subsection{Why ECDSA Signatures Are
Smaller}\label{_why_ecdsa_signatures_are_smaller}

The practical consequence of the ECDLP's difficulty is striking.
RSA-3072 provides the same security as ECC-256, but the RSA key is
twelve times larger. This size difference cascades through every part of
the system: smaller keys mean smaller certificates, smaller CMS
structures in the PDF's \texttt{/Contents} field, faster network
transmission of signed documents, and less storage overhead.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4000}}@{}}
\caption{Equivalent security levels across algorithms (NIST SP 800-57
Part 1, Rev. 5)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Security Level
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
RSA Key Size
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
ECC Key Size
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Ratio
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Security Level
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
RSA Key Size
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
ECC Key Size
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Ratio
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
112-bit & 2048-bit & 224-bit & 9:1 \\
128-bit & 3072-bit & 256-bit & 12:1 \\
192-bit & 7680-bit & 384-bit & 20:1 \\
256-bit & 15360-bit & 521-bit & 30:1 \\
\end{longtable}

For PDF signatures specifically, the \texttt{/Contents} hex string must
be large enough to hold the entire CMS \texttt{SignedData} structure,
including the signer's certificate chain. With RSA-4096 certificates and
a chain of three certificates, this blob can exceed 10 KB. With ECDSA
P-256, the same chain might be 3 KB. Chapter 13 covers why the size of
\texttt{/Contents} matters --- it determines the byte range gap, which
must be estimated before signing.

\subsection{ECDSA Adoption in PDF
Signatures}\label{_ecdsa_adoption_in_pdf_signatures}

ECDSA adoption in PDF has been slower than in TLS, where P-256 became
the dominant algorithm years ago. The reasons are institutional rather
than technical.

Certificate authorities have been issuing RSA signing certificates for
two decades, and existing workflows are built around RSA. Many
enterprise signing solutions --- particularly those using HSMs
provisioned in the 2010s --- have RSA keys burned into hardware. The
Adobe AATL program includes ECDSA certificates from several providers,
but the majority of AATL roots remain RSA.

That said, new deployments increasingly choose ECDSA. The eIDAS 2.0
Architecture Reference Framework (Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0))
lists ECDSA as a recommended algorithm for the EUDI wallet. If you are
starting a new signing project and you have control over the certificate
procurement process, ECDSA with P-256 is the technically superior
choice.

The random nonce \emph{k} in ECDSA is a notorious source of catastrophic
failures. If the same \emph{k} is used for two different messages, the
private key can be algebraically recovered. This is not a theoretical
concern --- it has happened in production. Sony's PlayStation 3
code-signing key was extracted in 2010 because they used a constant
value for \emph{k}. The Bitcoin Android wallet vulnerability in 2013 was
caused by a weak random number generator producing predictable \emph{k}
values. Modern implementations use deterministic nonce generation (RFC
6979) to eliminate this class of bugs entirely. BouncyCastle 1.80
supports RFC 6979 deterministic signatures.

\section{EdDSA and Ed25519: The Future
Direction}\label{_eddsa_and_ed25519_the_future_direction}

Edwards-curve Digital Signature Algorithm (EdDSA) is a modern signature
scheme designed by Daniel J. Bernstein and colleagues, published as RFC
8032 in 2017. It addresses several practical shortcomings of ECDSA while
providing equivalent or better security.

\subsection{Bernstein's Work and Design
Philosophy}\label{_bernsteins_work_and_design_philosophy}

Daniel J. Bernstein --- "djb" to everyone in the cryptographic
community --- designed Curve25519 in 2005 and the Ed25519 signature
scheme in 2011 with a very specific philosophy: eliminate every source
of implementation error.

The NIST curves (P-256, P-384) have been criticized for their opaque
parameter generation --- NIST never fully explained how the "random"
seed values were chosen, which fueled suspicion (especially after the
Dual\_EC\_DRBG backdoor scandal in 2013, where the NSA was found to have
inserted a backdoor into a NIST-standardized random number generator).
Bernstein designed Curve25519 with fully transparent, verifiable
parameters --- every constant has a documented justification.

More importantly, Ed25519 was designed to be \emph{implementable}
without the subtle pitfalls that plague ECDSA:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Deterministic signatures.} EdDSA does not use a random nonce.
  The nonce is derived deterministically from the private key and the
  message. This eliminates the entire class of nonce-reuse
  vulnerabilities. There is no Sony PS3 scenario with EdDSA --- it is
  structurally impossible.
\item
  \textbf{Constant-time by design.} The algorithm was designed from the
  ground up to run in constant time. There are no data-dependent
  branches or memory accesses that could leak information about the
  private key through timing, power analysis, or cache behavior.
\item
  \textbf{No parameter negotiation.} You use Ed25519 (128-bit security)
  or Ed448 (224-bit security). That is it. No curves to choose, no hash
  functions to configure, no padding schemes to get wrong.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Ed25519 and Ed448}\label{_ed25519_and_ed448}

EdDSA defines two concrete instances:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Ed25519}: Based on Curve25519 (a twisted Edwards curve).
  Provides \textasciitilde128-bit security. Signatures are 64 bytes,
  public keys are 32 bytes. This is the one everyone uses.
\item
  \textbf{Ed448}: Based on Curve448 (the "Goldilocks" curve, named by
  Mike Hamburg because 448 = 224 * 2, a "just right" security level).
  Provides \textasciitilde224-bit security. Signatures are 114 bytes,
  public keys are 57 bytes.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{EdDSA and PDF: Adoption
Status}\label{_eddsa_and_pdf_adoption_status}

Here is where the good news ends, at least for PDF signatures.

As of 2026, \textbf{EdDSA support in the PDF signature ecosystem is
minimal}. ISO 32000-2:2020 does not explicitly mention EdDSA. The PAdES
standards (ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04)) reference ECDSA but not
EdDSA. Adobe Acrobat does not support EdDSA signatures --- a PDF signed
with Ed25519 will show as "unknown algorithm" or "signature invalid" in
Acrobat.

\textbf{Do not use EdDSA for PDF signatures in production today.}

While the algorithm itself is excellent, the ecosystem does not support
it. A signature that cannot be validated by the dominant PDF viewer is,
for all practical purposes, useless.

That said, EdDSA adoption is accelerating in the broader PKI ecosystem
(TLS, SSH, code signing), and it is a matter of when --- not if --- PDF
viewers add support. The eIDAS 2.0 Architecture Reference Framework
(Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0)) includes EdDSA in its list of
acceptable algorithms for the EUDI wallet. When EUDI wallet-based PDF
signing becomes mainstream (Chapter 27), EdDSA support in PDF viewers
will follow.
