\section{Connecting to PDF: OIDs and
SignerInfo}\label{_connecting_to_pdf_oids_and_signerinfo}

When a PDF signature is created, the signing algorithm is encoded as an
ASN.1 Object Identifier (OID) inside the CMS \texttt{SignedData}
structure (RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS)).
Specifically, it appears in the
\texttt{SignerInfo.digestEncryptionAlgorithm} field (the CMS name for
the signature algorithm --- "digestEncryptionAlgorithm" is a historical
misnomer that has caused confusion for two decades, but we are stuck
with it).

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}@{}}
\caption{Common algorithm OIDs you will encounter in PDF
signatures}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Algorithm
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Where You See It
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Algorithm
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Where You See It
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
RSA (PKCS\#1 v1.5) & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.1} & Legacy signatures.
The hash algorithm is specified separately in
\texttt{digestAlgorithm}. \\
SHA-256 with RSA & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.11} & Most common in
modern PDF signatures. \\
SHA-384 with RSA & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.12} & Higher-security
RSA. \\
SHA-512 with RSA & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.13} & Government and
defense contexts. \\
RSASSA-PSS & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.10} & Probabilistic padding.
Hash specified in parameters. \\
ECDSA with SHA-256 & \texttt{1.2.840.10045.4.3.2} & Growing adoption. \\
ECDSA with SHA-384 & \texttt{1.2.840.10045.4.3.3} & Common with P-384
curves. \\
Ed25519 & \texttt{1.3.101.112} & Not yet seen in production PDF
signatures. \\
\end{longtable}

When you encounter the OID \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.1} (plain RSA) in
the \texttt{digestEncryptionAlgorithm} field, do not panic. This does
not mean the signature used no hash. It means the CMS was constructed in
the older style where the signature algorithm OID says "RSA" and the
hash algorithm is determined solely by the \texttt{digestAlgorithm}
field in the \texttt{SignerInfo}. Both styles --- the combined OID like
\texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.11} (SHA-256 with RSA) and the split
approach --- are valid per RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax
(CMS). PDFBox and BouncyCastle handle both transparently.

\section{Java's Cryptographic Provider Model and
BouncyCastle}\label{_javas_cryptographic_provider_model_and_bouncycastle}

Before we write any code, we need to understand how Java organizes its
cryptographic implementations, because this directly affects how you
generate keys, create signatures, and interact with BouncyCastle.

\subsection{JCA, JCE, and the Provider
Architecture}\label{_jca_jce_and_the_provider_architecture}

The \textbf{Java Cryptography Architecture (JCA)} defines abstract
interfaces --- \texttt{KeyPairGenerator}, \texttt{Signature},
\texttt{MessageDigest}, \texttt{Cipher} --- and a \textbf{provider
model} where concrete implementations are plugged in at runtime. The
\textbf{Java Cryptography Extension (JCE)} extends JCA with additional
capabilities. In modern Java (since JDK 9), the distinction is mostly
historical.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// This does NOT specify which implementation to use}
\BuiltInTok{KeyPairGenerator}\NormalTok{ kpg }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{KeyPairGenerator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"RSA"}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// The JCA searches registered providers in priority order}
\CommentTok{// and returns the first one that supports "RSA"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The JDK ships with built-in providers: \texttt{SUN},
\texttt{SunRsaSign}, \texttt{SunEC}, \texttt{SunJCE}. These cover the
basics --- RSA, ECDSA with NIST curves, AES, SHA-2 hashes. For many
applications, the built-in providers are sufficient.

For PDF signatures, they are not.

\subsection{Why BouncyCastle Exists}\label{_why_bouncycastle_exists}

The \textbf{Legion of the Bouncy Castle} (yes, that is the
organization's legal name, registered in Australia) provides the most
comprehensive open-source cryptographic library for Java. BouncyCastle
is not an alternative to the JCA --- it is a \textbf{JCA provider} that
plugs into the same framework.

We need BouncyCastle for PDF signatures for several reasons:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{CMS/PKCS\#7 implementation.} The JDK does not include a CMS
  library. BouncyCastle's \texttt{bcpkix} module provides
  \texttt{CMSSignedDataGenerator}, \texttt{CMSSignedData}, and
  everything else needed to construct and parse the CMS
  \texttt{SignedData} structures that PDF signatures require (RFC 5652
  --- Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS)).
\item
  \textbf{Algorithm breadth.} BouncyCastle supports algorithms the JDK
  does not, including EdDSA (Ed25519, Ed448), certain elliptic curves,
  and legacy algorithms that appear in older signed PDFs.
\item
  \textbf{ASN.1 library.} BouncyCastle includes a complete ASN.1 parsing
  and generation library --- essential for working with X.509
  certificates, CMS structures, and OCSP responses at the byte level.
\item
  \textbf{Certificate generation.} The \texttt{bcpkix} module includes
  \texttt{X509v3CertificateBuilder} for creating X.509 certificates
  programmatically --- something you cannot do with the JDK alone.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Provider Registration}\label{_provider_registration}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Security}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{provider}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{// Register BouncyCastle as a JCA provider}
\CommentTok{// This must happen before any cryptographic operations}
\BuiltInTok{Security}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{());}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

In a Spring Boot application, register the BouncyCastle provider in a
\texttt{@PostConstruct} method of a \texttt{@Configuration} class. This
ensures it is available before any bean that needs cryptographic
operations is initialized. PDFalyzer does this in
\texttt{BouncyCastleConfig.java}. If you forget this step, you will get
\texttt{NoSuchAlgorithmException} or \texttt{NoSuchProviderException} at
runtime --- and the error message will not tell you that a missing
provider registration is the cause.

\section{Practical Code: Generating Key
Pairs}\label{_practical_code_generating_key_pairs}

\subsection{RSA Key Pair Generation}\label{_rsa_key_pair_generation}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{KeyPair}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{KeyPairGenerator}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Security}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{interfaces}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{RSAPrivateKey}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{interfaces}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{RSAPublicKey}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{provider}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ RsaKeyPairExample }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \BuiltInTok{KeyPair} \FunctionTok{generateRsaKeyPair}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ keySize}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{Security}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{());}

        \BuiltInTok{KeyPairGenerator}\NormalTok{ kpg }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{KeyPairGenerator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"RSA"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        kpg}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{initialize}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{keySize}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ kpg}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generateKeyPair}\OperatorTok{();}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{main}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ args}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{KeyPair}\NormalTok{ kp }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{generateRsaKeyPair}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{3072}\OperatorTok{);}

        \BuiltInTok{RSAPublicKey}\NormalTok{ pub }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{RSAPublicKey}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ kp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPublic}\OperatorTok{();}
        \BuiltInTok{RSAPrivateKey}\NormalTok{ priv }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{RSAPrivateKey}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ kp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPrivate}\OperatorTok{();}

        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Algorithm:    "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ pub}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{());}
        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Key size:     "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ pub}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getModulus}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{bitLength}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" bits"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Pub exponent: "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ pub}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPublicExponent}\OperatorTok{());}
        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Pub format:   "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ pub}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFormat}\OperatorTok{());}  \CommentTok{// X.509}
        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Priv format:  "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ priv}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFormat}\OperatorTok{());} \CommentTok{// PKCS\#8}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The output will show a 3072-bit key with public exponent 65537, the
public key in X.509 (SubjectPublicKeyInfo) format, and the private key
in PKCS\#8 format. These format names --- X.509 for public keys, PKCS\#8
for private keys --- are the standard encodings that appear in PEM
files. Chapter 5 covers key material formats in detail.

\subsection{ECDSA Key Pair Generation}\label{_ecdsa_key_pair_generation}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{KeyPair}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{KeyPairGenerator}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Security}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{interfaces}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ECPrivateKey}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{interfaces}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ECPublicKey}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{spec}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ECGenParameterSpec}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{provider}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ EcdsaKeyPairExample }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \BuiltInTok{KeyPair} \FunctionTok{generateEcdsaKeyPair}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ curveName}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{Security}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{());}

        \BuiltInTok{KeyPairGenerator}\NormalTok{ kpg }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{KeyPairGenerator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"EC"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        kpg}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{initialize}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ECGenParameterSpec}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{curveName}\OperatorTok{));}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ kpg}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generateKeyPair}\OperatorTok{();}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{main}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ args}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \CommentTok{// P{-}256: use the SECG name, not the NIST name}
        \BuiltInTok{KeyPair}\NormalTok{ kp }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{generateEcdsaKeyPair}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"secp256r1"}\OperatorTok{);}

        \BuiltInTok{ECPublicKey}\NormalTok{ pub }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{ECPublicKey}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ kp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPublic}\OperatorTok{();}
        \BuiltInTok{ECPrivateKey}\NormalTok{ priv }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{ECPrivateKey}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ kp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPrivate}\OperatorTok{();}

        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Algorithm:   "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ pub}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{());}
        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Curve order: "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ pub}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getParams}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getOrder}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{bitLength}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" bits"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Pub format:  "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ pub}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFormat}\OperatorTok{());}  \CommentTok{// X.509}
        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Priv format: "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ priv}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFormat}\OperatorTok{());} \CommentTok{// PKCS\#8}

        \CommentTok{// The public key is a point on the curve (x, y)}
        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Pub X:       "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ pub}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getW}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getAffineX}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{bitLength}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" bits"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Pub Y:       "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ pub}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getW}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getAffineY}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{bitLength}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" bits"}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Note the curve name: \texttt{"secp256r1"}, not \texttt{"P-256"}. The JCA
uses SECG names in \texttt{ECGenParameterSpec}. BouncyCastle accepts
both, but for portability, use the SECG name.

\subsection{Performance Comparison}\label{_performance_comparison}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{KeyPair}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{KeyPairGenerator}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Security}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{spec}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ECGenParameterSpec}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{provider}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ KeyPairComparisonExample }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{main}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ args}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{Security}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{());}

        \CommentTok{// RSA{-}3072: \textasciitilde{}128{-}bit security}
        \DataTypeTok{long}\NormalTok{ start }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{nanoTime}\OperatorTok{();}
        \BuiltInTok{KeyPairGenerator}\NormalTok{ rsaKpg }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{KeyPairGenerator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"RSA"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        rsaKpg}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{initialize}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{3072}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{KeyPair}\NormalTok{ rsaKp }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ rsaKpg}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generateKeyPair}\OperatorTok{();}
        \DataTypeTok{long}\NormalTok{ rsaTime }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{nanoTime}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ start}\OperatorTok{;}

        \CommentTok{// ECDSA P{-}256: \textasciitilde{}128{-}bit security}
\NormalTok{        start }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{nanoTime}\OperatorTok{();}
        \BuiltInTok{KeyPairGenerator}\NormalTok{ ecKpg }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{KeyPairGenerator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"EC"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        ecKpg}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{initialize}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ECGenParameterSpec}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"secp256r1"}\OperatorTok{));}
        \BuiltInTok{KeyPair}\NormalTok{ ecKp }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ecKpg}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generateKeyPair}\OperatorTok{();}
        \DataTypeTok{long}\NormalTok{ ecTime }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{nanoTime}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ start}\OperatorTok{;}

        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"RSA{-}3072 keygen:    "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ rsaTime }\OperatorTok{/} \DecValTok{1\_000\_000} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" ms"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"ECDSA P{-}256 keygen: "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ ecTime }\OperatorTok{/} \DecValTok{1\_000\_000} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" ms"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"RSA pub key size:   "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ rsaKp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPublic}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{length} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" bytes"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"EC pub key size:    "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ ecKp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPublic}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{length} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" bytes"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"RSA priv key size:  "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ rsaKp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPrivate}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{length} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" bytes"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"EC priv key size:   "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ ecKp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPrivate}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{length} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" bytes"}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

On a typical machine, RSA-3072 key generation takes 200-1500 ms (it
varies because finding large primes is probabilistic), while ECDSA P-256
key generation completes in under 5 ms. The encoded key sizes will show
the RSA public key at roughly 400 bytes versus 90 bytes for the EC
public key.

These numbers matter in practice. If you are building a self-signed
certificate generator for testing (as we do in Chapter 29), RSA key
generation is the bottleneck. If you are deploying a signing service
that provisions per-user keys, the hundred-fold speed advantage of ECDSA
key generation is worth considering.

\section{Choosing an Algorithm for PDF
Signatures}\label{_choosing_an_algorithm_for_pdf_signatures}

Given all of the above, what should you actually use?

\textbf{For maximum compatibility: RSA-2048 or RSA-3072 with SHA-256.}
Every PDF viewer, every library, every validation service supports this
combination. If your signed PDFs need to be validated by the widest
possible audience --- and they usually do --- RSA with SHA-256 is the
safe choice. Use 3072-bit keys for new deployments; 2048-bit is
acceptable for existing systems but provides only 112-bit security.

\textbf{For smaller signatures and modern infrastructure: ECDSA with
P-256 and SHA-256.} If you control the validation environment (e.g., an
internal document management system) or if you are targeting modern
viewers (Adobe Acrobat 2020+, PDFBox 3.x, iText 8.x), ECDSA P-256 gives
you 128-bit security with much smaller key material. The CMS blob in
\texttt{/Contents} will be noticeably smaller, which simplifies byte
range estimation.

\textbf{For future-proofing: watch EdDSA, but do not deploy it yet.}
EdDSA is the superior algorithm by every technical measure, but the PDF
ecosystem has not caught up. When Adobe Acrobat adds EdDSA
support --- likely driven by EUDI wallet adoption --- it will become the
preferred choice.

\textbf{The algorithm choice affects your certificate, not your code.}

The PDF signing code in PDFBox does not care whether you give it an RSA
key or an ECDSA key. The
\texttt{SignatureInterface.sign(InputStream\ content)} method receives
the document bytes and returns a CMS blob. Whether that blob contains an
RSA signature or an ECDSA signature depends entirely on what key
material you provide.

This means you can switch algorithms by changing your certificate and
keystore --- the signing code remains identical. We demonstrate this in
Chapter 29.

\section{Summary}\label{_summary}

Asymmetric cryptography provides two operations that make digital
signatures possible: signing with a private key, and verifying with the
corresponding public key. The security of the system rests on
mathematical problems --- integer factorization for RSA, the elliptic
curve discrete logarithm for ECDSA and EdDSA --- that are easy to set up
but computationally infeasible to reverse.

RSA remains the dominant algorithm for PDF signatures due to universal
support across all viewers and libraries. ECDSA with P-256 offers
equivalent security at 128 bits with dramatically smaller keys and
faster operations. EdDSA (Ed25519, Ed448) is technically superior but
lacks ecosystem support for PDF signatures as of 2026.

Java's JCA provider model allows BouncyCastle to extend the JDK's
cryptographic capabilities, and BouncyCastle is essential for PDF
signatures because it provides the CMS, ASN.1, and certificate
generation capabilities that the JDK lacks.

In the next chapter, we examine hash functions and the mechanics of the
signing operation itself --- how the document's bytes are reduced to a
fixed-length digest, how that digest is signed, and what happens when
the hash algorithm is compromised (as SHA-1 was, with devastating
consequences for the PDF ecosystem).

\chapter{Hash Functions \& Digital Signing}\label{ch02}

This chapter covers the two primitives that every digital signature
depends on: cryptographic hash functions and the sign/verify operation.
We go deep on the SHA family, explain why MD5 and SHA-1 are still
haunting PDF signatures decades after they were broken, and walk through
the exact sequence of bytes that turns a document into a verifiable
commitment.

If you already know what a hash function does, skip to
\hyperref[ch02-sha-family]{The SHA Family} for the forensic details of
which algorithms you will actually encounter in the wild. If you want
code, jump to \hyperref[ch02-java-hashing]{Java: Computing Hashes}.

\section{What Hash Functions Do}\label{_what_hash_functions_do}

A cryptographic hash function takes an input of arbitrary length and
produces a fixed-size output --- the \emph{digest} or \emph{hash value}.
That is it. One operation, no keys involved, purely deterministic. Feed
in the same bytes, get the same digest, every time, on every machine.

But the properties that make this useful for digital signatures are
specific and non-negotiable:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.7143}}@{}}
\caption{Properties of a cryptographic hash function}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Property
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What It Means
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Property
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What It Means
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\textbf{Deterministic} & Same input always produces the same output. No
randomness, no state. \texttt{SHA-256("hello")} is the same value on
your laptop, on a server in Frankfurt, and on a Mars rover running Java
21. Always. \\
\textbf{Fixed-size output} & Regardless of whether the input is 1 byte
or 1 terabyte, the output is always the same length. SHA-256 produces 32
bytes (256 bits). SHA-512 produces 64 bytes (512 bits). This fixed-size
property is what makes hash functions useful as input to signing
algorithms. \\
\textbf{Avalanche effect} & Change a single bit of the input, and on
average 50\% of the output bits change. The output looks completely
unrelated to the original. There is no way to look at two digests and
determine whether their inputs were similar. \\
\textbf{Pre-image resistance} & Given a hash value \texttt{h}, it is
computationally infeasible to find any input \texttt{m} such that
\texttt{hash(m)\ =\ h}. You cannot reverse a hash function. This is not
encryption --- there is no key, no decryption, no way back. \\
\textbf{Second pre-image resistance} & Given an input \texttt{m1}, it is
computationally infeasible to find a different input \texttt{m2} such
that \texttt{hash(m1)\ =\ hash(m2)}. You cannot find a substitute
document that produces the same hash. \\
\textbf{Collision resistance} & It is computationally infeasible to find
\emph{any} two distinct inputs \texttt{m1} and \texttt{m2} such that
\texttt{hash(m1)\ =\ hash(m2)}. This is stronger than second pre-image
resistance --- the attacker gets to choose both inputs. \\
\end{longtable}

The distinction between second pre-image resistance and collision
resistance matters enormously for digital signatures. An attacker who
breaks collision resistance can prepare two documents with the same hash
\emph{before} either is signed --- get one signed, then substitute the
other. An attacker who breaks second pre-image resistance can find a
substitute for an \emph{already signed} document. Collision attacks are
easier (birthday paradox --- they require roughly 2\^{}(n/2) work for an
n-bit hash), which is why we retire algorithms at the collision
resistance boundary, not the pre-image boundary.

\section{Why We Hash Before Signing}\label{_why_we_hash_before_signing}

You might wonder: why not just sign the document directly?

Three reasons, and they all matter:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Performance.} RSA signing is slow. ECDSA signing is faster but
  still orders of magnitude slower than hashing. A 10 MB PDF would take
  an unacceptable time to sign directly with RSA-2048. Hashing reduces
  the input to 32 bytes (for SHA-256), and signing 32 bytes is fast
  regardless of the original document size.
\item
  \textbf{Fixed-size input.} RSA can only operate on data smaller than
  its modulus (typically 256 bytes for RSA-2048). You cannot feed a 10
  MB document directly into RSA. Hashing solves this by producing a
  fixed-size value that fits within the algorithm's constraints.
\item
  \textbf{Security proof structure.} The security of digital signature
  schemes is typically proven in two parts: the hash function provides
  collision resistance, and the signing algorithm provides
  unforgeability. Separating the two allows each component to be
  analyzed (and replaced) independently. When SHA-1 was broken, we could
  switch to SHA-256 without changing the RSA or ECDSA implementation.
\end{enumerate}

The process is always the same:

\begin{verbatim}
Sign:   digest = hash(data)  -->  signature = sign(digest, privateKey)
Verify: digest = hash(data)  -->  result    = verify(digest, signature, publicKey)
\end{verbatim}

The signer computes the hash of the data, then signs the hash with their
private key. The verifier computes the hash of the same data
independently, then checks the signature against that hash using the
signer's public key. If the hashes match and the signature is valid, the
data has not been modified since signing.

The verifier computes the hash independently. The hash is not sent
alongside the signature --- it is recomputed from the original data. If
an attacker modifies even one byte of the data, the verifier's
independently computed hash will differ from the hash that was signed,
and verification will fail. This is the fundamental integrity guarantee
of digital signatures.

\section{The SHA Family}\label{ch02-sha-family}

The Secure Hash Algorithm family, designed by the NSA and published by
NIST, is what you will encounter in virtually every PDF signature
created after 2010.

\subsection{SHA-1: The Algorithm That Refused to
Die}\label{_sha_1_the_algorithm_that_refused_to_die}

SHA-1 produces a 160-bit (20-byte) digest. It was published in 1995 as
FIPS PUB 180-1 and became the default hash algorithm for essentially
everything: TLS certificates, code signing, Git commits, and yes, PDF
signatures.

The theoretical weaknesses appeared early. In 2005, Xiaoyun Wang, Yiqun
Lisa Yin, and Hongbo Yu published an attack reducing the collision
resistance of SHA-1 from the expected 2\^{}80 operations to
approximately 2\^{}69 --- still impractical, but a clear signal that the
algorithm was weakening. NIST recommended transitioning away from SHA-1
by 2010 for digital signatures.

The practical break came on February 23, 2017.

\subsubsection{The SHAttered Attack}\label{_the_shattered_attack}

A team from CWI Amsterdam (Marc Stevens) and Google (Elie Bursztein,
Pierre Karpman) published the first practical SHA-1 collision. They
produced two distinct PDF files --- \texttt{shattered-1.pdf} and
\texttt{shattered-2.pdf} --- that had identical SHA-1 hashes but
displayed completely different content. One showed a blue background
with the text "SHAttered"; the other showed a red background with
different text.

The numbers:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The attack required approximately 2\^{}63.1 SHA-1 computations
\item
  That translated to roughly 6,500 CPU-years and 110 GPU-years of
  computation
\item
  Google estimated the cost at approximately \$110,000 using cloud
  computing resources
\item
  The collision was found using a technique called an
  \emph{identical-prefix collision attack}, where both files share a
  common prefix and the collision is achieved by appending carefully
  crafted "near-collision blocks"
\end{itemize}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.8000}}@{}}
\caption{How the SHAttered collision was constructed}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Component
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Component
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Common prefix & Both PDFs share an identical header and initial
structure --- valid PDF objects, page definitions, and the beginning of
a JPEG image stream. \\
Collision blocks & 128 bytes of carefully computed data inserted into
each file at the same offset. These blocks differ between the two files
but are constructed so that the SHA-1 internal state converges to the
same value after processing them. \\
Divergent suffix & After the collision blocks, the remainder of each
file can be \emph{anything}. Since the SHA-1 state is identical at that
point, any shared suffix produces the same final hash. The attackers
used this to embed different JPEG image data, producing visually
distinct documents. \\
\end{longtable}

The SHAttered attack has direct consequences for PDF signatures. If a
PDF signature uses SHA-1 as its digest algorithm (SubFilter
\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.sha1} or a CMS SignedData with SHA-1 as the digest
algorithm), an attacker who controls the document content \emph{before}
signing can prepare two versions with the same SHA-1 hash. Get one
version signed, and the other is also "signed" --- the signature
validates on both. This is not a theoretical concern: the SHAttered team
demonstrated it with actual PDF files.

\subsubsection{The Cost of \$110,000}\label{_the_cost_of_110000}

It is worth dwelling on the attack cost because it illustrates how
cryptographic security erodes over time. When SHAttered was published in
2017, \$110,000 in cloud compute seemed significant. By 2020, the cost
had dropped to an estimated \$45,000 due to GPU price improvements. A
2020 paper by Ga\{eacute\}tan Leurent and Thomas Peyrin ("SHA-1 is a
Shambles") demonstrated a \emph{chosen-prefix} collision attack (more
powerful than identical-prefix) for an estimated \$45,000.

By 2026, a SHA-1 collision can likely be generated for under \$20,000.
This is well within the budget of a motivated individual, let alone a
criminal organization or nation-state. The cost trajectory is clear: it
only gets cheaper.

\subsubsection{Consequences for PDF and the Deprecation
Timeline}\label{_consequences_for_pdf_and_the_deprecation_timeline}

The SHAttered attack triggered a cascade of changes across the industry:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  ISO 32000-2:2020 deprecated the \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.sha1} SubFilter
\item
  Adobe Acrobat began showing warnings for SHA-1 signatures (initially a
  yellow warning, later escalated to an error on newer Acrobat versions)
\item
  The PAdES standards (ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04)) require
  SHA-256 or stronger for all profiles
\item
  The CA/Browser Forum prohibited SHA-1 in TLS certificates effective
  January 1, 2017 --- a deadline that was already set before SHAttered,
  but the attack validated the urgency
\item
  Git, which used SHA-1 for commit hashes, began its multi-year
  migration to SHA-256 (still ongoing as of 2026)
\item
  Major browsers dropped SHA-1 certificate support: Chrome (January
  2017), Firefox (January 2017), Edge (May 2017)
\end{itemize}

If you encounter a PDF with a SHA-1 based signature, do not immediately
panic. A SHA-1 signature is not \emph{forged} --- it was created
legitimately at a time when SHA-1 was considered acceptable. The risk is
that someone \emph{could} have prepared a collision before signing. For
documents signed before 2017 by trusted parties, the practical risk is
low. For documents signed after 2017 with SHA-1, ask pointed questions
about why.

\subsection{SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512: The Modern
Workhorses}\label{_sha_256_sha_384_sha_512_the_modern_workhorses}

These are the algorithms you should be using. All three are members of
the SHA-2 family, published by NIST in 2001 as FIPS PUB 180-2.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\caption{SHA-2 family members used in PDF signatures}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Algorithm
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Output (bits)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Output (bytes)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Block size
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Usage in PDF signatures
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Algorithm
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Output (bits)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Output (bytes)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Block size
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Usage in PDF signatures
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
SHA-256 & 256 & 32 & 512 bits (64 bytes) & The default for virtually all
modern PDF signatures. PAdES-B-B requires SHA-256 as the minimum. If you
are signing a PDF today, use SHA-256 unless you have a specific reason
for a stronger algorithm. \\
SHA-384 & 384 & 48 & 1024 bits (128 bytes) & Used when organizational
policy requires stronger-than-256-bit hashing. Common in government and
defense contexts. SHA-384 is actually a truncated SHA-512 with different
initial values --- it uses the 64-bit word size of SHA-512, which makes
it faster than SHA-256 on 64-bit platforms. \\
SHA-512 & 512 & 64 & 1024 bits (128 bytes) & Maximum strength in the
SHA-2 family. Rare in PDF signatures because SHA-256 is considered
sufficient, and SHA-512 produces larger digests that increase CMS
signature size. Used in high-security environments. \\
\end{longtable}

SHA-384's performance characteristic is surprising and worth knowing.
Because it operates on 64-bit words (like SHA-512) rather than 32-bit
words (like SHA-256), SHA-384 is actually \emph{faster} than SHA-256 on
modern 64-bit processors. If you need to hash large documents and your
platform is 64-bit (it is), SHA-384 gives you more security bits at
equal or better performance. This is one of those facts that sounds
wrong but is measurably true.

\subsubsection{SHA-3: The Insurance
Policy}\label{_sha_3_the_insurance_policy}

SHA-3 (Keccak), published as FIPS 202 in 2015, is a completely different
design from SHA-2 --- it uses a sponge construction rather than the
Merkle-Damgard construction of SHA-1 and SHA-2. SHA-3 was selected by
NIST specifically because it has \emph{no} structural similarity to
SHA-2, meaning any attack on SHA-2 would not apply.

You will not encounter SHA-3 in PDF signatures today. No PDF
specification references it, no PAdES profile requires it, and no
mainstream PDF library defaults to it. It exists as insurance: if SHA-2
is ever broken, SHA-3 is ready to replace it without a multi-year
standardization process.

BouncyCastle 1.80 supports SHA-3 for signing, so the migration path
exists when needed.

\section{MD5: The Ghost in the
Machine}\label{_md5_the_ghost_in_the_machine}

MD5 produces a 128-bit (16-byte) digest. It was designed by Ronald
Rivest (the "R" in RSA) in 1991 and published as RFC 1321. It was once
the standard hash function for everything.

MD5 is \emph{broken}. Not theoretically, not in some academic
sense --- practically, completely, irrecoverably broken for collision
resistance.

\subsection{The Wang-Yu Attacks (2004)}\label{_the_wang_yu_attacks_2004}

In August 2004, Xiaoyun Wang and Hongbo Yu (the same Wang who later
weakened SHA-1) presented the first practical MD5 collision at CRYPTO
2004. They could find two distinct inputs with the same MD5 hash in less
than an hour on a standard PC. The collision required approximately
2\^{}39 MD5 operations --- billions of times easier than the expected
2\^{}64.

By 2006, Vlastimil Klima had reduced the attack to \emph{one minute} on
a single notebook computer. By 2008, Alexander Sotirov and Marc Stevens
demonstrated a rogue CA attack using MD5 collisions, creating a
fraudulent intermediate CA certificate that chained to a legitimate
root. This was not an academic exercise --- they obtained a real
certificate from a real CA (RapidSSL, which used MD5 for certificate
signing) and used MD5 collisions to create a rogue CA certificate that
any browser would trust.

Today, MD5 collisions can be generated in \emph{seconds} on commodity
hardware. The algorithm is fully broken for any security-sensitive
purpose.

\subsection{Why MD5 Still Appears in Legacy
PDFs}\label{_why_md5_still_appears_in_legacy_pdfs}

Despite being broken for collision resistance since 2004, you will
encounter MD5 in:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Old PDF signatures created before 2005 using
  \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} with MD5 as the digest algorithm
\item
  Legacy government and enterprise PDFs that were signed by systems
  deployed in the early 2000s
\item
  Some PKCS\#12 keystores that use MD5-based key derivation for the
  encryption of the private key (this is a different use of MD5 --- key
  derivation, not signature hashing --- and while not ideal, the risk
  profile is different from using MD5 for signing)
\item
  The \texttt{/ID} array in PDF trailers, which traditionally contained
  MD5 hashes of file metadata (this is not security-sensitive)
\end{itemize}

If you encounter a PDF signed with MD5 as the digest algorithm, the
signature provides \emph{no} collision resistance. An attacker who
controlled the document before signing could have prepared a collision.
For documents signed by trusted parties before 2004, the practical risk
is limited (the attack was not known yet). For anything signed after
2004, the signature's integrity guarantee is meaningless against a
determined attacker.

\section{The Signing Process Step by
Step}\label{_the_signing_process_step_by_step}

Now we connect the hash function to the asymmetric cryptography from
Chapter 1 and trace the exact sequence of operations that produces a PDF
digital signature.

\subsection{Step 1: Hash the Document}\label{_step_1_hash_the_document}

The signing library reads the bytes of the PDF specified by the
\texttt{/ByteRange} array. This is \emph{not} the entire file --- it is
everything except the \texttt{/Contents} hex string, which is where the
signature will be placed. The gap exists because you cannot include the
signature value in the data being signed (you would need to know the
signature before computing it --- a circular dependency).

The library feeds these bytes through the chosen hash algorithm:

\begin{verbatim}
SHA-256( byteRange[0..offset1] || byteRange[offset2..end] ) = digest (32 bytes)
\end{verbatim}

This 32-byte digest is the \emph{fingerprint} of the document at the
moment of signing.

\subsection{Step 2: Pad the Hash}\label{_step_2_pad_the_hash}

Before the hash can be signed with the private key, it must be padded to
the correct size for the signing algorithm. For RSA, there are two
padding schemes:

\textbf{PKCS\#1 v1.5 padding} (the traditional scheme): The hash is
prefixed with a DER-encoded \texttt{DigestInfo} structure that
identifies the hash algorithm, then padded with \texttt{0xFF} bytes to
fill the modulus size. The structure looks like:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{DigestInfo ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    digestAlgorithm  AlgorithmIdentifier,  }\CommentTok{{-}{-} OID for SHA{-}256}
\NormalTok{    digest           }\DataTypeTok{OCTET} \DataTypeTok{STRING}           \CommentTok{{-}{-} the 32{-}byte hash}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The padded message is:
\texttt{0x00\ 0x01\ {[}0xFF\ padding{]}\ 0x00\ {[}DigestInfo\ DER\ bytes{]}}.

\textbf{RSASSA-PSS} (Probabilistic Signature Scheme): A more modern
padding scheme that includes random salt, making signatures
non-deterministic. PSS provides a tighter security proof than PKCS\#1
v1.5, but it is less commonly used in PDF signatures because the entire
PDF ecosystem was built around PKCS\#1 v1.5. Some CAs issue PSS
certificates, and you may encounter the OID
\texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.10} (RSASSA-PSS) in the
\texttt{SignerInfo.signatureAlgorithm} field.

For ECDSA, there is no padding step --- the hash is used directly as
input to the ECDSA signing equation.

The choice of padding scheme is not just a technical detail. PKCS\#1
v1.5 has known theoretical weaknesses (Bleichenbacher's 1998 attack on
encryption; related issues apply to signatures in certain implementation
scenarios). RSASSA-PSS is provably secure under standard assumptions. In
practice, PKCS\#1 v1.5 remains safe for signatures (as opposed to
encryption, where the Bleichenbacher attack is devastating), but
security-conscious deployments should prefer PSS. The ETSI EN 319 142-1
V1.1.1 (2016-04) standards allow both.

\subsection{Step 3: Encrypt with Private
Key}\label{_step_3_encrypt_with_private_key}

The padded hash (for RSA) or raw hash (for ECDSA) is processed with the
private key:

For RSA: \emph{s = paddedHash\textsuperscript{d} mod n}, where \emph{d}
is the private exponent and \emph{n} is the modulus. The result \emph{s}
is the signature value --- a number the same size as the modulus (256
bytes for RSA-2048, 384 bytes for RSA-3072).

For ECDSA: The algorithm generates a nonce \emph{k} (either random or
deterministic per RFC 6979), computes a curve point \emph{(x1, y1) = k *
G}, and derives the signature pair \emph{(r, s)} from \emph{x1}, the
hash, and the private key \emph{d}. The signature is the pair \emph{(r,
s)}, each approximately the same size as the curve order (32 bytes each
for P-256, totaling 64 bytes).

\subsection{Step 4: Package as CMS
SignedData}\label{_step_4_package_as_cms_signeddata}

The raw signature value is not written directly into the PDF. It is
wrapped in a CMS \texttt{SignedData} structure (RFC 5652 ---
Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) Section 5) that includes:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The signature value itself
\item
  The signer's certificate (and often the full certificate chain)
\item
  The \texttt{SignerInfo} containing the digest algorithm, signature
  algorithm, and signed attributes
\item
  The \texttt{messageDigest} signed attribute --- the hash from Step 1
\item
  The \texttt{signingTime} attribute (optional but common)
\item
  The \texttt{contentType} attribute (always \texttt{id-data} for PDF)
\end{itemize}

This CMS blob is DER-encoded, then hex-encoded (each byte becomes two
hex characters), and written into the \texttt{/Contents} entry of the
signature dictionary.

\section{The Verification Process Step by
Step}\label{_the_verification_process_step_by_step}

Verification is the mirror image of signing, with one crucial addition:
the verifier must independently recompute the hash and compare it
against what the signer claims to have signed.

\subsection{Step 1: Extract the CMS
SignedData}\label{_step_1_extract_the_cms_signeddata}

The validator reads the \texttt{/Contents} hex string from the signature
dictionary and decodes it from hex to DER to obtain the CMS
\texttt{SignedData} structure.

\subsection{Step 2: Re-Hash the Byte
Range}\label{_step_2_re_hash_the_byte_range}

The validator reads the \texttt{/ByteRange} array, extracts the
specified byte ranges from the PDF file, and computes the hash using the
algorithm specified in the \texttt{SignerInfo.digestAlgorithm} field.

This is the critical integrity check: if anyone has modified the PDF
bytes after signing, this hash will differ from the original.

\subsection{Step 3: Compare Hashes}\label{_step_3_compare_hashes}

The validator extracts the \texttt{messageDigest} attribute from the CMS
\texttt{SignerInfo} signed attributes. This attribute contains the hash
that the signer computed over the byte range content. The validator
compares this with the independently computed hash from Step 2.

If they do not match, the document has been modified and validation
fails immediately. There is no need to check the cryptographic
signature.

\subsection{Step 4: Verify the Cryptographic
Signature}\label{_step_4_verify_the_cryptographic_signature}

If the hashes match, the validator checks that the signature over the
signed attributes (which include the \texttt{messageDigest} attribute)
is cryptographically valid. For RSA: compute \emph{m\textquotesingle{} =
s\textsuperscript{e} mod n} and verify that the result matches the
padded hash of the signed attributes. For ECDSA: verify the \emph{(r,
s)} pair against the hash using the public key point \emph{Q}.

If this check passes, the signature is \textbf{cryptographically
valid} --- it was produced by someone holding the private key that
corresponds to the public key in the certificate.

Cryptographic validity is necessary but not sufficient. A signature can
be cryptographically valid and still be \emph{untrustworthy}:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The certificate might be expired.
\item
  The certificate might have been revoked.
\item
  The certificate might not chain to a trusted root.
\item
  The signed byte range might not cover the entire document (the Bochum
  attacks exploited exactly this --- see Chapter 24).
\item
  The certificate might lack the \texttt{digitalSignature} or
  \texttt{nonRepudiation} key usage extension (see Chapter 3).
\end{itemize}

Signature \textbf{validation} --- as opposed to mere cryptographic
verification --- requires checking all of these conditions. Chapter 16
covers the full validation process.

\section{DigestMethod in PDF
Signatures}\label{_digestmethod_in_pdf_signatures}

The hash algorithm used for a PDF signature is determined by the
\texttt{/SubFilter} value and the CMS
\texttt{SignerInfo.digestAlgorithm} field.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4286}}@{}}
\caption{How the hash algorithm is specified}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
SubFilter
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Hash Determination

\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} & CMS \texttt{digestAlgorithm} field & The
hash algorithm is specified inside the CMS structure. Can be any
algorithm the signer chooses (SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512, or legacy
SHA-1/MD5). \\
\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.sha1} & Always SHA-1 & Deprecated by ISO
32000-2:2020. The SHA-1 hash of the entire byte range is embedded as the
CMS content. Do not create new signatures with this SubFilter. \\
\texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached} & CMS \texttt{digestAlgorithm} field &
PAdES-compliant signatures. Must use SHA-256 or stronger per ETSI EN 319
142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04). This is the recommended SubFilter for new
signatures. \\
\texttt{ETSI.RFC3161} & CMS \texttt{digestAlgorithm} field &
Document-level timestamp tokens (RFC 3161 --- Internet X.509 PKI
Time-Stamp Protocol (TSP)). The hash algorithm is specified in the
timestamp request. \\
\end{longtable}

\section{Hash Algorithm OIDs}\label{_hash_algorithm_oids}

When a hash algorithm appears inside a CMS SignedData structure, it is
identified by an ASN.1 Object Identifier (OID). You need to know these
OIDs because they appear in the raw bytes of every PDF signature, and
when something goes wrong, you will be reading hex dumps.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4286}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}@{}}
\caption{Hash algorithm OIDs used in CMS (RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic
Message Syntax (CMS) and NIST)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Algorithm
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Notes
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Algorithm
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Notes
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
MD5 & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.2.5} & Legacy only. Do not use for new
signatures. \\
SHA-1 & \texttt{1.3.14.3.2.26} & Deprecated. Still accepted by some
validators for backward compatibility. \\
SHA-224 & \texttt{2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.4} & Rarely used. Exists for
completeness. \\
SHA-256 & \texttt{2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.1} & The default for modern PDF
signatures. PAdES minimum. \\
SHA-384 & \texttt{2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.2} & Used in higher-security
contexts. \\
SHA-512 & \texttt{2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.3} & Maximum SHA-2 strength. \\
SHA3-256 & \texttt{2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.8} & Future use. Not currently
seen in PDF signatures. \\
SHA3-384 & \texttt{2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.9} & Future use. \\
SHA3-512 & \texttt{2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.10} & Future use. \\
\end{longtable}

The OID \texttt{2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.1} (SHA-256) is the one you will
see most often. The prefix \texttt{2.16.840.1.101.3.4} is the NIST
algorithm arc. The \texttt{.2} subtree is hash algorithms, and
\texttt{.1} is SHA-256 specifically. Memorize this OID --- you will
recognize it instantly in ASN.1 dumps and hex views.

The OID structure \texttt{1.2.840.113549} is the RSA Security arc (the
company, now part of EMC/Dell). MD5 (\texttt{…\hspace{0pt}2.5}) and
various PKCS algorithms live under this prefix. The OID
\texttt{1.3.14.3.2.26} for SHA-1 is under the OIW (Open Information
Warehouse) arc --- a historical artifact from the 1990s standardization
process.

\section{Signature Algorithm OIDs}\label{_signature_algorithm_oids}

The signing operation itself also has OIDs, which combine the hash
algorithm with the public-key algorithm:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}@{}}
\caption{Common signature algorithm OIDs in PDF
signatures}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Algorithm Combination
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Notes
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Algorithm Combination
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Notes
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
SHA-256 with RSA & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.11} & Most common
combination in current PDF signatures. \\
SHA-384 with RSA & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.12} & Higher-security
RSA. \\
SHA-512 with RSA & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.13} & Maximum SHA-2 +
RSA. \\
SHA-256 with ECDSA & \texttt{1.2.840.10045.4.3.2} & Growing in usage.
Smaller signatures than RSA. \\
SHA-384 with ECDSA & \texttt{1.2.840.10045.4.3.3} & Common with P-384
curves. \\
SHA-1 with RSA & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.5} & Deprecated. Legacy
signatures only. \\
RSASSA-PSS & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.10} & Probabilistic RSA. The
hash algorithm is specified in the parameters, not in the OID itself. \\
\end{longtable}

When parsing CMS SignedData from a PDF signature, you may encounter a
mismatch between the \texttt{digestAlgorithm} in the SignerInfo (which
specifies the hash algorithm) and the hash algorithm implied by the
\texttt{signatureAlgorithm} OID. For example, the
\texttt{digestAlgorithm} might say SHA-256, but the
\texttt{signatureAlgorithm} OID might be \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.1}
(RSA encryption, with no hash specified). This is technically allowed by
RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) (the signature algorithm
can be "RSA" and the hash is determined by the \texttt{digestAlgorithm}
field), but it confuses many parsers. Always use the
\texttt{digestAlgorithm} field as the authoritative source for which
hash was used.

\section{When Hash and Signature Algorithms
Collide}\label{_when_hash_and_signature_algorithms_collide}

An interesting question arises: what happens when you pair a hash
algorithm with a signature algorithm designed for a different security
level? For example, SHA-256 (128-bit security) with ECDSA P-384 (192-bit
security)?

The answer is straightforward: \textbf{the overall security level is the
minimum of the two components}. SHA-256 with P-384 provides 128-bit
security, not 192-bit. The hash function is the bottleneck.

Standard pairings:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{SHA-256 with RSA-3072 or ECDSA P-256}: 128-bit security across
  the board. The default for most use cases.
\item
  \textbf{SHA-384 with RSA-7680 or ECDSA P-384}: 192-bit security.
  Government/defense contexts.
\item
  \textbf{SHA-512 with ECDSA P-521}: 256-bit security. Rarely needed.
\end{itemize}

Mismatched pairings are not \emph{wrong} --- they work correctly --- but
they waste resources. Using SHA-512 with RSA-2048 gives you 112-bit
security (limited by RSA) while computing an unnecessarily expensive
hash. Using SHA-256 with P-384 gives you 128-bit security (limited by
SHA-256) while using a curve that provides more security than the hash
can deliver.

In practice, almost everyone uses SHA-256 with either RSA-2048/3072 or
ECDSA P-256. The matched security levels make the overall system
coherent, and SHA-256 is fast, universally supported, and has no known
weaknesses. Do not overthink this unless you have specific compliance
requirements that mandate higher security levels.

\section{Java: Computing Hashes}\label{ch02-java-hashing}

The \texttt{java.security.MessageDigest} class is Java's built-in hash
computation API. For basic hashing, you do not need BouncyCastle --- the
JDK provides SHA-256 and SHA-512 out of the box.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{NoSuchAlgorithmException}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ HashExample }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \CommentTok{/**}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Compute the SHA}\CommentTok{{-}256}\NormalTok{ hash of the given data}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Returns the raw }\CommentTok{32{-}}\NormalTok{byte digest}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*/}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{sha256}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ data}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{NoSuchAlgorithmException} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\NormalTok{ md }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"SHA{-}256"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ md}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{digest}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{data}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{/**}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Compute a hash using a specified algorithm}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Algorithm names}\CommentTok{:} \CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{SHA}\CommentTok{{-}256",} \CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{SHA}\CommentTok{{-}384",} \CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{SHA}\CommentTok{{-}512",} \CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{SHA}\CommentTok{{-}1",} \CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{MD5}\CommentTok{".}
     \CommentTok{*/}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{hash}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ data}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ algorithm}\OperatorTok{)}
            \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{NoSuchAlgorithmException} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\NormalTok{ md }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{algorithm}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ md}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{digest}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{data}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{/**}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Demonstrate the avalanche effect}\CommentTok{:}\NormalTok{ one}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{bit change produces}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ a completely different hash}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*/}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{demonstrateAvalanche}\OperatorTok{()} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{NoSuchAlgorithmException} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ original }\OperatorTok{=} \StringTok{"The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog"}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getBytes}\OperatorTok{();}
        \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ modified }\OperatorTok{=} \StringTok{"The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog."}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getBytes}\OperatorTok{();} 

        \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ hash1 }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{sha256}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{original}\OperatorTok{);}
        \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ hash2 }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{sha256}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{modified}\OperatorTok{);}

        \CommentTok{// hash1 and hash2 will be completely different despite}
        \CommentTok{// the inputs differing by a single period character}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The only difference is a trailing period. The SHA-256 outputs differ
  in approximately 50\% of their bits.
\end{itemize}

For PDF signing, you typically do not call \texttt{MessageDigest}
directly. Instead, the signing library (PDFBox + BouncyCastle) computes
the hash as part of the CMS signing operation. But knowing how to
compute hashes independently is essential for debugging --- when a
signature fails validation, one of the first things to check is whether
the hash of the byte range matches the \texttt{messageDigest} attribute
in the CMS SignerInfo.

\section{Java: Signing with
BouncyCastle}\label{_java_signing_with_bouncycastle}

Here is the complete signing flow using BouncyCastle 1.80. This code
signs a byte array and produces a CMS SignedData structure --- exactly
what ends up in a PDF signature's \texttt{/Contents} value.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{KeyStore}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PrivateKey}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Security}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{List}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaCertStore}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CMSProcessableByteArray}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CMSSignedData}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CMSSignedDataGenerator}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaSignerInfoGeneratorBuilder}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{provider}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{operator}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ContentSigner}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{operator}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaContentSignerBuilder}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{operator}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaDigestCalculatorProviderBuilder}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ SigningExample }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \DataTypeTok{static} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{Security}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{());}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{/**}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Sign data and return DER}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{encoded CMS SignedData}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@param}\CommentTok{ }\AnnotationTok{dataToSign}\CommentTok{     }\NormalTok{the raw bytes to sign }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{byte range content}\CommentTok{)}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@param}\CommentTok{ }\AnnotationTok{keyStore}\CommentTok{       }\NormalTok{loaded PKCS}\CommentTok{\#12}\NormalTok{ key store}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@param}\CommentTok{ }\AnnotationTok{alias}\CommentTok{          }\NormalTok{key entry alias}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@param}\CommentTok{ }\AnnotationTok{password}\CommentTok{       }\NormalTok{key password}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@param}\CommentTok{ }\AnnotationTok{hashAlgorithm}\CommentTok{  "}\NormalTok{SHA}\CommentTok{{-}256",} \CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{SHA}\CommentTok{{-}384",}\NormalTok{ or }\CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{SHA}\CommentTok{{-}512"}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@return}\NormalTok{               DER}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{encoded CMS SignedData}
     \CommentTok{*/}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{signData}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ dataToSign}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{KeyStore}\NormalTok{ keyStore}\OperatorTok{,}
                                   \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ alias}\OperatorTok{,} \DataTypeTok{char}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ password}\OperatorTok{,}
                                   \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ hashAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

        \BuiltInTok{PrivateKey}\NormalTok{ privateKey }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{PrivateKey}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ keyStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getKey}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{alias}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ password}\OperatorTok{);}    
        \BuiltInTok{Certificate}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ chain }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ keyStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificateChain}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{alias}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ signingCert }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ chain}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{];}

        \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{Certificate}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ certList }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}();}                           
        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ chain}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            certList}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cert}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ signatureAlgorithm }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ hashAlgorithm }\OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"withRSA"}\OperatorTok{;}                   

\NormalTok{        ContentSigner signer }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaContentSignerBuilder}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signatureAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{)}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{)}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{privateKey}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{        CMSSignedDataGenerator generator }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CMSSignedDataGenerator}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        generator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addSignerInfoGenerator}\OperatorTok{(}
                \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaSignerInfoGeneratorBuilder}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaDigestCalculatorProviderBuilder}\OperatorTok{()}
                                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{)}
                                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{()}
                \OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signer}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signingCert}\OperatorTok{)}
        \OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        generator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addCertificates}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaCertStore}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certList}\OperatorTok{));}                    

\NormalTok{        CMSProcessableByteArray content }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CMSProcessableByteArray}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{dataToSign}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        CMSSignedData signedData }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ generator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{content}\OperatorTok{,} \KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{);}            

        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ signedData}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{();}                                           
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Extract the private key from the key store. In production, use an HSM
  (see Chapter 5).
\item
  Include the full certificate chain so validators can build the path
  without fetching intermediates.
\item
  For ECDSA keys, use \texttt{hashAlgorithm\ +\ "withECDSA"} instead.
  The algorithm name must match the key type.
\item
  Embedding certificates in the CMS is critical --- without them, the
  validator has no chain to verify.
\item
  The \texttt{false} parameter means \emph{detached} signature: the
  content is not included in the CMS structure.
\item
  The returned bytes are DER-encoded and will be hex-encoded to fill
  \texttt{/Contents}.
\end{itemize}

The \texttt{false} parameter in
\texttt{generator.generate(content,\ false)} is the single most
important boolean in PDF signing. It means "detached signature" --- the
signed content is \emph{not} embedded in the CMS structure. PDF requires
detached signatures because the content (the byte range) lives in the
PDF file itself. If you pass \texttt{true}, the CMS will include the
content, and some validators will reject it because the
\texttt{/SubFilter} says \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} but the CMS
contains encapsulated content.

\section{Debugging Hash Mismatches}\label{_debugging_hash_mismatches}

When a PDF signature fails validation, the most common root cause is a
hash mismatch: the hash of the byte range content does not match the
\texttt{messageDigest} signed attribute in the CMS SignerInfo.

Here is how to debug this systematically:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ASN1OctetString}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Attribute}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CMSAttributes}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CMSSignedData}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{SignerInformation}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{encoders}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Hex}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ HashDebugExample }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \CommentTok{/**}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Compare the hash in the CMS messageDigest attribute against}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ a freshly computed hash of the byte range content}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*/}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{debugHashMismatch}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ cmsBytes}\OperatorTok{,} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ byteRangeContent}\OperatorTok{)}
            \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

\NormalTok{        CMSSignedData signedData }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CMSSignedData}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cmsBytes}\OperatorTok{);}

        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{SignerInformation signerInfo }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ signedData}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignerInfos}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getSigners}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}

            \CommentTok{// 1. Get the digest algorithm from the SignerInfo}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ digestAlgOid }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signerInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDigestAlgOID}\OperatorTok{();}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ digestAlgName }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{mapOidToName}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{digestAlgOid}\OperatorTok{);}

            \CommentTok{// 2. Compute the hash of the byte range content independently}
            \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\NormalTok{ md }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{digestAlgName}\OperatorTok{);}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ computedHash }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ md}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{digest}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{byteRangeContent}\OperatorTok{);}

            \CommentTok{// 3. Extract the messageDigest attribute from signed attributes}
            \BuiltInTok{Attribute}\NormalTok{ mdAttr }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signerInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignedAttributes}\OperatorTok{()}
                    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{CMSAttributes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{messageDigest}\OperatorTok{);}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ embeddedHash }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ASN1OctetString}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    mdAttr}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAttrValues}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getObjectAt}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)}
            \OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{getOctets}\OperatorTok{();}

            \CommentTok{// 4. Compare}
            \DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ match }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{isEqual}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{computedHash}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ embeddedHash}\OperatorTok{);}
            \CommentTok{// If match is false: the byte range was extracted incorrectly,}
            \CommentTok{// or the PDF was modified after signing}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{static} \BuiltInTok{String} \FunctionTok{mapOidToName}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ oid}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \ControlFlowTok{switch} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{oid}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.1"} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"SHA{-}256"}\OperatorTok{;}
            \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.2"} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"SHA{-}384"}\OperatorTok{;}
