            \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.3"} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"SHA{-}512"}\OperatorTok{;}
            \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"1.3.14.3.2.26"}           \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"SHA{-}1"}\OperatorTok{;}
            \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"1.2.840.113549.2.5"}      \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"MD5"}\OperatorTok{;}
            \KeywordTok{default} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \ControlFlowTok{throw} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{IllegalArgumentException}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Unknown OID: "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ oid}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\};}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

When debugging a hash mismatch, the most common causes are: (1) the byte
range was extracted incorrectly --- off by one byte, or not accounting
for the gap correctly; (2) the PDF was modified after signing (perhaps
by a tool that did a full save instead of incremental save); (3) the
byte range array values do not add up to cover the entire file (there
are bytes before or after the byte range that are not part of the signed
content but should be --- see Chapter 13 for the gory details).

\section{Summary}\label{_summary_2}

Digital signatures are built on two primitives: hash functions provide
integrity (any modification is detectable), and asymmetric signing
provides authentication (only the private key holder could have created
the signature) and non-repudiation (the signer cannot deny having
signed).

For PDF signatures:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Use SHA-256} as the digest algorithm. It is the PAdES minimum,
  universally supported, and has no known weaknesses.
\item
  \textbf{SHA-1 is deprecated.} The SHAttered attack proved practical
  collision attacks are feasible. Do not create new SHA-1 signatures.
\item
  \textbf{MD5 is broken.} Collisions can be generated in seconds. Legacy
  only.
\item
  \textbf{The hash is always computed over the byte range}, not the
  entire PDF file. The byte range excludes the \texttt{/Contents} value
  (which contains the signature itself --- you cannot sign your own
  signature).
\item
  \textbf{CMS detached signatures} are the standard: the content is not
  embedded in the CMS structure.
\item
  \textbf{PKCS\#1 v1.5 padding} is dominant in PDF signatures, but
  \textbf{RSASSA-PSS} is gaining adoption for new deployments.
\end{itemize}

The next chapter introduces X.509 certificates --- the mechanism that
binds a public key to an identity, turning a raw cryptographic operation
into a trust relationship.

\section{X.509 Certificates}\label{ch03}

\begin{quote}
A digital signature proves that someone with a specific private key
signed some data. But \emph{who} holds that private key? A public key is
just a number --- a very large number, but a number with no name
attached.

X.509 certificates solve this problem. A certificate is a signed
statement that binds a public key to an identity: "This public key
belongs to Alice Smith at Acme Corp, and I, DigiCert, vouch for it."
This chapter takes apart the X.509 certificate format, field by field,
extension by extension, with the level of detail you need to debug
real-world certificate problems in PDF signatures.
\end{quote}

\subsection{What a Certificate Is}\label{_what_a_certificate_is}

At its core, an X.509 certificate is a data structure containing three
things:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  A \textbf{subject} --- the identity (person, organization, or device)
  that the certificate represents
\item
  A \textbf{public key} --- the subject's public key, which can be used
  to verify signatures created with the corresponding private key
\item
  A \textbf{digital signature} from a \textbf{Certificate Authority
  (CA)} --- a trusted third party that vouches for the binding between
  the subject and the public key
\end{enumerate}

The CA's signature is what gives the certificate its authority. Without
it, you have a self-signed statement: "I am Alice, and here is my public
key, trust me." With it, you have a verifiable claim backed by an entity
that (presumably) verified Alice's identity before signing.

The X.509 standard is defined in ITU-T Recommendation X.509 (originally
from 1988, extensively revised since) and profiled for Internet use in
RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile. When we
talk about X.509 certificates in the context of PDF signatures, we mean
the RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile
profile --- the Internet PKI profile that every browser, every operating
system, and every PDF viewer actually implements.

\subsection{ASN.1 and DER Encoding}\label{_asn_1_and_der_encoding}

Before we look at the certificate structure, we need to talk about how
certificates are encoded, because you \emph{will} encounter raw
certificate bytes when debugging PDF signatures, and you need to
understand what you're looking at.

Certificates are defined using \textbf{ASN.1} (Abstract Syntax Notation
One), a schema language for describing data structures. ASN.1 is to
certificates what JSON Schema is to JSON --- it defines the structure,
types, and constraints. But unlike JSON, ASN.1 data is encoded in a
compact binary format.

The encoding used for certificates is \textbf{DER} (Distinguished
Encoding Rules), a subset of BER (Basic Encoding Rules) that produces
exactly one encoding for any given value. This uniqueness property is
critical: because a certificate's signature covers its DER encoding,
there can be only one valid encoding --- otherwise the signature
wouldn't verify.

\subsubsection{Tag-Length-Value}\label{_tag_length_value}

DER uses a Tag-Length-Value (TLV) structure. Every data element consists
of:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Tag} (1 or more bytes): identifies the type (SEQUENCE,
  INTEGER, BIT STRING, OID, etc.)
\item
  \textbf{Length} (1 or more bytes): the number of bytes in the value
\item
  \textbf{Value} (variable): the actual data
\end{itemize}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}@{}}
\caption{Common ASN.1 tags in certificates}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Tag (hex)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
ASN.1 Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Where You'll See It
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Tag (hex)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
ASN.1 Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Where You'll See It
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{30} & SEQUENCE (constructed) & The outermost wrapper of the
certificate, and of most sub-structures (tbsCertificate, extensions,
etc.) \\
\texttt{31} & SET (constructed) & Relative Distinguished Name components
in the issuer and subject fields \\
\texttt{02} & INTEGER & Serial number, RSA modulus and exponent, version
number \\
\texttt{03} & BIT STRING & The signature value, and the subject public
key value \\
\texttt{04} & OCTET STRING & Extension values, key identifiers \\
\texttt{05} & NULL & Algorithm parameters (RSA with SHA-256 has NULL
parameters) \\
\texttt{06} & OBJECT IDENTIFIER & Algorithm OIDs, extension OIDs, policy
OIDs \\
\texttt{0C} & UTF8String & Subject and issuer name components \\
\texttt{13} & PrintableString & Country codes and other
restricted-character fields \\
\texttt{17} & UTCTime & Validity dates (for dates before 2050) \\
\texttt{18} & GeneralizedTime & Validity dates (for dates from 2050
onward, per RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile
§4.1.2.5) \\
\texttt{A0} & Context-specific {[}0{]} & Version number (explicit tag),
extensions container \\
\texttt{A3} & Context-specific {[}3{]} & Extensions container in
tbsCertificate \\
\end{longtable}

When you see \texttt{30\ 82\ 03\ A5} at the start of a binary blob,
you're looking at a SEQUENCE with a two-byte length (the \texttt{82}
means "the next 2 bytes are the length"), specifically \texttt{03\ A5} =
933 bytes. This is almost certainly a certificate or a CMS structure.
The \texttt{82} prefix on the length byte is the tell: \texttt{81} means
one-byte length follows, \texttt{82} means two-byte length follows.
Certificate structures are almost always in the \texttt{82} range
(258-65535 bytes).

\subsubsection{PEM vs. DER}\label{_pem_vs_der}

You will encounter certificates in two physical formats:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{DER}: raw binary. File extensions: \texttt{.der},
  \texttt{.cer}, \texttt{.crt} (ambiguous)
\item
  \textbf{PEM}: Base64-encoded DER wrapped in
  \texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN\ CERTIFICATE-\/-\/-\/-\/-} /
  \texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-END\ CERTIFICATE-\/-\/-\/-\/-} headers. File
  extensions: \texttt{.pem}, \texttt{.crt} (ambiguous)
\end{itemize}

\begin{verbatim}
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
MIIFYjCCBEqgAwIBAgIQd70NbNs2+RrqIQ/E8FjTDTANBgkqhkiG9w0BAQsFAMDQ
... (Base64-encoded DER bytes) ...
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
\end{verbatim}

The content between the headers is the DER encoding, Base64-encoded with
line breaks every 64 characters. Nothing more, nothing less. To get the
DER bytes, strip the headers and Base64-decode.

The \texttt{.crt} extension is ambiguous --- it can be either DER or
PEM. If you're loading a certificate and get a parsing error, try the
other format. Java's \texttt{CertificateFactory} handles both, but
BouncyCastle's \texttt{PEMParser} expects PEM, and raw
\texttt{X509CertificateHolder(byte{[}{]})} expects DER.

\subsection{Certificate Structure}\label{_certificate_structure}

An X.509v3 certificate has three top-level fields, defined in RFC 5280
--- Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile Section 4.1:

\begin{verbatim}
Certificate ::= SEQUENCE {
    tbsCertificate       TBSCertificate,         
    signatureAlgorithm   AlgorithmIdentifier,     
    signatureValue       BIT STRING               
}
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The "to be signed" certificate --- all the data that the CA signs
\item
  The algorithm the CA used to create the signature (must match the
  algorithm in tbsCertificate)
\item
  The CA's digital signature over the DER encoding of tbsCertificate
\end{itemize}

The CA signs \texttt{tbsCertificate} --- the entire inner structure,
DER-encoded. The \texttt{signatureAlgorithm} and \texttt{signatureValue}
are outside the signed region (they can't be inside it --- the signature
can't sign itself, just as a PDF's \texttt{/Contents} can't be part of
its own byte range).

\subsubsection{tbsCertificate}\label{_tbscertificate}

This is where all the interesting data lives:

\begin{verbatim}
TBSCertificate ::= SEQUENCE {
    version          [0] EXPLICIT INTEGER DEFAULT v1,  
    serialNumber         INTEGER,                       
    signature            AlgorithmIdentifier,           
    issuer               Name,                          
    validity             Validity,                      
    subject              Name,                          
    subjectPublicKeyInfo SubjectPublicKeyInfo,          
    issuerUniqueID   [1] IMPLICIT BIT STRING OPTIONAL,  
    subjectUniqueID  [2] IMPLICIT BIT STRING OPTIONAL,  
    extensions       [3] EXPLICIT Extensions OPTIONAL    
}
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Version: 0 = v1, 1 = v2, 2 = v3. All modern certificates are v3. If
  you see v1, it's either very old or self-signed for testing.
\item
  Serial number: unique within the issuing CA. Must be a positive
  integer, at most 20 bytes. CAs use random serial numbers to prevent
  hash-based attacks.
\item
  Signature algorithm: must match the outer \texttt{signatureAlgorithm}
  field. This redundancy is specified by RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI
  Certificate and CRL Profile to prevent algorithm substitution attacks.
\item
  Issuer: the Distinguished Name (DN) of the CA that signed this
  certificate.
\item
  Validity: \texttt{notBefore} and \texttt{notAfter} timestamps defining
  the certificate's validity period.
\item
  Subject: the Distinguished Name of the entity this certificate
  represents.
\item
  Subject public key: the algorithm and the actual public key bytes.
\item
  Issuer/subject unique IDs: deprecated, never used in practice. Ignore
  them.
\item
  Extensions: the v3 extensions that make certificates actually useful.
  See \hyperref[ch03-extensions]{Certificate Extensions} below.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Distinguished Names}\label{_distinguished_names}

The issuer and subject fields use X.500 Distinguished Names --- a
hierarchical naming scheme from the 1980s that looks like this:

\begin{verbatim}
CN=Alice Smith, O=Acme Corporation, L=San Francisco, ST=California, C=US
\end{verbatim}

Each component is a Relative Distinguished Name (RDN) identified by an
OID:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}@{}}
\caption{Common RDN OIDs}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Abbreviation
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Meaning
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Abbreviation
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Meaning
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
CN & \texttt{2.5.4.3} & Common Name --- the certificate holder's name \\
O & \texttt{2.5.4.10} & Organization \\
OU & \texttt{2.5.4.11} & Organizational Unit (deprecated in TLS
certificates since 2022, but still used in document signing
certificates) \\
L & \texttt{2.5.4.7} & Locality (city) \\
ST & \texttt{2.5.4.8} & State or Province \\
C & \texttt{2.5.4.6} & Country (two-letter ISO 3166-1 code) \\
serialNumber & \texttt{2.5.4.5} & Subject serial number (not the
certificate serial number --- this is a personal or organizational
identifier, like a tax ID or VAT number) \\
E / emailAddress & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.9.1} & Email address
(legacy; modern certificates use the Subject Alternative Name extension
instead) \\
\end{longtable}

The DN ordering in X.509 certificates is \emph{most specific first} (CN
before O before C), which is the opposite of LDAP directory convention
(C before O before CN). This catches people off guard when comparing DNs
between different tools. Java's
\texttt{X509Certificate.getSubjectX500Principal().getName()} returns RFC
2253 format (CN first), while some tools show LDAP order (C first). When
comparing issuer DNs for chain building, always compare the DER
encoding, not the string representation.

\paragraph{Validity Period}\label{_validity_period}

\begin{verbatim}
Validity ::= SEQUENCE {
    notBefore   Time,    -- certificate becomes valid
    notAfter    Time     -- certificate expires
}
\end{verbatim}

RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile §4.1.2.5
specifies that dates through 2049 use UTCTime (\texttt{YYMMDDHHMMSSZ})
and dates in 2050 and beyond use GeneralizedTime
(\texttt{YYYYMMDDHHMMSSZ}). This means every certificate issued today
uses UTCTime for both \texttt{notBefore} and
\texttt{notAfter} --- unless the certificate is valid until 2050 or
later (some root CA certificates are).

For PDF signatures, the validity period has a nuanced interpretation. A
signature made while the certificate was valid remains meaningful
\emph{after} the certificate expires --- the certificate was valid
\emph{at the time of signing}. This is why timestamps are critical: a
timestamp proves that the signature existed at a specific point in time,
allowing validation even after certificate expiration. Without a
timestamp, a validator must decide whether to check the certificate
against the current time (strict) or the claimed signing time (lenient).
Different validators make different choices, which is one of the many
reasons the same signature shows "valid" in one viewer and "invalid" in
another.

\subsection{Certificate Extensions}\label{ch03-extensions}

Extensions are what make X.509v3 certificates useful for real-world
applications. Without extensions, a certificate is just a name bound to
a public key --- no information about what the key can be used for,
whether the certificate can issue other certificates, or how to check if
it's been revoked.

Each extension has three components:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{OID}: identifies the extension type
\item
  \textbf{Critical flag}: if true, a system that doesn't understand this
  extension \emph{must} reject the certificate
\item
  \textbf{Value}: the extension-specific data (an OCTET STRING
  containing DER-encoded content)
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{KeyUsage}\label{_keyusage}

OID: \texttt{2.5.29.15} \textbar{} Defined in: RFC 5280 --- Internet
X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile §4.2.1.3 \textbar{} Typically:
\textbf{Critical}

The KeyUsage extension is a bit string that specifies what the public
key in this certificate may be used for.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.6667}}@{}}
\caption{KeyUsage bits relevant to PDF signing}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Bit
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Relevance to PDF Signing
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Bit
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Relevance to PDF Signing
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
0 & \texttt{digitalSignature} & \textbf{Required for PDF signing.}
Indicates the key can be used to verify digital signatures (other than
certificate and CRL signatures). This is the bit you need for signing
documents. \\
1 & \texttt{nonRepudiation} (contentCommitment) & Indicates the key is
used for signatures where the signer cannot later deny having signed.
Some certificate profiles for document signing set this bit instead of
or in addition to \texttt{digitalSignature}. The RFC 5280 --- Internet
X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile renamed this to
\texttt{contentCommitment} but the old name persists in most tools. \\
5 & \texttt{keyCertSign} & Indicates the key is used to verify
signatures on certificates. Set on CA certificates. If this bit is set
on an end-entity certificate, something is wrong. \\
6 & \texttt{cRLSign} & Indicates the key is used to verify signatures on
CRLs. Set on CA certificates that issue CRLs. \\
\end{longtable}

Some document signing certificates have \emph{only}
\texttt{nonRepudiation} (bit 1) set, without \texttt{digitalSignature}
(bit 0). This is technically valid per RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI
Certificate and CRL Profile, but it causes problems: some validators
check for \texttt{digitalSignature} and reject certificates that only
have \texttt{nonRepudiation}. The safest approach for CAs issuing
document signing certificates is to set both bits. If you're validating
signatures, check for \emph{either} bit 0 or bit 1.

\subsubsection{ExtendedKeyUsage}\label{_extendedkeyusage}

OID: \texttt{2.5.29.37} \textbar{} Defined in: RFC 5280 --- Internet
X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile §4.2.1.12 \textbar{} Typically:
\textbf{Non-critical}

While KeyUsage is a coarse-grained bitmask, ExtendedKeyUsage specifies
more precise purposes using OIDs.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4444}}@{}}
\caption{ExtendedKeyUsage OIDs relevant to PDF signing}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Meaning
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Meaning
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.4} & \texttt{id-kp-emailProtection} &
Historically used for document signing certificates (S/MIME). Many older
PDF signing certificates have this EKU. \\
\texttt{1.3.6.1.4.1.311.10.3.12} & \texttt{id-kp-documentSigning}
(Microsoft) & Microsoft's document signing EKU. Common on certificates
from CAs that target Windows environments. \\
\texttt{1.2.840.113583.1.1.5} & Adobe Authentic Documents Trust &
Adobe-specific EKU for AATL certificates. If a certificate has this EKU
and chains to an AATL root, Acrobat shows maximum trust. \\
\texttt{1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.9} & \texttt{id-kp-OCSPSigning} & For OCSP
responder certificates only. Not for document signing. \\
\end{longtable}

When a PDF signature shows as "untrusted" in Adobe Acrobat but validates
fine in other viewers, the first thing to check is the ExtendedKeyUsage.
Acrobat is stricter about EKU than most other validators. A certificate
without an explicit document signing EKU may be rejected by Acrobat even
if it has \texttt{digitalSignature} in KeyUsage.

\subsubsection{BasicConstraints}\label{_basicconstraints}

OID: \texttt{2.5.29.19} \textbar{} Defined in: RFC 5280 --- Internet
X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile §4.2.1.9 \textbar{} Typically:
\textbf{Critical}

\begin{verbatim}
BasicConstraints ::= SEQUENCE {
    cA                BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
    pathLenConstraint INTEGER (0..MAX) OPTIONAL
}
\end{verbatim}

This extension distinguishes CA certificates from end-entity
certificates:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{cA:\ TRUE} --- this certificate can issue other certificates
\item
  \texttt{cA:\ FALSE} (or absent) --- this is an end-entity certificate,
  it cannot issue other certificates
\item
  \texttt{pathLenConstraint} --- if present, limits how many
  intermediate CAs can follow this one in a chain
\end{itemize}

If you encounter a PDF signed by a certificate with
\texttt{BasicConstraints:\ cA=TRUE}, something is deeply wrong. CA
certificates should never be used for document signing --- their private
keys should be locked in HSMs and used only to sign other certificates.
A document signed by a CA certificate suggests either a misconfiguration
or a test setup that accidentally used the CA key for signing.

\subsubsection{SubjectKeyIdentifier and
AuthorityKeyIdentifier}\label{_subjectkeyidentifier_and_authoritykeyidentifier}

\textbf{SubjectKeyIdentifier} (OID: \texttt{2.5.29.14}): a hash of the
certificate's public key, used as a compact identifier. Typically the
SHA-1 hash of the BIT STRING value of the subjectPublicKey (not the
entire SubjectPublicKeyInfo structure --- just the key bytes).

\textbf{AuthorityKeyIdentifier} (OID: \texttt{2.5.29.35}): identifies
the CA certificate that signed this one. Contains the CA's
SubjectKeyIdentifier (keyIdentifier), and optionally the CA's issuer
name and serial number.

These two extensions are the primary mechanism for chain building:
follow the AuthorityKeyIdentifier from a certificate to find the CA
certificate whose SubjectKeyIdentifier matches.

The SubjectKeyIdentifier is typically a 20-byte SHA-1 hash. Yes,
SHA-1 --- even in 2026. This is not a security concern because the SKI
is used as an identifier, not for integrity verification. There is no
attack value in finding a collision for a SubjectKeyIdentifier.

\subsubsection{CRLDistributionPoints}\label{_crldistributionpoints}

OID: \texttt{2.5.29.31} \textbar{} Defined in: RFC 5280 --- Internet
X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile §4.2.1.13

Contains one or more URLs where CRLs (Certificate Revocation Lists) can
be downloaded to check if this certificate has been revoked.

\begin{verbatim}
CRLDistributionPoints ::= SEQUENCE SIZE (1..MAX) OF DistributionPoint

DistributionPoint ::= SEQUENCE {
    distributionPoint [0] DistributionPointName OPTIONAL,
    reasons           [1] ReasonFlags OPTIONAL,
    cRLIssuer         [2] GeneralNames OPTIONAL
}
\end{verbatim}

In practice, this is almost always a single HTTP URL:

\begin{verbatim}
http://crl.example.com/issuing-ca.crl
\end{verbatim}

\subsubsection{AuthorityInfoAccess}\label{_authorityinfoaccess}

OID: \texttt{1.3.6.1.5.5.7.1.1} \textbar{} Defined in: RFC 5280 ---
Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile §4.2.2.1

This extension is critical for chain building and revocation checking.
It contains two types of access descriptions:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4444}}@{}}
\caption{AuthorityInfoAccess methods}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Method
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Purpose
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Method
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Purpose
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\textbf{OCSP} & \texttt{1.3.6.1.5.5.7.48.1} & URL of the OCSP responder
for checking certificate revocation in real time. The validator sends a
request with the certificate's serial number and gets back a signed
response saying "good," "revoked," or "unknown." \\
\textbf{CA Issuers} & \texttt{1.3.6.1.5.5.7.48.2} & URL where the
issuing CA's certificate can be downloaded. This is how validators fetch
missing intermediate certificates during chain building. If a PDF
signature doesn't embed the full chain, the validator can use this URL
to find the intermediates. \\
\end{longtable}

The "CA Issuers" access method is the safety net for incomplete
certificate chains. When a signing tool embeds only the end-entity
certificate in the CMS (not the full chain), the validator must fetch
the intermediates. It uses the CA Issuers URL to download the next
certificate up the chain, then follows \emph{that} certificate's CA
Issuers URL, and so on until it reaches a trusted root. This
works --- when you have network access. In offline validation scenarios,
the chain must be complete. Always embed the full chain in your CMS
signatures.

\subsection{Certificate Profiles}\label{_certificate_profiles}

Not all certificates are created equal. The purpose of a certificate is
determined by its extensions, particularly KeyUsage and
ExtendedKeyUsage.

\subsubsection{End-Entity vs. CA
Certificates}\label{_end_entity_vs_ca_certificates}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\caption{End-entity vs. CA certificate comparison}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Property
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
End-Entity Certificate
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
CA Certificate
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Property
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
End-Entity Certificate
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
CA Certificate
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
BasicConstraints & \texttt{cA:\ FALSE} or absent & \texttt{cA:\ TRUE},
optionally with pathLenConstraint \\
KeyUsage & \texttt{digitalSignature}, \texttt{nonRepudiation},
\texttt{keyEncipherment}, \texttt{dataEncipherment} (varies) &
\texttt{keyCertSign}, \texttt{cRLSign} \\
Purpose & Signs documents, authenticates TLS sessions, signs email &
Signs other certificates and CRLs \\
Private key location & Software (PKCS\#12), HSM, smart card & HSM
(always, for production CAs) \\
Validity period & 1-3 years typically & 10-30 years for roots, 5-10
years for intermediates \\
\end{longtable}

\subsubsection{Document Signing
Certificates}\label{_document_signing_certificates}

A certificate profile specifically for document signing typically has:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  KeyUsage: \texttt{digitalSignature} and/or \texttt{nonRepudiation}
\item
  ExtendedKeyUsage: document signing OID (varies by CA)
\item
  Subject: identifies the signer (person or organization)
\item
  Validity: 1-3 years
\item
  CRL and OCSP: both present for revocation checking
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{How Different CAs Structure Their
Certificates}\label{_how_different_cas_structure_their_certificates}

Different CAs make different choices, and these choices affect
interoperability:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1818}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2727}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2727}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2727}}@{}}
\caption{CA certificate profile comparison}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Property
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
DigiCert
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
GlobalSign
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
National CAs (e.g., Austrian A-Trust)
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Property
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
DigiCert
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
GlobalSign
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
National CAs (e.g., Austrian A-Trust)
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Key algorithm & RSA 2048/4096 or ECC P-256/P-384 & RSA 2048/4096 or ECC
P-256/P-384 & RSA 2048/4096 (ECC adoption varies) \\
Hash algorithm & SHA-256 or SHA-384 & SHA-256 or SHA-384 & SHA-256 \\
Subject DN & CN = person/org name, O = organization & CN = person/org
name, O = organization & CN = person name, serialNumber = national ID, C
= country code \\
EKU & Adobe document signing OID + email protection & Adobe document
signing OID & Varies widely --- some use EU-specific OIDs for qualified
certificates \\
AATL membership & Yes (since 2012) & Yes & Varies --- many EU national
CAs are on AATL \\
eIDAS qualified & Some certificate types & Some certificate types &
Typically yes (this is their primary purpose) \\
OCSP & Yes, with OCSP stapling & Yes & Yes (required by eIDAS) \\
Certificate Transparency & Yes (required by Chrome/Apple) & Yes &
Sometimes (not required for non-TLS certificates) \\
\end{longtable}

When debugging why a PDF signature is trusted in one viewer but not
another, the CA's AATL status is usually the answer. Adobe Acrobat
trusts AATL certificates. Other viewers (macOS Preview, Okular,
Firefox's built-in PDF viewer) use the OS trust store, which is
different. A DigiCert document signing certificate will be trusted in
Acrobat (AATL member), probably trusted in Windows (DigiCert roots are
in the Windows trust store), and possibly untrusted in Firefox (which
uses its own trust store). Same certificate, same signature, three
different trust decisions.

\subsection{Common Certificate Problems in the
Wild}\label{_common_certificate_problems_in_the_wild}

Years of debugging PDF signatures have revealed a pattern of recurring
certificate problems. Here are the ones you will encounter most often:

\subsubsection{Missing Extensions}\label{_missing_extensions}

\textbf{Symptom:} Signature validates cryptographically but shows
"untrusted" or "cannot determine if the signer is who they claim to be."

\textbf{Cause:} The signing certificate is missing critical
extensions --- typically ExtendedKeyUsage or SubjectKeyIdentifier. Some
legacy CAs (particularly older European government CAs) issued
certificates with minimal extensions that predate the current RFC 5280
--- Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile profile requirements.

\textbf{Fix:} The certificate needs to be reissued with proper
extensions. There is no workaround --- you cannot add extensions to an
existing certificate without re-signing it (which means the CA must do
it).

\subsubsection{Wrong Key Usage}\label{_wrong_key_usage}

\textbf{Symptom:} Signature fails validation with "key usage does not
permit digital signature" or similar.

\textbf{Cause:} The certificate has KeyUsage set to
\texttt{keyEncipherment} or \texttt{dataEncipherment} but not
\texttt{digitalSignature} or \texttt{nonRepudiation}. This happens when
someone uses a TLS server certificate (designed for key exchange) to
sign a PDF document.

\textbf{Fix:} Use a certificate with the correct KeyUsage for document
signing. A TLS certificate is not a signing certificate.

\subsubsection{Expired Certificates}\label{_expired_certificates}

\textbf{Symptom:} "Certificate has expired" warning, even though the
document was signed years ago when the certificate was valid.

\textbf{Cause:} The validator is checking the certificate against the
\emph{current} time instead of the \emph{signing} time. Without a
trusted timestamp, the validator has no proof of when the signature was
actually created.

\textbf{Fix:} Include a timestamp in the signature (see Chapter 18 on
LTV). A timestamp from a trusted TSA proves the signature existed at a
specific time, allowing validators to check the certificate's validity
against that time rather than the current time.

\subsubsection{Self-Signed
Certificates}\label{_self_signed_certificates}

\textbf{Symptom:} "The signer's identity is unknown" or "certificate is
not issued by a trusted CA."

\textbf{Cause:} The signing certificate is self-signed (the issuer and
subject are the same, and it signed itself). Self-signed certificates
have no external trust anchor.

\textbf{Fix:} For production use, obtain a certificate from a recognized
CA. For internal/testing use, you can add the self-signed certificate to
the viewer's trust store manually --- but this defeats the purpose of
the CA trust model.

Self-signed certificates are perfectly valid for testing and
development. PDFalyzer's self-signed certificate generation feature is
designed exactly for this purpose. The cryptographic integrity of the
signature is identical regardless of whether the certificate is
self-signed or CA-issued --- the difference is purely about trust.

\subsubsection{Incomplete Certificate
Chain}\label{_incomplete_certificate_chain}

\textbf{Symptom:} "Unable to build certificate chain" or "issuer
certificate not found."

\textbf{Cause:} The CMS signature embeds only the end-entity
certificate, not the intermediate CA certificates. The validator cannot
build a path from the end-entity to a trusted root.

\textbf{Fix:} Include the full certificate chain (end-entity + all
intermediates) in the CMS \texttt{certificates} field. Do NOT include
the root CA --- the root must already be in the validator's trust store.

This is one of the most common PDF signature problems. Always embed the
full chain (minus the root) in your CMS signatures. The signing code in
Chapter 2 already does this --- note how it includes \texttt{chain}
(from the PKCS\#12 key store) in the \texttt{CMSSignedDataGenerator}. If
your PKCS\#12 file doesn't contain the intermediates, you need to add
them before signing.

\subsection{Java: Loading and Inspecting
Certificates}\label{_java_loading_and_inspecting_certificates}

Here is how to load certificates and extract their fields using
BouncyCastle 1.80.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{FileInputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CertificateFactory}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Date}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{List}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ASN1ObjectIdentifier}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{x509}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{AuthorityInformationAccess}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{x509}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{BasicConstraints}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{x509}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CRLDistPoint}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{x509}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{DistributionPoint}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{x509}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Extension}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{x509}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{GeneralName}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{x509}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{KeyUsage}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaX509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ CertificateInspector }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \CommentTok{/**}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Load a certificate from a DER or PEM file and inspect its fields}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*/}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{inspectCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ filePath}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \CommentTok{// Load the certificate using JCA}
        \BuiltInTok{CertificateFactory}\NormalTok{ cf }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{CertificateFactory}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"X.509"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ javaCert}\OperatorTok{;}
        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{FileInputStream}\NormalTok{ fis }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{FileInputStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{filePath}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            javaCert }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ cf}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generateCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{fis}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \CommentTok{// Wrap in BouncyCastle holder for extension access}
\NormalTok{        X509CertificateHolder holder }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaX509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{javaCert}\OperatorTok{);}

        \CommentTok{// {-}{-}{-} Basic fields {-}{-}{-}}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ subject }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ javaCert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSubjectX500Principal}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getName}\OperatorTok{();}      
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ issuer }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ javaCert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getIssuerX500Principal}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getName}\OperatorTok{();}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ serial }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ javaCert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSerialNumber}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{toString}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{16}\OperatorTok{);}            
        \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ version }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ javaCert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getVersion}\OperatorTok{();}
        \BuiltInTok{Date}\NormalTok{ notBefore }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ javaCert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getNotBefore}\OperatorTok{();}
        \BuiltInTok{Date}\NormalTok{ notAfter }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ javaCert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getNotAfter}\OperatorTok{();}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ sigAlg }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ javaCert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSigAlgName}\OperatorTok{();}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ pubKeyAlg }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ javaCert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPublicKey}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{();}
        \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ keySize }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{getKeySize}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{javaCert}\OperatorTok{);}

        \CommentTok{// {-}{-}{-} KeyUsage {-}{-}{-}}
        \DataTypeTok{boolean}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ keyUsageBits }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ javaCert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getKeyUsage}\OperatorTok{();}                    
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{keyUsageBits }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ digitalSignature }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ keyUsageBits}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{];}
            \DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ nonRepudiation }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ keyUsageBits}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{];}
            \DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ keyEncipherment }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ keyUsageBits}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{];}
            \DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ keyCertSign }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ keyUsageBits}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{5}\OperatorTok{];}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \CommentTok{// {-}{-}{-} BasicConstraints {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{        Extension bcExt }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ holder}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getExtension}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Extension}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{basicConstraints}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{bcExt }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            BasicConstraints bc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ BasicConstraints}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    bcExt}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getParsedValue}\OperatorTok{());}
            \DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ isCA }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ bc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{isCA}\OperatorTok{();}
            \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ pathLen }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ bc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPathLenConstraint}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}
                    \OperatorTok{?}\NormalTok{ bc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPathLenConstraint}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{intValue}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{:} \OperatorTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{;}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \CommentTok{// {-}{-}{-} ExtendedKeyUsage {-}{-}{-}}
        \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ ekuOids }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ javaCert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getExtendedKeyUsage}\OperatorTok{();}              
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ekuOids }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ oid }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ ekuOids}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \CommentTok{// Map OID to human{-}readable name}
                \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ name }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{mapEkuOid}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{oid}\OperatorTok{);}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \CommentTok{// {-}{-}{-} AuthorityInfoAccess (OCSP + CA Issuers) {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{        Extension aiaExt }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ holder}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getExtension}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Extension}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{authorityInfoAccess}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{aiaExt }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            AuthorityInformationAccess aia }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ AuthorityInformationAccess}
                    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{aiaExt}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getParsedValue}\OperatorTok{());}

            \CommentTok{// OCSP responder URLs}
            \DataTypeTok{var}\NormalTok{ ocspAccessDescriptions }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ aia}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAccessDescriptions}\OperatorTok{();}
            \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{var}\NormalTok{ ad }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ ocspAccessDescriptions}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ad}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAccessMethod}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{equals}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{ASN1ObjectIdentifier}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"1.3.6.1.5.5.7.48.1"}\OperatorTok{)))} \OperatorTok{\{}
                    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ ocspUrl }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ad}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAccessLocation}\OperatorTok{()}
                            \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getName}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{toString}\OperatorTok{();}                          
                \OperatorTok{\}}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \CommentTok{// {-}{-}{-} CRL Distribution Points {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{        Extension crlExt }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ holder}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getExtension}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Extension}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{cRLDistributionPoints}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crlExt }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            CRLDistPoint crlDistPoint }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ CRLDistPoint}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    crlExt}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getParsedValue}\OperatorTok{());}
            \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{DistributionPoint dp }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ crlDistPoint}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDistributionPoints}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \CommentTok{// Extract URL from the distribution point}
                \DataTypeTok{var}\NormalTok{ dpName }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ dp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDistributionPoint}\OperatorTok{();}
                \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{dpName }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                    \DataTypeTok{var}\NormalTok{ names }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ org}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{x509}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{GeneralNames}
                            \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{dpName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getName}\OperatorTok{());}
                    \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{GeneralName name }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ names}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getNames}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
                        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{name}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTagNo}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{==}\NormalTok{ GeneralName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{uniformResourceIdentifier}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ crlUrl }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ name}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getName}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{toString}\OperatorTok{();}
                        \OperatorTok{\}}
                    \OperatorTok{\}}
                \OperatorTok{\}}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{int} \FunctionTok{getKeySize}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ algorithm }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPublicKey}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{();}
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"RSA"}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{equals}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{algorithm}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \DataTypeTok{var}\NormalTok{ rsaKey }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{interfaces}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{RSAPublicKey}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPublicKey}\OperatorTok{();}
            \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ rsaKey}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getModulus}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{bitLength}\OperatorTok{();}
        \OperatorTok{\}} \ControlFlowTok{else} \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"EC"}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{equals}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{algorithm}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \DataTypeTok{var}\NormalTok{ ecKey }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{interfaces}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{ECPublicKey}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPublicKey}\OperatorTok{();}
            \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ ecKey}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getParams}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getOrder}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{bitLength}\OperatorTok{();}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \OperatorTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{static} \BuiltInTok{String} \FunctionTok{mapEkuOid}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ oid}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \ControlFlowTok{switch} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{oid}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.4"} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"emailProtection"}\OperatorTok{;}
            \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"1.3.6.1.4.1.311.10.3.12"} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"documentSigning (Microsoft)"}\OperatorTok{;}
            \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"1.2.840.113583.1.1.5"} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"adobeAuthenticDocumentsTrust"}\OperatorTok{;}
            \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.1"} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"serverAuth (TLS)"}\OperatorTok{;}
            \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.9"} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"OCSPSigning"}\OperatorTok{;}
            \KeywordTok{default} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"unknown ("} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ oid }\OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{")"}\OperatorTok{;}
        \OperatorTok{\};}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Returns the subject DN in RFC 2253 format (CN first). For display, you
  might want to parse this into individual components.
\item
  Serial numbers are typically displayed in hexadecimal. Some CAs use
  very large random serial numbers (up to 20 bytes per RFC 5280 ---
  Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile).
\item
  \texttt{getKeyUsage()} returns a boolean array where index 0 =
  digitalSignature, 1 = nonRepudiation, \ldots\hspace{0pt}, 5 =
  keyCertSign, 6 = cRLSign. Returns null if the extension is absent.
\item
  Java's \texttt{getExtendedKeyUsage()} returns OIDs as strings. You
  need to map them to human-readable names yourself.
\item
  The OCSP URL is what you send revocation check requests to. This URL
  must be accessible from the validator's network.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dissecting a Real Signing
Certificate}\label{_dissecting_a_real_signing_certificate}

Let's walk through a real document signing certificate, field by field,
to see how the theory maps to practice.

\begin{verbatim}
Certificate:
    Data:
        Version: 3 (0x2)
        Serial Number:
            0a:55:14:f1:28:1d:45:87:21:f1:27:3c:e5:b6:4f:2a
        Signature Algorithm: sha256WithRSAEncryption
        Issuer: C=US, O=DigiCert Inc, CN=DigiCert SHA2 Assured ID CA
        Validity
            Not Before: Jan 15 00:00:00 2024 GMT
            Not After : Jan 14 23:59:59 2027 GMT
        Subject: C=US, ST=California, L=San Francisco,
                 O=Acme Corporation, CN=Acme Document Signing
        Subject Public Key Info:
            Public Key Algorithm: rsaEncryption
                RSA Public-Key: (2048 bit)
\end{verbatim}

Let's annotate each field:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.7143}}@{}}
\caption{Certificate field analysis}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Field
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Analysis
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Field
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Analysis
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Version 3 & Expected. All modern certificates are v3 to support
extensions. \\
Serial Number & 16 bytes (128 bits) of random data. DigiCert uses large
random serials to prevent hash-based attacks on the serial number. \\
Signature Algorithm & \texttt{sha256WithRSAEncryption} --- SHA-256 hash
with RSA signature. This is the algorithm the CA (DigiCert) used to sign
this certificate. \\
Issuer & \texttt{DigiCert\ SHA2\ Assured\ ID\ CA} --- this is an
intermediate CA certificate. The "SHA2" in the name indicates it's a
post-SHA-1 intermediate. You'll need to find \emph{this} certificate to
build the chain. \\
Validity & 3-year validity (Jan 2024 to Jan 2027). Typical for document
signing certificates. The signer needs to renew before Jan 14, 2027, or
new signatures will be created with an expired certificate. \\
Subject & Identifies the organization (Acme Corporation) and the purpose
(Document Signing). The CN often contains the signer's name or the
certificate's purpose rather than a hostname. \\
Public Key & RSA 2048-bit. The minimum for document signing. Some
organizations require 4096-bit, but 2048 is widely accepted and
faster. \\
\end{longtable}

The extensions would look like:

\begin{verbatim}
X509v3 extensions:
    X509v3 Key Usage: critical
        Digital Signature
    X509v3 Extended Key Usage:
        1.2.840.113583.1.1.5
    X509v3 Basic Constraints: critical
        CA:FALSE
    X509v3 Subject Key Identifier:
        A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6:...
    X509v3 Authority Key Identifier:
        keyid:F8:7E:34:22:...
    Authority Information Access:
        OCSP - URI:http://ocsp.digicert.com
        CA Issuers - URI:http://cacerts.digicert.com/DigiCertSHA2AssuredIDCA.crt
    X509v3 CRL Distribution Points:
        Full Name:
          URI:http://crl3.digicert.com/sha2-assured-id-ca.crl
          URI:http://crl4.digicert.com/sha2-assured-id-ca.crl
\end{verbatim}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.7143}}@{}}
\caption{Extension analysis}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Extension
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Analysis
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Extension
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Analysis
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
KeyUsage: digitalSignature & Correct for document signing. This
certificate can create digital signatures. The \texttt{critical} flag
means validators must enforce this. \\
ExtendedKeyUsage: 1.2.840.113583.1.1.5 & This is the Adobe Authentic
Documents Trust OID. It means this certificate is intended for PDF
document signing and is likely on the AATL. \\
BasicConstraints: CA:FALSE & Correct. This is an end-entity certificate.
It cannot issue other certificates. \\
SubjectKeyIdentifier & A hash of the public key. Used for chain building
(other certificates reference this to identify this certificate as their
issuer). \\
AuthorityKeyIdentifier & Points to the issuing CA's
SubjectKeyIdentifier. Follow this to find
\texttt{DigiCert\ SHA2\ Assured\ ID\ CA}. \\
AIA: OCSP & The OCSP responder URL. Send a request here to check if this
certificate has been revoked. \\
AIA: CA Issuers & The URL to download the issuing CA's certificate. If
the CMS signature doesn't include the intermediate, a validator can
fetch it here. \\
CRL Distribution Points & Two CRL URLs (redundancy). Download the CRL to
check for revocation without OCSP. \\
\end{longtable}

DigiCert provides two CRL URLs (crl3 and crl4) for redundancy. If one
CDN is down, the other should still work. This is a best practice --- if
your CA provides only one CRL URL and it becomes unreachable, validators
that use CRL checking will fail to validate signatures. OCSP is the
preferred revocation checking method (real-time, smaller responses), but
CRL is the fallback.

\subsection{Summary}\label{_summary_3}

X.509 certificates are the bridge between raw cryptography and trust.
They bind a public key to an identity, and the CA's signature on that
binding is what makes the trust model work --- or fail, when CAs are
compromised (DigiNotar), when extensions are missing, or when the chain
is incomplete.

For PDF signatures:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Every signed PDF embeds one or more X.509 certificates} in the
  CMS SignedData structure.
\item
  \textbf{The signing certificate must have \texttt{digitalSignature} or
  \texttt{nonRepudiation}} in KeyUsage.
\item
  \textbf{The full chain} (end-entity + intermediates, not the root)
  should be embedded in the CMS.
\item
  \textbf{AIA extensions} provide fallback URLs for chain building and
  OCSP revocation checking.
\item
  \textbf{Trust is viewer-dependent}: Acrobat uses AATL, browsers use
  their own trust stores, OS-level trust stores vary. The same
  certificate can be trusted in one context and untrusted in another.
\end{itemize}

The next chapter follows these certificates up the chain --- from the
end-entity signing certificate, through intermediates, to the root CA
that anchors the entire trust hierarchy.

\chapter{Certificate Chains \& Trust}\label{ch04}

\section{Why Chains Exist}\label{_why_chains_exist}

In Chapter 3 we established that an X.509 certificate binds a public key
to an identity. A certificate authority (CA) vouches for that binding by
signing the certificate with its own private key. But who vouches for
the CA?

This is the trust delegation problem. A root CA cannot practically issue
certificates to every person and organization on the planet. The
operational burden would be staggering: vetting applicants, maintaining
infrastructure, handling revocations, passing audits --- all for
millions of subscribers. Even if it could, concentrating all certificate
issuance in a single entity would create a catastrophic single point of
failure. If that one root CA were compromised, every certificate it had
ever issued would become suspect.

The solution is \textbf{hierarchical delegation}. A root CA issues
certificates not (primarily) to end users, but to \textbf{intermediate
CAs}. Those intermediate CAs, in turn, issue certificates to other
intermediates or to end-entity subscribers. The root CA's private key
stays in a vault --- literally, in most cases, inside an HSM in a
physically secured facility that is powered on only for scheduled
signing ceremonies.

This creates a chain:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  The \textbf{root CA} self-signs its own certificate. It is the trust
  anchor --- the starting point that must be accepted on faith (or, more
  precisely, accepted because it appears in a trust store you have
  chosen to rely on).
\item
  One or more \textbf{intermediate CAs} hold certificates signed by the
  root CA (or by another intermediate above them). They perform the
  day-to-day work of issuing certificates.
\item
  The \textbf{end-entity} (sometimes called the "leaf") certificate is
  the one that belongs to the actual signer --- the person or service
  that signs PDFs. This certificate is signed by an intermediate CA.
\end{enumerate}

The term "chain" is somewhat misleading. In practice, a certificate's
trust path can be a \textbf{directed acyclic graph} rather than a simple
linear chain. An intermediate CA might hold multiple
certificates --- one signed by Root A, another signed by Root
B --- enabling cross-certification between different PKI hierarchies.
However, for PDF signatures, you almost always deal with simple linear
chains of depth two or three: root → intermediate → end-entity, or root
→ intermediate → intermediate → end-entity. The rare cases where
cross-certification matters are in government PKIs and the EU Trusted
List hierarchy, which we cover later in this chapter.

\section{Chain Structure in Detail}\label{_chain_structure_in_detail}

A typical PDF signing chain has three certificates:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5714}}@{}}
\caption{Certificate chain for a PDF signing certificate}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Position
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Role
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Characteristics
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Position
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Role
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Characteristics
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
1 (leaf) & End-entity (signer) & Contains the signer's public key. Has
\texttt{keyUsage} with \texttt{digitalSignature} or
\texttt{nonRepudiation} set. Has \texttt{extendedKeyUsage} with document
signing or email protection OIDs. Issued by the intermediate CA. Short
validity period (1-3 years). \\
2 & Intermediate CA & Contains the intermediate CA's public key. Has
\texttt{basicConstraints} with \texttt{cA=TRUE} and a
\texttt{pathLenConstraint}. Has \texttt{keyUsage} with
\texttt{keyCertSign} and \texttt{cRLSign}. Issued by the root CA. Medium
validity period (5-10 years). \\
3 (anchor) & Root CA & Self-signed: the issuer and subject are
identical. Has \texttt{basicConstraints} with \texttt{cA=TRUE}. Present
in trust stores. Long validity period (20-30 years). Never transmitted
in the CMS structure of a PDF signature (validators must already have it
in their trust store). \\
\end{longtable}

The CMS \texttt{SignedData} structure in a PDF signature's
\texttt{/Contents} field typically includes certificates at positions 1
and 2 --- the end-entity and intermediate(s). The root CA certificate is
\textbf{not} included. This is by design: the validator must already
trust the root, and including it in the signature would just waste
space. The validator checks the chain by building a path from the
end-entity up through the intermediates until it reaches a certificate
that matches one in its trust store.

Some signing implementations include the root certificate in the CMS
\texttt{certificates} field. This is not wrong per se --- RFC 5652 ---
Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) allows it --- but it serves no
purpose. A validator will not trust a root certificate just because it
appears inside a signature. Trust comes from the trust store, not from
the document. If anything, including the root wastes space in the
\texttt{/Contents} hex string, which matters when you are estimating the
size of the content gap (Chapter 13). PDFBox does not include the root
by default. iText does. Adobe Acrobat tolerates both approaches.

A chain with a \textbf{missing intermediate} is the single most common
cause of "certificate chain not trusted" errors in PDF signatures. The
end-entity certificate is present, the root is in the trust store, but
the intermediate that links them is nowhere to be found. The CMS
structure does not contain it, and the validator cannot download it (or
is configured not to). Always include the full chain (minus the root) in
your CMS \texttt{certificates} field.

\section{Building the Chain}\label{_building_the_chain}

Given a bag of certificates --- perhaps from a CMS \texttt{SignedData}
structure, perhaps from a PKCS\#12 file, perhaps assembled from multiple
sources --- how do you construct the actual chain?

The algorithm relies on two X.509 extensions that exist specifically for
this purpose:

\subsection{AuthorityKeyIdentifier and
SubjectKeyIdentifier}\label{_authoritykeyidentifier_and_subjectkeyidentifier}

Every well-formed certificate contains:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{SubjectKeyIdentifier (SKI)}: an extension on the certificate
  itself, identifying the public key it contains. Typically a SHA-1 hash
  of the public key's bit string. Defined in RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509
  PKI Certificate and CRL Profile section 4.2.1.2.
\item
  \textbf{AuthorityKeyIdentifier (AKI)}: an extension identifying the
  certificate that signed this one. Contains the SKI of the issuer's
  certificate. Defined in RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI Certificate
  and CRL Profile section 4.2.1.1.
\end{itemize}

The chain-building algorithm is conceptually simple:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Start with the end-entity certificate (the signer's cert from the CMS
  \texttt{SignerInfo}).
\item
  Read its AKI extension. This gives you the SKI of the issuer.
\item
  Search the available certificates for one whose SKI matches that AKI.
\item
  When you find it, that is the next link in the chain. Read \emph{its}
  AKI.
\item
  Repeat until you find a certificate where the subject equals the
  issuer (self-signed) or one that matches a trust anchor.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Arrays}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Collection}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{List}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{x509}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{AuthorityKeyIdentifier}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{x509}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Extension}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{x509}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{SubjectKeyIdentifier}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaX509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Build a certificate chain from an unordered collection of certificates}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Matches AuthorityKeyIdentifier }\CommentTok{{-}\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ SubjectKeyIdentifier}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ ChainBuilder }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{buildChain}\OperatorTok{(}
            \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ endEntity}\OperatorTok{,}
            \BuiltInTok{Collection}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ candidates}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

        \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ chain }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}();}
\NormalTok{        chain}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{endEntity}\OperatorTok{);}

        \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ current }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ endEntity}\OperatorTok{;}
        \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ maxDepth }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{10}\OperatorTok{;} \CommentTok{// prevent infinite loops from malformed chains}

        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ i }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{;}\NormalTok{ i }\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{ maxDepth}\OperatorTok{;}\NormalTok{ i}\OperatorTok{++)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \CommentTok{// Self{-}signed? We\textquotesingle{}ve reached a root.}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{current}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSubjectX500Principal}\OperatorTok{()}
                    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{equals}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{current}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getIssuerX500Principal}\OperatorTok{()))} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;}
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ akiBytes }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{getAuthorityKeyIdentifier}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{current}\OperatorTok{);}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{akiBytes }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;} \CommentTok{// no AKI {-}{-} cannot chain further}
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ issuer }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{findBySki}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{candidates}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ akiBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{issuer }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;} \CommentTok{// issuer not in our candidate set}
            \OperatorTok{\}}

\NormalTok{            chain}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{issuer}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{            current }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ issuer}\OperatorTok{;}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ chain}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{getAuthorityKeyIdentifier}\OperatorTok{(}
            \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

\NormalTok{        X509CertificateHolder holder }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaX509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cert}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        Extension akiExt }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ holder}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getExtension}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                Extension}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{authorityKeyIdentifier}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{akiExt }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{;}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
\NormalTok{        AuthorityKeyIdentifier aki }\OperatorTok{=}
\NormalTok{                AuthorityKeyIdentifier}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{akiExt}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getParsedValue}\OperatorTok{());}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ aki}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getKeyIdentifier}\OperatorTok{();}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{static} \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate} \FunctionTok{findBySki}\OperatorTok{(}
            \BuiltInTok{Collection}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ certs}\OperatorTok{,}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ targetSki}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ candidate }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ certs}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            X509CertificateHolder holder }\OperatorTok{=}
                    \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaX509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{candidate}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{            Extension skiExt }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ holder}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getExtension}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    Extension}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{subjectKeyIdentifier}\OperatorTok{);}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{skiExt }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{continue}\OperatorTok{;}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
\NormalTok{            SubjectKeyIdentifier ski }\OperatorTok{=}
\NormalTok{                    SubjectKeyIdentifier}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{skiExt}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getParsedValue}\OperatorTok{());}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Arrays}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{equals}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ski}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getKeyIdentifier}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ targetSki}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ candidate}\OperatorTok{;}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

In practice, chain building can fail for several reasons. The AKI
extension might be absent (legal but rare in modern certificates). The
SKI might not match because the issuing CA generated the SKI using a
non-standard method (SHA-1 of the public key is the norm, but RFC 5280
--- Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile does not mandate it).
Or the intermediate certificate might simply not be available. When
debugging chain failures, always dump both the AKI of the child and the
SKI of the expected parent, and compare them byte by byte.

\subsection{Fallback: Issuer Distinguished Name
Matching}\label{_fallback_issuer_distinguished_name_matching}

If the AKI/SKI matching fails --- or if the certificates lack these
extensions (which happens with very old CAs) --- the fallback is
matching the certificate's \textbf{Issuer DN} against candidate
certificates\textquotesingle{} \textbf{Subject DN}. This is less
reliable because distinguished name comparison is notoriously tricky:
leading/trailing whitespace, different string encodings (PrintableString
vs UTF8String), case sensitivity of certain components --- all of these
can cause a mismatch even when the certificates are part of the same
chain.

RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile section 7.1
defines the comparison rules for distinguished names, and they are
considerably more complex than string equality. BouncyCastle's
\texttt{X500Name.equals()} handles this correctly; Java's
\texttt{X500Principal.equals()} usually does too, but edge cases exist
with certain encoding combinations.

\section{Path Validation: RFC 5280 Section
6}\label{_path_validation_rfc_5280_section_6}

Building the chain gives you an ordered list of certificates.
\textbf{Path validation} verifies that this chain is actually
valid --- that every link is cryptographically sound and that the
certificates meet all the required constraints.

RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile section 6
defines the algorithm in six phases. The full specification runs to
several pages of pseudocode; here we distill the essential checks that
matter for PDF signature validation.

\subsection{The Six Steps}\label{_the_six_steps}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.6250}}@{}}
\caption{Certificate path validation (RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI
Certificate and CRL Profile section 6)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Step
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What It Checks
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Step
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What It Checks
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
1 & Initialization & Set the initial trust anchor (root CA certificate),
