the expected issuer name, the expected public key, and policy
constraints. These are the "inputs" to the validation algorithm. \\
2 & Basic processing & For each certificate in the chain (from root to
leaf): verify the signature on the certificate using the issuer's public
key, check that the certificate is within its validity period (notBefore
⇐ current time ⇐ notAfter), check that the certificate has not been
revoked. \\
3 & Name constraints & Check that the subject name and any subject
alternative names comply with any name constraints imposed by
certificates higher in the chain. A CA can restrict its subordinates to
only issue certificates for certain domains or email addresses. \\
4 & Policy processing & Process the certificate policies extension and
any policy constraints. This determines which policies are valid for the
entire chain. Policy processing is the most complex part of path
validation and is rarely used in PDF signature contexts --- but some
government PKIs rely on it. \\
5 & Basic constraints & Verify that every CA certificate in the chain
has \texttt{basicConstraints} with \texttt{cA=TRUE}, and that the
\texttt{pathLenConstraint} is not exceeded. A \texttt{pathLenConstraint}
of 0 means the CA can only issue end-entity certificates, not
sub-CAs. \\
6 & Wrap-up & After processing all certificates, check the final
certificate (the end-entity) against the expected usage. For PDF
signing, this means checking \texttt{keyUsage} for
\texttt{digitalSignature} or \texttt{nonRepudiation}, and optionally
checking \texttt{extendedKeyUsage}. \\
\end{longtable}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CertPathValidator}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CertificateFactory}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CertPath}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PKIXParameters}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PKIXCertPathValidatorResult}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{TrustAnchor}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Collections}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{List}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Validate a certificate chain using the JCA PKIX path validator}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ This performs the full RFC }\CommentTok{5280}\NormalTok{ section }\CommentTok{6}\NormalTok{ algorithm}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ PathValidationExample }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \BuiltInTok{PKIXCertPathValidatorResult} \FunctionTok{validateChain}\OperatorTok{(}
            \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ chain}\OperatorTok{,}
            \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ trustAnchor}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

        \CommentTok{// The chain list should NOT include the trust anchor}
        \BuiltInTok{CertificateFactory}\NormalTok{ cf }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{CertificateFactory}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"X.509"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{CertPath}\NormalTok{ certPath }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cf}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generateCertPath}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{chain}\OperatorTok{);}

        \BuiltInTok{TrustAnchor}\NormalTok{ anchor }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{TrustAnchor}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{trustAnchor}\OperatorTok{,} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{PKIXParameters}\NormalTok{ params }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{PKIXParameters}\OperatorTok{(}
                \BuiltInTok{Collections}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{singleton}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{anchor}\OperatorTok{));}

        \CommentTok{// Disable revocation checking here {-}{-} we handle it separately}
\NormalTok{        params}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRevocationEnabled}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{);}

        \BuiltInTok{CertPathValidator}\NormalTok{ validator }\OperatorTok{=}
                \BuiltInTok{CertPathValidator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"PKIX"}\OperatorTok{);}

        \ControlFlowTok{return} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{PKIXCertPathValidatorResult}\OperatorTok{)}
\NormalTok{                validator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{validate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certPath}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ params}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The JCA \texttt{CertPathValidator} expects the chain in a specific
order: \textbf{end-entity first, root last (or root omitted)}. If you
pass the chain in the wrong order, validation will fail with a confusing
error message about "unable to find valid certification path to
requested target." This is the single most common mistake when using the
JCA path validation API.

\subsection{Validity Period Checking and Signing
Time}\label{_validity_period_checking_and_signing_time}

There is a subtlety in validity period checking that matters enormously
for PDF signatures. A certificate might be expired \emph{now} but was
valid at the time the document was signed. Should the signature be
considered valid?

The answer depends on whether the signature includes a trusted
timestamp. If the CMS \texttt{SignedData} contains a timestamp token in
the unsigned attributes (see Chapter 18), and that timestamp was
produced while the certificate was still valid, then the signature
remains valid even after the certificate expires. This is the foundation
of \textbf{long-term validation (LTV)} --- the mechanism that allows a
signed PDF to remain verifiable for decades.

Without a timestamp, most validators check the certificate against the
current time. A signature made with a certificate that has since expired
will show warnings or errors in Adobe Acrobat, even if the signature was
perfectly valid when it was created.

This is why timestamps matter so much for PDF signatures. A timestamp
cryptographically proves \emph{when} the signature was created, allowing
validators to check the certificate against the signing time rather than
the current time. If you are building a signing service, always include
a timestamp. Chapter 18 covers timestamps and LTV in exhaustive detail.

\section{Revocation Checking}\label{_revocation_checking}

A certificate may be revoked before it expires. The private key might
have been compromised, the subscriber might have left the organization,
or the CA might have discovered that the certificate was issued
incorrectly. Revocation checking is the mechanism by which validators
discover whether a certificate has been revoked.

There are two mechanisms: \textbf{Certificate Revocation Lists (CRLs)}
and the \textbf{Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP)}. Both have
strengths and weaknesses, and a robust validator should support both.

\subsection{CRL Deep Dive}\label{_crl_deep_dive}

A Certificate Revocation List is a signed data structure published by a
CA that lists all certificates the CA has revoked. CRLs are defined in
RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile section 5.

\subsubsection{CRL Structure}\label{_crl_structure}

A CRL contains:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.6667}}@{}}
\caption{CRL structure (simplified from RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI
Certificate and CRL Profile section 5.1)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Field
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Field
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{tbsCertList} & The "to-be-signed" portion, containing the issuer
name, the \texttt{thisUpdate} timestamp (when the CRL was issued), the
\texttt{nextUpdate} timestamp (when the next CRL will be issued), and
the list of revoked certificates. \\
\texttt{signatureAlgorithm} & The algorithm used to sign the CRL (e.g.,
SHA-256 with RSA). \\
\texttt{signatureValue} & The CA's signature over the
\texttt{tbsCertList}, proving the CRL is authentic and unmodified. \\
\end{longtable}

Each entry in the \texttt{revokedCertificates} list contains:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The \textbf{serial number} of the revoked certificate
\item
  The \textbf{revocation date}
\item
  Optional \textbf{CRL entry extensions}, including the
  \texttt{reasonCode} (keyCompromise, cACompromise, affiliationChanged,
  superseded, cessationOfOperation, certificateHold, removeFromCRL,
  privilegeWithdrawn, aACompromise)
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{CRL Distribution Points}\label{_crl_distribution_points}

How does a validator find the CRL? The certificate itself contains a
\textbf{CRL Distribution Points} extension (RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509
PKI Certificate and CRL Profile section 4.2.1.13) with one or more URLs
where the CRL can be downloaded. Typically these are HTTP URLs, though
LDAP URLs appear in older enterprise PKIs.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{CRLDistributionPoints ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE} \KeywordTok{SIZE}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{..MAX) }\DataTypeTok{OF}\NormalTok{ DistributionPoint}

\NormalTok{DistributionPoint ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    distributionPoint  [}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{] DistributionPointName }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{    reasons            [}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] ReasonFlags }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{    cRLIssuer          [}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{] GeneralNames }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsubsection{The Size Problem}\label{_the_size_problem}

CRLs have a fundamental scalability problem: they grow monotonically.
Every revoked certificate is listed until the certificate's natural
expiration date passes. A busy CA that revokes hundreds or thousands of
certificates per year will produce CRLs that are megabytes in size.

Let's Encrypt, for example, issues millions of certificates. A full CRL
from Let's Encrypt would be enormous. This is why Let's Encrypt does not
publish CRLs for individual certificates (they rely on OCSP and, more
recently, CRL sharding).

For PDF signatures, the CRL size matters because long-term validation
(LTV) requires embedding revocation data \emph{inside the PDF}. A 2 MB
CRL embedded in the Document Security Store (DSS) inflates the PDF by 2
MB. If the chain has three certificates and each CRL is large, the
overhead becomes significant.

\subsubsection{Delta CRLs}\label{_delta_crls}

Delta CRLs (RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile
section 5.2.4) were designed to mitigate the size problem. A delta CRL
contains only the changes since the last "base" CRL was published. In
theory, this allows validators to download a small delta rather than a
large full CRL.

In practice, delta CRLs are rarely used. Most CAs do not publish them.
Most validators do not request them. The overhead of tracking base/delta
CRL relationships adds complexity that the industry has largely chosen
to avoid, preferring OCSP for real-time status and full CRLs for
archival purposes.

\subsection{OCSP Deep Dive}\label{_ocsp_deep_dive}

The \textbf{Online Certificate Status Protocol} (RFC 6960 --- Online
Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP)) provides real-time, per-certificate
revocation status. Instead of downloading a list of all revoked
certificates, the validator sends a request asking "is this specific
certificate revoked?" and gets back a signed response.

\subsubsection{OCSP Request Structure}\label{_ocsp_request_structure}

An OCSP request identifies the certificate by:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The hash of the issuer's distinguished name
\item
  The hash of the issuer's public key
\item
  The certificate's serial number
\item
  An optional nonce to prevent replay attacks
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{OCSPRequest ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    tbsRequest     TBSRequest,}
\NormalTok{    optionalSignature [}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{] EXPLICIT Signature }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{TBSRequest ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    version        [}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{] EXPLICIT Version }\KeywordTok{DEFAULT}\NormalTok{ v1,}
\NormalTok{    requestorName  [}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] EXPLICIT GeneralName }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{    requestList    }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE} \DataTypeTok{OF}\NormalTok{ Request,}
\NormalTok{    requestExtensions [}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{] EXPLICIT Extensions }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{Request ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    reqCert        CertID,}
\NormalTok{    singleRequestExtensions [}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{] EXPLICIT Extensions }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{CertID ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    hashAlgorithm  AlgorithmIdentifier,}
\NormalTok{    issuerNameHash }\DataTypeTok{OCTET} \DataTypeTok{STRING}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{    issuerKeyHash  }\DataTypeTok{OCTET} \DataTypeTok{STRING}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{    serialNumber   CertificateSerialNumber}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsubsection{OCSP Response Structure}\label{_ocsp_response_structure}

The responder returns one of: \textbf{good} (not revoked),
\textbf{revoked} (with the revocation time and optional reason), or
\textbf{unknown} (the responder does not know about this certificate).

The response is signed, either by the CA itself or by a specially
designated \textbf{OCSP responder certificate} --- a certificate issued
by the CA with the \texttt{id-pkix-ocsp-nocheck} extension, indicating
it is authorized to sign OCSP responses on behalf of the CA.

\subsubsection{The Nonce Problem}\label{_the_nonce_problem}

OCSP supports a nonce extension: the requester includes a random value,
and the responder echoes it back. This prevents an attacker from
replaying an old "good" response for a certificate that has since been
revoked.

In practice, many OCSP responders \textbf{ignore the nonce}. They
pre-compute responses for all active certificates and serve them from
cache, which is incompatible with per-request nonces. Adobe's OCSP
responder, for example, does not support nonces. The security trade-off
is real: without nonces, a replay window exists equal to the response's
\texttt{nextUpdate} time. For most PDF signature scenarios, this is
acceptable --- the replay window is typically hours, not days.

When embedding OCSP responses in a PDF for LTV, nonce-less responses are
actually preferable. A response with a nonce is bound to a specific
request and cannot be reused. A cached, nonce-less response can be
embedded in the PDF and validated later by anyone, which is exactly what
LTV requires. This is a case where the "less secure" option (no nonce)
is the correct choice for the specific use case.

\subsubsection{OCSP Stapling}\label{_ocsp_stapling}

\textbf{OCSP stapling} is a mechanism where the entity presenting a
certificate also presents a recent OCSP response for that certificate,
pre-fetched from the OCSP responder. In TLS, the server staples the OCSP
response to the TLS handshake.

For PDF signatures, the equivalent is embedding the OCSP response in the
CMS \texttt{SignedData} structure or in the PDF's Document Security
Store (DSS). This eliminates the need for the validator to contact the
OCSP responder at all --- the revocation proof travels with the
document, just like the certificate chain.

\subsection{CRL vs OCSP: When to Use
Which}\label{_crl_vs_ocsp_when_to_use_which}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\caption{CRL vs OCSP comparison for PDF signatures}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Aspect
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
CRL
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OCSP
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Aspect
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
CRL
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OCSP
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Granularity & All revocations for a CA & Single certificate status \\
Freshness & Updated periodically (hours to days) & Real-time or
near-real-time (minutes) \\
Size & Can be megabytes (proportional to revocations) & Typically 1-3 KB
per response \\
Availability & Can be cached locally; works offline once downloaded &
Requires network access (unless stapled/embedded) \\
LTV suitability & Good: can be embedded in DSS & Good: can be embedded
in DSS (prefer nonce-less) \\
Privacy & Good: the CA does not learn which certificates you check &
Poor: the OCSP responder learns exactly which certificates you are
validating (which documents you are opening) \\
\end{longtable}

For PDF signing services, use OCSP for real-time validation during
signing, and embed both the OCSP response and relevant CRLs in the DSS
for long-term validation. Belt and suspenders. Adobe Acrobat's LTV
process follows exactly this approach.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{InputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{OutputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{math}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{BigInteger}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{net}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{HttpURLConnection}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{net}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{URI}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{DEROctetString}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ocsp}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{OCSPObjectIdentifiers}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{x509}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Extension}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{x509}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Extensions}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaX509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ocsp}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{BasicOCSPResp}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ocsp}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CertificateID}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ocsp}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{OCSPReq}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ocsp}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{OCSPReqBuilder}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ocsp}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{OCSPResp}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ocsp}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{SingleResp}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{operator}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaDigestCalculatorProviderBuilder}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Send an OCSP request and check the response}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Demonstrates the full OCSP round}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{trip with BouncyCastle}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ OcspCheckExample }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static}\NormalTok{ SingleResp }\FunctionTok{checkOcsp}\OperatorTok{(}
            \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{,}
            \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ issuer}\OperatorTok{,}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ ocspUrl}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

        \CommentTok{// Build the CertificateID that identifies the cert to check}
\NormalTok{        CertificateID certId }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CertificateID}\OperatorTok{(}
                \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaDigestCalculatorProviderBuilder}\OperatorTok{()}
                        \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{()}
                        \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{CertificateID}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HASH\_SHA1}\OperatorTok{),}
                \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaX509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{issuer}\OperatorTok{),}
\NormalTok{                cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSerialNumber}\OperatorTok{());}

        \CommentTok{// Build the OCSP request}
\NormalTok{        OCSPReqBuilder reqBuilder }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OCSPReqBuilder}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        reqBuilder}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addRequest}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certId}\OperatorTok{);}

        \CommentTok{// Add a nonce (optional {-}{-} many responders ignore it)}
        \BuiltInTok{BigInteger}\NormalTok{ nonce }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{BigInteger}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{valueOf}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{currentTimeMillis}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{        Extension nonceExt }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{Extension}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                OCSPObjectIdentifiers}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{id\_pkix\_ocsp\_nonce}\OperatorTok{,}
                \KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{,}
                \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{DEROctetString}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{nonce}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toByteArray}\OperatorTok{()));}
\NormalTok{        reqBuilder}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestExtensions}\OperatorTok{(}
                \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{Extensions}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new}\NormalTok{ Extension}\OperatorTok{[]\{}\NormalTok{ nonceExt }\OperatorTok{\}));}

\NormalTok{        OCSPReq ocspReq }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ reqBuilder}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{();}

        \CommentTok{// Send the request via HTTP POST}
        \BuiltInTok{URI}\NormalTok{ uri }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{URI}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ocspUrl}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\NormalTok{ conn }\OperatorTok{=}
                \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ uri}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toURL}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{openConnection}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestMethod}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"POST"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestProperty}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Content{-}Type"}\OperatorTok{,}
                \StringTok{"application/ocsp{-}request"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDoOutput}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}

        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{OutputStream}\NormalTok{ os }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getOutputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            os}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ocspReq}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{());}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \CommentTok{// Parse the response}
\NormalTok{        OCSPResp ocspResp}\OperatorTok{;}
        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{InputStream}\NormalTok{ is }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            ocspResp }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OCSPResp}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{is}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ocspResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getStatus}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{!=}\NormalTok{ OCSPResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SUCCESSFUL}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \ControlFlowTok{throw} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{RuntimeException}\OperatorTok{(}
                    \StringTok{"OCSP response status: "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ ocspResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getStatus}\OperatorTok{());}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

\NormalTok{        BasicOCSPResp basicResp }\OperatorTok{=}
                \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{BasicOCSPResp}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ ocspResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getResponseObject}\OperatorTok{();}

        \CommentTok{// Find the response for our certificate}
        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{SingleResp singleResp }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ basicResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getResponses}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{singleResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertID}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{equals}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certId}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ singleResp}\OperatorTok{;}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{;} \CommentTok{// no matching response found}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Trust Stores}\label{_trust_stores}

A trust store is a collection of root CA certificates that a system has
decided to trust. Without a trust store, there is no anchor for chain
validation --- every chain ends in a self-signed certificate, and if
that certificate is not in a trust store, the chain is untrusted
regardless of how cryptographically correct it is.

Different ecosystems maintain different trust stores, and understanding
which one applies to your PDF signatures is critical.

\subsection{Adobe Approved Trust List
(AATL)}\label{_adobe_approved_trust_list_aatl}

The \textbf{Adobe Approved Trust List} is a curated set of root
certificates that Adobe Acrobat and Adobe Reader trust for signature
validation. If your signing certificate chains to an AATL root, Acrobat
shows a green checkmark with "Signed and all signatures are valid." If
it does not --- even if the certificate is perfectly valid, issued by a
reputable CA, and trusted by your operating system --- Acrobat shows a
yellow warning triangle.

This is the single most important trust store for PDF signatures in
practice, because Adobe Acrobat has approximately 75\% of the worldwide
PDF viewer market share.

\subsubsection{How AATL Works}\label{_how_aatl_works}

Adobe publishes the AATL as a signed file that Acrobat downloads
automatically (the URL is hardcoded). The list is refreshed every 30
days. It contains approximately 250 root certificates from CAs around
the world.

To get on the AATL, a CA must:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Pass a WebTrust or ETSI audit for PDF signing
\item
  Meet Adobe's technical requirements (minimum RSA-2048, SHA-256 or
  stronger)
\item
  Sign an agreement with Adobe
\item
  Pay an annual fee (reportedly in the range of \$10,000-\$50,000 USD,
  though Adobe does not publish the exact figure)
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection{The Monopoly Problem}\label{_the_monopoly_problem}

The AATL creates a de facto monopoly on "valid" PDF signatures in the
world's dominant viewer. If you want your signed PDFs to show a green
checkmark in Acrobat, you need a certificate from an AATL-member CA.
Those certificates cost significantly more than standard TLS or
code-signing certificates --- typically \$200-\$500 per year for a basic
document signing certificate, and considerably more for organizational
certificates.

This pricing creates a barrier to entry. An individual developer who
wants to sign PDFs "properly" (by Acrobat's standards) must pay for an
AATL-rooted certificate, even though the cryptographic security of a
self-signed certificate is identical. The trust model imposes a
financial cost on trust itself.

This is not unique to Adobe --- the TLS ecosystem has a similar dynamic
with browser trust stores. But in TLS, Let's Encrypt disrupted the
pricing model by offering free certificates. There is no Let's Encrypt
for PDF signing certificates. The AATL's requirements (particularly the
audit requirements) make a free document signing CA impractical.

\subsection{EU Trusted List (EUTL)}\label{_eu_trusted_list_eutl}

The \textbf{European Union Trusted List} is a fundamentally different
approach to trust. Instead of a single vendor curating a list, the EUTL
is a hierarchy of machine-readable, cryptographically signed XML
documents maintained by the 27 EU member states under the authority of
the eIDAS regulation (Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS)).

\subsubsection{Structure: The LOT
Hierarchy}\label{_structure_the_lot_hierarchy}

The EUTL follows a \textbf{List of Trusted Lists (LOT)} architecture:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  The \textbf{European Commission} publishes the LOT --- a signed XML
  document listing the URLs of every member state's national trusted
  list.
\item
  Each \textbf{member state} publishes its own trusted list --- a signed
  XML document listing every Qualified Trust Service Provider (QTSP) in
  that country, along with the services they offer and the certificates
  associated with those services.
\item
  Each QTSP entry includes the \textbf{service type} (e.g., issuing
  qualified certificates for electronic signatures), the \textbf{service
  status} (granted, withdrawn, etc.), and the relevant certificates.
\end{enumerate}

The LOT is defined by ETSI TS 119 612 V2.1.1 (2015-07) and is publicly
accessible at a well-known URL maintained by the European Commission.

\subsubsection{How EUTL Differs from
AATL}\label{_how_eutl_differs_from_aatl}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\caption{AATL vs EUTL comparison}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Aspect
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
AATL
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
EUTL
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Aspect
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
AATL
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
EUTL
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Governance & Adobe (private company) & European Commission + member
states (public authority) \\
Legal basis & Adobe's terms of service & eIDAS regulation (Regulation
(EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS)), legally binding across 27 member states \\
Format & Proprietary signed file & Machine-readable XML, signed per ETSI
TS 119 612 V2.1.1 (2015-07) \\
Scope & Any CA willing to pay and pass audit & Only Qualified Trust
Service Providers recognized under eIDAS \\
Cost to CAs & Annual fee to Adobe & No fee to EU; the QTSP status is
granted by the national supervisory body \\
Viewer support & Adobe Acrobat (dominant), some others & Adobe Acrobat
(imports EUTL), EU DSS validation framework, national validators \\
Legal effect & None (Acrobat showing "valid" has no legal meaning) &
Qualified electronic signatures have the legal equivalent of handwritten
signatures across the EU \\
\end{longtable}

Adobe Acrobat can import the EUTL in addition to the AATL. When
configured to use both, Acrobat validates against the union of AATL and
EUTL trust anchors. In European deployments, this is the standard
configuration.

A signature that validates against the EUTL as a qualified electronic
signature has legal standing that an AATL-only signature does not. The
EUTL is not just a technical trust store --- it is a legal instrument.
If you are operating in the EU, the EUTL is the authoritative trust
store for determining whether a signature meets the highest legal tier.
Chapter 19 covers the eIDAS legal framework in detail.

\subsection{Operating System Trust
Stores}\label{_operating_system_trust_stores}

Every major operating system maintains its own root CA trust store:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Windows}: The Microsoft Root Certificate Program. Certificates
  are distributed via Windows Update. The store is managed through the
  \texttt{certmgr.msc} snap-in or via \texttt{certutil}. Programs using
  the Windows CryptoAPI (Schannel, .NET) use this store automatically.
\item
  \textbf{macOS}: The Apple Root Certificate Program. Certificates are
  in the System Keychain. Managed through Keychain Access or
  \texttt{security} command-line tool. Programs using Security.framework
  (Safari, macOS apps) use this store.
\item
  \textbf{Linux}: Varies by distribution. Debian/Ubuntu use
  \texttt{/etc/ssl/certs/} and the \texttt{ca-certificates} package. Red
  Hat/Fedora use \texttt{/etc/pki/tls/certs/} and the \texttt{ca-trust}
  system. Updated via package manager.
\end{itemize}

For PDF signatures, OS trust stores are relevant primarily when using
system-level PDF viewers (Preview on macOS, Evince on Linux) or when a
Java application is configured to use the OS trust store instead of
Java's cacerts. Adobe Acrobat on Windows and macOS does \textbf{not} use
the OS trust store for signature validation by default --- it uses the
AATL (and optionally the EUTL).

\subsection{Java cacerts}\label{_java_cacerts}

Java ships with its own trust store: a PKCS\#12 file (or JKS in older
JDKs) located at \texttt{\$JAVA\_HOME/lib/security/cacerts}. The default
password is the string \texttt{changeit} (and almost nobody changes it).

The Java cacerts store is maintained by the JDK vendor (Oracle, Eclipse
Adoptium, Amazon Corretto, etc.) and typically mirrors the major
browser/OS trust stores with some additions and removals.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{FileInputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{KeyStore}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Enumeration}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ List all trusted root certificates in the Java cacerts store}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ CacertsExample }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{listTrustedRoots}\OperatorTok{()} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ cacertsPath }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getProperty}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"java.home"}\OperatorTok{)}
                \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"/lib/security/cacerts"}\OperatorTok{;}

        \BuiltInTok{KeyStore}\NormalTok{ ks }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{KeyStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"PKCS12"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{FileInputStream}\NormalTok{ fis }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{FileInputStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cacertsPath}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            ks}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{load}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{fis}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"changeit"}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toCharArray}\OperatorTok{());}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \BuiltInTok{Enumeration}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ aliases }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ks}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{aliases}\OperatorTok{();}
        \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ count }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{;}
        \ControlFlowTok{while} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{aliases}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{hasMoreElements}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ alias }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ aliases}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{nextElement}\OperatorTok{();}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ks}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{isCertificateEntry}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{alias}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{)}
\NormalTok{                        ks}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{alias}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{                count}\OperatorTok{++;}
                \CommentTok{// cert.getSubjectX500Principal().getName()}
                \CommentTok{// gives the root CA\textquotesingle{}s distinguished name}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
        \CommentTok{// Typical count: 100{-}150 root certificates}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

To add a custom root CA to the Java trust store (for testing or internal
PKIs):

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ExtensionTok{keytool} \AttributeTok{{-}importcert} \DataTypeTok{\textbackslash{}}
    \AttributeTok{{-}alias} \StringTok{"my{-}internal{-}ca"} \DataTypeTok{\textbackslash{}}
    \AttributeTok{{-}file}\NormalTok{ my{-}root{-}ca.pem }\DataTypeTok{\textbackslash{}}
    \AttributeTok{{-}keystore} \VariableTok{$JAVA\_HOME}\NormalTok{/lib/security/cacerts }\DataTypeTok{\textbackslash{}}
    \AttributeTok{{-}storepass}\NormalTok{ changeit}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Modifying the system cacerts file affects all Java applications on that
machine. For production deployments, prefer creating a separate trust
store and configuring your application to use it via
\texttt{javax.net.ssl.trustStore} system property or programmatic
\texttt{TrustManagerFactory} configuration. Do not modify the global
cacerts unless you are administering the entire machine.

\subsection{Custom Trust Stores}\label{_custom_trust_stores}

Sometimes none of the standard trust stores are appropriate:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Internal PKIs}: Your organization runs its own CA hierarchy
  for internal document signing. The root CA is not in AATL, EUTL, or
  any OS trust store, because it was never intended to be publicly
  trusted.
\item
  \textbf{Restricted trust}: You want to validate PDF signatures but
  only trust a specific subset of CAs --- perhaps only EUTL-listed
  QTSPs, not the entire AATL.
\item
  \textbf{Air-gapped environments}: Systems with no network access
  cannot download CRL/OCSP responses or refresh trust stores. All trust
  material must be pre-loaded.
\end{itemize}

In Java, creating a custom trust store is straightforward:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{FileOutputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{KeyStore}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CertificateFactory}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{FileInputStream}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Create a custom trust store containing only your organization}\CommentTok{\textquotesingle{}}\NormalTok{s}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ root CA certificate}\CommentTok{. }\NormalTok{Use this for internal PKI validation}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ CustomTrustStoreExample }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{createCustomTrustStore}\OperatorTok{(}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ rootCertPath}\OperatorTok{,}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ trustStorePath}\OperatorTok{,}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ password}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

        \BuiltInTok{CertificateFactory}\NormalTok{ cf }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{CertificateFactory}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"X.509"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ rootCert}\OperatorTok{;}
        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{FileInputStream}\NormalTok{ fis }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{FileInputStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{rootCertPath}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            rootCert }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ cf}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generateCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{fis}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \BuiltInTok{KeyStore}\NormalTok{ trustStore }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{KeyStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"PKCS12"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        trustStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{load}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{,} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{);} \CommentTok{// initialize empty store}
\NormalTok{        trustStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCertificateEntry}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"internal{-}root"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ rootCert}\OperatorTok{);}

        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{FileOutputStream}\NormalTok{ fos }\OperatorTok{=}
                \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{FileOutputStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{trustStorePath}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            trustStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{store}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{fos}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ password}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toCharArray}\OperatorTok{());}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

update \\

EU validators & Evince, Preview, system viewers & PDFBox-based apps
(depends on config) \\
Cost for CA inclusion & Annual fee + audit & National supervisory body
approval & Vendor program requirements & JDK vendor decision \\
\end{longtable}

\section{Debugging Chain Failures}\label{_debugging_chain_failures}

When a PDF signature shows "certificate chain not trusted," here is the
systematic approach:

\subsection{Step 1: Extract the
Certificates}\label{_step_1_extract_the_certificates}

Open the PDF in PDFalyzer or use PDFBox to extract the CMS
\texttt{SignedData} from \texttt{/Contents}. List every certificate in
the CMS \texttt{certificates} field. Note the subject DN, issuer DN,
serial number, SKI, and AKI of each.

\subsection{Step 2: Build the Chain
Manually}\label{_step_2_build_the_chain_manually}

Starting from the signing certificate, match AKI to SKI. Can you build a
complete chain from the signer to a self-signed root? If a link is
missing, that is your problem --- the CMS is missing an intermediate.

\subsection{Step 3: Check the Trust
Anchor}\label{_step_3_check_the_trust_anchor}

Is the root certificate in the relevant trust store? If you are
validating in Acrobat, check the AATL. If you are validating in your
Java application, check cacerts or your custom trust store. The root
being "well-known" is not enough --- it must be in the specific trust
store the validator is using.

\subsection{Step 4: Check Validity
Periods}\label{_step_4_check_validity_periods}

Is every certificate in the chain currently within its validity period?
Or, if the signature has a timestamp, was every certificate valid at the
time the timestamp was created?

\subsection{Step 5: Check Revocation}\label{_step_5_check_revocation}

Is every certificate in the chain un-revoked? Can the validator reach
the CRL distribution points or OCSP responders listed in the
certificates? Firewall rules, proxy settings, and DNS resolution
failures are common culprits in enterprise environments.

\subsection{Step 6: Check Key Usage}\label{_step_6_check_key_usage}

Does the signing certificate have the correct \texttt{keyUsage} bits
set? Some validators (including Adobe Acrobat) require
\texttt{nonRepudiation} (also called \texttt{contentCommitment}) for
document signatures. Others accept \texttt{digitalSignature}. If neither
is set, the certificate is not suitable for signing regardless of chain
validity.

When you encounter a chain failure in production, resist the urge to
immediately add the root certificate to your trust store. First,
understand \emph{why} the chain is failing. Is it a missing
intermediate? A revoked certificate? A validator misconfiguration?
Adding a root to a trust store should be a deliberate decision, not a
debugging shortcut. Every root you add is a root you trust to issue
certificates for any purpose --- including, potentially, certificates
that could impersonate other entities.

\section{Cross-Certification and Bridge
CAs}\label{_cross_certification_and_bridge_cas}

We mentioned earlier that trust paths can be graphs, not just chains.
\textbf{Cross-certification} is the mechanism that creates these more
complex topologies.

When two independent PKI hierarchies need to trust each other's
certificates, they can cross-certify: Root A issues a certificate for
Root B's public key (or an intermediate in B's hierarchy), and vice
versa. This creates a trust bridge between the two hierarchies without
requiring end-entity certificates to be re-issued.

The US Federal PKI uses a \textbf{Bridge CA} architecture: the Federal
Bridge CA cross-certifies with individual agency CAs, enabling
certificates issued by the Department of Defense to be validated by
systems that trust the Department of State's root, and vice versa.

For PDF signatures, cross-certification is relevant when a signing
certificate chains to a root that is not directly in the validator's
trust store, but \emph{is} cross-certified with a root that is. The
chain-building algorithm must consider cross-certificates as additional
links, potentially finding a path through an unexpected route.

This adds complexity to chain building, and not all validators handle it
correctly. Adobe Acrobat supports cross-certification for AATL and EUTL
roots. Java's \texttt{CertPathValidator} supports it if you provide the
cross-certificates as additional material via
\texttt{PKIXBuilderParameters.addCertStore()}. PDFBox relies on
BouncyCastle for chain building and validation, and BouncyCastle handles
cross-certification correctly in most cases.

If you are debugging a chain failure and the certificates seem correct
but the path does not reach a trust anchor, check for
cross-certificates. The missing link might not be a traditional
intermediate CA but a cross-certificate that bridges two PKI
hierarchies. Cross-certificates are sometimes stored in LDAP directories
rather than embedded in the CMS structure, making them easy to overlook.

\section{Summary}\label{_summary_4}

Certificate chains exist because no single CA can practically serve
everyone. The hierarchical delegation model --- root CAs issuing to
intermediate CAs, intermediates issuing to end-entities --- distributes
the operational burden while maintaining a chain of trust back to a
known anchor.

Chain building matches AuthorityKeyIdentifier to SubjectKeyIdentifier,
walking from the end-entity up through intermediates to the root. Path
validation (RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile
section 6) verifies that every link in the chain is cryptographically
sound, temporally valid, and compliant with the constraints imposed by
certificates higher in the chain.

Revocation checking --- via CRLs for bulk status and OCSP for real-time,
per-certificate status --- ensures that compromised or withdrawn
certificates are detected. For PDF signatures, embedding revocation data
in the document (via the DSS or CMS unsigned attributes) enables
long-term validation without network access.

The trust store anchoring the chain determines whether a signature is
considered "trusted." In the PDF world, the AATL dominates because Adobe
Acrobat dominates. In the EU, the EUTL provides legally binding trust
anchors for qualified electronic signatures. OS trust stores and Java's
cacerts serve broader ecosystems but are not the primary validation
paths for most PDF viewers.

When chain validation fails, the debugging process is methodical:
extract certificates, build the chain manually, check the trust anchor,
verify validity periods, check revocation status, and verify key usage.
Most chain failures in production are caused by missing intermediates,
not by actual trust problems.

The next chapter examines the formats in which keys and certificates are
stored and transported --- PKCS\#12, PEM, DER, JKS, and
PKCS\#8 --- because loading the wrong format is the quickest way to turn
a working certificate chain into an inscrutable exception stack trace.

\chapter{Key Material Formats}\label{ch05}

\section{PKCS\#12 / PFX: The Universal
Container}\label{_pkcs12_pfx_the_universal_container}

\textbf{PKCS\#12} is the format you will use most often for PDF signing.
It bundles a private key, the corresponding certificate, and (usually)
the full certificate chain into a single, password-protected file. The
standard file extensions are \texttt{.p12} and \texttt{.pfx}.

\subsection{History}\label{_history}

The lineage is tangled, as most things in cryptography are.

Microsoft created \textbf{PFX} (Personal Information Exchange) in the
mid-1990s as a container format for exporting keys and certificates from
Internet Explorer and IIS. RSA Laboratories, working on their PKCS
(Public-Key Cryptography Standards) series, developed \textbf{PKCS\#12}
as a platform-neutral alternative. The two formats converged over time:
PKCS\#12 v1.0 (1999) incorporated Microsoft's PFX design, and
\textbf{RFC 7292} (2014) formalized the standard.

Today, \texttt{.p12} and \texttt{.pfx} are effectively interchangeable
extensions for the same format. Some tools (notably Windows) prefer
\texttt{.pfx}; others (Java, OpenSSL) prefer \texttt{.p12}. The binary
content is identical.

You will occasionally encounter ancient \texttt{.pfx} files from the
late 1990s that use a pre-standard PFX format incompatible with
PKCS\#12. These are rare in 2026 but not extinct. OpenSSL will refuse to
parse them, producing an error about "unknown bag type." If you
encounter one, the only reliable conversion path is through a legacy
Windows system or Internet Explorer's certificate export.

\subsection{Internal Structure}\label{_internal_structure}

A PKCS\#12 file is a DER-encoded ASN.1 structure containing:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.7143}}@{}}
\caption{PKCS\#12 internal structure}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Component
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Component
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
PFX PDU & The outermost wrapper. Contains a version number (always 3),
the authenticated safe, and an optional MAC (Message Authentication
Code) for integrity verification. \\
AuthenticatedSafe & A SEQUENCE of ContentInfo values, each containing
one or more SafeBag objects. The ContentInfo can be unencrypted
(\texttt{data}), encrypted with a password-based cipher
(\texttt{encryptedData}), or encrypted with a public key
(\texttt{envelopedData} --- rarely used). \\
SafeBag & The actual content container. Types include \texttt{keyBag}
(unencrypted private key), \texttt{pkcs8ShroudedKeyBag} (encrypted
private key --- the most common), \texttt{certBag} (certificate),
\texttt{crlBag} (CRL), and \texttt{secretBag} (arbitrary secret). \\
MAC & An HMAC computed over the AuthenticatedSafe using a key derived
from the password. This verifies that the password is correct and that
the file has not been tampered with. The MAC uses PBKDF2 or a similar
key derivation function. \\
\end{longtable}

The typical PKCS\#12 file for PDF signing contains:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  One \texttt{pkcs8ShroudedKeyBag} --- the private key, encrypted with a
  password-derived key
\item
  One or more \texttt{certBag} entries --- the signer's certificate and
  the intermediate CA certificate(s)
\end{enumerate}

The private key encryption within the PKCS\#12 uses a password-based
encryption scheme. Historically, this was \textbf{PBE-SHA1-3DES} (also
known as \texttt{pbeWithSHAAnd3-KeyTripleDES-CBC}), which is the default
in many older tools and in Java's PKCS\#12 implementation prior to JDK
17.

PBE-SHA1-3DES is weak by modern standards. SHA-1 for key derivation and
Triple DES for encryption are both deprecated. OpenSSL 3.x defaults to
AES-256-CBC with PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 for new PKCS\#12 files, which is
much stronger. However, some tools --- including older versions of Adobe
Acrobat --- cannot read PKCS\#12 files encrypted with the newer
algorithms. If you generate a PKCS\#12 with OpenSSL 3.x and Acrobat
cannot open it, try re-exporting with the legacy flag:
\texttt{openssl\ pkcs12\ -export\ -legacy\ -in\ cert.pem\ -inkey\ key.pem\ -out\ keystore.pfx}.

\subsection{Loading PKCS\#12 in Java}\label{_loading_pkcs12_in_java}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{FileInputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{KeyStore}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PrivateKey}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Enumeration}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Load a PKCS}\CommentTok{\#12}\NormalTok{ keystore and extract the signing key and chain}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ Pkcs12LoadExample }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{loadAndInspect}\OperatorTok{(}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ p12Path}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ password}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

        \BuiltInTok{KeyStore}\NormalTok{ ks }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{KeyStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"PKCS12"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{FileInputStream}\NormalTok{ fis }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{FileInputStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{p12Path}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            ks}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{load}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{fis}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ password}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toCharArray}\OperatorTok{());}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \CommentTok{// Enumerate all entries}
        \BuiltInTok{Enumeration}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ aliases }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ks}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{aliases}\OperatorTok{();}
        \ControlFlowTok{while} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{aliases}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{hasMoreElements}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ alias }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ aliases}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{nextElement}\OperatorTok{();}

            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ks}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{isKeyEntry}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{alias}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \BuiltInTok{PrivateKey}\NormalTok{ key }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{PrivateKey}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ ks}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getKey}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                        alias}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ password}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toCharArray}\OperatorTok{());}    
                \BuiltInTok{Certificate}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ chain }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ks}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificateChain}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{alias}\OperatorTok{);}

                \CommentTok{// key.getAlgorithm() {-}\textgreater{} "RSA" or "EC"}
                \CommentTok{// key.getFormat() {-}\textgreater{} "PKCS\#8"}
                \CommentTok{// chain[0] is the end{-}entity certificate}
                \CommentTok{// chain[chain.length{-}1] is closest to the root}

                \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ signingCert }\OperatorTok{=}
                        \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ chain}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{];}
                \CommentTok{// signingCert.getSubjectX500Principal() {-}\textgreater{} signer DN}
                \CommentTok{// signingCert.getNotAfter() {-}\textgreater{} expiration date}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The password for the key entry is typically the same as the store
  password for PKCS\#12 files, but the API allows them to be different.
  Some tools (notably \texttt{keytool}) set the key password equal to
  the store password when creating PKCS\#12 files.
\end{itemize}

When a PKCS\#12 file fails to load with
\texttt{IOException:\ keystore\ password\ was\ incorrect}, the error is
misleading. It can mean (a) the password is actually wrong, (b) the file
is corrupted, (c) the file uses an encryption algorithm not supported by
your JDK, or (d) the file is not actually PKCS\#12 format at all
(perhaps it is PEM-encoded and was given a \texttt{.p12} extension by
mistake). Always check the file format first:
\texttt{openssl\ pkcs12\ -info\ -in\ file.p12\ -nokeys\ -passin\ pass:test\ 2\textgreater{}\&1\ \textbar{}\ head\ -5}
will quickly tell you whether the file is valid PKCS\#12 and whether the
password works.

\section{PEM: The Text Format}\label{_pem_the_text_format}

\textbf{PEM} (Privacy-Enhanced Mail) is the most human-friendly key and
certificate format --- and, paradoxically, has nothing to do with email
privacy anymore.

\subsection{History}\label{_history_2}

PEM was defined in RFCs 1421-1424 (1993) as a framework for email
encryption. The framework itself was complex, poorly adopted, and
quickly superseded by S/MIME and PGP. But the \textbf{encoding
format} --- Base64-encoded DER data bracketed by header and footer
lines --- became the de facto standard for exchanging cryptographic
objects in text form.

The format is simple:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}BEGIN CERTIFICATE{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{MIIDXTCCAkWgAwIBAgIJAJC1HiIAZAiUMA0GCSqGSIb3Qw0teleBAUAMEUxCzAJ}
\NormalTok{BgNVBAYTAkFVMRMwEQYDVQQIDApTb21lLVN0YXRlMSEwHwYDVQQKDBhJbnRlcm5l}
\NormalTok{... (Base64{-}encoded DER data, typically 64 or 76 characters per line) ...}
\NormalTok{dGhlIGNlcnRpZmljYXRl}
\NormalTok{{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}END CERTIFICATE{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The header line identifies the content type. Common headers include:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4286}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5714}}@{}}
\caption{PEM header types}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Header
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Content
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Header
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Content
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN\ CERTIFICATE-\/-\/-\/-\/-} & X.509
certificate (DER-encoded) \\
\texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN\ PRIVATE\ KEY-\/-\/-\/-\/-} & PKCS\#8
unencrypted private key \\
\texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN\ ENCRYPTED\ PRIVATE\ KEY-\/-\/-\/-\/-} &
PKCS\#8 encrypted private key \\
\texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN\ RSA\ PRIVATE\ KEY-\/-\/-\/-\/-} & PKCS\#1
RSA-specific private key (legacy OpenSSL format) \\
\texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN\ PUBLIC\ KEY-\/-\/-\/-\/-} &
SubjectPublicKeyInfo (X.509) public key \\
\texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN\ CERTIFICATE\ REQUEST-\/-\/-\/-\/-} &
PKCS\#10 certificate signing request (CSR) \\
\texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN\ X509\ CRL-\/-\/-\/-\/-} & Certificate
Revocation List \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{Multiple Objects in One
File}\label{_multiple_objects_in_one_file}

A PEM file can contain multiple objects --- this is one of its most
useful features. A common pattern for PDF signing is to concatenate the
certificate chain into a single file:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}BEGIN CERTIFICATE{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{(end{-}entity certificate)}
\NormalTok{{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}END CERTIFICATE{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}BEGIN CERTIFICATE{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{(intermediate CA certificate)}
\NormalTok{{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}END CERTIFICATE{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}BEGIN CERTIFICATE{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{(root CA certificate {-}{-} optional)}
\NormalTok{{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}END CERTIFICATE{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Java's \texttt{CertificateFactory.generateCertificates(InputStream)}
(note the plural) reads all certificates from such a file.
BouncyCastle's \texttt{PEMParser} reads any PEM objects sequentially.

\subsection{Loading PEM with
BouncyCastle}\label{_loading_pem_with_bouncycastle}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{FileReader}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{KeyPair}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PrivateKey}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaX509CertificateConverter}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{openssl}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PEMDecryptorProvider}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{openssl}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PEMEncryptedKeyPair}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{openssl}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PEMKeyPair}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{openssl}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PEMParser}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{openssl}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaPEMKeyConverter}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{openssl}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcePEMDecryptorProviderBuilder}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Load certificates and private keys from PEM files using BouncyCastle}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ PemLoadExample }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \CommentTok{/**}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Load a private key from a PEM file}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Handles both encrypted and unencrypted keys}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*/}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \BuiltInTok{PrivateKey} \FunctionTok{loadPrivateKey}\OperatorTok{(}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ pemPath}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ password}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PEMParser parser }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PEMParser}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{FileReader}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pemPath}\OperatorTok{)))} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \BuiltInTok{Object}\NormalTok{ obj }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ parser}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{readObject}\OperatorTok{();}

\NormalTok{            JcaPEMKeyConverter converter }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaPEMKeyConverter}\OperatorTok{()}
                    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{);}

            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{obj }\KeywordTok{instanceof}\NormalTok{ PEMEncryptedKeyPair}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{                PEMDecryptorProvider decryptor }\OperatorTok{=}
                        \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcePEMDecryptorProviderBuilder}\OperatorTok{()}
                                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{password}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toCharArray}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{                PEMKeyPair decrypted }\OperatorTok{=}
                        \OperatorTok{((}\NormalTok{PEMEncryptedKeyPair}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ obj}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{decryptKeyPair}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{decryptor}\OperatorTok{);}
                \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ converter}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getKeyPair}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{decrypted}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{getPrivate}\OperatorTok{();}
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{obj }\KeywordTok{instanceof}\NormalTok{ PEMKeyPair}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ converter}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getKeyPair}\OperatorTok{((}\NormalTok{PEMKeyPair}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ obj}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{getPrivate}\OperatorTok{();}
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{obj }\KeywordTok{instanceof}\NormalTok{ org}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{pkcs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{PrivateKeyInfo}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ converter}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPrivateKey}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{pkcs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{PrivateKeyInfo}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ obj}\OperatorTok{);}
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \ControlFlowTok{throw} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{IllegalArgumentException}\OperatorTok{(}
                    \StringTok{"Unexpected PEM object: "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ obj}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getClass}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getName}\OperatorTok{());}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{/**}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Load an X}\CommentTok{.509}\NormalTok{ certificate from a PEM file}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*/}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate} \FunctionTok{loadCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ pemPath}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PEMParser parser }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PEMParser}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{FileReader}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pemPath}\OperatorTok{)))} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \BuiltInTok{Object}\NormalTok{ obj }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ parser}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{readObject}\OperatorTok{();}

            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{obj }\KeywordTok{instanceof}\NormalTok{ X509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaX509CertificateConverter}\OperatorTok{()}
                        \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{)}
                        \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificate}\OperatorTok{((}\NormalTok{X509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ obj}\OperatorTok{);}
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \ControlFlowTok{throw} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{IllegalArgumentException}\OperatorTok{(}
                    \StringTok{"Not a certificate: "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ obj}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getClass}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getName}\OperatorTok{());}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The \texttt{PEMParser} class reads objects sequentially. If your PEM
file contains a private key followed by certificates, you must call
\texttt{parser.readObject()} once for the key and then again for each
certificate. A common mistake is to read only the first object and close
the parser, missing the certificates entirely.

\section{DER: The Binary Encoding}\label{_der_the_binary_encoding}

\textbf{DER} (Distinguished Encoding Rules) is the canonical binary
encoding for ASN.1 data structures. Every cryptographic object you
encounter --- certificates, private keys, CMS structures, OCSP
responses --- has a DER encoding. PEM is just Base64-encoded DER with
headers.

\subsection{Relationship to PEM}\label{_relationship_to_pem}

The conversion between DER and PEM is trivial:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{DER to PEM}: Base64-encode the DER bytes, split into
  64-character lines, wrap with the appropriate header/footer.
\item
  \textbf{PEM to DER}: Strip the header/footer lines, concatenate the
  remaining lines, Base64-decode.
\end{itemize}

DER files have no standard extension --- you will see \texttt{.der},
\texttt{.cer}, \texttt{.crt}, and even \texttt{.pem} used for
DER-encoded data (incorrectly, but it happens). The reliable way to
distinguish DER from PEM is to check whether the file starts with
\texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN}: if yes, PEM; if no, DER (or something else
entirely).

\subsection{When DER Matters}\label{_when_der_matters}

For PDF signatures, DER encoding is everywhere:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The CMS \texttt{SignedData} in the \texttt{/Contents} field is
  DER-encoded (then hex-encoded)
\item
  Each certificate in the CMS is DER-encoded
\item
  OCSP responses embedded in the DSS are DER-encoded
\item
  CRLs embedded in the DSS are DER-encoded
\end{itemize}

When you parse these structures with BouncyCastle, the library handles
the DER encoding/decoding transparently. You only deal with DER directly
when debugging at the byte level --- dumping the hex content of
\texttt{/Contents} and feeding it to \texttt{openssl\ asn1parse}, for
example.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Dump the ASN.1 structure of a DER{-}encoded CMS blob}
\ExtensionTok{openssl}\NormalTok{ asn1parse }\AttributeTok{{-}inform}\NormalTok{ DER }\AttributeTok{{-}in}\NormalTok{ signature.der}

\CommentTok{\# Convert DER certificate to PEM}
\ExtensionTok{openssl}\NormalTok{ x509 }\AttributeTok{{-}inform}\NormalTok{ DER }\AttributeTok{{-}in}\NormalTok{ cert.der }\AttributeTok{{-}outform}\NormalTok{ PEM }\AttributeTok{{-}out}\NormalTok{ cert.pem}

\CommentTok{\# Convert PEM certificate to DER}
\ExtensionTok{openssl}\NormalTok{ x509 }\AttributeTok{{-}inform}\NormalTok{ PEM }\AttributeTok{{-}in}\NormalTok{ cert.pem }\AttributeTok{{-}outform}\NormalTok{ DER }\AttributeTok{{-}out}\NormalTok{ cert.der}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{JKS: Java KeyStore (Legacy)}\label{_jks_java_keystore_legacy}

\textbf{JKS} (Java KeyStore) was Java's original proprietary keystore
format, introduced in JDK 1.2 (1998). It served the Java ecosystem for
nearly two decades and is still encountered in legacy enterprise
systems.

\subsection{Why JKS Is Being Replaced}\label{_why_jks_is_being_replaced}

JKS has several problems:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Proprietary format}: Only Java tools can read it. OpenSSL
  cannot parse JKS. Non-Java applications cannot use JKS keystores.
\item
  \textbf{Weak encryption}: The private key protection in JKS uses a
  custom, non-standard algorithm based on password-derived key material
  and a proprietary obfuscation scheme. It is not PBE by any recognized
  standard.
\item
  \textbf{Case-insensitive aliases}: JKS converts all aliases to
  lowercase, which can cause subtle bugs when migrating to PKCS\#12
  (which is case-sensitive).
\item
  \textbf{No standard for interoperability}: Migrating keys between JKS
  and non-Java systems always requires format conversion.
\end{enumerate}

Since JDK 9, the default keystore type in Java is \textbf{PKCS12}, not
JKS. The \texttt{keytool} utility creates PKCS\#12 keystores by default.
If you are starting a new project, always use PKCS\#12.

To check which keystore type you are dealing with:
\texttt{keytool\ -list\ -keystore\ mystore.jks} will prompt for a
password and display entries. If you see "Keystore type: jks" in the
output, it is a JKS file. If you see "Keystore type: PKCS12", it is
PKCS\#12. To convert JKS to PKCS\#12:
\texttt{keytool\ -importkeystore\ -srckeystore\ old.jks\ -destkeystore\ new.p12\ -deststoretype\ PKCS12}.

\section{PKCS\#8: Private Key Format}\label{_pkcs8_private_key_format}

\textbf{PKCS\#8} (RFC 5958, originally RSA PKCS\#8) is the standard
format for private keys, independent of the key algorithm. When you
extract a private key from a PKCS\#12 file in Java
(\texttt{keyStore.getKey()}), the key's \texttt{getFormat()} method
returns \texttt{"PKCS\#8"} --- that is the encoding format of the key
bytes.

\subsection{Unencrypted vs Encrypted}\label{_unencrypted_vs_encrypted}

PKCS\#8 defines two structures:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{PrivateKeyInfo} (unencrypted): Contains the algorithm
  identifier and the raw private key data. Stored in PEM files with the
  header \texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN\ PRIVATE\ KEY-\/-\/-\/-\/-}.
\item
  \textbf{EncryptedPrivateKeyInfo}: The PrivateKeyInfo wrapped in a
  password-based encryption layer. Stored in PEM files with the header
  \texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN\ ENCRYPTED\ PRIVATE\ KEY-\/-\/-\/-\/-}.
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{PrivateKeyInfo ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    version                Version,}
\NormalTok{    privateKeyAlgorithm    AlgorithmIdentifier,}
\NormalTok{    privateKey             }\DataTypeTok{OCTET} \DataTypeTok{STRING}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{EncryptedPrivateKeyInfo ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    encryptionAlgorithm    AlgorithmIdentifier,}
\NormalTok{    encryptedData          }\DataTypeTok{OCTET} \DataTypeTok{STRING}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The \texttt{privateKey} octet string contains the algorithm-specific key
data --- an RSA private key (PKCS\#1 structure) for RSA, or an EC
private key structure for ECDSA. PKCS\#8 wraps these algorithm-specific
formats in a uniform container.

PKCS\#8 is the format that the JCA uses internally. When you call
\texttt{privateKey.getEncoded()}, the returned bytes are PKCS\#8
\texttt{PrivateKeyInfo}. When you call \texttt{publicKey.getEncoded()},
the returned bytes are \texttt{SubjectPublicKeyInfo} (the X.509 public
key format, defined in RFC 5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and
CRL Profile). These format names --- PKCS\#8 for private keys, X.509 for
public keys --- appear in Java's \texttt{Key.getFormat()} and in PEM
headers.

\section{PKCS\#1: RSA-Specific Key
Format}\label{_pkcs1_rsa_specific_key_format}

\textbf{PKCS\#1} (RFC 8017) defines the RSA-specific private key
structure:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{RSAPrivateKey ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    version           Version,}
