\NormalTok{    modulus           }\DataTypeTok{INTEGER}\NormalTok{,  }\CommentTok{{-}{-} n}
\NormalTok{    publicExponent    }\DataTypeTok{INTEGER}\NormalTok{,  }\CommentTok{{-}{-} e}
\NormalTok{    privateExponent   }\DataTypeTok{INTEGER}\NormalTok{,  }\CommentTok{{-}{-} d}
\NormalTok{    prime1            }\DataTypeTok{INTEGER}\NormalTok{,  }\CommentTok{{-}{-} p}
\NormalTok{    prime2            }\DataTypeTok{INTEGER}\NormalTok{,  }\CommentTok{{-}{-} q}
\NormalTok{    exponent1         }\DataTypeTok{INTEGER}\NormalTok{,  }\CommentTok{{-}{-} d mod (p{-}1)}
\NormalTok{    exponent2         }\DataTypeTok{INTEGER}\NormalTok{,  }\CommentTok{{-}{-} d mod (q{-}1)}
\NormalTok{    coefficient       }\DataTypeTok{INTEGER}   \CommentTok{{-}{-} (inverse of q) mod p}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This is the format stored inside a PKCS\#8 \texttt{PrivateKeyInfo} when
the algorithm is RSA. It is also the format used in PEM files with the
header
\texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN\ RSA\ PRIVATE\ KEY-\/-\/-\/-\/-} --- OpenSSL's
"traditional" format.

The distinction between PKCS\#1 and PKCS\#8 confuses many developers:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN\ RSA\ PRIVATE\ KEY-\/-\/-\/-\/-} is PKCS\#1
  (RSA-specific, no algorithm identifier wrapper)
\item
  \texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN\ PRIVATE\ KEY-\/-\/-\/-\/-} is PKCS\#8
  (algorithm-agnostic, contains an algorithm identifier)
\end{itemize}

Both contain the same RSA key data. PKCS\#8 simply wraps the PKCS\#1
structure with an additional layer that identifies the algorithm. If you
try to load a PKCS\#1 PEM file using code that expects PKCS\#8, you will
get a parsing error --- and vice versa.

OpenSSL's default output format changed between versions. OpenSSL 1.x
used PKCS\#1
(\texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN\ RSA\ PRIVATE\ KEY-\/-\/-\/-\/-}) by default
for RSA keys. OpenSSL 3.x uses PKCS\#8
(\texttt{-\/-\/-\/-\/-BEGIN\ PRIVATE\ KEY-\/-\/-\/-\/-}) by default. If
you are following a tutorial written for an older OpenSSL version, the
key files it produces may be in a different format than your current
OpenSSL generates. Always check the PEM header.

\section{Format Conversion
Reference}\label{_format_conversion_reference}

This matrix shows how to convert between formats using \texttt{openssl}
and \texttt{keytool}. Every PDF signing project eventually needs at
least one of these conversions.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5556}}@{}}
\caption{Key and certificate format conversion commands}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
From
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
To
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Command
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
From
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
To
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Command
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
PKCS\#12 & PEM (key + cert) &
\texttt{openssl\ pkcs12\ -in\ store.p12\ -out\ bundle.pem\ -nodes} \\
PKCS\#12 & Separate PEM files &
\texttt{openssl\ pkcs12\ -in\ store.p12\ -nocerts\ -out\ key.pem} (key)
then \texttt{openssl\ pkcs12\ -in\ store.p12\ -nokeys\ -out\ cert.pem}
(cert) \\
PEM (key + cert) & PKCS\#12 &
\texttt{openssl\ pkcs12\ -export\ -in\ cert.pem\ -inkey\ key.pem\ -out\ store.p12\ -name\ "alias"} \\
PEM certificate & DER certificate &
\texttt{openssl\ x509\ -in\ cert.pem\ -outform\ DER\ -out\ cert.der} \\
DER certificate & PEM certificate &
\texttt{openssl\ x509\ -inform\ DER\ -in\ cert.der\ -outform\ PEM\ -out\ cert.pem} \\
PKCS\#1 key & PKCS\#8 key &
\texttt{openssl\ pkcs8\ -topk8\ -in\ rsa.pem\ -out\ pkcs8.pem\ -nocrypt} \\
PKCS\#8 key & PKCS\#1 key &
\texttt{openssl\ rsa\ -in\ pkcs8.pem\ -out\ rsa.pem\ -traditional} \\
JKS & PKCS\#12 &
\texttt{keytool\ -importkeystore\ -srckeystore\ old.jks\ -destkeystore\ new.p12\ -deststoretype\ PKCS12} \\
PKCS\#12 & JKS &
\texttt{keytool\ -importkeystore\ -srckeystore\ store.p12\ -srcstoretype\ PKCS12\ -destkeystore\ new.jks\ -deststoretype\ JKS} \\
PEM certificate & JKS (trust entry) &
\texttt{keytool\ -importcert\ -file\ cert.pem\ -keystore\ trust.jks\ -alias\ "mycert"} \\
\end{longtable}

The \texttt{-nodes} flag in the OpenSSL PKCS\#12 export command means
"no DES" --- the private key will not be encrypted in the output PEM
file. This is convenient for testing but dangerous in production. Never
leave unencrypted private keys on disk in production environments. Use
\texttt{-aes256} instead of \texttt{-nodes} to encrypt the output
private key with AES-256.

\section{HSM Integration: When Keys Cannot
Leave}\label{_hsm_integration_when_keys_cannot_leave}

A \textbf{Hardware Security Module (HSM)} is a dedicated hardware device
that generates, stores, and uses cryptographic keys internally. The
private key \textbf{never leaves the HSM} --- all signing operations
happen inside the device. This provides the strongest possible key
protection: even if the server is completely compromised, the attacker
cannot extract the private key.

\subsection{PKCS\#11}\label{_pkcs11}

The standard interface for HSM access is \textbf{PKCS\#11} (also called
Cryptoki), defined by OASIS (originally by RSA Laboratories). PKCS\#11
defines a C API for cryptographic operations: key generation, signing,
encryption, etc. The HSM vendor provides a shared library (\texttt{.so}
on Linux, \texttt{.dll} on Windows) that implements the PKCS\#11
interface.

Java accesses PKCS\#11 via the \textbf{SunPKCS11 provider} --- a JCA
provider that bridges the Java cryptographic API to a PKCS\#11 library.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{KeyStore}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Provider}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Security}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Load keys from an HSM via PKCS}\CommentTok{\#11.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ The private key never leaves the HSM }\CommentTok{{-}{-}}\NormalTok{ signing happens on}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{device}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ Pkcs11Example }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \BuiltInTok{KeyStore} \FunctionTok{loadFromHsm}\OperatorTok{(}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ pkcs11ConfigPath}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ pin}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

        \CommentTok{// Configure the SunPKCS11 provider}
        \BuiltInTok{Provider}\NormalTok{ p }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{Security}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"SunPKCS11"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        p }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ p}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{configure}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pkcs11ConfigPath}\OperatorTok{);}      
        \BuiltInTok{Security}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{p}\OperatorTok{);}

        \CommentTok{// Load the "keystore" {-}{-} this actually enumerates}
        \CommentTok{// objects on the HSM token}
        \BuiltInTok{KeyStore}\NormalTok{ ks }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{KeyStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"PKCS11"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ p}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        ks}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{load}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ pin}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toCharArray}\OperatorTok{());}        

        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ ks}\OperatorTok{;}
        \CommentTok{// ks.getKey() returns a key handle, not the actual key bytes}
        \CommentTok{// Signing with this key executes on the HSM}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The config file specifies the PKCS\#11 library path, slot number, and
  other HSM-specific parameters.
\item
  The PIN authenticates to the HSM token. Unlike PKCS\#12 passwords, HSM
  PINs are typically short numeric codes enforced by the hardware.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Why This Matters for PDF
Signing}\label{_why_this_matters_for_pdf_signing}

When you use PDFBox's \texttt{SignatureInterface.sign()} with an
HSM-backed key, the signing operation is transparently delegated to the
HSM. The \texttt{PrivateKey} object returned by \texttt{ks.getKey()} is
a \textbf{handle}, not the actual key material. The JCA provider
intercepts the signing call, forwards it to the HSM via PKCS\#11, and
returns the result. Your signing code does not change --- the same
\texttt{CMSSignedDataGenerator} code from Chapter 2 works with HSM keys
and software keys identically.

This is the elegance of the JCA provider model: the signing code is
agnostic to key storage. Whether the key lives in a PKCS\#12 file, a JKS
keystore, or an HSM, the API is the same.

For eIDAS qualified electronic signatures, the private key \emph{must}
be stored in a \textbf{Qualified Signature Creation Device
(QSCD)} --- an HSM or smart card that has been certified to Common
Criteria EAL 4+ or equivalent. Software keystores (PKCS\#12, JKS) are
not QSCDs. If you are building a signing service that needs to produce
qualified electronic signatures, HSM integration is not optional --- it
is a legal requirement under Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) Article
29.

\section{Why Format Conversion Goes
Wrong}\label{_why_format_conversion_goes_wrong}

Format conversions are a perennial source of frustration. Here are the
most common failure modes:

\subsection{Password Encoding
Mismatch}\label{_password_encoding_mismatch}

PKCS\#12 passwords can be encoded as either \textbf{UTF-8} or
\textbf{UTF-16BE} (BMPString in ASN.1 terminology) for the key
derivation function. The PKCS\#12 standard specifies BMPString, but some
implementations use UTF-8.

If you create a PKCS\#12 with a password containing non-ASCII characters
(umlauts, accented characters, CJK characters) using OpenSSL, and then
try to open it in Java, the key derivation may produce a different key
because the two implementations encode the password differently.

The fix: use only ASCII characters in PKCS\#12 passwords. This is not a
theoretical recommendation --- it is a practical necessity in
cross-platform environments.

\subsection{OpenSSL Legacy Format vs
PKCS\#8}\label{_openssl_legacy_format_vs_pkcs8}

As mentioned above, OpenSSL's default private key format changed between
major versions. A script that worked with OpenSSL 1.1.1 may produce keys
that your PKCS\#8-expecting Java code cannot parse when run with OpenSSL
3.x, or vice versa.

Always check the PEM header and convert as needed.

\subsection{Certificate Chain Order in
PKCS\#12}\label{_certificate_chain_order_in_pkcs12}

The PKCS\#12 specification does not mandate a specific order for
certificates in the file. Some tools store the end-entity certificate
first, followed by intermediates. Others store them in reverse order.
Java's \texttt{KeyStore.getCertificateChain()} returns them in order
(end-entity first), regardless of how they are stored in the
file --- but only if the certificates are associated with the correct
key alias.

If the chain is missing intermediates (because they were not included
when the PKCS\#12 was created), \texttt{getCertificateChain()} returns
only the end-entity certificate. This produces the classic "chain not
trusted" error at signing or validation time.

When creating a PKCS\#12 for PDF signing, always include the full chain
(minus the root). With OpenSSL:
\texttt{openssl\ pkcs12\ -export\ -in\ signer.pem\ -inkey\ signer-key.pem\ -certfile\ intermediate-chain.pem\ -out\ signing.p12}.
The \texttt{-certfile} flag adds the intermediate certificates. Without
it, only the end-entity certificate is included.

\subsection{DER vs PEM Confusion}\label{_der_vs_pem_confusion}

Renaming a file's extension does not change its format. A file named
\texttt{cert.pem} that is actually DER-encoded will cause a Base64
decode error. A file named \texttt{cert.der} that is actually
PEM-encoded will cause an ASN.1 parse error.

To reliably detect the format:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# If the file starts with "{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}\RegionMarkerTok{BEGIN}\CommentTok{", it is PEM}
\FunctionTok{head} \AttributeTok{{-}1}\NormalTok{ cert.pem}

\CommentTok{\# Try parsing as DER; if it fails, it is probably PEM (or corrupted)}
\ExtensionTok{openssl}\NormalTok{ x509 }\AttributeTok{{-}inform}\NormalTok{ DER }\AttributeTok{{-}in}\NormalTok{ cert.der }\AttributeTok{{-}noout} \DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}\&}\DecValTok{1}

\CommentTok{\# The file command on Linux/macOS can often identify the format}
\FunctionTok{file}\NormalTok{ cert.pem}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Summary}\label{_summary_5}

Key material comes in several formats, each with its own history,
strengths, and failure modes:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{PKCS\#12} is the universal container for private keys and
  certificate chains. Use it for PDF signing keystores.
\item
  \textbf{PEM} is the human-readable, Base64-encoded format for
  individual certificates, keys, and other cryptographic objects. Use it
  for interchange with OpenSSL and when you need to inspect contents
  visually.
\item
  \textbf{DER} is the binary ASN.1 encoding underlying everything. You
  encounter it inside PDF signatures (the CMS blob in
  \texttt{/Contents}), in OCSP responses, and in CRLs.
\item
  \textbf{JKS} is Java's legacy keystore format. Migrate to PKCS\#12.
\item
  \textbf{PKCS\#8} is the standard private key format
  (algorithm-agnostic). Java uses it internally.
\item
  \textbf{PKCS\#1} is the RSA-specific key format. Watch for the PEM
  header to distinguish it from PKCS\#8.
\end{itemize}

For HSM-backed signing (mandatory for eIDAS qualified signatures), keys
are accessed via PKCS\#11 and never leave the hardware. The JCA provider
model makes this transparent to your signing code.

Format conversion problems --- password encoding, legacy vs modern
defaults, missing chain certificates, DER/PEM confusion --- are among
the most common issues in PDF signing deployments. The conversion
reference table in this chapter and a systematic approach to checking
file formats will save you hours of debugging.

In the next chapter, we examine the CMS SignedData structure --- the
envelope that carries the cryptographic signature, the certificate
chain, and the signed attributes from the signer to the validator, all
encoded inside the PDF's \texttt{/Contents} hex string.

\chapter{CMS/PKCS\#7 Signed Data}\label{ch06}

Every PDF digital signature carries its cryptographic payload inside a
CMS \texttt{SignedData} structure --- a binary envelope defined by RFC
5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) that bundles the signature
value, the signer's certificate chain, the digest algorithm, and a set
of signed metadata attributes into a single DER-encoded blob. This
chapter dissects that envelope layer by layer, from the outermost
\texttt{ContentInfo} wrapper down to the individual signed attributes,
with annotated ASN.1 dumps and BouncyCastle 1.80 code on Java 21. If you
understand CMS \texttt{SignedData}, you understand the heart of what
lives inside the \texttt{/Contents} hex string of every signed PDF.

If you have parsed CMS structures before, skip to
\hyperref[ch06-signerinfo-deep-dive]{SignerInfo: The Core of the
Signature} for the signed attributes encoding trap that has caused more
validation failures than any other single issue. If you want code, jump
to \hyperref[ch06-java-parsing]{Java: Parsing CMS SignedData}.

\section{From PKCS\#7 to CMS: A
History}\label{_from_pkcs7_to_cms_a_history}

The Cryptographic Message Syntax did not spring into existence fully
formed. It evolved over sixteen years through three distinct phases,
each driven by different organizations and different needs.
Understanding this lineage matters because the older formats are still
embedded in millions of signed PDFs, and the naming confusion between
"PKCS\#7" and "CMS" persists in specifications, code comments, and error
messages to this day.

\subsection{PKCS\#7: RSA Laboratories,
1993}\label{_pkcs7_rsa_laboratories_1993}

RSA Laboratories published \textbf{PKCS\#7} (Public-Key Cryptography
Standard \#7) in November 1993 as a general-purpose format for signed
and encrypted data. The full title was "Cryptographic Message Syntax
Standard," which is ironic because it was not yet the CMS we know
today --- it was a proprietary RSA specification, not an IETF standard.

PKCS\#7 version 1.5 defined the core structure that survives almost
unchanged:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  A \texttt{ContentInfo} wrapper with an OID identifying the content
  type
\item
  A \texttt{SignedData} type containing digest algorithms, signer
  information, certificates, and CRLs
\item
  A \texttt{SignerInfo} type containing the actual signature and
  per-signer metadata
\item
  Support for both \emph{attached} (encapsulated) and \emph{detached}
  signatures
\end{itemize}

The design was influenced by the PEM (Privacy-Enhanced Mail) framework
from RFCs 1421-1424 and by the emerging S/MIME work at RSA. The goal was
a format that could carry signed data for email, file signing, code
signing, and any other context where a digital signature needed to
travel alongside or separately from its content.

The "PKCS\#7" name persists everywhere. The PDF specification uses
\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} and \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.sha1} as SubFilter
values. Java's \texttt{KeyStore.getInstance("PKCS7")} exists. OpenSSL
has \texttt{pkcs7} commands. In all of these contexts, the actual format
in use is CMS as defined by RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax
(CMS), not the original 1993 PKCS\#7. The names are legacy; the
structures have evolved.

\subsection{S/MIME and RFC 2315, 1998}\label{_smime_and_rfc_2315_1998}

The S/MIME (Secure/Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions) working group
at the IETF took PKCS\#7 and brought it into the standards process.
\textbf{RFC 2315} (March 1998) republished PKCS\#7 v1.5 essentially
verbatim as an informational RFC, giving it a stable, citable reference
in the IETF document series.

But the S/MIME group needed enhancements that RSA's original design did
not provide: better algorithm agility, support for attribute
certificates, and a cleaner separation between the content being signed
and the signature metadata. This work produced the first true CMS
specification.

\subsection{CMS: RFC 2630 (1999) and RFC 3852
(2004)}\label{_cms_rfc_2630_1999_and_rfc_3852_2004}

\textbf{RFC 2630} (June 1999) was the first IETF-standards-track CMS
specification. It renamed the format from "PKCS\#7" to "Cryptographic
Message Syntax" and introduced several changes:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The \texttt{SignedData} version number advanced from 1 to 3 (with
  version selection rules based on which optional fields are present)
\item
  \texttt{EncapsulatedContentInfo} replaced the old \texttt{ContentInfo}
  for the inner content, allowing non-data content types
\item
  The \texttt{SignerIdentifier} was formalized with two choices:
  \texttt{issuerAndSerialNumber} (the classic) and
  \texttt{subjectKeyIdentifier} (new)
\item
  Attribute certificates were added to the \texttt{certificates} field
  as an option alongside X.509 certificates
\item
  The \texttt{digestAlgorithms} SET was required to be consistent with
  the algorithms actually used in \texttt{SignerInfo} entries
\end{itemize}

\textbf{RFC 3852} (July 2004) was a revision that clarified ambiguities
and tightened requirements, particularly around the encoding of signed
attributes.

\subsection{RFC 5652: The Current Standard
(2009)}\label{_rfc_5652_the_current_standard_2009}

\textbf{RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS)} (September
2009) is the current and authoritative CMS specification. It obsoletes
RFC 3852 and is the normative reference used by ISO 32000-2:2020, the
PAdES standards (ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04)), and BouncyCastle.

The changes from RFC 3852 were modest --- mostly editorial
clarifications and alignment with updated ASN.1 modules. The core
structures are stable and have not changed since 1999. When we refer to
"CMS" in this book, we mean RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax
(CMS) unless otherwise noted.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5714}}@{}}
\caption{CMS evolution timeline}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Year
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Document
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Significance
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Year
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Document
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Significance
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
1993 & PKCS\#7 v1.5 & RSA Laboratories\textquotesingle{} original
specification. Defined SignedData, EnvelopedData, and the ContentInfo
wrapper. Still referenced by name in PDF SubFilter values. \\
1998 & RFC 2315 & PKCS\#7 republished as an informational RFC. No
technical changes, but gave the format an IETF-citable reference. \\
1999 & RFC 2630 & First standards-track CMS. Introduced version 3
SignedData, SubjectKeyIdentifier as an alternative SignerIdentifier, and
formalized attribute handling. \\
2004 & RFC 3852 & Clarified signed attributes encoding requirements.
Tightened consistency rules for digestAlgorithms SET. \\
2009 & RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) & Current
standard. Obsoletes RFC 3852. The normative reference for all modern PDF
signature work. \\
\end{longtable}

\section{Why PDF Uses CMS}\label{_why_pdf_uses_cms}

When Adobe added digital signatures to PDF 1.3 in 1999, they needed a
container format for the cryptographic signature. They chose PKCS\#7
(which was becoming CMS at exactly the same time) for pragmatic reasons:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{It was the only real option.} In 1999, the choices for
  cryptographic message formats were PKCS\#7/CMS and PGP. PKCS\#7/CMS
  was the enterprise and government standard; PGP was associated with
  personal email encryption. For a document format targeting business
  use, PKCS\#7/CMS was the obvious choice.
\item
  \textbf{It bundles everything needed for validation.} A CMS
  \texttt{SignedData} structure can carry the signature value, the
  signer's certificate, intermediate CA certificates, CRLs, and
  timestamp tokens --- everything a validator needs without making
  network requests. This self-contained property is critical for PDF,
  where a signed document might be validated years after signing, on an
  air-gapped system with no network access.
\item
  \textbf{It supports detached signatures natively.} PDF needs the
  signed content (the byte range) to live in the PDF file, not inside
  the CMS structure. CMS's detached signature mode was designed for
  exactly this use case.
\end{enumerate}

The practical consequence is that the \texttt{/Contents} value in every
PDF signature dictionary is a hex-encoded, DER-encoded CMS
\texttt{SignedData} structure. When ISO 32000-2:2020 says the
\texttt{/Contents} field contains "the DER-encoded PKCS\#7 binary data
object," it means a CMS \texttt{SignedData} as defined by RFC 5652 ---
Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS). The terms are used interchangeably
in the spec, which is historically understandable but terminologically
sloppy.

\section{The ContentInfo Wrapper}\label{_the_contentinfo_wrapper}

Every CMS structure is wrapped in a \texttt{ContentInfo} --- the
outermost ASN.1 envelope that identifies what type of CMS object is
inside.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{ContentInfo ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    contentType     ContentType,     }\CommentTok{{-}{-} OID identifying the CMS type}
\NormalTok{    content    [}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{]  EXPLICIT ANY DEFINED BY contentType}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{ContentType ::= OBJECT IDENTIFIER}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

For a PDF signature, the \texttt{contentType} is always:

\begin{verbatim}
1.2.840.113549.1.7.2   -- id-signedData
\end{verbatim}

This OID lives under the RSA Security arc (\texttt{1.2.840.113549}) in
the PKCS\#7 namespace (\texttt{.1.7}), with \texttt{.2} identifying
\texttt{SignedData} specifically. The \texttt{content} field contains
the \texttt{SignedData} structure itself, tagged with EXPLICIT
\texttt{{[}0{]}}.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\caption{CMS ContentType OIDs}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Usage in PDF
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Usage in PDF
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.7.1} & \texttt{id-data} & Used as the
\texttt{eContentType} inside SignedData to indicate that the signed
content is arbitrary data (the PDF byte range) \\
\texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.7.2} & \texttt{id-signedData} & The outermost
ContentInfo type for all PDF signatures \\
\texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.7.3} & \texttt{id-envelopedData} & Not used in
PDF signatures (used for encryption) \\
\texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.1.4} & \texttt{id-ct-TSTInfo} & Used as
the \texttt{eContentType} for RFC 3161 timestamp tokens embedded in PDF
signatures \\
\end{longtable}

The OID \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.7.2} is the first thing a CMS parser
encounters when reading a PDF signature. If this OID is missing or
wrong, the entire structure is unparsable. When you are debugging a
broken signature by examining raw hex, look for the DER encoding of this
OID near the beginning of the \texttt{/Contents} value:
\texttt{06\ 09\ 2A\ 86\ 48\ 86\ F7\ 0D\ 01\ 07\ 02}. Those eleven bytes
(tag \texttt{06}, length \texttt{09}, then the nine OID bytes) are the
signature of a CMS \texttt{SignedData} structure.

\section{The SignedData Structure}\label{_the_signeddata_structure}

Inside the \texttt{ContentInfo} wrapper, the \texttt{SignedData}
structure contains everything related to the signature:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{SignedData ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    version            CMSVersion,                          }
\NormalTok{    digestAlgorithms   DigestAlgorithmIdentifiers,          }
\NormalTok{    encapContentInfo   EncapsulatedContentInfo,             }
\NormalTok{    certificates  [}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{]  IMPLICIT CertificateSet }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}\NormalTok{,    }
\NormalTok{    crls          [}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{]  IMPLICIT RevocationInfoChoices }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}\NormalTok{, }
\NormalTok{    signerInfos        SignerInfos                          }
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Version number: 1, 3, 4, or 5, depending on which optional fields are
  present. For PDF signatures with X.509 certificates and
  \texttt{issuerAndSerialNumber} in SignerInfo, this is typically
  \textbf{1}.
\item
  SET OF digest algorithm identifiers. Must include every algorithm used
  by any SignerInfo in this structure. For a single-signer PDF, this is
  a set with one element (e.g., SHA-256).
\item
  The content that was signed. For PDF detached signatures, the
  \texttt{eContent} field is \textbf{absent} --- the content lives in
  the PDF file itself.
\item
  The certificate collection. Typically contains the signer's end-entity
  certificate plus all intermediate CA certificates needed to build the
  chain to a trust anchor.
\item
  CRLs or OCSP responses for revocation checking. Rarely populated in
  basic PDF signatures; more common in PAdES-B-LT and PAdES-B-LTA
  profiles where revocation data is embedded for long-term validation.
\item
  SET OF SignerInfo structures --- one per signer. PDF signatures almost
  always have exactly one SignerInfo.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Version Number Rules}\label{_version_number_rules}

The version field is not arbitrary --- RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic
Message Syntax (CMS) Section 5.1 specifies strict rules:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.8000}}@{}}
\caption{SignedData version selection (RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic
Message Syntax (CMS) Section 5.1)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Version
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Condition
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Version
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Condition
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
1 & No attribute certificates in \texttt{certificates}, no
\texttt{OtherRevocationInfoFormat} in \texttt{crls}, no version-3
\texttt{SignerInfo}, and the \texttt{encapContentInfo.eContentType} is
\texttt{id-data} \\
3 & The \texttt{encapContentInfo.eContentType} is other than
\texttt{id-data} (e.g., \texttt{id-ct-TSTInfo} for a timestamp token),
OR the structure contains \texttt{SignerInfo} version 3 \\
4 & Attribute certificates with version 2 are present in
\texttt{certificates} \\
5 & \texttt{OtherRevocationInfoFormat} is present in \texttt{crls} \\
\end{longtable}

For the vast majority of PDF signatures, the version is \textbf{1}.
Timestamp tokens embedded as unsigned attributes use version \textbf{3}
because their inner \texttt{eContentType} is \texttt{id-ct-TSTInfo}
rather than \texttt{id-data}.

Some older signing libraries (notably early versions of iText before
version 5.3) hardcoded the version to 1 regardless of the content type.
This produced technically non-compliant CMS structures that happened to
validate in most tools but failed strict validators like the EU's DSS
library. If you encounter a CMS blob with version 1 but a
non-\texttt{id-data} content type, that is a bug in the signing library,
not a feature.

\subsection{The digestAlgorithms SET}\label{_the_digestalgorithms_set}

This is a SET OF \texttt{AlgorithmIdentifier}, declared at the top level
of \texttt{SignedData} to allow a parser to identify all digest
algorithms in use before reading any \texttt{SignerInfo} entries. The
intent is optimization: a validator reading a large detached signature
can start computing digests in parallel before it reaches the
\texttt{SignerInfo} structures.

For PDF signatures with a single signer using SHA-256:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{digestAlgorithms:}
  \DataTypeTok{SET}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{ element)}
    \DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}
\NormalTok{      OID: }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{.}\DecValTok{16}\NormalTok{.}\DecValTok{840}\NormalTok{.}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{.}\DecValTok{101}\NormalTok{.}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{.}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{.}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{.}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{  (SHA}\DecValTok{{-}256}\NormalTok{)}
      \DataTypeTok{NULL}\NormalTok{                           (parameters, absent for SHA}\DecValTok{{-}2}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The \texttt{NULL} parameters field for SHA-2 algorithms is a persistent
source of interoperability issues. RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message
Syntax (CMS) says the parameters "SHOULD be omitted" for SHA-256 and
related algorithms, but some older implementations include an explicit
\texttt{NULL} (tag \texttt{05\ 00}), and some validators reject
structures where it is absent. BouncyCastle 1.80 includes the explicit
\texttt{NULL} by default, which is the safe choice for maximum
compatibility.

\subsection{EncapsulatedContentInfo: Detached vs
Encapsulated}\label{_encapsulatedcontentinfo_detached_vs_encapsulated}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{EncapsulatedContentInfo ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    eContentType    ContentType,}
\NormalTok{    eContent   [}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{]  EXPLICIT }\DataTypeTok{OCTET} \DataTypeTok{STRING} \KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This is where detached and encapsulated signatures diverge:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Detached} (PDF standard): \texttt{eContent} is
  \textbf{absent}. The signed content lives outside the CMS
  structure --- in the PDF file, specified by the \texttt{/ByteRange}
  array. The \texttt{eContentType} is \texttt{id-data}
  (\texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.7.1}).
\item
  \textbf{Encapsulated}: \texttt{eContent} is \textbf{present} and
  contains the signed data. This mode is used in S/MIME email signatures
  where the message body is embedded in the CMS structure.
\end{itemize}

For the SubFilter \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.sha1} (deprecated), the CMS uses a
hybrid approach: the \texttt{eContent} field contains the SHA-1 hash of
the byte range (not the byte range itself), making it technically an
encapsulated signature over a hash rather than a detached signature over
the content. This design was misguided and is one of the reasons
\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.sha1} was deprecated.

If you are building a PDF signing implementation and you accidentally
pass \texttt{true} for the encapsulate parameter in
\texttt{CMSSignedDataGenerator.generate()}, the entire byte range
content will be embedded inside the CMS structure, inside the
\texttt{/Contents} hex string, inside the PDF. The result is a signature
that is dramatically larger than necessary (potentially megabytes),
wastes space, and will be rejected by validators that expect a detached
signature when the SubFilter is \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} or
\texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached}.

\subsection{The certificates Field}\label{_the_certificates_field}

The \texttt{certificates} field is an IMPLICIT SET that can contain:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  X.509 certificates (\texttt{Certificate} type from RFC 5280 ---
  Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile)
\item
  Attribute certificates (v1 or v2) --- rarely used in PDF signatures
\item
  Other certificate formats --- essentially never used
\end{itemize}

For PDF signatures, this field should contain at a minimum the signer's
end-entity certificate and all intermediate CA certificates needed to
chain to a trust anchor. The root CA certificate is traditionally
\emph{not} included because the validator must already trust
it --- including it adds bytes without adding trust.

The ordering of certificates in this SET is \textbf{undefined} by RFC
5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS). A validator must be
prepared to receive certificates in any order and build the chain by
matching issuer/subject Distinguished Names and Authority/Subject Key
Identifiers.

\section{SignerInfo: The Core of the
Signature}\label{ch06-signerinfo-deep-dive}

Each \texttt{SignerInfo} structure represents one signer's contribution
to the \texttt{SignedData}:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{SignerInfo ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    version               CMSVersion,                    }
\NormalTok{    sid                   SignerIdentifier,               }
\NormalTok{    digestAlgorithm       DigestAlgorithmIdentifier,      }
\NormalTok{    signedAttrs      [}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{]  IMPLICIT SignedAttributes }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}\NormalTok{, }
\NormalTok{    signatureAlgorithm    SignatureAlgorithmIdentifier,   }
\NormalTok{    signature             SignatureValue,                 }
\NormalTok{    unsignedAttrs    [}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{]  IMPLICIT UnsignedAttributes }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL} 
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Version 1 if \texttt{sid} is \texttt{issuerAndSerialNumber}, version 3
  if \texttt{sid} is \texttt{subjectKeyIdentifier}.
\item
  Identifies which certificate belongs to this signer.
\item
  The hash algorithm used to compute the message digest (e.g., SHA-256).
\item
  Attributes that are included in the signature computation --- critical
  for PDF signatures.
\item
  The signature algorithm (e.g., SHA-256 with RSA, or ECDSA with
  SHA-256).
\item
  The raw signature bytes.
\item
  Attributes added after signing --- used for timestamps,
  counter-signatures.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{SignerIdentifier: Finding the Signer's
Certificate}\label{_signeridentifier_finding_the_signers_certificate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{SignerIdentifier ::= }\DataTypeTok{CHOICE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    issuerAndSerialNumber    IssuerAndSerialNumber,}
\NormalTok{    subjectKeyIdentifier [}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{] SubjectKeyIdentifier}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The \texttt{issuerAndSerialNumber} form is dominant in PDF signatures.
It contains the issuer DN and serial number of the signer's
certificate --- enough to uniquely identify the certificate within the
\texttt{certificates} SET.

The \texttt{subjectKeyIdentifier} form uses the Subject Key Identifier
extension from the certificate. It is more compact but less commonly
used because older validators do not always support it.

\subsection{Signed Attributes: What Gets
Signed}\label{_signed_attributes_what_gets_signed}

When signed attributes are present (and for PDF signatures, they
\textbf{must} be present per ISO 32000-2:2020), the signature is
computed over the DER encoding of the signed attributes --- \emph{not}
over the content itself. The content's digest is included as a signed
attribute, creating an indirect binding:

\begin{verbatim}
content  --(hash)-->  messageDigest attribute
                            |
signed attributes  --(sign)-->  signature value
\end{verbatim}

The mandatory signed attributes for PDF signatures are:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4444}}@{}}
\caption{Required signed attributes in PDF CMS
signatures}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Attribute
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Attribute
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{contentType} & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.9.3} & Must be
\texttt{id-data} (\texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.7.1}). Identifies the type of
content that was signed. Required by RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message
Syntax (CMS) whenever signed attributes are present. \\
\texttt{messageDigest} & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.9.4} & The hash of the
content (the PDF byte range). This is the attribute that binds the CMS
signature to the PDF document bytes. The validator recomputes this hash
and compares it against the value in this attribute. \\
\texttt{signingTime} & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.9.5} & The time the
signer claims to have signed. This is self-asserted and not
cryptographically verified --- use a timestamp authority for trusted
time (see \texttt{unsignedAttrs}). \\
\texttt{ESS-signing-certificate-v2} &
\texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.47} & Required by PAdES (ETSI EN 319
122-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04)). Contains a hash of the signer's certificate,
binding the signature to a specific certificate and preventing
certificate substitution attacks. Without this attribute, an attacker
could replace the signer's certificate with a different certificate that
has the same public key. \\
\end{longtable}

The \texttt{signingTime} attribute is optional per RFC 5652 ---
Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) but effectively mandatory in
practice. Every PDF viewer displays the signing time, and a signature
without one looks suspicious to users. However, the signing time is
\emph{not} cryptographically trustworthy --- the signer controls the
clock. For legally binding timestamps, use an RFC 3161 timestamp token
in the unsigned attributes (Chapter 18).

\subsection{The ESS-signing-certificate-v2
Attribute}\label{_the_ess_signing_certificate_v2_attribute}

This attribute deserves special attention because it is the dividing
line between a basic CMS signature and a CAdES-compliant
signature --- and therefore between a signature that merely works and
one that meets European regulatory requirements.

The structure contains:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{SigningCertificateV2 ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    certs        }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE} \DataTypeTok{OF}\NormalTok{ ESSCertIDv2,}
\NormalTok{    policies     }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE} \DataTypeTok{OF}\NormalTok{ PolicyInformation }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{ESSCertIDv2 ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    hashAlgorithm  AlgorithmIdentifier }\KeywordTok{DEFAULT}\NormalTok{ \{sha}\DecValTok{{-}256}\NormalTok{\},}
\NormalTok{    certHash       Hash,              }\CommentTok{{-}{-} hash of the signer\textquotesingle{}s certificate}
\NormalTok{    issuerSerial   IssuerSerial }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The \texttt{certHash} is a hash of the signer's DER-encoded certificate.
A validator computes this hash independently and compares it against the
attribute value, ensuring that the certificate in the CMS structure is
the one the signer intended to use.

Without this attribute, a subtle attack is possible: if two certificates
share the same public key (which can happen if a certificate is
re-issued with different extensions or validity periods), an attacker
could swap the certificate in the CMS structure without invalidating the
signature. The ESS-signing-certificate-v2 attribute prevents this by
binding the signature to the exact certificate, not just the public key.

PAdES baseline profiles (PAdES-B-B and above) require
ESS-signing-certificate-v2. A PDF signature that uses the SubFilter
\texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached} but lacks this attribute is non-compliant
with ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04). The EU's DSS validation library
will flag it as a format failure. If you are implementing PDF signing
and need European compliance, this attribute is not optional.

\section{The Signed Attributes DER Encoding
Trap}\label{_the_signed_attributes_der_encoding_trap}

This section describes the single most common cause of CMS signature
validation failures. It has tripped up every signing library author at
least once, and it will trip you up too if you are building or debugging
a CMS implementation at the byte level.

\subsection{The Problem}\label{_the_problem}

RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) Section 5.4 specifies
how signed attributes are encoded for signature computation:

\begin{quote}
The result of the message digest calculation process depends on whether
the signedAttrs field is present. When the field is present,
{[}\ldots\hspace{0pt}{]} the complete DER encoding of the SignedAttrs
value contained in the signedAttrs field is {[}used{]}.
{[}\ldots\hspace{0pt}{]} the IMPLICIT {[}0{]} tag in the signedAttrs is
not used for the DER encoding, rather an EXPLICIT SET OF tag is used.

---  RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) Section 5.4
\end{quote}

Here is what this means in practice:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  In the \texttt{SignerInfo} structure, the \texttt{signedAttrs} field
  is tagged \texttt{{[}0{]}\ IMPLICIT}, meaning the ASN.1 tag byte is
  \texttt{A0} (context class, constructed, tag 0).
\item
  But when computing the signature over the signed attributes, the tag
  must be replaced with \texttt{31} (UNIVERSAL SET OF) --- the tag that
  would be used if the attributes were a standalone SET OF, not an
  implicitly tagged field.
\item
  The rest of the encoding (length bytes and content) remains identical.
\end{enumerate}

This means the bytes that are signed are \emph{not} the bytes that
appear in the CMS structure. You must re-tag the signed attributes
before computing or verifying the signature.

\subsection{Why This Exists}\label{_why_this_exists}

The reason is rooted in ASN.1 encoding theory. The \texttt{signedAttrs}
field uses IMPLICIT tagging to save one byte in the encoding (implicit
tagging replaces the original tag; explicit tagging adds a wrapper). But
the signature must be computed over the \emph{canonical} encoding of the
attributes as a SET OF, not over their implicitly tagged representation
within the SignerInfo. The two encodings differ only in the first byte
(\texttt{A0} vs \texttt{31}), but that single byte difference is enough
to invalidate the signature.

If you are computing a CMS signature manually (perhaps because you are
building a remote signing integration where the hash is computed locally
and the signature is applied remotely), you \textbf{must} re-tag the
signed attributes before hashing them. Use tag \texttt{31} (SET OF), not
\texttt{A0} (IMPLICIT {[}0{]}). BouncyCastle handles this correctly in
its \texttt{CMSSignedDataGenerator}, but if you are working at a lower
level --- constructing CMS structures from raw ASN.1 objects, or
implementing a signature interface for an HSM --- this is the byte that
will break you.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ASN1EncodableVector}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ASN1Set}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{BERTags}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{DERSet}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Demonstrate the signed attributes re}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{tagging requirement}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ In practice}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ BouncyCastle handles this internally }\CommentTok{{-}{-}}\NormalTok{ this}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ code is for understanding}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ not production use}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ SignedAttrsEncodingExample }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \CommentTok{/**}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Re}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{encode signed attributes with SET OF tag for signing}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ The input is the IMPLICIT }\CommentTok{[0]{-}}\NormalTok{tagged bytes from SignerInfo}\CommentTok{;}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ the output replaces tag }\CommentTok{0xA0}\NormalTok{ with }\CommentTok{0x31.}
     \CommentTok{*/}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{retagForSigning}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ implicitTaggedAttrs}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ retagged }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ implicitTaggedAttrs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{clone}\OperatorTok{();}
        \CommentTok{// Replace IMPLICIT [0] tag (0xA0) with SET OF tag (0x31)}
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{retagged}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{]} \OperatorTok{==} \OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{)} \BaseNTok{0xA0}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            retagged}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{]} \OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{BERTags}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SET} \OperatorTok{|}\NormalTok{ BERTags}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{CONSTRUCTED}\OperatorTok{);} \CommentTok{// 0x31}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ retagged}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

When debugging a signature that fails cryptographic verification but the
\texttt{messageDigest} attribute matches the content hash, the
re-tagging issue is the first thing to check. Extract the signed
attributes bytes from the CMS structure, verify that the first byte is
\texttt{31} (not \texttt{A0}) when fed into the signature verification,
and compare the hash of those bytes against the decrypted signature
value.

\section{Unsigned Attributes: Post-Signature
Additions}\label{_unsigned_attributes_post_signature_additions}

Unsigned attributes are added to the \texttt{SignerInfo} \emph{after}
the signature has been computed. They are not covered by the signature
and can be added or modified without invalidating it. This is by
design --- the primary use case is adding information that is not
available at signing time.

The most important unsigned attributes for PDF signatures:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4444}}@{}}
\caption{Common unsigned attributes in PDF CMS
signatures}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Attribute
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Attribute
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{signature-time-stamp} & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.14} & An
RFC 3161 timestamp token over the signature value. Provides a trusted,
third-party assertion of the time the signature existed. This is the
mechanism for timestamping described in Chapter 18. \\
\texttt{counterSignature} & \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.9.6} & A
counter-signature --- another signer's signature over the original
signature value. Rarely used in PDF signatures directly; timestamps are
the modern replacement. \\
\end{longtable}

The timestamp token in the \texttt{signature-time-stamp} attribute is
itself a CMS \texttt{SignedData} structure, with its
\texttt{eContentType} set to \texttt{id-ct-TSTInfo}
(\texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.1.4}). This creates a nested
CMS-within-CMS structure: the outer \texttt{SignedData} is the PDF
signature, and the inner \texttt{SignedData} (inside an unsigned
attribute of the outer) is the timestamp.

\section{CAdES: CMS Advanced Electronic
Signatures}\label{_cades_cms_advanced_electronic_signatures}

The basic CMS \texttt{SignedData} structure defined by RFC 5652 ---
Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) is cryptographically complete but
lacks features needed for European regulatory compliance. \textbf{CAdES}
(CMS Advanced Electronic Signatures), defined in ETSI EN 319 122-1
V1.1.1 (2016-04), extends CMS with additional signed attributes and
profile rules.

The key additions CAdES makes to CMS for PDF signing:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{ESS-signing-certificate-v2} (discussed above) --- mandatory in
  all CAdES profiles, binds the signature to a specific certificate.
\item
  \textbf{Signature policy identifier} --- optional attribute that
  references a specific signature policy (a set of rules defining the
  meaning and validation requirements of the signature). Used in some
  government contexts.
\item
  \textbf{Commitment type indication} --- optional attribute declaring
  what the signer intends by signing (proof of origin, proof of receipt,
  proof of approval, etc.).
\item
  \textbf{Signer location} --- optional attribute with the signer's
  claimed location at signing time.
\end{enumerate}

The relationship between CMS, CAdES, and PAdES is hierarchical:

\begin{verbatim}
CMS (RFC 5652)
  |
  +-- CAdES (ETSI EN 319 122) = CMS + mandatory signed attributes
        |
        +-- PAdES (ETSI EN 319 142) = CAdES embedded in PDF per ISO 32000-2
\end{verbatim}

A PAdES signature is a CAdES signature is a CMS signature. Each layer
adds constraints but does not remove any. A basic
\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} signature is CMS but not necessarily CAdES.
An \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached} signature is CMS, CAdES, and PAdES.

\section{Old PKCS\#7 vs Modern CMS: What
Changed}\label{_old_pkcs7_vs_modern_cms_what_changed}

If you work with PDF signatures long enough, you will encounter CMS
structures produced by older libraries that do not quite match what RFC
5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) specifies. The most common
issues trace back to libraries that implemented the original PKCS\#7
v1.5 rather than CMS, or that implemented an intermediate version with
bugs that were never fixed because "it worked in Acrobat."

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\caption{Differences between legacy PKCS\#7 and modern CMS in the
wild}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Issue
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Legacy PKCS\#7 Behavior
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Modern CMS (RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS))
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Issue
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Legacy PKCS\#7 Behavior
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Modern CMS (RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS))
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
SignedData version & Always 1, regardless of content type & Version
depends on which optional fields are present (1, 3, 4, or 5) \\
digestAlgorithms consistency & May list algorithms not actually used by
any SignerInfo & Must be consistent with SignerInfo entries \\
Signed attributes encoding & Some libraries used BER (not DER) encoding
for the SET OF, causing non-deterministic byte ordering & Must use DER,
which enforces a specific ordering of SET elements \\
ESS-signing-certificate & Absent (not part of original PKCS\#7) &
Required by CAdES and PAdES \\
Certificate ordering & Some libraries assumed specific ordering & No
ordering defined; validators must handle any order \\
NULL parameters in AlgorithmIdentifier & Inconsistent --- some included,
some omitted & SHA-2 algorithms SHOULD omit parameters, but explicit
NULL is tolerated \\
\end{longtable}

Early versions of iText (before 5.3.x) and some older .NET signing
libraries produced CMS structures with BER-encoded signed attributes.
BER allows multiple valid encodings for the same data --- specifically,
SET OF elements may appear in any order. DER requires a specific
ordering (by encoded value). If the signing library used one ordering
and the validator expects another, the hash of the signed attributes
will not match and the signature will fail cryptographic verification.
The signature is not actually broken --- it was computed over
BER-encoded bytes, and if you verify against the same bytes, it passes.
But strict DER-only validators will reject it. This is the most common
"it works in Acrobat but fails in DSS" scenario.

\section{Java: Parsing CMS SignedData}\label{ch06-java-parsing}

Here is how to parse a CMS \texttt{SignedData} structure extracted from
a PDF signature's \texttt{/Contents} field, using BouncyCastle 1.80:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Collection}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ASN1OctetString}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ASN1UTCTime}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Attribute}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CMSAttributes}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaX509CertificateConverter}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CMSSignedData}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{SignerInformation}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{SignerInformationStore}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Store}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{encoders}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Hex}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Parse and inspect a CMS SignedData structure from a PDF signature}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ The input is the raw DER bytes from the }\CommentTok{/}\NormalTok{Contents hex string}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ CmsParsingExample }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{inspectCmsSignature}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ cmsBytes}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        CMSSignedData signedData }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CMSSignedData}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cmsBytes}\OperatorTok{);}               

        \CommentTok{// {-}{-} SignedData version {-}{-}}
        \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ version }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signedData}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getVersion}\OperatorTok{();}
        \CommentTok{// version 1 = standard PDF signature}
        \CommentTok{// version 3 = timestamp token or SubjectKeyIdentifier used}

        \CommentTok{// {-}{-} Certificates embedded in the CMS {-}{-}}
\NormalTok{        Store}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{X509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ certStore }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signedData}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificates}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        JcaX509CertificateConverter converter }\OperatorTok{=}
                \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaX509CertificateConverter}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{setProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{);}

        \CommentTok{// {-}{-} Iterate over SignerInfos (usually exactly one) {-}{-}}
\NormalTok{        SignerInformationStore signerInfoStore }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signedData}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignerInfos}\OperatorTok{();}

        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{SignerInformation signerInfo }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ signerInfoStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSigners}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}    

            \CommentTok{// Digest algorithm (e.g., "2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.1" = SHA{-}256)}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ digestOid }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signerInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDigestAlgOID}\OperatorTok{();}

            \CommentTok{// Signature algorithm (e.g., "1.2.840.113549.1.1.11" = SHA256withRSA)}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ sigAlgOid }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signerInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getEncryptionAlgOID}\OperatorTok{();}

            \CommentTok{// {-}{-} Signed attributes {-}{-}}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signerInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignedAttributes}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}

                \CommentTok{// messageDigest: the hash of the PDF byte range}
                \BuiltInTok{Attribute}\NormalTok{ mdAttr }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signerInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignedAttributes}\OperatorTok{()}
                        \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{CMSAttributes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{messageDigest}\OperatorTok{);}
                \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{mdAttr }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ digest }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ASN1OctetString}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                            mdAttr}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAttrValues}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getObjectAt}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)}
                    \OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{getOctets}\OperatorTok{();}
                    \CommentTok{// Hex.toHexString(digest) for human{-}readable output}
                \OperatorTok{\}}

                \CommentTok{// signingTime: the claimed signing time}
                \BuiltInTok{Attribute}\NormalTok{ stAttr }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signerInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignedAttributes}\OperatorTok{()}
                        \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{CMSAttributes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{signingTime}\OperatorTok{);}
                \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{stAttr }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{                    ASN1UTCTime time }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ASN1UTCTime}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                            stAttr}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAttrValues}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getObjectAt}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{));}
                    \CommentTok{// time.getDate() returns a java.util.Date}
                \OperatorTok{\}}
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \CommentTok{// {-}{-} Find the signer\textquotesingle{}s certificate {-}{-}}
            \BuiltInTok{Collection}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{X509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ matches }\OperatorTok{=}
\NormalTok{                    certStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getMatches}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signerInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSID}\OperatorTok{());}                 
            \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{X509CertificateHolder holder }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ matches}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ converter}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{holder}\OperatorTok{);}
                \CommentTok{// cert.getSubjectX500Principal() = signer identity}
                \CommentTok{// cert.getNotAfter() = certificate expiration}
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \CommentTok{// {-}{-} Raw signature bytes {-}{-}}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ signatureBytes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signerInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignature}\OperatorTok{();}                 
            \CommentTok{// signatureBytes.length = 256 for RSA{-}2048, 384 for RSA{-}3072, etc.}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The \texttt{CMSSignedData} constructor parses the DER bytes into an
  in-memory object graph.
\item
  PDF signatures have exactly one SignerInfo. Multiple signers use
  multiple signature dictionaries, not multiple SignerInfos in one CMS
  structure.
\item
  The \texttt{getSID()} method returns a selector that matches against
  the \texttt{SignerIdentifier} --- either
  \texttt{issuerAndSerialNumber} or \texttt{subjectKeyIdentifier}.
\item
  The raw signature value. For RSA, this is the encrypted hash. For
  ECDSA, this is the DER-encoded \texttt{(r,\ s)} pair.
\end{itemize}

\section{Java: Building CMS
SignedData}\label{_java_building_cms_signeddata}

Creating a CMS \texttt{SignedData} for PDF signing is the reverse of
parsing. The following example uses \texttt{CMSSignedDataGenerator} with
explicit control over the signed attributes:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PrivateKey}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Security}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{List}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaCertStore}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CMSProcessableByteArray}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CMSSignedData}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CMSSignedDataGenerator}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaSignerInfoGeneratorBuilder}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{provider}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{operator}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ContentSigner}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{operator}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaContentSignerBuilder}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{operator}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaDigestCalculatorProviderBuilder}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Build a detached CMS SignedData suitable for embedding}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ in a PDF signature}\CommentTok{\textquotesingle{}}\NormalTok{s }\CommentTok{/}\NormalTok{Contents field}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ CmsBuildingExample }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \DataTypeTok{static} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{Security}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{());}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{/**}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Create a detached CMS SignedData over the given byte range content}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@param}\CommentTok{ }\AnnotationTok{byteRangeContent}\CommentTok{  }\NormalTok{the PDF bytes specified by }\CommentTok{/}\NormalTok{ByteRange}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@param}\CommentTok{ }\AnnotationTok{privateKey}\CommentTok{        }\NormalTok{the signer}\CommentTok{\textquotesingle{}}\NormalTok{s private key}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@param}\CommentTok{ }\AnnotationTok{chain}\CommentTok{             }\NormalTok{the certificate chain }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{end}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{entity first}\CommentTok{)}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@return}\NormalTok{ DER}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{encoded CMS SignedData bytes}
     \CommentTok{*/}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{createDetachedSignature}\OperatorTok{(}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ byteRangeContent}\OperatorTok{,}
            \BuiltInTok{PrivateKey}\NormalTok{ privateKey}\OperatorTok{,}
            \BuiltInTok{Certificate}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ chain}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

        \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ signingCert }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ chain}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{];}

        \CommentTok{// Determine signature algorithm from key type}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ sigAlg }\OperatorTok{=} \ControlFlowTok{switch} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{privateKey}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"RSA"} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"SHA256withRSA"}\OperatorTok{;}
            \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"EC"}  \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"SHA256withECDSA"}\OperatorTok{;}
            \KeywordTok{default} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \ControlFlowTok{throw} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{IllegalArgumentException}\OperatorTok{(}
                    \StringTok{"Unsupported key type: "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ privateKey}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{());}
        \OperatorTok{\};}

\NormalTok{        ContentSigner signer }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaContentSignerBuilder}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sigAlg}\OperatorTok{)}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{)}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{privateKey}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{        CMSSignedDataGenerator generator }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CMSSignedDataGenerator}\OperatorTok{();}

\NormalTok{        generator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addSignerInfoGenerator}\OperatorTok{(}
                \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaSignerInfoGeneratorBuilder}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaDigestCalculatorProviderBuilder}\OperatorTok{()}
                                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{)}
                                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{()}
                \OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signer}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signingCert}\OperatorTok{)}                                  
        \OperatorTok{);}

        \CommentTok{// Include the full certificate chain}
        \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{Certificate}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ certList }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}();}
        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ chain}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            certList}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cert}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
\NormalTok{        generator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addCertificates}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaCertStore}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certList}\OperatorTok{));}

        \CommentTok{// Generate DETACHED signature (false = do not encapsulate content)}
\NormalTok{        CMSProcessableByteArray content }\OperatorTok{=}
                \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CMSProcessableByteArray}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{byteRangeContent}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        CMSSignedData signedData }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ generator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{content}\OperatorTok{,} \KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{);}        

        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ signedData}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{();}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{JcaSignerInfoGeneratorBuilder.build()} automatically adds the
  standard signed attributes (\texttt{contentType},
  \texttt{messageDigest}, \texttt{signingTime}). For CAdES compliance,
  you need to add ESS-signing-certificate-v2 explicitly using a
  \texttt{CMSAttributeTableGenerator}.
\item
  The \texttt{false} parameter is critical: detached signature. The byte
  range content is hashed but not embedded in the CMS structure.
\end{itemize}

\section{Annotated ASN.1 Dump of a Real PDF
Signature}\label{_annotated_asn_1_dump_of_a_real_pdf_signature}

The following is the output of \texttt{openssl\ asn1parse} run against
the DER-decoded \texttt{/Contents} of an actual PDF signature. Each line
is annotated with what it represents in the CMS structure.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
    \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{ l=}\DecValTok{2417}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}              \CommentTok{{-}{-} ContentInfo}
    \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=   }\DecValTok{9}\NormalTok{ prim: OBJECT   :pkcs7{-}signedData}
                                                     \CommentTok{{-}{-} OID 1.2.840.113549.1.7.2}
   \DecValTok{15}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{ l=}\DecValTok{2402}\NormalTok{ cons: cont [ }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{ ]             }\CommentTok{{-}{-} EXPLICIT [0] content}
   \DecValTok{19}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{ l=}\DecValTok{2398}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}                \CommentTok{{-}{-} SignedData}
   \DecValTok{23}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=   }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{ prim: }\DataTypeTok{INTEGER}\NormalTok{  :}\DecValTok{01}            \CommentTok{{-}{-} version = 1}
   \DecValTok{26}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=  }\DecValTok{15}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SET}                     \CommentTok{{-}{-} digestAlgorithms}
   \DecValTok{28}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=  }\DecValTok{13}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}
   \DecValTok{30}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=   }\DecValTok{9}\NormalTok{ prim: OBJECT   :sha256        }\CommentTok{{-}{-} OID 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.1}
   \DecValTok{41}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=   }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{ prim: }\DataTypeTok{NULL}                    \CommentTok{{-}{-} parameters (explicit NULL)}
   \DecValTok{43}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=  }\DecValTok{11}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}                \CommentTok{{-}{-} encapContentInfo}
   \DecValTok{45}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=   }\DecValTok{9}\NormalTok{ prim: OBJECT   :pkcs7{-}data    }\CommentTok{{-}{-} eContentType = id{-}data}
                                                     \CommentTok{{-}{-} OID 1.2.840.113549.1.7.1}
                                                     \CommentTok{{-}{-} (eContent ABSENT = detached)}
   \DecValTok{56}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{ l=}\DecValTok{1735}\NormalTok{ cons: cont [ }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{ ]             }\CommentTok{{-}{-} certificates [0] IMPLICIT}
   \DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{ l= }\DecValTok{872}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}                \CommentTok{{-}{-} Certificate \#1 (end{-}entity)}
  \DecValTok{936}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{ l= }\DecValTok{855}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}                \CommentTok{{-}{-} Certificate \#2 (intermediate CA)}
 \DecValTok{1795}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{ l= }\DecValTok{624}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SET}                     \CommentTok{{-}{-} signerInfos}
 \DecValTok{1799}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{ l= }\DecValTok{620}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}                \CommentTok{{-}{-} SignerInfo}
 \DecValTok{1803}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=   }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{ prim: }\DataTypeTok{INTEGER}\NormalTok{  :}\DecValTok{01}            \CommentTok{{-}{-} version = 1}
 \DecValTok{1806}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{ l= }\DecValTok{141}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}                \CommentTok{{-}{-} sid = issuerAndSerialNumber}
 \DecValTok{1809}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{ l= }\DecValTok{118}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}                \CommentTok{{-}{-}   issuer DN}
