 \DecValTok{1930}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=  }\DecValTok{19}\NormalTok{ prim: }\DataTypeTok{INTEGER}\NormalTok{  :...           }\CommentTok{{-}{-}   serialNumber}
 \DecValTok{1951}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=  }\DecValTok{13}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}                \CommentTok{{-}{-} digestAlgorithm}
 \DecValTok{1953}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=   }\DecValTok{9}\NormalTok{ prim: OBJECT   :sha256        }\CommentTok{{-}{-}   SHA{-}256}
 \DecValTok{1964}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=   }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{ prim: }\DataTypeTok{NULL}
 \DecValTok{1966}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{ l= }\DecValTok{189}\NormalTok{ cons: cont [ }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{ ]             }\CommentTok{{-}{-} signedAttrs [0] IMPLICIT}
 \DecValTok{1969}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=  }\DecValTok{24}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}                \CommentTok{{-}{-}   contentType attribute}
 \DecValTok{1995}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=  }\DecValTok{47}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}                \CommentTok{{-}{-}   messageDigest attribute}
 \DecValTok{2044}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=  }\DecValTok{28}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}                \CommentTok{{-}{-}   signingTime attribute}
 \DecValTok{2074}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=  }\DecValTok{79}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}                \CommentTok{{-}{-}   ESS{-}signing{-}certificate{-}v2}
 \DecValTok{2155}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=  }\DecValTok{13}\NormalTok{ cons: }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}                \CommentTok{{-}{-} signatureAlgorithm}
 \DecValTok{2157}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=   }\DecValTok{9}\NormalTok{ prim: OBJECT   :sha256WithRSAEncryption}
                                                     \CommentTok{{-}{-} OID 1.2.840.113549.1.1.11}
 \DecValTok{2168}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=   }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{ prim: }\DataTypeTok{NULL}
 \DecValTok{2170}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{ l= }\DecValTok{256}\NormalTok{ prim: }\DataTypeTok{OCTET} \DataTypeTok{STRING}            \CommentTok{{-}{-} signature (256 bytes = RSA{-}2048)}
 \DecValTok{2430}\NormalTok{:d=}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{  hl=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ l=   }\DecValTok{7}\NormalTok{ cons: cont [ }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{ ]             }\CommentTok{{-}{-} unsignedAttrs [1] IMPLICIT}
                                                     \CommentTok{{-}{-} (timestamp token would be here)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The offsets in this dump are from the start of the DER-encoded CMS blob,
not from the start of the PDF file. To get the absolute file offset, add
the starting offset of the \texttt{/Contents} hex string (as specified
by \texttt{/ByteRange}) plus the hex-to-binary decoding offset. When
debugging with a hex editor, remember that the \texttt{/Contents} value
is hex-encoded: each byte in the ASN.1 dump corresponds to two hex
characters in the PDF file.

\section{Summary}\label{_summary_6}

CMS \texttt{SignedData} is the envelope that carries every PDF digital
signature. Its lineage stretches from RSA's PKCS\#7 in 1993 through the
S/MIME working group to the current RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message
Syntax (CMS) standard. The core design has been stable for over
twenty-five years, which is both its strength (mature, well-understood,
universally supported) and its burden (legacy quirks from 1993 are still
with us).

The key points for PDF signature work:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{ContentInfo wraps everything.} The OID
  \texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.7.2} identifies the CMS as
  \texttt{SignedData}. Learn to recognize its DER encoding
  (\texttt{06\ 09\ 2A\ 86\ 48\ 86\ F7\ 0D\ 01\ 07\ 02}) in hex dumps.
\item
  \textbf{PDF signatures are detached.} The \texttt{eContent} in
  \texttt{EncapsulatedContentInfo} is absent. The signed content lives
  in the PDF file, addressed by \texttt{/ByteRange}.
\item
  \textbf{Signed attributes are signed, not the content.} The signature
  covers the DER-encoded signed attributes, which include the
  \texttt{messageDigest} (hash of the content). This two-level
  indirection is essential to understand.
\item
  \textbf{The DER re-tagging trap is real.} The signed attributes must
  be re-tagged from \texttt{A0} to \texttt{31} before hashing for
  signature computation. BouncyCastle handles this, but any lower-level
  implementation must get this right.
\item
  \textbf{ESS-signing-certificate-v2 is the CAdES requirement.} Without
  it, the signature is CMS-valid but not CAdES-valid and therefore not
  PAdES-compliant.
\item
  \textbf{Unsigned attributes carry timestamps.} The
  \texttt{signature-time-stamp} attribute contains a nested CMS
  structure --- a timestamp token from an RFC 3161 TSA.
\item
  \textbf{Legacy PKCS\#7 differences still cause interop failures.} BER
  vs DER encoding of signed attributes is the most common culprit.
\end{itemize}

The next part of this book turns to PDF internals --- the file
structure, incremental updates, and form fields that the CMS
\texttt{SignedData} is embedded within. Understanding the container is
meaningless without understanding the document it lives in.

\chapter{PDF Internals}\label{_pdf_internals}

\chapter{PDF File Structure}\label{ch07}

Every PDF file on earth --- whether it is a single-page receipt from
your corner bakery or a 4,000-page securities filing with twelve nested
signatures --- is built from the same four structural components,
arranged in the same order, governed by the same rules that John Warnock
and Chuck Geschke laid down at Adobe in 1993. If you want to understand
why a signature's \texttt{/ByteRange} array contains raw file offsets,
why cross-reference tables use fixed-width 20-byte entries, or why some
PDFs have three \texttt{\%\%EOF} markers, you have to understand this
structure at the byte level.

This chapter dismantles a PDF file from the first byte to the last. We
will trace headers, decode every object type the specification defines,
read cross-reference tables in both the classic ASCII format and the
newer compressed stream format, examine the trailer dictionary, walk
through a minimal PDF byte by byte, and then connect all of it back to
the one thing we care about most: digital signatures.

\section{The Four Sections}\label{_the_four_sections}

ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5 defines the layout. Every conforming PDF
file consists of these four parts, in this order:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Header} --- a single line declaring the PDF version, plus an
  optional binary comment
\item
  \textbf{Body} --- the collection of indirect objects that define
  everything in the document: pages, fonts, images, form fields,
  annotations, and signatures
\item
  \textbf{Cross-reference table} --- an index that maps object numbers
  to byte offsets in the file, enabling random access without linear
  scanning
\item
  \textbf{Trailer} --- a dictionary that tells the parser where to find
  the cross-reference table, which object is the document root, and (for
  incremental updates) where to find the previous cross-reference
  section
\end{enumerate}

This four-part structure has been stable since PDF 1.0. PDF 1.5
introduced an alternative binary representation for the cross-reference
data (cross-reference streams), and PDF 2.0 deprecated some legacy
metadata keys, but the fundamental anatomy has not changed in over three
decades.

\subsection{The Header}\label{_the_header}

A PDF file begins with a version identifier on the first line:

\begin{verbatim}
%PDF-1.7
\end{verbatim}

That is nine bytes: the five characters \texttt{\%PDF-}, the version
number, and a line ending. The \texttt{\%} character is the PDF comment
delimiter, which means the header line is technically a comment --- a
design choice that lets non-PDF-aware tools skip it without error.

The version in the header is the \emph{file} version. ISO 32000-2:2020
Section 7.5.2 specifies that a \texttt{/Version} key in the document
catalog may override this value. In practice, many generators write
\texttt{\%PDF-1.7} in the header and then set \texttt{/Version\ /2.0} in
the catalog when they need PDF 2.0 features. This dual-version mechanism
exists for backward compatibility: an older parser that does not
understand the catalog \texttt{/Version} will still see a version number
it can process (or at least attempt to process) in the header.

\subsubsection{The Binary Comment
Trick}\label{_the_binary_comment_trick}

Immediately after the header line, you will almost always find a second
comment line containing four or more bytes with values above 127 (0x80):

\begin{verbatim}
%âãÏÓ
\end{verbatim}

This is not decoration. Its purpose is purely practical: it forces
file-type detection heuristics --- in web servers, email gateways, FTP
clients, operating systems --- to classify the file as binary rather
than ASCII text. Without this line, a transfer in ASCII mode (remember
the FTP \texttt{ASCII} vs \texttt{BINARY} setting?) could mangle the
binary stream data inside the PDF. ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5.2
recommends but does not require this binary hint.

You will occasionally encounter PDFs without the binary
comment --- usually from minimalist generators or test harnesses. The
file is still valid, but it is more vulnerable to transfer corruption.

When debugging PDF files at the byte level, the header is your first
sanity check. If \texttt{\%PDF-} is not the first five bytes, something
has happened upstream: a Unicode BOM was prepended, an HTML wrapper was
added, a proxy rewrote the response body, or the file was opened as text
and line endings were converted. We have encountered every one of these
in production systems.

\subsection{The Body}\label{_the_body}

The body contains all the PDF objects that constitute the document. Page
trees, content streams, font dictionaries, image data, form fields,
metadata, and --- critically for us --- signature dictionaries and their
CMS payloads.

Objects in the body are written as \textbf{indirect objects}, each
wrapped in a numbered envelope:

\begin{verbatim}
7 0 obj
<< /Type /Page /MediaBox [0 0 612 792] /Contents 12 0 R >>
endobj
\end{verbatim}

The prefix \texttt{7\ 0\ obj} means "object number 7, generation number
0." The keyword \texttt{endobj} marks the end of the object. Between
them is the object's value --- here, a dictionary describing a page.

Objects may appear in any order. There is no requirement that object 1
precedes object 2, or that page objects come before font objects. The
cross-reference table handles the mapping from logical object number to
physical byte offset. This is a fundamental design principle:
\textbf{the physical ordering of objects in the file has no bearing on
the logical structure of the document}.

Different PDF generators produce wildly different orderings. Adobe
Acrobat tends to cluster related objects together. Apache PDFBox writes
objects in the order they were allocated in memory. Linearized PDFs (the
"fast web view" format described in ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.6)
deliberately reorder objects so that the first page can be displayed
before the entire file is downloaded. None of this changes the logical
content --- but it changes the byte offsets, which in turn changes the
byte range that a signature covers.

Some tools assume objects appear in numerical order. They do not. If you
are writing a PDF parser or debugging one, never assume object N appears
before object N+1 in the file. Always consult the cross-reference table.

\subsection{The Cross-Reference Table}\label{_the_cross_reference_table}

After the body comes the cross-reference table (commonly called the
"xref table") --- an index that records the byte offset where each
object starts in the file.

\begin{verbatim}
xref
0 8
0000000000 65535 f
0000000015 00000 n
0000000078 00000 n
0000000178 00000 n
0000000331 00000 n
0000000409 00000 n
0000000553 00000 n
0000000715 00000 n
\end{verbatim}

The keyword \texttt{xref} marks the beginning. The line \texttt{0\ 8}
means "this section starts at object number 0 and contains 8 entries."
Each subsequent line is exactly 20 bytes (including the line terminator)
and contains three fields:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Byte offset} (10 digits, zero-padded) --- the position in the
  file where the object starts
\item
  \textbf{Generation number} (5 digits, zero-padded) --- used for object
  versioning; almost always \texttt{00000}
\item
  \textbf{In-use flag} --- \texttt{n} means the object is in use;
  \texttt{f} means it is free (deleted)
\end{itemize}

The very first entry (object 0) is always free, with generation number
65535. It serves as the head of a linked list of free objects. Each free
entry's "byte offset" field actually contains the object number of the
\emph{next} free entry, forming a chain. This mechanism allows PDF
writers to reuse deleted object numbers --- a feature that mattered when
PDFs were small and object numbers precious, and that is now mostly
vestigial.

The 20-byte-per-entry format is non-negotiable. This rigidity is
deliberate: it enables random access to any entry via arithmetic
(\texttt{entry\_offset\ =\ xref\_start\ +\ header\_bytes\ +\ object\_number\ *\ 20})
without scanning the entire table. This random-access property is what
allows a viewer to display a single page from a 10,000-page document
without loading the whole file into memory.

The generation number increments when an object is deleted and its
number is later reused, preventing stale references (\texttt{7\ 0\ R})
from resolving to the wrong content. In practice, nearly every object
you encounter in the wild has generation 0. The mechanism exists, the
specification defines it carefully, and production PDFs almost never
exercise it.

\subsection{The Trailer}\label{_the_trailer}

Immediately after the cross-reference table comes the trailer:

\begin{verbatim}
trailer
<<
  /Size 8
  /Root 1 0 R
  /Info 6 0 R
  /ID [<a9f2c74b...> <a9f2c74b...>]
>>
startxref
4892
%%EOF
\end{verbatim}

The trailer dictionary is the most important dictionary in the file,
because \emph{everything} starts here. A PDF parser begins at the
\textbf{end} of the file. It searches backward for \texttt{\%\%EOF},
then backward for \texttt{startxref}, reads the byte offset (4892), and
jumps to that offset to find the cross-reference table. From the xref
table, it reads the trailer dictionary. From the trailer dictionary's
\texttt{/Root} key, it finds the document catalog. From the catalog, it
can navigate to every object in the document.

\textbf{Parsing a PDF starts from the end, not the beginning.} This
backward-search strategy is fundamental to how incremental updates work
(Chapter 8), and it is the reason a PDF can be extended by appending
data without rewriting the beginning of the file.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.6000}}@{}}
\caption{Trailer Dictionary Keys --- ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5.5,
Table 15}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/Size} & integer & Total number of entries in the
cross-reference table (including object 0). One greater than the highest
object number used. \\
\texttt{/Root} & indirect reference & The document catalog --- root of
the entire logical document tree. \\
\texttt{/Info} & indirect reference & Document information dictionary.
Optional and deprecated in PDF 2.0 (replaced by XMP metadata), but still
universally present in real-world files. \\
\texttt{/ID} & array of two byte strings & File identifiers. The first
element is assigned at creation and never changes; the second is updated
on each save. Signatures depend on \texttt{/ID} --- PAdES profiles
strongly recommend it. \\
\texttt{/Prev} & integer & Byte offset of the \emph{previous}
cross-reference section. This is how incremental updates chain together
(Chapter 8). \\
\texttt{/Encrypt} & dictionary or reference & Encryption dictionary. The
signature value itself (\texttt{/Contents}) is never encrypted even in
encrypted PDFs, but the surrounding content may be. \\
\end{longtable}

The \texttt{/ID} array is not cosmetic. Some signing tools use the first
element as input to seed value dictionaries. Some validators check
\texttt{/ID} consistency across incremental revisions. If a tool strips
or regenerates \texttt{/ID} during processing, existing signatures may
fail validation. We have seen this happen with document management
systems that "normalize" PDFs on upload.

\section{PDF Object Types}\label{_pdf_object_types}

Every value in a PDF file is one of exactly ten types, defined in ISO
32000-2:2020 Section 7.3. Understanding them is necessary for reading
raw PDF structure, debugging signatures at the COS level, and working
with PDFBox's object model.

\subsection{Boolean, Integer, Real,
Null}\label{_boolean_integer_real_null}

\textbf{Boolean} --- the keywords \texttt{true} or \texttt{false}. Used
in flags like \texttt{/NeedAppearances\ true}.

\textbf{Integer} --- a signed whole number with no decimal point:
\texttt{42}, \texttt{-7}, \texttt{0}. The four values in a
\texttt{/ByteRange} array are integers. Integers in PDF have no fixed
bit width; implementations typically use 64-bit storage.

\textbf{Real} --- a signed number with a decimal point: \texttt{3.14},
\texttt{-0.001}, \texttt{72.0}. Used in coordinate values,
transformation matrices, and font metrics.

\textbf{Null} --- the keyword \texttt{null}. A dictionary key mapped to
\texttt{null} is equivalent to the key being absent. Null references are
resolved as "object not found."

\subsection{String (Literal and
Hexadecimal)}\label{_string_literal_and_hexadecimal}

PDF has two string representations:

\textbf{Literal string} --- parentheses-delimited:
\texttt{(Hello\ World)}. Backslash escapes handle special characters:
\texttt{\textbackslash{}(}, \texttt{\textbackslash{})},
\texttt{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}, \texttt{\textbackslash{}n},
\texttt{\textbackslash{}r}, \texttt{\textbackslash{}t}, and octal
escapes like \texttt{\textbackslash{}101} (the letter A). Unbalanced
parentheses must be escaped; balanced ones need not be.

\textbf{Hexadecimal string} --- angle-bracket-delimited hex pairs:
\texttt{\textless{}48656C6C6F\textgreater{}}. Each pair of hex digits
represents one byte. The \texttt{/Contents} value in a signature
dictionary is always a hex string because it contains raw binary CMS
data. The hex string is right-padded with zeros to fill the space
reserved during signing.

\subsection{Name}\label{_name}

A slash-prefixed identifier: \texttt{/Type}, \texttt{/Page},
\texttt{/Sig}, \texttt{/ByteRange}. Names are atoms, not
strings --- they serve as dictionary keys and enumeration values. Names
containing characters outside the printable ASCII range use a
\texttt{\#xx} hex escape: \texttt{/F\#23oo} represents the name
\texttt{F\#oo}.

\subsection{Array}\label{_array}

An ordered collection enclosed in square brackets:
\texttt{{[}0\ 14000\ 30408\ 14592{]}}. Arrays can contain any mix of
types, including other arrays and dictionaries. The \texttt{/ByteRange}
value is an array of four integers.

\subsection{Dictionary}\label{_dictionary}

Key-value pairs enclosed in double angle brackets:

\begin{verbatim}
<< /Type /Sig /Filter /Adobe.PPKLite /SubFilter /adbe.pkcs7.detached >>
\end{verbatim}

Keys are always names. Values can be any type. Dictionaries are the
workhorse of PDF --- every page, every font, every annotation, every
signature is a dictionary or contains one.

\subsection{Stream}\label{_stream}

A dictionary followed by raw binary data, delimited by \texttt{stream}
and \texttt{endstream}:

\begin{verbatim}
8 0 obj
<< /Type /XObject /Subtype /Image /Length 12345 /Filter /FlateDecode >>
stream
...binary data (12345 bytes, possibly compressed)...
endstream
endobj
\end{verbatim}

Streams are the only mechanism for storing bulk data: page drawing
instructions, images, embedded font programs, cross-reference data, and
CMap tables. The \texttt{/Length} key must be exact --- if it is wrong,
the parser reads too few or too many bytes, corrupting everything that
follows.

A wrong \texttt{/Length} is one of the most common forms of PDF
corruption from buggy generators. When debugging a corrupt PDF, always
compare the \texttt{/Length} value against the actual byte count between
\texttt{stream} and \texttt{endstream}. QPDF's \texttt{-\/-check} flag
catches this automatically.

\subsection{Indirect Object and Indirect
Reference}\label{_indirect_object_and_indirect_reference}

An \textbf{indirect object} wraps any value with an object number and
generation number: \texttt{12\ 0\ obj\ …\hspace{0pt}\ endobj}. An
\textbf{indirect reference} is a pointer to such an object:
\texttt{12\ 0\ R}. The cross-reference table resolves these: given
\texttt{12\ 0\ R}, the parser looks up object 12, generation 0, in the
xref table, seeks to the recorded byte offset, and reads the object.

Indirect references are the glue that holds a PDF together. The catalog
references the page tree, the page tree references individual pages,
pages reference content streams and annotations, annotations reference
appearance streams --- all through indirect references.

\section{Cross-Reference Streams}\label{ch07-xref-streams}

PDF 1.5 introduced \textbf{cross-reference streams} --- an alternative
to the classic ASCII xref table that stores the same data in a
compressed binary stream object.

\subsection{Why They Exist}\label{_why_they_exist}

The classic xref table has two problems. First, the 20-bytes-per-entry
format is wasteful: a PDF with 100,000 objects needs a 2 MB xref table
of pure ASCII text. Cross-reference streams compress this data 5-10x
using standard stream filters (typically FlateDecode). Second, the
classic format has no entry type for objects stored inside object
streams (covered next); cross-reference streams introduce a Type 2 entry
for exactly this purpose.

\subsection{Structure}\label{_structure}

\begin{verbatim}
42 0 obj
<<
  /Type /XRef
  /Size 100
  /W [1 3 1]
  /Index [0 100]
  /Root 1 0 R
  /Info 5 0 R
  /ID [<abc123> <abc123>]
  /Filter /FlateDecode
  /Length 487
>>
stream
...compressed binary data...
endstream
endobj
\end{verbatim}

The stream dictionary serves double duty: it contains both the stream
parameters (\texttt{/Filter}, \texttt{/Length}) and the trailer
parameters (\texttt{/Root}, \texttt{/Info}, \texttt{/ID},
\texttt{/Prev}, \texttt{/Size}). There is no separate \texttt{trailer}
keyword when cross-reference streams are used.

The \texttt{/W} array specifies field widths in bytes for each entry.
\texttt{/W\ {[}1\ 3\ 1{]}} means: 1 byte for the type field, 3 bytes for
field 2, 1 byte for field 3 --- 5 bytes total per entry.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1111}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4444}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4444}}@{}}
\caption{Cross-Reference Stream Entry Types --- ISO 32000-2:2020 Section
7.5.8, Table 18}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Field 2 Meaning
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Field 3 Meaning
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Field 2 Meaning
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Field 3 Meaning
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
0 & Next free object number & Generation number of the free entry \\
1 & Byte offset of the object in the file & Generation number \\
2 & Object number of the containing object stream & Index of this object
within that object stream \\
\end{longtable}

Type 2 is the key innovation. It says "this object is not a standalone
indirect object in the file body; it is packed inside object stream N,
at position M." Classic xref tables cannot express this.

The \texttt{/Index} array specifies which object numbers are covered.
\texttt{/Index\ {[}0\ 100{]}} means entries for objects 0 through 99. A
cross-reference stream can have multiple subsections:
\texttt{/Index\ {[}0\ 50\ 200\ 10{]}} covers objects 0-49 and 200-209.

Some PDF libraries produce files that start with a classic xref table
but switch to cross-reference streams in incremental updates (or vice
versa). This is legal per the specification, but it trips up parsers
that expect consistency. PDFBox handles the transition transparently;
not all tools do.

\section{Object Streams}\label{_object_streams}

PDF 1.5 also introduced \textbf{object streams}: multiple indirect
objects packed into a single compressed stream, eliminating the repeated
\texttt{N\ 0\ obj}\ldots\hspace{0pt}\texttt{endobj} framing overhead.

\begin{verbatim}
10 0 obj
<< /Type /ObjStm /N 5 /First 32 /Filter /FlateDecode /Length 418 >>
stream
...compressed data containing 5 objects...
endstream
endobj
\end{verbatim}

The \texttt{/N} key gives the count of packed objects; \texttt{/First}
gives the byte offset within the decompressed data where the first
object's value begins (the preceding bytes contain a table of object
numbers and offsets).

Not everything can live in an object stream. ISO 32000-2:2020 Section
7.5.7 excludes: stream objects (they have their own
\texttt{stream}/\texttt{endstream} framing), the encryption dictionary,
objects with non-zero generation numbers, and the cross-reference stream
itself. Signature dictionaries \emph{can} technically live in object
streams (they are dictionaries, not streams), but signing tools almost
never place them there because it would complicate byte-range
calculation.

If you need to find a signature dictionary in a raw PDF, do not search
for \texttt{/Type\ /Sig} in the file bytes --- it might be compressed
inside an object stream. Use \texttt{qpdf\ -\/-show-object=N} where N is
the object number. QPDF decompresses object streams transparently.

\section{Hex Dump Walkthrough: A Minimal Valid
PDF}\label{_hex_dump_walkthrough_a_minimal_valid_pdf}

Let us trace through a complete, minimal PDF file, annotating every
section:

\begin{verbatim}
%PDF-1.7                                          
%âãÏÓ                                             

1 0 obj                                            
<< /Type /Catalog /Pages 2 0 R >>
endobj

2 0 obj                                            
<< /Type /Pages /Kids [3 0 R] /Count 1 >>
endobj

3 0 obj                                            
<< /Type /Page /Parent 2 0 R /MediaBox [0 0 612 792]
   /Contents 4 0 R
   /Resources << /Font << /F1 5 0 R >> >> >>
endobj

4 0 obj                                            
<< /Length 44 >>
stream
BT /F1 24 Tf 100 700 Td (Hello) Tj ET
endstream
endobj

5 0 obj                                            
<< /Type /Font /Subtype /Type1 /BaseFont /Helvetica >>
endobj

xref                                               
0 6
0000000000 65535 f
0000000015 00000 n
0000000066 00000 n
0000000121 00000 n
0000000286 00000 n
0000000384 00000 n

trailer                                            
<< /Size 6 /Root 1 0 R >>
startxref                                          
453
%%EOF                                              
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Header: PDF version 1.7
\item
  Binary hint: four bytes above 0x80, forcing binary classification
\item
  Catalog: root of the document tree; \texttt{/Pages} points to the page
  tree root
\item
  Pages node: page tree root with one child page
\item
  Page: defines the media size (US Letter), references a content stream
  and font resources
\item
  Content stream: drawing instructions --- Begin Text, set font F1 at
  24pt, move to (100,700), show "Hello", End Text
\item
  Font: a reference to standard Helvetica (not embedded --- viewers
  provide a substitute)
\item
  Cross-reference table: six entries mapping object numbers to byte
  offsets
\item
  Trailer: \texttt{/Size\ 6} (six xref entries), \texttt{/Root} points
  to the catalog
\item
  \texttt{startxref}: byte offset of the \texttt{xref} keyword
\item
  \texttt{\%\%EOF}: end-of-file marker
\end{itemize}

A parser reads this \emph{backward}: find \texttt{\%\%EOF}, find
\texttt{startxref}, jump to byte 453, parse the xref table, read the
trailer, follow \texttt{/Root} to object 1, and navigate from there.

This minimal PDF omits a \texttt{/Info} dictionary, \texttt{/ID} array,
and font embedding. Production PDFs have dozens to hundreds of objects,
but the structural skeleton is identical.

\section{How Different Generators Produce Different
Structures}\label{_how_different_generators_produce_different_structures}

Not all PDFs are created equal at the byte level, and the differences
matter when you are computing byte ranges for signatures.

\textbf{Adobe Acrobat} produces linearized files by default, with
objects reordered for progressive display and a hint table at the
beginning. It uses cross-reference streams in modern versions.

\textbf{Apache PDFBox} (3.0.0) writes objects in memory-allocation
order, uses classic xref tables by default, and does not linearize. Its
incremental save mode (\texttt{saveIncremental()}) produces clean
append-only updates.

\textbf{iText / OpenPDF} frequently uses cross-reference streams even
for PDF 1.7 files, and its object ordering varies by version.

\textbf{LibreOffice} produces classic xref tables with object ordering
that appears semi-random (it reflects internal processing order).

\textbf{Microsoft Word} (Save as PDF) produces well-structured files
with cross-reference streams and object streams, heavily compressed.

\textbf{QPDF} can transform between any of these representations:
linearize, de-linearize, convert xref tables to streams or vice versa,
unpack object streams, and normalize whitespace. It is the Swiss Army
knife of PDF structural debugging.

When comparing two versions of a signed PDF at the structural level:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Show all xref entries and their origins}
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}show{-}xref}\NormalTok{ document.pdf}

\CommentTok{\# Convert to JSON for semantic comparison}
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}json}\NormalTok{ document{-}v1.pdf }\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ /tmp/v1.json}
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}json}\NormalTok{ document{-}v2.pdf }\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ /tmp/v2.json}
\FunctionTok{diff}\NormalTok{ /tmp/v1.json /tmp/v2.json}

\CommentTok{\# Decompress everything for human{-}readable byte{-}level comparison}
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}qdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}object{-}streams}\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{disable input.pdf readable.pdf}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Why File Structure Matters for
Signatures}\label{_why_file_structure_matters_for_signatures}

We have spent this chapter on raw PDF anatomy for a specific reason:
\textbf{PDF digital signatures operate at the byte level}, not the
logical-object level.

The \texttt{/ByteRange} array in a signature dictionary specifies
exactly which bytes in the physical file are hashed and signed.
Verification reads those exact bytes, computes a cryptographic hash, and
compares it against the hash embedded in the CMS \texttt{SignedData}
structure. If a single byte differs --- because an object was
renumbered, a cross-reference entry was reformatted, whitespace was
normalized, or a stream was recompressed --- the hash will not match,
and the signature is invalid.

This has concrete consequences:

\textbf{Re-saving breaks signatures.} Loading a signed PDF into memory
and writing it back out --- even without changing any logical
content --- rewrites the file from scratch: new object ordering, new
byte offsets, new xref entries. The bytes change. The signature dies.
Signed PDFs must be saved incrementally (Chapter 8).

\textbf{Linearization affects byte layout.} Linearizing a PDF after
signing rearranges objects for progressive display. The bytes move. The
signature breaks. Always linearize \emph{before} signing, never after.

\textbf{Cross-reference format affects layout.} Whether a file uses
classic xref tables or xref streams changes the physical bytes in the
file. A signature computed against one format will not validate if the
format changes, even when the logical content is identical.

\textbf{Object streams affect findability.} If a signature dictionary is
packed inside an object stream, byte-level tools that search for
\texttt{/Type\ /Sig} in the raw file will miss it. Always use proper PDF
parsing, never raw byte scanning, when searching for signature-related
objects.

\section{PDFBox COS Layer: Mapping PDF Types to
Java}\label{_pdfbox_cos_layer_mapping_pdf_types_to_java}

PDFBox's COS (Carousel Object System) layer provides a one-to-one
mapping from PDF types to Java classes:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4444}}@{}}
\caption{PDFBox COS Type Mapping}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
PDF Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
PDFBox Class
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Notes
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
PDF Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
PDFBox Class
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Notes
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Boolean & \texttt{COSBoolean} & Singleton
\texttt{TRUE}/\texttt{FALSE} \\
Integer & \texttt{COSInteger} & Wraps \texttt{long}; common values
cached \\
Real & \texttt{COSFloat} & Wraps \texttt{float} \\
String & \texttt{COSString} & Both literal and hex;
\texttt{setForceHexForm(true)} for hex output \\
Name & \texttt{COSName} & Interned: \texttt{COSName.TYPE},
\texttt{COSName.SIG}, custom via
\texttt{COSName.getPDFName("ByteRange")} \\
Null & \texttt{COSNull} & Singleton \texttt{COSNull.NULL} \\
Array & \texttt{COSArray} & Ordered list of \texttt{COSBase} \\
Dictionary & \texttt{COSDictionary} & \texttt{getItem()} returns raw
value; \texttt{getDictionaryObject()} dereferences indirect refs \\
Stream & \texttt{COSStream} & Extends \texttt{COSDictionary};
\texttt{createInputStream()} for decoded data \\
Indirect ref & \texttt{COSObject} & Wraps \texttt{COSBase} with object
number and generation \\
\end{longtable}

The distinction between \texttt{getItem()} and
\texttt{getDictionaryObject()} is critical when working with signatures.
\texttt{getItem()} returns the raw value --- if the entry is an indirect
reference, you get the \texttt{COSObject} wrapper (which carries the
object number). \texttt{getDictionaryObject()} follows the reference and
returns the target dictionary. Use \texttt{getDictionaryObject()} when
you need the content; use \texttt{getItem()} when you need the object
number (e.g., to correlate with xref byte offsets).

Here is how to traverse the file structure from trailer to signature
dictionaries using PDFBox 3.0.0:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Loader}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cos}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{COSArray}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cos}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{COSDictionary}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cos}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cos}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{COSString}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDDocument}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{interactive}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{form}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDAcroForm}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{interactive}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{form}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDField}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{interactive}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{form}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDSignatureField}\OperatorTok{;}

\ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \CommentTok{// Inspect the trailer}
\NormalTok{    COSDictionary trailer }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDocument}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getTrailer}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    COSArray id }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ trailer}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSArray}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{ID}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"File ID present: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ id }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Trailer /Size: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ trailer}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInt}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SIZE}\OperatorTok{));}

    \CommentTok{// Navigate to signatures through the AcroForm}
\NormalTok{    PDAcroForm acroForm }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDocumentCatalog}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getAcroForm}\OperatorTok{();}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{acroForm }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"No AcroForm {-}{-} no form fields or signatures"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDField field }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ acroForm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFieldTree}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{field }\KeywordTok{instanceof}\NormalTok{ PDSignatureField sigField}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            COSDictionary sigDict }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sigField}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSObject}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{            COSDictionary sigValue }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sigDict}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSDictionary}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{V}\OperatorTok{);}

            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sigValue }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{                COSName subFilter }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sigValue}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSName}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SUB\_FILTER}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{                COSArray byteRange }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sigValue}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSArray}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"ByteRange"}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{                COSString contents }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSString}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ sigValue}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDictionaryObject}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{CONTENTS}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{                log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Signature \textquotesingle{}\{\}\textquotesingle{}: SubFilter=\{\}, ByteRange=\{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                    field}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFullyQualifiedName}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ subFilter}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{                log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"  /Contents hex length: \{\} bytes"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                    contents}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getBytes}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{length}\OperatorTok{);}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Common Structural Problems and
Debugging}\label{_common_structural_problems_and_debugging}

\subsection{Incorrect /Length Values}\label{_incorrect_length_values}

A stream's \texttt{/Length} does not match its actual byte count. This
corrupts parsing downstream and can shift xref offsets, making
byte-range verification fail. QPDF catches this:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}check}\NormalTok{ suspect.pdf}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Missing /ID Array}\label{_missing_id_array}

Some generators omit \texttt{/ID} entirely. While technically optional,
its absence can trip up validators that check it as part of integrity
verification. Use the PDFBox code above to detect missing \texttt{/ID}
programmatically.

\subsection{Multiple \%\%EOF Markers}\label{_multiple_eof_markers}

A PDF with incremental updates (Chapter 8) will have multiple
\texttt{\%\%EOF} markers. This is normal for signed documents. But some
web-based PDF processors strip everything after the first
\texttt{\%\%EOF}, destroying all incremental updates and all signatures.

\subsection{Trailing Bytes After Final
\%\%EOF}\label{_trailing_bytes_after_final_eof}

Bytes after the last \texttt{\%\%EOF} are not part of the PDF. For
signed documents, the \texttt{/ByteRange} of the outermost signature
must cover every byte up to the end of the file. Trailing bytes outside
the byte range create a validation gap that the Bochum ISA attacks
(Chapter 24) exploited.

\subsection{Cross-Reference Table
Corruption}\label{_cross_reference_table_corruption}

Byte offsets in the xref table do not match actual object positions.
This happens when a tool modifies the body without rebuilding the xref,
or when binary transfer corrupts bytes.

QPDF can repair broken xref tables, but repair involves a full rewrite,
which invalidates all signatures. Use \texttt{-\/-check} for diagnosis
without modification. For signed documents, repair is not an
option --- you need the original uncorrupted file.

\section{What Comes Next}\label{_what_comes_next}

We have now dissected the complete physical structure of a PDF file:
header, body, cross-reference table (in both classic ASCII and
compressed stream formats), object streams, trailer, and
\texttt{\%\%EOF}. We have mapped every PDF object type to its PDFBox COS
class, walked through a minimal file byte by byte, and connected the
physical structure to the requirements of digital signatures.

Chapter 8 builds directly on this foundation with \textbf{incremental
updates} --- the append-only mechanism that makes PDF signatures
possible in the first place. If this chapter was about the structure of
a single snapshot, Chapter 8 is about how those snapshots stack on top
of each other, forming the layered history that signatures depend on to
survive.

\chapter{Incremental Updates}\label{ch08}

\section{What Incremental Updates
Are}\label{_what_incremental_updates_are}

A PDF file, at its simplest, has four parts: a header
(\texttt{\%PDF-1.x} or \texttt{\%PDF-2.0}), a body of numbered objects,
a cross-reference table (or cross-reference stream) that maps object
numbers to byte offsets, and a trailer that points to the root object
and the cross-reference table. The file ends with the \texttt{\%\%EOF}
marker.

Chapter 7 covered this structure in detail. What Chapter 7 did not cover
is what happens when you \emph{modify} a PDF.

There are exactly two ways to save changes to a PDF file:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Full rewrite} --- rebuild the entire file from scratch,
  writing a new body, a new cross-reference table, and a new trailer.
  The old file is replaced entirely.
\item
  \textbf{Incremental update} (also called \textbf{incremental save} or
  \textbf{append mode}) --- leave the original file untouched and
  \emph{append} new data to the end. The new data contains only the
  objects that changed (or were added), a new cross-reference section
  mapping those objects, and a new trailer. The file now has two
  \texttt{\%\%EOF} markers: the original one (buried in the middle of
  the file) and the new one at the end.
\end{enumerate}

ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5.6 defines incremental updates:

\begin{quote}
The updated PDF file shall contain the original data of the PDF file
plus appended update data. Updates may be appended to a file multiple
times. The update data added each time a file is updated shall contain
only those objects that have been added or modified, along with a
cross-reference section and trailer for those objects.

---  ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5.6
\end{quote}

The key phrase is "the original data of the PDF file" --- the original
bytes are preserved, byte for byte, untouched. This is not a diff or a
delta. It is raw appending.

\section{Why Incremental Updates
Exist}\label{_why_incremental_updates_exist}

Incremental updates solve three distinct problems, and understanding all
three explains why the mechanism is designed the way it is.

\subsection{Problem 1: Performance on Large
Documents}\label{_problem_1_performance_on_large_documents}

A 500-page PDF with embedded fonts and high-resolution images might be
200 MB. If a user fills in a single form field --- changing perhaps 50
bytes of actual content --- a full rewrite would require reading,
processing, and writing all 200 MB. An incremental update appends a few
kilobytes.

This was especially important in the 1990s when the format was designed.
Disk I/O was slow, memory was scarce, and rewriting a large file for a
minor change was unacceptable. Even today, incremental saves are
noticeably faster for large documents.

\subsection{Problem 2: Preserving Previous
State}\label{_problem_2_preserving_previous_state}

When a PDF is incrementally updated, the previous revision is still
there --- it is the bytes before the appended data. This means you can
"undo" an incremental update by truncating the file at the previous
\texttt{\%\%EOF} boundary, recovering the exact document as it existed
before the change.

This property is not a side effect. It is a design goal. ISO
32000-2:2020 Section 7.5.6 explicitly states that conforming readers
should be able to retrieve previous revisions of a document by following
the chain of \texttt{/Prev} pointers in the trailers.

For workflows where audit trails matter --- legal documents, contracts,
government forms --- this built-in version history is valuable. You do
not need an external version control system. The PDF file \emph{is} the
version history.

\subsection{Problem 3: Digital
Signatures}\label{_problem_3_digital_signatures}

This is the reason we are here, and it deserves its own section
(\hyperref[ch08-why-signatures-need-incremental]{Why Incremental Updates
Are Critical for Signatures}). The short version: a digital signature
covers a specific byte range. If you rewrite the file, the bytes change,
and the signature becomes invalid. Incremental updates allow
modifications \emph{after} signing without disturbing the signed bytes.

Problem 3 is not optional. If you are building any tool that edits PDFs
which may contain signatures --- even if your tool has nothing to do
with signing --- you must support incremental saves. A full rewrite of a
signed PDF destroys every signature in it. There is no workaround, no
clever trick, no way to reassemble the pieces. The signed bytes are
gone.

\section{Anatomy of an Incremental
Update}\label{_anatomy_of_an_incremental_update}

Let us walk through exactly what gets appended during an incremental
update. Suppose we start with a simple PDF containing three objects: a
catalog (object 1), a page (object 2), and a page content stream (object
3).

\subsection{The Original File}\label{_the_original_file}

\begin{verbatim}
%PDF-1.7
1 0 obj                         
<< /Type /Catalog /Pages 2 0 R >>
endobj

2 0 obj
<< /Type /Page /Contents 3 0 R /MediaBox [0 0 612 792] >>
endobj

3 0 obj
<< /Length 44 >>
stream
BT /F1 12 Tf 100 700 Td (Hello) Tj ET
endstream
endobj

xref                            
0 4
0000000000 65535 f
0000000009 00000 n
0000000066 00000 n
0000000148 00000 n

trailer                         
<< /Size 4 /Root 1 0 R >>

startxref                       
252
%%EOF                           
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Three objects in the body
\item
  Cross-reference table mapping object numbers to byte offsets
\item
  Trailer with \texttt{/Size} (total object count) and \texttt{/Root}
  (catalog reference)
\item
  \texttt{startxref} gives the byte offset of the xref table
\item
  End-of-file marker
\end{itemize}

Nothing surprising here. This is the standard PDF structure from Chapter
7. Let us say this file is 350 bytes long.

\subsection{Appending an Incremental
Update}\label{_appending_an_incremental_update}

Now suppose we want to change the text on the page from "Hello" to
"Hello World". We need a new version of object 3. Instead of rewriting
the entire file, we append:

\begin{verbatim}
3 0 obj                         
<< /Length 50 >>
stream
BT /F1 12 Tf 100 700 Td (Hello World) Tj ET
endstream
endobj

xref                            
0 1
0000000000 65535 f
3 1                             
0000000351 00000 n

trailer                         
<< /Size 4 /Root 1 0 R /Prev 252 >>

startxref                       
460
%%EOF                           
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The new version of object 3, with updated content
\item
  A new cross-reference section --- but only for changed objects
\item
  The xref entry for object 3 now points to byte offset 351 (the start
  of the new object 3)
\item
  The new trailer includes a \texttt{/Prev} key pointing to the
  \emph{previous} xref table (at byte 252)
\item
  \texttt{startxref} points to the new xref section
\item
  A second \texttt{\%\%EOF} marker
\end{itemize}

The file now contains \emph{two} copies of object 3: the original at
byte offset 148 and the new one at byte offset 351. The original
cross-reference table (at byte 252) still points to the old version. The
new cross-reference section (at byte 460) points to the new version.

A conforming reader starts from the end of the file, finds the last
\texttt{startxref}, reads the new xref section, and sees that object 3
is at byte 351. For objects 1 and 2, the new xref section has no entries
--- so the reader follows the \texttt{/Prev} pointer to the previous
xref table and finds them there.

The result: the reader sees the updated document. But the old bytes are
still present, untouched, in the first 350 bytes of the file.

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{The \texttt{/Prev}
Chain}{The /Prev Chain}}\label{_the_prev_chain}

The \texttt{/Prev} key in each trailer forms a linked list of
cross-reference sections, ordered from newest to oldest. A reader
reconstructing the full object map starts with the newest xref section,
then walks the \texttt{/Prev} chain, adding entries for any object
numbers it has not yet seen. The first entry wins --- newer revisions
override older ones.

For a PDF that has been incrementally updated three times, the chain
looks like this:

\begin{verbatim}
%%EOF  (revision 3 — current)
  startxref -> xref_3
  trailer_3: /Prev -> xref_2

%%EOF  (revision 2)
  startxref -> xref_2
  trailer_2: /Prev -> xref_1

%%EOF  (revision 1 — original)
  startxref -> xref_1
  trailer_1: (no /Prev — this is the first revision)
\end{verbatim}

Each revision's trailer may also update the \texttt{/Root} reference if
the catalog object was modified. In practice, the \texttt{/Root} often
stays the same --- the catalog object number does not change, but the
catalog \emph{object} might be replaced with a new version that
references new page trees or updated AcroForm dictionaries.

\textbf{Counting \texttt{\%\%EOF} markers tells you the number of
revisions.} A freshly created PDF has one \texttt{\%\%EOF}. After one
incremental save, it has two. After \emph{n} incremental saves, it has
\emph{n} + 1\ldots\hspace{0pt} unless some tool has done a full rewrite
at some point, collapsing all revisions back to one. A signed PDF with
two signatures typically has three \texttt{\%\%EOF} markers: the
original document, the first signature, and the second signature.

\section{How Objects Are Overridden}\label{_how_objects_are_overridden}

The override mechanism is simple: same object number, new xref entry.

When a conforming reader encounters object \texttt{3\ 0} in both the new
xref section and a previous one, it uses the entry from the newest
section. The old object still exists in the file --- it occupies bytes,
it can be found by parsing --- but it is logically superseded.

This has important implications:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{No object is ever deleted.} You can add an xref entry marking
  an object as "free" (generation number incremented, next free object
  pointer updated), but the bytes of the old object remain in the file.
  Deleting content from a PDF does not remove it from the file --- it
  merely hides it behind a newer xref section.
\item
  \textbf{Object numbers are stable.} Object 3 is always object 3,
  whether you are looking at revision 1 or revision 5. The
  \emph{content} of object 3 may differ across revisions, but the number
  does not change. This is critical for signatures because the
  \texttt{/ByteRange} and cross-reference tables use byte offsets and
  object numbers as their addressing scheme.
\item
  \textbf{Generation numbers exist but are rarely used.} The spec allows
  incrementing the generation number when an object is freed and reused,
  but in practice, most tools reuse generation 0 for everything. Do not
  write code that assumes generation numbers will be non-zero --- you
  will almost never see them in the wild.
\end{itemize}

Some tools claim to "optimize" PDFs by removing unused objects and
rebuilding the xref table. This is a full rewrite. If the PDF contains
signatures, every signature is destroyed. Tools that do this include
some versions of Ghostscript's \texttt{pdfwrite} device, QPDF's
\texttt{-\/-linearize} option (when not combined with
\texttt{-\/-preserve-unreferenced-objects}), and various online "PDF
optimizer" services. If you are processing signed PDFs in an automated
pipeline, test your tools carefully.

\section{Cross-Reference Streams and Incremental
Updates}\label{_cross_reference_streams_and_incremental_updates}

PDF 1.5 introduced cross-reference streams as an alternative to the
traditional ASCII cross-reference table. Instead of a plaintext table,
the cross-reference data is stored as a compressed binary stream object.
The trailer dictionary is merged into the stream dictionary --- there is
no separate \texttt{trailer} keyword.

Incremental updates work identically with cross-reference streams. The
appended data contains a new cross-reference stream object with entries
for modified objects, and the stream dictionary includes a
\texttt{/Prev} key pointing to the previous cross-reference stream's
byte offset.

\begin{verbatim}
15 0 obj                                    
<< /Type /XRef
   /Size 16
   /W [1 3 1]                              
   /Root 1 0 R
   /Prev 8432                              
   /Filter /FlateDecode
   /Length 87 >>
stream
...compressed binary xref data...
endstream
endobj

startxref
9150                                        
%%EOF
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The cross-reference stream is itself a PDF object (object 15 in this
  case)
\item
  \texttt{/W} defines the width of each field in the binary data: 1 byte
  for type, 3 bytes for offset, 1 byte for generation
\item
  \texttt{/Prev} points to the previous cross-reference stream, just as
  in the classic trailer
\item
  \texttt{startxref} points to this stream object
\end{itemize}

The \texttt{/Prev} chain works exactly the same way. A reader walks the
chain, building the complete object map from newest to oldest, using the
first entry encountered for each object number.

A PDF file can mix classic xref tables and cross-reference streams
across revisions. The original file might use a classic table, and an
incremental update might append a cross-reference stream (or vice
versa). A conforming reader must handle both. PDFBox handles this
transparently --- \texttt{Loader.loadPDF()} follows the \texttt{/Prev}
chain regardless of whether each link is a table or a stream.

\section{Why Incremental Updates Are Critical for
Signatures}\label{ch08-why-signatures-need-incremental}

This is the most important section in this chapter. Everything else ---
the performance benefits, the revision history, the object override
mechanics --- is useful context. But for digital signatures, incremental
updates are not a convenience. They are a \emph{requirement}.

\subsection{The Fundamental
Constraint}\label{_the_fundamental_constraint}

A PDF digital signature covers a specific byte range (see Chapter 13 for
the complete deep dive). The \texttt{/ByteRange} array in the signature
dictionary specifies exactly which bytes in the file are hashed and
signed. For the signature to remain valid, those bytes must not change.
Not a single one.

If you do a full rewrite of a signed PDF --- reorganizing objects,
recomputing byte offsets, rebuilding the cross-reference table --- the
bytes at the signed offsets will be different. The hash will not match.
The signature is dead.

An incremental update avoids this problem by \emph{not touching the
original bytes}. The signed region (which covers everything from the
start of the file through the \texttt{/Contents} gap and out to the
\texttt{\%\%EOF} of the revision that was signed) remains intact. New
content is appended \emph{after} the signed region.

\subsection{Signing Is an Incremental
Update}\label{_signing_is_an_incremental_update}

When PDFBox (or any compliant library) signs a PDF, it performs an
incremental update. The signing operation appends:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  A new or updated signature field object (with the \texttt{/V}
  reference pointing to the signature dictionary)
\item
  The signature dictionary object itself (containing
  \texttt{/ByteRange}, \texttt{/Contents}, \texttt{/Filter},
  \texttt{/SubFilter}, and the rest)
\item
  An updated AcroForm dictionary (with the new field added to
  \texttt{/Fields} and \texttt{/SigFlags} set)
\item
  A new cross-reference section mapping these new/updated objects
\item
  A new trailer with \texttt{/Prev} pointing to the previous revision
\item
  A new \texttt{\%\%EOF}
\end{itemize}

The signature's byte range covers everything from byte 0 to the end of
this new \texttt{\%\%EOF} --- \emph{including} the bytes of all previous
revisions. The signature literally covers the entire file, minus the gap
where the CMS blob sits.

\subsection{Multiple Signatures}\label{_multiple_signatures}

Now consider what happens when a second person signs the same PDF. The
second signature is another incremental update, appended after the
first. The second signature's byte range starts at byte 0 and extends to
the end of \emph{its} \texttt{\%\%EOF} --- which means it covers the
first signature's bytes too.

The first signature's byte range covers bytes up to the first
signature's \texttt{\%\%EOF}. The second signature's appended data falls
\emph{after} that range. As far as the first signature is concerned,
those bytes do not exist --- they are outside the range it covers, and
that is fine.

This is how multiple signatures coexist:

\begin{verbatim}
|  Original document          |  Revision 1 (%%EOF)
|  + First signature append   |  Revision 2 (%%EOF)
|  + Second signature append  |  Revision 3 (%%EOF)

First signature's byte range:  [0 .. end of revision 2]   (minus its /Contents gap)
Second signature's byte range: [0 .. end of revision 3]   (minus its /Contents gap)
\end{verbatim}

The first signature validates against revisions 1 and 2. The second
signature validates against revisions 1, 2, and 3. Neither signature
invalidates the other.

This only works because each signature is appended incrementally. If the
second signer's tool did a full rewrite instead of an incremental save,
the first signature's bytes would be destroyed, and both signatures
would be invalid. This is why \texttt{/SigFlags} bit 2 (AppendOnly)
exists --- it tells editors "you must use incremental save, or you will
break things."

\subsection{Form Filling After
Signing}\label{_form_filling_after_signing}

The same principle applies to form filling after a certification
signature with \texttt{/DocMDP} permission level 2 (which allows form
filling and signing but no other changes). When a user fills in a form
field, the PDF viewer should save the change as an incremental update.
The filled field value is a new object appended to the file, and the
certification signature remains valid because its byte range is
untouched.

If the viewer does a full save instead, the certification signature
breaks. The viewer has just violated the signer's explicit instruction
(via \texttt{/DocMDP}) that the document should only be modified
incrementally.

\section{What Goes Wrong: Flattening and Full
Rewrites}\label{_what_goes_wrong_flattening_and_full_rewrites}

Now that we understand why incremental updates matter, let us catalog
the ways they get destroyed.

\subsection{The Flattening Problem}\label{_the_flattening_problem}

"Flattening" a PDF typically means merging all incremental updates into
a single revision --- rewriting the file with one body, one
cross-reference table, one trailer, and one \texttt{\%\%EOF}. Some tools
call this "optimizing," "linearizing," "compressing," or just "saving."

The result is a smaller, cleaner file. It is also a file in which every
digital signature is invalid.

Common tools that flatten by default or by option:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Ghostscript} (\texttt{gs\ -sDEVICE=pdfwrite}) --- always does
  a full rewrite; there is no incremental mode
\item
  \textbf{QPDF} with \texttt{-\/-linearize} --- reorganizes the file for
  progressive loading, destroying incremental history
\item
  \textbf{Some online PDF tools} --- "compress PDF" or "optimize PDF"
  services typically do a full rewrite
\item
  \textbf{LibreOffice} (depending on version and configuration) ---
  older versions did not always use incremental save, even when
  \texttt{/SigFlags} bit 2 was set
\item
  \textbf{Adobe Acrobat} "Save As" vs "Save" --- "Save" uses incremental
  update; "Save As" does a full rewrite (destroying signatures unless
  the user re-signs)
\end{itemize}

If you are building an automated PDF processing pipeline and signed PDFs
will pass through it, you must audit every tool in the pipeline to
ensure it uses incremental saves (or does not modify the file at all). A
single non-incremental save anywhere in the pipeline will invalidate
every signature. This is the number one cause of "signatures were valid
when I sent the PDF but invalid when the recipient opened it."

\subsection{The "Save" vs "Save As"
Trap}\label{_the_save_vs_save_as_trap}

Adobe Acrobat's behavior is the one most users encounter:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{File \textgreater{} Save} performs an incremental update.
  Signatures are preserved.
\item
  \textbf{File \textgreater{} Save As} writes a new file from scratch.
  Every signature in the document is destroyed.
\end{itemize}

Acrobat shows a warning dialog when you attempt Save As on a signed
document, but many users click through warnings without reading them.
Other PDF editors may not even warn.

When training users who work with signed PDFs, the single most important
instruction is: "Never use Save As. Always use Save." This one piece of
advice prevents more signature breakage than any amount of technical
configuration.

\section{Extracting a Specific
Revision}\label{_extracting_a_specific_revision}

Because incremental updates are purely additive --- new bytes appended
after the previous \texttt{\%\%EOF} --- extracting a previous revision
is trivial: truncate the file at the appropriate \texttt{\%\%EOF}
boundary.

If a PDF has three \texttt{\%\%EOF} markers (three revisions), you can
extract:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Revision 1}: all bytes from the start of the file up to and
  including the first \texttt{\%\%EOF}
\item
  \textbf{Revision 2}: all bytes from the start up to and including the
  second \texttt{\%\%EOF}
\item
  \textbf{Revision 3}: the entire file (up to and including the third
  \texttt{\%\%EOF})
\end{itemize}

This is not a diff extraction --- each "revision" is a complete,
self-contained PDF file that a reader can open and render. Revision 1 is
the original document. Revision 2 is the original plus the first set of
changes. And so on.

\subsection{Extracting with PDFBox}\label{_extracting_with_pdfbox}

PDFBox does not provide a built-in "extract revision N" method, but the
approach is straightforward: find the \emph{N}-th \texttt{\%\%EOF}
marker and copy bytes from 0 to that position.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{IOException}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{nio}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{charset}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{List}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Extracts individual revisions from a PDF with incremental updates}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Each revision is a complete}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ valid PDF file}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ RevisionExtractor }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{final} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ EOF\_MARKER }\OperatorTok{=} \StringTok{"\%\%EOF"}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getBytes}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{US\_ASCII}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{/**}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Find byte offsets of all }\CommentTok{\%\%}\NormalTok{EOF markers in the PDF}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*/}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{Integer}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{findEofOffsets}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{Integer}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ offsets }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}();}
        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ i }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{;}\NormalTok{ i }\OperatorTok{\textless{}=}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ EOF\_MARKER}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length}\OperatorTok{;}\NormalTok{ i}\OperatorTok{++)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ match }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{;}
            \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ j }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{;}\NormalTok{ j }\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{ EOF\_MARKER}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length}\OperatorTok{;}\NormalTok{ j}\OperatorTok{++)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{[}\NormalTok{i }\OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ j}\OperatorTok{]} \OperatorTok{!=}\NormalTok{ EOF\_MARKER}\OperatorTok{[}\NormalTok{j}\OperatorTok{])} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{                    match }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{;}
                    \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;}
                \OperatorTok{\}}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{match}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \CommentTok{// Include the newline after \%\%EOF if present}
                \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ end }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ i }\OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ EOF\_MARKER}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length}\OperatorTok{;}
                \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{end }\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length} \OperatorTok{\&\&}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{[}\NormalTok{end}\OperatorTok{]} \OperatorTok{==} \CharTok{\textquotesingle{}\textbackslash{}r\textquotesingle{}}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ end}\OperatorTok{++;}
                \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{end }\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length} \OperatorTok{\&\&}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{[}\NormalTok{end}\OperatorTok{]} \OperatorTok{==} \CharTok{\textquotesingle{}\textbackslash{}n\textquotesingle{}}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ end}\OperatorTok{++;}
\NormalTok{                offsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{end}\OperatorTok{);}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ offsets}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{/**}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Extract revision N }\CommentTok{(1{-}}\NormalTok{based}\CommentTok{)}\NormalTok{ as a complete PDF byte array}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*/}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{extractRevision}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{,} \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ revision}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{Integer}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ eofOffsets }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{findEofOffsets}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{revision }\OperatorTok{\textless{}} \DecValTok{1} \OperatorTok{||}\NormalTok{ revision }\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ eofOffsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \ControlFlowTok{throw} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{IllegalArgumentException}\OperatorTok{(}
                \StringTok{"Revision "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ revision }\OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" out of range. "}
                \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"File has "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ eofOffsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" revisions."}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
        \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ endOffset }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ eofOffsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{revision }\OperatorTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{);}
        \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ result }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[}\NormalTok{endOffset}\OperatorTok{];}
        \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{arraycopy}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ result}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ endOffset}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ result}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This approach works for classic incremental updates. It does \emph{not}
handle linearized PDFs correctly (see
\hyperref[ch08-linearized]{Linearized PDFs and Incremental Updates}),
because linearized files have a \texttt{\%\%EOF} at the end of the first
part that is not a revision boundary. For production code, you should
also handle the case where \texttt{\%\%EOF} appears inside a stream
object (rare but technically possible in pathological files).

\subsection{Extracting with QPDF}\label{_extracting_with_qpdf}

QPDF provides a more robust approach from the command line:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Show all cross{-}reference sections and their offsets}
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}show{-}xref}\NormalTok{ input.pdf}

\CommentTok{\# Extract a specific page range (not exactly revision extraction,}
\CommentTok{\# but useful for isolating content)}
\ExtensionTok{qpdf}\NormalTok{ input.pdf }\AttributeTok{{-}{-}pages}\NormalTok{ . 1{-}5 }\AttributeTok{{-}{-}}\NormalTok{ output.pdf}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

QPDF does not have a built-in "extract revision N" command, but
\texttt{-\/-show-xref} gives you all the information you need to
determine the \texttt{\%\%EOF} boundaries manually.

\section{How PDFBox Handles Incremental
Saves}\label{_how_pdfbox_handles_incremental_saves}

PDFBox 3.0.0 provides two distinct save methods, and choosing the wrong
one is a common source of signature breakage.

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{doc.save()} --- Full
Rewrite}{doc.save() --- Full Rewrite}}\label{_doc_save_full_rewrite}

The standard \texttt{save()} method writes the entire document from
scratch:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{inputBytes}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \CommentTok{// Make changes...}
\NormalTok{    doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{save}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{outputStream}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// Full rewrite — destroys signatures!}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This produces a clean, single-revision PDF. All incremental update
history is lost. All signatures are invalid.

Use \texttt{save()} only when:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The document has no signatures and you do not need to preserve
  revision history
\item
  You are creating a new document from scratch
\item
  You explicitly want to flatten the file (and accept the consequences)
\end{itemize}

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{doc.saveIncremental()} --- Append
Mode}{doc.saveIncremental() --- Append Mode}}\label{_doc_saveincremental_append_mode}

The \texttt{saveIncremental()} method appends only the changed objects:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{inputBytes}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \CommentTok{// Make changes...}
\NormalTok{    doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{saveIncremental}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{outputStream}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// Appends — preserves signatures}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This is what you must use when the document contains signatures. PDFBox
tracks which objects have been modified (via the COSUpdateInfo
mechanism) and writes only those objects, plus a new cross-reference
section and trailer, to the output.

\texttt{saveIncremental()} requires that the document was loaded from a
stream or byte array --- PDFBox needs the original bytes to copy them to
the output before appending. If you loaded the document from a
\texttt{File} and then try to write incrementally to a different output
stream, the original bytes are read from the file. If you try to
overwrite the same file, you will corrupt it. Always load from bytes and
write to a new output.

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{The \texttt{saveIncremental()} and Signing
Workflow}{The saveIncremental() and Signing Workflow}}\label{_the_saveincremental_and_signing_workflow}

When signing a PDF, you do not call \texttt{saveIncremental()} directly.
Instead, you use the signing API:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{inputBytes}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    PDSignature signature }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFilter}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FILTER\_ADOBE\_PPKLITE}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSubFilter}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SUBFILTER\_ADBE\_PKCS7\_DETACHED}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSignDate}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Calendar}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{());}

\NormalTok{    SignatureOptions options }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{SignatureOptions}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    options}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setPreferredSignatureSize}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{16384}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// Reserve 16KB for CMS}

\NormalTok{    doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addSignature}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signingInterface}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ options}\OperatorTok{);}

    \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\NormalTok{ baos }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{saveIncremental}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{baos}\OperatorTok{);}
