    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ signedPdf }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ baos}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toByteArray}\OperatorTok{();}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{saveIncremental()} here does three things: writes the original
  bytes, appends the new objects (including the signature dictionary
  with a placeholder \texttt{/Contents}), computes the hash over the
  byte ranges, calls your \texttt{SignatureInterface.sign()} method to
  get the CMS bytes, and writes them into the placeholder.
\end{itemize}

The signing operation is \emph{always} an incremental save. PDFBox
enforces this --- there is no \texttt{doc.save()} variant that supports
signing, because signing with a full rewrite is logically impossible
(the signed byte range would cover a file that no longer exists).

\section{\texorpdfstring{Multiple \texttt{\%\%EOF} Markers: What They
Mean}{Multiple \%\%EOF Markers: What They Mean}}\label{_multiple_eof_markers_what_they_mean}

Every \texttt{\%\%EOF} marker in a PDF represents the end of a revision.
Counting them tells you the document's history:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{1 \texttt{\%\%EOF}}: A single-revision PDF. Never been
  incrementally updated. Either freshly created, or previously
  incrementally updated but later flattened by a full rewrite.
\item
  \textbf{2 \texttt{\%\%EOF}}: One incremental update after the
  original. Common for a single signature, a single form fill, or a
  single annotation addition.
\item
  \textbf{3 \texttt{\%\%EOF}}: Two incremental updates. Common for a
  document with two signatures, or one signature plus a subsequent form
  fill.
\item
  \textbf{N \texttt{\%\%EOF}}: N-1 incremental updates after the
  original. Documents that have been through complex workflows (multiple
  signers, multiple form fill rounds, annotation rounds) can have many
  revisions.
\end{itemize}

A quick way to count revisions in a PDF is:
\texttt{grep\ -c\ \textquotesingle{}\%\%EOF\textquotesingle{}\ document.pdf}.
This works for most files, but be aware that \texttt{\%\%EOF} could
theoretically appear inside a stream object (unlikely in practice). For
reliable counting, parse the \texttt{/Prev} chain instead.

\subsection{What Revision Counts Tell You About a Signed
PDF}\label{_what_revision_counts_tell_you_about_a_signed_pdf}

For signature validation, the revision count carries diagnostic
information:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  A signed PDF with exactly 2 \texttt{\%\%EOF} markers is the simplest
  case: original document + one signature. The signature covers
  everything in revision 1 and 2.
\item
  A signed PDF with 3 \texttt{\%\%EOF} markers might mean: original +
  signature + post-signature modification (form fill, annotation, second
  signature). The first signature covers revisions 1-2. Whatever is in
  revision 3 was added after signing --- whether it is permissible
  depends on the \texttt{/TransformMethod} and \texttt{/DocMDP}
  settings.
\item
  A signed PDF with only 1 \texttt{\%\%EOF} is suspicious. Either the
  signature was created during the initial save (some tools do this), or
  the file was flattened after signing (which means the signature is
  almost certainly invalid, even if the bytes were carefully
  reconstructed --- see the discussion in Chapter 24 about the
  Incremental Saving Attack).
\end{itemize}

\section{Tracing the Revision
History}\label{_tracing_the_revision_history}

To reconstruct the complete revision history of a PDF, you walk the
\texttt{/Prev} chain from the newest trailer back to the oldest:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Loader}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cos}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{COSDictionary}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cos}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDDocument}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{List}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Traces the }\CommentTok{/}\NormalTok{Prev chain to enumerate all cross}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{reference sections}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ RevisionTracer }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{Long}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{traceXrefOffsets}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{Long}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ offsets }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}();}
        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            COSDictionary trailer }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDocument}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getTrailer}\OperatorTok{();}
            \CommentTok{// The last startxref value}
            \DataTypeTok{long}\NormalTok{ currentOffset }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDocument}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getStartXref}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{            offsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{currentOffset}\OperatorTok{);}

            \CommentTok{// Walk the /Prev chain}
            \ControlFlowTok{while} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{trailer}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{containsKey}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{PREV}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \DataTypeTok{long}\NormalTok{ prev }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ trailer}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLong}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{PREV}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{                offsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{prev}\OperatorTok{);}
                \CommentTok{// Note: to get the previous trailer, we would need to}
                \CommentTok{// parse the xref section at the /Prev offset.}
                \CommentTok{// PDFBox\textquotesingle{}s internal parser does this, but the API}
                \CommentTok{// does not expose individual revision trailers directly.}
                \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;} \CommentTok{// Simplified — full implementation needs xref parsing}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ offsets}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

PDFBox's public API does not expose a convenient "iterate over all
revisions" method. The internal \texttt{COSParser} follows the
\texttt{/Prev} chain during loading, but the individual revision
trailers are not directly accessible after parsing. For production
revision analysis, consider using QPDF's \texttt{-\/-show-xref} output
(see \hyperref[ch08-debugging]{Debugging Incremental Updates}) or
writing a custom parser that reads the raw bytes and follows the
\texttt{/Prev} pointers manually.

\section{Linearized PDFs and Incremental Updates}\label{ch08-linearized}

Linearized PDFs (sometimes called "optimized for fast web view") have a
special structure designed for progressive loading over HTTP. A
linearized PDF reorganizes its objects so that the first page can be
displayed before the entire file has been downloaded.

The linearization adds a \textbf{linearization dictionary} (object 1,
typically) and restructures the file into two parts separated by an
internal \texttt{\%\%EOF} --- but this internal \texttt{\%\%EOF} is
\emph{not} a revision boundary. It is part of the linearization
structure.

This creates a compatibility problem with incremental updates:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  A linearized PDF already has an unusual structure with cross-reference
  data at both the beginning and end of the file.
\item
  An incremental update appends data after the final \texttt{\%\%EOF},
  which is fine for non-linearized PDFs but technically invalidates the
  linearization (because the linearization dictionary's \texttt{/L} key
  records the total file length, which is now wrong).
\item
  Some viewers re-linearize on save, which is a full rewrite, which
  destroys signatures.
\end{itemize}

ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5.6 states:

\begin{quote}
If an incremental update is made to a linearized PDF file, the result is
a valid non-linearized PDF file.
\end{quote}

In other words, an incremental update on a linearized PDF destroys the
linearization but produces a valid PDF. This is acceptable --- the file
simply loses its "fast web view" optimization.

If your workflow requires both linearization and digital signatures, you
must linearize \emph{before} signing, not after. Linearizing after
signing is a full rewrite that destroys signatures. Signing after
linearizing produces a valid signed PDF that is no longer linearized.
There is no way to have a linearized, signed PDF that remains linearized
after signing --- the incremental update required by signing inherently
breaks the linearization structure.

\section{The Incremental Saving Attack}\label{ch08-isa}

The incremental update mechanism that makes PDF signatures possible is
also the mechanism that attackers have exploited to \emph{bypass} those
signatures.

The \textbf{Incremental Saving Attack (ISA)}, described by the Ruhr
University Bochum team in their CCS 2019 paper, works as follows:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  The attacker starts with a legitimately signed PDF
\item
  The attacker appends a new incremental update to the file --- adding,
  modifying, or replacing objects (pages, text, images, form field
  values)
\item
  The attacker does \emph{not} touch the signed bytes; the incremental
  update is appended after the signed region
\item
  The attacker presents the modified file to a PDF viewer
\item
  The viewer reads the file from the end, uses the newest
  cross-reference section, and renders the attacker's content
\item
  The viewer validates the signature by checking the original byte range
  --- which is untouched --- and reports "Signature is valid"
\end{enumerate}

The signature \emph{is} valid --- over the bytes it covers. But the
viewer is displaying content from \emph{outside} the signed byte range.
The user sees a green checkmark and attacker-controlled content.

The defense against ISA is conceptually simple but difficult to
implement correctly: a viewer must detect that content has been appended
after the signed revision and warn the user. Specifically, the viewer
must check whether the byte range of the last signature covers the
entire file. If the file extends beyond the end of the signed byte
range, there are unsigned changes.

Whether those changes are permissible (e.g., a subsequent signature, an
allowed form fill under DocMDP level 2) or malicious requires deeper
analysis of what the appended objects actually do --- which is where the
complexity lies.

Chapter 24 covers the ISA and its variants in complete technical detail,
including the specific techniques attackers used to bypass even the
"unsigned content detected" warnings that some viewers implemented as an
initial defense.

\section{Debugging Incremental Updates}\label{ch08-debugging}

When something goes wrong with a signed PDF --- the signature shows as
invalid, a form field value disappeared, a viewer crashes --- the first
thing to check is the incremental update structure.

\subsection{QPDF: Your Best Friend}\label{_qpdf_your_best_friend}

QPDF is an indispensable tool for PDF debugging. For incremental update
analysis, the key commands are:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Show all cross{-}reference entries with their types and offsets}
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}show{-}xref}\NormalTok{ signed{-}document.pdf}

\CommentTok{\# Show the structure of the file (objects, streams, references)}
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}show{-}object}\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{trailer signed{-}document.pdf}

\CommentTok{\# Check the file for structural errors}
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}check}\NormalTok{ signed{-}document.pdf}

\CommentTok{\# Show a specific object}
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}show{-}object}\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{42,0 signed{-}document.pdf}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The \texttt{-\/-show-xref} output is particularly valuable. It shows
every object number, its byte offset, and which cross-reference section
it belongs to. From this output, you can determine:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  How many cross-reference sections exist (i.e., how many revisions)
\item
  Which objects were added or modified in each revision
\item
  Whether the xref entries are consistent (offsets point to actual
  objects)
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Comparing Revisions}\label{_comparing_revisions}

To understand what changed between revisions, extract each revision (by
truncating at \texttt{\%\%EOF} boundaries as described earlier) and
compare them:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Extract revision 1 (original document)}
\CommentTok{\# Suppose the first \%\%EOF is at byte 45230}
\FunctionTok{head} \AttributeTok{{-}c}\NormalTok{ 45230 signed{-}document.pdf }\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ revision1.pdf}

\CommentTok{\# Extract revision 2 (after first signature)}
\CommentTok{\# Suppose the second \%\%EOF is at byte 67890}
\FunctionTok{head} \AttributeTok{{-}c}\NormalTok{ 67890 signed{-}document.pdf }\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ revision2.pdf}

\CommentTok{\# Compare the objects in each revision}
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}show{-}xref}\NormalTok{ revision1.pdf }\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ xref1.txt}
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}show{-}xref}\NormalTok{ revision2.pdf }\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ xref2.txt}
\FunctionTok{diff}\NormalTok{ xref1.txt xref2.txt}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The diff will show you exactly which objects were added or modified in
the incremental update. For a signature, you should see the signature
dictionary object, the updated AcroForm, and possibly an updated catalog
or page annotation list.

\subsection{Hex-Level Inspection}\label{_hex_level_inspection}

For the most stubborn problems, there is no substitute for looking at
the raw bytes. Open the PDF in a hex editor and search for
\texttt{\%\%EOF}. Each occurrence marks a revision boundary. The bytes
between two consecutive \texttt{\%\%EOF} markers (plus the preceding
\texttt{startxref} and trailer) constitute one incremental update.

\textbf{A debugging checklist for incremental update problems:}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Count \texttt{\%\%EOF} markers --- does the number match the expected
  number of revisions?
\item
  Check each trailer's \texttt{/Prev} value --- does the chain form a
  consistent linked list?
\item
  Verify that \texttt{startxref} in each revision points to a valid xref
  section
\item
  For signed PDFs: does the byte range of each signature end at a
  \texttt{\%\%EOF} boundary?
\item
  Are there any bytes after the last \texttt{\%\%EOF}? (There should not
  be --- trailing garbage can confuse some parsers.)
\item
  Open each extracted revision independently --- does it render
  correctly?
\item
  Compare the object sets across revisions --- do the changes make sense
  for the expected workflow?
\end{enumerate}

\section{Real-World Scenarios}\label{_real_world_scenarios}

The theory of incremental updates is clean. The practice is messy. Here
are the scenarios you will encounter most often.

\subsection{Scenario 1: Single
Signature}\label{_scenario_1_single_signature}

The simplest case. A document is created, then signed. The file has two
revisions (two \texttt{\%\%EOF} markers). The signature's byte range
covers the entire file. No complications.

\subsection{Scenario 2: Two Approval
Signatures}\label{_scenario_2_two_approval_signatures}

Alice signs first, then Bob. The file has three revisions: original,
Alice's signature, Bob's signature. Alice's byte range covers revisions
1-2. Bob's byte range covers revisions 1-3. When validating Alice's
signature, a viewer should check the byte range of Alice's signature and
warn if changes between revision 2 and 3 go beyond what Alice's
\texttt{/TransformMethod} permits (typically, adding more signatures is
allowed).

\subsection{Scenario 3: Certification +
Approval}\label{_scenario_3_certification_approval}

The document author applies a certification signature with
\texttt{/DocMDP} permission level 2 (form fill and sign allowed). Later,
a user fills form fields and applies an approval signature. The file has
three or four revisions (original, certification, form fill, approval
--- or the form fill and approval might be in the same incremental
update). The certification signature permits the form fill and the
additional signature. A viewer must verify that the changes in the later
revisions are within the scope of the DocMDP permissions.

\subsection{Scenario 4: Annotations After
Signing}\label{_scenario_4_annotations_after_signing}

A user adds a sticky note annotation to a signed PDF. If the editor
saves incrementally, a new revision is appended with the annotation
object and an updated page annotations array. The original signature's
byte range is untouched. Whether the annotation is \emph{allowed}
depends on the signature's \texttt{/TransformMethod}:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  DocMDP level 1 (no changes allowed): the annotation violates the
  permissions. A conforming viewer should warn that the document has
  been modified in a way that exceeds the signer's permissions.
\item
  DocMDP level 2 (form fill and sign only): annotations are not form
  filling, so this is a violation.
\item
  DocMDP level 3 (annotations, form filling, and signing allowed): the
  annotation is permitted.
\end{itemize}

The incremental update itself is structurally fine in all three cases.
The question is whether the \emph{content} of the update is semantically
permissible. This distinction --- structural validity vs. semantic
permissibility --- is at the heart of the Incremental Saving Attack
(Chapter 24).

\subsection{Scenario 5: The Flattened
Disaster}\label{_scenario_5_the_flattened_disaster}

A signed PDF passes through an automated document management system that
"optimizes" PDFs for storage. The optimization tool does a full rewrite.
The output PDF has one \texttt{\%\%EOF}, no \texttt{/Prev} chain, and
the signature dictionary's byte range references offsets that no longer
correspond to the correct content. The signature is permanently invalid.
The signed bytes are gone.

There is no recovery. The original file must be retrieved from backup
(if it exists) or the document must be re-signed.

This scenario is \emph{extremely} common in enterprise environments.
Document management systems, email gateways, PDF/A conversion tools, and
print spoolers all potentially rewrite PDFs. If your organization
processes signed PDFs, audit your entire document pipeline for
non-incremental saves. Every tool that touches a signed PDF must either
leave it completely unmodified or use incremental saves exclusively.

\subsection{Scenario 6: The Double-Linearization
Trap}\label{_scenario_6_the_double_linearization_trap}

A document is created with linearization enabled (for fast web view),
signed, and then processed by a tool that re-linearizes it. The
re-linearization is a full rewrite. The signature is destroyed.

The fix: disable linearization in any tool that processes signed PDFs,
or ensure that no post-signing processing step involves linearization.

\section{Summary}\label{_summary_7}

Incremental updates are the mechanism that makes PDF digital signatures
possible. Without them, signing a PDF would require that the document
never be modified again --- no additional signatures, no form filling,
no annotations, nothing. With them, a signed PDF can evolve through a
controlled sequence of revisions, each one adding new content without
disturbing the bytes that previous signatures cover.

The rules are simple:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  A signed PDF must be saved incrementally. Always.
\item
  The byte range of each signature covers a specific set of revisions.
\item
  New content is appended after the signed byte range.
\item
  The \texttt{/Prev} chain in the trailers links all revisions together.
\item
  Extracting a specific revision means truncating at the appropriate
  \texttt{\%\%EOF} boundary.
\item
  A full rewrite destroys all incremental history and all signatures.
\item
  Linearization and incremental updates are incompatible after the first
  incremental save.
\end{itemize}

The next chapter (Chapter 9) examines AcroForm fields --- the form
infrastructure that signature fields are built on. Chapter 12 will bring
incremental updates and signature architecture together, and Chapter 13
will dissect the byte range in complete detail. If the byte range is
where signatures live or die, incremental updates are the ground they
stand on.

\chapter{AcroForm \& Interactive Forms}\label{ch09}

\section{The AcroForm Dictionary}\label{_the_acroform_dictionary}

Every PDF that contains interactive form fields has an
\texttt{/AcroForm} entry in the document catalog. This is the root of
all form infrastructure in the document: the field list, the signature
flags, the default appearance string, the default resources. If
\texttt{/AcroForm} is missing from the catalog, the document has no
interactive forms --- period. Some tools create signature fields and
forget to create the AcroForm entry, which leads to signatures that
exist as orphaned objects in the file but are invisible to every
conforming viewer.

The AcroForm dictionary lives at:

\begin{verbatim}
trailer -> /Root (Catalog) -> /AcroForm
\end{verbatim}

Here is a representative AcroForm dictionary with all keys that matter
for signature work:

\begin{verbatim}
<<
  /Fields [10 0 R 11 0 R 12 0 R]    % 
  /SigFlags 3                         % 
  /NeedAppearances false               % 
  /DA (/Helv 0 Tf 0 g)                % 
  /DR <<                               % 
    /Font << /Helv 8 0 R /ZaDb 9 0 R >>
  >>
>>
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{/Fields} --- array of references to the top-level field
  dictionaries. Every field in the document must be reachable from this
  array (directly or through parent-child relationships).
\item
  \texttt{/SigFlags} --- a bit field that controls signature-related
  behavior. Value 3 means both bit 1 (SignaturesExist) and bit 2
  (AppendOnly) are set.
\item
  \texttt{/NeedAppearances} --- tells the viewer whether to regenerate
  appearance streams before rendering.
\item
  \texttt{/DA} --- default appearance string, used for fields that do
  not have their own \texttt{/DA}.
\item
  \texttt{/DR} --- default resources dictionary, providing fonts and
  other resources for appearance generation.
\end{itemize}

Let's examine each of these in the depth they deserve.

\subsection{/Fields --- The Root of the Field
Tree}\label{_fieldsthe_root_of_the_field_tree}

The \texttt{/Fields} array contains indirect references to the
document's top-level field dictionaries. "Top-level" means fields that
have no parent --- they are the roots of potentially nested field trees.
A flat form with ten independent fields will have ten entries in
\texttt{/Fields}. A form with a hierarchical namespace (like
\texttt{address.city}, \texttt{address.zip}) will have a single
\texttt{address} parent in \texttt{/Fields}, with \texttt{city} and
\texttt{zip} as children.

A field that is not reachable from \texttt{/Fields} does not exist as
far as a conforming reader is concerned. We have seen PDFs where a
signing tool created a signature field object, set its \texttt{/V} to
point at a signature dictionary, added a widget annotation to a
page --- and forgot to add the field to \texttt{/Fields}. The signature
data is physically present in the file. The CMS bytes are valid. But no
viewer will find it, because the AcroForm field tree does not reference
it.

If you are debugging a "missing signature" problem, check
\texttt{/Fields} first.

\subsection{/SigFlags --- The Two Bits That Protect
Everything}\label{_sigflagsthe_two_bits_that_protect_everything}

We cover \texttt{/SigFlags} in depth here because getting it wrong
destroys signatures --- silently, irreversibly, and usually long after
the signing ceremony is over.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.6250}}@{}}
\caption{SigFlags bit definitions --- ISO 32000-2:2020 S12.7.2, Table
225}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Bit
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What it does
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Bit
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What it does
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{SignaturesExist} & 1 & Declares that the document contains at
least one signature field with a value. Conforming readers use this as a
signal to check for signatures and display validation status. Without
it, some viewers will not even look for signatures. \\
\texttt{AppendOnly} & 2 & Mandates that any modifications to the
document \textbf{shall} use incremental updates only. This is the single
most important protection for existing signatures. If a viewer or editor
does a full save (rewriting the entire file from scratch), every
signature in the document becomes invalid --- the bytes have changed,
the byte ranges no longer match, and the cryptographic verification will
fail. \\
\end{longtable}

The only correct value for \texttt{/SigFlags} in a signed document is
\textbf{3} (both bits set). A value of 1 (SignaturesExist without
AppendOnly) is technically allowed by the spec but is a bug in
practice --- it tells viewers that signatures exist but does not protect
them from being destroyed by a full save.

Many PDF generation tools --- including some versions of LibreOffice,
older versions of iText, and various government form generators --- set
\texttt{/SigFlags} to 1 or omit it entirely. If you receive a signed PDF
with \texttt{/SigFlags} missing or set to 1, the signatures are at risk.
Any editor that performs a full save will destroy them.

AppendOnly matters because incremental updates are what make
multi-signature workflows possible --- each signer appends their changes
without moving the bytes that previous signers\textquotesingle{} byte
ranges reference. Without AppendOnly, an editor may do a full rewrite,
destroying every signature.

We have also seen the converse: \texttt{/SigFlags} is 0 but signed
fields exist with valid CMS data. Acrobat shows "No Signatures" because
bit 1 was never set, so it never looked.

The AppendOnly flag is advisory, not enforced by the file format. A
malicious or poorly written tool can ignore it. The flag protects
against \emph{accidental} destruction by well-behaved editors.
Intentional tampering is what the cryptographic signature itself guards
against.

\subsection{/NeedAppearances --- The Flag That Breaks
Signatures}\label{_needappearancesthe_flag_that_breaks_signatures}

The \texttt{/NeedAppearances} flag (boolean, default \texttt{false})
tells the viewer to regenerate appearance streams before rendering. For
ordinary fields this is a convenience --- skip generating appearances
and let the viewer handle it. For signature fields, it is a disaster.

When a viewer regenerates appearances, it modifies the document bytes.
If the document is signed, the signature becomes invalid. It does not
matter that the viewer was "just" fixing up visuals --- the bytes
changed.

If \texttt{/NeedAppearances} is \texttt{true} in a signed document,
either: (1) the viewer regenerates appearances and the signature fails,
or (2) the viewer skips regeneration and some fields show blank
rectangles. \textbf{Never set \texttt{/NeedAppearances} to \texttt{true}
in a document that will be signed.} Always generate complete appearance
streams for every field before signing.

\subsection{/DA --- Default Appearance
String}\label{_dadefault_appearance_string}

The \texttt{/DA} (Default Appearance) string is a PDF content stream
fragment that specifies the default font, size, and color for variable
text in form fields. It typically looks like:

\begin{verbatim}
/Helv 0 Tf 0 g
\end{verbatim}

This means: use the font aliased as \texttt{/Helv}, at size 0
(auto-size), in black (\texttt{0\ g}). The font name \texttt{/Helv} must
be defined in the \texttt{/DR} (Default Resources) dictionary.

Why does this matter for signatures? Because if a signing tool creates a
visible signature appearance that contains text --- the signer's name,
the date, the reason --- and the font referenced in the appearance
stream is not available in \texttt{/DR} \emph{and} not embedded in the
appearance's own \texttt{/Resources}, some viewers will fall back to
\texttt{/DA} and render the text in Helvetica or not at all.

Always embed the font resources directly in the signature field's
appearance stream \texttt{/Resources} dictionary. Do not rely on
\texttt{/DR}. The appearance stream is a self-contained Form XObject and
should carry everything it needs to render correctly, regardless of what
the parent AcroForm provides.

\subsection{/DR --- Default Resources}\label{_drdefault_resources}

The \texttt{/DR} dictionary provides fonts, color spaces, and other
resources available to all form fields. The two fonts you will encounter
in virtually every AcroForm document are \texttt{/Helv} (Helvetica, for
text) and \texttt{/ZaDb} (ZapfDingbats, for checkbox ticks and radio
button bullets).

The \texttt{/DR} fonts are \emph{not} the same as page-level fonts. A
page's \texttt{/Resources} dictionary is independent. If a signature
appearance stream references a font from the page's resources but not
from \texttt{/DR} or the appearance's own resources, rendering may fail
in viewers that strictly follow the spec.

\section{Field Tree Hierarchy}\label{_field_tree_hierarchy}

\subsection{Parent-Child Relationships and Fully Qualified
Names}\label{_parent_child_relationships_and_fully_qualified_names}

The AcroForm field tree is not flat. Fields can be nested using
\texttt{/Parent} and \texttt{/Kids} keys. A parent field
\texttt{\textless{}\textless{}\ /T\ (address)\ /Kids\ {[}20\ 0\ R\ 21\ 0\ R{]}\ \textgreater{}\textgreater{}}
with a child field
\texttt{\textless{}\textless{}\ /T\ (city)\ /Parent\ 19\ 0\ R\ /FT\ /Tx\ \textgreater{}\textgreater{}}
produces the fully qualified name \texttt{address.city}.

The fully qualified name is constructed by concatenating \texttt{/T}
(partial name) values from root to leaf, separated by periods. This is
the name you use in PDFBox's \texttt{acroForm.getField("address.city")},
in JavaScript, and in FDF data files.

\textbf{Debugging field-not-found errors:} If
\texttt{acroForm.getField("Signature1")} returns null, check whether the
field is nested. It might be \texttt{form.Signature1} or
\texttt{page1.Signature1}. Use \texttt{acroForm.getFieldTree()} to
iterate every field in the hierarchy and inspect the fully qualified
names.

\subsection{Terminal vs. Non-Terminal
Fields}\label{_terminal_vs_non_terminal_fields}

A \textbf{terminal field} is a field with no \texttt{/Kids}, or whose
\texttt{/Kids} are all widget annotations (not other fields). Terminal
fields have a \texttt{/FT} (field type) key --- \texttt{/Tx},
\texttt{/Btn}, \texttt{/Ch}, or \texttt{/Sig}.

A \textbf{non-terminal field} is a pure grouping node. It has
\texttt{/Kids} that are other fields, and it does \emph{not} have
\texttt{/FT}. Its only purpose is to provide a namespace prefix.

Some PDF generators create non-terminal fields that also carry
\texttt{/FT}. This is technically a spec violation (ISO 32000-2:2020
S12.7.4.1), but it happens. PDFBox handles it gracefully in most cases,
but other libraries may choke. If you are building a field tree, keep
non-terminal nodes clean --- no \texttt{/FT}, no \texttt{/V}, no
\texttt{/Rect}.

\section{Field Types}\label{_field_types}

ISO 32000-2:2020 S12.7.4 defines four field types. All of them can
appear in the same AcroForm, side by side, and a single document
frequently contains all four --- especially government and legal forms
that combine text input, checkboxes, dropdown selections, and signature
fields.

\subsection{/Tx --- Text Fields}\label{_txtext_fields}

Text fields hold user-entered text (value in \texttt{/V} as a text
string). Key properties: \texttt{/MaxLen} (maximum character count),
\texttt{/Q} (text alignment: 0=left, 1=center, 2=right). Text fields
interact with signatures in one important way: if a text field is filled
\emph{after} a document is signed, the fill operation is an incremental
update. Whether this invalidates the signature depends on the
\texttt{/Lock} and \texttt{/FieldMDP} settings (covered in Chapter 14).

\subsection{/Btn --- Button Fields}\label{_btnbutton_fields}

Button fields come in three flavors, distinguished by the \texttt{/Ff}
(field flags) bits: \textbf{pushbuttons} (bit 17 set, no persistent
value), \textbf{checkboxes} (bit 17 clear, bit 16 clear, two-state
toggles with values like \texttt{/Yes} or \texttt{/Off}), and
\textbf{radio buttons} (bit 17 clear, bit 16 set, mutually exclusive
options). For signatures, button fields matter primarily in the context
of form flattening and field locking.

\subsection{/Ch --- Choice Fields}\label{_chchoice_fields}

Choice fields present a list of options: \textbf{combo boxes} (bit 18
set, a dropdown) and \textbf{list boxes} (bit 18 clear, a scrollable
list). Options are defined in the \texttt{/Opt} array, where each entry
can be a simple text string or a two-element array
\texttt{{[}export\_value,\ display\_text{]}}.

\subsection{/Sig --- Signature Fields}\label{_sigsignature_fields}

Signature fields are where form infrastructure meets cryptographic
infrastructure. A \texttt{/Sig} field is, structurally, just another
form field in the AcroForm tree. But its \texttt{/V} (value) is not a
text string or a name --- it is a \textbf{signature dictionary}
(\texttt{/Type\ /Sig}), which is a complex structure containing the byte
range, the CMS data, the signing filter, timestamps, and more.

A signature field before signing:

\begin{verbatim}
<<
  /FT /Sig
  /T (Signature1)
  /V null                    % or /V absent entirely
  /Rect [100 600 300 650]
  /Subtype /Widget
  /P 5 0 R
>>
\end{verbatim}

The same field after signing:

\begin{verbatim}
<<
  /FT /Sig
  /T (Signature1)
  /V 42 0 R                  % reference to the /Sig dictionary
  /Rect [100 600 300 650]
  /Subtype /Widget
  /P 5 0 R
  /AP << /N 43 0 R >>        % appearance stream added by the signing tool
>>
\end{verbatim}

Object 42 is the signature dictionary (covered in depth in Chapter 12).
Object 43 is the visual appearance of the signature.

The signature field is \emph{not} the signature. The field is the
\textbf{container}. The signature dictionary (the \texttt{/V} value) is
the \textbf{content}. A document can have signature fields with no
value --- these are placeholder fields, pre-positioned by a template
author, waiting for a signer to fill them in.

This distinction matters because:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  A document with an empty signature field still has an entry in
  \texttt{/Fields}.
\item
  The \texttt{/SigFlags} should \emph{not} have bit 1 set until at least
  one field actually has a value.
\item
  Validation logic should check \texttt{/V}, not just the presence of
  \texttt{/FT\ /Sig} fields.
\end{itemize}

What makes \texttt{/Sig} fields unique: (1) the value is a complex
dictionary, not a simple string; (2) signing computes a hash over the
entire file, not just the field; (3) the \texttt{/SV} (Seed Value) and
\texttt{/Lock} dictionaries allow the field to constrain which
algorithms, certificates, and post-signing modifications are acceptable;
and (4) the widget's \texttt{/Rect} determines visibility --- a
zero-size rect means an invisible signature.

\section{Widget Annotations}\label{_widget_annotations}

\subsection{The Connection Between Fields and
Pages}\label{_the_connection_between_fields_and_pages}

A form field is an abstract data object (name, type, value). A widget
annotation is its visual representation on a specific page (coordinates,
size, appearance).

In the most common case, the field and widget are \textbf{merged into a
single PDF object} --- the same dictionary carries both \texttt{/FT}
(field type) and \texttt{/Subtype\ /Widget}, both \texttt{/T} (field
name) and \texttt{/Rect} (position). The spec (ISO 32000-2:2020
S12.5.6.19) also allows separating them: a field's \texttt{/Kids} array
can contain widget annotations, each with \texttt{/Parent} pointing back
to the field. This allows a single field to have widgets on multiple
pages --- unusual, but legal.

When debugging, check whether the field and widget are merged or
separate. \texttt{PDSignatureField.getWidgets()} returns the list of
widgets. An empty list means no visual representation --- fine for
invisible signatures, a bug if you expected a visible one.

\subsection{Page Association}\label{_page_association}

A widget must be associated with a page via two links: the widget's
\texttt{/P} key referencing the page dictionary, and the page's
\texttt{/Annots} array containing a reference to the widget. Both should
be present. If the widget is missing from \texttt{/Annots}, it will not
render --- even if \texttt{/P} points to the correct page.

Some signing tools add the widget to the field tree but forget to add it
to the page's \texttt{/Annots} array. The signature is valid
cryptographically but invisible on every page. This is one of the most
common "invisible signature" bugs that is not actually an invisible
signature --- it is a missing annotation reference.

\section{Appearance Streams}\label{_appearance_streams}

\subsection{Normal, Rollover, and Down}\label{_normal_rollover_and_down}

The \texttt{/AP} dictionary can define three appearance streams:
\texttt{/N} (Normal --- the only one that matters for signatures),
\texttt{/R} (Rollover --- mouse hover, rarely used), and \texttt{/D}
(Down --- click state, irrelevant for signatures).

Each can be a single Form XObject stream or a \textbf{subdictionary}
mapping state names to streams. Checkboxes use a subdictionary
(\texttt{\textless{}\textless{}\ /Yes\ 60\ 0\ R\ /Off\ 61\ 0\ R\ \textgreater{}\textgreater{}}).
Signature fields always use a single stream --- signatures do not have
states.

\subsection{Why Appearance Streams Matter for
Signatures}\label{_why_appearance_streams_matter_for_signatures}

If the appearance stream is missing, the viewer either renders a blank
rectangle, tries to generate one on the fly (dangerous if the document
is signed --- see \texttt{/NeedAppearances} above), or shows a generic
placeholder. For signed fields, the only correct approach is to have the
signing tool generate a complete appearance stream before signing.

The appearance stream is included in the signed byte range. Modifying it
after signing invalidates the signature. However, the appearance is
\emph{not} semantically bound to the CMS content --- the appearance
could say "Signed by Alice" while the certificate belongs to Bob. The
viewer must verify both independently.

\subsection{Generating Appearances with
PDFBox}\label{_generating_appearances_with_pdfbox}

PDFBox provides the \texttt{PDAcroForm.refreshAppearances()} method (and
its per-field equivalent) to generate appearance streams for all fields.
This is the equivalent of setting \texttt{/NeedAppearances} to
\texttt{true} and having the viewer regenerate --- except you do it
programmatically, before signing, so the appearances are part of the
signed bytes.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    PDAcroForm acroForm }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDocumentCatalog}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getAcroForm}\OperatorTok{();}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{acroForm }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        acroForm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setNeedAppearances}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// clear the flag}
\NormalTok{        acroForm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{refreshAppearances}\OperatorTok{();}        \CommentTok{// generate all appearances}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{refreshAppearances()} will overwrite existing appearance
streams. If you have custom signature appearances (hand-drawn images,
company logos), call this \emph{before} setting up your custom
appearances, or call it only on non-signature fields. For signature
appearances, most signing workflows build the appearance stream manually
using a \texttt{PDFormXObject} --- Chapter 15 covers this in detail.

\section{XFA Forms: The Deprecated
Complication}\label{_xfa_forms_the_deprecated_complication}

\subsection{What XFA Was and Why It Was
Deprecated}\label{_what_xfa_was_and_why_it_was_deprecated}

XFA (XML Forms Architecture) was Adobe's attempt to replace the limited
AcroForm system with a powerful XML-based form description language. An
XFA form embeds XML data inside the PDF file (in the \texttt{/XFA} key
of the AcroForm dictionary), and a viewer that supports XFA renders the
form from XML rather than from the AcroForm field tree. XFA could do
things AcroForm could not: dynamic layouts, complex validation rules,
repeating sections, and data binding to external schemas.

ISO 32000-2:2020 deprecated XFA for three reasons: (1) only Adobe
implemented it fully --- interoperability was abysmal; (2) XFA and
AcroForm coexist awkwardly in the same file, with the two
representations frequently disagreeing about field values; and (3) XFA's
scripting capabilities have been exploited in multiple CVEs.

\subsection{Why XFA Still Causes
Problems}\label{_why_xfa_still_causes_problems}

Deprecation does not mean disappearance. Millions of XFA-based PDF forms
are still in circulation --- government tax forms, insurance
applications, healthcare records. When these documents are signed, the
signature covers the entire file, including the XFA data.

The problems:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{PDFBox does not support XFA rendering.} If you load an XFA
  document in PDFBox, you get the AcroForm fallback. The field values
  you see may differ from what an XFA-aware viewer shows. If you sign
  based on the AcroForm representation, the signature covers bytes that
  an XFA viewer interprets differently.
\item
  \textbf{Field names may not match.} XFA and AcroForm can use different
  naming conventions. A signature field named
  \texttt{form1{[}0{]}.\#subform{[}0{]}.SignatureField1{[}0{]}} in XFA
  might be \texttt{SignatureField1} in the AcroForm fallback.
\item
  \textbf{Validation ambiguity.} If the XFA representation says one
  thing and the signed AcroForm says another, which one did the signer
  actually see and intend to sign?
\end{itemize}

If you encounter an XFA document in a signing workflow, the safest
approach is to flatten the XFA to pure AcroForm (removing the
\texttt{/XFA} key and ensuring all field values are represented in the
AcroForm structure) \emph{before} any signatures are applied. PDFBox's
\texttt{PDAcroForm.flatten()} can help, but test thoroughly --- XFA
flattening is lossy by nature.

\section{Form Flattening and
Signatures}\label{_form_flattening_and_signatures}

Form flattening converts interactive fields into static page content:
field values are "burned in" to page content streams, widget annotations
are removed, and the AcroForm structure is deleted. The result is a
static document that looks like the filled-in form but cannot be edited.

Flattening destroys every signature in the document. The signature
fields themselves are removed (they are form fields), the byte ranges no
longer point at the original bytes, and the AcroForm structure
referencing the signatures is gone.

\textbf{Never flatten a signed document.} This sounds obvious, but it
happens constantly in automated workflows --- sign, then flatten "for
archival" or "to prevent editing." The result is a static PDF with no
valid signatures. If you need to prevent editing after signing, use
field locking (\texttt{/Lock}) and certification signatures
(\texttt{/DocMDP}) --- not flattening.

\section{PDFBox AcroForm API}\label{_pdfbox_acroform_api}

\subsection{PDAcroForm: The Entry
Point}\label{_pdacroform_the_entry_point}

In PDFBox 3.0.0, the AcroForm is accessed through the document catalog:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    PDAcroForm acroForm }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDocumentCatalog}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getAcroForm}\OperatorTok{();}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{acroForm }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\OperatorTok{;}  \CommentTok{// No interactive forms in this document}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
    \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDField field }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ acroForm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFields}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{debug}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Field: \{\} (type: \{\}, fqn: \{\})"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            field}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPartialName}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ field}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFieldType}\OperatorTok{(),}
\NormalTok{            field}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFullyQualifiedName}\OperatorTok{());}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{acroForm.getFields()} returns only top-level fields. To traverse
the full tree including children, use \texttt{acroForm.getFieldTree()},
which returns a flat iterable over every field in the hierarchy.

\subsection{Field Tree Traversal}\label{_field_tree_traversal}

\texttt{acroForm.getFieldTree()} returns a flat iterable over every
field in the hierarchy --- almost always what you want. For finding
signature fields specifically:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDField field }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ acroForm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFieldTree}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{field }\KeywordTok{instanceof}\NormalTok{ PDSignatureField sigField}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Signature field: \{\} (signed: \{\})"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            sigField}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFullyQualifiedName}\OperatorTok{(),}
\NormalTok{            sigField}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignature}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

If you need tree depth or explicit parent-child relationships, use
\texttt{PDNonTerminalField.getChildren()} and recurse manually.

\subsection{PDSignatureField and
PDSignature}\label{_pdsignaturefield_and_pdsignature}

\texttt{PDSignatureField} is the PDFBox class representing a
\texttt{/Sig} field. It provides access to the signature value (the
\texttt{/V} dictionary) through \texttt{getSignature()}, which returns a
\texttt{PDSignature} or \texttt{null} if the field is unsigned.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{PDSignatureField sigField }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignatureField}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ acroForm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getField}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Signature1"}\OperatorTok{);}
\ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sigField }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{} \ControlFlowTok{return}\OperatorTok{;} \OperatorTok{\}}  \CommentTok{// field not found {-}{-} check fully qualified name}

\NormalTok{PDSignature sig }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sigField}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignature}\OperatorTok{();}
\ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sig }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{} \ControlFlowTok{return}\OperatorTok{;} \OperatorTok{\}}       \CommentTok{// field exists but is unsigned}

\NormalTok{log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Filter: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFilter}\OperatorTok{());}       \CommentTok{// e.g. "Adobe.PPKLite"}
\NormalTok{log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"SubFilter: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSubFilter}\OperatorTok{());} \CommentTok{// e.g. "adbe.pkcs7.detached"}
\NormalTok{log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Name: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getName}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Sign date: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignDate}\OperatorTok{());}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{PDAcroForm.getField(String\ name)} uses the \textbf{fully
qualified name}. If the field is nested (e.g.,
\texttt{signatures.approval}), you must pass the full path. Passing just
\texttt{approval} will return \texttt{null}. This is one of the most
common sources of "field not found" bugs.

\subsection{Creating Signature Fields
Programmatically}\label{_creating_signature_fields_programmatically}

Creating a signature field involves three steps: create the field,
create the widget annotation, and wire them into the AcroForm and page
structure.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Ensure the AcroForm exists}
\NormalTok{PDAcroForm acroForm }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDocumentCatalog}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getAcroForm}\OperatorTok{();}
\ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{acroForm }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    acroForm }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDAcroForm}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDocumentCatalog}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{setAcroForm}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{acroForm}\OperatorTok{);}
\OperatorTok{\}}

\CommentTok{// Create the signature field and configure its widget}
\NormalTok{PDSignatureField sigField }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDSignatureField}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{acroForm}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{sigField}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setPartialName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Signature1"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{PDAnnotationWidget widget }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sigField}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getWidgets}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{PDPage targetPage }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPage}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{widget}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRectangle}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{100}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{600}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{200}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{50}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{widget}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setPage}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{targetPage}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// Wire into AcroForm and page structure}
\NormalTok{targetPage}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAnnotations}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{widget}\OperatorTok{);}
\BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{PDField}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ fields }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}(}\NormalTok{acroForm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFields}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{fields}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sigField}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{acroForm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFields}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{fields}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{acroForm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSObject}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{setInt}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SIG\_FLAGS}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{3}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Common mistakes when creating signature fields:}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Forgetting to add the widget to the page's \texttt{/Annots}
  array.} The field exists in the AcroForm but is invisible.
\item
  \textbf{Forgetting to add the field to \texttt{/Fields}.} The widget
  is on the page but no viewer considers it a form field.
\item
  \textbf{Not setting \texttt{/SigFlags}.} The field is there, it gets
  signed, but editors may do a full save and destroy it.
\item
  \textbf{Using \texttt{new\ PDRectangle(x,\ y,\ w,\ h)} vs. raw array.}
  PDFBox's \texttt{PDRectangle} constructor takes (x, y, width, height),
  but the raw PDF \texttt{/Rect} array is
  \texttt{{[}llx\ lly\ urx\ ury{]}}. PDFBox handles the conversion, but
  if you're debugging raw COS dictionaries, remember the coordinate
  difference.
\end{enumerate}

\section{Common Form/Signature Interaction
Bugs}\label{_common_formsignature_interaction_bugs}

Years of debugging have revealed a predictable set of bugs that occur at
the intersection of AcroForm infrastructure and signature functionality.
These are not edge cases --- they are the everyday reality of working
with signed PDF forms.

\subsection{Missing Appearance
Streams}\label{_missing_appearance_streams}

\textbf{Symptom:} Signature validates, but the rectangle on the page is
blank. \textbf{Cause:} No \texttt{/AP} entry on the widget, or
\texttt{/NeedAppearances} is \texttt{true} and the viewer destroyed the
original appearance during regeneration. \textbf{Fix:} Always generate a
complete \texttt{PDAppearanceStream} during signing and set it as the
normal appearance on the widget (see Chapter 15 for details).

\subsection{Wrong Field Hierarchy}\label{_wrong_field_hierarchy}

\textbf{Symptom:} PDFBox finds the field but Acrobat does not, or vice
versa. \textbf{Cause:} Inconsistent parent-child chain --- a child
references a parent that does not list it in \texttt{/Kids}, or the
fully qualified name computed by concatenating \texttt{/T} values does
not match expectations. \textbf{Fix:} Validate the tree bidirectionally:
every child's \texttt{/Parent} must list it in \texttt{/Kids}, and vice
versa.

\subsection{SigFlags Not Set or Wrong}\label{_sigflags_not_set_or_wrong}

\textbf{Symptom:} Signatures are valid, but a subsequent edit does a
full save and destroys them all. \textbf{Cause:} \texttt{/SigFlags} is
0, 1, or absent. \textbf{Fix:} Set \texttt{/SigFlags} to 3 when creating
the first signature field --- before signing, not after.

\subsection{Orphaned Signature Fields}\label{_orphaned_signature_fields}

\textbf{Symptom:} Signature panel shows a signature with no visual
element on any page; or shows nothing despite signature data existing in
the file. \textbf{Cause:} Widget is missing from a page's
\texttt{/Annots} (variant 1), or the field is not referenced from
\texttt{/Fields} (variant 2). \textbf{Fix:} Verify both paths: field →
AcroForm \texttt{/Fields}, and widget → page \texttt{/Annots}.

\subsection{Acrobat vs. PDFBox Rendering
Differences}\label{_acrobat_vs_pdfbox_rendering_differences}

\textbf{Symptom:} A form that looks perfect in Acrobat renders
differently in PDFBox (or vice versa).

\textbf{Common causes:} Acrobat aggressively substitutes missing fonts
(PDFBox does not --- it uses only what is embedded or available on the
system). Acrobat regenerates appearances when \texttt{/NeedAppearances}
is \texttt{true} (PDFBox requires an explicit
\texttt{refreshAppearances()} call). Acrobat is lenient about malformed
COS dictionaries (a field with \texttt{/FT} on a non-terminal node may
work in Acrobat but cause unexpected behavior in PDFBox). And Acrobat
interprets \texttt{/DA} strings flexibly --- font size 0 means
"auto-size," missing color operators are silently defaulted --- while
PDFBox parses more strictly.

When debugging cross-viewer differences, compare the raw COS structure.
In PDFBox: \texttt{field.getCOSObject().toString()}. In Acrobat:
Preflight tool or JavaScript console
(\texttt{this.getField("name").valueAsString}). The differences are
almost always at the COS level.

\subsection{Real-World Scenario: The Government Tax
Form}\label{_real_world_scenario_the_government_tax_form}

A government tax authority distributes a PDF form designed in Adobe
LiveCycle Designer. The form uses XFA internally with an AcroForm
fallback. A citizen fills it in using Chrome's built-in PDF viewer
(which uses the AcroForm fallback because it does not support XFA). The
citizen's tax advisor opens the filled form in Acrobat (which
\emph{does} support XFA), sees different field values because the XFA
data was never updated, and signs the document.

The signature is technically valid --- it covers the bytes that were
present at signing time. But the signed content includes XFA data that
disagrees with the AcroForm data. There is no clean fix short of
removing XFA from the document entirely. This is precisely why ISO
32000-2:2020 deprecated XFA, and why the deprecation matters for
real-world signature workflows.

\chapter{PDF Coordinate System}\label{_pdf_coordinate_system}

The PDF coordinate system is the single most common source of signature
placement bugs. It is conceptually simple --- an origin at the
bottom-left corner, units in points --- but it collides head-on with how
every screen coordinate system works, how page rotation transforms
visual layout without changing coordinates, and how annotation
rectangles encode position in a way that rewards precision and punishes
guessing.

If you have ever placed a signature at what you thought was the bottom
of a page and found it at the top, or created a perfectly valid
signature that appeared as a mirror image on a rotated page, or spent an
afternoon trying to figure out why your annotation rectangle produced a
zero-height invisible box --- this chapter is for you.

We will cover the coordinate system from first principles, work through
every page box that ISO 32000-2:2020 defines, explain how page rotation
interacts with coordinates, show how the Current Transformation Matrix
works, and then tie it all back to signature placement with practical
PDFBox code and debugging techniques.

\section{User Space: The Default Coordinate
System}\label{_user_space_the_default_coordinate_system}

Every PDF page has a \textbf{user space} coordinate system defined in
ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 8.3.2. The rules are few but non-negotiable:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The \textbf{origin} is at the \textbf{bottom-left} corner of the page.
\item
  The \textbf{x-axis} increases to the \textbf{right}.
\item
  The \textbf{y-axis} increases \textbf{upward}.
\item
  One unit equals \textbf{1/72 of an inch} --- which is exactly one
  \textbf{typographic point}.
\end{itemize}

That last property is not a coincidence. PDF was born as a page
description language for printing, and the typographic point has been
the standard unit of measure in the printing industry since the 18th
century. When the specification says a page is 612 units wide and 792
units tall, that's 8.5 by 11 inches --- US Letter size. A4 paper (the
ISO 216 standard used nearly everywhere outside the US) is 595.28 by
841.89 points (210 by 297 millimetres).

The 1/72-inch definition is a convenient approximation. The traditional
printer's point (the Didot point, the Pica point) varies slightly
depending on which historical tradition you follow. PDF sidesteps this
ambiguity by defining one user space unit as \emph{exactly} 1/72 inch,
period. ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 8.3.2.3 makes this explicit.

The bottom-left origin is where most coordinate confusion begins. Every
windowing system you have used --- HTML, Java Swing, JavaFX, Android,
iOS, Win32 --- places the origin at the \textbf{top-left} corner with
the y-axis increasing \textbf{downward}. We are trained to think of y=0
as the top. In PDF, y=0 is the bottom.

This means that when you want to place a signature 50 points from the
top of a US Letter page, the y-coordinate is not 50 --- it is 792 minus
50 minus the height of the signature rectangle. We will return to this
conversion formula repeatedly throughout this chapter because it is the
root cause of the most common signature positioning bugs.

\section{Page Boxes: The Five
Rectangles}\label{_page_boxes_the_five_rectangles}

A PDF page does not have a single "size." It has up to five nested
rectangles, each serving a different purpose. ISO 32000-2:2020 Section
14.11.2 defines them all. Understanding which box matters for signature
placement --- and which boxes most documents actually define ---
prevents an entire category of positioning errors.

\subsection{MediaBox (Required)}\label{_mediabox_required}

The \textbf{MediaBox} defines the physical medium --- the full extent of
the page as it would be printed or rendered. It is the only required
page box; every other box defaults to the MediaBox if absent.

For a US Letter page:

\begin{verbatim}
/MediaBox [0 0 612 792]
\end{verbatim}

The array is \texttt{{[}llx\ lly\ urx\ ury{]}} --- lower-left x,
lower-left y, upper-right x, upper-right y. The MediaBox almost always
starts at the origin \texttt{{[}0\ 0\ …\hspace{0pt}{]}}, but it does not
have to. A MediaBox of \texttt{{[}100\ 100\ 712\ 892{]}} is perfectly
legal and defines the same 612-by-792 point area, just shifted. Most
tools normalize to zero, but when debugging coordinates, never assume.

\subsection{CropBox}\label{_cropbox}

The \textbf{CropBox} defines the region to which the page contents are
clipped when displayed or printed. Think of it as the visible window
into the MediaBox. If absent, the CropBox defaults to the MediaBox.

A common use case: a document designed for A4 paper with crop marks. The
MediaBox includes the crop marks (larger than the final page), and the
CropBox clips to the actual content area.

For signature placement, the CropBox is the relevant boundary in most
viewers. Adobe Acrobat positions annotations relative to the CropBox,
not the MediaBox, when they differ.

If you compute signature coordinates against the MediaBox but the
document has a smaller CropBox, your signature may appear to be shifted
or partially off-screen. Always check \texttt{page.getCropBox()} in
PDFBox and use its dimensions as the reference frame.

\subsection{BleedBox}\label{_bleedbox}

The \textbf{BleedBox} defines the region to which the page contents are
clipped when output in a production (printing) environment. It is
slightly larger than the TrimBox to allow for bleed --- the area where
ink extends beyond the final cut edge to avoid white margins from
imprecise cutting.

The BleedBox is relevant for professional printing workflows and has no
meaningful impact on digital signature placement. We mention it for
completeness.

\subsection{TrimBox}\label{_trimbox}

The \textbf{TrimBox} defines the intended final dimensions of the page
after trimming. In a print-production workflow, the TrimBox is the
"real" page boundary --- the finished product.

Like the BleedBox, the TrimBox is irrelevant for signature placement in
digital workflows. However, if you are building a signing service that
handles print-ready PDFs, be aware that the TrimBox may be significantly
smaller than the MediaBox, and your users\textquotesingle{} mental model
of "the page" aligns with the TrimBox, not the MediaBox.

\subsection{ArtBox}\label{_artbox}

The \textbf{ArtBox} defines the extent of the page's "meaningful
content" as intended by the author. It is rarely used in practice and
has no impact on signature placement.

\subsection{Which Box Matters for
Signatures?}\label{_which_box_matters_for_signatures}

For digital signature placement, the decision tree is simple:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Use the CropBox} as your reference frame. In PDFBox,
  \texttt{page.getCropBox()} returns the CropBox if defined, otherwise
  it falls back to the MediaBox. This is the correct default.
\item
  If you need to verify that your annotation rectangle falls within the
  visible area, check against the CropBox.
\item
  Never assume the CropBox starts at \texttt{{[}0\ 0\ …\hspace{0pt}{]}}.
  A CropBox of \texttt{{[}50\ 50\ 562\ 742{]}} shifts the visible
  origin.
\end{enumerate}

PDFBox's \texttt{PDPage.getCropBox()} handles the fallback-to-MediaBox
logic automatically. You almost never need to call
\texttt{getMediaBox()} directly for positioning purposes. The one
exception is when you need to know the physical page dimensions for
coordinate translation on pages with a non-zero CropBox origin.

\section{Annotation Rectangles}\label{_annotation_rectangles}

Every annotation --- including signature widget annotations --- has a
\texttt{/Rect} entry that specifies its position on the page. The
\texttt{/Rect} is an array of four numbers:

\begin{verbatim}
/Rect [x1 y1 x2 y2]
\end{verbatim}

Where \texttt{{[}x1\ y1{]}} is the \textbf{lower-left} corner and
\texttt{{[}x2\ y2{]}} is the \textbf{upper-right} corner, both in user
space coordinates relative to the page's coordinate system.

For a signature that should appear 100 points from the left edge, 50
points from the bottom edge, and is 200 points wide by 60 points tall:

\begin{verbatim}
/Rect [100 50 300 110]
\end{verbatim}

That gives us: lower-left at (100, 50), upper-right at (100+200, 50+60)
= (300, 110).

The convention is lower-left to upper-right. If you accidentally swap
these --- providing upper-left to lower-right, for example --- some
viewers will silently normalize the rectangle, while others will render
a zero-area or inverted annotation. PDFBox's \texttt{PDRectangle}
constructor takes \texttt{(x,\ y,\ width,\ height)} where
\texttt{(x,\ y)} is the lower-left corner, so it handles this correctly
if you feed it the right values.

\section{Why Signature Positioning Goes
Wrong}\label{_why_signature_positioning_goes_wrong}

Here is the bug that every developer writes exactly once. We want a
signature at the visual top-right corner of a US Letter page. In screen
coordinates (top-left origin), that would be approximately
\texttt{(400,\ 30)} for a rectangle starting near the top-right. So we
write:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{PDRectangle rect }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{400}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{30}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{200}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{50}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

And the signature appears at the \textbf{bottom-right} of the page.
Because y=30 in PDF user space is 30 points from the \emph{bottom}, not
30 points from the top.

The correct conversion is:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ pageHeight }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCropBox}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getHeight}\OperatorTok{();}
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ desiredTopOffset }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{30}\OperatorTok{;}    \CommentTok{// 30 points from the visual top}
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ signatureHeight }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{50}\OperatorTok{;}

\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ pdfY }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ pageHeight }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ desiredTopOffset }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ signatureHeight}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{PDRectangle rect }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{400}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ pdfY}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{200}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signatureHeight}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The formula is:
\texttt{pdfY\ =\ pageHeight\ -\ topOffset\ -\ rectangleHeight}.

This is the single most important formula in this chapter. Memorize it,
put it in a utility method, and never compute it inline.

If your signing UI lets users click a position on a rendered page, the
click coordinates are in screen space (origin top-left). You must invert
the y-axis before creating the PDF annotation rectangle. Failing to do
so produces signatures that appear to be vertically "mirrored" --- a
signature placed at the top appears at the bottom, and vice versa.

\section{Page Rotation}\label{_page_rotation}

The \texttt{/Rotate} key in a page dictionary specifies a rotation angle
that viewers should apply when displaying the page. Legal values are 0,
90, 180, and 270 (degrees, clockwise).

Here is the critical insight that trips up even experienced developers:
\textbf{page rotation does not change the coordinate system}. It changes
how the viewer \emph{displays} the coordinates, but the coordinates
themselves --- and the annotation rectangles --- remain in the original,
unrotated space.

Consider a US Letter page with \texttt{/Rotate\ 90}. The viewer displays
it in landscape orientation: 792 points wide and 612 points tall. But
the MediaBox is still \texttt{{[}0\ 0\ 612\ 792{]}} and the coordinate
system still has its origin at the bottom-left corner of the
\emph{unrotated} page.

This means that if you compute an annotation rectangle for a
90-degree-rotated page using the visual (rotated) dimensions, your
coordinates will be wrong. The annotation will appear in a completely
unexpected location because the viewer applies the rotation \emph{after}
positioning the annotation in the unrotated coordinate space.

\subsection{Rotation and Signature
Placement}\label{_rotation_and_signature_placement}

To place a signature at a specific visual position on a rotated page, we
must think in the \emph{unrotated} coordinate space and account for the
rotation.

For a page rotated 90 degrees clockwise:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The visual width (what the user sees) corresponds to the unrotated
  height (792 for US Letter).
\item
  The visual height corresponds to the unrotated width (612 for US
  Letter).
\item
  A point at visual position (vx, vy) --- where vy is measured from the
  visual top --- maps to unrotated coordinates (vy\_from\_bottom, vx)
  approximately, depending on the rotation direction.
\end{itemize}

The general approach in PDFBox:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{PDRectangle cropBox }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCropBox}\OperatorTok{();}
\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ rotation }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getRotation}\OperatorTok{();}

\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ pageWidth}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ pageHeight}\OperatorTok{;}
\ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{rotation }\OperatorTok{==} \DecValTok{90} \OperatorTok{||}\NormalTok{ rotation }\OperatorTok{==} \DecValTok{270}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \CommentTok{// Visual dimensions are swapped}
\NormalTok{    pageWidth }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getHeight}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    pageHeight }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getWidth}\OperatorTok{();}
\OperatorTok{\}} \ControlFlowTok{else} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    pageWidth }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getWidth}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    pageHeight }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getHeight}\OperatorTok{();}
\OperatorTok{\}}

\CommentTok{// Now compute visual position}
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ visualX }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{400}\OperatorTok{;}                          \CommentTok{// from left of visual page}
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ visualTopOffset }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{30}\OperatorTok{;}                   \CommentTok{// from top of visual page}
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ sigWidth }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{200}\OperatorTok{;}
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ sigHeight }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{50}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{// Convert to PDF coordinates depending on rotation}
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ pdfX}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ pdfY}\OperatorTok{;}
\ControlFlowTok{switch} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{rotation}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \ControlFlowTok{case} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{:}
\NormalTok{        pdfX }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLowerLeftX}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ visualX}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{        pdfY }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getUpperRightY}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ visualTopOffset }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ sigHeight}\OperatorTok{;}
        \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{case} \DecValTok{90}\OperatorTok{:}
\NormalTok{        pdfX }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLowerLeftX}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ visualTopOffset}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{        pdfY }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLowerLeftY}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ visualX}\OperatorTok{;}
        \CommentTok{// Width and height swap in the annotation rect}
        \DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ temp }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sigWidth}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{        sigWidth }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sigHeight}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{        sigHeight }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ temp}\OperatorTok{;}
        \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{case} \DecValTok{180}\OperatorTok{:}
\NormalTok{        pdfX }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getUpperRightX}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ visualX }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ sigWidth}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{        pdfY }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLowerLeftY}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ visualTopOffset}\OperatorTok{;}
        \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{case} \DecValTok{270}\OperatorTok{:}
\NormalTok{        pdfX }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getUpperRightX}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ visualTopOffset }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ sigHeight}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{        pdfY }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getUpperRightY}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ visualX }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ sigWidth}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{        temp }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sigWidth}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{        sigWidth }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sigHeight}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{        sigHeight }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ temp}\OperatorTok{;}
        \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;}
    \KeywordTok{default}\OperatorTok{:}
        \ControlFlowTok{throw} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{IllegalArgumentException}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Invalid rotation: "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ rotation}\OperatorTok{);}
\OperatorTok{\}}

\NormalTok{PDRectangle signatureRect }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfX}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ pdfY}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sigWidth}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sigHeight}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Wrap this coordinate transformation in a dedicated utility method and
unit-test it for all four rotation values. This is the kind of code
where an off-by-one error in a single case produces a bug that only
manifests for rotated pages --- which may be 5\% of your input
documents, ensuring the bug survives initial testing and surfaces in
production.

\section{Transformation Matrices and the
CTM}\label{_transformation_matrices_and_the_ctm}

ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 8.3.3 defines the \textbf{Current
Transformation Matrix (CTM)} --- a 3x3 affine transformation matrix that
maps user space coordinates to device space coordinates. Every content
stream operates under a CTM that controls translation, scaling,
rotation, and skewing.

The CTM is represented as six numbers \texttt{{[}a\ b\ c\ d\ e\ f{]}},
corresponding to the matrix:

\begin{verbatim}
| a  b  0 |
| c  d  0 |
| e  f  1 |
\end{verbatim}

For signature appearances, the CTM matters because the appearance stream
(the Form XObject that draws the visual signature) has its own
coordinate system. The \texttt{/BBox} of the appearance stream defines
the drawing area, and the viewer maps this BBox into the annotation
rectangle using a transformation derived from the rectangle dimensions
and the BBox dimensions.

When the appearance stream's BBox does not match the annotation
rectangle's aspect ratio, the viewer scales the appearance to fit ---
potentially distorting the signature image. If the BBox is
\texttt{{[}0\ 0\ 200\ 50{]}} but the annotation rectangle is 200 by 100
points, the appearance will be stretched vertically by a factor of 2.

When creating signature appearances in PDFBox, always ensure the
appearance stream's BBox matches the dimensions of the annotation
rectangle. If you use \texttt{PDRectangle} for both and create them from
the same width and height values, this happens automatically. The bug
occurs when one is constructed independently of the other, or when the
annotation rectangle is modified after the appearance is created.

Content streams use operators like \texttt{cm} (concatenate matrix) to
modify the CTM:

\begin{verbatim}
1 0 0 1 100 200 cm    % translate by (100, 200)
0.5 0 0 0.5 0 0 cm    % scale by 50%
\end{verbatim}

For signature placement, you rarely need to manipulate the CTM directly.
PDFBox handles the coordinate mapping between the appearance stream and
the annotation rectangle. But when debugging a signature that appears at
the wrong size or position, checking the CTM in the appearance stream's
content is a valuable diagnostic step.

\section{Signature Appearance Rectangles and Page
Coordinates}\label{_signature_appearance_rectangles_and_page_coordinates}

The annotation rectangle (\texttt{/Rect}) positions the signature on the
page. The appearance stream's BBox defines the drawing canvas. The
