relationship between them determines how the visual signature is
rendered.

Here is the complete chain:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  The page has a coordinate system defined by its MediaBox (or CropBox).
\item
  The annotation's \texttt{/Rect} specifies where on the page the
  annotation sits, in page coordinates.
\item
  The appearance stream has a \texttt{/BBox} that defines its internal
  drawing area.
\item
  The viewer computes a transformation from the BBox to the Rect ---
  scaling and translating the appearance stream content to fill the
  annotation rectangle.
\item
  If the page has a \texttt{/Rotate} value, the viewer rotates the
  entire visual output \emph{after} all of the above.
\end{enumerate}

This chain explains why a signature appearance that looks correct on one
page can appear distorted on another: if the target page has different
dimensions or a different rotation, and you reuse the same appearance
stream without adjusting the annotation rectangle, the scaling
transformation changes.

\section{PDFBox Coordinate Handling}\label{_pdfbox_coordinate_handling}

PDFBox provides \texttt{PDRectangle} as the primary class for working
with rectangles in PDF user space. The key methods:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{PDRectangle mediaBox }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getMediaBox}\OperatorTok{();}   \CommentTok{// Physical page extent}
\NormalTok{PDRectangle cropBox }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCropBox}\OperatorTok{();}     \CommentTok{// Visible page extent (falls back to MediaBox)}
\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ rotation }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getRotation}\OperatorTok{();}           \CommentTok{// 0, 90, 180, or 270}

\CommentTok{// PDRectangle stores lower{-}left corner + width/height}
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ llx }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLowerLeftX}\OperatorTok{();}
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ lly }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLowerLeftY}\OperatorTok{();}
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ urx }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getUpperRightX}\OperatorTok{();}
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ ury }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getUpperRightY}\OperatorTok{();}
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ width }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getWidth}\OperatorTok{();}
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ height }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cropBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getHeight}\OperatorTok{();}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{PDRectangle} predefined constants include
\texttt{PDRectangle.A4} (595.28 x 841.89) and
\texttt{PDRectangle.LETTER} (612 x 792). These are useful for creating
new pages but should never be used as the reference for positioning on
an existing page --- always query the actual page's CropBox.

\subsection{Practical Example: Placing a Visible
Signature}\label{_practical_example_placing_a_visible_signature}

The following complete example places a visible signature rectangle at a
specific visual position on a page, correctly handling rotation:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Loader}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDDocument}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDPage}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{common}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ SignaturePositionCalculator }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \CommentTok{/**}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Computes the PDF annotation rectangle for a signature at a given}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ visual position}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ accounting for page rotation and CropBox offset}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@param}\CommentTok{ }\AnnotationTok{page}\CommentTok{         }\NormalTok{the target page}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@param}\CommentTok{ }\AnnotationTok{visualX}\CommentTok{      }\NormalTok{x offset from the left of the visual page }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{points}\CommentTok{)}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@param}\CommentTok{ }\AnnotationTok{visualY}\CommentTok{      }\NormalTok{y offset from the top of the visual page }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{points}\CommentTok{)}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@param}\CommentTok{ }\AnnotationTok{visualWidth}\CommentTok{  }\NormalTok{desired visual width of the signature }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{points}\CommentTok{)}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@param}\CommentTok{ }\AnnotationTok{visualHeight}\CommentTok{ }\NormalTok{desired visual height of the signature }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{points}\CommentTok{)}
     \CommentTok{*} \AnnotationTok{@return}\NormalTok{ the annotation rectangle in PDF user space coordinates}
     \CommentTok{*/}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static}\NormalTok{ PDRectangle }\FunctionTok{computeSignatureRect}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            PDPage page}\OperatorTok{,}
            \DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ visualX}\OperatorTok{,} \DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ visualY}\OperatorTok{,}
            \DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ visualWidth}\OperatorTok{,} \DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ visualHeight}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}

\NormalTok{        PDRectangle crop }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCropBox}\OperatorTok{();}
        \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ rotation }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getRotation}\OperatorTok{();}

        \DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ pdfX}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ pdfY}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ pdfW}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ pdfH}\OperatorTok{;}

        \ControlFlowTok{switch} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{rotation}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \ControlFlowTok{case} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{:}
\NormalTok{                pdfX }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ crop}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLowerLeftX}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ visualX}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{                pdfY }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ crop}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getUpperRightY}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ visualY }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ visualHeight}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{                pdfW }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ visualWidth}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{                pdfH }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ visualHeight}\OperatorTok{;}
                \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;}
            \ControlFlowTok{case} \DecValTok{90}\OperatorTok{:}
\NormalTok{                pdfX }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ crop}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLowerLeftX}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ visualY}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{                pdfY }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ crop}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLowerLeftY}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ visualX}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{                pdfW }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ visualHeight}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{                pdfH }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ visualWidth}\OperatorTok{;}
                \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;}
            \ControlFlowTok{case} \DecValTok{180}\OperatorTok{:}
\NormalTok{                pdfX }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ crop}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getUpperRightX}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ visualX }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ visualWidth}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{                pdfY }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ crop}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLowerLeftY}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ visualY}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{                pdfW }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ visualWidth}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{                pdfH }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ visualHeight}\OperatorTok{;}
                \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;}
            \ControlFlowTok{case} \DecValTok{270}\OperatorTok{:}
\NormalTok{                pdfX }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ crop}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getUpperRightX}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ visualY }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ visualHeight}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{                pdfY }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ crop}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getUpperRightY}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ visualX }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ visualWidth}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{                pdfW }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ visualHeight}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{                pdfH }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ visualWidth}\OperatorTok{;}
                \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;}
            \KeywordTok{default}\OperatorTok{:}
                \ControlFlowTok{throw} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{IllegalArgumentException}\OperatorTok{(}
                    \StringTok{"Unsupported rotation: "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ rotation}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfX}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ pdfY}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ pdfW}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ pdfH}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Call this with visual coordinates --- "200 points from the left, 50
points from the top, 180 wide, 40 tall" --- and it returns the correct
PDF rectangle regardless of rotation.

\section{Common Coordinate Bugs}\label{_common_coordinate_bugs}

Over years of building PDF signing tools, we have seen the same bugs
recur. Here is a catalog.

\subsection{Flipped Signatures (Y-axis
Inversion)}\label{_flipped_signatures_y_axis_inversion}

\textbf{Symptom}: The signature appears at the vertical opposite of
where it should be. A signature placed near the top of the page appears
near the bottom.

\textbf{Cause}: Using screen-space y-coordinates (origin top-left)
directly as PDF y-coordinates (origin bottom-left) without inverting.

\textbf{Fix}: Apply the formula
\texttt{pdfY\ =\ cropBox.getUpperRightY()\ -\ screenY\ -\ signatureHeight}.

\subsection{Signatures on the Wrong Page Position (Rotated
Pages)}\label{_signatures_on_the_wrong_page_position_rotated_pages}

\textbf{Symptom}: The signature appears in a completely unexpected
location --- not just vertically flipped, but seemingly random --- and
only on certain documents.

\textbf{Cause}: The page has a non-zero \texttt{/Rotate} value, and the
coordinate calculation does not account for it. Many developers test
exclusively with non-rotated pages and never encounter this until a
customer submits a rotated document.

\textbf{Fix}: Always check \texttt{page.getRotation()} and apply the
rotation-aware coordinate transformation shown above.

\subsection{Zero-Area Annotation (Invisible Signature Becomes Truly
Invisible)}\label{_zero_area_annotation_invisible_signature_becomes_truly_invisible}

\textbf{Symptom}: The signature field exists (it shows in the signature
panel), but nothing is visible on the page.

\textbf{Cause}: The annotation rectangle has zero width or height,
typically because the lower-left and upper-right corners are identical,
or because a coordinate calculation produced a negative dimension that
the viewer clamped to zero.

\textbf{Fix}: Always validate that the computed rectangle has positive
width and height before creating the annotation. Log the rectangle
values during development:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{debug}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Signature rect: x=\{\}, y=\{\}, w=\{\}, h=\{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{    rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLowerLeftX}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLowerLeftY}\OperatorTok{(),}
\NormalTok{    rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getWidth}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getHeight}\OperatorTok{());}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Signature Appears
Off-Page}\label{_signature_appears_off_page}

\textbf{Symptom}: The signature field exists, the signature validates,
but nothing is visible. Unlike the zero-area case, the rectangle has
positive dimensions --- it's just outside the visible CropBox.

\textbf{Cause}: Coordinates were computed against the MediaBox, but the
CropBox is smaller. Or the CropBox has a non-zero origin that was not
accounted for.

\textbf{Fix}: Always compute against \texttt{page.getCropBox()} and
include the CropBox's lower-left offset in your calculations.

\subsection{Distorted Signature
Appearance}\label{_distorted_signature_appearance}

\textbf{Symptom}: The signature image or text appears stretched or
squashed.

\textbf{Cause}: The appearance stream's BBox has a different aspect
ratio than the annotation rectangle. The viewer scales the BBox to fit
the Rect, distorting the content.

\textbf{Fix}: Ensure the appearance stream's BBox dimensions match the
annotation rectangle's width and height.

\section{Debugging Coordinates with QPDF and
RUPS}\label{_debugging_coordinates_with_qpdf_and_rups}

When a signature appears in the wrong place, you need to inspect the raw
PDF objects. Two tools are indispensable.

\subsection{QPDF}\label{_qpdf}

QPDF (\url{https://qpdf.sourceforge.io/}) is a command-line tool that
can dump every object in a PDF in a human-readable JSON format:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}json}\NormalTok{ input.pdf }\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ /tmp/structure.json}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Then search for the annotation rectangle:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{grep} \AttributeTok{{-}A2} \StringTok{\textquotesingle{}"Rect"\textquotesingle{}}\NormalTok{ /tmp/structure.json}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

You will see the raw \texttt{/Rect} array values. Compare them against
the page's \texttt{/MediaBox} and \texttt{/CropBox} to verify they fall
within the visible area.

QPDF can also show page-level dictionaries:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}show{-}object}\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{3 input.pdf   }\CommentTok{\# show object 3 (a page, for example)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{RUPS (Reading and Updating PDF
Syntax)}\label{_rups_reading_and_updating_pdf_syntax}

RUPS is a Java Swing application from the iText team that provides a
tree view of every PDF object. It is invaluable for visual debugging
because you can navigate the object tree, inspect annotation rectangles,
and see the page boxes side by side.

To inspect a signature's coordinates in RUPS:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Open the PDF in RUPS.
\item
  Navigate to \textbf{Pages} → \textbf{Page N} → \textbf{Annots} (the
  annotations array).
\item
  Find the signature widget annotation.
\item
  Check the \texttt{/Rect} value --- this is the annotation rectangle in
  page coordinates.
\item
  Go back to the page and check \texttt{/MediaBox}, \texttt{/CropBox},
  and \texttt{/Rotate}.
\item
  Verify that the Rect falls within the CropBox and that you've
  accounted for the rotation.
\end{enumerate}

RUPS also lets you inspect the appearance stream's BBox. Navigate into
the annotation's \texttt{/AP} → \texttt{/N} and check the \texttt{/BBox}
entry. If the BBox dimensions don't match the Rect dimensions, you've
found your distortion bug.

\subsection{PDFBox Programmatic
Inspection}\label{_pdfbox_programmatic_inspection}

You can also inspect coordinates programmatically, which is useful in
automated tests:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{File}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"signed.pdf"}\OperatorTok{)))} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDPage page }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPages}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Page MediaBox: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getMediaBox}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Page CropBox:  \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCropBox}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Page Rotation: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getRotation}\OperatorTok{());}

        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{var}\NormalTok{ annot }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAnnotations}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Sig"}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{equals}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{annot}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSubtype}\OperatorTok{()))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{                PDRectangle rect }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ annot}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getRectangle}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{                log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Sig annotation Rect: [\{\}, \{\}, \{\}, \{\}]"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                    rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLowerLeftX}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLowerLeftY}\OperatorTok{(),}
\NormalTok{                    rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getUpperRightX}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getUpperRightY}\OperatorTok{());}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

When writing integration tests for signature placement, always test with
at least four documents: one for each rotation value (0, 90, 180, 270).
Also test with a document that has a CropBox smaller than its MediaBox.
These five test cases catch the vast majority of coordinate bugs before
they reach production.

\section{Summary}\label{_summary_8}

The PDF coordinate system is a solved problem --- the rules are clear
and have not changed since PDF 1.0. The difficulty lies entirely in the
mismatch between PDF's bottom-left origin and the top-left origin of
every UI framework, compounded by page rotation's effect on visual
layout.

For signature placement, remember these principles:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Always use the CropBox (via \texttt{page.getCropBox()}) as your
  reference frame.
\item
  Always invert the y-axis when converting from screen coordinates:
  \texttt{pdfY\ =\ cropBoxTop\ -\ screenY\ -\ height}.
\item
  Always check \texttt{page.getRotation()} and transform coordinates
  accordingly.
\item
  Always ensure the appearance stream BBox matches the annotation
  rectangle dimensions.
\item
  Always test with rotated pages and non-standard CropBoxes.
\end{itemize}

These are not complex algorithms. They are simple arithmetic applied
consistently. The bugs come from skipping a step, not from the math
being hard.

\chapter{Font Embedding in PDF}\label{_font_embedding_in_pdf}

Fonts are the invisible infrastructure of every PDF document. When they
work, nobody notices. When they break, the document becomes
unreadable --- and if that document contains a digital signature, the
signature appearance becomes a row of blank rectangles or missing-glyph
squares that make your legally binding signature look like a rendering
error.

This chapter explains how fonts work inside PDF files, how they get
embedded (or not), what goes wrong when they don't, and how all of this
matters specifically for digital signatures. We cover the font types
defined by ISO 32000-2:2020, the encoding models, the mechanics of
embedding and subsetting, and the practical reality of getting text to
render correctly in signature appearance streams across different
viewers.

If you have ever seen a signature block that shows \texttt{.notdef}
squares where a signer's name should be, or a PDF where the signature
text vanishes entirely on one viewer but renders fine on another, this
chapter explains why.

\section{Why Fonts Matter for Digital
Signatures}\label{_why_fonts_matter_for_digital_signatures}

A PDF digital signature has two independent representations: the
\textbf{cryptographic} representation (the CMS \texttt{SignedData}
structure, the byte range, the certificate chain) and the
\textbf{visual} representation (the appearance stream that shows the
signer's name, date, and reason on the page). Chapter 15 covers how
appearance streams are structured; this chapter covers the font
infrastructure that makes the \emph{text} inside those appearances
render correctly.

The visual appearance of a signature is a Form XObject --- a miniature
PDF page with its own resource dictionary, its own content stream, and
its own font references. When that content stream contains a \texttt{Tj}
or \texttt{TJ} operator to draw text, the PDF viewer must resolve the
font reference, find the glyph outlines, and render them. If any step in
that chain fails --- the font reference points to a font that isn't
embedded, the encoding doesn't map the character code to the right
glyph, the font descriptor is missing, the font file stream is
corrupt --- the text doesn't render.

This matters more for signatures than for regular document text, for
three reasons:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Signature appearances are generated programmatically.} When a
  human creates a PDF in a word processor, the word processor embeds the
  fonts it uses. When your signing code creates a signature appearance,
  \emph{your code} is responsible for font handling. If you get it
  wrong, nobody catches the error until a user reports that the
  signature looks broken.
\item
  \textbf{Signature appearances must survive across viewers.} A
  signature created on a server using PDFBox must render correctly in
  Adobe Acrobat, Foxit Reader, browser-based viewers using pdf.js,
  LibreOffice, and Preview.app. Each viewer has its own font fallback
  behavior, its own interpretation of encoding, and its own handling of
  missing fonts. The only way to guarantee consistent rendering is to
  embed the font.
\item
  \textbf{Signature text often contains names with non-ASCII
  characters.} Signer names like "Muller" are fine. Signer names like
  "Muller" with an umlaut, "Gonzalez" with an accent, or any East Asian,
  Arabic, or Cyrillic name will fail if the font and encoding aren't set
  up correctly. In a global signing system, "works for ASCII names" is
  not good enough.
\end{enumerate}

\section{Font Types in PDF}\label{_font_types_in_pdf}

ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 9.6 defines several font types. The
distinctions matter because each type has different embedding rules,
different encoding models, and different failure modes.

\subsection{Type 1 Fonts (PostScript
Era)}\label{_type_1_fonts_postscript_era}

Type 1 fonts are the original PostScript outline fonts, defined by Adobe
in 1984. They use cubic Bezier curves for glyph outlines and a custom
PostScript-based encoding scheme.

In PDF, a Type 1 font dictionary has \texttt{/Subtype\ /Type1} and
references a font program (the glyph outlines) via the
\texttt{/FontDescriptor} dictionary's \texttt{/FontFile} entry. The font
program is a stream containing the PostScript font data in PFB (Printer
Font Binary) format.

Type 1 fonts are limited to a maximum of 256 glyphs per font
instance --- a consequence of the single-byte encoding model inherited
from PostScript. This makes them unsuitable for CJK scripts, complex
scripts like Arabic or Devanagari, or any language that needs more than
256 distinct glyphs.

Adobe officially ended support for Type 1 fonts in January 2023,
removing the ability to author content with Type 1 fonts in their
Creative Cloud applications. Existing PDFs with embedded Type 1 font
programs still render correctly (the outlines are in the file), but this
signals the industry's long-term direction: TrueType and OpenType are
the future.

\subsection{The Standard 14 Fonts}\label{_the_standard_14_fonts}

PDF defines fourteen fonts that conforming readers are expected to have
available without embedding. These are the \textbf{Standard 14 fonts},
specified in ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 9.6.2.2:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\caption{The Standard 14 font families}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Family
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Regular / Bold / Italic / BoldItalic
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Notes
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Family
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Regular / Bold / Italic / BoldItalic
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Notes
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Helvetica & Helvetica, Helvetica-Bold, Helvetica-Oblique,
Helvetica-BoldOblique & Sans-serif. The default choice for most
programmatic PDF generation. \\
Times & Times-Roman, Times-Bold, Times-Italic, Times-BoldItalic & Serif.
Common in academic and legal documents. \\
Courier & Courier, Courier-Bold, Courier-Oblique, Courier-BoldOblique &
Monospace. Used for code listings and form data. \\
Symbol & Symbol & Mathematical and Greek symbols. \\
ZapfDingbats & ZapfDingbats & Decorative symbols (checkmarks, arrows,
etc.). \\
\end{longtable}

The critical word is "expected." ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 9.6.2.2 states
that conforming readers \emph{should} provide these fonts, but PDF 2.0
actually recommends embedding even the Standard 14 fonts to ensure
consistent rendering. The practical reality is that most viewers do
provide metrics-compatible substitutes for these fonts, but the
substitutes are not identical --- a document typeset with Helvetica on
one viewer may render with Arial or Liberation Sans on another, causing
slight differences in character widths, line breaks, and text
positioning.

For signature appearances, these differences are usually cosmetic and
acceptable. But if your signature appearance calculates text positions
based on precise Helvetica metrics (e.g., centering text in the
signature rectangle), a substitute font with slightly different widths
will misalign the text.

For signature appearances, the Standard 14 fonts are "good enough" for
ASCII text. They are available everywhere, they don't require embedding,
and the rendering differences between substitute fonts are typically
invisible at the font sizes used in signature blocks (8-12 points).

For non-ASCII text --- accented characters, umlauts, CJK
characters --- the Standard 14 fonts are \emph{not} sufficient, because
their built-in encoding (WinAnsiEncoding) cannot represent characters
outside the Windows-1252 code page. If your signers have names with
characters outside that range, you must embed a TrueType or OpenType
font with the appropriate glyph coverage.

\subsubsection{PDFBox 3.x and the Standard 14 API
Change}\label{_pdfbox_3_x_and_the_standard_14_api_change}

If you've migrated from PDFBox 2.x to 3.x, you've likely encountered
this:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// PDFBox 2.x {-}{-} this no longer compiles in 3.x}
\NormalTok{PDFont font }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HELVETICA}\OperatorTok{;}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

In PDFBox 2.x, the Standard 14 fonts were available as static constants
on \texttt{PDType1Font}. PDFBox 3.x removed these constants and replaced
them with an enum-based API:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// PDFBox 3.x {-}{-} the correct way}
\NormalTok{PDFont font }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FontName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HELVETICA}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// Other examples:}
\NormalTok{PDFont bold }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FontName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HELVETICA\_BOLD}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{PDFont courier }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FontName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{COURIER}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{PDFont times }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FontName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{TIMES\_ROMAN}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The reason for this change is that the old static constants were shared
mutable objects --- every caller that obtained
\texttt{PDType1Font.HELVETICA} got the same instance, which could cause
subtle threading and state pollution issues when multiple documents were
processed concurrently. The new constructor creates a fresh instance
each time, eliminating the shared-state problem.

If you search the internet for PDFBox font examples, the majority of
results still show the 2.x API (\texttt{PDType1Font.HELVETICA}). These
examples will not compile with PDFBox 3.0.0. Always verify that code
examples match the major version of PDFBox you are using.

\subsection{TrueType Fonts}\label{_truetype_fonts}

TrueType fonts, developed jointly by Apple and Microsoft in the late
1980s as a competitor to Adobe's Type 1, use quadratic Bezier curves for
glyph outlines. They are the most common font format in modern computing
and the recommended choice for PDF font embedding.

In PDF, a TrueType font dictionary has \texttt{/Subtype\ /TrueType}. The
font program is embedded via the \texttt{/FontDescriptor} dictionary's
\texttt{/FontFile2} entry, which contains the raw TrueType font data (a
\texttt{.ttf} file, essentially).

TrueType fonts in PDF can be either \textbf{simple} (single-byte
encoding, max 256 glyphs per instance) or embedded as part of a
\textbf{composite} font (Type 0 + CIDFont, described below) for full
Unicode coverage. When used as a simple font, TrueType has the same
256-glyph limitation as Type 1. The composite font mechanism removes
this limitation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Loading and embedding a TrueType font in PDFBox 3.x}
\NormalTok{PDFont font }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ PDType0Font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{load}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{document}\OperatorTok{,} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{File}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"/path/to/DejaVuSans.ttf"}\OperatorTok{));}

\CommentTok{// The font is now embedded in the document.}
\CommentTok{// It will be automatically subsetted when the document is saved,}
\CommentTok{// including only the glyphs actually used.}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{PDType0Font.load()} is the PDFBox 3.x method for loading
TrueType and OpenType fonts. Despite the name suggesting Type 0, this
method creates the full composite font structure (Type 0 wrapper +
CIDFont descendant) needed for Unicode text support. The font file is
embedded in the document, and PDFBox automatically subsets it on save.

\subsection{Type 0 / CIDFont (Composite
Fonts)}\label{_type_0_cidfont_composite_fonts}

Type 0 fonts --- also called composite fonts --- are the mechanism PDF
uses to support large character sets (CJK scripts, Arabic, Devanagari,
and any script requiring more than 256 glyphs).

A Type 0 font consists of two linked dictionaries:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{The Type 0 font dictionary} (\texttt{/Subtype\ /Type0}): the
  "wrapper" that the content stream references. It contains a
  \texttt{/DescendantFonts} array pointing to the CIDFont dictionary and
  a \texttt{/Encoding} entry specifying the CMap that maps character
  codes to CIDs (Character IDs).
\item
  \textbf{The CIDFont dictionary} (\texttt{/Subtype\ /CIDFontType0} or
  \texttt{/CIDFontType2}): the actual font data. CIDFontType0 wraps a
  PostScript CID-keyed font; CIDFontType2 wraps a TrueType font. In
  practice, CIDFontType2 (TrueType-based) is overwhelmingly more common.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{verbatim}
% Type 0 composite font structure in a PDF
10 0 obj
<< /Type /Font
   /Subtype /Type0
   /BaseFont /ABCDEF+DejaVuSans         
   /Encoding /Identity-H                 
   /DescendantFonts [11 0 R]            
   /ToUnicode 12 0 R                     
>>
endobj

11 0 obj
<< /Type /Font
   /Subtype /CIDFontType2               
   /BaseFont /ABCDEF+DejaVuSans
   /CIDSystemInfo << /Registry (Adobe) /Ordering (Identity) /Supplement 0 >>
   /FontDescriptor 13 0 R
   /W [3 [278] 36 [722] 68 [556 500]]  
>>
endobj
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The \texttt{/BaseFont} name includes a subset prefix
  (ABCDEF+) --- we'll explain this below
\item
  \texttt{/Identity-H} means character codes map directly to CIDs
  (horizontal writing mode)
\item
  The descendant CIDFont dictionary
\item
  The \texttt{/ToUnicode} CMap enables text extraction --- critical for
  accessibility and search
\item
  CIDFontType2: the actual glyph data comes from an embedded TrueType
  font
\item
  The \texttt{/W} (Widths) array defines per-glyph widths for precise
  text positioning
\end{itemize}

When PDFBox's \texttt{PDType0Font.load()} embeds a TrueType font, it
creates exactly this structure. You don't need to build it manually, but
understanding it helps when debugging font issues in existing PDFs.

\section{Font Embedding vs.
Referencing}\label{_font_embedding_vs_referencing}

There are two ways a PDF can use a font: \textbf{embed} it (include the
font program inside the PDF file) or \textbf{reference} it (include only
the font name and metrics, relying on the viewer to provide the actual
glyphs).

\subsection{Referenced Fonts}\label{_referenced_fonts}

A referenced font includes the font dictionary and metrics but
\emph{not} the font program. The viewer must find a matching font on the
system or use a fallback. The Standard 14 fonts are the canonical
example --- they're designed to be used without embedding because every
viewer is expected to have compatible substitutes.

Referenced non-standard fonts are a recipe for rendering failures. If
your PDF references "CompanyBrandFont-Regular" without embedding it, the
viewer will substitute whatever it can find --- usually a generic serif
or sans-serif font --- and the text will look wrong. Character widths
will differ, glyphs may be missing, and text positioning will be off.

For signature appearances, \textbf{never reference non-standard fonts}.
The signing server has the font file; the recipient's PDF viewer
probably doesn't.

\subsection{Embedded Fonts}\label{_embedded_fonts}

An embedded font includes the complete font program (or a subset of it)
as a stream inside the PDF. The viewer uses the embedded data to render
glyphs, regardless of what fonts are installed on the system.

Embedding guarantees consistent rendering across all viewers and all
platforms. It increases the PDF file size --- a full TrueType font can
be several megabytes --- but subsetting (described below) reduces the
impact dramatically.

The font program is stored as a stream referenced by the
\texttt{/FontDescriptor} dictionary:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}@{}}
\caption{Font embedding streams by font type --- ISO 32000-2:2020 Table
123}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Font Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
FontDescriptor Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Stream Contents
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Font Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
FontDescriptor Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Stream Contents
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Type 1 & \texttt{/FontFile} & PFB (Printer Font Binary) data --- the
Type 1 font program \\
TrueType & \texttt{/FontFile2} & Complete TrueType font file
(\texttt{.ttf}) \\
CFF / OpenType & \texttt{/FontFile3} & CFF (Compact Font Format) data,
or OpenType font program. The stream's \texttt{/Subtype} distinguishes:
\texttt{/CIDFontType0C} for CID-keyed CFF, \texttt{/Type1C} for simple
CFF, \texttt{/OpenType} for OpenType. \\
\end{longtable}

The distinction between \texttt{/FontFile}, \texttt{/FontFile2}, and
\texttt{/FontFile3} is not just metadata --- the stream contents are in
fundamentally different binary formats. A viewer that tries to parse a
CFF stream as TrueType (or vice versa) will get corrupted glyph outlines
or crash. Getting the key wrong is a common source of font rendering
failures in programmatically generated PDFs.

\section{Font Subsetting}\label{_font_subsetting}

Embedding a full font file --- especially a CJK font with tens of
thousands of glyphs --- adds significant bulk to the PDF. \textbf{Font
subsetting} solves this by embedding only the glyphs actually used in
the document.

A typical signature appearance uses fewer than 100 distinct glyphs (the
signer's name, a date, a reason string). Subsetting a 2 MB TrueType font
down to just those glyphs produces a subset of perhaps 10-30 KB.

\subsection{The Subset Prefix
Convention}\label{_the_subset_prefix_convention}

ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 9.6.4 defines a naming convention for subsetted
fonts: the \texttt{/BaseFont} name is prefixed with six uppercase
letters followed by a plus sign.

\begin{verbatim}
/BaseFont /ABCDEF+DejaVuSans
\end{verbatim}

The prefix "ABCDEF+" indicates that this is a subset --- it does not
contain all glyphs from the original DejaVuSans font. The six letters
are arbitrary (PDFBox generates them randomly); their only purpose is to
make the subset's \texttt{/BaseFont} name unique within the document,
preventing different subsets of the same font from being confused with
each other.

A font \emph{without} the subset prefix:

\begin{verbatim}
/BaseFont /DejaVuSans
\end{verbatim}

This indicates the full font is embedded (or the font is referenced
without embedding).

When debugging font issues, the subset prefix is your first clue. If you
see \texttt{/ABCDEF+Helvetica}, the font is subsetted. If a glyph is
missing from the rendered output, it may not have been included in the
subset --- meaning the glyph was not used (or not \emph{known} to be
used) when the subset was created. This is a common bug when signature
text is modified after the font was subsetted.

\subsection{How PDFBox Handles
Subsetting}\label{_how_pdfbox_handles_subsetting}

PDFBox 3.0.0 performs automatic font subsetting when saving a document.
When you load a TrueType font with \texttt{PDType0Font.load()}, PDFBox
tracks which glyphs are used during text drawing operations and includes
only those glyphs in the final embedded subset.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// PDFBox tracks glyph usage automatically}
\NormalTok{PDFont font }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ PDType0Font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{load}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{document}\OperatorTok{,} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{File}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"DejaVuSans.ttf"}\OperatorTok{));}

\CommentTok{// When we write text, PDFBox notes which glyphs are needed}
\ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{OutputStream}\NormalTok{ os }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ formXObject}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getStream}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{createOutputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \CommentTok{// ... write content stream using this font ...}
\OperatorTok{\}}

\CommentTok{// On save, PDFBox subsets the font to include only used glyphs}
\NormalTok{document}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{save}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{outputStream}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

There is one catch. If you write raw content stream operators directly
(as we must for signature appearance Form XObjects in PDFBox 3.x, since
\texttt{PDPageContentStream} does not support Form XObjects), PDFBox may
not automatically track glyph usage. In this case, you must encode the
text through the font to ensure the glyph tracking is triggered:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Ensure glyph tracking when writing raw content streams}
\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ encodedText }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{encode}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Digitally signed by: Muller"}\OperatorTok{);}
\CommentTok{// Use encodedText in your content stream, not the raw string}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

If you bypass PDFBox's text encoding and write raw character codes
directly into the content stream, the subset may not include all
required glyphs. The result: some characters render as \texttt{.notdef}
(the missing glyph symbol, typically a small rectangle) in viewers. This
is one of the most common causes of broken signature appearances.

\section{Encoding}\label{_encoding}

Encoding is the mapping between \textbf{character codes} (the byte
values in the content stream) and \textbf{glyphs} (the visual shapes the
viewer renders). Getting encoding right is essential for correct text
rendering, and getting it wrong is the most common cause of garbled or
missing text in PDF documents.

\subsection{Simple Font Encoding
(Single-Byte)}\label{_simple_font_encoding_single_byte}

For simple fonts (Type 1 and simple TrueType), each character code is a
single byte (0-255). The encoding maps each byte value to a glyph name,
and the font program maps glyph names to outlines.

PDF defines two built-in encodings:

\textbf{WinAnsiEncoding} (ISO 32000-2:2020 Table D.1): Corresponds
closely to Windows-1252. Covers Western European characters: basic
Latin, accented characters (a, e, u, n, etc.), and common symbols. This
is the default for most PDF generators and the encoding used by the
Standard 14 fonts.

\textbf{MacRomanEncoding} (ISO 32000-2:2020 Table D.2): The classic
Macintosh character set. Rarely used in modern PDFs, but you'll
encounter it in documents created by older Mac applications.

A font dictionary can also specify a \textbf{custom encoding} via the
\texttt{/Encoding} key, using a \texttt{/Differences} array that
overrides individual code-to-glyph mappings:

\begin{verbatim}
/Encoding <<
  /Type /Encoding
  /BaseEncoding /WinAnsiEncoding
  /Differences [
    128 /Euro             
    160 /space /exclamdown /cent /sterling
  ]
>>
\end{verbatim}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Maps byte value 128 to the Euro sign glyph, overriding the
  WinAnsiEncoding mapping
\end{itemize}

\subsection{The 256-Glyph Wall}\label{_the_256_glyph_wall}

Single-byte encoding means a simple font can address at most 256 glyphs.
This is fine for English and most Western European languages, but it's a
hard wall for:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{CJK languages}: Chinese alone requires thousands of glyphs
\item
  \textbf{Arabic and Hebrew}: need contextual glyph shaping (the same
  character looks different depending on position in a word)
\item
  \textbf{Devanagari, Thai, Georgian}: scripts with large glyph
  inventories
\item
  \textbf{Mixed-script text}: a signature appearance showing a name in
  Cyrillic with a date in Latin
\end{itemize}

For any of these, you need a composite font (Type 0 + CIDFont) with a
multi-byte encoding.

\subsection{Composite Font Encoding}\label{_composite_font_encoding}

Composite fonts use a \textbf{CMap} (Character Map) to map multi-byte
character codes to CIDs. The most common CMap in modern PDFs is
\texttt{/Identity-H}, which maps character codes directly to CIDs as a
simple identity function --- code 0 maps to CID 0, code 1 to CID 1, and
so on.

With \texttt{/Identity-H}, the character codes in the content stream are
raw CID values encoded as two-byte big-endian integers. This gives
access to the full 65,536 CID range, more than enough for any single
font.

The downside of \texttt{/Identity-H} is that the character codes have no
inherent semantic meaning --- they're just glyph indices. Without
additional mapping information, a viewer cannot extract the text content
or determine what Unicode characters the glyphs represent. That's where
the \texttt{/ToUnicode} CMap comes in.

\section{The ToUnicode CMap}\label{_the_tounicode_cmap}

The \texttt{/ToUnicode} entry in a font dictionary maps character codes
to Unicode values. It is not used for rendering --- the viewer uses the
font's glyph outlines for that. Instead, it is used for \textbf{text
extraction}: copy-paste, search, and accessibility.

For signature appearances, the \texttt{/ToUnicode} CMap matters because:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Accessibility tools} read the \texttt{/ToUnicode} CMap to
  extract text from the appearance stream. A signature appearance
  without \texttt{/ToUnicode} mappings will appear as empty or garbled
  text to screen readers.
\item
  \textbf{PDF validators} (including veraPDF) check for the presence of
  \texttt{/ToUnicode} CMaps as a compliance requirement for PDF/A. If
  your signed PDFs must be PDF/A-compliant, the fonts in your signature
  appearance must have proper \texttt{/ToUnicode} entries.
\item
  \textbf{Text search} in the document depends on \texttt{/ToUnicode}.
  Without it, searching for the signer's name will not find the text in
  the signature appearance.
\end{enumerate}

A \texttt{/ToUnicode} CMap is a PDF stream containing a CMap program in
PostScript-like syntax:

\begin{verbatim}
/CIDInit /ProcSet findresource begin
12 dict begin
begincmap
/CIDSystemInfo
  << /Registry (Adobe) /Ordering (UCS) /Supplement 0 >> def
/CMapName /Adobe-Identity-UCS def
/CMapType 2 def
1 begincodespacerange
<0000> <FFFF>
endcodespacerange
5 beginbfchar
<0024> <0044>        % CID 36 -> U+0044 (D)
<004C> <0069>        % CID 76 -> U+0069 (i)
<004E> <0067>        % CID 78 -> U+0067 (g)
endbfchar
endcmap
CMapName currentdict /CMap defineresource pop
end
end
\end{verbatim}

PDFBox generates the \texttt{/ToUnicode} CMap automatically when you use
\texttt{PDType0Font.load()} and write text through the font's encoding
methods. You do not need to create it manually --- but understanding the
structure helps when you're debugging a PDF where text extraction
returns garbled results.

In PDFBox 3.x, the method \texttt{PDFont.getToUnicodeCMap()} is
\emph{not} publicly accessible. To inspect the \texttt{/ToUnicode} CMap
of a font, you must access the COS dictionary directly:
\texttt{font.getCOSObject().getDictionaryObject(COSName.TO\_UNICODE)}.
This is a common stumbling block when writing font inspection or
diagnostic tools.

\section{Font Descriptors}\label{_font_descriptors}

Every embedded font (and many referenced fonts) has a \textbf{font
descriptor} dictionary (\texttt{/Type\ /FontDescriptor}) that provides
metrics and metadata about the font. The font descriptor is
essential --- it tells the viewer how to render the font even before it
looks at the glyph outlines.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.6250}}@{}}
\caption{Key entries in the font descriptor --- ISO 32000-2:2020 Table
122}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Purpose
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Purpose
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/FontName} & name & The PostScript name of the font (e.g.,
\texttt{/DejaVuSans}) \\
\texttt{/Flags} & integer & Bit field describing the font: FixedPitch
(bit 1), Serif (bit 2), Symbolic (bit 3), Script (bit 4), Nonsymbolic
(bit 6), Italic (bit 7), AllCap (bit 17), SmallCap (bit 18), ForceBold
(bit 19) \\
\texttt{/FontBBox} & array & The bounding box of all glyphs:
\texttt{{[}llx\ lly\ urx\ ury{]}} in glyph coordinate space \\
\texttt{/ItalicAngle} & number & Angle of the italic slant (0 for
upright fonts) \\
\texttt{/Ascent} & number & The maximum height above the baseline \\
\texttt{/Descent} & number & The maximum depth below the baseline
(typically negative) \\
\texttt{/CapHeight} & number & The height of capital letters \\
\texttt{/StemV} & number & The dominant stem width (vertical
strokes) --- affects rendering weight \\
\texttt{/FontFile} & stream & Type 1 font program (PFB format) \\
\texttt{/FontFile2} & stream & TrueType font program \\
\texttt{/FontFile3} & stream & CFF or OpenType font program (with
\texttt{/Subtype} to distinguish) \\
\end{longtable}

A font descriptor with all metrics but \emph{no} font file stream
describes a \textbf{referenced} font --- the viewer must find a
substitute. If the descriptor includes a font file stream, the font is
\textbf{embedded}. Checking whether \texttt{/FontFile},
\texttt{/FontFile2}, or \texttt{/FontFile3} exists is the definitive way
to determine if a font is embedded.

\section{PDFBox Font Handling in
3.x}\label{_pdfbox_font_handling_in_3_x}

PDFBox 3.0.0 made several breaking changes to its font API compared to
2.x. Understanding these changes is essential for writing correct font
handling code.

\subsection{Loading Fonts}\label{_loading_fonts}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Standard 14 font (no embedding needed)}
\NormalTok{PDFont helvetica }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FontName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HELVETICA}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// Embed a TrueType font from a file}
\NormalTok{PDFont dejaVu }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ PDType0Font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{load}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{document}\OperatorTok{,} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{File}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"DejaVuSans.ttf"}\OperatorTok{));}

\CommentTok{// Embed a TrueType font from an InputStream}
\ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{InputStream}\NormalTok{ is }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{getClass}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getResourceAsStream}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"/fonts/NotoSans{-}Regular.ttf"}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    PDFont notoSans }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ PDType0Font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{load}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{document}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ is}\OperatorTok{);}
\OperatorTok{\}}

\CommentTok{// Embed a TrueType font WITHOUT subsetting}
\CommentTok{// (include all glyphs {-}{-} larger file, but no risk of missing glyphs)}
\NormalTok{PDFont fullFont }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ PDType0Font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{load}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{document}\OperatorTok{,} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{File}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"DejaVuSans.ttf"}\OperatorTok{),} \KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The \texttt{false} parameter to \texttt{PDType0Font.load()} disables
subsetting. This is rarely needed, but can be useful during debugging to
rule out subsetting as the cause of missing glyphs.

\subsection{The getEncoding() Removal}\label{_the_getencoding_removal}

PDFBox 2.x provided a \texttt{PDFont.getEncoding()} method that returned
the font's encoding object. PDFBox 3.x \textbf{removed} this method.

If you need to inspect a font's encoding in PDFBox 3.0.0, you must
access the COS dictionary directly:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// PDFBox 2.x {-}{-} no longer works}
\CommentTok{// Encoding encoding = font.getEncoding();}

\CommentTok{// PDFBox 3.x {-}{-} access the COS dictionary}
\NormalTok{COSBase encodingObj }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSObject}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getDictionaryObject}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{ENCODING}\OperatorTok{);}

\ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{encodingObj }\KeywordTok{instanceof}\NormalTok{ COSName encodingName}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \CommentTok{// Named encoding: /WinAnsiEncoding, /MacRomanEncoding, etc.}
\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Font uses named encoding: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ encodingName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getName}\OperatorTok{());}
\OperatorTok{\}} \ControlFlowTok{else} \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{encodingObj }\KeywordTok{instanceof}\NormalTok{ COSDictionary encodingDict}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \CommentTok{// Custom encoding with /Differences array}
\NormalTok{    COSArray differences }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ encodingDict}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDictionaryObject}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Differences"}\OperatorTok{),}\NormalTok{ COSArray}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{class}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{differences }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Font has custom encoding with \{\} difference entries"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                differences}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{());}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Inspecting Font Embedding
Programmatically}\label{_inspecting_font_embedding_programmatically}

A common diagnostic task is checking whether a font in a PDF is
embedded. Here's a complete example:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{boolean} \FunctionTok{isFontEmbedded}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDFont font}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    PDFontDescriptor descriptor }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFontDescriptor}\OperatorTok{();}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{descriptor }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \CommentTok{// Standard 14 fonts may not have a descriptor}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ descriptor}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFontFile}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}
        \OperatorTok{||}\NormalTok{ descriptor}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFontFile2}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}
        \OperatorTok{||}\NormalTok{ descriptor}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFontFile3}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Why Signature Text Shows as Squares or
Blanks}\label{_why_signature_text_shows_as_squares_or_blanks}

This section addresses the most common font-related failures in
signature appearances, what causes them, and how to fix them.

\subsection{Missing Font}\label{_missing_font}

\textbf{Symptom}: The signature block is empty, or shows substituted
text in a different font than intended.

\textbf{Cause}: The signature appearance references a font that is not
embedded and not available on the viewer's system.

\textbf{Fix}: Always embed fonts used in signature appearances. For
basic ASCII text, the Standard 14 fonts are a safe choice because
viewers provide substitutes. For anything else, embed a TrueType font
using \texttt{PDType0Font.load()}.

\subsection{Wrong Encoding}\label{_wrong_encoding}

\textbf{Symptom}: Some characters render correctly, but accented
characters (e, u, n) show as wrong characters or \texttt{.notdef}
squares.

\textbf{Cause}: The text contains characters outside the font's encoding
range. WinAnsiEncoding covers codes 32-255 corresponding to
Windows-1252, which includes most Western European accented characters.
But characters outside this range --- em-dash at certain positions,
certain ligatures, or any non-Latin script --- will fail.

\textbf{Fix}: Use a composite font (Type 0) with \texttt{/Identity-H}
encoding and an embedded TrueType font. This provides full Unicode
coverage. In PDFBox: use \texttt{PDType0Font.load()} instead of the
Standard 14 fonts.

\subsection{Unembedded Font with Non-Standard
Glyphs}\label{_unembedded_font_with_non_standard_glyphs}

\textbf{Symptom}: Text renders as squares or blank space on some viewers
but works on others.

\textbf{Cause}: The font is referenced but not embedded. Viewers that
happen to have a compatible font installed render the text correctly;
viewers without the font substitute a generic font that may not have the
required glyphs.

\textbf{Fix}: Embed the font.

\subsection{Subset Missing Glyphs}\label{_subset_missing_glyphs}

\textbf{Symptom}: Most text renders correctly, but specific characters
are missing.

\textbf{Cause}: The font was subsetted, but the subset does not include
all required glyphs. This typically happens when text is written to the
content stream \emph{after} the font subsetting decision was made, or
when glyph usage tracking was bypassed.

\textbf{Fix}: Ensure all text is written through PDFBox's font encoding
methods \emph{before} saving. If the problem persists, try loading the
font with subsetting disabled
(\texttt{PDType0Font.load(document,\ file,\ false)}) to confirm
subsetting is the cause.

When writing raw content stream operators for a Form XObject (which
PDFBox 3.x requires for signature appearances), make sure to encode text
through the font \emph{before} writing the encoded bytes to the stream.
Skipping this step means PDFBox won't know which glyphs are needed and
may produce a subset that's missing characters.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// WRONG: writing raw text bypasses glyph tracking}
\NormalTok{os}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"(John Muller) Tj"}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getBytes}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{US\_ASCII}\OperatorTok{));}

\CommentTok{// RIGHT: encode through the font first}
\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ encoded }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{encode}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"John Muller"}\OperatorTok{);}
\CommentTok{// Then write the encoded bytes as a hex string in the content stream}
\NormalTok{os}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{((}\StringTok{"\textless{}"} \OperatorTok{+} \FunctionTok{bytesToHex}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{encoded}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"\textgreater{} Tj"}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{getBytes}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{US\_ASCII}\OperatorTok{));}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Font Licensing and the fsType
Flag}\label{_font_licensing_and_the_fstype_flag}

TrueType and OpenType fonts contain a licensing flag called
\textbf{fsType} in the OS/2 table. This flag indicates what the font
vendor permits in terms of embedding and editing:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5714}}@{}}
\caption{fsType embedding permission bits}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Bits
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Meaning
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Bits
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Meaning
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
0x0000 & Installable & No restrictions. The font may be embedded and
used freely. \\
0x0002 & Restricted & The font must not be embedded in documents.
Period. \\
0x0004 & Preview \& Print & The font may be embedded in read-only
documents. The recipient can view and print but should not edit. \\
0x0008 & Editable & The font may be embedded, and the recipient may edit
the document. \\
\end{longtable}

Most free and open-source fonts (DejaVu, Noto, Liberation) have fsType
0x0000 (Installable). Many commercial fonts (fonts you pay for) have
restrictive fsType values.

PDF generators typically respect the fsType flag --- PDFBox will emit a
warning (but not throw an exception) when embedding a font with
restrictive fsType. Some tools refuse to embed the font entirely.

For signature appearances:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Use fonts with fsType 0x0000} (Installable) to avoid any
  licensing complications.
\item
  The \textbf{Google Noto font family} (\texttt{Noto\ Sans},
  \texttt{Noto\ Serif}, \texttt{Noto\ Sans\ CJK}) covers virtually every
  script in Unicode and is Apache-2.0 licensed with fsType 0x0000. It is
  the best general-purpose choice for internationalized signature text.
\item
  \textbf{DejaVu Sans} covers Latin, Greek, Cyrillic, Armenian,
  Georgian, and many symbols. Free, well-tested, and widely deployed.
\item
  \textbf{Liberation Sans/Serif/Mono} are metrics-compatible with
  Arial/Times New Roman/Courier New. Licensed under SIL Open Font
  License.
\end{itemize}

Even if your PDF generator embeds a font with restrictive fsType, the
PDF viewer will render it. The fsType flag is a licensing statement, not
a technical enforcement mechanism. But distributing PDFs with improperly
embedded commercial fonts exposes your organization to licensing claims.
For server-side signing operations that produce thousands of PDFs, use
only fonts whose license explicitly permits embedding.

\section{Practical Advice for Signature
Appearances}\label{_practical_advice_for_signature_appearances}

Drawing from everything in this chapter, here is the decision framework
for choosing and configuring fonts in signature appearance streams:

\subsection{For ASCII-Only Signatures}\label{_for_ascii_only_signatures}

If your signer names, dates, and reasons are guaranteed to be ASCII only
(a-z, A-Z, 0-9, basic punctuation):

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Simple, reliable, zero{-}cost approach}
\NormalTok{PDFont font }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FontName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HELVETICA}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This works everywhere. No embedding needed, no licensing concerns, no
subsetting complications. The text will render in Helvetica or a
metrics-compatible substitute on every viewer.

\subsection{For International
Signatures}\label{_for_international_signatures}

If signer names may contain non-ASCII characters (accented Latin,
Cyrillic, CJK, Arabic, or anything beyond Windows-1252):

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Embed a Unicode{-}capable font}
\ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{InputStream}\NormalTok{ is }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{getClass}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getResourceAsStream}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"/fonts/NotoSans{-}Regular.ttf"}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    PDFont font }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ PDType0Font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{load}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{document}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ is}\OperatorTok{);}
    \CommentTok{// Now you can write any Unicode character the font covers}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Bundle the font file with your application (in
\texttt{src/main/resources/fonts/} for a Maven project). Do not rely on
system-installed fonts --- the signing server may not have the same
fonts as your development machine.

\subsection{Font Size Guidelines}\label{_font_size_guidelines}

Signature appearance text is typically rendered at small sizes (8-12
points) in a rectangle that's often only 150-300 points wide. Keep these
guidelines in mind:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{10pt} is a good default for signer name and date
\item
  \textbf{8pt} works for secondary information (reason, location,
  certificate details)
\item
  \textbf{Never go below 7pt} --- some viewers render small text poorly,
  and the text becomes unreadable when printed
\item
  \textbf{Calculate text width} before rendering to ensure it fits the
  signature rectangle; truncation or overflow beyond the bounding box
  results in clipped text that viewers may or may not display
\end{itemize}

\section{Debugging Font Issues}\label{_debugging_font_issues}

When a signature appearance renders incorrectly, the following
diagnostic steps will identify the root cause.

\subsection{Step 1: Identify the Font}\label{_step_1_identify_the_font}

Open the PDF in a tool that shows the raw object structure (PDFalyzer,
QPDF, or \texttt{PDFDebugger} from PDFBox's tools module). Navigate to
the signature field's widget annotation, then to \texttt{/AP\ /N} (the
normal appearance stream), then to \texttt{/Resources\ /Font}. Note the
font name, subtype, and whether a font descriptor with a font file
stream exists.

\subsection{Step 2: Check Embedding}\label{_step_2_check_embedding}

Look for \texttt{/FontFile}, \texttt{/FontFile2}, or \texttt{/FontFile3}
in the font descriptor. If none exists, the font is not
embedded --- that's likely the problem.

\subsection{Step 3: Check Encoding}\label{_step_3_check_encoding}

For simple fonts, check the \texttt{/Encoding} entry. Is it
\texttt{/WinAnsiEncoding}? If the problematic characters are outside
Windows-1252, the encoding is the cause.

For composite fonts, check the \texttt{/Encoding} entry on the Type 0
dictionary. It should be \texttt{/Identity-H} for a properly structured
modern PDF.

\subsection{Step 4: Check the ToUnicode
CMap}\label{_step_4_check_the_tounicode_cmap}

If the text \emph{renders} correctly but copy-paste or search returns
garbled results, the \texttt{/ToUnicode} CMap is missing or incorrect.

\subsection{Step 5: veraPDF
Validation}\label{_step_5_verapdf_validation}

veraPDF provides font-specific validation rules that catch a wide range
of font issues:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Run veraPDF validation focusing on font issues}
\ExtensionTok{verapdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}format}\NormalTok{ text }\AttributeTok{{-}{-}flavour}\NormalTok{ 2b input.pdf }\DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}\&}\DecValTok{1} \KeywordTok{|} \FunctionTok{grep} \AttributeTok{{-}i}\NormalTok{ font}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

veraPDF checks for embedded fonts (required for PDF/A), valid
\texttt{/ToUnicode} mappings, correct font descriptor entries, and
consistent glyph widths. Its validation messages are often the fastest
way to identify exactly what's wrong with a font configuration.

\subsection{Step 6: PDFBox Font
Inspection}\label{_step_6_pdfbox_font_inspection}

For programmatic inspection, PDFBox can enumerate all fonts in a page or
appearance stream:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{inspectAppearanceFonts}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDAnnotationWidget widget}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    PDAppearanceStream appearance }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ widget}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAppearance}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getNormalAppearance}\OperatorTok{();}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{appearance }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{warn}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Signature widget has no normal appearance stream"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

\NormalTok{    PDResources resources }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ appearance}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getResources}\OperatorTok{();}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{resources }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{warn}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Appearance stream has no resources"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName fontName }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ resources}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFontNames}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        PDFont font }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ resources}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFont}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{fontName}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Font /\{\}: type=\{\}, baseFont=\{\}, embedded=\{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                fontName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getName}\OperatorTok{(),}
\NormalTok{                font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getClass}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getSimpleName}\OperatorTok{(),}
\NormalTok{                font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getName}\OperatorTok{(),}
                \FunctionTok{isFontEmbedded}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{font}\OperatorTok{));}

        \CommentTok{// Check for ToUnicode CMap}
\NormalTok{        COSBase toUnicode }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSObject}\OperatorTok{()}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDictionaryObject}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{TO\_UNICODE}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"  ToUnicode CMap: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                toUnicode }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null} \OperatorTok{?} \StringTok{"present"} \OperatorTok{:} \StringTok{"MISSING"}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

When debugging font issues in production, always capture the raw bytes
of the appearance stream and the font dictionary's COS structure. The
visual symptom (squares, blanks, wrong characters) tells you
\emph{something} is wrong; the COS structure tells you \emph{what}. A
tool that shows both the rendered appearance and the underlying object
tree --- the way PDFalyzer does --- saves enormous debugging time
compared to flipping between a viewer and a hex editor.

\section{Summary}\label{_summary_9}

Fonts in PDF are a deceptively deep topic. For digital signatures
specifically, the key takeaways are:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Embed your fonts.} The Standard 14 fonts are safe for ASCII
  text; everything else must be embedded.
\item
  \textbf{Use composite fonts (Type 0) for non-ASCII text.} Simple fonts
  are limited to 256 glyphs.
\item
  \textbf{Encode text through the font API} to ensure glyph tracking for
  subsetting works correctly.
\item
  \textbf{Use freely licensed fonts} (Noto Sans, DejaVu, Liberation) to
  avoid fsType complications.
\item
  \textbf{Test in multiple viewers} --- what works in Adobe Acrobat may
  fail in pdf.js or LibreOffice.
\item
  \textbf{When debugging, look at the COS structure}, not just the
  rendered output. The font dictionary, font descriptor, encoding, and
  \texttt{/ToUnicode} CMap tell the full story.
\end{itemize}

The next chapter --- Chapter 12 --- moves from the document
infrastructure we've built up across Part II into the core subject of
this book: the architecture of PDF digital signatures themselves.

\chapter{PDF Digital Signatures}\label{_pdf_digital_signatures}

\section{Signature Architecture}\label{ch12}

\begin{quote}
This chapter dissects the internal architecture of PDF digital
signatures as specified in ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8. We examine every
dictionary, every key, and every byte --- from the AcroForm field tree
down to the raw CMS \texttt{SignedData} blob embedded in
\texttt{/Contents}.

But more importantly, we talk about what actually matters when you're
staring at a broken signature at 2 AM, wondering why Adobe says
"invalid" while your code says "fine."
\end{quote}

\subsection{Overview: What a PDF Signature Actually
Is}\label{_overview_what_a_pdf_signature_actually_is}

Here's the thing most people get wrong about PDF signatures: they think
of them as something \emph{attached to} a document, like a wax seal on
an envelope. They're not.

A PDF digital signature is a set of \textbf{PDF objects embedded within
the document itself}. The signature \emph{is part of the document}. This
seemingly minor architectural choice has profound consequences --- it's
why PDF signatures are simultaneously powerful and maddening, why they
survive copy operations but break on re-saves, and why you can have a
perfectly valid signature on a document that's been modified after
signing (yes, really).

The four pieces of a PDF signature:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  A \textbf{signature field} (\texttt{PDSignatureField}) in the AcroForm
  field tree --- the form field that "holds" the signature
\item
  A \textbf{signature dictionary} (\texttt{/Type\ /Sig}) --- the actual
  cryptographic payload and metadata
\item
  A \textbf{widget annotation} --- the visual rectangle on a page (may
  be zero-size for invisible signatures)
\item
  An \textbf{appearance stream} (\texttt{/AP}) --- what the viewer
  renders in the signature rectangle
\end{enumerate}

The signature field is a form field like any other (\texttt{/FT\ /Sig}).
The signature dictionary is the \emph{value} of that field
(\texttt{/V}). An unsigned field has \texttt{/V} absent or null --- the
field exists but has no signature value yet.

Think of it like an empty checkbox on a paper form. The box is there,
waiting to be checked. A document author can place signature fields in a
PDF template, and signers fill them in later --- each one independently,
at different times, possibly on different continents.

\textbf{When you encounter a PDF with signature problems, start here.}
Open the document in a COS-level viewer (like PDFalyzer, or even a hex
editor) and check:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Does the AcroForm exist? (Some PDFs have signature fields floating
  without an AcroForm --- instant headache.)
\item
  Does \texttt{/SigFlags} include bit 2 (AppendOnly)? If not, any editor
  can destroy all signatures with a simple save.
\item
  Is the \texttt{/V} entry present on the signature field? If absent,
  the field is unsigned.
\item
  Does the widget actually have a page reference? Some tools create
  orphaned widgets.
\end{enumerate}

This five-minute checklist saves hours of debugging.

\subsection{Signature Fields in the AcroForm
Tree}\label{ch12-field-tree}

\begin{quote}
A signature field (field type Sig) shall represent a digital signature
that may or may not have a signature value.

---  ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.7.4.5
\end{quote}

Signature fields live in the document's interactive form structure:

\subsubsection{The SigFlags Trap}\label{_the_sigflags_trap}

The \texttt{/SigFlags} entry is one of those things that's easy to
overlook and devastating to get wrong.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1818}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.0909}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.0909}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1818}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4545}}@{}}
\caption{SigFlags --- ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.7.2, Table
225}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Bit
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Effect
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Bit
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Effect
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{SignaturesExist} & 1 & Required & §12.7.2 & Tells the viewer
"this document contains at least one signature field." Conforming
readers \textbf{shall not} allow saving in a way that invalidates
existing signatures. \\
\texttt{AppendOnly} & 2 & Required & §12.7.4.5 & The document
\textbf{shall} be saved using incremental updates only. This is the
mechanism that protects existing signatures from being destroyed by a
full rewrite. \\
\end{longtable}

Setting \texttt{/SigFlags} to 3 (both bits) is mandatory when a document
contains signatures. If bit 2 is missing, a naive PDF editor may do a
full save and destroy all signatures. Many real-world PDFs get this
wrong.

Here's what happens in practice: someone creates a PDF with LibreOffice,
adds a signature field, signs it --- and LibreOffice doesn't set bit 2.
The document gets emailed to a colleague who opens it in some random PDF
editor to add an annotation, hits save, and the editor does a full
rewrite. Every signature is now invalid. The colleague has no idea what
happened. The signer gets an angry phone call.

\textbf{The fix is always the same:} if you're building a tool that
creates signature fields, set \texttt{/SigFlags} to 3 the moment any
field has a value. And if you're building a tool that edits PDFs, check
for \texttt{/SigFlags} bit 2 before saving --- if it's set, you
\emph{must} use incremental save. If your tool doesn't support
incremental save\ldots\hspace{0pt} well, you need to support it now.
Welcome to PDF.

\subsubsection{Field Dictionary
Structure}\label{_field_dictionary_structure}

A signature field's COS dictionary looks like this. Every key matters,
and I'll explain why:

Here is the complete COS dictionary of a typical signature field. In
most PDFs, the field and its widget annotation are merged into a single
object --- the spec (ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.5.6.19) allows splitting them,
but few tools bother:

\begin{verbatim}
<<
  /Type /Annot
  /Subtype /Widget
  /FT /Sig
  /T (Signature1)
  /V 42 0 R
  /Rect [100 500 300 550]
  /AP << /N 43 0 R >>
  /P 7 0 R
  /Lock <<
    /Type /SigFieldLock
    /Action /All
  >>
  /SV <<
    /Type /SV
    /Filter /Adobe.PPKLite
    /SubFilter [/adbe.pkcs7.detached /ETSI.CAdES.detached]
    /DigestMethod [/SHA256 /SHA384 /SHA512]
