    /Reasons [(Approved)(Reviewed)]
    /MDP << /P 2 >>
  >>
>>
\end{verbatim}

The keys fall into three groups: annotation/widget keys (how it looks
and where it lives), signature field keys (what it is and what it points
to), and sub-dictionaries that constrain signing behavior.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.0833}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4167}}@{}}
\caption{Annotation/Widget Keys --- ISO 32000-2:2020
§12.5.6.19}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/Type} & name & Optional & §12.5.6 & Always \texttt{/Annot} for
the merged field+widget. \\
\texttt{/Subtype} & name & Required & §12.5.6 & Always \texttt{/Widget}
--- this is a widget annotation. \\
\texttt{/Rect} & rectangle & Required & §12.5.6 & Widget rectangle in
page coordinates: \texttt{{[}llx\ lly\ urx\ ury{]}}. If
\texttt{{[}0\ 0\ 0\ 0{]}}, the signature is invisible. \\
\texttt{/P} & reference & Optional & §12.5.6 & Page reference --- which
page this widget is on. Some poorly generated PDFs omit this, and then
viewers have to guess. Don't be that tool. \\
\texttt{/AP} & dictionary & Optional & §12.5.6.19 & Appearance stream
dictionary. \texttt{/N} is the normal appearance --- what the viewer
draws. Without this, many viewers show a generic placeholder. Adobe is
particularly opinionated about appearances. \\
\end{longtable}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\caption{Signature Field Keys --- ISO 32000-2:2020
§12.7.5.5}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/FT} & name & Required & §12.7.4.1 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Field type --- \texttt{/Sig} marks this as a signature field.
Non-negotiable. If you see \texttt{/FT\ /Tx} on something that's
supposed to be a signature, someone made a mistake.} \\
\texttt{/T} & text string & Required & §12.7.4.1 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Partial field name --- used to identify the field programmatically. This
is how your code finds "the signature field called Signature1."} \\
\texttt{/V} & reference & Optional & §12.7.5.5 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Reference to the \textbf{signature dictionary} (\texttt{/Type\ /Sig})
--- the cryptographic payload. If absent, the field is unsigned. When
present, this is the key entry that connects the form field to the
actual signature. The signature dictionary is detailed in
\hyperref[ch12-sig-dict]{The Signature Dictionary ()}.} \\
\end{longtable}

The remaining two keys --- \texttt{/Lock} and \texttt{/SV} --- are
sub-dictionaries that deserve their own tables.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\caption{Lock Dictionary (\protect\texttt{/Type\ /SigFieldLock}) --- ISO
32000-2:2020 §12.7.5.5, Table 237}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/Type} & name & Optional & §12.7.5.5 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Always \texttt{/SigFieldLock}.} \\
\texttt{/Action} & name & Required & §12.7.5.5, Table 237 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
What to lock after signing: \texttt{/All} (lock everything),
\texttt{/Include} (lock fields listed in \texttt{/Fields}), or
\texttt{/Exclude} (lock everything \emph{except} fields listed in
\texttt{/Fields}). This is how approval signatures can lock specific
fields --- see \hyperref[ch14]{???}.} \\
\texttt{/Fields} & array & Conditional & §12.7.5.5, Table 237 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Array of field names to include or exclude from locking. Required when
\texttt{/Action} is \texttt{/Include} or \texttt{/Exclude}.} \\
\end{longtable}

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Seed Value Dictionary (\texttt{/SV}): The
Unsung
Hero}{Seed Value Dictionary (/SV): The Unsung Hero}}\label{_seed_value_dictionary_sv_the_unsung_hero}

\begin{quote}
The seed value dictionary provides constraint information that shall be
used at the time the signature is applied.

---  ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.7.5.5
\end{quote}

The seed value dictionary is one of the most powerful and underused
features in the entire PDF signature specification. Most people don't
even know it exists, which is a shame, because it solves a problem that
plagues every organization deploying PDF signatures at scale:
\textbf{how do you ensure signers use the right algorithm, the right
format, and the right handler?}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\caption{Seed Value Dictionary (\protect\texttt{/Type\ /SV}) --- ISO
32000-2:2020 §12.7.5.5, Table 238}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/Filter} & name & Optional & §12.7.5.5, Table 238 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Required signature handler (e.g., \texttt{/Adobe.PPKLite}). Use this to
ensure your documents work with specific software.} \\
\texttt{/SubFilter} & array of names & Optional & §12.7.5.5, Table
238 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Acceptable signature formats. Set this to
\texttt{{[}/ETSI.CAdES.detached{]}} if you need PAdES compliance. The
signer's software \textbf{must} use one of these formats.} \\
\texttt{/DigestMethod} & array of names & Optional & §12.7.5.5, Table
238 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Acceptable hash algorithms. Set this to
\texttt{{[}/SHA256\ /SHA384\ /SHA512{]}} --- and for the love of all
that is holy, \textbf{do not include \texttt{/SHA1}}. It's 2026. SHA-1
has been broken since 2017.} \\
\texttt{/Reasons} & array of strings & Optional & §12.7.5.5, Table
238 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Pre-defined reason strings the signer must choose from. Great for audit
trails --- instead of "asdf" in the reason field, signers pick from
"Approved", "Reviewed", "Authorized".} \\
\texttt{/LegalAttestation} & array of strings & Optional & §12.7.5.5,
Table 238 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Legal attestation strings --- rarely used, but available if you need
legal boilerplate attached to the signature.} \\
\texttt{/Ff} & integer & Optional & §12.7.5.5, Table 238 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Flags indicating which seed values are \textbf{required} (vs.
recommended). If bit 1 is set on a constraint, it's mandatory --- the
signer's software must enforce it, not just suggest it.} \\
\end{longtable}

The \texttt{/MDP} and \texttt{/TimeStamp} entries are sub-dictionaries
with their own structure:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}

  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.0833}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4167}}@{}}

\texttt{/P} & integer & Required & §12.7.5.5 & The DocMDP permission
level this signature must enforce: 1 (no changes), 2 (form fills +
signatures), 3 (form fills + signatures + annotations). This is how a
template author says "whoever signs this field must also lock the
document." \\
\end{longtable}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.0833}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4167}}@{}}
\caption{Seed Value TimeStamp Dictionary (\protect\texttt{/TimeStamp})
--- ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.7.5.5}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/URL} & ASCII string & Required & §12.7.5.5 & URL of the RFC
3161 timestamp server. \\
\texttt{/Ff} & integer & Optional & §12.7.5.5 & If bit 1 is set,
timestamping is required (not just recommended). If you need PAdES-B-T,
set this. The signer's software will contact the TSA automatically. \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{Practical advice for template authors:}

If you're creating PDF templates that others will sign, use seed values
aggressively. Don't trust signers to pick the right options --- they
won't. A financial services company once sent us PDFs where half the
signatures used SHA-1 because some signers had old software and nobody
constrained the algorithm. Those signatures were technically valid but
would fail compliance audits.

Set \texttt{/DigestMethod} to \texttt{{[}/SHA256{]}}, set
\texttt{/SubFilter} to \texttt{{[}/ETSI.CAdES.detached{]}}, and set the
\texttt{/Ff} flags to make them mandatory. Your future self will thank
you.

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{The Signature Dictionary
(\texttt{/Type\ /Sig})}{The Signature Dictionary (/Type /Sig)}}\label{ch12-sig-dict}

The signature dictionary is the heart of a PDF digital signature. It's
referenced from the field's \texttt{/V} entry, and it's where the real
action is. Everything else --- the field, the widget, the appearance ---
is just scaffolding. This dictionary is where the cryptographic proof
lives.

Here is the complete structure of a typical signature dictionary ---
every key you'll encounter in production:

\begin{verbatim}
<<
  /Type /Sig
  /Filter /Adobe.PPKLite
  /SubFilter /ETSI.CAdES.detached
  /ByteRange [0 15000 15500 34500]
  /Contents <308206A7...00000000>
  /M (D:20260306120000+00'00')
  /Name (John Doe)
  /Location (Vienna, Austria)
  /Reason (Approved)
  /ContactInfo (john@example.com)
  /Reference [
    <<
      /Type /SigRef
      /TransformMethod /DocMDP
      /TransformParams <<
        /Type /TransformParams
        /P 2
        /V /1.2
      >>
      /DigestMethod /SHA256
    >>
  ]
  /Prop_Build <<
    /App << /Name /PDFalyzer >>
    /Filter << /Name /Adobe.PPKLite >>
  >>
>>
\end{verbatim}

The table below dissects each key. The core cryptographic keys come
first, then metadata, then sub-dictionaries.

\subsubsection{Core Signature Keys}\label{_core_signature_keys}

These five keys define the cryptographic signature itself. Get any of
them wrong and the signature is broken.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4286}}@{}}
\caption{Core keys of the Signature Dictionary
(\protect\texttt{/Type\ /Sig}) --- ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8.1, Table
255}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/Type} & name & Optional & §12.8.1 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Always \texttt{/Sig} when present. Identifies this dictionary as a
signature value. Optional per the spec, but always include it ---
defensive parsers rely on it.} \\
\texttt{/Filter} & name & Required & §12.8.1, Table 255 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
The signature handler name. In theory, this identifies which software
can process the signature. In practice, it's always
\texttt{/Adobe.PPKLite}. Always. Even when the signing software has
nothing to do with Adobe. The name stuck because Adobe defined the
original signature spec in 1999 and everyone just\ldots\hspace{0pt} went
with it.} \\
\texttt{/SubFilter} & name & Required & §12.8.1, Table 255 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
The actual encoding format of the signature value. \emph{This} is the
key that determines how \texttt{/Contents} is structured --- see
\hyperref[ch12-subfilter]{SubFilter Values: A Brief History of Getting
It Right} for the full history and current recommendation.} \\
\texttt{/ByteRange} & array & Required & §12.8.1, Table 255 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Four integers \texttt{{[}offset1\ length1\ offset2\ length2{]}} defining
which bytes of the file are signed. The gap between the two ranges is
where \texttt{/Contents} lives. The most important array in the entire
dictionary --- a deep dive is in \hyperref[ch13]{???}.} \\
\texttt{/Contents} & string & Required & §12.8.1, Table 255 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Hex-encoded CMS \texttt{SignedData} blob (RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic
Message Syntax (CMS) §5), padded with zeros. This is the actual
cryptographic signature. Everything else in this dictionary is metadata.
This is the payload. See \hyperref[ch12-contents]{The Value: CMS
SignedData}.} \\
\end{longtable}

\subsubsection{Metadata Keys}\label{_metadata_keys}

These keys carry informational data about the signing event. They are
part of the signed bytes and cannot be changed after signing --- but
they are set by whoever controls the signing process, \textbf{not
verified against any external authority}.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.0833}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4167}}@{}}
\caption{Metadata keys of the Signature Dictionary --- ISO 32000-2:2020
§12.8.1, Table 255}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/M} & date string & Optional & §12.8.1 & Signing time in PDF
date format. \textbf{Do not trust this.} It's the signer's local clock.
Use the CMS timestamp attribute for authoritative time. \\
\texttt{/Name} & text string & Optional & §12.8.1 & Signer's name.
\textbf{Do not trust this either.} It's free text. The authoritative
name is in the X.509 certificate inside \texttt{/Contents}. \\
\texttt{/Location} & text string & Optional & §12.8.1 & Claimed signing
location. Informational, unverified. \\
\texttt{/Reason} & text string & Optional & §12.8.1 & Claimed signing
reason (e.g., "Approved", "Reviewed"). Informational, unverified. \\
\texttt{/ContactInfo} & text string & Optional & §12.8.1 & Contact info.
Informational, optional, you see the pattern. \\
\end{longtable}

The \texttt{/M}, \texttt{/Name}, \texttt{/Location}, \texttt{/Reason},
and \texttt{/ContactInfo} fields are \textbf{unauthenticated metadata}.
Yes, they're part of the signed bytes. Yes, they can't be changed after
signing. But they're set \emph{before} signing, by whoever controls the
signing process. If a malicious signer puts "Barack Obama" in the
\texttt{/Name} field and signs with a self-signed certificate, the
signature will be structurally valid. \textbf{The only trustworthy
identity information is the X.509 certificate in \texttt{/Contents}.}
Never display \texttt{/Name} to users as if it were verified identity.
This mistake is shockingly common in PDF viewer implementations.

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Signature Reference Dictionary
(\texttt{/Reference})}{Signature Reference Dictionary (/Reference)}}\label{_signature_reference_dictionary_reference}

The \texttt{/Reference} entry is an array of \textbf{signature reference
dictionaries} (\texttt{/Type\ /SigRef}) that define transform methods
--- rules about what can and cannot change after signing. This is the
mechanism behind certification signatures and field-level locking
(covered in depth in \hyperref[ch14]{???}).

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\caption{Signature Reference Dictionary
(\protect\texttt{/Type\ /SigRef}) --- ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8.1, Table
256}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/Type} & name & Optional & §12.8.1, Table 256 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Always \texttt{/SigRef}.} \\
\texttt{/TransformMethod} & name & Required & §12.8.1, Table 256 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
The transform method: \texttt{/DocMDP} (document modification detection
and prevention), \texttt{/FieldMDP} (field-level locking), or
\texttt{/UR} (usage rights --- deprecated in PDF 2.0). DocMDP is by far
the most common. See \hyperref[ch14]{???}.} \\
\texttt{/TransformParams} & dictionary & Optional & §12.8.1, Table
256 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Parameters for the transform method. For DocMDP, this contains
\texttt{/P} (permission level: 1 = no changes, 2 = form fills +
signatures, 3 = form fills + signatures + annotations) and \texttt{/V}
(version).} \\
\texttt{/DigestMethod} & name & Required & §12.8.1, Table 256 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Hash algorithm for this reference (e.g., \texttt{/SHA256}). Must match
the algorithm used in the CMS structure.} \\
\end{longtable}

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Build Properties Dictionary
(\texttt{/Prop\_Build})}{Build Properties Dictionary (/Prop\_Build)}}\label{_build_properties_dictionary_prop_build}

The \texttt{/Prop\_Build} dictionary identifies the software that
created the signature. It's optional and purely informational --- but
surprisingly useful for debugging.

  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4167}}@{}}

Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/App} & dictionary & Optional & §12.8.1 & Application that
created the signature. Contains \texttt{/Name} (e.g.,
\texttt{/PDFalyzer}, \texttt{/AdobeAcrobatDC}). \\
\texttt{/Filter} & dictionary & Optional & §12.8.1 & Signature handler
info. Contains \texttt{/Name} (e.g., \texttt{/Adobe.PPKLite}). \\
\texttt{/PubSec} & dictionary & Optional & §12.8.1 & Public security
handler info. Rarely populated. \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{The \texttt{/Prop\_Build} dictionary is your friend when
debugging.}

When a signature doesn't validate, the first thing I check is
\texttt{/Prop\_Build}. It tells you which software created the
signature. If it says \texttt{/iText} with a version from 2015, you know
you might be dealing with an old CMS structure. If it says
\texttt{/Adobe\ Acrobat} and the signature is invalid, something is
genuinely wrong because Adobe's own tool should produce signatures that
Adobe validates. If \texttt{/Prop\_Build} is missing entirely, the
signing software was lazy --- which tells you something about the
quality of the rest of the signature, too.

\subsubsection{SubFilter Values: A Brief History of Getting It
Right}\label{ch12-subfilter}

The \texttt{/SubFilter} value determines how the \texttt{/Contents}
bytes are structured. Getting this right is the difference between
"signature works everywhere" and "signature works in Adobe but nowhere
else" (or vice versa).

The history of SubFilter values is essentially the history of PDF
signatures trying --- and sometimes failing --- to keep up with
cryptographic best practices:

The first two SubFilter values are deprecated --- they use SHA-1, which
has been broken for collision resistance since the SHAttered attack of
February 2017. You will encounter them in legacy documents, but you
should never use them for new signatures.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\caption{Deprecated SubFilter Values (do not use for new
signatures)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
SubFilter
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Standard
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
SubFilter
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Standard
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/adbe.x509.rsa\_sha1} & PDF 1.3+ & Deprecated & ISO 32000-2:2020
§12.8.3.2 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
\textbf{Do not use.} Raw RSA signature with the certificate in a
separate \texttt{/Cert} key instead of inside \texttt{/Contents}. No CMS
wrapper. SHA-1 only. This was PDF's first attempt at digital signatures
in 1999. It made sense at the time. It doesn't anymore.} \\
\texttt{/adbe.pkcs7.sha1} & PDF 1.3+ & Deprecated & ISO 32000-2:2020
§12.8.3.4 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
\textbf{Do not use.} CMS \texttt{SignedData} where the SHA-1 digest of
the byte range is \emph{encapsulated} in the CMS. This is the worst of
both worlds: SHA-1 (broken) and encapsulated content (unnecessary
complexity). Nobody misses this format.} \\
\end{longtable}

The following three values are the current standard. For new signatures,
use \texttt{/ETSI.CAdES.detached} unless you have a specific reason not
to.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\caption{Current SubFilter Values}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
SubFilter
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Standard
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
SubFilter
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Standard
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/adbe.pkcs7.detached} & PDF 1.3+ & Current & ISO 32000-2:2020
§12.8.3.3 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
CMS \texttt{SignedData} with detached content. The workhorse format ---
widely supported since PDF 1.3. The signed bytes are the PDF byte range,
not embedded in CMS. If you need maximum compatibility with older
software, this is your format.} \\
\texttt{/ETSI.CAdES.detached} & PDF 2.0 / PAdES & Modern & ETSI EN 319
142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04) §5.1 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
CMS \texttt{SignedData} with mandatory CAdES signed attributes,
specifically the \texttt{signing-certificate-v2} attribute. Required for
PAdES-B-B and higher. \textbf{This is the modern standard. Use this.}
The CMS structure is identical to \texttt{/adbe.pkcs7.detached} at the
container level --- the difference is one signed attribute
(\textasciitilde50 bytes).} \\
\texttt{/ETSI.RFC3161} & PDF 2.0 / PAdES & Modern & ETSI EN 319 142-1
V1.1.1 (2016-04) §5.4 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
RFC 3161 timestamp token --- not a signature, but a document-level
timestamp. Used for PAdES-B-T and long-term archival. You'll encounter
these when dealing with LTV (Long-Term Validation).} \\
\end{longtable}

Here's a trap that catches people all the time: Adobe Acrobat and most
viewers accept \emph{all} SubFilter values for backward compatibility.
Your signature will "work" in Acrobat regardless of which SubFilter you
use. But \textbf{only} \texttt{/ETSI.CAdES.detached} is valid for
PAdES-compliant signatures under eIDAS. If you need legal standing in
the EU --- and increasingly, that's everyone --- this is the only
acceptable value. Don't let "it works in Acrobat" lull you into using
\texttt{/adbe.pkcs7.detached} for new signatures. Acrobat is not the
arbiter of legal validity; the eIDAS regulation is.

\textbf{The compatibility question everyone asks:}

"Can I use \texttt{/ETSI.CAdES.detached} if my users have old PDF
readers?"

Yes. The CMS structure is identical to \texttt{/adbe.pkcs7.detached} at
the container level. Old readers that don't understand the SubFilter
name will typically fall back to parsing the \texttt{/Contents} as
generic CMS/PKCS\#7 --- and it will work. The difference is one signed
attribute (\texttt{signing-certificate-v2}), which old readers simply
ignore.

The only scenario where it breaks: truly ancient software (pre-2010)
that strictly checks the SubFilter name against a hardcoded whitelist
and rejects unknown values. If your users are running PDF readers from
2008, they have bigger problems than SubFilter compatibility.

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{The \texttt{/Contents} Value: CMS
SignedData}{The /Contents Value: CMS SignedData}}\label{ch12-contents}

The \texttt{/Contents} entry is a \textbf{hex-encoded byte string}
containing a DER-encoded CMS \texttt{SignedData} structure (RFC 5652 ---
Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) §5).

This is where the actual cryptographic signature lives. Everything else
in the signature dictionary is metadata. This is the payload.

\subsubsection{Why Hex-Encoded?}\label{_why_hex_encoded}

PDF strings can be literal \texttt{(…\hspace{0pt})} or hex
\texttt{\textless{}…\hspace{0pt}\textgreater{}}. The hex form is used
because CMS blobs contain arbitrary binary data (DER-encoded ASN.1), and
literal strings would need extensive escaping for bytes like
\texttt{\textbackslash{}(}, \texttt{\textbackslash{})},
\texttt{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}. Hex encoding doubles the size
but is safe and unambiguous.

A typical \texttt{/Contents} value looks like
\texttt{\textless{}308206A7…\hspace{0pt}several\ thousand\ hex\ characters…\hspace{0pt}0000000000\textgreater{}}.
The leading bytes (\texttt{3082…\hspace{0pt}}) are the DER-encoded ASN.1
SEQUENCE tag, and the trailing zeros are padding. Which brings us to one
of the more elegant hacks in the PDF specification:

\subsubsection{Pre-allocation: The Chicken-and-Egg
Problem}\label{_pre_allocation_the_chicken_and_egg_problem}

Here's the fundamental problem with PDF signatures:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  The signature covers the file's bytes (including the signature
  dictionary itself)
\item
  But the signature dictionary must contain the CMS blob
\item
  But the CMS blob can only be computed \emph{after} you know which
  bytes to sign
\item
  But you can't know which bytes to sign until the dictionary is written
\end{enumerate}

The solution is a trick that every PDF signing implementation uses:
\textbf{pre-allocate} a large enough \texttt{/Contents} value filled
with zeros, write the file, \emph{then} overwrite the zeros with the
actual CMS bytes:

The pre-allocated size must be estimated accurately, and this is where
things go wrong in the real world:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Too small} --- the CMS blob doesn't fit --- signing fails.
  You'll get an exception from PDFBox and no output file. This is the
  most common "my signing suddenly broke" bug, usually triggered by
  adding a certificate chain or a timestamp that makes the CMS blob
  bigger than expected.
\item
  \textbf{Too large} --- wasted bytes, but the signature is valid. A few
  KB of zeros at the end of the file never hurt anyone.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Always err on the side of too large.} Here are sensible
defaults:

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Scenario
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Hex Size
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Bytes
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Basic signature (no chain, no timestamp) & \texttt{0x2500} (PDFBox
default) & \textasciitilde9 KB \\
Signature with certificate chain & \texttt{0x4000} & \textasciitilde16
KB \\
PAdES-B-T (with RFC 3161 timestamp) & \texttt{0x6000} &
\textasciitilde24 KB \\
PAdES-B-LT (embedded OCSP + CRLs) & \texttt{0x18000} & \textasciitilde96
KB \\
PAdES-B-LTA (archival, large CRL) & \texttt{0x28000} &
\textasciitilde160 KB \\
\end{longtable}
}

I've seen production systems fail because someone used the PDFBox
default (\texttt{0x2500}) for PAdES-B-T signatures. The first 500
signatures worked fine because the TSA responses were small. Then the
TSA renewed its certificate with a longer chain, the responses grew by 2
KB, and every signing operation started failing on a Friday afternoon.
Set it to \texttt{0x6000} minimum and forget about it.

\subsubsection{CMS SignedData: The Cryptographic
Core}\label{_cms_signeddata_the_cryptographic_core}

The CMS blob inside \texttt{/Contents} follows RFC 5652 ---
Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) §5. If you've worked with S/MIME
email signatures or code signing, this structure will look familiar ---
it's the same \texttt{SignedData} format used across the entire PKI
ecosystem:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{SignedData ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    version            CMSVersion,              }
\NormalTok{    digestAlgorithms   DigestAlgorithmIdentifiers,  }
\NormalTok{    encapContentInfo   EncapsulatedContentInfo,     }
\NormalTok{    certificates   [}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{] IMPLICIT CertificateSet }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}\NormalTok{, }
\NormalTok{    crls           [}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] IMPLICIT RevocationInfoChoices }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}\NormalTok{, }
\NormalTok{    signerInfos        SignerInfos              }
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Version: 1 for basic CMS, 3 if attribute certificates are present
\item
  Set of digest algorithms used (typically just \texttt{\{SHA-256\}})
\item
  Content type: \texttt{id-data} (1.2.840.113549.1.7.1) with
  \textbf{absent} content --- this is what "detached" means. The signed
  data (the PDF byte range) is not inside the CMS structure. It's
  outside, in the PDF file itself.
\item
  Certificates --- the signer's certificate and optionally the full
  chain (intermediates, possibly root). \textbf{Always include the full
  chain.} I know it makes the blob bigger. I know storage is a concern.
  Do it anyway. A signature without a chain is a signature that can only
  be validated by someone who already has all your certificates. That's
  almost nobody.
\item
  CRLs --- rarely embedded here in the modern PAdES world. In PAdES-B-LT
  and later, revocation data goes in the DSS dictionary instead.
\item
  Signer information --- the actual signature value and signed
  attributes. This is where the cryptography lives.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{SignerInfo: Where the Cryptography
Lives}\label{_signerinfo_where_the_cryptography_lives}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{SignerInfo ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    version                  CMSVersion,}
\NormalTok{    sid                      SignerIdentifier,    }
\NormalTok{    digestAlgorithm          DigestAlgorithmIdentifier, }
\NormalTok{    signedAttrs          [}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{] IMPLICIT SignedAttributes }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}\NormalTok{, }
\NormalTok{    signatureAlgorithm       SignatureAlgorithmIdentifier, }
\NormalTok{    signature                SignatureValue,      }
\NormalTok{    unsignedAttrs        [}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] IMPLICIT UnsignedAttributes }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL} 
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Identifies the signer's certificate --- either by issuer DN + serial
  number, or by subject key identifier. Both work. Issuer+serial is more
  traditional; subject key identifier is more robust when certificates
  are reissued.
\item
  Hash algorithm used to digest the PDF byte range. SHA-256 is the
  minimum acceptable. SHA-384 or SHA-512 if you're paranoid or work in
  defense.
\item
  \textbf{Signed attributes} --- authenticated metadata. See the
  critical attributes table below. These are \emph{signed}, meaning any
  modification invalidates the signature.
\item
  Signature algorithm (e.g., SHA256withRSA, SHA256withECDSA). Note: the
  \emph{signature} is computed over the DER-encoded signed attributes,
  not directly over the PDF bytes. This is a CMS subtlety that trips up
  many implementers.
\item
  The actual cryptographic signature bytes.
\item
  \textbf{Unsigned attributes} --- unauthenticated extensions.
  Timestamps go here.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{The Signed Attributes That
Matter}\label{_the_signed_attributes_that_matter}

The first two attributes are required by CMS whenever signed attributes
are present. The remaining attributes add PAdES compliance and
defense-in-depth.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1111}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\caption{Required CMS Signed Attributes --- RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic
Message Syntax (CMS) §11}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Attribute
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Attribute
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{content-type} & 1.2.840.113549.1.9.3 & Required & RFC 5652 ---
Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) §11.1 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Must be \texttt{id-data} (1.2.840.113549.1.7.1). Required by CMS when
signed attributes are present. Forgetting this is a classic
implementation bug --- the signature appears valid in some tools but
fails in others.} \\
\texttt{message-digest} & 1.2.840.113549.1.9.4 & Required & RFC 5652 ---
Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) §11.2 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
SHA-256 hash of the PDF byte range. This is what's actually verified
during validation. If this hash doesn't match the file bytes, the
signature is invalid. Full stop.} \\
\end{longtable}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1111}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\caption{PAdES/CAdES Signed Attributes}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Attribute
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Attribute
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{signing-certificate-v2} & 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.47 & Required
(PAdES) & RFC 5035 §3 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Hash of the signer's certificate (SHA-256). \textbf{Required for PAdES /
CAdES.} This single attribute prevents certificate substitution attacks
--- an attacker can't swap in a different certificate without breaking
the signature. It's \textasciitilde50 bytes. There is no reason not to
include it.} \\
\texttt{signing-time} & 1.2.840.113549.1.9.5 & Optional & RFC 5652 ---
Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) §11.3 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Claimed signing time. Not authoritative --- it's whatever the signer's
clock said. For authoritative time, you need a timestamp (see unsigned
attributes). Optional, but almost everyone includes it.} \\
\texttt{algorithm-protection} & 1.2.840.113549.1.9.52 & Recommended &
RFC 6211 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Protects against algorithm substitution attacks by binding the digest
and signature algorithms into the signed attributes. Good hygiene to
include.} \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{The \texttt{signing-certificate-v2} attribute is what makes the
difference between \texttt{/adbe.pkcs7.detached} and
\texttt{/ETSI.CAdES.detached}.}

Without it: plain CMS (PKCS\#7) --- vulnerable to certificate
substitution attacks. With it: CAdES --- the signer's certificate is
bound to the signature by hash.

This single attribute is the gateway to PAdES compliance. It's about 50
bytes in the CMS structure. There is no reason not to include it, even
if you're using \texttt{/adbe.pkcs7.detached} as your SubFilter.

Certificate substitution attacks are not theoretical. In 2019,
researchers at Ruhr University Bochum demonstrated practical attacks
against multiple PDF viewers that exploited the absence of certificate
binding. The fix was trivially simple: check
\texttt{signing-certificate-v2}. Several major viewers had to be
patched.

\paragraph{Unsigned Attributes: The Afterthought That Became
Essential}\label{_unsigned_attributes_the_afterthought_that_became_essential}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1111}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\caption{Unsigned Attributes --- RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message
Syntax (CMS) §11}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Attribute
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Attribute
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{signature-time-stamp} & 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.14 & Required
(PAdES-B-T) & RFC 3161 --- Internet X.509 PKI Time-Stamp Protocol (TSP),
ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04) §5.3 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
RFC 3161 timestamp token over the signature value. Provides
authoritative proof of signing time from a trusted TSA. This is the only
way to prove \emph{when} a signature was made. The timestamp token has
its own signature from the TSA, making it self-authenticating --- which
is why it's safe as an unsigned attribute.} \\
\texttt{certificate-values} & 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.23 & Optional &
RFC 5126 §5.6.1 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Full certificate chain values. Used in CAdES-X-Long. In the PAdES world,
these typically go in the DSS dictionary instead (see
\hyperref[ch18]{???}).} \\
\texttt{revocation-values} & 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.24 & Optional & RFC
5126 §5.6.1 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
OCSP responses and CRLs. Used in CAdES-X-Long. In PAdES, these go in the
DSS dictionary instead.} \\
\end{longtable}

The timestamp attribute deserves special attention. Unsigned attributes
are, by definition, not protected by the signature --- they can be added
after signing without invalidating anything. This is \emph{by design}. A
timestamp proves that the signature existed at a certain time, and it's
intentionally added after the signature is created (because you need the
signature value to timestamp it).

This is also why you should never put anything security-critical in
unsigned attributes that isn't self-authenticating (like a timestamp
token, which has its own signature from the TSA).

\subsection{The Signing Process: Step by
Step}\label{ch12-signing-process}

The process of creating a PDF digital signature is intricate because the
signature must cover bytes that include the signature dictionary itself
--- a chicken-and-egg problem solved by the \texttt{/Contents}
pre-allocation we discussed above.

Here's the full sequence, and understanding it is essential for
debugging:

\subsubsection{Step-by-Step Detail}\label{_step_by_step_detail}

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.0909}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.9091}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Step
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Detail
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\textbf{1} & \textbf{Create or locate the signature field.} If the
document already has an unsigned field (\texttt{/FT\ /Sig} with no
\texttt{/V}), use it. Otherwise, create a new field in the AcroForm
\texttt{/Fields} array with a widget annotation on the desired page.
\emph{Practical note:} if the document was prepared by someone else (a
template), always prefer using existing unsigned fields. Creating new
fields in a document that already has signatures is technically possible
via incremental save, but it makes some validators nervous. \\
\textbf{2} & \textbf{Build the signature dictionary} with placeholder
values. \texttt{/ByteRange} is set to \texttt{{[}0\ 0\ 0\ 0{]}} (will be
overwritten). \texttt{/Contents} is filled with \texttt{00} bytes at the
estimated maximum size. All other keys (\texttt{/Filter},
\texttt{/SubFilter}, \texttt{/M}, \texttt{/Name}, \texttt{/Reason},
\texttt{/Reference}) are set to their final values \emph{now} --- they
become part of the signed bytes and can't change later. \\
\textbf{3} & \textbf{Write the PDF using incremental save.} The new
objects (signature dict, updated field, updated xref) are appended after
the existing \texttt{\%\%EOF}. This is critical --- a full rewrite would
invalidate any existing signatures. \emph{This is non-negotiable.} Even
if the document has no prior signatures, use incremental save. It's the
only safe habit. \\
\textbf{4} & \textbf{Compute actual byte offsets.} After writing, the
exact byte positions of the \texttt{/Contents} hex string are known.
Update \texttt{/ByteRange} to
\texttt{{[}0,\ contentsStart,\ contentsEnd,\ fileLength\ -\ contentsEnd{]}}.
The two segments cover everything \emph{except} the \texttt{/Contents}
value itself. \\
\textbf{5} & \textbf{Read the signed bytes.} Concatenate
\texttt{file{[}0\ ..\ contentsStart-1{]}} and
\texttt{file{[}contentsEnd\ ..\ fileLength-1{]}}. These are the exact
bytes the signature covers --- everything except the \texttt{/Contents}
hex value. \emph{Debug tip:} if you suspect byte range issues, dump
these bytes to a file and hash them manually. Compare with the
\texttt{message-digest} attribute in the CMS. \\
\textbf{6} & \textbf{Hash the signed bytes.} Compute SHA-256 (or
SHA-384/512) over the concatenated byte ranges. This hash becomes the
\texttt{message-digest} signed attribute in the CMS structure. \\
\textbf{7--8} & \textbf{Sign the hash.} The private key (RSA or ECDSA)
signs the DER-encoded signed attributes (which include the hash). This
may happen locally or via a remote HSM/signing service. In the remote
case, only the hash leaves your system --- the private key never does.
More on remote signing in \hyperref[ch22]{???}. \\
\textbf{9} & \textbf{Build the CMS \texttt{SignedData} structure.}
Include: digest algorithm, signer certificate, certificate chain, signed
attributes (content-type, message-digest, signing-certificate-v2),
signature value. DER-encode the result. \\
\textbf{10} & \textbf{Write CMS bytes into \texttt{/Contents}.}
Hex-encode the DER bytes and write them at the exact byte offset of the
\texttt{/Contents} value in the file. Remaining space is left as
\texttt{00} padding. No other bytes in the file are modified. \\
\end{longtable}
}

Step 10 is a \textbf{raw byte write} --- not a PDF object update. The
file is opened as a random-access binary stream, the cursor is
positioned at the \texttt{/Contents} hex string's start offset, and the
hex chars are overwritten in place. If the CMS blob is larger than the
pre-allocated space, signing fails. If even a single byte is written at
the wrong offset, the signature is invalid and the PDF may be corrupted.
This is why you don't implement PDF signing from scratch --- you use
PDFBox or a similar library that handles the byte-level bookkeeping for
you.

\subsection{Incremental Save: The Foundation of
Multi-Signature}\label{ch12-incremental}

Every signature operation uses \textbf{incremental save} (ISO
32000-2:2020 §7.5.6). The original file bytes are never modified --- new
objects are appended after the existing \texttt{\%\%EOF}.

This is the mechanism that makes multiple independent signatures
possible. Without incremental save, adding a second signature would
modify the bytes covered by the first signature, invalidating it. With
incremental save, the first signature's bytes remain untouched --- the
second signature's data is simply appended at the end.

Each signature's \texttt{/ByteRange} covers everything up to and
including its own incremental update --- but \textbf{not} the updates
that come after:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Signature 1's byte range:
  \texttt{{[}0,\ …\hspace{0pt},\ …\hspace{0pt},\ M{]}} --- covers
  original + update \#1
\item
  Signature 2's byte range:
  \texttt{{[}0,\ …\hspace{0pt},\ …\hspace{0pt},\ P{]}} --- covers
  original + update \#1 + update \#2
\end{itemize}

This is why Signature 1 remains valid after Signature 2 is added:
Signature 2's bytes are \textbf{appended after} the range that Signature
1 covers. From Signature 1's perspective, those bytes are "trailing" ---
present in the file but outside the signed range.

\textbf{"The file has been modified since signing" --- don't panic.}

This is the single most common false alarm in PDF signature validation.
A validator detects trailing bytes after a signature's byte range and
flags it as "modified." But in the vast majority of cases, those
trailing bytes are simply a later incremental update --- another
signature, a form fill, or an annotation.

\textbf{What to check:}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Is the trailing data a valid incremental update? (Look for xref table
  + trailer + \%\%EOF.)
\item
  Does it correspond to an expected operation? (Another signature, a
  form field being filled.)
\item
  Did the byte range change? (The original signature's bytes at their
  original offsets should be byte-for-byte identical.)
\end{enumerate}

If the answers are yes, yes, and no --- the signature is fine. The
"modification" is just the normal operation of PDF's append-only
architecture.

\textbf{When to actually worry:}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Trailing bytes that aren't a valid incremental update --- raw data
  appended to the file
\item
  An incremental update that modifies objects covered by an existing
  signature in unexpected ways (e.g., page content changes)
\item
  Multiple \%\%EOF markers where the trailing section isn't a proper PDF
  update
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Widget Annotations and Page Placement}\label{ch12-widget}

Every signature field has an associated widget annotation (ISO
32000-2:2020 §12.5.6.19) that defines its visual presence on a page.

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{The \texttt{/Rect} Coordinate
System}{The /Rect Coordinate System}}\label{_the_rect_coordinate_system}

The widget's \texttt{/Rect} array specifies
\texttt{{[}llx,\ lly,\ urx,\ ury{]}} in \textbf{default user space}
coordinates:

PDF coordinates have \textbf{origin at lower-left} with Y increasing
upward. This is the opposite of screen coordinates (origin top-left, Y
increasing downward). This will bite you. It bites everyone. You will
write the conversion wrong the first time. Here it is, so you can copy
it:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// viewport = page.getViewport(\{ scale: scale \})}
\KeywordTok{var}\NormalTok{ left }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ bbox[}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{] }\OperatorTok{*}\NormalTok{ scale}\OperatorTok{;}                              
\KeywordTok{var}\NormalTok{ top  }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ viewport}\OperatorTok{.}\AttributeTok{height} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ (bbox[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ bbox[}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{]) }\OperatorTok{*}\NormalTok{ scale}\OperatorTok{;} 
\KeywordTok{var}\NormalTok{ width }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ bbox[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{] }\OperatorTok{*}\NormalTok{ scale}\OperatorTok{;}                             
\KeywordTok{var}\NormalTok{ height }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ bbox[}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{] }\OperatorTok{*}\NormalTok{ scale}\OperatorTok{;}                            
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  X translates directly (both coordinate systems have X increasing
  rightward)
\item
  Y must be flipped: PDF Y=0 is bottom, screen Y=0 is top. Note the
  \texttt{bbox{[}1{]}\ +\ bbox{[}3{]}} --- that's lower-left-Y plus
  height, giving the top edge in PDF space, which maps to the top edge
  in screen space.
\item
  Width scales linearly
\item
  Height scales linearly
\end{itemize}

Where \texttt{bbox} is
\texttt{{[}lowerLeftX,\ lowerLeftY,\ width,\ height{]}} as extracted by
PDFBox's \texttt{PDRectangle}. Note that PDFBox gives you
\texttt{{[}x,\ y,\ width,\ height{]}} while the raw PDF \texttt{/Rect}
is \texttt{{[}llx,\ lly,\ urx,\ ury{]}} --- PDFBox does the conversion
for you. Don't convert twice.

\subsubsection{Invisible Signatures}\label{_invisible_signatures}

A signature can be \textbf{invisible} --- having no visual appearance on
the page:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Zero-size rect:} \texttt{/Rect\ {[}0\ 0\ 0\ 0{]}} --- the
  widget exists but has no area
\item
  \textbf{No widget at all:} The field has no annotation entry (valid
  per spec, but some viewers complain)
\end{itemize}

Invisible signatures are common for:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Certification signatures --- the document author certifies the
  document without cluttering the layout
\item
  Machine-generated signatures --- automated workflows where nobody's
  looking at the PDF
\item
  Counter-signatures --- additional approval signatures in complex
  chains where visual space is limited
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Should your signature be visible or invisible?}

For approval signatures intended for human signers: make them visible.
Users expect to \emph{see} their signature on the page. A visible
signature provides a visual anchor --- "I signed here" --- and helps in
audit scenarios.

For certification signatures and automated workflows: invisible is
usually fine. The certification status is shown in the viewer's
signature panel, not on the page.

For machine-to-machine: always invisible. No human is looking at the
page. Adding a visual appearance just wastes space and processing time.

One exception: if you're in a regulated industry where auditors review
printed copies of signed PDFs (yes, this still happens in 2026), visible
signatures are essential because auditors can't check digital signatures
on paper. In that case, include the signer's name, the signing time, and
ideally a note like "Digitally signed --- verify electronically."

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{AcroForm \texttt{/SigFlags}: Document-Level
Signature
Metadata}{AcroForm /SigFlags: Document-Level Signature Metadata}}\label{ch12-pdfsigflags}

\begin{quote}
If the document contains signature fields, the AcroForm dictionary shall
include a SigFlags entry.

---  ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.7.2, Table 225
\end{quote}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4286}}@{}}
\caption{SigFlags Bit Values --- ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.7.2, Table
225}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Bit
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Bit
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{SignaturesExist} & 1 (value 1) & Required & §12.7.2, Table
225 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
At least one signature field has a value. Readers should present
signature-specific UI (e.g., the blue bar at the top of Adobe Acrobat).
Must be set whenever any signature field contains a \texttt{/V}
entry.} \\
\texttt{AppendOnly} & 2 (value 2) & Required & §12.7.2, Table 225 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
The document \textbf{shall} only be saved incrementally. A conforming
reader that allows editing \textbf{must not} do a full save --- it must
append. This is the single most important flag for signature
preservation. Without it, a naive editor may do a full rewrite and
destroy every signature.} \\
\end{longtable}

In practice, both bits should be set (\texttt{/SigFlags\ 3}) whenever
the document contains any signed signature field. This was already
discussed above, but it bears repeating: if you get this wrong,
signatures will be destroyed by innocent save operations, and the blame
will be on your tool.

\subsection{PDFBox API Mapping}\label{ch12-pdfbox-api}

For those of you who skipped here --- I don't blame you, this is the
section with the code --- here's how the spec concepts map to PDFBox 3.x
classes:

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3000}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
PDF Concept
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
PDFBox Class
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key Methods
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Signature field & \texttt{PDSignatureField} & \texttt{getSignature()},
\texttt{getWidgets()}, \texttt{getPartialName()} \\
Signature dictionary & \texttt{PDSignature} & \texttt{getByteRange()},
\texttt{getContents(byte{[}{]})}, \texttt{getSubFilter()},
\texttt{getName()}, \texttt{getSignDate()} \\
AcroForm & \texttt{PDAcroForm} & \texttt{getFields()},
\texttt{getFieldTree()}, \texttt{setSignaturesExist()},
\texttt{setAppendOnly()} \\
Widget annotation & \texttt{PDAnnotationWidget} &
\texttt{getRectangle()}, \texttt{getPage()},
\texttt{getNormalAppearanceStream()} \\
Appearance stream & \texttt{PDFormXObject} & \texttt{getStream()},
\texttt{getBBox()}, \texttt{getResources()} \\
Adding a signature & \texttt{PDDocument} &
\texttt{addSignature(PDSignature,\ SignatureInterface,\ SignatureOptions)} \\
Incremental save & \texttt{PDDocument} &
\texttt{saveIncremental(OutputStream)} \\
\end{longtable}
}

\subsubsection{Minimal Signing Code (Key
Lines)}\label{_minimal_signing_code_key_lines}

The essential PDFBox signing code in \textasciitilde20 lines. This is
the skeleton --- the full, production-ready version is in Appendix A,
but these are the lines that matter:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{);}                 
\NormalTok{PDAcroForm acroForm }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDocumentCatalog}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getAcroForm}\OperatorTok{();}

\NormalTok{PDSignature signature }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFilter}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FILTER\_ADOBE\_PPKLITE}\OperatorTok{);}         
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSubFilter}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SUBFILTER\_ETSI\_CADES\_DETACHED}\OperatorTok{);} 
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"John Doe"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSignDate}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Calendar}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{());}

\NormalTok{SignatureOptions options }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{SignatureOptions}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{options}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setPreferredSignatureSize}\OperatorTok{(}\BaseNTok{0x6000}\OperatorTok{);}                     
\NormalTok{options}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setVisualSignature}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{appearanceStream}\OperatorTok{);}                  

\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addSignature}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signingInterface}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ options}\OperatorTok{);}        
\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{saveIncremental}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{outputStream}\OperatorTok{);}                             
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Always load from bytes. Don't create a new \texttt{PDDocument()} and
  try to \texttt{saveIncremental()} --- PDFBox 3.x will throw
  \texttt{IllegalStateException:\ document\ was\ not\ loaded\ from\ a\ file\ or\ a\ stream}.
  Yes, this error message has confused every single person who's ever
  encountered it.
\item
  Filter is always \texttt{Adobe.PPKLite} for interoperability. Don't be
  creative here.
\item
  SubFilter: use \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached} for PAdES compliance. See
  the earlier section for why.
\item
  Pre-allocate 24KB for the CMS blob. \textbf{Not} the PDFBox default of
  9KB. See the pre-allocation table above.
\item
  Visual appearance --- set this to \texttt{null} for invisible
  signatures. Or provide an \texttt{InputStream} of a pre-built
  appearance.
\item
  Register the signature and the \texttt{SignatureInterface}. The
  interface's \texttt{sign()} method will be called during
  \texttt{saveIncremental()}.
\item
  \textbf{Incremental save.} There is no other option. Do not call
  \texttt{save()} on a document you're signing.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{The \texttt{SignatureInterface}: Your
Extension
Point}{The SignatureInterface: Your Extension Point}}\label{_the_signatureinterface_your_extension_point}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{interface}\NormalTok{ SignatureInterface }\OperatorTok{\{}
    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{sign}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{InputStream}\NormalTok{ content}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{IOException}\OperatorTok{;} 
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Called by PDFBox during \texttt{saveIncremental()}. The
  \texttt{InputStream} provides the concatenated byte ranges --- this is
  the data you need to hash and sign. Your implementation builds the CMS
  \texttt{SignedData} blob and returns the DER-encoded bytes.
\end{itemize}

This is the \textbf{only thing you need to implement}. PDFBox handles
the PDF structure, the byte range calculation, the incremental save, the
widget creation, the appearance stream embedding. You handle the
cryptography.

\textbf{Keep your \texttt{SignatureInterface} implementation stateless
and testable.}

Don't put PDF logic in there. Don't reference the \texttt{PDDocument}.
Your \texttt{sign()} method receives an \texttt{InputStream} of bytes
and returns CMS bytes. That's it. This makes it trivially testable ---
pass in any byte stream, check that the output is valid CMS. It also
makes it easy to swap between local signing (BouncyCastle) and remote
signing (HSM, cloud) without touching any PDF code.

\subsection{Common Mistakes and How to Avoid
Them}\label{ch12-common-mistakes}

After years of working with PDF signatures, the same problems come up
over and over. Here's the hit list:

{\def\LTcaptype{none} % do not increment counter
\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\#
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Mistake
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Fix
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
1 & \textbf{Trusting \texttt{/Name} for signer identity.} Many systems
display the \texttt{/Name} field from the signature dictionary as if
it's the verified signer name. It's not. It's free-text set by whoever
controls the signing process. & Always extract the signer's identity
from the X.509 certificate in the CMS structure. Display the
certificate's Subject CN (or the full Subject DN for enterprise
contexts). \\
2 & \textbf{Using \texttt{PDDocument.save()} instead of
\texttt{saveIncremental()}.} Full save rewrites the entire file,
destroying all existing signatures. & Always use
\texttt{saveIncremental()}. Make it a code review checklist item: "Does
this code path call \texttt{save()} on a signed document? Reject." \\
3 & \textbf{Pre-allocating too little space for \texttt{/Contents}.}
Works fine in testing with simple certificates, breaks in production
when chains get longer or timestamps are added. & Use \texttt{0x6000}
(24KB) minimum. For PAdES-B-LT, use \texttt{0x18000} (96KB). Test with
your actual TSA and certificate chain, not with test certificates. \\
4 & \textbf{Forgetting \texttt{signing-certificate-v2} in the CMS
attributes.} Technically valid as PKCS\#7, but not PAdES-compliant and
vulnerable to certificate substitution. & Use BouncyCastle's
\texttt{JcaSignerInfoGeneratorBuilder} with the correct
\texttt{DigestCalculatorProvider}. It adds the attribute automatically
when configured correctly. See the code in Appendix A. \\
5 & \textbf{Not including the certificate chain.} A signature with just
the signer's certificate can't be validated by anyone who doesn't
already have the issuer certificates. & Always include the full chain
(signer + intermediates). You can omit the root CA --- validators
typically have their own trust stores. But include everything else. \\
6 & \textbf{Creating a fresh \texttt{PDDocument} and calling
\texttt{saveIncremental()}.} PDFBox 3.x requires the document to have
been loaded from bytes. & Create a blank PDF, save it to bytes, load it
back, then sign. See the test code in this chapter. \\
7 & \textbf{Setting \texttt{/SigFlags} to 1 instead of 3.} Bit 1 alone
means "signatures exist" but doesn't enforce append-only saving. &
Always set \texttt{/SigFlags} to 3. No exceptions. \\
8 & \textbf{Modifying the PDF between adding the signature and calling
\texttt{saveIncremental()}.} Any change to the document graph between
\texttt{addSignature()} and \texttt{saveIncremental()} may change byte
offsets, breaking the signature. & Set up everything before calling
\texttt{addSignature()}. Once you've added the signature, the very next
call should be \texttt{saveIncremental()}. Nothing in between. \\
\end{longtable}
}

\subsection{Key Points}\label{ch12-key-points}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  A PDF signature = signature field + signature dictionary + widget +
  appearance stream --- all embedded in the document
\item
  The signature dictionary's \texttt{/Contents} holds a CMS
  \texttt{SignedData} blob (hex-encoded, zero-padded)
\item
  \texttt{/ByteRange} defines exactly which file bytes are signed ---
  everything except \texttt{/Contents} itself
\item
  \texttt{/SubFilter} determines the signature format: use
  \texttt{/ETSI.CAdES.detached} for PAdES compliance --- there's no good
  reason not to
\item
  The \texttt{signing-certificate-v2} attribute is what distinguishes
  CAdES from plain PKCS\#7 --- and what prevents certificate
  substitution attacks
\item
  All signatures use incremental save --- original bytes are never
  modified, ever
\item
  \texttt{/SigFlags} must be set to 3 (SignaturesExist + AppendOnly) to
  protect existing signatures --- getting this wrong destroys other
  people's signatures
\item
  Seed value dictionaries (\texttt{/SV}) can constrain how a field is
  signed --- use them in templates to prevent signer mistakes
\item
  Widget \texttt{/Rect} uses PDF coordinates (origin lower-left, Y up)
  --- you will get the conversion wrong the first time, and that's OK
\item
  PDFBox's \texttt{SignatureInterface.sign(InputStream)} is the single
  extension point for all signing logic --- keep it clean, stateless,
  and testable
\item
  When in doubt, pre-allocate more space for \texttt{/Contents} ---
  wasted bytes are cheap, failed signatures are expensive
\item
  "The file has been modified since signing" is usually not a problem
  --- it's usually just a later incremental update
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Specification References}\label{ch12-spec-refs}

\chapter{Byte Range Deep Dive}\label{_byte_range_deep_dive}

The byte range is where PDF signatures live or die. Everything in
Chapter 12 --- the \texttt{/Sig} dictionary, the \texttt{/SubFilter},
the certificate chain, the CMS structure --- all of it is meaningless if
the byte range is wrong. A single off-by-one error in the
\texttt{/ByteRange} array, and the signature is cryptographically
invalid. A subtle gap in coverage, and an attacker can inject content
that the signature appears to endorse.

This chapter takes the byte range apart byte by byte. We will trace
exactly which bytes are signed, where the gap is, why the gap exists,
what lives in the gap, and what goes wrong when any of these assumptions
are violated. We will look at hex dumps of real PDFs, visualize the byte
range geometry, walk through multi-signature scenarios, and examine the
exact attacks that exploit byte range weaknesses.

If Chapter 12 taught you \emph{what} a byte range is, this chapter
teaches you \emph{how} it actually works in the raw file --- and how to
verify that it works correctly.

\section{The Byte Range Model}\label{_the_byte_range_model}

\subsection{What the Spec Says}\label{_what_the_spec_says}

ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8.1 defines the \texttt{/ByteRange} array as:

\begin{quote}
An array of pairs of integers (starting byte offset, length in bytes)
that shall describe the exact byte range for the digest computation.
Multiple discontiguous byte ranges shall be used to describe a digest
that does not include the signature value itself.
\end{quote}

In practice, every PDF signature uses exactly \textbf{one pair of byte
ranges} --- two segments --- with a gap between them. The gap contains
the \texttt{/Contents} value (the hex-encoded CMS signature). The two
segments cover everything \emph{else} in the file.

The \texttt{/ByteRange} array always has exactly four integers:

\begin{verbatim}
/ByteRange [offset1 length1 offset2 length2]
\end{verbatim}

Where:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{offset1} = 0 (always --- the signed region starts at the first
  byte of the file)
\item
  \texttt{length1} = number of bytes from the start of the file to the
  opening \texttt{\textless{}} of the \texttt{/Contents} hex string
\item
  \texttt{offset2} = byte offset immediately after the closing
  \texttt{\textgreater{}} of the \texttt{/Contents} hex string
\item
  \texttt{length2} = number of bytes from \texttt{offset2} to the end of
  the file
\end{itemize}

The math is simple:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Signed bytes} = \texttt{{[}0,\ length1)} +
  \texttt{{[}offset2,\ offset2\ +\ length2)}
\item
  \textbf{Unsigned gap} = \texttt{{[}length1,\ offset2)} --- this
  contains only the \texttt{/Contents} hex string value
\item
  \textbf{Total file size} = \texttt{offset2\ +\ length2}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Visualizing the Byte
Range}\label{_visualizing_the_byte_range}

To make this concrete, let's look at a real signed PDF. Suppose we have
a signed file of 45,000 bytes with a 32KB (32,768 hex characters =
16,384 bytes) reserved \texttt{/Contents} value:

\begin{verbatim}
File layout (not to scale):

Byte 0                                                     Byte 44,999
|================================================================|
|  PDF header, objects, pages,    |  /Contents  |  xref, trailer |
|  /Sig dictionary (up to the    |  hex string |  %%EOF         |
|  opening < of /Contents)       |  (the gap)  |                |
|================================|=============|================|
|<---- length1 = 14,000 -------->|<-- gap  --->|<-- length2 -->|
|                                 |  = 16,408   |   = 14,592    |
|                                 |             |                |
|  SIGNED (segment 1)            |  NOT SIGNED |  SIGNED (seg 2)|
|================================|=============|================|

/ByteRange [0 14000 30408 14592]
\end{verbatim}
