The gap is slightly larger than 16,384 bytes because it includes the
\texttt{\textless{}} and \texttt{\textgreater{}} delimiters that bracket
the hex string, plus the `/Contents ` key prefix. The exact gap size
depends on how PDFBox (or whatever library produced the file) serializes
the signature dictionary.

\subsection{Why the Gap Exists: The Chicken-and-Egg
Problem}\label{_why_the_gap_exists_the_chicken_and_egg_problem}

The gap exists because of a fundamental bootstrapping problem:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  To \textbf{compute the signature}, you need to hash the bytes of the
  file
\item
  To \textbf{write the file}, you need to include the signature (inside
  \texttt{/Contents})
\item
  But the signature is \emph{part of} the file --- so how can you hash a
  file that doesn't exist yet?
\end{enumerate}

The solution: \textbf{exclude the signature value from the hash}. The
\texttt{/Contents} hex string is the one part of the file that is
\emph{not} covered by the signature. Everything else --- the PDF header,
all objects, the signature dictionary itself (including
\texttt{/ByteRange}, \texttt{/Filter}, \texttt{/SubFilter}, and all
other keys), the cross-reference table, and the trailer --- is signed.

This means the signing process works like this:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Write the entire PDF file, including the \texttt{/Sig} dictionary, but
  leave the \texttt{/Contents} value as a placeholder (all zeros)
\item
  Record the byte offsets of where the placeholder starts and ends
\item
  Compute the hash over everything \emph{except} the placeholder:
  \texttt{hash\ =\ SHA256(segment1\ \textbar{}\textbar{}\ segment2)}
\item
  Construct the CMS \texttt{SignedData} using this hash
\item
  Hex-encode the CMS bytes and write them into the placeholder space
\item
  If the CMS is shorter than the reserved space (it almost always is),
  pad the remainder with zeros
\end{enumerate}

This is why PDFBox's \texttt{SignatureInterface.sign(InputStream)}
receives an \texttt{InputStream} --- that stream contains the
concatenation of segment 1 and segment 2 (the bytes \emph{outside} the
gap), ready to be hashed.

The \texttt{/ByteRange} array itself is part of the signed region. This
is critical for security: if an attacker could modify the
\texttt{/ByteRange} after signing, they could redirect the signature to
cover different content. The byte range is committed before the hash is
computed, and any change to it invalidates the signature.

\section{The Gap in Detail}\label{_the_gap_in_detail}

\subsection{What's Actually in the
Gap}\label{_whats_actually_in_the_gap}

Let's look at a hex dump of the gap region. In a properly signed PDF,
the gap contains exactly one thing: the hex-encoded CMS
\texttt{SignedData}, padded with zeros:

\begin{verbatim}
Byte 14000:  3C                                           -- opening '<'
Byte 14001:  30 38 30 36 30 39 ...                        -- CMS bytes (hex-encoded)
             ...
             ... (several KB of hex characters)
             ...
Byte 28500:  30 30 30 30 30 30 30 30 30 30 30 30 ...     -- zero padding ("000000...")
             ...
Byte 30407:  3E                                           -- closing '>'
\end{verbatim}

The hex encoding doubles the size: a 4,096-byte CMS signature becomes
8,192 hex characters. The reserved space is typically 16KB (32,768 hex
characters = 16,384 bytes of actual CMS capacity), which is enough for
most PAdES-B-B signatures.

\textbf{PAdES-B-LTA signatures are much larger} than PAdES-B-B. A B-LTA
signature includes:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The CMS \texttt{SignedData} itself (\textasciitilde2--4 KB)
\item
  The signing certificate and full chain (\textasciitilde2--5 KB)
\item
  The timestamp token from the TSA (\textasciitilde2--4 KB)
\item
  OCSP responses for each certificate in the chain (\textasciitilde1--3
  KB each)
\item
  CRL data if OCSP is not available (\textasciitilde5--50 KB)
\end{itemize}

A B-LTA signature can easily reach 20--30 KB, which requires 40--60 KB
of hex-encoded space. If you're producing B-LTA signatures, reserve at
least \textbf{64 KB} for \texttt{/Contents}:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// PDFBox: set signature content size (in bytes, before hex encoding)}
\NormalTok{SignatureOptions options }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{SignatureOptions}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{options}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setPreferredSignatureSize}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{65536}\OperatorTok{);} \CommentTok{// 64 KB}
\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addSignature}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signingInterface}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ options}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

If the CMS exceeds the reserved space, PDFBox throws an
\texttt{IOException} --- the signing fails silently in the sense that no
file is produced. This is the \#1 cause of "signing worked in
development but fails in production" --- development certificates have
short chains and no timestamps, production certificates have full
chains, OCSP responses, and timestamps that exceed the default buffer.

\subsection{The Zero-Padding Question}\label{_the_zero_padding_question}

The zero padding at the end of the \texttt{/Contents} hex string is not
part of the CMS. It's just filler --- the hex string \texttt{00} decodes
to a null byte, and CMS parsers stop reading at the end of the
DER-encoded ASN.1 structure, ignoring trailing zeros.

Some validators are stricter than others about this padding:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Adobe Acrobat}: Ignores trailing zero padding --- works fine
\item
  \textbf{LibreOffice}: Ignores trailing zeros --- works fine
\item
  \textbf{Some custom validators}: May compute the hash of the entire
  \texttt{/Contents} hex string (including padding) and fail because the
  hash doesn't match what the signer computed over just the CMS bytes.
  This is a bug in the validator, not in the signed PDF.
\end{itemize}

When writing validation code, always parse the \texttt{/Contents} hex
string as DER and stop at the end of the ASN.1 structure. Do not hash
the raw hex string --- decode it to binary, find the end of the DER
sequence (the total length indicated in the ASN.1 header), and ignore
everything after that.

\section{Incremental Saves and the Byte
Range}\label{_incremental_saves_and_the_byte_range}

\subsection{How Signing Creates an Incremental
Update}\label{_how_signing_creates_an_incremental_update}

When PDFBox signs a PDF, it performs an \textbf{incremental save} --- it
appends new data to the end of the existing file rather than rewriting
the entire file. This is not optional for signing; it is how signing
must work.

An incrementally saved PDF looks like this:

\begin{figure}
\centering
\pandocbounded{\includegraphics[keepaspectratio,alt={byterange incremental}]{diagrams/pdf/byterange-incremental.pdf}}
\caption{PDF file before and after signing --- incremental update
appends new objects without modifying the original file}
\end{figure}

The "new objects" include the signature dictionary (or modifications to
an existing signature field), any new annotation objects for the visual
appearance, and updated AcroForm entries. The "new xref" is a
cross-reference section that records the locations of these new objects,
and the "new trailer" points to the new xref.

The entire appended region --- including the new \texttt{\%\%EOF} --- is
covered by the byte range of the signature. The byte range starts at
byte 0 (the original file's header) and extends to the end of the
appended data, with only the \texttt{/Contents} gap excluded.

This means:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The signature covers the \textbf{original file} in its entirety
\item
  The signature covers the \textbf{new objects} added during signing
  (the signature dictionary, appearances, etc.)
\item
  The signature covers the \textbf{new cross-reference table} and
  \textbf{new trailer}
\item
  The only thing not covered is the CMS signature value itself
\end{itemize}

\subsection{What Happens After Signing: The Trailing Bytes
Problem}\label{_what_happens_after_signing_the_trailing_bytes_problem}

Here is one of the most security-critical aspects of byte range
handling: \textbf{what happens to bytes that appear after the signed
region?}

Consider this scenario:

\begin{figure}
\centering
\pandocbounded{\includegraphics[keepaspectratio,alt={byterange single}]{diagrams/pdf/byterange-single.pdf}}
\caption{Byte range coverage --- the signature covers two segments with
a gap for \protect\texttt{/Contents}}
\end{figure}

If \texttt{offset2\ +\ length2} equals the total file size, the
signature covers everything --- good.

But what if someone appends data \emph{after} the signed region?

\begin{figure}
\centering
\pandocbounded{\includegraphics[keepaspectratio,alt={byterange trailing}]{diagrams/pdf/byterange-trailing.pdf}}
\caption{Trailing bytes after the signed region --- the Incremental
Saving Attack surface}
\end{figure}

The byte range still validates --- the hash over the signed segments
hasn't changed. But the file now contains unsigned data at the end. If
this unsigned data is a valid incremental update (with its own xref,
trailer, and \texttt{\%\%EOF}), a PDF viewer might load it and display
the \emph{attacker's} content while showing the original signature as
valid.

This is exactly the \textbf{Incremental Saving Attack (ISA)} that the
Bochum team demonstrated in 2019 (Chapter 24). The attack works because
PDF viewers before the patches would happily load incremental updates
appended after the signed byte range, display the new content, and still
report the signature as valid --- because the signed bytes hadn't been
modified.

\textbf{The trailing bytes check is the most critical validation step.}

When verifying a PDF signature, you \textbf{must} verify that:

\begin{verbatim}
offset2 + length2 == actual file size
\end{verbatim}

If the file is larger than the signed region, something was appended
after signing. This doesn't necessarily mean the file was tampered with
--- a second legitimate signature would also append data --- but it
means you must validate the second revision's signature independently.

If there is unsigned trailing data that is \emph{not} a valid subsequent
signature revision, the document has been modified after signing and the
signature should be treated as covering only the original content.

Chapter 24 provides the full attack taxonomy and defense code.

\subsection{Multi-Signature Scenarios}\label{_multi_signature_scenarios}

Enterprise PDF workflows often involve multiple signatures --- a
document might be signed by the author, countersigned by a manager, and
certified by a compliance officer. Each signature creates its own
incremental update.

The byte range geometry for a multiply-signed PDF looks like this:

\begin{figure}
\centering
\pandocbounded{\includegraphics[keepaspectratio,alt={byterange multi}]{diagrams/pdf/byterange-multi.pdf}}
\caption{Multi-signature byte range nesting --- each subsequent
signature covers everything except its own gap}
\end{figure}

Each signature covers everything before its own gap (including previous
signatures\textquotesingle{} gaps --- the hex bytes of
\texttt{/Contents1} and \texttt{/Contents2} are part of the signed data
for signatures 2 and 3). This nesting means:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Signature 1} covers the original PDF + the objects and xref
  added during the first signing
\item
  \textbf{Signature 2} covers everything that signature 1 covers PLUS
  the objects added during the second signing. This means signature 2
  implicitly endorses signature 1's existence (though not necessarily
  its validity)
\item
  \textbf{Signature 3} covers everything, providing the broadest
  coverage
\end{itemize}

Each signature is a snapshot of the document at the time it was applied.
Signature 1 saw the document in state S1. Signature 2 saw it in state S2
(which includes signature 1). If you need to know what the document
looked like when signature 1 was applied, you must extract revision 1 by
truncating the file at the end of signature 1's byte range ---
everything after that was added later.

PDFBox provides this capability:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Extract the revision that a specific signature covers}
\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedFile}\OperatorTok{);}
\BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ signatures }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignatureDictionaries}\OperatorTok{();}
\ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature sig }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ signatures}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \DataTypeTok{int}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ byteRange }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getByteRange}\OperatorTok{();}
    \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ revisionEnd }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{]} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{3}\OperatorTok{];}

    \CommentTok{// Extract this revision}
    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ revision }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[}\NormalTok{revisionEnd}\OperatorTok{];}
    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{InputStream}\NormalTok{ is }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{FileInputStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedFile}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        is}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{readNBytes}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{revision}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ revisionEnd}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
    \CommentTok{// \textquotesingle{}revision\textquotesingle{} is now the PDF as it existed when this signature was applied}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Permission-Based Restrictions: What Can Change After
Signing?}\label{_permission_based_restrictions_what_can_change_after_signing}

Not all changes after signing are attacks. The PDF specification defines
three \textbf{modification detection and prevention (MDP)} permission
levels that control what changes are allowed after a certification
signature:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}@{}}
\caption{DocMDP Permission Levels (ISO 32000-2:2020
§12.8.2.2.2)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Level
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Level
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
P=1 & No changes & Optional & No changes whatsoever are allowed. Any
modification --- including filling form fields or adding annotations ---
invalidates the signature. This is the most restrictive level. \\
P=2 & Form filling + signing & Default & Users may fill form fields and
apply additional signatures. Other changes (adding pages, modifying
text, changing form field definitions) invalidate the signature. This is
the most common level for government forms. \\
P=3 & Form filling + signing + annotations & Optional & Users may fill
form fields, sign, and add annotations (comments, highlights, stamps).
This is the most permissive level while still detecting unauthorized
modifications. \\
\end{longtable}

When validating a signature with MDP restrictions, you must check not
only whether the byte range is intact, but also whether the
\emph{content} of subsequent incremental updates falls within the
allowed permissions. This is where validation gets complex --- you need
to compare the PDF objects in the signed revision against the current
revision and determine whether the differences are permitted.

Adobe Acrobat does this analysis automatically and shows warnings like
"The document has been altered since the signature was applied, but the
changes are permitted." Building this analysis yourself requires
understanding of the PDF object model at a level covered in Chapters 7
and 8.

\section{Size Estimation: How Much Space to
Reserve}\label{_size_estimation_how_much_space_to_reserve}

\subsection{The Problem}\label{_the_problem_2}

Before you can sign a PDF, you must write it to disk with a placeholder
for \texttt{/Contents}. The placeholder must be large enough to hold the
final CMS signature. If it's too small, the signing fails. If it's too
large, you waste space (and the file contains kilobytes of zero
padding).

The challenge: the exact CMS size depends on:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{The signing certificate} --- larger key sizes (RSA-4096 vs
  RSA-2048) produce larger signatures
\item
  \textbf{The certificate chain} --- more intermediates = more bytes
\item
  \textbf{The signed attributes} --- PAdES requires
  ESS-signing-certificate-v2, content-type, message-digest; more
  attributes = more bytes
\item
  \textbf{Timestamps} --- a TSA response is itself a CMS
  \texttt{SignedData}, typically 2--4 KB
\item
  \textbf{OCSP responses} --- each certificate in the chain may have a
  1--3 KB OCSP response
\item
  \textbf{CRL data} --- if a certificate's revocation information is
  provided via CRL instead of OCSP, CRLs can be 5--500 KB depending on
  the CA's revocation volume
\end{itemize}

You won't know the exact sizes until you've fetched the timestamp and
revocation data --- but you need to commit the space \emph{before}
computing the hash.

\subsection{Estimation Strategies}\label{_estimation_strategies}

\textbf{Strategy 1: Fixed generous buffer (simple, recommended)}

Reserve a fixed buffer that's large enough for worst-case scenarios:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Signature size estimates by PAdES profile}
\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ estimatedSize}\OperatorTok{;}
\ControlFlowTok{switch} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{profile}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \ControlFlowTok{case}\NormalTok{ B\_B}\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{   estimatedSize }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{16384}\OperatorTok{;}  \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;} \CommentTok{// 16 KB — basic CMS, no timestamp}
    \ControlFlowTok{case}\NormalTok{ B\_T}\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{   estimatedSize }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{32768}\OperatorTok{;}  \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;} \CommentTok{// 32 KB — CMS + timestamp token}
    \ControlFlowTok{case}\NormalTok{ B\_LT}\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{  estimatedSize }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{65536}\OperatorTok{;}  \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;} \CommentTok{// 64 KB — CMS + timestamp + OCSP/CRL}
    \ControlFlowTok{case}\NormalTok{ B\_LTA}\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ estimatedSize }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{131072}\OperatorTok{;} \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;} \CommentTok{// 128 KB — CMS + timestamp + OCSP/CRL + archival TS}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\NormalTok{SignatureOptions options }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{SignatureOptions}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{options}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setPreferredSignatureSize}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{estimatedSize}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This wastes some space (a B-B signature typically needs only 4--8 KB of
the 16 KB reserved), but the waste is negligible compared to the content
of a typical PDF. A 100 KB PDF with 16 KB of zero padding in
\texttt{/Contents} is 116 KB --- nobody cares.

\textbf{Strategy 2: Pre-compute exact size (complex, rarely necessary)}

If you're generating millions of signed PDFs per day and storage costs
matter, you can:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Fetch the timestamp and revocation data \emph{before} creating the
  placeholder
\item
  Compute the CMS size exactly
\item
  Reserve only the exact space needed (plus a small safety margin)
\end{enumerate}

This is significantly more complex because you need to construct a "dry
run" CMS to measure it, and the timestamp/OCSP data might change between
the dry run and the actual signing. In practice, this optimization is
almost never worth the complexity.

\textbf{Just use 64 KB (65536 bytes) as your default.} It's enough for
PAdES-B-LT in the vast majority of cases, it wastes a trivial amount of
space, and it eliminates the entire class of "signature too large for
reserved space" failures. Only reduce it if you have a specific reason
(embedded systems with extreme storage constraints) and only increase it
if you're producing B-LTA signatures with CRLs from large CAs.

\subsection{What Happens When the Estimate Is
Wrong}\label{_what_happens_when_the_estimate_is_wrong}

If the CMS signature is \textbf{smaller} than the reserved space: no
problem. The remaining space is filled with zero bytes. All conforming
validators ignore the padding.

If the CMS signature is \textbf{larger} than the reserved space:
\textbf{the signing fails}. PDFBox throws an \texttt{IOException} with a
message like:

\begin{verbatim}
IOException: Can't write signature, not enough space
\end{verbatim}

When this happens:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  The output file is corrupted (partial write) --- discard it
\item
  Increase \texttt{SignatureOptions.setPreferredSignatureSize()} and
  retry
\item
  To debug: log the actual CMS size from your
  \texttt{SignatureInterface.sign()} method and compare it to the
  reserved size
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\AttributeTok{@Override}
\KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{sign}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{InputStream}\NormalTok{ content}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{IOException} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ cmsBytes }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{createCMSSignature}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{content}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"CMS signature size: \{\} bytes (reserved: \{\} bytes)"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{             cmsBytes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ reservedSize}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ cmsBytes}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Byte Range Verification: How to Check
It}\label{_byte_range_verification_how_to_check_it}

\subsection{The Verification
Algorithm}\label{_the_verification_algorithm}

When validating a signature, the byte range verification follows these
steps:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Read the \texttt{/ByteRange} array} from the signature
  dictionary: \texttt{{[}offset1,\ length1,\ offset2,\ length2{]}}
\item
  \textbf{Verify structural sanity}:

  \begin{itemize}
  \item
    \texttt{offset1} must be 0
  \item
    \texttt{length1} must be positive
  \item
    \texttt{offset2} must equal \texttt{length1} + gap size (where gap
    size = the length of the \texttt{/Contents} hex string including
    delimiters)
  \item
    \texttt{length2} must be positive
  \item
    \texttt{offset2\ +\ length2} should equal the file size (or the size
    of the revision, for multi-signature documents)
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Verify the gap contains only \texttt{/Contents}}: the bytes in
  \texttt{{[}length1,\ offset2)} should be a hex string containing the
  CMS signature value
\item
  \textbf{Extract the signed bytes}: concatenate
  \texttt{{[}0,\ length1)} and
  \texttt{{[}offset2,\ offset2\ +\ length2)}
\item
  \textbf{Compute the hash}: \texttt{hash\ =\ SHA-256(signed\_bytes)}
  (or whatever digest algorithm the CMS specifies)
\item
  \textbf{Verify the CMS}: parse the CMS \texttt{SignedData} from
  \texttt{/Contents}, extract the signed \texttt{messageDigest}
  attribute, and compare it to the hash you just computed. If they
  match, and the CMS cryptographic verification passes, the byte range
  is intact.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Implementation in Java}\label{_implementation_in_java}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Verifies the byte range integrity of a PDF signature}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Returns true if the byte range covers the expected regions}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ and the hash matches the CMS digest}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{boolean} \FunctionTok{verifyByteRange}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ PDSignature signature}\OperatorTok{)}
        \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{IOException}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ CMSException }\OperatorTok{\{}
    \DataTypeTok{int}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ byteRange }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getByteRange}\OperatorTok{();}

    \CommentTok{// Step 1: Structural checks}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{byteRange}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length} \OperatorTok{!=} \DecValTok{4}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{error}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"ByteRange must have exactly 4 elements, got \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ offset1 }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{];}
    \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ length1 }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{];}
    \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ offset2 }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{];}
    \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ length2 }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{3}\OperatorTok{];}

    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{offset1 }\OperatorTok{!=} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{error}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"ByteRange offset1 must be 0, got \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ offset1}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{// Step 2: Check coverage}
    \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ signedEnd }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ offset2 }\OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ length2}\OperatorTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedEnd }\OperatorTok{!=}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{warn}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"ByteRange ends at \{\} but file size is \{\} — "} \OperatorTok{+}
                 \StringTok{"\{\} trailing bytes are unsigned"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                 signedEnd}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ signedEnd}\OperatorTok{);}
        \CommentTok{// This is a }\AlertTok{WARNING}\CommentTok{, not necessarily an error —}
        \CommentTok{// subsequent signatures would extend the file}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{// Step 3: Extract and hash signed bytes}
    \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\NormalTok{ digest }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"SHA{-}256"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    digest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{update}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ offset1}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ length1}\OperatorTok{);}       \CommentTok{// segment 1}
\NormalTok{    digest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{update}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ offset2}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ length2}\OperatorTok{);}        \CommentTok{// segment 2}
    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ computedHash }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ digest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{digest}\OperatorTok{();}

    \CommentTok{// Step 4: Parse CMS and compare}
    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ cmsBytes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getContents}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    CMSSignedData cmsData }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CMSSignedData}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cmsBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    SignerInformation signer }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cmsData}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignerInfos}\OperatorTok{()}
            \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSigners}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{iterator}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{next}\OperatorTok{();}

    \CommentTok{// Extract the messageDigest from signed attributes}
    \BuiltInTok{Attribute}\NormalTok{ digestAttr }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signer}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignedAttributes}\OperatorTok{()}
            \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{CMSAttributes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{messageDigest}\OperatorTok{);}
    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ cmsDigest }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ASN1OctetString}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            digestAttr}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAttrValues}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getObjectAt}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)).}\FunctionTok{getOctets}\OperatorTok{();}

    \DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ hashMatch }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{isEqual}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{computedHash}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ cmsDigest}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(!}\NormalTok{hashMatch}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{error}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Hash mismatch: computed=\{\} vs CMS=\{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                  Hex}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toHexString}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{computedHash}\OperatorTok{),}\NormalTok{ Hex}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toHexString}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cmsDigest}\OperatorTok{));}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ hashMatch}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The code above verifies that the \textbf{hash} matches --- it does not
verify the \textbf{CMS signature} (the RSA/ECDSA computation over the
signed attributes). Full verification requires both:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Byte range hash matches CMS \texttt{messageDigest} attribute (shown
  above)
\item
  CMS signature over \texttt{signedAttrs} verifies with the signer's
  certificate
\end{enumerate}

Chapter 31 provides complete verification code that covers both steps
plus certificate chain validation.

\section{Hex Dump Walkthrough: A Real Signed
PDF}\label{_hex_dump_walkthrough_a_real_signed_pdf}

Let's trace the byte range through an actual signed PDF, byte by byte.
This is the kind of analysis you do when debugging --- you open a hex
editor (or use \texttt{xxd}, \texttt{hexdump}, or QPDF) and look at the
raw bytes.

\subsection{Finding the Signature
Dictionary}\label{_finding_the_signature_dictionary}

First, locate the signature dictionary in the file. With QPDF:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}show{-}object}\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{15 signed{-}document.pdf}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

(Where object 15 is the signature dictionary --- find it by tracing from
the AcroForm → Fields → {[}signature field{]} → /V.)

Output:

\begin{verbatim}
15 0 obj
<<
  /ByteRange [0 14231 47007 1849]
  /ContactInfo ()
  /Contents <3082...0000>
  /Filter /Adobe.PPKLite
  /Location ()
  /M (D:20260308120000+01'00')
  /Name (Test Signer)
  /Reason ()
  /SubFilter /adbe.pkcs7.detached
  /Type /Sig
>>
endobj
\end{verbatim}

Now we know:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Segment 1}: bytes \texttt{{[}0,\ 14231)} --- 14,231 bytes from
  file start
\item
  \textbf{Gap}: bytes \texttt{{[}14231,\ 47007)} --- 32,776 bytes
  containing \texttt{/Contents}
\item
  \textbf{Segment 2}: bytes \texttt{{[}47007,\ 48856)} --- 1,849 bytes
  at file end
\item
  \textbf{Total file size}: 47,007 + 1,849 = 48,856 bytes
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Examining the Gap
Boundaries}\label{_examining_the_gap_boundaries}

Let's look at the bytes right around the gap:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ExtensionTok{xxd} \AttributeTok{{-}s}\NormalTok{ 14220 }\AttributeTok{{-}l}\NormalTok{ 30 signed{-}document.pdf}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Output:

\begin{verbatim}
0000378c: 2f43 6f6e 7465 6e74 7320 3c30 3830 3230  /Contents <08020
0000379c: 3036 3032 3031 3030 3330 3832 3032 3032  060201003082020
\end{verbatim}

We can see \texttt{/Contents\ \textless{}} at offset
\textasciitilde14,223. The \texttt{\textless{}} delimiter is at the byte
just before the hex string begins. The \texttt{/ByteRange} says segment
1 ends at offset 14,231 --- which is the \texttt{\textless{}} character.
Actually, the exact offset of \texttt{\textless{}} depends on how PDFBox
serializes the dictionary.

Now the end of the gap:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ExtensionTok{xxd} \AttributeTok{{-}s}\NormalTok{ 47000 }\AttributeTok{{-}l}\NormalTok{ 20 signed{-}document.pdf}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Output:

\begin{verbatim}
0000b798: 3030 3030 3030 3030 3e0a 2f46 696c 7465  00000000>./Filte
\end{verbatim}

The \texttt{\textgreater{}} delimiter is at byte 47,007 (approximately),
followed by a newline and the next dictionary key. Segment 2 starts
immediately after the \texttt{\textgreater{}}.

\subsection{Verifying No Trailing
Bytes}\label{_verifying_no_trailing_bytes}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{wc} \AttributeTok{{-}c}\NormalTok{ signed{-}document.pdf}
\CommentTok{\# Output: 48856 signed{-}document.pdf}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Byte range says: offset2 + length2 = 47,007 + 1,849 = 48,856. File size
= 48,856. They match --- no trailing unsigned bytes. The signature
covers the entire file (minus the gap).

\section{Common Byte Range Problems}\label{_common_byte_range_problems}

\subsection{Problem 1: Trailing Bytes After Signed
Region}\label{_problem_1_trailing_bytes_after_signed_region}

\textbf{Symptom}: Signature validates cryptographically, but the file
contains content not covered by the signature.

\textbf{Cause}: Something was appended to the file after signing ---
possibly a legitimate incremental update (second signature, form fill),
or possibly an Incremental Saving Attack.

\textbf{Detection}:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ signedEnd }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{]} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{3}\OperatorTok{];}
\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ fileSize }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length}\OperatorTok{;}
\ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedEnd }\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{ fileSize}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ trailingBytes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ fileSize }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ signedEnd}\OperatorTok{;}
\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{warn}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"\{\} bytes of unsigned data after signed region"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ trailingBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
    \CommentTok{// Analyze the trailing data to determine if it\textquotesingle{}s a legitimate revision}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Resolution}: Parse the trailing data. If it contains a valid
cross-reference section and trailer pointing to a subsequent signature,
it's likely a legitimate multi-signature document. If it contains
modified objects (changed page content, replaced fonts, new pages), it
may be an attack. Chapter 24 provides detailed analysis code.

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{Problem 2: \texttt{/Contents} Size
Exceeded}{Problem 2: /Contents Size Exceeded}}\label{_problem_2_contents_size_exceeded}

\textbf{Symptom}:
\texttt{IOException:\ Can’t\ write\ signature,\ not\ enough\ space}
during signing.

\textbf{Cause}: The CMS \texttt{SignedData} (after hex encoding) exceeds
the reserved \texttt{/Contents} space.

\textbf{Fix}: Increase
\texttt{SignatureOptions.setPreferredSignatureSize()}. See the size
estimation section above for recommended values by PAdES profile.

\subsection{Problem 3: ByteRange Doesn't Start at
Zero}\label{_problem_3_byterange_doesnt_start_at_zero}

\textbf{Symptom}: Validator reports "invalid byte range" or "signature
does not cover document header."

\textbf{Cause}: Extremely rare --- would indicate a buggy signing
library. The spec requires that the signed region start at byte 0 to
cover the PDF header.

\textbf{Resolution}: This is unfixable after the fact --- the PDF must
be re-signed.

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{Problem 4: Gap Contains More Than Just
\texttt{/Contents}}{Problem 4: Gap Contains More Than Just /Contents}}\label{_problem_4_gap_contains_more_than_just_contents}

\textbf{Symptom}: Validator reports "unexpected data in signature gap"
or hash mismatch.

\textbf{Cause}: The bytes between the two signed segments should contain
\emph{only} the \texttt{/Contents} hex string. If any other data exists
in this gap --- for example, if a tool inserted extra whitespace or a
stray dictionary key --- the byte range geometry is broken.

\textbf{Detection}: Parse the gap region and verify it matches the
pattern \texttt{\textless{}{[}0-9A-Fa-f{]}+\textgreater{}} with optional
zero padding.

\subsection{Problem 5: Hash Algorithm
Mismatch}\label{_problem_5_hash_algorithm_mismatch}

\textbf{Symptom}: Computed hash doesn't match CMS \texttt{messageDigest}
attribute, even though the byte range looks correct.

\textbf{Cause}: You're hashing with a different algorithm than the one
specified in the CMS. The CMS \texttt{SignerInfo.digestAlgorithm} tells
you which algorithm was used --- it might be SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512,
or (for legacy documents) SHA-1.

\textbf{Fix}: Always read the digest algorithm from the CMS before
computing the hash:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{SignerInformation signer }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cmsData}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignerInfos}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getSigners}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{iterator}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{next}\OperatorTok{();}
\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ digestAlgOid }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signer}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDigestAlgOID}\OperatorTok{();}
\CommentTok{// Map OID to algorithm name}
\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ algName }\OperatorTok{=} \ControlFlowTok{switch} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{digestAlgOid}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.1"} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"SHA{-}256"}\OperatorTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.2"} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"SHA{-}384"}\OperatorTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.3"} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"SHA{-}512"}\OperatorTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{case} \StringTok{"1.3.14.3.2.26"}          \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \StringTok{"SHA{-}1"}\OperatorTok{;}   \CommentTok{// deprecated}
    \KeywordTok{default} \OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \ControlFlowTok{throw} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{IllegalArgumentException}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Unknown digest OID: "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ digestAlgOid}\OperatorTok{);}
\OperatorTok{\};}
\BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\NormalTok{ digest }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{algName}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Key Takeaways}\label{_key_takeaways}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The byte range divides a signed PDF into exactly \textbf{two signed
  segments} with \textbf{one unsigned gap} between them
\item
  The gap contains only the hex-encoded CMS signature value (the
  \texttt{/Contents} string) --- nothing else
\item
  \texttt{offset2\ +\ length2} must equal the file size; any trailing
  bytes are unsigned and potentially malicious
\item
  Each signature in a multi-signature document covers all previous
  signatures, creating a nested coverage model
\item
  Always reserve generous space for \texttt{/Contents} --- 64 KB is a
  safe default for most PAdES profiles
\item
  The \texttt{/ByteRange} array itself is signed --- it cannot be
  modified after signing without detection
\item
  Byte range verification is the \textbf{first step} of signature
  validation --- if it fails, there is no need to check the CMS
\item
  The Incremental Saving Attack (ISA) exploits the trailing bytes
  problem; the trailing bytes check is your primary defense
\end{itemize}

\chapter{Approval vs. Certification
Signatures}\label{_approval_vs_certification_signatures}

PDF digital signatures come in two fundamentally different flavors, and
confusing them is one of the most common mistakes in signature
implementation. An \textbf{approval signature} says "I have reviewed and
agree with this document." A \textbf{certification signature} says "I am
the author of this document, and I am defining what changes are allowed
after I sign it."

The difference is not just semantic --- it is structural. A
certification signature carries a \texttt{/DocMDP} (Modification
Detection and Prevention) transform that defines permission levels,
restricts what subsequent signers and form fillers can do, and triggers
different validation behavior in viewers. Getting this wrong means your
signatures either restrict too much (form fields that can't be filled
after signing) or too little (no protection against unauthorized
modifications).

This chapter explains both signature types, the DocMDP permission model,
Lock and FieldMDP dictionaries, and the interaction rules that govern
what happens when multiple signatures coexist.

\section{The Two Signature Types}\label{_the_two_signature_types}

\subsection{Approval Signatures}\label{_approval_signatures}

An \textbf{approval signature} is what most people think of when they
hear "digital signature." It means: "I, the signer, have reviewed the
document and give my approval."

Technically, an approval signature is a \texttt{/Sig} dictionary with:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{/Filter}: \texttt{/Adobe.PPKLite}
\item
  \texttt{/SubFilter}: \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} or
  \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached}
\item
  \texttt{/ByteRange}: covers the document up to this point
\item
  No \texttt{/TransformMethod} in any associated signature reference
  dictionary
\end{itemize}

Approval signatures have these properties:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Multiple allowed}: A document can have any number of approval
  signatures. Each one adds an incremental update.
\item
  \textbf{No permission restrictions}: An approval signature does not
  limit what subsequent users can do with the document. After you apply
  an approval signature, someone else can add another signature, fill
  form fields, add annotations, or even modify the document (though
  modifications will cause the signature to show as "modified since
  signing").
\item
  \textbf{Order matters}: Each approval signature covers everything up
  to the point it was applied. Signature 2 covers signature 1. If the
  document is modified between signature 1 and signature 2, signature 1
  will show as "document modified" while signature 2 will show as
  "valid" (because it was applied after the modification).
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Certification Signatures}\label{_certification_signatures}

A \textbf{certification signature} (also called an "author signature" or
"MDP signature") is structurally different. It carries a
\texttt{/TransformMethod} of \texttt{/DocMDP} in its signature reference
dictionary, which embeds a set of permission rules into the signature.

Technically, a certification signature includes:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Everything an approval signature has
\item
  A \texttt{/Reference} array in the signature dictionary containing a
  \texttt{/SigRef} (Signature Reference) dictionary
\item
  The \texttt{/SigRef} dictionary specifies
  \texttt{/TransformMethod\ /DocMDP}
\item
  A \texttt{/TransformParams} dictionary with a \texttt{/P} key (the
  permission level)
\end{itemize}

And at the document level:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The document catalog's \texttt{/Perms} dictionary includes a
  \texttt{/DocMDP} entry pointing to the certifying signature
\end{itemize}

Certification signatures have these properties:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Only one per document}: A document can have at most one
  certification signature. It is always the first signature applied. If
  you try to add a certification signature to a document that already
  has one, the behavior is undefined --- some viewers will reject it,
  others will ignore the previous one.
\item
  \textbf{Defines permissions}: The \texttt{/P} value (1, 2, or 3)
  controls what changes are allowed after certification. This is the
  defining feature that makes certification signatures different from
  approval signatures.
\item
  \textbf{First in the chain}: The certification signature is always the
  foundational signature. All subsequent signatures are approval
  signatures that build on the certified baseline.
\end{itemize}

\begin{verbatim}
<< /Type /Sig
   /Filter /Adobe.PPKLite
   /SubFilter /adbe.pkcs7.detached
   /ByteRange [0 14231 47007 1849]
   /Contents <3082...>
   /Reference [
     << /Type /SigRef
        /TransformMethod /DocMDP
        /TransformParams << /Type /TransformParams /P 2 /V /1.2 >>
     >>
   ]
   /Name (Document Author)
   /M (D:20260308120000+01'00')
>>
\end{verbatim}

The document catalog connects this signature to the document-level
permission system:

\begin{verbatim}
<< /Type /Catalog
   /AcroForm << ... >>
   /Perms << /DocMDP 15 0 R >>    % Reference to the certifying signature object
   /Pages 3 0 R
>>
\end{verbatim}

\section{The DocMDP Permission
Model}\label{_the_docmdp_permission_model}

\subsection{Permission Level 1: No Changes
Allowed}\label{_permission_level_1_no_changes_allowed}

\texttt{/P\ 1} is the strictest level. After certification with P=1,
\textbf{no changes whatsoever are allowed} to the document. This means:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  No form fields can be filled
\item
  No additional signatures can be added
\item
  No annotations can be created
\item
  No pages can be added or removed
\item
  Any incremental update invalidates the certification
\end{itemize}

This level is appropriate for documents that are complete and final ---
archived records, published regulations, completed certificates. In
practice, it is rarely used because most signing workflows require at
least form filling or countersigning.

P=1 is so restrictive that it prevents the document from being
countersigned. If your workflow requires multiple signatures (author
signs, then reviewer countersigns), \textbf{do not use P=1}. Use P=2
instead --- it allows additional signatures while preventing content
modifications.

\subsection{Permission Level 2: Form Filling and Signing
Only}\label{_permission_level_2_form_filling_and_signing_only}

\texttt{/P\ 2} is the \textbf{default and most common} permission level.
After certification with P=2:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Allowed}: Filling form fields, applying additional (approval)
  signatures, adding signature appearances
\item
  \textbf{Not allowed}: Modifying page content, adding pages, removing
  pages, changing form field definitions (adding/removing fields),
  modifying JavaScript actions
\item
  Any change beyond form filling and signing invalidates the
  certification
\end{itemize}

P=2 is the standard for government forms, contracts, and regulated
documents. The document author sets up the form (fields, layout,
instructions), certifies it, and then the form recipient fills in the
fields and applies their own approval signature --- without being able
to alter the form structure or content.

This is the level you should use unless you have a specific reason to
choose P=1 or P=3.

\subsection{Permission Level 3: Form Filling, Signing, and
Annotations}\label{_permission_level_3_form_filling_signing_and_annotations}

\texttt{/P\ 3} is the most permissive certification level. After
certification with P=3:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Allowed}: Everything P=2 allows, plus adding annotations
  (comments, highlights, stamps, sticky notes, text markup)
\item
  \textbf{Not allowed}: Modifying page content, adding or removing
  pages, changing form field definitions
\end{itemize}

P=3 is appropriate for review workflows where multiple people need to
comment on a certified document without altering its content. Think of a
contract that needs review comments from legal, compliance, and
management before final signatures.

\subsection{What "Invalidates the Certification" Actually
Means}\label{_what_invalidates_the_certification_actually_means}

When we say a change "invalidates the certification," we mean:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The \textbf{byte range} of the certification signature remains intact
  --- the hash still matches
\item
  The \textbf{CMS signature} still verifies cryptographically
\item
  But a \textbf{conforming viewer} detects that the modifications in
  subsequent revisions exceed the allowed permissions and displays a
  warning
\end{itemize}

In Adobe Acrobat, this shows as: "The document has been altered or
corrupted since the Signature was applied." In LibreOffice, it shows as:
"Document has been modified since it was last signed."

The exact wording varies by viewer, and --- this is important ---
\textbf{not all viewers enforce MDP permissions correctly}. Some viewers
only check whether the byte range is intact and don't analyze the
incremental updates for permission compliance. This is one of the
weaknesses that the Bochum team exploited in the Incremental Saving
Attack (Chapter 24).

\section{Lock Dictionaries: Field-Level
Protection}\label{_lock_dictionaries_field_level_protection}

\subsection{What Lock Dictionaries Do}\label{_what_lock_dictionaries_do}

Beyond document-level MDP permissions, PDF supports \textbf{field-level
locking} via the \texttt{/Lock} dictionary on a signature field. A Lock
dictionary specifies which form fields should be locked (made read-only)
once this particular signature is applied.

The Lock dictionary lives in the signature field dictionary (not the
signature value dictionary), and it applies when the field is signed:

\begin{verbatim}
<< /Type /Annot
   /Subtype /Widget
   /FT /Sig
   /T (SignatureField1)
   /V 15 0 R               % Reference to the /Sig dictionary
   /Lock <<
     /Type /SigFieldLock
     /Action /Include       % or /Exclude or /All
     /Fields [(FieldA) (FieldB)]
   >>
>>
\end{verbatim}

\subsection{Lock Actions}\label{_lock_actions}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1111}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1111}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5556}}@{}}
\caption{Signature Field Lock Actions (ISO 32000-2:2020
§12.8.2.4)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Action
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Action
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/All} & name & --- & Lock all fields in the document. No
\texttt{/Fields} array is needed. After this signature is applied, every
form field in the document becomes read-only. This is the simplest but
most restrictive lock. \\
\texttt{/Include} & name & --- & Lock only the fields listed in the
\texttt{/Fields} array. The listed fields become read-only; all other
fields remain fillable. Use this when you want to protect specific
fields (e.g., "Price" and "Total") while allowing others (e.g.,
"Customer Signature") to be filled. \\
\texttt{/Exclude} & name & --- & Lock all fields \textbf{except} those
listed in the \texttt{/Fields} array. The listed fields remain fillable;
everything else is locked. Use this when most fields should be locked
but a few specific fields must remain editable (e.g., a "Date Received"
field that an administrator fills after the signer has signed). \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{The \texttt{/Fields}
Array}{The /Fields Array}}\label{_the_fields_array}

The \texttt{/Fields} array in the Lock dictionary contains the
\textbf{fully qualified field names} of the fields to include or
exclude. In a hierarchical field tree, the fully qualified name is the
concatenation of parent and child names separated by periods:

\begin{verbatim}
/Fields [(PersonalInfo.FirstName) (PersonalInfo.LastName) (Signature2)]
\end{verbatim}

Field name matching is case-sensitive and must be exact. A typo in the
Lock dictionary's \texttt{/Fields} array means the field won't be locked
--- and there will be no error message. The signature applies
successfully, the Lock dictionary is present, but the misspelled field
remains editable.

\textbf{Always test your Lock configurations} by:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Signing the document with the Lock dictionary
\item
  Opening it in Adobe Acrobat
\item
  Attempting to edit the fields that should be locked
\item
  Verifying that Acrobat prevents editing and shows the fields as
  read-only
\end{enumerate}

\section{FieldMDP: Fine-Grained Modification
Detection}\label{_fieldmdp_fine_grained_modification_detection}

\subsection{FieldMDP vs. DocMDP}\label{_fieldmdp_vs_docmdp}

While DocMDP controls what categories of changes are allowed (form
filling, annotations, etc.), \textbf{FieldMDP} controls which specific
fields can be modified. It is a more targeted mechanism.

FieldMDP uses the same \texttt{/Reference} array mechanism as DocMDP but
with \texttt{/TransformMethod\ /FieldMDP}:

\begin{verbatim}
<< /Type /SigRef
   /TransformMethod /FieldMDP
   /TransformParams <<
     /Type /TransformParams
     /Action /Include
     /Fields [(ProtectedField1) (ProtectedField2)]
     /V /2.0
   >>
>>
\end{verbatim}

The \texttt{/Action} and \texttt{/Fields} keys work identically to Lock
dictionary actions --- \texttt{/Include}, \texttt{/Exclude}, and
\texttt{/All} with the same semantics.

The difference from Lock dictionaries:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Lock dictionary}: Controls whether a field is
  \textbf{editable} in the viewer UI (read-only vs. writable). It's a
  presentation-level restriction --- the viewer \emph{should} prevent
  the user from typing in a locked field.
\item
  \textbf{FieldMDP}: Controls whether modifications to specific fields
  are \textbf{detected} during validation. If a FieldMDP-protected field
  is changed after signing, the signature shows as modified. It's a
  validation-level mechanism.
\end{itemize}

In practice, Lock and FieldMDP are often used together: Lock prevents
casual editing in a UI, and FieldMDP ensures that any bypass of the Lock
(via direct file manipulation) is detected during validation.

\section{Interaction Rules: When Multiple Signatures
Coexist}\label{_interaction_rules_when_multiple_signatures_coexist}

\subsection{The Certification + Approval
Pattern}\label{_the_certification_approval_pattern}

The most common multi-signature pattern is:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Author creates} the document with form fields
\item
  \textbf{Author certifies} with P=2 (form filling and signing allowed)
  and optionally locks specific fields
\item
  \textbf{Recipient fills} form fields
\item
  \textbf{Recipient applies} an approval signature (which adds an
  incremental update with the filled values and the new signature)
\end{enumerate}

This works because:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The certification (P=2) allows form filling and additional signatures
\item
  The approval signature is applied to a document that already has a
  certification
\item
  The certification signature remains valid because the changes (form
  fill + new signature) are within the P=2 permissions
\end{itemize}

\subsection{What Happens When Permissions
Conflict}\label{_what_happens_when_permissions_conflict}

Things get complicated when:

\textbf{Scenario 1: Approval signature before certification}

Technically, any signature can be applied to any document. If someone
applies an approval signature first and then a certification signature
second, the certification signature covers (signs over) the approval
signature. But the certification's MDP permissions only apply to changes
\emph{after} the certification --- they don't retroactively validate the
approval signature's changes.

In practice, this scenario is unusual and may confuse validators.
\textbf{Always apply the certification signature first.}

\textbf{Scenario 2: Lock dictionary conflicts with MDP}

If the certification says P=2 (form filling allowed) but a Lock
dictionary locks all fields --- the Lock takes precedence for the locked
fields. The MDP permission defines the ceiling (what's \emph{possible}),
and Lock dictionaries further restrict within that ceiling.

\textbf{Scenario 3: Multiple Lock dictionaries from different
signatures}

Each signature field can have its own Lock dictionary. When multiple
signatures with Lock dictionaries coexist:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Locks are cumulative --- each new signature can lock additional fields
\item
  Locks are permanent --- once a field is locked by any signature, no
  subsequent signature can unlock it
\item
  A field is locked if \emph{any} Lock dictionary in the chain locks it
\end{itemize}

This means the first signer can lock fields A and B, the second signer
can lock fields C and D, and after both signatures are applied, fields
A, B, C, and D are all locked.

\begin{verbatim}
// Signature 1 (Author): Lock fields A, B
SignatureField1.Lock = { Action: Include, Fields: [A, B] }

// After Sig1: A=locked, B=locked, C=editable, D=editable

// Signature 2 (Reviewer): Lock fields C, D
SignatureField2.Lock = { Action: Include, Fields: [C, D] }

// After Sig2: A=locked, B=locked, C=locked, D=locked
\end{verbatim}

\section{Implementation: Certification Signatures with
PDFBox}\label{_implementation_certification_signatures_with_pdfbox}

\subsection{Creating a Certification
Signature}\label{_creating_a_certification_signature}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{inputFile}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// Create the signature}
\NormalTok{PDSignature signature }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFilter}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FILTER\_ADOBE\_PPKLITE}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSubFilter}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SUBFILTER\_ADBE\_PKCS7\_DETACHED}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Document Author"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSignDate}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Calendar}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{());}

\CommentTok{// Set certification (MDP) permissions — P=2 is recommended}
\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addSignature}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signingInterface}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// Set the access permissions}
\CommentTok{// 2 = form filling and signing allowed}
\NormalTok{AccessPermission accessPermission }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{AccessPermission}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{accessPermission}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDocMDP}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDocumentPermissions}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ accessPermission}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{saveIncremental}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{outputStream}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{close}\OperatorTok{();}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

PDFBox's API for certification signatures evolved between versions. In
PDFBox 2.x, you used \texttt{doc.addSignature()} with a
\texttt{SignatureOptions} and separately configured the MDP via the
catalog's \texttt{/Perms} dictionary. In PDFBox 3.x, the
\texttt{AccessPermission} class provides a cleaner API, but the
underlying mechanism is the same --- writing the \texttt{/DocMDP}
reference into the catalog.

\subsection{Adding Lock Dictionaries}\label{_adding_lock_dictionaries}

Lock dictionaries are configured on the signature field before signing:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Get or create the signature field}
\NormalTok{PDSignatureField sigField }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{findOrCreateSignatureField}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"AuthorSignature"}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// Create lock dictionary}
\NormalTok{COSDictionary lockDict }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{COSDictionary}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{lockDict}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setName}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{TYPE}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"SigFieldLock"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{lockDict}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setName}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Action"}\OperatorTok{),} \StringTok{"Include"}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{COSArray fieldsToLock }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{COSArray}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{fieldsToLock}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{COSString}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"PriceField"}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{fieldsToLock}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{COSString}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"TotalField"}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{fieldsToLock}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{COSString}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"ContractTerms"}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{lockDict}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setItem}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Fields"}\OperatorTok{),}\NormalTok{ fieldsToLock}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// Attach lock dictionary to the signature field}
\NormalTok{sigField}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSObject}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{setItem}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Lock"}\OperatorTok{),}\NormalTok{ lockDict}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// Now sign the document}
\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addSignature}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signingInterface}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{saveIncremental}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{outputStream}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Seed Value Dictionaries: Constraining How a Signature Field Is
Signed}\label{_seed_value_dictionaries_constraining_how_a_signature_field_is_signed}

\subsection{Purpose of Seed Values}\label{_purpose_of_seed_values}

A \textbf{seed value dictionary} (\texttt{/SV}) on a signature field
constrains \emph{how} the field can be signed. It does not sign anything
itself --- it provides instructions to the signing tool about what
certificate to use, what algorithm to use, which SubFilter is required,
and other signing parameters.

This is the mechanism that government form creators use to ensure that
form fillers sign with the correct certificate type, use the correct
algorithm, and produce signatures that will pass validation.

\subsection{Key Seed Value Entries}\label{_key_seed_value_entries}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}@{}}
\caption{Seed Value Dictionary Keys (ISO 32000-2:2020
§12.8.2.3)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/Filter} & name & Optional & §12.8.2.3, Table 237 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
The signature handler to use. In practice, always
\texttt{/Adobe.PPKLite}. If set, the signing tool should use this
handler --- if a different handler is selected, the signing tool should
warn the user.} \\
\texttt{/SubFilter} & array & Optional & §12.8.2.3, Table 237 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Array of acceptable SubFilter values. The signing tool should use one of
these SubFilter values. Example:
\texttt{{[}/adbe.pkcs7.detached\ /ETSI.CAdES.detached{]}} means either
detached format is acceptable.} \\
\texttt{/DigestMethod} & array & Optional & §12.8.2.3, Table 237 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Array of acceptable digest algorithms. Example:
\texttt{{[}/SHA256\ /SHA384\ /SHA512{]}}. If set, the signing tool must
use one of these algorithms. \texttt{/SHA1} is deprecated and should not
be included.} \\
\texttt{/Reasons} & array & Optional & §12.8.2.3, Table 237 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Array of text strings presenting acceptable signing reasons. The signing
tool should let the user choose from this list. Example:
\texttt{{[}(I\ approve\ this\ document)\ (I\ have\ reviewed\ this\ document)\ (I\ am\ the\ authorized\ signatory){]}}.} \\
\texttt{/MDP} & dictionary & Optional & §12.8.2.3, Table 237 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
The MDP parameters dictionary, specifying the default DocMDP permission
level for signatures applied to this field.} \\
\texttt{/TimeStamp} & dictionary & Optional & §12.8.2.3, Table 237 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Timestamp seed value dictionary specifying a timestamp authority URL and
whether a timestamp is required. If the \texttt{/Ff} flag is set to 1
(required), the signing tool must include a timestamp from the specified
TSA.} \\
\texttt{/Ff} & integer (flags) & Optional & §12.8.2.3, Table 237 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Flags indicating which seed values are mandatory (bit 1 set) vs.
recommended (bit 1 clear). If a seed value's corresponding Ff bit is
set, the signing tool must respect the constraint --- failure to do so
should prevent the signature from being applied.} \\
\texttt{/LegalAttestation} & array & Optional & §12.8.2.3 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Array of legal attestation strings. The signer may be required to accept
one of these attestations as part of the signing ceremony.} \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{Timestamp Seed Values}\label{_timestamp_seed_values}

The \texttt{/TimeStamp} sub-dictionary in seed values is particularly
important for PAdES compliance:

\begin{verbatim}
/SV <<
  /TimeStamp <<
    /URL (http://tsa.example.com/timestamp)
    /Ff 1              % 1 = timestamp required, 0 = optional
  >>
  /SubFilter [/ETSI.CAdES.detached]
  /DigestMethod [/SHA256 /SHA384 /SHA512]
  /Ff 1                % All seed values are mandatory
>>
\end{verbatim}

This seed value dictionary says: "When signing this field, you must use
CAdES detached format, SHA-256 or better, and you must include a
timestamp from \texttt{tsa.example.com}. All of these requirements are
mandatory (Ff=1)."

A conforming signing tool (Adobe Acrobat, for example) will enforce
these constraints automatically. PDFBox-based signing code must read and
respect these seed values manually --- the library does not enforce them
automatically.

\textbf{Always read the seed value dictionary before signing.} If you're
building a signing service that signs arbitrary PDFs, the seed values
may impose constraints that your default configuration doesn't meet.
Ignoring them produces a signature that appears valid cryptographically
but violates the form author's intent --- and may fail validation in
systems that check seed value compliance.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{PDSignatureField sigField }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{...}\OperatorTok{;} \CommentTok{// the field you\textquotesingle{}re about to sign}
\NormalTok{COSDictionary sv }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sigField}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSObject}\OperatorTok{()}
    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSDictionary}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"SV"}\OperatorTok{));}
\ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sv }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \CommentTok{// Check SubFilter requirements}
\NormalTok{    COSArray subFilters }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sv}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSArray}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"SubFilter"}\OperatorTok{));}
    \CommentTok{// Check DigestMethod requirements}
\NormalTok{    COSArray digestMethods }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sv}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSArray}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"DigestMethod"}\OperatorTok{));}
    \CommentTok{// Check Timestamp requirements}
\NormalTok{    COSDictionary ts }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sv}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSDictionary}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"TimeStamp"}\OperatorTok{));}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ts }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ tsaUrl }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ts}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getString}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"URL"}\OperatorTok{));}
        \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ tsFlags }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ts}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInt}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Ff"}\OperatorTok{),} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{);}
        \DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ timestampRequired }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsFlags }\OperatorTok{\&} \DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{!=} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Real-World Patterns and
Mistakes}\label{_real_world_patterns_and_mistakes}

\subsection{Mistake 1: Using Certification When Approval Is
Needed}\label{_mistake_1_using_certification_when_approval_is_needed}

\textbf{Scenario}: A developer creates a multi-signature workflow where
three people sign sequentially. They make the first signature a
certification signature with P=1 (no changes allowed).

\textbf{Result}: The second and third signatures fail or invalidate the
certification, because P=1 prohibits additional signatures.

\textbf{Fix}: Use P=2 for the first (certification) signature if
additional signatures are needed. Or use approval signatures for all
three if no MDP restrictions are needed.

\subsection{Mistake 2: Not Setting MDP When It's
Needed}\label{_mistake_2_not_setting_mdp_when_its_needed}

\textbf{Scenario}: A government form is meant to be filled and signed by
the citizen, with no other modifications allowed. The form creator signs
it as a plain approval signature without MDP.

\textbf{Result}: The form has no modification restrictions. Anyone can
modify the form structure, add fields, remove fields, or alter content
--- and the original signature only indicates "document has been
modified" without specifying what was allowed.

\textbf{Fix}: Use a certification signature with P=2 to allow form
filling while preventing structural changes.

\subsection{Mistake 3: Lock Dictionary with Wrong Field
Names}\label{_mistake_3_lock_dictionary_with_wrong_field_names}

\textbf{Scenario}: The signature is configured to lock fields "Price",
"Total", and "Terms". But the actual field names in the PDF are
"price\_field", "total\_amount", and "contract\_terms" (because the form
designer used different naming).

\textbf{Result}: The Lock dictionary is present and syntactically
correct, but no fields are actually locked --- the names don't match.
There is no error message or warning.

\textbf{Fix}: Enumerate all field names before configuring the Lock
dictionary. In PDFBox:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{PDAcroForm acroForm }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDocumentCatalog}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getAcroForm}\OperatorTok{();}
\ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDField field }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ acroForm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFieldTree}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Field: \{\} (type: \{\})"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{             field}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFullyQualifiedName}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ field}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getFieldType}\OperatorTok{());}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Mistake 4: Assuming All Viewers Enforce
MDP}\label{_mistake_4_assuming_all_viewers_enforce_mdp}

\textbf{Scenario}: A developer relies on MDP P=2 to prevent content
modifications, assuming all viewers will refuse to let users edit
non-form content after certification.

\textbf{Result}: Adobe Acrobat enforces MDP correctly. Foxit enforces it
mostly correctly. LibreOffice enforces it partially. PDFBox (when used
as a library) does not enforce MDP at all --- it allows any modification
programmatically. Browser-based PDF viewers (Chrome, Firefox) often
ignore MDP entirely because they don't support editing.

\textbf{Lesson}: MDP is a \emph{validation} mechanism, not an
\emph{access control} mechanism. It tells you whether changes were
permitted, not whether they were prevented. A determined user with a hex
editor or a programmatic tool can always modify a PDF --- MDP ensures
you can \emph{detect} that the modification occurred.

\section{Key Takeaways}\label{_key_takeaways_2}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Approval signatures}: "I approve this document." Multiple
  allowed, no restrictions on subsequent changes, each covers all
  previous content.
\item
  \textbf{Certification signatures}: "I authored this document and
  define what can change." Only one per document, must be first, carries
  DocMDP permissions (P=1/2/3).
\item
  \textbf{P=2 is the default and most common} permission level ---
  allows form filling and additional signatures while preventing content
  modifications.
\item
  \textbf{Lock dictionaries} control which fields become read-only after
  a specific signature is applied. Cumulative and permanent.
\item
  \textbf{FieldMDP} provides validation-level detection of changes to
  specific fields --- complements Lock dictionaries.
\item
  \textbf{Seed value dictionaries} constrain how a signature field must
  be signed --- SubFilter, digest algorithm, timestamp requirements.
\item
  MDP is a \textbf{detection mechanism}, not a prevention mechanism. Not
  all viewers enforce it. Always verify compliance programmatically.
\item
  \textbf{Always apply certification signatures first}, then approval
  signatures. The certification establishes the permission framework.
\end{itemize}

\chapter{Visual Appearance of
Signatures}\label{_visual_appearance_of_signatures}

A digital signature has two faces. The cryptographic face --- the CMS
\texttt{SignedData}, the byte range, the certificate chain --- is what
makes the signature \emph{valid}. The visual face --- what the user sees
in the PDF viewer --- is what makes the signature \emph{trustworthy to a
human reader}. These two faces are entirely independent, and
understanding their independence is critical for both security and
usability.

The visual appearance of a PDF signature is an \textbf{annotation
appearance stream} --- a miniature PDF drawing that shows the signer's
name, date, reason, and optionally a handwritten signature image, a
company logo, or other graphical elements. This appearance stream is
part of the signed bytes (it lives inside the signature field's widget
annotation), but it is \emph{not} cryptographically bound to the CMS in
any meaningful way. A valid CMS signature does not guarantee that the
visual appearance accurately represents the signer --- it only
guarantees that the bytes haven't changed.

This distinction is exactly what Shadow Attacks (Chapter 24) exploit: an
attacker can create a PDF where the visual appearance shows one thing to
the signer and something different to the recipient, with the signature
validating correctly in both cases.

This chapter explains how signature appearances work, how to create them
with PDFBox, what the layer model means, and how different viewers
handle appearance rendering.

\section{Appearance Streams: The
Basics}\label{_appearance_streams_the_basics}

\subsection{What an Appearance Stream
Is}\label{_what_an_appearance_stream_is}
