Every annotation in a PDF --- not just signatures --- can have an
\textbf{appearance stream}. An appearance stream is a Form XObject (a
\texttt{/Type\ /XObject\ /Subtype\ /Form} stream) that contains the
drawing instructions for how the annotation looks.

For a signature widget annotation, the appearance stream is stored under
the \texttt{/AP} (Appearance) key:

\begin{verbatim}
<< /Type /Annot
   /Subtype /Widget
   /FT /Sig
   /T (SignatureField1)
   /V 15 0 R                    % the /Sig dictionary
   /Rect [100 600 300 650]      % position on the page (x1 y1 x2 y2)
   /AP <<
     /N 20 0 R                  % Normal appearance stream
   >>
>>
\end{verbatim}

The \texttt{/N} key holds the \textbf{normal} appearance --- the one
shown when the annotation is not being interacted with. Annotations can
also have \texttt{/R} (rollover --- shown on mouse hover) and
\texttt{/D} (down --- shown when clicked), but signature appearances
only use \texttt{/N}.

The appearance stream (object 20 in this example) is a Form XObject:

\begin{verbatim}
20 0 obj
<< /Type /XObject
   /Subtype /Form
   /BBox [0 0 200 50]          % bounding box in the stream's coordinate space
   /Resources << /Font << /F1 21 0 R >> >>
   /Length 142
>>
stream
BT
/F1 10 Tf
0 35 Td
(Digitally signed by: Test Signer) Tj
0 -15 Td
(Date: 2026-03-08 12:00:00 UTC) Tj
0 -15 Td
(Reason: I approve this document) Tj
ET
endstream
endobj
\end{verbatim}

This is a complete miniature PDF page: it has a bounding box, font
resources, and drawing instructions using the standard PDF content
stream operators (\texttt{BT}/\texttt{ET} for text blocks, \texttt{Tf}
for font selection, \texttt{Td} for positioning, \texttt{Tj} for showing
text).

\subsection{Visible vs. Invisible
Signatures}\label{_visible_vs_invisible_signatures}

Not all signatures need a visual appearance. A signature can be:

\textbf{Visible}: The signature field has a non-zero \texttt{/Rect}
(annotation rectangle) and an appearance stream. The user sees a
signature block on a specific page.

\textbf{Invisible}: The signature field either has a zero-size
\texttt{/Rect} (\texttt{{[}0\ 0\ 0\ 0{]}}), or has no appearance stream,
or is not associated with any page. The signature exists
cryptographically but doesn't appear on any page.

Invisible signatures are common in automated signing workflows where the
PDF is being signed programmatically (e.g., a server signing all
outgoing invoices) and there's no need for a visual indicator on the
document. The signature is still listed in the viewer's signature panel
--- the user can still inspect it, verify the certificate, and check the
validation status.

Even for invisible signatures, some viewers (notably Adobe Acrobat)
display a blue banner at the top of the document saying "Signed and all
signatures are valid" or similar. This banner is not part of the PDF
content --- it's the viewer's UI.

\section{The Layer Model: n0, n2, and
n4}\label{_the_layer_model_n0_n2_and_n4}

\subsection{Adobe's Legacy Layer
System}\label{_adobes_legacy_layer_system}

Adobe Acrobat uses a layer-based approach to compose signature
appearances. This system was designed by Adobe for their own
implementation and is not formally part of the ISO 32000 specification
--- but because Adobe Acrobat is the dominant viewer, understanding it
matters.

The layers are:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{n0} (Layer 0 --- Background): Contains the background graphic
  and a static validity indicator. Adobe Acrobat uses this to show the
  blue "valid" checkmark or the red "invalid" X. This layer is generated
  and updated by Acrobat itself --- it is not typically set by the
  signing application.
\item
  \textbf{n2} (Layer 2 --- Content): Contains the signer's information
  --- name, date, reason, location, and optionally a handwritten
  signature image or company logo. \textbf{This is the layer you create}
  when building a visual signature appearance.
\item
  \textbf{n4} (Layer 4 --- Text overlay): An optional text overlay,
  rarely used.
\end{itemize}

In practice, most signing applications (including PDFBox-based
implementations) create a single appearance stream that serves as the
entire appearance --- they don't use the n0/n2/n4 layer decomposition.
Adobe Acrobat, when it encounters a signature without layer-based
appearances, creates its own composite appearance using the provided
stream as the content layer and adding its own validity indicators.

The layer model is an implementation detail of Adobe Acrobat, not a spec
requirement. When you create a signature appearance with PDFBox, you
create a single Form XObject for the \texttt{/AP\ /N} entry. Acrobat
will overlay its own validity graphics on top. Other viewers
(LibreOffice, Foxit, browser-based viewers) each handle the appearance
differently --- some show your appearance as-is, some add their own
decorations.

\subsection{What This Means for Your
Implementation}\label{_what_this_means_for_your_implementation}

When building a signature appearance with PDFBox:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Create a single Form XObject} with the signer's information
\item
  \textbf{Don't worry about n0/n2/n4 layers} --- the single appearance
  works across all viewers
\item
  \textbf{Set reasonable fonts and sizes} --- the appearance will be
  rendered in the space defined by the signature field's \texttt{/Rect}
\item
  \textbf{Include key information}: signer's name, signing date/time,
  reason (if applicable)
\item
  \textbf{Test in multiple viewers} --- Acrobat, Foxit, LibreOffice, and
  a browser-based viewer --- to ensure the appearance renders correctly
  in each
\end{enumerate}

\section{Creating Signature Appearances with
PDFBox}\label{_creating_signature_appearances_with_pdfbox}

\subsection{Basic Text Appearance}\label{_basic_text_appearance}

The simplest appearance shows the signer's name and date:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{private}\NormalTok{ PDFormXObject }\FunctionTok{createSignatureAppearance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDDocument doc}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{        PDRectangle rect}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ signerName}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{Calendar}\NormalTok{ signDate}\OperatorTok{)}
        \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{IOException} \OperatorTok{\{}

\NormalTok{    PDFormXObject form }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDFormXObject}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDocument}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{createCOSStream}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{    form}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setResources}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDResources}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{    form}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setBBox}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getWidth}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getHeight}\OperatorTok{()));}

\NormalTok{    PDFont font }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FontName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HELVETICA}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    form}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getResources}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{put}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"F1"}\OperatorTok{),}\NormalTok{ font}\OperatorTok{);}

    \DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ fontSize }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{8}\OperatorTok{;}
    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ dateStr }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{SimpleDateFormat}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"yyyy{-}MM{-}dd HH:mm:ss z"}\OperatorTok{)}
            \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{format}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signDate}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTime}\OperatorTok{());}

    \CommentTok{// Build the content stream manually}
    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{OutputStream}\NormalTok{ os }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ form}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getStream}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{createOutputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ content }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{join}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{,}
            \StringTok{"BT"}\OperatorTok{,}
            \StringTok{"/F1 "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ fontSize }\OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" Tf"}\OperatorTok{,}
            \StringTok{"0 "} \OperatorTok{+} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getHeight}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ fontSize }\OperatorTok{{-}} \DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" Td"}\OperatorTok{,}
            \StringTok{"("} \OperatorTok{+} \FunctionTok{escapeForPdf}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signerName}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{") Tj"}\OperatorTok{,}
            \StringTok{"0 {-}"} \OperatorTok{+} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{fontSize }\OperatorTok{+} \DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" Td"}\OperatorTok{,}
            \StringTok{"(Date: "} \OperatorTok{+} \FunctionTok{escapeForPdf}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{dateStr}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{") Tj"}\OperatorTok{,}
            \StringTok{"ET"}
        \OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        os}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{content}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getBytes}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{US\_ASCII}\OperatorTok{));}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ form}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}

\KeywordTok{private} \BuiltInTok{String} \FunctionTok{escapeForPdf}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ text}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ text}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{replace}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{)}
               \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{replace}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"("}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{("}\OperatorTok{)}
               \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{replace}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{")"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{)"}\OperatorTok{);}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

In PDFBox 3.x, you \textbf{cannot} use \texttt{PDPageContentStream} to
write to a Form XObject. \texttt{PDPageContentStream} is strictly for
page content. For Form XObjects, you must write raw PDF operators to the
stream's output stream, as shown above.

This is one of the breaking changes from PDFBox 2.x, where
\texttt{PDPageContentStream} could be used with Form XObjects via a
constructor overload that accepted a \texttt{PDFormXObject} parameter.
That constructor no longer exists in 3.x.

\subsection{Appearance with Signature
Image}\label{_appearance_with_signature_image}

Many signing workflows include a handwritten signature image --- either
captured via a canvas on a web page or tablet, or uploaded as a PNG/JPEG
file. Adding an image to the appearance requires embedding it as a PDF
image XObject:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{private}\NormalTok{ PDFormXObject }\FunctionTok{createAppearanceWithImage}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDDocument doc}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{        PDRectangle rect}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ signerName}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{Calendar}\NormalTok{ signDate}\OperatorTok{,}
        \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ signatureImagePng}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{IOException} \OperatorTok{\{}

\NormalTok{    PDFormXObject form }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDFormXObject}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDocument}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{createCOSStream}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{    form}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setResources}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDResources}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{    form}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setBBox}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getWidth}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getHeight}\OperatorTok{()));}

    \CommentTok{// Add font}
\NormalTok{    PDFont font }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FontName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HELVETICA}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    form}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getResources}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{put}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"F1"}\OperatorTok{),}\NormalTok{ font}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// Add signature image}
\NormalTok{    PDImageXObject image }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ PDImageXObject}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{createFromByteArray}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            doc}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signatureImagePng}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"signature"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    form}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getResources}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{put}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Img0"}\OperatorTok{),}\NormalTok{ image}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// Layout: image on the left, text on the right}
    \DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ imgWidth }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getWidth}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{*} \FloatTok{0.4f}\OperatorTok{;}
    \DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ imgHeight }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getHeight}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}} \DecValTok{4}\OperatorTok{;}
    \DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ textX }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ imgWidth }\OperatorTok{+} \DecValTok{5}\OperatorTok{;}
    \DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ fontSize }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{7}\OperatorTok{;}
    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ dateStr }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{SimpleDateFormat}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"yyyy{-}MM{-}dd HH:mm:ss z"}\OperatorTok{)}
            \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{format}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signDate}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTime}\OperatorTok{());}

    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{OutputStream}\NormalTok{ os }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ form}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getStream}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{createOutputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{StringBuilder}\NormalTok{ sb }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{StringBuilder}\OperatorTok{();}
        \CommentTok{// Draw image}
\NormalTok{        sb}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"q}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// save graphics state}
\NormalTok{        sb}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{imgWidth}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{" 0 0 "}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{imgHeight}\OperatorTok{)}
          \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{" 2 2 cm}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// transform matrix}
\NormalTok{        sb}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"/Img0 Do}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// draw image}
\NormalTok{        sb}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Q}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// restore graphics state}
        \CommentTok{// Draw text}
\NormalTok{        sb}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"BT}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        sb}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"/F1 "}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{fontSize}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{" Tf}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        sb}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{textX}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{" "}\OperatorTok{)}
          \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{rect}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getHeight}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ fontSize }\OperatorTok{{-}} \DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{" Td}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        sb}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"(Digitally signed by:) Tj}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        sb}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"0 {-}"}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{fontSize }\OperatorTok{+} \DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{" Td}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        sb}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"("}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\FunctionTok{escapeForPdf}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signerName}\OperatorTok{)).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{") Tj}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        sb}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"0 {-}"}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{fontSize }\OperatorTok{+} \DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{" Td}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        sb}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"(Date: "}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\FunctionTok{escapeForPdf}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{dateStr}\OperatorTok{)).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{") Tj}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        sb}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"ET}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        os}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sb}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toString}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getBytes}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{US\_ASCII}\OperatorTok{));}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ form}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Attaching the Appearance to the
Signature}\label{_attaching_the_appearance_to_the_signature}

After creating the appearance Form XObject, attach it to the signature
field's widget annotation:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Create signature field and widget annotation}
\NormalTok{PDSignature signature }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{();}
\CommentTok{// ... set signature properties ...}

\CommentTok{// Set the visual rectangle on page 1}
\NormalTok{PDPage page }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPage}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{PDRectangle rect }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{100}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{600}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{200}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{50}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{SignatureOptions options }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{SignatureOptions}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{options}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setPreferredSignatureSize}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{65536}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{options}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setPage}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// page index (0{-}based)}
\NormalTok{options}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setVisualSignature}\OperatorTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{createSignatureAppearance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ rect}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"Test Signer"}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{Calendar}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{()));}

\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addSignature}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signingInterface}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ options}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{saveIncremental}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{outputStream}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Appearance Rendering Across
Viewers}\label{_appearance_rendering_across_viewers}

\subsection{How Different Viewers Handle
Appearances}\label{_how_different_viewers_handle_appearances}

One of the most frustrating aspects of PDF signature appearances is that
different viewers render them differently --- even when the appearance
stream is identical.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.7143}}@{}}
\caption{Signature appearance rendering behavior across major
viewers}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Viewer
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Behavior
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Viewer
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Behavior
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\textbf{Adobe Acrobat} & Overlays its own validity indicator (blue
checkmark / red X) on layer n0. Your appearance stream is rendered on
layer n2. Acrobat may resize your content to fit its layer model. If
\texttt{/NeedAppearances} is true in the AcroForm, Acrobat may
regenerate appearances from scratch. \\
\textbf{Foxit Reader} & Renders the appearance stream mostly as
provided. Adds a small validity icon in the corner. Generally the most
faithful rendering of custom appearances. \\
\textbf{LibreOffice Draw} & Renders the appearance stream as-is. Does
not overlay validity indicators on the appearance itself --- shows
validation status in a separate panel. Good for testing that your raw
appearance looks correct. \\
\textbf{Chrome (built-in viewer)} & Renders appearance streams but does
not validate signatures. No validity indicators. The appearance is what
you see --- useful for checking visual layout. \\
\textbf{Firefox (pdf.js)} & Renders appearance streams. Does not
validate signatures (though experimental validation support has been
discussed). Like Chrome, useful for layout testing. \\
\textbf{Okular (KDE)} & Renders appearances and provides signature
validation. May show validity status differently from other viewers. \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{Always test your signature appearances in at least three
viewers} --- Adobe Acrobat, a non-Adobe desktop viewer (Foxit or
LibreOffice), and a browser. Appearances that look perfect in Acrobat
may render differently in other viewers due to:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Different font fallback behavior (if the font isn't embedded)
\item
  Different handling of the coordinate system and bounding box
\item
  Different overlay/decoration behavior
\item
  Different treatment of transparency and blending
\end{itemize}

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{The \texttt{/NeedAppearances}
Problem}{The /NeedAppearances Problem}}\label{_the_needappearances_problem}

The AcroForm dictionary has a boolean flag \texttt{/NeedAppearances}
that tells viewers whether to regenerate form field appearances from
scratch. If this flag is \texttt{true}, some viewers (notably Adobe
Acrobat) will ignore the existing appearance streams and generate their
own based on the field's value.

For signature fields, this is usually not what you want. If
\texttt{/NeedAppearances} is true and Acrobat regenerates the
signature's appearance, your carefully crafted visual (with the signer's
image, custom layout, etc.) gets replaced with Acrobat's default
rendering.

\textbf{Always set \texttt{/NeedAppearances} to \texttt{false}} (or
remove the key entirely --- the default is \texttt{false}) before
signing:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{PDAcroForm acroForm }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDocumentCatalog}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getAcroForm}\OperatorTok{();}
\ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{acroForm }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    acroForm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSObject}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{setBoolean}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{NEED\_APPEARANCES}\OperatorTok{,} \KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{);}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Positioning: Getting the Rectangle
Right}\label{_positioning_getting_the_rectangle_right}

\subsection{The Coordinate System}\label{_the_coordinate_system}

PDF uses a \textbf{bottom-left origin} coordinate system where (0, 0) is
at the lower-left corner of the page. The y-axis goes \emph{up}, not
down. This is the opposite of most screen coordinate systems and is a
common source of positioning errors.

The signature field's \texttt{/Rect} array defines the annotation
rectangle as \texttt{{[}x1\ y1\ x2\ y2{]}}, where:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{(x1,\ y1)} is the lower-left corner
\item
  \texttt{(x2,\ y2)} is the upper-right corner
\end{itemize}

So a signature field at the bottom-right of an A4 page (595 x 842
points) might have:

\begin{verbatim}
/Rect [395 50 575 100]    % 180pt wide, 50pt tall, near bottom-right
\end{verbatim}

A common mistake: specifying the rectangle with screen coordinates
(origin at top-left, y going down). If you compute
\texttt{y\ =\ pageHeight\ -\ desiredY}, make sure you do this for
\emph{both} y1 and y2, and that y1 \textless{} y2.

\textbf{Debug tip}: If your signature appearance shows up in the wrong
position, check:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Is the page rotated? (A \texttt{/Rotate} key on the page dictionary
  changes the visible coordinate system)
\item
  Is the \texttt{MediaBox} the expected size? (Custom page sizes shift
  everything)
\item
  Did you flip y1 and y2? (The lower-left corner must have the smaller y
  value)
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Handling Page Rotation}\label{_handling_page_rotation}

PDF pages can have a \texttt{/Rotate} key that specifies a rotation
angle (0, 90, 180, or 270 degrees). This rotation affects how the page
is \emph{displayed} but does not change the coordinate system of the
page content or annotations.

This means that on a 90-degree rotated page (landscape orientation
achieved via rotation), the annotation rectangle coordinates are still
in the unrotated coordinate space. To place a signature at the visual
bottom-right of a rotated page, you need to account for the rotation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{PDPage page }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPage}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pageIndex}\OperatorTok{);}
\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ rotation }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getRotation}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{PDRectangle mediaBox }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getMediaBox}\OperatorTok{();}

\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ sigWidth }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{200}\OperatorTok{;}
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ sigHeight }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{50}\OperatorTok{;}
\DataTypeTok{float}\NormalTok{ margin }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{30}\OperatorTok{;}

\NormalTok{PDRectangle sigRect}\OperatorTok{;}
\ControlFlowTok{switch} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{rotation}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \ControlFlowTok{case} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{:}   \CommentTok{// Normal portrait}
\NormalTok{        sigRect }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            mediaBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getWidth}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ sigWidth }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ margin}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            margin}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            sigWidth}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sigHeight}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{case} \DecValTok{90}\OperatorTok{:}  \CommentTok{// Landscape (rotated clockwise)}
\NormalTok{        sigRect }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            margin}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            margin}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            sigHeight}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sigWidth}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// swap width/height}
        \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{case} \DecValTok{180}\OperatorTok{:} \CommentTok{// Upside{-}down portrait}
\NormalTok{        sigRect }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            margin}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            mediaBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getHeight}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ sigHeight }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ margin}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            sigWidth}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sigHeight}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{case} \DecValTok{270}\OperatorTok{:} \CommentTok{// Landscape (rotated counter{-}clockwise)}
\NormalTok{        sigRect }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            mediaBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getWidth}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ sigHeight }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ margin}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            mediaBox}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getHeight}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ sigWidth }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ margin}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            sigHeight}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sigWidth}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{break}\OperatorTok{;}
    \KeywordTok{default}\OperatorTok{:}
\NormalTok{        sigRect }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{margin}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ margin}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sigWidth}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sigHeight}\OperatorTok{);}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Security Implications of Visual
Appearances}\label{_security_implications_of_visual_appearances}

\subsection{The Appearance/Content
Disconnect}\label{_the_appearancecontent_disconnect}

The visual appearance of a signature is stored in the PDF's object
structure, which is covered by the byte range. But the appearance is not
cryptographically bound to the \emph{identity} of the signer. The
appearance can show anything --- "Signed by Alice" while the CMS is
signed by Bob's certificate.

A conforming viewer should display the certificate information from the
CMS (not from the appearance) in its signature properties panel. But
what the user \emph{sees} on the page is the appearance --- and most
users trust the visual appearance more than the signature properties
panel.

This is a deliberate design trade-off in PDF: the signer (or the signing
application) controls what the appearance looks like, and the viewer
overlays validity information. The validity indicators (checkmarks,
status banners) come from the viewer's own analysis of the CMS --- they
don't come from the appearance stream.

\subsection{Shadow Attacks and Visual
Appearances}\label{_shadow_attacks_and_visual_appearances}

Shadow Attacks (Chapter 24) exploit the gap between what a signer sees
and what the document contains. In the \textbf{Replace} variant, the
attacker creates a form field with a specific visual appearance, then
changes the field's value (and thus its appearance) after the signer
certifies the document. Because the certification allows form filling
(P=2 or P=3), the change is permitted --- but the visual content of the
document has changed dramatically.

The defense is the same as for all Shadow Attacks: validate not just the
cryptographic integrity but also the \emph{semantic} integrity --- what
changed between revisions, and were those changes consistent with the
displayed content?

\textbf{For maximum security}: When validating a signed PDF, don't just
check the signature's validity. Extract the revision that was signed (by
truncating the file at the byte range end --- see Chapter 13) and
compare its visual rendering to the current document. If they differ,
the document has been modified since signing --- even if the
modifications were technically "permitted" by the MDP level.

\section{Common Appearance Problems}\label{_common_appearance_problems}

\subsection{Problem: Blank Signature
Rectangle}\label{_problem_blank_signature_rectangle}

\textbf{Symptom}: The signature field shows as an empty box in the
viewer.

\textbf{Causes}:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  No appearance stream set (the \texttt{/AP} dictionary is missing)
\item
  \texttt{/NeedAppearances} is \texttt{true}, and the viewer regenerated
  an empty appearance
\item
  The Form XObject has an empty stream (no drawing instructions)
\item
  The bounding box (\texttt{/BBox}) is {[}0 0 0 0{]} or has zero
  dimensions
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Problem: Text Appears Clipped or
Off-Screen}\label{_problem_text_appears_clipped_or_off_screen}

\textbf{Symptom}: Only part of the signature text is visible, or the
text appears outside the signature rectangle.

\textbf{Causes}:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The text positioning (\texttt{Td} operators) exceeds the Form
  XObject's \texttt{/BBox}
\item
  The font size is too large for the available space
\item
  The \texttt{/BBox} doesn't match the annotation's \texttt{/Rect}
  dimensions
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Fix}: Ensure the Form XObject's \texttt{/BBox} width and height
match the annotation \texttt{/Rect} dimensions, and that all drawing
instructions fall within the \texttt{/BBox}.

\subsection{Problem: Font Not
Rendering}\label{_problem_font_not_rendering}

\textbf{Symptom}: Text appears as squares, dots, or is completely
invisible.

\textbf{Causes}:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The font resource is not declared in the Form XObject's
  \texttt{/Resources}
\item
  The font name in the content stream (\texttt{/F1}) doesn't match the
  resource key
\item
  Non-ASCII characters used with a non-embedded Standard 14 font
  (Standard 14 fonts only support WinAnsiEncoding)
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Fix}: For text beyond basic Latin characters (accented letters,
CJK, etc.), embed a TrueType or Type0 font. Standard 14 fonts
(Helvetica, Times, Courier, etc.) only support a limited character set.

\section{Key Takeaways}\label{_key_takeaways_3}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Signature appearance is an \textbf{annotation appearance stream} --- a
  Form XObject with drawing instructions
\item
  The appearance is \textbf{visually independent} from the cryptographic
  signature --- they can show different things
\item
  \textbf{Invisible signatures} have zero-size rectangles or no
  appearance stream --- they exist cryptographically but don't show on
  the page
\item
  The \textbf{n0/n2/n4 layer model} is Adobe's implementation detail,
  not a spec requirement --- create a single Form XObject for maximum
  compatibility
\item
  \textbf{PDFBox 3.x} requires writing raw PDF operators to Form XObject
  streams --- \texttt{PDPageContentStream} only works for pages
\item
  \textbf{Always set \texttt{/NeedAppearances} to \texttt{false}} before
  signing to prevent viewers from regenerating your custom appearance
\item
  \textbf{Test in multiple viewers} --- Acrobat, Foxit, LibreOffice, and
  browsers all render appearances differently
\item
  The \textbf{coordinate system} is bottom-left origin with y going up
  --- account for page rotation when positioning
\item
  Visual appearances are a \textbf{security surface} --- Shadow Attacks
  exploit the disconnect between appearance and content
\end{itemize}

\chapter{Signature Validation}\label{_signature_validation}

Signing a PDF is the easy part. Validating a signed PDF --- correctly,
securely, and in a way that matches what other validators produce --- is
where the real complexity lives. A signature can fail validation for
dozens of reasons, and the error messages from different viewers are
often vague or contradictory. Adobe says "valid." LibreOffice says
"signature cannot be verified." Your custom validator throws a
\texttt{CMSException}. Which one is right?

This chapter walks through the complete validation process, from the
first byte range check to the final certificate chain verification. We
cover what each validation step checks, what can go wrong at each step,
how different viewers handle each step, and how to debug validation
failures systematically.

\section{The Five Steps of Signature
Validation}\label{_the_five_steps_of_signature_validation}

Signature validation is not a single check --- it is a pipeline of five
independent verifications, each of which must pass. A failure at any
step means the signature is invalid (or at least questionable),
regardless of whether the other steps pass.

\subsection{Step 1: Structural Integrity (Byte
Range)}\label{_step_1_structural_integrity_byte_range}

\textbf{Question answered}: "Does the signature cover the expected bytes
of the file?"

\textbf{What to check}:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  The \texttt{/ByteRange} array has exactly 4 elements:
  \texttt{{[}offset1\ length1\ offset2\ length2{]}}
\item
  \texttt{offset1} is 0 (signature covers from the start of the file)
\item
  \texttt{offset2\ +\ length2} equals the file size (or revision size
  for multi-signature documents)
\item
  The gap \texttt{{[}length1,\ offset2)} contains only the
  \texttt{/Contents} hex string
\item
  No trailing unsigned bytes exist after the signed region (unless they
  are a subsequent legitimate revision)
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Common failures}:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Trailing bytes after the signed region --- possible Incremental Saving
  Attack (Chapter 24)
\item
  ByteRange doesn't start at 0 --- broken signing implementation
\item
  Gap contains unexpected data --- corrupted or manually edited PDF
\end{itemize}

Chapter 13 covers this step in exhaustive detail.

\subsection{Step 2: Hash Verification (Digest
Match)}\label{_step_2_hash_verification_digest_match}

\textbf{Question answered}: "Does the hash of the signed bytes match
what the CMS claims was signed?"

\textbf{What to check}:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Read the digest algorithm from the CMS
  \texttt{SignerInfo.digestAlgorithm}
\item
  Compute the hash over the byte range segments:
  \texttt{hash\ =\ algo(segment1\ \textbar{}\textbar{}\ segment2)}
\item
  Extract the \texttt{messageDigest} attribute from the CMS
  \texttt{signedAttrs}
\item
  Compare the computed hash to the CMS-provided hash
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Common failures}:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Hash algorithm mismatch --- you computed SHA-256 but the CMS used
  SHA-384
\item
  Byte range computation error --- you read the wrong bytes
\item
  Modified file --- someone changed the file after signing
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Step 2: Compute and compare hash}
\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ digestAlgOid }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signerInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDigestAlgOID}\OperatorTok{();}
\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ algName }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{mapOidToAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{digestAlgOid}\OperatorTok{);}
\BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\NormalTok{ digest }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{algName}\OperatorTok{);}

\DataTypeTok{int}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ byteRange }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getByteRange}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{digest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{update}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{],}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{]);}
\NormalTok{digest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{update}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{],}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{3}\OperatorTok{]);}
\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ computedHash }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ digest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{digest}\OperatorTok{();}

\BuiltInTok{Attribute}\NormalTok{ digestAttr }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signerInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignedAttributes}\OperatorTok{()}
        \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{CMSAttributes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{messageDigest}\OperatorTok{);}
\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ claimedHash }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ASN1OctetString}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{        digestAttr}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAttrValues}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getObjectAt}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)).}\FunctionTok{getOctets}\OperatorTok{();}

\ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(!}\BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{isEqual}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{computedHash}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ claimedHash}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{error}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Hash mismatch — document was modified after signing"}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ ValidationResult}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HASH\_MISMATCH}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Step 3: Cryptographic Verification (CMS
Signature)}\label{_step_3_cryptographic_verification_cms_signature}

\textbf{Question answered}: "Was the CMS \texttt{SignedData} actually
created by the claimed signer's private key?"

\textbf{What to check}:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Extract the signer's certificate from the CMS
  \texttt{SignedData.certificates} collection
\item
  Verify that the \texttt{SignerInfo.signatureAlgorithm} matches the
  certificate's key type (RSA cert with RSA signature, ECDSA cert with
  ECDSA signature)
\item
  Verify the signature over the \texttt{signedAttrs} using the signer's
  public key
\item
  Verify that the signed attributes include the required fields:
  \texttt{contentType}, \texttt{messageDigest}, and (for PAdES)
  \texttt{ESS-signing-certificate-v2}
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Common failures}:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Wrong certificate used for verification --- the CMS contains multiple
  certificates, and you picked an intermediate instead of the signer
\item
  Algorithm mismatch --- rare, but can happen with misconfigured
  BouncyCastle providers
\item
  Missing \texttt{ESS-signing-certificate-v2} --- not a cryptographic
  failure, but a PAdES compliance failure
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Step 3: CMS cryptographic verification}
\NormalTok{CMSSignedData cmsData }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CMSSignedData}\OperatorTok{(}
        \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CMSProcessableByteArray}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedBytes}\OperatorTok{),}\NormalTok{ signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getContents}\OperatorTok{());}

\NormalTok{SignerInformationStore signerStore }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cmsData}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignerInfos}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{SignerInformation signerInfo }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signerStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSigners}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{iterator}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{next}\OperatorTok{();}

\CommentTok{// Find the signer\textquotesingle{}s certificate}
\NormalTok{X509CertificateHolder signerCert }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{findSignerCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{        cmsData}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificates}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ signerInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSID}\OperatorTok{());}

\CommentTok{// Verify the CMS signature}
\DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ cmsValid }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signerInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{verify}\OperatorTok{(}
        \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaSimpleSignerInfoVerifierBuilder}\OperatorTok{()}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{)}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signerCert}\OperatorTok{));}

\ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(!}\NormalTok{cmsValid}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{error}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"CMS signature verification failed — "} \OperatorTok{+}
              \StringTok{"signature was not created by the claimed certificate"}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ ValidationResult}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{CMS\_INVALID}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Step 4: Certificate Chain
Validation}\label{_step_4_certificate_chain_validation}

\textbf{Question answered}: "Is the signer's certificate trustworthy?
Was it valid at the time of signing?"

\textbf{What to check}:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Build the certificate chain from the signer's certificate up to a
  trusted root
\item
  Verify each certificate's signature (each cert is signed by its
  issuer)
\item
  Check validity periods --- was each certificate valid at the signing
  time?
\item
  Check revocation status --- was any certificate revoked at or before
  the signing time?
\item
  Check key usage --- does the signer's certificate allow digital
  signatures (\texttt{digitalSignature} or \texttt{nonRepudiation} key
  usage)?
\item
  Check basic constraints --- are intermediate certificates properly
  marked as CA certificates?
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Common failures}:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Self-signed certificate}: The signer's certificate is not
  issued by a recognized CA. This is technically valid (the CMS
  signature will verify) but the certificate is not trusted. Most
  viewers show "signature is valid but the identity of the signer could
  not be verified."
\item
  \textbf{Expired certificate}: The certificate was valid when the
  signature was created, but has since expired. With timestamps, this is
  not a problem (the timestamp proves the signature was created before
  expiration). Without timestamps, viewers may warn about an expired
  certificate.
\item
  \textbf{Revoked certificate}: A certificate in the chain was revoked
  (compromised, or the CA issued it in error). Revocation checking
  requires OCSP or CRL lookup --- which may fail if the revocation
  servers are unreachable.
\item
  \textbf{Missing intermediate}: The CMS doesn't include all
  intermediate certificates, and the validator can't build the chain.
  This is the most common real-world certificate chain failure.
\item
  \textbf{Not in trust store}: The root certificate is not in the
  validator's trust store. Different validators have different trust
  stores --- Adobe uses AATL, operating systems use their own, and EU
  validators use the EUTL.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Step 5: Trust Evaluation}\label{_step_5_trust_evaluation}

\textbf{Question answered}: "Do I trust the entity that signed this
document?"

This is the most subjective step, and it's where validators diverge the
most. Trust evaluation is not a purely technical question --- it depends
on context:

\textbf{Adobe Acrobat trust model}: Acrobat trusts certificates that
chain to a root in the \textbf{Adobe Approved Trust List (AATL)}. If the
certificate chains to an AATL root, Acrobat shows a green checkmark. If
not --- even if the certificate is perfectly valid, issued by a
reputable CA, and chains to a root in the operating system's trust store
--- Acrobat shows a yellow warning: "At least one signature has
problems."

This is one of the most frustrating aspects of PDF signature validation
for developers. Your signature is cryptographically valid, the
certificate is issued by a recognized CA, the certificate is not expired
or revoked --- but Acrobat shows a warning because the CA is not on
Adobe's list. Getting on the AATL requires the CA to apply to Adobe and
pay fees.

\textbf{EU validation model}: EU validators (like the DSS demonstration
webapp, or validators used by EU government services) trust certificates
that chain to a root in the \textbf{EU Trusted List (EUTL)}. The EUTL is
the authoritative registry of Qualified Trust Service Providers. A
signature is a "qualified electronic signature" --- with the legal
weight of a handwritten signature under eIDAS --- only if the
certificate chains to an EUTL-listed QTSP.

\textbf{Operating system trust model}: Some applications (like
LibreOffice) use the operating system's certificate store for trust
evaluation. This means the set of trusted roots varies by OS and by the
user's system configuration.

\textbf{Custom trust model}: Your application can define its own trust
store --- a specific set of root certificates that you trust for your
use case. This is common in enterprise environments where the signing
certificates are issued by an internal CA.

\textbf{When debugging trust failures, ask three questions:}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  What trust store is the validator using? (AATL, EUTL, OS store,
  custom)
\item
  Is the signer's root certificate in that trust store?
\item
  Are all intermediates available? (Either embedded in the CMS or
  fetchable via the certificate's Authority Information Access
  extension)
\end{enumerate}

The answer to question 1 determines everything else. A signature can be
"valid" in one validator and "untrusted" in another, purely because of
trust store differences.

\section{Validation Differences Across
Viewers}\label{_validation_differences_across_viewers}

One of the most confusing aspects of PDF signature validation is that
different viewers produce different results for the same PDF. This is
not a bug --- it's a consequence of the five-step validation model,
where each viewer makes different choices at each step.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}@{}}
\caption{Validation behavior comparison across major PDF
viewers}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Check
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Acrobat
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Foxit
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
LibreOffice
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
PDFBox
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
EU DSS
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Check
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Acrobat
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Foxit
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
LibreOffice
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
PDFBox
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
EU DSS
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Byte range trailing bytes & Warns & Warns & Partial & Manual & Warns \\
Hash verification & Yes & Yes & Yes & Manual & Yes \\
CMS signature & Yes & Yes & Yes & Manual & Yes \\
Certificate chain & AATL & Mixed & OS store & Manual & EUTL \\
Revocation (OCSP) & Yes & Yes & Limited & Manual & Yes \\
Revocation (CRL) & Yes & Yes & Yes & Manual & Yes \\
MDP enforcement & Full & Mostly & Partial & None & Full \\
Timestamp verification & Yes & Yes & Partial & Manual & Yes \\
PAdES compliance & Partial & Partial & No & Manual & Full \\
\end{longtable}

"Manual" for PDFBox means the library provides the primitives (signature
extraction, byte range access, CMS parsing) but does not perform the
validation automatically --- you must write the validation logic
yourself. This is by design: PDFBox is a PDF library, not a validation
service.

EU DSS provides the most complete automated validation, including
ETSI-compliant validation reports.

\section{Debugging Validation
Failures}\label{_debugging_validation_failures}

\subsection{The Systematic Approach}\label{_the_systematic_approach}

When a signature fails validation, resist the urge to guess. Instead,
walk through the five steps systematically:

\textbf{Step 1: Is the byte range intact?}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Check byte range with QPDF}
\ExtensionTok{qpdf} \AttributeTok{{-}{-}show{-}object}\OperatorTok{=\textless{}}\NormalTok{sig{-}obj{-}number}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ document.pdf}
\CommentTok{\# Look at /ByteRange — does offset2 + length2 == file size?}
\FunctionTok{wc} \AttributeTok{{-}c}\NormalTok{ document.pdf}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Step 2: Does the hash match?}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Compute the hash yourself and compare to the CMS messageDigest}
\CommentTok{// (Code in Chapter 13)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Step 3: Does the CMS verify?}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Extract /Contents, decode hex, verify with OpenSSL}
\ExtensionTok{openssl}\NormalTok{ cms }\AttributeTok{{-}inform}\NormalTok{ DER }\AttributeTok{{-}in}\NormalTok{ signature.der }\AttributeTok{{-}verify} \DataTypeTok{\textbackslash{}}
    \AttributeTok{{-}content}\NormalTok{ signed{-}bytes.bin }\AttributeTok{{-}noverify} \AttributeTok{{-}out}\NormalTok{ /dev/null}
\CommentTok{\# {-}noverify skips certificate verification — tests only the CMS math}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Step 4: Is the certificate chain valid?}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Extract the signer\textquotesingle{}s certificate and verify the chain}
\ExtensionTok{openssl}\NormalTok{ cms }\AttributeTok{{-}inform}\NormalTok{ DER }\AttributeTok{{-}in}\NormalTok{ signature.der }\AttributeTok{{-}noout} \AttributeTok{{-}cmsout} \AttributeTok{{-}print} \KeywordTok{|} \DataTypeTok{\textbackslash{}}
    \FunctionTok{grep} \AttributeTok{{-}A}\NormalTok{ 5 }\StringTok{"cert:"}
\ExtensionTok{openssl}\NormalTok{ verify }\AttributeTok{{-}CAfile}\NormalTok{ trusted{-}roots.pem signer{-}cert.pem}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Step 5: Is the certificate trusted?}

Check the signer's root against the relevant trust store (AATL list,
EUTL, OS store).

\subsection{Common Error Messages and Their
Meaning}\label{_common_error_messages_and_their_meaning}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4444}}@{}}
\caption{PDF signature error messages decoded}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Error Message (Viewer)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Step
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What It Actually Means
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Error Message (Viewer)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Step
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What It Actually Means
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
"Signature is INVALID" (Acrobat) & 2 or 3 & Hash mismatch or CMS
verification failure. The document was modified after signing, or the
CMS is malformed. \\
"At least one signature has problems" (Acrobat) & 5 & The certificate
does not chain to an AATL root. The signature may be cryptographically
perfect --- Acrobat just doesn't trust the CA. \\
"Document has been altered or corrupted since the Signature was applied"
(Acrobat) & 1 or MDP & Either the byte range doesn't cover the full file
(trailing bytes), or modifications after signing exceed the MDP
permissions. \\
"Signature is valid, but the identity of the signer could not be
verified" (Acrobat) & 4 or 5 & CMS is valid, but the certificate chain
is incomplete or the root is not trusted. \\
"The signature cannot be verified" (LibreOffice) & 3, 4, or 5 & Generic
error --- LibreOffice doesn't distinguish well between CMS failure and
trust failure. Check the signature details panel. \\
\texttt{CMSException:\ message-digest\ attribute\ value\ does\ not\ match\ calculated\ value}
(BouncyCastle) & 2 & Hash mismatch. The bytes you passed to the verifier
don't match what was signed. \\
\texttt{CertPathValidatorException:\ validity\ check\ failed} (Java) & 4
& A certificate in the chain has expired. \\
\texttt{CertPathValidatorException:\ no\ valid\ CRL\ found} (Java) & 4 &
Revocation checking failed because the CRL distribution point is
unreachable or the CRL is not cached. \\
\end{longtable}

\section{Timestamp Verification}\label{_timestamp_verification}

\subsection{Why Timestamps Matter for
Validation}\label{_why_timestamps_matter_for_validation}

A digital signature proves that a specific certificate holder signed
specific bytes. But it doesn't prove \emph{when} the signing happened.
Without a timestamp, you can only say "the signature was created
sometime before now."

This matters because certificates expire and can be revoked. If a
certificate was valid on March 1 but expired on March 31, and you
validate the signature on April 15:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Without timestamp}: The certificate is expired. Some
  validators will flag the signature as questionable.
\item
  \textbf{With timestamp}: The timestamp proves the signature was
  created on March 1, when the certificate was valid. The signature
  remains valid despite the certificate's expiration.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Verifying the Timestamp}\label{_verifying_the_timestamp}

A signature timestamp is embedded as an unsigned attribute
(\texttt{id-aa-signatureTimeStampToken}, OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.14)
in the CMS \texttt{SignerInfo}:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Extract and verify the signature timestamp}
\NormalTok{AttributeTable unsignedAttrs }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signerInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getUnsignedAttributes}\OperatorTok{();}
\ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{unsignedAttrs }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \BuiltInTok{Attribute}\NormalTok{ tsAttr }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ unsignedAttrs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            PKCSObjectIdentifiers}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{id\_aa\_signatureTimeStampToken}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsAttr }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        CMSSignedData tsToken }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CMSSignedData}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                tsAttr}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getAttrValues}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getObjectAt}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{toASN1Primitive}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{());}

        \CommentTok{// Verify the timestamp\textquotesingle{}s CMS signature}
\NormalTok{        SignerInformation tsSigner }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ tsToken}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignerInfos}\OperatorTok{()}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSigners}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{iterator}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{next}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        X509CertificateHolder tsCert }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{findSignerCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                tsToken}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificates}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ tsSigner}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSID}\OperatorTok{());}

        \DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ tsValid }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ tsSigner}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{verify}\OperatorTok{(}
                \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaSimpleSignerInfoVerifierBuilder}\OperatorTok{()}
                        \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsCert}\OperatorTok{));}

        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsValid}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \CommentTok{// Extract the timestamp time}
\NormalTok{            TimeStampToken tst }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{TimeStampToken}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsToken}\OperatorTok{);}
            \BuiltInTok{Date}\NormalTok{ signingTime }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ tst}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTimeStampInfo}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getGenTime}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{            log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Signature was timestamped at: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signingTime}\OperatorTok{);}

            \CommentTok{// Verify that the timestamp covers the signature value}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ tsDigest }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ tst}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTimeStampInfo}\OperatorTok{()}
                    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getMessageImprintDigest}\OperatorTok{();}
            \CommentTok{// This digest should match the hash of the signature value}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Timestamp Chain of Trust}\label{_timestamp_chain_of_trust}

The timestamp itself is a CMS signature --- signed by the TSA's
certificate. This certificate has its own chain and trust requirements:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  The TSA certificate must have the \texttt{id-kp-timeStamping} extended
  key usage (OID 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.8)
\item
  The TSA certificate chain must be valid at the time of verification
\item
  The TSA's root must be in your trust store
\end{enumerate}

If the TSA's certificate has also expired by the time you validate
(years later), you need \textbf{long-term validation (LTV)} --- which is
the subject of Chapter 18.

\section{Building a Complete Validation
Pipeline}\label{_building_a_complete_validation_pipeline}

\subsection{Architecture}\label{_architecture}

A production validation pipeline typically follows this structure:

\begin{verbatim}
Input: PDF file
  │
  ├─→ Step 1: Extract all signatures
  │     └─→ For each signature:
  │           ├─→ Step 2: Byte range check
  │           ├─→ Step 3: Hash verification
  │           ├─→ Step 4: CMS verification
  │           ├─→ Step 5: Certificate chain
  │           ├─→ Step 6: Revocation check
  │           ├─→ Step 7: Timestamp verification (if present)
  │           ├─→ Step 8: MDP compliance (if certification sig)
  │           └─→ Step 9: Trust evaluation
  │
  └─→ Output: Validation report
        ├─→ Per-signature results
        ├─→ Overall document status
        └─→ Detailed diagnostics
\end{verbatim}

Chapter 31 provides a complete Java implementation of this pipeline.

\subsection{When to Use EU DSS
Instead}\label{_when_to_use_eu_dss_instead}

If you need to produce \textbf{ETSI-compliant validation reports} (ETSI
TS 119 102-2) --- for example, because you're building a validation
service for a government or regulated industry --- use the EU DSS
framework (Chapter 23) instead of building the pipeline yourself. EU DSS
handles all validation steps, produces standards-compliant reports, and
has been tested against the full range of PAdES profiles and edge cases.

Building your own pipeline is appropriate when:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  You need custom trust evaluation logic
\item
  You need to validate signatures in a specific context (e.g., your own
  application's signing workflow)
\item
  You want to understand every step of the validation process (which is
  the point of this book)
\item
  You're building a specialized tool (forensic analysis, security
  research)
\end{itemize}

\section{Key Takeaways}\label{_key_takeaways_4}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Signature validation is a \textbf{five-step pipeline}: byte range →
  hash → CMS → certificate chain → trust
\item
  Each step is independent --- a failure at any step means the signature
  is invalid or questionable
\item
  \textbf{Different viewers produce different results} because they use
  different trust stores and enforce different rules
\item
  Adobe Acrobat uses \textbf{AATL} for trust; EU validators use
  \textbf{EUTL}; LibreOffice uses the \textbf{OS trust store}
\item
  The most common real-world failure is \textbf{trust evaluation} ---
  the signature is cryptographically perfect but the root CA isn't in
  the validator's trust store
\item
  \textbf{Timestamps} prove when a signature was created --- essential
  for handling expired certificates and building long-term validation
\item
  \textbf{Debug systematically}: walk through the five steps in order,
  using QPDF, OpenSSL, and BouncyCastle to isolate the failure
\item
  \textbf{EU DSS} provides the most complete automated validation with
  ETSI-compliant reports
\item
  PDFBox provides the \textbf{primitives} for validation but does not
  automate the pipeline --- you build it yourself
\end{itemize}

\chapter{PAdES Profiles (ETSI EN 319
142)}\label{_pades_profiles_etsi_en_319_142}

ISO 32000-2 defines \emph{how} a digital signature is embedded in a PDF.
PAdES defines \emph{what} that signature must contain to be legally
meaningful in Europe --- and, increasingly, everywhere else.

PAdES --- PDF Advanced Electronic Signatures --- is a set of standards
published by the \textbf{European Telecommunications Standards Institute
(ETSI)} that constrain PDF signatures to meet specific compliance
requirements. A PAdES signature is always a valid ISO 32000-2 signature,
but the reverse is not true. PAdES adds mandatory signed attributes,
requires specific SubFilter values, mandates timestamp handling, and
defines four progressively more complete signature profiles.

Understanding PAdES is not optional if your signatures need to be
validated by European government services, if your customers require
eIDAS-compliant signatures, or if you want your signatures to remain
verifiable for years or decades. Even if you're not operating in the EU,
PAdES profiles have become the de facto standard for "properly
structured" PDF signatures worldwide --- Adobe Acrobat increasingly
favors PAdES-structured signatures in its validation logic.

This chapter explains each PAdES profile, what it requires, how the
profiles relate to each other, and how to produce each one.

\section{The Four PAdES Baseline
Profiles}\label{_the_four_pades_baseline_profiles}

PAdES defines four \textbf{baseline profiles}, each building on the
previous one. They are formally named PAdES-B-B, PAdES-B-T, PAdES-B-LT,
and PAdES-B-LTA. The naming follows the pattern:
PAdES-B(aseline)-\textless level\textgreater.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.6000}}@{}}
\caption{PAdES Baseline Profile Summary}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Profile
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Full Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What It Adds
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Profile
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Full Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What It Adds
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\textbf{B-B} & Baseline --- Basic & The minimum PAdES signature. CMS
\texttt{SignedData} with mandatory signed attributes including
\texttt{ESS-signing-certificate-v2}. The signature itself --- no
timestamps, no revocation data. \\
\textbf{B-T} & Baseline --- Timestamp & B-B + a \textbf{signature
timestamp} from a trusted TSA. Proves when the signature was created.
The timestamp is an unsigned CMS attribute
(\texttt{id-aa-signatureTimeStampToken}). \\
\textbf{B-LT} & Baseline --- Long-Term & B-T + embedded
\textbf{revocation data} (OCSP responses and/or CRLs) and the full
certificate chain in the PDF's \textbf{DSS} (Document Security Store)
dictionary. Everything needed to validate the signature offline, without
contacting any external servers. \\
\textbf{B-LTA} & Baseline --- Long-Term with Archive timestamp & B-LT +
a \textbf{document timestamp} signature that covers the entire file
(including the DSS dictionary). Enables re-timestamping decades later,
creating an indefinite chain of trust that outlives any individual
certificate. \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{The Progression: Why Four
Levels?}\label{_the_progression_why_four_levels}

Each profile addresses a specific failure mode:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{B-B} fails when: the signing certificate expires (validators
  may warn), or when you need to prove \emph{when} the signature was
  created (there's no timestamp)
\item
  \textbf{B-T} fails when: the OCSP responder or CRL distribution point
  goes offline years later, and you can't check revocation status
\item
  \textbf{B-LT} fails when: the timestamp's certificate, or the signing
  certificate's root CA, expires or is removed from trust stores decades
  later
\item
  \textbf{B-LTA} handles all of these by providing a renewable chain of
  timestamps
\end{itemize}

In practice:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{B-B} is sufficient for short-lived documents that will be
  validated within the signing certificate's validity period (typically
  1-3 years)
\item
  \textbf{B-T} is the minimum for any document with legal significance
  --- you need to prove when it was signed
\item
  \textbf{B-LT} is required for long-term archival (10+ years) where you
  can't assume external services will remain available
\item
  \textbf{B-LTA} is the gold standard for documents that must be
  verifiable indefinitely --- government records, notarized documents,
  regulatory filings
\end{itemize}

\section{PAdES-B-B: The Baseline
Signature}\label{_pades_b_b_the_baseline_signature}

\subsection{Requirements}\label{_requirements}

A PAdES-B-B signature must include:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{SubFilter}: Must be \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached} (not
  \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached})
\item
  \textbf{Mandatory signed attributes in CMS}:

  \begin{itemize}
  \item
    \texttt{content-type} (OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.3) --- must be
    \texttt{id-data}
  \item
    \texttt{message-digest} (OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.4) --- the hash of
    the signed bytes
  \item
    \texttt{ESS-signing-certificate-v2} (OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.47)
    --- identifies the signing certificate by its hash, preventing
    certificate substitution attacks
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Signing certificate embedded in CMS}: The signer's certificate
  (and ideally the full chain up to but not including the root) must be
  embedded in the CMS \texttt{SignedData.certificates} collection
\item
  \textbf{Digest algorithm}: Must be SHA-256 or stronger (SHA-384,
  SHA-512). SHA-1 is \textbf{not} PAdES-compliant.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{The ESS-signing-certificate-v2
Attribute}\label{_the_ess_signing_certificate_v2_attribute_2}

This attribute is the key difference between a basic ISO 32000-2
signature and a PAdES-B-B signature. It binds the CMS signature to a
specific certificate by including a hash of the certificate in the
signed attributes.

Why this matters: without \texttt{ESS-signing-certificate-v2}, an
attacker could theoretically substitute a different certificate into the
CMS \texttt{certificates} collection --- one with the same public key
but different identity information. The CMS signature would still verify
(same key), but the identity would be wrong. The ESS attribute prevents
this by cryptographically committing to a specific certificate.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Creating the ESS{-}signing{-}certificate{-}v2 attribute with BouncyCastle}
\BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\NormalTok{ certDigest }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"SHA{-}256"}\OperatorTok{);}
\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ certHash }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ certDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{digest}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signingCertificate}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{());}

\NormalTok{ESSCertIDv2 essCertId }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{ESSCertIDv2}\OperatorTok{(}
        \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{AlgorithmIdentifier}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{NISTObjectIdentifiers}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{id\_sha256}\OperatorTok{),}
\NormalTok{        certHash}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{SigningCertificateV2 signingCertV2 }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{SigningCertificateV2}\OperatorTok{(}
        \KeywordTok{new}\NormalTok{ ESSCertIDv2}\OperatorTok{[]} \OperatorTok{\{}\NormalTok{ essCertId }\OperatorTok{\});}

\CommentTok{// Add to signed attributes}
\NormalTok{CMSAttributeTableGenerator signedAttrGen }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{DefaultSignedAttributeTableGenerator}\OperatorTok{(}
        \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{AttributeTable}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{Hashtable}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}()} \OperatorTok{\{\{}
            \FunctionTok{put}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PKCSObjectIdentifiers}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{id\_aa\_signingCertificateV2}\OperatorTok{,}
                \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{Attribute}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PKCSObjectIdentifiers}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{id\_aa\_signingCertificateV2}\OperatorTok{,}
                    \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{DERSet}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signingCertV2}\OperatorTok{)));}
        \OperatorTok{\}\}));}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

A common mistake: using \texttt{ESS-signing-certificate} (v1, OID
1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.12) instead of
\texttt{ESS-signing-certificate-v2} (OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.47).
The v1 attribute uses SHA-1 for the certificate hash, which is
deprecated. PAdES requires v2, which uses SHA-256 by default.

iText 2.x (and old versions of some other libraries) produced v1
attributes. If you encounter a PDF with \texttt{ESS-signing-certificate}
(v1), it's likely from a legacy signing implementation.

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{SubFilter: \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached} vs.
\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached}}{SubFilter: ETSI.CAdES.detached vs. adbe.pkcs7.detached}}\label{_subfilter_etsi_cades_detached_vs_adbe_pkcs7_detached}

PAdES requires the SubFilter value \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached}. This is
structurally identical to \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} --- both produce
a detached CMS signature embedded in the \texttt{/Contents} hex string.
The difference is semantic: \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached} signals that
the signature follows the CAdES (CMS Advanced Electronic Signatures)
profile, which includes the ESS-signing-certificate-v2 attribute and
other CAdES requirements.

In practice, many validators accept both SubFilter values for PAdES
validation. Adobe Acrobat treats \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached} and
\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} similarly. But for strict PAdES compliance
--- especially when dealing with EU government validators --- use
\texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached}.

\section{PAdES-B-T: Adding a
Timestamp}\label{_pades_b_t_adding_a_timestamp}

\subsection{What B-T Adds}\label{_what_b_t_adds}

PAdES-B-T is PAdES-B-B plus a \textbf{signature timestamp} --- a
timestamp token from a Trusted Timestamp Authority (TSA) that proves
when the signature was created.

The timestamp is embedded as an \textbf{unsigned attribute}
(\texttt{id-aa-signatureTimeStampToken}, OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.14)
in the CMS \texttt{SignerInfo}. It's unsigned because it's applied
\emph{after} the signature --- you sign first, then request a timestamp
over the signature value.

\subsection{How Timestamping Works}\label{_how_timestamping_works}

The timestamp workflow:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Sign the document} --- produce the CMS \texttt{SignedData}
  with all signed attributes (B-B level)
\item
  \textbf{Hash the signature value} --- compute SHA-256 over the CMS
  \texttt{SignerInfo.signature} bytes
\item
  \textbf{Send a timestamp request} --- send the hash to a TSA using RFC
  3161 (HTTP POST to the TSA's URL)
\item
  \textbf{Receive the timestamp response} --- the TSA returns a
  \texttt{TimeStampToken} (which is itself a CMS \texttt{SignedData}
  signed by the TSA's certificate)
\item
  \textbf{Embed the timestamp} --- add the \texttt{TimeStampToken} as an
  unsigned attribute in the CMS
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Requesting a timestamp from a TSA}
\NormalTok{TimeStampRequestGenerator tsReqGen }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{TimeStampRequestGenerator}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{tsReqGen}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCertReq}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// Request TSA\textquotesingle{}s certificate in the response}

\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ signatureHash }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"SHA{-}256"}\OperatorTok{)}
        \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{digest}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signerInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignature}\OperatorTok{());}

\NormalTok{TimeStampRequest tsReq }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ tsReqGen}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generate}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{        TSPAlgorithms}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SHA256}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signatureHash}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// Send to TSA via HTTP}
\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ tsReqBytes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ tsReq}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{();}
\BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\NormalTok{ conn }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\OperatorTok{)}
        \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{URL}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsaUrl}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{openConnection}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDoOutput}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestMethod}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"POST"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestProperty}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Content{-}Type"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"application/timestamp{-}query"}\OperatorTok{);}
