\NormalTok{conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getOutputStream}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsReqBytes}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{TimeStampResponse tsResp }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{TimeStampResponse}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInputStream}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{tsResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{validate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsReq}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// Verify the response matches our request}

\NormalTok{TimeStampToken tsToken }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ tsResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTimeStampToken}\OperatorTok{();}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Choosing a TSA}\label{_choosing_a_tsa}

Choosing a TSA is a non-trivial decision. The TSA's certificate must be
trusted by the validators that will check your signatures. Key
considerations:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{For Adobe Acrobat}: The TSA's root must be in the AATL, or the
  timestamp will show a warning
\item
  \textbf{For EU compliance}: The TSA should be a Qualified Trust
  Service Provider (QTSP) listed in the EUTL
\item
  \textbf{For availability}: The TSA must be reachable when you sign ---
  if the TSA is down, your signing fails (or you produce a B-B signature
  without a timestamp)
\item
  \textbf{For cost}: Some TSAs are free (FreeTSA, DigiCert's free tier),
  others charge per timestamp or per certificate
\end{itemize}

Chapter 18 covers timestamps in depth, including TSA selection, failover
strategies, and the Document Security Store.

\section{PAdES-B-LT: Long-Term Data}\label{_pades_b_lt_long_term_data}

\subsection{What B-LT Adds}\label{_what_b_lt_adds}

PAdES-B-LT is PAdES-B-T plus \textbf{long-term validation data} embedded
directly in the PDF. This data ensures that the signature can be
validated years from now, even if the signing certificate's CA has gone
out of business, the OCSP responder has been decommissioned, and the CRL
distribution points return 404.

The long-term data is stored in the \textbf{DSS} (Document Security
Store) dictionary, which is added to the document catalog:

\begin{verbatim}
<< /Type /Catalog
   /AcroForm << ... >>
   /DSS <<
     /Certs [30 0 R 31 0 R 32 0 R]       % Certificate chain as DER-encoded streams
     /OCSPs [33 0 R]                       % OCSP responses as DER-encoded streams
     /CRLs [34 0 R]                        % CRLs as DER-encoded streams
     /VRI <<
       /A1B2C3D4... <<                     % Hash-keyed VRI (Validation Related Info)
         /Cert [30 0 R 31 0 R]
         /OCSP [33 0 R]
         /TU (D:20260308120000Z)           % Time of validation
       >>
     >>
   >>
>>
\end{verbatim}

\subsection{The DSS Dictionary}\label{_the_dss_dictionary}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}@{}}
\caption{DSS Dictionary Keys (ISO 32000-2:2020
§12.8.4.3)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Req
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Spec Ref
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/Certs} & array of streams & Optional & §12.8.4.3 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
DER-encoded certificates --- the full chain for every signature and
timestamp in the document. Include the signer's certificate, all
intermediates, and optionally the root.} \\
\texttt{/OCSPs} & array of streams & Optional & §12.8.4.3 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
DER-encoded OCSP responses for certificate revocation checking. Each
response covers a specific certificate at a specific point in time.} \\
\texttt{/CRLs} & array of streams & Optional & §12.8.4.3 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
DER-encoded Certificate Revocation Lists. Used when OCSP is not
available for a certificate.} \\
\texttt{/VRI} & dictionary & Optional & §12.8.4.3 \\
\multicolumn{4}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{1.0000} + 6\tabcolsep}@{}}{%
Validation Related Information --- a dictionary keyed by uppercase hex
SHA-1 hashes of signature values, mapping each signature to its specific
validation data.} \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{Why B-LT Matters}\label{_why_b_lt_matters}

Without B-LT, validating a signature requires contacting external
servers:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  OCSP responder to check revocation status
\item
  CRL distribution point to download the revocation list
\item
  CA's AIA (Authority Information Access) URL to fetch intermediate
  certificates
\end{itemize}

These servers may be unreachable years from now. CAs shut down, merge,
change URLs, or decommission infrastructure. With B-LT, everything
needed for validation is embedded in the PDF itself --- the document is
self-contained.

\section{PAdES-B-LTA: Archival
Timestamps}\label{_pades_b_lta_archival_timestamps}

\subsection{What B-LTA Adds}\label{_what_b_lta_adds}

PAdES-B-LTA is PAdES-B-LT plus a \textbf{document timestamp} --- a
timestamp signature that covers the entire PDF file, including the DSS
dictionary.

This timestamp is not part of the original signature's CMS --- it's a
separate signature dictionary with \texttt{/SubFilter\ /ETSI.RFC3161}:

\begin{verbatim}
<< /Type /Sig
   /Filter /Adobe.PPKLite
   /SubFilter /ETSI.RFC3161
   /ByteRange [0 52000 84776 2100]
   /Contents <3082...>
>>
\end{verbatim}

This timestamp signature covers the entire file (including the original
signature, the DSS, and everything else) and proves that all of this
data existed at the time the timestamp was created.

\subsection{The Archival Chain}\label{_the_archival_chain}

The power of B-LTA is that it creates a \textbf{renewable chain of
trust}:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  The original signature (signed by the signer's certificate, valid for
  1-3 years)
\item
  The signature timestamp (signed by the TSA's certificate, valid for
  10-20 years)
\item
  The archival timestamp (signed by the TSA's certificate, covers
  everything)
\item
  Years later: add another archival timestamp (covers the previous
  archival timestamp)
\item
  Repeat indefinitely
\end{enumerate}

Each archival timestamp extends the provable lifetime of the signature.
Even after the original signing certificate has expired, been revoked,
and the CA has gone out of business --- the chain of archival timestamps
proves that the signature was valid at the time it was created.

This is the mechanism that national archives use to preserve the legal
validity of digitally signed documents for decades.

\subsection{Re-Timestamping}\label{_re_timestamping}

To extend a B-LTA signature's validity:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Fetch fresh OCSP responses / CRLs for the archival timestamp's
  certificate chain
\item
  Add them to the DSS dictionary
\item
  Apply a new archival timestamp (document timestamp) over the entire
  file
\end{enumerate}

This can be done without the original signer's involvement --- it's a
maintenance operation performed by the document custodian.

\textbf{Automated re-timestamping} should be a scheduled process in any
document management system that stores B-LTA signed documents. Before
the current archival timestamp's TSA certificate expires (check the
certificate's \texttt{notAfter} date), apply a new archival timestamp.
This keeps the chain of trust alive.

\section{Profile Comparison Matrix}\label{_profile_comparison_matrix}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}

  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 8\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}@{}}

\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Feature
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
B-B

\endlastfoot
CMS with ESS-signing-certificate-v2 & Yes & Yes & Yes & Yes \\
Signature timestamp & No & Yes & Yes & Yes \\
Embedded revocation data (DSS) & No & No & Yes & Yes \\
Embedded certificate chain (DSS) & No & No & Yes & Yes \\
Document timestamp (archival) & No & No & No & Yes \\
Offline validation & No & Partial & Yes & Yes \\
Validity beyond cert expiry & No & Yes (with TS) & Yes & Yes
(indefinite) \\
EU/eIDAS suitability & Minimal & Short-term & Good & \textbf{Full
compliance} \\
Typical use case & Internal docs & Contracts & Regulated docs &
Archival \\
\end{longtable}

\section{Migrating Between Profiles}\label{_migrating_between_profiles}

One of the elegant properties of PAdES profiles is that you can
\textbf{upgrade} a signature's profile without re-signing:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{B-B → B-T}: Add a timestamp (unsigned attribute to the CMS).
  Requires the original file and the CMS.
\item
  \textbf{B-T → B-LT}: Fetch revocation data and certificates, create
  the DSS dictionary, add as incremental update.
\item
  \textbf{B-LT → B-LTA}: Add a document timestamp covering the entire
  file.
\end{itemize}

This progression means you can sign with B-B today (fast, no TSA
dependency) and upgrade to B-LTA later as a batch operation. EU DSS
provides methods for exactly this:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// EU DSS: Extend a B{-}B signature to B{-}LTA}
\NormalTok{PAdESService service }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PAdESService}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{service}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setTspSource}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tspSource}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{DSSDocument extendedDoc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ service}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{extendDocument}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{    signedDocument}\OperatorTok{,}
    \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PAdESSignatureParameters}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{\{\{}
        \FunctionTok{setSignatureLevel}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{SignatureLevel}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{PAdES\_BASELINE\_LTA}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}\});}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Key Takeaways}\label{_key_takeaways_5}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  PAdES constrains ISO 32000-2 signatures for legal and regulatory
  compliance --- a PAdES signature is always a valid PDF signature, but
  not vice versa
\item
  \textbf{Four profiles}: B-B (basic), B-T (timestamped), B-LT
  (long-term data), B-LTA (archival)
\item
  \textbf{ESS-signing-certificate-v2} is the mandatory attribute that
  makes a signature PAdES-compliant --- without it, you have a valid ISO
  32000-2 signature but not PAdES
\item
  \textbf{SubFilter \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached}} is required for strict
  PAdES compliance
\item
  The \textbf{DSS dictionary} (B-LT and above) embeds certificates, OCSP
  responses, and CRLs directly in the PDF for offline validation
\item
  \textbf{B-LTA} provides indefinite validity through renewable archival
  timestamps
\item
  Profiles can be \textbf{upgraded} without re-signing --- B-B → B-T →
  B-LT → B-LTA as incremental updates
\item
  For EU/eIDAS compliance, \textbf{B-LTA is the target} --- anything
  less may not meet long-term archival requirements
\end{itemize}

\chapter{Timestamps \& Long-Term
Validation}\label{_timestamps_long_term_validation}

A digital signature without a timestamp is like a contract without a
date. You can prove \emph{who} signed and \emph{what} they signed, but
not \emph{when}. And in the world of digital signatures, "when" is often
the most important question --- because certificates expire, keys get
compromised, algorithms get broken, and the difference between "signed
before the certificate expired" and "signed after the certificate
expired" can be the difference between a legally binding document and an
invalid one.

This chapter covers everything about timestamps and long-term validation
(LTV): how RFC 3161 timestamps work, how they're embedded in PDF
signatures, how the Document Security Store (DSS) enables offline
validation, how archival timestamps create indefinite validity, and how
to build a complete LTV infrastructure.

\section{RFC 3161: The Timestamp
Protocol}\label{_rfc_3161_the_timestamp_protocol}

\subsection{What a Timestamp Proves}\label{_what_a_timestamp_proves}

An RFC 3161 timestamp is a cryptographic proof that a specific piece of
data existed at a specific point in time. The proof comes from a
\textbf{Trusted Timestamp Authority (TSA)} --- a server operated by a
trusted third party that:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Receives a hash of the data to be timestamped
\item
  Combines the hash with the current time
\item
  Signs the combination with the TSA's private key
\item
  Returns the signed timestamp token
\end{enumerate}

The timestamp token is a CMS \texttt{SignedData} structure (yes, a
timestamp is itself a CMS signature) containing a \texttt{TSTInfo}
(Timestamp Token Info) structure with:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The hash algorithm and hash value of the timestamped data
\item
  The timestamp time (UTC)
\item
  The TSA's name
\item
  A serial number (unique per timestamp)
\item
  An accuracy indication (how precise the time is)
\end{itemize}

\subsection{The Timestamp Request/Response
Flow}\label{_the_timestamp_requestresponse_flow}

\begin{figure}
\centering
\pandocbounded{\includegraphics[keepaspectratio,alt={seq tsa timestamp}]{diagrams/pdf/seq-tsa-timestamp.pdf}}
\caption{RFC 3161 timestamp request/response flow}
\end{figure}

The protocol uses HTTP POST with content type
\texttt{application/timestamp-query} for the request and
\texttt{application/timestamp-reply} for the response. The request and
response are DER-encoded ASN.1 structures defined in RFC 3161.

\subsection{Implementation with
BouncyCastle}\label{_implementation_with_bouncycastle}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Requests a timestamp from a TSA for the given data}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Returns the TimeStampToken that can be embedded as an unsigned CMS attribute}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public}\NormalTok{ TimeStampToken }\FunctionTok{requestTimestamp}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ dataToTimestamp}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ tsaUrl}\OperatorTok{)}
        \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

    \CommentTok{// 1. Hash the data}
    \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\NormalTok{ digest }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"SHA{-}256"}\OperatorTok{);}
    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ hash }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ digest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{digest}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{dataToTimestamp}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// 2. Create the timestamp request}
\NormalTok{    TimeStampRequestGenerator reqGen }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{TimeStampRequestGenerator}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    reqGen}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCertReq}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// Include TSA\textquotesingle{}s cert in the response}
\NormalTok{    TimeStampRequest tsReq }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ reqGen}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{TSPAlgorithms}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SHA256}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ hash}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// 3. Send to TSA via HTTP}
    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ reqBytes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ tsReq}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{();}
    \BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\NormalTok{ conn }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\OperatorTok{)}
            \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{URL}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsaUrl}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{openConnection}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDoOutput}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestMethod}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"POST"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestProperty}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Content{-}Type"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"application/timestamp{-}query"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setConnectTimeout}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{10000}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setReadTimeout}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{30000}\OperatorTok{);}

    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{OutputStream}\NormalTok{ out }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getOutputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{reqBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{// 4. Read the response}
    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ respBytes}\OperatorTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{InputStream}\NormalTok{ in }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        respBytes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ in}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{readAllBytes}\OperatorTok{();}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{// 5. Parse and validate the response}
\NormalTok{    TimeStampResponse tsResp }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{TimeStampResponse}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{respBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    tsResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{validate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsReq}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// Verify it matches our request}

    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getStatus}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{!=}\NormalTok{ PKIStatus}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{GRANTED}
            \OperatorTok{\&\&}\NormalTok{ tsResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getStatus}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{!=}\NormalTok{ PKIStatus}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{GRANTED\_WITH\_MODS}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \ControlFlowTok{throw} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{TSPException}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"TSA rejected request: status="} \OperatorTok{+}
\NormalTok{                tsResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getStatus}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{", statusString="} \OperatorTok{+}
\NormalTok{                tsResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getStatusString}\OperatorTok{());}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

\NormalTok{    TimeStampToken tsToken }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ tsResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTimeStampToken}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Timestamp obtained: time=\{\}, serial=\{\}, TSA=\{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            tsToken}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTimeStampInfo}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getGenTime}\OperatorTok{(),}
\NormalTok{            tsToken}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTimeStampInfo}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getSerialNumber}\OperatorTok{(),}
\NormalTok{            tsToken}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTimeStampInfo}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getTsa}\OperatorTok{());}

    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ tsToken}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{TSA Selection Criteria}\label{_tsa_selection_criteria}

Choosing the right TSA is critical. A timestamp is only as trustworthy
as the TSA that produced it.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.6667}}@{}}
\caption{TSA selection criteria for different use cases}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Criterion
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Considerations
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Criterion
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Considerations
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\textbf{Trust store compatibility} & For Adobe Acrobat: TSA's root must
be in AATL. For EU compliance: TSA must be a QTSP in the EUTL. For
general use: TSA's root should be widely trusted. \\
\textbf{Availability \& SLA} & TSAs can go down. For production signing,
use a TSA with a published SLA. Consider a fallback TSA if the primary
is unreachable. \\
\textbf{Cost} & Free TSAs exist (FreeTSA.org, some DigiCert endpoints)
but may not be suitable for production. Commercial TSAs charge per
timestamp or per annual subscription. Costs range from free to several
thousand euros per year. \\
\textbf{Accuracy} & TSAs report their time accuracy (e.g., ±1 second).
For most use cases, sub-second accuracy is not critical. \\
\textbf{Geographic location} & Some regulations require the TSA to be
operated within specific jurisdictions. EU regulations may require a TSA
operated by an EU-based QTSP. \\
\textbf{Response time} & Network latency + TSA processing time. Free
TSAs may be slower. For high-volume signing (thousands per minute), TSA
response time matters. \\
\textbf{Certificate lifetime} & The TSA's signing certificate has an
expiry date. Longer-lived TSA certificates mean your timestamps are
verifiable for longer before re-timestamping is needed. \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{Never hardcode a single TSA URL} without a fallback strategy. If
the TSA is unreachable when your application needs to sign, the signing
fails. At minimum, implement:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  A configurable TSA URL (not hardcoded)
\item
  A reasonable timeout (10-30 seconds)
\item
  A retry with backoff (the TSA may be temporarily overloaded)
\item
  Optionally, a fallback TSA URL
\item
  A graceful degradation path: if no TSA is available, produce a B-B
  signature and log a warning for later B-T upgrade
\end{enumerate}

\section{TSA Health Probing}\label{_tsa_health_probing}

\subsection{Why You Need Probing}\label{_why_you_need_probing}

Before your application signs a document, you need to know which TSAs
are actually reachable. A signing operation that fails at the
timestamping step --- after the user has already authenticated, after
the CSC API has consumed a SAD token, after the CMS signature has been
computed --- is one of the most frustrating failure modes in digital
signing. You can avoid it by probing TSAs in the background.

\subsection{Building a TSA Probe}\label{_building_a_tsa_probe}

A TSA probe sends a minimal RFC 3161 timestamp request --- using a dummy
hash, with \texttt{certReq=false} to minimize response size --- and
measures whether the server responds correctly:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Probes a TSA server by sending a minimal timestamp request}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Returns the response time in milliseconds}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ or }\CommentTok{{-}1}\NormalTok{ if the probe fails}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{long} \FunctionTok{probeTsa}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ tsaUrl}\OperatorTok{,} \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ timeoutMs}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \CommentTok{// Hash of dummy data — we don\textquotesingle{}t care about the content}
        \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ dummyHash }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"SHA{-}256"}\OperatorTok{)}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{digest}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"tsa{-}probe"}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getBytes}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{UTF\_8}\OperatorTok{));}

\NormalTok{        TimeStampRequestGenerator gen }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{TimeStampRequestGenerator}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        gen}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCertReq}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// Minimize response size}
\NormalTok{        TimeStampRequest req }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ gen}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{TSPAlgorithms}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SHA256}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ dummyHash}\OperatorTok{);}

        \DataTypeTok{long}\NormalTok{ start }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{nanoTime}\OperatorTok{();}

        \BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\NormalTok{ conn }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\OperatorTok{)}
                \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{URL}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsaUrl}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{openConnection}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDoOutput}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestMethod}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"POST"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestProperty}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Content{-}Type"}\OperatorTok{,}
                \StringTok{"application/timestamp{-}query"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setConnectTimeout}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{timeoutMs}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setReadTimeout}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{timeoutMs}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getOutputStream}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{req}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{());}

        \CommentTok{// Validate Content{-}Type — some misconfigured servers}
        \CommentTok{// return an HTML error page with HTTP 200}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ contentType }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getContentType}\OperatorTok{();}
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{contentType }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}
                \OperatorTok{||} \OperatorTok{!}\NormalTok{contentType}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{contains}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"timestamp{-}reply"}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{warn}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"TSA \{\} returned unexpected Content{-}Type: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                    tsaUrl}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ contentType}\OperatorTok{);}
            \ControlFlowTok{return} \OperatorTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{;}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ respBytes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInputStream}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{readAllBytes}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        TimeStampResponse resp }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{TimeStampResponse}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{respBytes}\OperatorTok{);}

        \DataTypeTok{long}\NormalTok{ elapsed }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{nanoTime}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ start}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{/} \DecValTok{1\_000\_000}\OperatorTok{;}

        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{resp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getStatus}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{==}\NormalTok{ PKIStatus}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{GRANTED}
                \OperatorTok{||}\NormalTok{ resp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getStatus}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{==}\NormalTok{ PKIStatus}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{GRANTED\_WITH\_MODS}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ elapsed}\OperatorTok{;}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \OperatorTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}} \ControlFlowTok{catch} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Exception}\NormalTok{ e}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \OperatorTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Content-Type validation is essential.} Some TSA endpoints sit
behind reverse proxies or WAFs that return an HTTP 200 response with an
HTML error page when the backend is down. Without checking
\texttt{Content-Type:\ application/timestamp-reply}, your application
might accept a garbage response and only fail later when trying to parse
it as ASN.1. Always validate the response \texttt{Content-Type} before
parsing.

\subsection{Probe Strategy}\label{_probe_strategy}

For production applications, run probes on a schedule:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{At startup}: Probe all configured TSAs concurrently using a
  thread pool. Mark each as "online" or "offline" with its measured
  response time.
\item
  \textbf{Periodically}: Re-probe every 5-15 minutes. TSA availability
  can change --- maintenance windows, network issues, certificate
  rotations.
\item
  \textbf{On failure}: If a signing operation fails at the timestamping
  step, immediately probe all TSAs and update their status. The failure
  may be transient (timeout) or permanent (certificate expired, endpoint
  removed).
\end{enumerate}

Present the probe results to the user: a green/red status indicator with
response time helps them choose the fastest available TSA for their
signing operation.

\section{Caching TSA and OCSP
Responses}\label{_caching_tsa_and_ocsp_responses}

\subsection{Why Caching Matters}\label{_why_caching_matters}

Timestamp and OCSP requests are network calls to external servers. In a
high-volume signing scenario --- batch signing hundreds of documents ---
making a fresh TSA request and OCSP lookup for every single signature is
wasteful and slow. More importantly, it creates an availability
dependency: if the TSA or OCSP responder goes down mid-batch, the entire
batch fails.

\subsection{TSA Response Caching}\label{_tsa_response_caching}

TSA responses are \textbf{not cacheable} in the traditional sense ---
each timestamp token contains a unique serial number and a specific
timestamp, making every response unique by design. However, you can
optimize for batch scenarios:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Connection pooling}: Reuse HTTP connections to the TSA server.
  TLS handshakes are expensive; a keep-alive connection eliminates that
  overhead for subsequent requests.
\item
  \textbf{Pre-warming}: Establish the connection before the signing flow
  begins. The first request to a TSA often takes 2-5x longer than
  subsequent requests due to DNS resolution, TLS negotiation, and
  server-side session setup.
\item
  \textbf{Batch authorization}: Some CSC providers and TSA operators
  support batch requests --- signing multiple hashes or timestamping
  multiple values in a single API call. If your TSA supports it, prefer
  batch requests over individual calls.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Never reuse a timestamp token across multiple signatures.} Each
timestamp proves existence at a specific instant for a specific hash.
Sharing a timestamp token between two signatures is technically invalid
(the hash won't match) and a compliance violation.

\subsection{OCSP Response Caching}\label{_ocsp_response_caching}

Unlike timestamps, OCSP responses \textbf{are cacheable} --- and you
should cache them aggressively. An OCSP response contains a
\texttt{thisUpdate} and \texttt{nextUpdate} field that define a validity
window. Within that window, the response is authoritative: the CA is
asserting that the certificate's revocation status has not changed since
\texttt{thisUpdate} and will not change before \texttt{nextUpdate}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ OCSP response cache}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Caches responses by certificate serial number}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ respects nextUpdate}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{Map}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{BigInteger}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ CachedOcspResponse}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ ocspCache}
        \OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ConcurrentHashMap}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}();}

\KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{getOcspResponse}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{,}
        \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ issuer}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \BuiltInTok{BigInteger}\NormalTok{ serial }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSerialNumber}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    CachedOcspResponse cached }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ocspCache}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{serial}\OperatorTok{);}

    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cached }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null} \OperatorTok{\&\&}\NormalTok{ cached}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{isValid}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{debug}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"OCSP cache hit for serial=\{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ serial}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ cached}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{responseBytes}\OperatorTok{();}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{// Cache miss or expired — fetch fresh response}
    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ respBytes }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{fetchOCSPResponse}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cert}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ issuer}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{respBytes }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        OCSPResp ocspResp }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OCSPResp}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{respBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        BasicOCSPResp basic }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{BasicOCSPResp}\OperatorTok{)}
\NormalTok{                ocspResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getResponseObject}\OperatorTok{();}
        \BuiltInTok{Date}\NormalTok{ nextUpdate }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ basic}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getResponses}\OperatorTok{()[}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{].}\FunctionTok{getNextUpdate}\OperatorTok{();}

\NormalTok{        ocspCache}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{put}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{serial}\OperatorTok{,}
                \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CachedOcspResponse}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{respBytes}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ nextUpdate}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{debug}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"OCSP cached for serial=\{\}, valid until \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                serial}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ nextUpdate}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ respBytes}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Typical OCSP response validity windows:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Commercial CAs}: 1-4 hours (short, to limit exposure from
  compromised responder)
\item
  \textbf{Government CAs}: 4-24 hours (longer, to reduce load on
  centralized infrastructure)
\item
  \textbf{Qualified CAs (eIDAS)}: 24 hours maximum (ETSI EN 319 411-2
  requirement)
\end{itemize}

For batch signing of 100 documents with the same certificate, a cached
OCSP response eliminates 99 round trips to the OCSP responder.

\subsection{CRL Caching}\label{_crl_caching}

CRLs are even more cacheable than OCSP responses. Every CRL contains a
\texttt{nextUpdate} date, and some CAs publish CRLs on fixed schedules
(every 6, 12, or 24 hours). Cache the CRL bytes keyed by the CRL
Distribution Point URL and check \texttt{nextUpdate} before re-fetching:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ CRL cache }\CommentTok{—}\NormalTok{ caches full CRLs by distribution point URL}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ CRLs can be large }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{hundreds of KB}\CommentTok{),}\NormalTok{ but they change infrequently}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{Map}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ CachedCrl}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ crlCache}
        \OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ConcurrentHashMap}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}();}

\KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{getCrl}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ crlUrl}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    CachedCrl cached }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ crlCache}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crlUrl}\OperatorTok{);}

    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cached }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null} \OperatorTok{\&\&}\NormalTok{ cached}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{nextUpdate}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{after}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{Date}\OperatorTok{()))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{debug}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"CRL cache hit for \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ crlUrl}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ cached}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{crlBytes}\OperatorTok{();}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ crlBytes }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{downloadCrl}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crlUrl}\OperatorTok{);}
    \BuiltInTok{CertificateFactory}\NormalTok{ cf }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{CertificateFactory}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"X.509"}\OperatorTok{);}
    \BuiltInTok{X509CRL}\NormalTok{ crl }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509CRL}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ cf}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generateCRL}\OperatorTok{(}
            \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayInputStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crlBytes}\OperatorTok{));}

\NormalTok{    crlCache}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{put}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crlUrl}\OperatorTok{,}
            \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CachedCrl}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crlBytes}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ crl}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getNextUpdate}\OperatorTok{()));}
\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{debug}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"CRL cached for \{\}, valid until \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            crlUrl}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ crl}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getNextUpdate}\OperatorTok{());}

    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ crlBytes}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Cache warming for DSS construction.} When building a DSS
dictionary (for PAdES-B-LT or B-LTA), you need OCSP responses and CRLs
for the signer's entire certificate chain \emph{plus} the TSA's
certificate chain. A single signature can require 4-8 revocation data
lookups. Pre-fetch and cache this data before the signing flow begins
--- the DSS construction should be an offline operation that reads from
cache, not a live network operation.

\section{Signature Timestamps vs. Document
Timestamps}\label{_signature_timestamps_vs_document_timestamps}

PDF supports two kinds of timestamps, and they serve different purposes:

\subsection{Signature Timestamps}\label{_signature_timestamps}

A \textbf{signature timestamp} is embedded \emph{inside} the CMS
\texttt{SignedData} as an unsigned attribute of the \texttt{SignerInfo}.
It timestamps the \emph{signature value} --- proving when the signature
was created.

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Where it lives}: CMS
  \texttt{SignerInfo.unsignedAttrs.id-aa-signatureTimeStampToken}
\item
  \textbf{What it timestamps}: The bytes of the
  \texttt{SignerInfo.signature} field
\item
  \textbf{PAdES level}: Required for PAdES-B-T and above
\item
  \textbf{Who creates it}: The signing application, immediately after
  computing the CMS signature
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Document Timestamps}\label{_document_timestamps}

A \textbf{document timestamp} is a separate signature in the PDF --- a
\texttt{/Sig} dictionary with \texttt{/SubFilter\ /ETSI.RFC3161} ---
that timestamps the \emph{entire document}.

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Where it lives}: A separate signature dictionary in the PDF,
  applied as an incremental update
\item
  \textbf{What it timestamps}: The entire PDF file (via byte range),
  including all previous signatures and the DSS
\item
  \textbf{PAdES level}: Required for PAdES-B-LTA (the archival
  timestamp)
\item
  \textbf{Who creates it}: The document management system, as a
  maintenance operation
\end{itemize}

The key difference: a signature timestamp proves when a \emph{specific
signature} was created. A document timestamp proves that the
\emph{entire document} (including all signatures and validation data)
existed at a specific time.

\begin{verbatim}
// Signature timestamp (inside CMS)
/Sig <<
  /SubFilter /ETSI.CAdES.detached
  /Contents <CMS SignedData:
    SignerInfo:
      signedAttrs: [contentType, messageDigest, ESS-signing-certificate-v2]
      signature: <RSA/ECDSA signature bytes>
      unsignedAttrs: [
        signatureTimeStampToken: <TimeStampToken>  ← THIS
      ]
  >
>>

// Document timestamp (separate signature)
/Sig <<
  /SubFilter /ETSI.RFC3161             ← THIS
  /ByteRange [0 ... ... ...]          ← Covers entire file
  /Contents <TimeStampToken as CMS>
>>
\end{verbatim}

\section{The Document Security Store
(DSS)}\label{_the_document_security_store_dss}

\subsection{Purpose}\label{_purpose}

The DSS is a dictionary in the document catalog that stores all the data
needed to validate every signature in the document \emph{offline} ---
without contacting any external servers.

Without a DSS, validating a signature requires:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Fetching the signer's certificate chain from the CMS and/or AIA URLs
\item
  Contacting OCSP responders to check revocation status
\item
  Downloading CRLs from distribution points
\end{itemize}

All of these require network access and functioning servers. Years from
now, these servers may not exist. The DSS solves this by embedding
everything locally.

\subsection{Building the DSS}\label{_building_the_dss}

Building a DSS requires collecting all the validation data and embedding
it as an incremental update:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Adds a DSS }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{Document Security Store}\CommentTok{)}\NormalTok{ dictionary to the PDF}\CommentTok{,}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ embedding certificates}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ OCSP responses}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ and CRLs for all signatures}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{addDSS}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDDocument doc}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{ValidationData}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ validationData}\OperatorTok{)}
        \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{IOException} \OperatorTok{\{}

\NormalTok{    COSDictionary dssDictionary }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{COSDictionary}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    COSArray certsArray }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{COSArray}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    COSArray ocspsArray }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{COSArray}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    COSArray crlsArray }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{COSArray}\OperatorTok{();}

    \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ValidationData vd }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ validationData}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \CommentTok{// Embed certificates}
        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ certBytes }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ vd}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificateChain}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            PDStream certStream }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{);}
            \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{OutputStream}\NormalTok{ os }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ certStream}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{createOutputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{                os}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
\NormalTok{            certsArray}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certStream}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSObject}\OperatorTok{());}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \CommentTok{// Embed OCSP responses}
        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ ocspBytes }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ vd}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getOcspResponses}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            PDStream ocspStream }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{);}
            \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{OutputStream}\NormalTok{ os }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ocspStream}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{createOutputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{                os}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ocspBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
\NormalTok{            ocspsArray}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ocspStream}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSObject}\OperatorTok{());}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \CommentTok{// Embed CRLs}
        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ crlBytes }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ vd}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCrls}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            PDStream crlStream }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{);}
            \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{OutputStream}\NormalTok{ os }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ crlStream}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{createOutputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{                os}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crlBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
\NormalTok{            crlsArray}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crlStream}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSObject}\OperatorTok{());}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certsArray}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ dssDictionary}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setItem}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Certs"}\OperatorTok{),}\NormalTok{ certsArray}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ocspsArray}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ dssDictionary}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setItem}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"OCSPs"}\OperatorTok{),}\NormalTok{ ocspsArray}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crlsArray}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ dssDictionary}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setItem}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"CRLs"}\OperatorTok{),}\NormalTok{ crlsArray}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// Add DSS to document catalog}
\NormalTok{    doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDocumentCatalog}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getCOSObject}\OperatorTok{()}
            \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setItem}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"DSS"}\OperatorTok{),}\NormalTok{ dssDictionary}\OperatorTok{);}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{VRI: Validation Related
Information}\label{_vri_validation_related_information}

The DSS can optionally include a \texttt{/VRI} sub-dictionary that maps
each signature to its specific validation data. The VRI keys are the
uppercase hex SHA-1 hash of the signature value (the bytes in
\texttt{/Contents}):

\begin{verbatim}
/DSS <<
  /Certs [...]
  /OCSPs [...]
  /VRI <<
    /A1B2C3D4E5F6... <<        % SHA-1 hash of signature 1's /Contents
      /Cert [30 0 R 31 0 R]    % Certs for this specific signature
      /OCSP [33 0 R]           % OCSP for this specific signature
      /TU (D:20260308120000Z)  % Time of validation
    >>
    /F6E5D4C3B2A1... <<        % SHA-1 hash of signature 2's /Contents
      /Cert [30 0 R 32 0 R]
      /OCSP [34 0 R]
      /TU (D:20260309140000Z)
    >>
  >>
>>
\end{verbatim}

VRI is useful for multi-signature documents where each signature has
different validation data. Without VRI, a validator must try all
certificates and OCSP responses in the DSS against each signature to
find the right match. With VRI, the mapping is explicit.

The VRI key is a SHA-1 hash --- yes, SHA-1. This is a historical
artifact from when the DSS was first specified. SHA-1 is used here only
as a key derivation function (to generate a unique dictionary key from
the signature value), not for cryptographic security. The security of
the DSS does not depend on SHA-1 collision resistance.

\section{Fetching Revocation Data}\label{_fetching_revocation_data}

\subsection{OCSP (Online Certificate Status
Protocol)}\label{_ocsp_online_certificate_status_protocol}

OCSP is the preferred mechanism for checking certificate revocation. You
send a request to the CA's OCSP responder with the certificate's serial
number, and it responds with "good," "revoked," or "unknown."

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Fetches an OCSP response for the given certificate}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Returns the DER}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{encoded OCSP response bytes}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{fetchOCSPResponse}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{,}
        \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ issuer}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

    \CommentTok{// 1. Find the OCSP responder URL from the certificate\textquotesingle{}s AIA extension}
    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ ocspUrl }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{extractOcspUrl}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cert}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ocspUrl }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{warn}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"No OCSP URL in certificate AIA for: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSubjectX500Principal}\OperatorTok{());}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{// 2. Build the OCSP request}
\NormalTok{    CertificateID certId }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CertificateID}\OperatorTok{(}
            \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaDigestCalculatorProviderBuilder}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{()}
                    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{CertificateID}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HASH\_SHA1}\OperatorTok{),}
            \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaX509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{issuer}\OperatorTok{),}
\NormalTok{            cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSerialNumber}\OperatorTok{());}

\NormalTok{    OCSPReqBuilder reqBuilder }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OCSPReqBuilder}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    reqBuilder}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addRequest}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certId}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    OCSPReq ocspReq }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ reqBuilder}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{();}

    \CommentTok{// 3. Send via HTTP}
    \BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\NormalTok{ conn }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\OperatorTok{)}
            \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{URL}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ocspUrl}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{openConnection}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDoOutput}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestMethod}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"POST"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestProperty}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Content{-}Type"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"application/ocsp{-}request"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getOutputStream}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ocspReq}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{());}

    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ respBytes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInputStream}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{readAllBytes}\OperatorTok{();}

    \CommentTok{// 4. Validate the response}
\NormalTok{    OCSPResp ocspResp }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OCSPResp}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{respBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ocspResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getStatus}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{!=}\NormalTok{ OCSPResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SUCCESSFUL}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{error}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"OCSP response status: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ ocspResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getStatus}\OperatorTok{());}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

\NormalTok{    BasicOCSPResp basicResp }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{BasicOCSPResp}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ ocspResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getResponseObject}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    SingleResp singleResp }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ basicResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getResponses}\OperatorTok{()[}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{];}

    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{singleResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertStatus}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{==}\NormalTok{ CertificateStatus}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{GOOD}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"OCSP: certificate is GOOD (produced: \{\})"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                basicResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getProducedAt}\OperatorTok{());}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ respBytes}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}} \ControlFlowTok{else} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{error}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"OCSP: certificate status is NOT GOOD: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                singleResp}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertStatus}\OperatorTok{());}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{;}  \CommentTok{// Certificate is revoked or unknown}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{CRL (Certificate Revocation
List)}\label{_crl_certificate_revocation_list}

CRLs are the fallback when OCSP is not available. A CRL is a signed list
of all revoked certificates issued by a CA.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Downloads a CRL from the certificate}\CommentTok{\textquotesingle{}}\NormalTok{s CRL Distribution Point}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Returns the DER}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{encoded CRL bytes}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{fetchCRL}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ crlUrl }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{extractCrlUrl}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cert}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crlUrl }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{warn}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"No CRL URL in certificate for: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSubjectX500Principal}\OperatorTok{());}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\NormalTok{ conn }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{URL}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crlUrl}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{openConnection}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setConnectTimeout}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{10000}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setReadTimeout}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{30000}\OperatorTok{);}

    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ crlBytes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInputStream}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{readAllBytes}\OperatorTok{();}

    \CommentTok{// Validate the CRL}
    \BuiltInTok{CertificateFactory}\NormalTok{ cf }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{CertificateFactory}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"X.509"}\OperatorTok{);}
    \BuiltInTok{X509CRL}\NormalTok{ crl }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509CRL}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ cf}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generateCRL}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayInputStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crlBytes}\OperatorTok{));}

\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"CRL downloaded: issuer=\{\}, entries=\{\}, nextUpdate=\{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            crl}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getIssuerX500Principal}\OperatorTok{(),}
\NormalTok{            crl}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getRevokedCertificates}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null} \OperatorTok{?}
\NormalTok{                    crl}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getRevokedCertificates}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{:} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            crl}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getNextUpdate}\OperatorTok{());}

    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ crlBytes}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

CRLs can be \textbf{very large} --- some CAs have CRLs of 500 KB or
more. Embedding a large CRL in the DSS significantly increases the PDF
file size. Prefer OCSP when available --- OCSP responses are typically
1-3 KB.

Also be aware of \textbf{delta CRLs} --- some CAs publish only the
changes since the last full CRL. If the certificate's CRL Distribution
Point specifies a delta CRL, you may need both the full CRL and the
delta CRL for complete validation.

\section{The Complete B-LTA
Workflow}\label{_the_complete_b_lta_workflow}

Putting it all together, here's the end-to-end workflow for producing a
PAdES-B-LTA signature:

\begin{verbatim}
1. Create PAdES-B-B signature
   └── CMS with ESS-signing-certificate-v2, SHA-256, SubFilter ETSI.CAdES.detached

2. Add signature timestamp → PAdES-B-T
   └── Request RFC 3161 timestamp over the signature value
   └── Embed as unsigned CMS attribute

3. Fetch validation data
   └── Build certificate chain (signer → intermediates → root)
   └── Fetch OCSP response for each certificate
   └── Fetch CRL as fallback where OCSP unavailable
   └── Fetch TSA's certificate chain and revocation data too

4. Create DSS dictionary → PAdES-B-LT
   └── Embed all certificates as streams
   └── Embed all OCSP responses as streams
   └── Embed all CRLs as streams
   └── Optionally create VRI entries
   └── Add DSS to document catalog as incremental update

5. Add document timestamp → PAdES-B-LTA
   └── Request RFC 3161 timestamp over the entire file (including DSS)
   └── Apply as separate /Sig dictionary with SubFilter ETSI.RFC3161
   └── Save as incremental update
\end{verbatim}

Each step is an incremental update to the PDF. The final file contains
the original document, the signature, the DSS, and the document
timestamp --- everything needed for indefinite validation.

\section{Key Takeaways}\label{_key_takeaways_6}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{RFC 3161 timestamps} are CMS-signed proofs that data existed
  at a specific time --- issued by Trusted Timestamp Authorities
\item
  \textbf{Signature timestamps} (in CMS unsigned attributes) prove when
  a specific signature was created
\item
  \textbf{Document timestamps} (separate \texttt{/Sig} with SubFilter
  \texttt{ETSI.RFC3161}) prove when the entire document existed --- used
  for archival
\item
  The \textbf{DSS dictionary} embeds certificates, OCSP responses, and
  CRLs directly in the PDF for offline validation
\item
  \textbf{VRI entries} map specific signatures to their validation data
  using SHA-1 hash keys
\item
  \textbf{OCSP} is preferred over CRLs for revocation checking (smaller,
  more specific)
\item
  \textbf{TSA selection} matters: trust store compatibility (AATL/EUTL),
  availability, cost, and response time
\item
  \textbf{PAdES-B-LTA} is the complete package: signature + timestamp +
  validation data + archival timestamp
\item
  \textbf{Re-timestamping} extends validity indefinitely --- a scheduled
  maintenance operation for archival documents
\item
  Always implement \textbf{TSA failover} --- a TSA outage should not
  prevent signing
\item
  \textbf{Probe TSAs} in the background at startup and periodically ---
  validate Content-Type headers, not just HTTP 200
\item
  \textbf{Cache OCSP responses} aggressively --- they have explicit
  validity windows (\texttt{thisUpdate}/\texttt{nextUpdate}) and
  eliminate redundant network calls in batch scenarios
\item
  \textbf{Cache CRLs} by distribution point URL --- CRLs change
  infrequently and can be very large; avoid re-downloading within the
  validity window
\item
  \textbf{TSA responses cannot be cached} --- each is unique by design
  --- but connection pooling and pre-warming significantly reduce
  latency
\end{itemize}

\chapter{eIDAS \& Qualified
Signatures}\label{_eidas_qualified_signatures}

\chapter{The eIDAS Regulation}\label{_the_eidas_regulation}

In 2014, the European Union did something that no other jurisdiction had
managed: it created a legal framework where a digital signature --- a
sequence of bytes produced by a cryptographic algorithm --- carries the
same legal weight as ink on paper, in all 27 member states
simultaneously. That framework is called eIDAS, and whether you love it
or hate it, if your software produces or validates digital signatures
that touch Europe, you need to understand it.

The Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) --- its full title is
"Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 of the European Parliament and of the
Council of 23 July 2014 on electronic identification and trust services
for electronic transactions in the internal market" --- is a 38-page
regulation that created the legal and institutional infrastructure for
electronic signatures, seals, timestamps, and related trust services
across the entire European Union. It replaced a failed directive that
had been limping along for 15 years, and it did so by making one
critical architectural decision: it chose to be a \emph{regulation}, not
a \emph{directive}.

That distinction matters enormously, and understanding why requires a
brief detour through European law.

\section{A Brief History: The Electronic Signatures
Directive}\label{_a_brief_history_the_electronic_signatures_directive}

\subsection{Directive 1999/93/EC}\label{_directive_199993ec}

Before eIDAS, there was the \textbf{Electronic Signatures Directive}
(Directive 1999/93/EC), adopted on December 13, 1999. The directive was
born in the heady optimism of the dot-com era, when legislators across
the world were scrambling to give electronic commerce a legal
foundation. The United States had passed the E-Sign Act. UNCITRAL had
published a Model Law on Electronic Signatures. Europe wanted its own
framework, and it wanted one that would work across all (then 15) member
states.

The directive's core idea was sound: define what an "advanced electronic
signature" is, define what a "qualified certificate" is, establish that
qualified electronic signatures should not be denied legal effect, and
require member states to set up supervisory bodies for certification
service providers.

\subsection{Why Directives Fail}\label{_why_directives_fail}

Here is where European institutional mechanics become relevant to your
code.

In EU law, a \textbf{directive} tells member states \emph{what result to
achieve} but lets each country decide \emph{how} to achieve it through
national legislation. A \textbf{regulation}, by contrast, is directly
applicable in all member states without any national transposition ---
it becomes law everywhere on the same day, with the same text.

The Electronic Signatures Directive was a directive. This meant that
each member state had to transpose it into national law, and each one
did so differently. Germany created the \emph{Signaturgesetz} (Signature
Law). France created \emph{le decret n. 2001-272}. Italy created the
\emph{Codice dell'Amministrazione Digitale}. Each country interpreted
the directive's requirements through its own legal tradition, its own
bureaucratic structures, and its own technical preferences.

The result was exactly what you would predict: 15 (later 28) different
national implementations of the "same" legal framework, with subtly
different definitions of what counted as an "advanced" signature,
different requirements for certification service providers, different
supervisory structures, and --- critically for developers --- different
technical standards that qualified certificates had to meet.

The Electronic Signatures Directive used the term "Certification Service
Provider" (CSP). eIDAS renamed these to "Trust Service Provider" (TSP)
and, for the supervised ones, "Qualified Trust Service Provider" (QTSP).
The terminology change was deliberate: eIDAS covers more than just
certificates --- it covers timestamps, seals, website authentication,
and electronic delivery services. If you encounter "CSP" in older
documentation, read it as "TSP."

\subsection{Cross-Border Failure}\label{_cross_border_failure}

The most damaging consequence of the directive's fragmentation was the
failure of cross-border recognition. In theory, a qualified electronic
signature from Germany should have been accepted in France, and vice
versa. In practice, it often was not. Each country's government
procurement portals, tax systems, and regulatory filing platforms had
been built to recognize only their own national qualified certificates.
A German doctor trying to sign a cross-border prescription
electronically for a French patient would find that the French system
did not recognize the German qualified certificate.

A 2012 study by the European Commission found that cross-border
recognition of electronic signatures was "marginal at best." Of 13
member states surveyed, only 5 had any mechanism at all for recognizing
qualified certificates from other EU countries. The directive had been
in force for 13 years, and the internal market for electronic signatures
was essentially non-existent.

This was the political context in which eIDAS was born.

\subsection{The Lessons Learned}\label{_the_lessons_learned}

The Commission identified several root causes for the directive's
failure:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Directive vs. Regulation}: national transposition created
  divergent implementations
\item
  \textbf{No mandatory trust infrastructure}: there was no EU-wide list
  of qualified providers that all member states were required to accept
\item
  \textbf{Vague technical requirements}: the directive referenced
  "secure signature creation devices" without specifying what that meant
  technically
\item
  \textbf{No supervision framework}: how supervisory bodies should
  operate, what they should check, and what consequences non-compliance
  should carry were all left to national discretion
\item
  \textbf{Certificates only}: the directive covered only electronic
  signatures, ignoring related services like timestamps, seals, and
  electronic delivery
\end{enumerate}

eIDAS addressed every single one of these failures.

\section{eIDAS: Architecture of the
Regulation}\label{_eidas_architecture_of_the_regulation}

\subsection{Regulation, Not Directive}\label{_regulation_not_directive}

eIDAS is a regulation. Article 288 of the Treaty on the Functioning of
the European Union says: "A regulation shall have general application.
It shall be binding in its entirety and directly applicable in all
Member States."

This means: - The text is the same in all 27 member states (plus EEA
countries through incorporation) - No national transposition is needed
or permitted --- the regulation \emph{is} the law - National courts
apply the regulation directly; citizens and businesses can invoke it
directly - Divergent national interpretations can be challenged at the
Court of Justice of the European Union

For developers, this has a profound practical consequence: the rules are
the same everywhere. When eIDAS says that a qualified electronic
signature has the legal effect equivalent to a handwritten signature, it
means exactly that in Germany, France, Italy, Estonia, and all other
member states. You do not need to check 27 different national laws to
know what your signature is worth.

\subsection{Scope and Structure}\label{_scope_and_structure}

The eIDAS regulation has two main parts:

\textbf{Chapter II: Electronic Identification} (Articles 6-12) ---
establishes a framework for mutual recognition of national electronic
identification schemes (eID). This is the part that deals with
government-issued digital identities (like the German
\emph{Personalausweis} or the Estonian \emph{ID-kaart}) and how they
should be recognized across borders. We will not cover this in detail
because it relates to identity management rather than document signing.

\textbf{Chapter III: Trust Services} (Articles 13-45) --- this is where
the electronic signature framework lives, and it is the focus of this
chapter and the next four chapters.

The trust services chapter covers: - Electronic signatures (Articles
25-27) - Electronic seals (Articles 35-40) - Electronic timestamps
(Articles 41-42) - Electronic delivery services (Articles 43-44) -
Website authentication certificates (Article 45) - General provisions on
trust service providers (Articles 13-24)

eIDAS does not mandate \emph{how} electronic signatures must be
implemented technically. It defines \emph{legal categories} and
\emph{legal effects}, then defers to ETSI and CEN for the technical
standards. The regulation deliberately separates legal requirements from
technical implementation --- a wise decision that has allowed the
technical standards (PAdES, CAdES, XAdES) to evolve independently of the
legal framework.

When eIDAS says "advanced electronic signature," it is defining a legal
concept with four properties. When ETSI EN 319 142 says "PAdES-B-T," it
is defining a technical implementation that satisfies those properties.
The mapping between legal categories and technical standards is
documented in Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2015/1506.

\section{The Three Tiers of Electronic
Signatures}\label{_the_three_tiers_of_electronic_signatures}

The heart of eIDAS for developers is its three-tier classification of
electronic signatures. Each tier has progressively stricter requirements
and progressively stronger legal effects.

\subsection{Tier 1: Electronic Signature (Article
3(10))}\label{_tier_1_electronic_signature_article_310}

The broadest tier. Article 3(10) defines an electronic signature as:

\begin{quote}
"electronic signature" means data in electronic form which is attached
to or logically associated with other data in electronic form and which
is used by the signatory to sign

---  Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS), Article 3(10)
\end{quote}

That is it. An electronic signature under eIDAS can be: - A scanned
image of a handwritten signature pasted into a PDF - A typed name at the
bottom of an email - A checkbox on a web form saying "I agree" - A click
of a "Sign" button that records user intent

There is no cryptographic requirement. There is no certificate
requirement. There is no requirement for any specific technology at all.

The legal effect of a basic electronic signature is defined in Article
25(1): it "shall not be denied legal effect and admissibility as
evidence in legal proceedings solely on the grounds that it is in an
electronic form." This is a non-discrimination principle --- courts
cannot reject evidence \emph{just} because it is electronic. But it says
nothing about the \emph{weight} of that evidence. A typed name has much
less evidentiary value than a cryptographic signature with a qualified
certificate.

For developers building signing workflows: many business processes do
not require more than a basic electronic signature. Click-to-sign,
draw-to-sign, and type-to-sign all count as electronic signatures under
eIDAS. If your use case is internal corporate approvals, non-binding
agreements, or informal consent, a basic electronic signature may be
legally sufficient. Check with your legal team, but do not assume you
always need cryptography.

\subsection{Tier 2: Advanced Electronic Signature (Article 3(11) and
Article
26)}\label{_tier_2_advanced_electronic_signature_article_311_and_article_26}

The middle tier. Article 3(11) defines an advanced electronic signature
as an electronic signature that meets the requirements set out in
Article 26.

Article 26 specifies four requirements:

\begin{quote}
An advanced electronic signature shall meet the following requirements:

(a) it is uniquely linked to the signatory;

(b) it is capable of identifying the signatory;

(c) it is created using electronic signature creation data that the
signatory can, with a high level of confidence, use under his sole
control; and

(d) it is linked to the data signed therewith in such a way that any
subsequent change in the data is detectable.

---  Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS), Article 26
\end{quote}

Let us examine each requirement from a technical perspective.

\subsubsection{(a) Uniquely linked to the
signatory}\label{_a_uniquely_linked_to_the_signatory}

The signature must be produced using data (typically a private key) that
is uniquely associated with one specific natural person. In practice,
this means: a private key that belongs to exactly one person, used to
produce a cryptographic signature that can only have been created with
that key.

A shared private key used by multiple employees would fail this
requirement. A department-level signing key would fail this requirement.
The key must be tied to a specific individual.

\subsubsection{(b) Capable of identifying the
signatory}\label{_b_capable_of_identifying_the_signatory}

The signature (together with its associated data, such as a certificate)
must make it possible to determine \emph{who} signed. A raw RSA or ECDSA
signature over a hash does not, by itself, identify anyone --- it just
proves that whoever held the private key produced the signature.
Combined with an X.509 certificate that binds the public key to an
identity (name, organization, email), the requirement is satisfied.

\subsubsection{(c) Sole control}\label{_c_sole_control}

The signatory must be the only person who can use the signing key. "With
a high level of confidence" is the qualifier --- absolute sole control
is impossible to guarantee (keys can be stolen), but the system must
make unauthorized use difficult.

Sole control mechanisms include: - \textbf{Password-protected PKCS\#12
keystore}: the signer must know the password - \textbf{Smart card with
PIN}: the signer must possess the card and know the PIN - \textbf{HSM
with authentication}: the signer must authenticate (username + password
+ OTP) before the HSM will sign - \textbf{Biometric activation}:
fingerprint or face recognition to authorize signing

"Sole control" is where many implementations fail the advanced signature
test. If a signing key is stored in a server-side keystore and an
application signs on behalf of users without meaningful per-signature
authentication, the resulting signatures are arguably \emph{not}
advanced electronic signatures --- because the user does not have sole
control over the key.

The fact that the user logged into the application is not sufficient.
The authentication must be linked to the \emph{signing action}, not just
the \emph{session}. This is the distinction that the SCAL (Sole Control
Assurance Level) framework addresses --- more on this in Chapter 22.

\subsubsection{(d) Linked to the data}\label{_d_linked_to_the_data}

Any change to the signed data must be detectable. This is the integrity
property that every digital signature scheme provides: if you change
even one bit of the signed data, the signature will no longer verify.
For PDF signatures, the byte range mechanism (Chapter 13) ensures this
linkage.

\subsection{Tier 3: Qualified Electronic Signature (Article
3(12))}\label{_tier_3_qualified_electronic_signature_article_312}

The highest tier, and the most consequential. Article 3(12) defines a
qualified electronic signature as:

\begin{quote}
"qualified electronic signature" means an advanced electronic signature
that is created by a qualified electronic signature creation device, and
which is based on a qualified certificate for electronic signatures

---  Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS), Article 3(12)
\end{quote}

A QES is an advanced electronic signature (all four Article 26
properties) that \emph{additionally}: 1. Uses a \textbf{Qualified
Signature Creation Device (QSCD)} --- certified hardware that protects
the signing key 2. Is based on a \textbf{Qualified Certificate} ---
issued by a Qualified Trust Service Provider (QTSP) listed in the EU
Trusted List

The legal effect of a QES is defined in Article 25(2): "A qualified
electronic signature shall have the equivalent legal effect of a
handwritten signature." This is not a non-discrimination principle ---
it is an \emph{equivalence} rule. A QES is legally equal to pen on
paper. No court in any EU member state can treat it differently.

Furthermore, Article 25(3) establishes mutual recognition: "A qualified
electronic signature based on a qualified certificate issued in one
Member State shall be recognised as a qualified electronic signature in
all other Member States." This is the cross-border recognition that the
1999 directive failed to achieve.
