\section{Key Articles in Detail}\label{_key_articles_in_detail}

\subsection{Article 25: Legal Effects of Electronic
Signatures}\label{_article_25_legal_effects_of_electronic_signatures}

Article 25 is the most cited provision in eIDAS, and it deserves careful
reading.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4286}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4286}}@{}}
\caption{Article 25 --- Legal effects summarized}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Paragraph
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Rule
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Practical Meaning
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Paragraph
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Rule
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Practical Meaning
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
25(1) & Electronic signatures shall not be denied legal effect solely
because they are electronic & Non-discrimination: courts cannot reject
electronic evidence just because it is not paper \\
25(2) & QES shall have the equivalent legal effect of a handwritten
signature & Legal equivalence: a QES \emph{is} a handwritten signature
for all legal purposes \\
25(3) & QES based on a qualified certificate from one member state shall
be recognized in all member states & Cross-border recognition: no
country can reject another country's QES \\
\end{longtable}

Note what Article 25 does \emph{not} say: it does not say that advanced
electronic signatures (AdES) have the legal effect of handwritten
signatures. An AdES benefits only from the non-discrimination rule of
25(1) --- it cannot be rejected \emph{solely} because it is electronic,
but a court is free to assign it less evidentiary weight than a
handwritten signature based on the circumstances.

This creates a clear hierarchy: - \textbf{QES}: automatic legal
equivalence to handwritten (Article 25(2)) - \textbf{AdES}: cannot be
dismissed as evidence just for being electronic (Article 25(1)), but the
court decides its weight - \textbf{Basic electronic signature}: same as
AdES --- cannot be dismissed for being electronic, but its evidentiary
weight is at the court's discretion

Many developers (and their managers) misunderstand Article 25(1) as
meaning "all electronic signatures are legally valid." That is not what
it says. It says they cannot be denied legal effect \emph{solely}
because they are electronic. A typed name at the bottom of an email is
an electronic signature under eIDAS, but it provides very little
evidence about who actually sent the email, whether they intended to be
bound by its contents, or whether the email was tampered with. A court
will weigh this evidence accordingly.

Only QES gets automatic equivalence. Everything else is a matter of
evidence and judicial discretion.

\subsection{Article 28: Qualified Certificates for Electronic
Signatures}\label{_article_28_qualified_certificates_for_electronic_signatures}

Article 28 requires that qualified certificates contain specific
information, listed in Annex I of the regulation. A qualified
certificate must contain:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  An indication (at least in machine-processable form) that the
  certificate is a qualified certificate
\item
  A set of data representing the QTSP, including the member state where
  it is established and its name
\item
  The signatory's name (or a pseudonym, clearly identified as such)
\item
  Electronic signature validation data (the public key) corresponding to
  the electronic signature creation data (the private key)
\item
  The period of validity
\item
  The certificate serial number
\item
  The advanced electronic signature or seal of the issuing QTSP
\item
  The location where the certificate supporting the QTSP's
  signature/seal is available free of charge
\item
  The location of the QTSP's certificate revocation status services
\end{enumerate}

In X.509 terms, this translates to specific extensions and naming
conventions: - The certificate policies extension must contain the OID
for qualified certificates (0.4.0.194112.1.2 for QES) - The subject DN
must contain the signatory's name - The issuer DN identifies the QTSP -
CRL Distribution Points and/or OCSP responder URLs must be present - The
QCStatements extension (OID 0.4.0.1862.1) carries machine-readable
qualification information

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{{-}{-} QCStatements extension (ETSI EN 319 412{-}5)}
\NormalTok{QCStatements ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE} \DataTypeTok{OF}\NormalTok{ QCStatement}

\NormalTok{QCStatement ::= }\DataTypeTok{SEQUENCE}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    statementId   OBJECT IDENTIFIER,}
\NormalTok{    statementInfo ANY DEFINED BY statementId }\KeywordTok{OPTIONAL}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{{-}{-} Key OIDs:}
\CommentTok{{-}{-} id{-}etsi{-}qcs{-}QcCompliance  (0.4.0.1862.1.1)  = "this is a qualified cert"}
\CommentTok{{-}{-} id{-}etsi{-}qcs{-}QcSSCD        (0.4.0.1862.1.4)  = "key is on a QSCD"}
\CommentTok{{-}{-} id{-}etsi{-}qcs{-}QcType         (0.4.0.1862.1.6)  = type (esign, eseal, web)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Article 29: Requirements for Qualified Signature Creation
Devices}\label{_article_29_requirements_for_qualified_signature_creation_devices}

Article 29 states that qualified electronic signature creation data (the
private key) must be managed by a QSCD that meets the requirements of
Annex II.

Annex II requires that QSCDs must:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Ensure the confidentiality of the electronic signature creation data
\item
  Ensure that the electronic signature creation data used for signature
  creation can occur only once (practical uniqueness)
\item
  Ensure that the electronic signature creation data cannot be derived
  with reasonable assurance, and the signature is reliably protected
  against forgery using currently available technology
\item
  Ensure that the electronic signature creation data can be reliably
  protected by the legitimate signatory against use by others
\end{enumerate}

In practice, this means: the private key must be generated on, and never
leave, a certified hardware device. The certification is typically
Common Criteria EAL4+ (or equivalent) for the device, plus specific
Protection Profiles defined by CEN and ETSI.

Examples of certified QSCDs: - Smart cards (e.g., IDPrime MD from
Thales, CardOS from Atos) - USB tokens (e.g., SafeNet eToken 5110) -
Hardware Security Modules (e.g., Thales Luna Network HSM, Utimaco
SecurityServer) - Remote QSCDs operated by a QTSP (the key lives in an
HSM in a data center, accessed via API)

The rise of remote QSCDs has been the biggest practical change since
eIDAS entered into force. The original assumption was that QES required
a physical device in the signer's possession (a smart card in their
wallet). But eIDAS Annex II does not require \emph{physical possession}
--- it requires sole control. A private key in an HSM operated by a
QTSP, accessible only through strong multi-factor authentication
specific to the signer and the signing operation, satisfies the
requirement. This opened the door to remote signing (Chapter 22), which
is now the dominant model for QES in practice.

\subsection{Article 35-40: Electronic
Seals}\label{_article_35_40_electronic_seals}

Electronic seals are the organizational counterpart to electronic
signatures. While a signature is created by a natural person (a human),
a seal is created by a legal person (a company, government agency,
association).

The seal articles mirror the signature articles almost exactly: -
Article 35(1): non-discrimination for electronic seals - Article 35(2):
qualified electronic seal enjoys a presumption of integrity and origin -
Article 36: AdESeal requirements (same four properties as AdES, adapted
for legal persons) - Article 38: qualified certificates for electronic
seals (same as Art. 28, but for organizations)

The key difference: Article 35(2) says a qualified seal enjoys a
"presumption of integrity of the data and of correctness of the origin
of that data." Note the careful wording --- it says nothing about the
legal \emph{intent} behind the seal. A signature expresses consent or
approval. A seal proves origin and integrity. Chapter 21 explores this
distinction in depth.

\section{Mutual Recognition}\label{_mutual_recognition}

\subsection{Within the EU}\label{_within_the_eu}

Article 25(3) establishes that a QES based on a qualified certificate
from one member state is recognized as a QES in all member states.
Article 35(3) does the same for qualified electronic seals.

This means: - An Austrian QES is valid in Portugal - A Lithuanian
qualified seal is valid in Spain - An Estonian qualified timestamp is
valid in Germany

Member states \textbf{cannot} impose additional requirements for
cross-border recognition. They cannot require that QES certificates be
issued by domestic QTSPs. They cannot require additional technical
features beyond what eIDAS specifies.

The mechanism that makes this work is the \textbf{EU Trusted List
(EUTL)} --- a machine-readable, cryptographically signed registry of all
QTSPs in the EU. If a certificate chains to a QTSP on the EUTL, it
qualifies for cross-border recognition. Chapter 20 covers the EUTL in
detail.

In practice, cross-border recognition is still imperfect. While the
\emph{legal} framework is clear, many national IT systems have not been
updated to consume the EUTL and accept certificates from other member
states. A 2021 European Commission survey found that while most member
states had updated their legal frameworks, actual system-level
interoperability lagged significantly.

If your software validates signatures against a fixed list of trusted
certificates (rather than dynamically checking the EUTL), you are likely
rejecting valid QES from other member states. This is a compliance
failure under eIDAS --- Article 25(3) does not have exceptions for "we
haven't updated our trust store yet."

\subsection{Non-EU Recognition}\label{_non_eu_recognition}

eIDAS Article 14 allows the European Commission to adopt implementing
acts recognizing electronic identification schemes from third countries,
provided they meet equivalent requirements.

In practice, non-EU recognition is sparse: - \textbf{Switzerland} has a
bilateral agreement with the EU that provides for mutual recognition of
qualified trust services under the ZertES (Swiss Electronic Signatures
Act) - \textbf{United Kingdom} post-Brexit: the UK retained eIDAS as
domestic law (the UK eIDAS regulation) but mutual recognition lapsed; UK
QES are not automatically recognized in the EU and vice versa -
\textbf{United States}: no formal equivalence arrangement; US digital
signatures have no special status under eIDAS (and eIDAS signatures have
no special status under US law)

For developers serving global markets, this means: do not assume that a
qualified certificate from a non-EU QTSP will be recognized as
"qualified" in the EU, even if the QTSP provides technically identical
services. Qualification is a \emph{legal status} granted by an EU member
state's supervisory body, not a technical property of the certificate.

\section{eIDAS 2.0: Regulation (EU)
2024/1183}\label{_eidas_2_0_regulation_eu_20241183}

On April 11, 2024, the revised eIDAS regulation --- commonly called
\textbf{eIDAS 2.0} or Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0) --- entered
into force. The revision keeps the trust services framework largely
intact but adds a transformative new component: the \textbf{European
Digital Identity Wallet (EUDI Wallet)}.

\subsection{What Changed}\label{_what_changed}

The key changes in eIDAS 2.0:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{EUDI Wallet}: Every member state must provide a digital
  identity wallet to its citizens and residents by 2026. The wallet will
  carry government-issued Person Identification Data (PID) and Qualified
  Electronic Attestations of Attributes (QEAAs). Crucially, the wallet
  will be able to create QES --- making qualified signatures accessible
  to every EU citizen from their smartphone.
\item
  \textbf{Qualified Electronic Attestations of Attributes (QEAAs)}: A
  new trust service type. QEAAs are digitally signed attestations of
  specific attributes (age, professional qualifications, university
  degrees) that can be presented through the EUDI wallet. Think of them
  as digital equivalents of physical certificates and credentials.
\item
  \textbf{Remote QSCD in the wallet}: The EUDI wallet can use a remote
  QSCD operated by a QTSP for creating qualified signatures. The private
  key lives in an HSM in the QTSP's data center, but the wallet provides
  the authentication and sole control mechanisms.
\item
  \textbf{Free QES for citizens}: Article 45e requires that natural
  persons be offered QES creation through the wallet free of charge for
  non-professional use. This is a dramatic change --- until now, QES
  required purchasing a certificate from a commercial QTSP, which
  typically costs 50-300 EUR per year.
\item
  \textbf{Qualified Electronic Ledgers}: A new trust service type for
  distributed ledger technology (blockchain). ETSI is currently
  developing the technical standards.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Impact on Developers}\label{_impact_on_developers}

For PDF signature developers, eIDAS 2.0 means:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Expect a surge in QES volume}: Free QES through the wallet
  will dramatically increase the number of qualified signatures in
  circulation. Your validation code needs to handle EUTL lookups
  efficiently.
\item
  \textbf{Wallet-initiated signing flows}: The EUDI wallet defines a new
  interaction pattern where the wallet app initiates a signing flow with
  a remote QSCD. If your application accepts signed documents, you need
  to support the signature formats the wallet produces.
\item
  \textbf{QEAA validation}: You may need to validate not just the
  signature on a document, but also QEAAs presented alongside it (e.g.,
  a doctor signing a prescription presents their medical qualification
  attestation).
\end{itemize}

The EUDI wallet's Architecture Reference Framework (ARF) is publicly
available and under active development. As of early 2026, large-scale
pilots are running in multiple sectors across the EU. The technical
specifications are not yet final, but the direction is clear: QES will
move from a niche enterprise technology to a consumer feature on
smartphones. Plan your architecture accordingly.

\section{The Gap Between Regulation and
Reality}\label{_the_gap_between_regulation_and_reality}

\subsection{What the Regulation Says}\label{_what_the_regulation_says}

eIDAS paints an elegant picture: three clearly defined tiers, qualified
trust services supervised by competent national bodies, mutual
recognition across borders, machine-readable trusted lists that software
can consume programmatically.

\subsection{What Actually Happens}\label{_what_actually_happens}

The reality is more complicated.

\textbf{National implementation varies despite the regulation's direct
applicability.} While member states cannot transpose the regulation
differently (it is directly applicable), they \emph{can} adopt
supplementary national measures. Germany's
\emph{Vertrauensdienstegesetz} (Trust Services Act), France's
\emph{decret relatif a la signature electronique}, and Italy's
\emph{Codice dell'Amministrazione Digitale} all contain national
provisions that fill gaps the regulation left open. These national
provisions sometimes create practical differences in how QES is
implemented and accepted.

\textbf{EUTL consumption is not universal.} Many government and
enterprise systems still validate signatures against hardcoded trust
stores rather than dynamically checking the EUTL. This means they accept
domestic QES but reject equally valid QES from other member states.

\textbf{QSCD certification is slow and expensive.} Getting a signature
creation device certified as a QSCD takes 12-24 months and costs
hundreds of thousands of euros. This limits the number of available
QSCDs and concentrates the market among a few hardware vendors.

\textbf{The "qualified" premium.} Qualified certificates cost
significantly more than standard certificates --- typically 50-300 EUR
per year for individuals, and more for organizations. This price barrier
has limited adoption, particularly among small businesses and
individuals. eIDAS 2.0's free QES through the wallet aims to address
this, but the wallet is not yet widely deployed.

\textbf{Interoperability testing is ongoing.} The European Commission
funds regular interoperability testing events (called "plugtests")
through ETSI, where QTSPs and software vendors test their
implementations against each other. These events consistently reveal
interoperability issues --- signatures that validate in one tool but
fail in another, timestamps that are accepted by some validators but
rejected by others, EUTL entries that are ambiguous about the services a
QTSP provides.

\section{Common Misconceptions}\label{_common_misconceptions}

\subsection{"eIDAS makes all digital signatures
legal"}\label{_eidas_makes_all_digital_signatures_legal}

No. eIDAS creates a \emph{framework} with three tiers. Only QES gets
automatic legal equivalence to handwritten signatures. Basic electronic
signatures and advanced electronic signatures benefit only from the
non-discrimination principle --- they cannot be rejected \emph{solely}
for being electronic, but their legal weight is a matter for the court
to decide.

\subsection{"If my signature uses a certificate, it's
advanced"}\label{_if_my_signature_uses_a_certificate_its_advanced}

Not necessarily. An advanced electronic signature must satisfy all four
requirements of Article 26: uniquely linked to the signatory, capable of
identifying them, under sole control, and linked to the data. A
certificate-based signature where the private key is stored in a shared
server-side keystore, accessible to multiple system administrators,
arguably fails the "sole control" requirement.

\subsection{"PAdES = eIDAS compliance"}\label{_pades_eidas_compliance}

Not automatically. PAdES defines technical profiles for PDF signatures.
Using PAdES-B-LTA with a qualified certificate on a QSCD produces a QES.
Using PAdES-B-LTA with a self-signed certificate produces a technically
well-structured signature with zero eIDAS qualification. The eIDAS tier
depends on \emph{who issued the certificate} and \emph{where the key is
stored}, not on the PDF signature format alone.

\subsection{"My country's signatures are recognized everywhere in the
EU"}\label{_my_countrys_signatures_are_recognized_everywhere_in_the_eu}

Only if they are \emph{qualified}. A non-qualified signature from
Germany has no special status in France --- it is just evidence, to be
evaluated by the French court on its merits. Only QES enjoys automatic
cross-border recognition under Article 25(3).

\subsection{"eIDAS applies outside the
EU"}\label{_eidas_applies_outside_the_eu}

eIDAS is EU law. It applies within the EU, the EEA (through the EEA
Agreement), and countries with specific bilateral arrangements
(Switzerland). It does not apply in the United States, China, Japan, or
anywhere else, unless those jurisdictions choose to recognize
eIDAS-qualified signatures under their own legal frameworks.

However, eIDAS has become a de facto global reference model. Countries
designing their own electronic signature frameworks often look to eIDAS
for inspiration. The three-tier model (basic, advanced, qualified) has
been adopted in various forms by multiple non-EU jurisdictions.

\section{What Your Code Needs to Do}\label{_what_your_code_needs_to_do}

For developers building PDF signature creation or validation software,
eIDAS imposes specific technical requirements depending on the tier you
are targeting:

\subsection{For Basic Electronic
Signatures}\label{_for_basic_electronic_signatures}

Nothing beyond what PDF already requires. Any valid PDF signature ---
even a simple \texttt{/SubFilter\ /adbe.pkcs7.detached} with a
self-signed certificate --- is an electronic signature under Article
3(10).

\subsection{For Advanced Electronic
Signatures}\label{_for_advanced_electronic_signatures}

Your signing workflow must ensure: 1. \textbf{Unique linkage}: one
private key per signer (never share keys) 2. \textbf{Identification}:
the certificate must contain the signer's identity (name, at minimum) 3.
\textbf{Sole control}: meaningful per-signature authentication (not just
session login) 4. \textbf{Data linkage}: the signature must cover the
entire document (standard byte range coverage)

Technically, this means: PAdES-B-B or higher, with a certificate issued
to a specific individual, and a signing flow that authenticates the
signer for each signature.

\subsection{For Qualified Electronic
Signatures}\label{_for_qualified_electronic_signatures}

All of the above, plus: 1. \textbf{Qualified certificate}: issued by a
QTSP listed on the EUTL 2. \textbf{QSCD}: the private key must be on a
certified qualified signature creation device 3. \textbf{Recommendation:
PAdES-B-LTA}: while not strictly required by eIDAS, PAdES-B-LTA ensures
long-term verifiability

\subsection{For Validation}\label{_for_validation}

If your software validates signatures and needs to determine the eIDAS
tier:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Determines the eIDAS tier of a PDF signature}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ This is a simplified illustration }\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ production code should use EU DSS}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ which handles the full complexity of EUTL parsing and chain validation}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public}\NormalTok{ EidasTier }\FunctionTok{determineEidasTier}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ signerCert}\OperatorTok{,}
                                     \DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ keyOnQscd}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \CommentTok{// Check if the certificate is qualified (issued by a QTSP)}
    \DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ isQualifiedCert }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{isListedOnEutl}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signerCert}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// Check for QCStatements extension}
    \DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ hasQcCompliance }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{hasQcStatement}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signerCert}\OperatorTok{,}
            \StringTok{"0.4.0.1862.1.1"}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// id{-}etsi{-}qcs{-}QcCompliance}
    \DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ hasQscdStatement }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{hasQcStatement}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signerCert}\OperatorTok{,}
            \StringTok{"0.4.0.1862.1.4"}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// id{-}etsi{-}qcs{-}QcSSCD}

    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{isQualifiedCert }\OperatorTok{\&\&}\NormalTok{ hasQcCompliance }\OperatorTok{\&\&} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{keyOnQscd }\OperatorTok{||}\NormalTok{ hasQscdStatement}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ EidasTier}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{QUALIFIED}\OperatorTok{;}     \CommentTok{// QES {-} Art. 25(2) applies}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\FunctionTok{meetsArticle26Requirements}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signerCert}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ EidasTier}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{ADVANCED}\OperatorTok{;}      \CommentTok{// AdES {-} Art. 25(1) applies}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ EidasTier}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{BASIC}\OperatorTok{;}            \CommentTok{// Basic {-} Art. 25(1) applies}
\OperatorTok{\}}

\KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{boolean} \FunctionTok{hasQcStatement}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ oid}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ extValue }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getExtensionValue}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"1.3.6.1.5.5.7.1.3"}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{extValue }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
    \CommentTok{// Parse QCStatements ASN.1 structure}
\NormalTok{    ASN1Sequence qcStatements }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ASN1Sequence}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            ASN1OctetString}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{extValue}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{getOctets}\OperatorTok{());}
    \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ASN1Encodable statement }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ qcStatements}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        ASN1Sequence seq }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ASN1Sequence}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{statement}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        ASN1ObjectIdentifier statementId }\OperatorTok{=}
\NormalTok{                ASN1ObjectIdentifier}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{seq}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getObjectAt}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{));}
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{statementId}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getId}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{equals}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{oid}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{;}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
    \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The code above is a simplified illustration. Production eIDAS tier
determination is significantly more complex because:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  EUTL lookup requires parsing the trusted list XML, validating its
  signature, and traversing the LOT hierarchy
\item
  Certificate chain validation must check revocation status for every
  certificate in the chain
\item
  QSCD status may need to be inferred from the certificate's
  QCStatements rather than verified directly
\item
  Some QTSPs issue certificates that are qualified for some services but
  not others
\end{itemize}

For production use, the \textbf{EU DSS framework} (Chapter 23) provides
a complete, tested implementation of eIDAS tier determination. Do not
implement this from scratch unless you have very specific requirements
that EU DSS cannot meet.

\section{Key Takeaways}\label{_key_takeaways_7}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  eIDAS (Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS)) is an EU regulation ---
  directly applicable in all member states, no national transposition
  required
\item
  It replaced the failed Electronic Signatures Directive (1999/93/EC),
  which suffered from fragmented national implementations
\item
  Three tiers: basic electronic signature (any electronic indication of
  signing intent), advanced electronic signature (four properties:
  unique linkage, identification, sole control, data linkage), qualified
  electronic signature (advanced + QSCD + qualified certificate)
\item
  Only QES has automatic legal equivalence to a handwritten signature
  (Article 25(2))
\item
  Cross-border recognition is mandatory for QES (Article 25(3)) ---
  enforced through the EU Trusted List
\item
  eIDAS 2.0 (Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0)) adds the EUDI
  wallet, making QES accessible to all EU citizens via smartphone
\item
  The gap between regulation and implementation is real --- code that
  hardcodes trust stores instead of checking the EUTL violates the
  mutual recognition principle
\item
  For production eIDAS validation, use the EU DSS framework (Chapter 23)
  rather than implementing from scratch
\end{itemize}

\chapter{Qualified Certificates \& Trust
Services}\label{_qualified_certificates_trust_services}

A certificate is just a data structure --- some ASN.1 fields, a public
key, and a signature from someone who claims to be a Certificate
Authority. What makes a certificate \emph{qualified} is not a technical
property you can detect by parsing bytes alone. It is a legal status,
conferred by a government supervisory body, published in a
machine-readable trust infrastructure, and backed by an institutional
apparatus that most developers have never heard of, let alone queried
programmatically.

This chapter takes you through that entire apparatus: the Qualified
Trust Service Providers that issue qualified certificates, the EU
Trusted List that publishes who they are, the technical requirements
that separate a qualified certificate from an ordinary one, the QSCD
hardware that protects the keys, and the Java code you need to actually
parse and verify all of this. If Chapter 19 was the legal framework,
this chapter is the plumbing.

\section{Qualified Trust Service Providers
(QTSPs)}\label{_qualified_trust_service_providers_qtsps}

\subsection{What a QTSP Is}\label{_what_a_qtsp_is}

A \textbf{Qualified Trust Service Provider} is an organization that has
been audited, approved, and listed by a national supervisory body within
the EU as meeting the requirements of Regulation (EU) No 910/2014
(eIDAS) Articles 20-24. The word "qualified" is doing serious work
here --- it is not a marketing term, not a self-declaration, and not
something you obtain by installing software. It is a legal status,
analogous to a bank receiving a banking license: you cannot call
yourself a QTSP unless a government body says you are one.

A QTSP can provide one or more qualified trust services:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Qualified certificates for electronic signatures} (natural
  persons)
\item
  \textbf{Qualified certificates for electronic seals} (legal persons)
\item
  \textbf{Qualified timestamps} (RFC 3161 tokens from a qualified TSA)
\item
  \textbf{Qualified electronic delivery services} (certified email
  equivalents)
\item
  \textbf{Qualified website authentication certificates} (the
  controversial Article 45, rewritten in eIDAS 2.0)
\item
  \textbf{Qualified electronic attestations of attributes} (new in
  Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0))
\end{itemize}

The qualification is \emph{per-service}, not per-organization. A
provider might be qualified for timestamps but not for certificates, or
for seals but not for signatures. This matters when you are parsing the
EU Trusted List --- you need to check not just whether the provider is
listed, but \emph{which services} are qualified.

\subsection{How to Become a QTSP}\label{_how_to_become_a_qtsp}

The path to becoming a QTSP is neither quick nor cheap. Here is the
process, simplified but accurate:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Establish the infrastructure.} You need HSMs (certified to
  Common Criteria EAL4+ or equivalent), a secure data center (typically
  ISO 27001 certified), redundant systems, trained personnel, and
  documented processes for every aspect of certificate lifecycle
  management --- issuance, suspension, revocation, key generation, key
  destruction.
\item
  \textbf{Write a Certificate Practice Statement (CPS).} This is the
  document that describes, in exhaustive detail, how you operate:
  identity verification procedures, key management, certificate
  profiles, revocation handling, disaster recovery, incident response. A
  CPS for a QTSP is typically 80-150 pages long.
\item
  \textbf{Get a conformity assessment.} An accredited \textbf{Conformity
  Assessment Body (CAB)} evaluates your infrastructure, processes, and
  CPS against Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) and the applicable
  ETSI standards (primarily ETSI EN 319 401 for general TSP requirements
  and ETSI EN 319 411-1/411-2 for certificate issuance). The assessment
  produces a \textbf{Conformity Assessment Report (CAR)}.
\item
  \textbf{Submit to the national supervisory body.} Each EU member state
  designates a supervisory body for QTSPs: in Germany, the
  \textbf{Bundesnetzagentur} (Federal Network Agency); in France,
  \textbf{ANSSI}; in Italy, \textbf{AgID} (Agenzia per l'Italia
  Digitale). You submit the CAR, your CPS, and supporting documentation.
\item
  \textbf{Supervisory body review and approval.} The supervisory body
  reviews everything, may request additional information or on-site
  inspections, and eventually grants or denies qualified status.
\item
  \textbf{Listing on the national Trusted List.} Once approved, the
  supervisory body adds your service(s) to the national \textbf{Trusted
  Service Status List (TSL)} --- the country's portion of the EU Trusted
  List. Your qualified status takes effect when the updated TSL is
  published.
\item
  \textbf{Ongoing supervision.} Qualification is not a one-time event.
  Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) Article 20(1) mandates audits at
  least every 24 months. Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) Article
  19(2) requires QTSPs to report security incidents to the supervisory
  body within 24 hours.
\end{enumerate}

The entire process from initial infrastructure setup to EUTL listing
typically takes \textbf{18-36 months} and costs \textbf{several hundred
thousand to several million euros}, depending on the scope of services
and the jurisdiction. This is by design: the barrier to entry ensures
that only well-resourced, professionally operated organizations can
issue qualified certificates.

If someone offers you "qualified certificates" from a provider that is
not on the EUTL, they are either confused about what "qualified" means
or deliberately misleading you. Always verify against the EUTL.

\subsection{The Supervision Model}\label{_the_supervision_model}

The eIDAS supervision model is a two-layer structure:

\textbf{National layer:} Each member state's supervisory body is
responsible for QTSPs established in its territory. The supervisory body
grants and revokes qualified status, maintains the national TSL,
conducts or commissions audits, handles incident reports, and imposes
sanctions for non-compliance.

\textbf{EU layer:} The European Commission maintains the \textbf{List of
Trusted Lists (LOTL)} --- a master document that points to every
national TSL. The Commission does not supervise individual QTSPs; it
supervises the supervisory bodies indirectly, through the mutual
evaluation mechanism and through the eIDAS Expert Group.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4286}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4286}}@{}}
\caption{Supervision hierarchy}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Level
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Entity
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Responsibility
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Level
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Entity
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Responsibility
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
EU & European Commission & Maintains LOTL, sets technical standards via
implementing acts, coordinates mutual evaluations \\
National & Supervisory Body (Bundesnetzagentur, ANSSI, AgID, etc.) &
Grants/revokes qualified status, maintains national TSL, audits QTSPs,
handles incidents \\
Provider & QTSP (DigiCert, D-Trust, InfoCert, etc.) & Operates trust
services, issues certificates, complies with ETSI standards, reports
incidents \\
\end{longtable}

\section{The EU Trusted List (EUTL)}\label{_the_eu_trusted_list_eutl}

\subsection{Architecture: The LOT
Hierarchy}\label{_architecture_the_lot_hierarchy}

The EUTL is not a single list --- it is a \textbf{hierarchy of
cryptographically signed XML documents}:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  The \textbf{LOTL (List of Trusted Lists)} is the root. Maintained by
  the European Commission, published at a well-known URL. The LOTL
  contains pointers (URLs) to every national TSL, along with the
  certificates used to verify each national TSL's signature.
\item
  Each \textbf{national TSL (Trusted Service Status List)} lists the
  trust service providers in that country, their services, the status of
  each service (granted, withdrawn, deprecated), and the CA certificates
  associated with each service.
\item
  Each TSL is \textbf{digitally signed} using an XML Advanced Electronic
  Signature (XAdES) --- the same trust-no-one verification model applied
  to the trust infrastructure itself.
\end{enumerate}

The LOTL URL is:

\begin{verbatim}
https://ec.europa.eu/tools/lotl/eu-lotl.xml
\end{verbatim}

This URL has been stable for years, but production systems should make
it configurable. The official reference for the LOTL location is
Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2015/1505. The LOTL itself
contains a \texttt{NextUpdate} field indicating when the next version
will be published. National TSLs are updated on varying
schedules --- some monthly, some quarterly, some whenever a change
occurs.

\subsection{XML Schema}\label{_xml_schema}

The TSL XML schema is defined in ETSI TS 119 612 (replacing the older
ETSI TS 102 231). The schema is verbose, deeply nested, and frankly
exhausting to read --- but it is well-structured and consistent.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\textless{}!{-}{-} Simplified LOTL structure {-}{-}\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{TrustServiceStatusList} \OtherTok{xmlns=}\StringTok{"http://uri.etsi.org/02231/v2\#"}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  \textless{}}\KeywordTok{SchemeInformation}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}}\KeywordTok{TSLVersionIdentifier}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}5\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{TSLVersionIdentifier}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}}\KeywordTok{TSLSequenceNumber}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}321\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{TSLSequenceNumber}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}}\KeywordTok{TSLType}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{      http://uri.etsi.org/TrstSvc/TrustedList/TSLType/EUlistofthelists}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}/}\KeywordTok{TSLType}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}}\KeywordTok{SchemeOperatorName}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{      \textless{}}\KeywordTok{Name} \OtherTok{xml:lang=}\StringTok{"en"}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}European Commission\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{Name}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}/}\KeywordTok{SchemeOperatorName}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}}\KeywordTok{ListIssueDateTime}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}2026{-}02{-}15T00:00:00Z\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{ListIssueDateTime}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}}\KeywordTok{NextUpdate}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{      \textless{}}\KeywordTok{dateTime}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}2026{-}08{-}15T00:00:00Z\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{dateTime}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}/}\KeywordTok{NextUpdate}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  \textless{}/}\KeywordTok{SchemeInformation}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}

\NormalTok{  \textless{}}\KeywordTok{TrustServiceProviderList}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}}\KeywordTok{TrustServiceProvider}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{      \textless{}}\KeywordTok{TSPInformation}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{        \textless{}}\KeywordTok{TSPName}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}\textless{}}\KeywordTok{Name} \OtherTok{xml:lang=}\StringTok{"en"}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}Germany\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{Name}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{TSPName}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{      \textless{}/}\KeywordTok{TSPInformation}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{      \textless{}}\KeywordTok{TSPServices}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{        \textless{}}\KeywordTok{TSPService}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{          \textless{}}\KeywordTok{ServiceInformation}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{            \textless{}}\KeywordTok{ServiceTypeIdentifier}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{              http://uri.etsi.org/TrstSvc/Svctype/TLIssuer}
\NormalTok{            \textless{}/}\KeywordTok{ServiceTypeIdentifier}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{            \textless{}}\KeywordTok{ServiceSupplyPoints}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{              \textless{}}\KeywordTok{ServiceSupplyPoint}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{                https://www.bundesnetzagentur.de/.../TrustedList.xml}
\NormalTok{              \textless{}/}\KeywordTok{ServiceSupplyPoint}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{            \textless{}/}\KeywordTok{ServiceSupplyPoints}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}

\NormalTok{        \textless{}/}\KeywordTok{TSPService}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}

\NormalTok{    \textless{}/}\KeywordTok{TrustServiceProvider}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
    \CommentTok{\textless{}!{-}{-} ... 26 more countries ... {-}{-}\textgreater{}}

\NormalTok{\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{TrustServiceStatusList}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}

Each \textbf{national TSL} then contains the actual QTSP entries with
their service certificates:

Statuses}\label{_key_service_types_and_statuses}

\bottomrule\noalign{}

\texttt{…\hspace{0pt}/Svcstatus/granted} & Currently qualified --- this
is what you are looking for \\
\texttt{…\hspace{0pt}/Svcstatus/withdrawn} & Qualification has been
withdrawn \\
\texttt{…\hspace{0pt}/Svcstatus/recognisedatnationallevel} & Not
EU-qualified, but recognized under national law \\
\texttt{…\hspace{0pt}/Svcstatus/deprecated} & Service deprecated by the
supervisory body \\
\end{longtable}

A common mistake in EUTL parsing is to check only the current status.
You also need to check the \textbf{service history}. A service that was
\texttt{granted} from 2019-01-01 to 2023-06-30 and then
\texttt{withdrawn} was qualified during that window. A certificate
issued during the \texttt{granted} period is still a qualified
certificate, even though the service is now withdrawn.

The TSL includes a \texttt{ServiceHistory} element with
\texttt{ServiceHistoryInstance} entries recording every status change.
Your validation logic must check whether the certificate was issued (and
the signature created) during a period when the service status was
\texttt{granted}.

\subsection{Cryptographic Signing of the
TSL}\label{_cryptographic_signing_of_the_tsl}

Every TSL (including the LOTL) is signed with an XAdES signature. To
trust the contents, you must:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Verify the XML signature} on the TSL document
\item
  \textbf{Verify the signing certificate} against the LOTL (for national
  TSLs) or against the Commission's well-known certificate (for the LOTL
  itself)
\item
  \textbf{Check freshness} --- the TSL must not be past its
  \texttt{NextUpdate} date (or not past it by more than a configurable
  grace period)
\end{enumerate}

The LOTL signing certificate is published by the European Commission at
a well-known URL. When the signing certificate rotates, the new
certificate is announced in advance in the LOTL itself --- this is the
\textbf{pivot mechanism}, and it is one of the trickiest parts of EUTL
parsing to implement correctly.

For production systems, implement a scheduled refresh --- daily or
weekly --- that re-downloads, re-verifies, and re-parses the entire EUTL
hierarchy. The EU DSS library (Chapter 23) provides a
\texttt{TrustedListsCertificateSource} that handles all of this
automatically, including caching, refresh scheduling, signature
verification, and the pivot mechanism.

\section{Qualified Certificates: What Makes Them
Qualified}\label{_qualified_certificates_what_makes_them_qualified}

A certificate is "qualified" when three conditions are simultaneously
true:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  It is issued by a CA whose certificate issuance service has
  \texttt{granted} status in the EUTL
\item
  The certificate contains specific technical indicators (the
  QCStatements extension)
\item
  The CA followed the procedures defined in its CPS, which was approved
  during the conformity assessment
\end{enumerate}

Condition 3 is not something you can verify programmatically --- it is
an institutional guarantee, backed by the supervisory body's ongoing
oversight. Conditions 1 and 2 are what your code checks.

\subsection{The QCStatements
Extension}\label{_the_qcstatements_extension}

The technical indicator of a qualified certificate is the
\textbf{QCStatements extension} (OID \texttt{1.3.6.1.5.5.7.1.3}),
defined in ETSI EN 319 412-5. This extension contains a sequence of
statements, each identified by an OID:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4444}}@{}}
\caption{QCStatements OIDs relevant to PDF signatures}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Short Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Meaning
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Short Name
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Meaning
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{0.4.0.1862.1.1} & id-etsi-qcs-QcCompliance & This certificate is
issued as a \textbf{qualified certificate} under Regulation (EU) No
910/2014 (eIDAS) \\
\texttt{0.4.0.1862.1.4} & id-etsi-qcs-QcSSCD & The private key resides
on a \textbf{Qualified Signature/Seal Creation Device} \\
\texttt{0.4.0.1862.1.6} & id-etsi-qcs-QcType & The type: esign
(\texttt{…\hspace{0pt}6.1} = signature), eseal
(\texttt{…\hspace{0pt}6.2} = seal), web (\texttt{…\hspace{0pt}6.3}) \\
\texttt{0.4.0.1862.1.3} & id-etsi-qcs-QcRetentionPeriod & How long the
CA retains registration information after certificate expiry \\
\texttt{0.4.0.1862.1.5} & id-etsi-qcs-QcPDS & URL of the PKI Disclosure
Statement (human-readable CPS summary) \\
\texttt{0.4.0.1862.1.2} & id-etsi-qcs-QcLimitValue & Monetary
transaction limit (rarely used) \\
\end{longtable}

Do \textbf{not} trust the QCStatements extension alone. Anyone can put
\texttt{id-etsi-qcs-QcCompliance} in a self-signed certificate. The
QCStatements assertion is only meaningful when the issuing CA is listed
in the EUTL with \texttt{granted} status for a \texttt{CA/QC} service.

The correct validation logic is:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Check that the issuing CA's certificate matches one in the EUTL with
  \texttt{ServiceTypeIdentifier} of \texttt{CA/QC} and status
  \texttt{granted}
\item
  Check the period: the certificate's \texttt{notBefore} date must fall
  within the service's \texttt{granted} status period
\item
  \emph{Then} check QCStatements for the specific type (esign, eseal,
  web)
\item
  \emph{Then} check for QcSSCD if you need to verify QSCD usage
\end{enumerate}

Steps 1-2 without steps 3-4: you know the CA is qualified, but not
necessarily this specific certificate. Steps 3-4 without steps 1-2: you
have a self-asserted claim with no institutional backing. You need all
four.

\subsection{Certificate Policy OIDs}\label{_certificate_policy_oids}

Qualified certificates also carry specific \textbf{Certificate Policy}
OIDs in the \texttt{certificatePolicies} extension (OID
\texttt{2.5.29.32}), defined in ETSI EN 319 411-2:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4286}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5714}}@{}}
\caption{Certificate Policy OIDs for qualified
certificates}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Meaning
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Meaning
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{0.4.0.194112.1.2} & QCP-n: Qualified Certificate Policy for
\textbf{natural persons} (signatures) \\
\texttt{0.4.0.194112.1.3} & QCP-l: Qualified Certificate Policy for
\textbf{legal persons} (seals) \\
\texttt{0.4.0.194112.1.1} & QCP-n-qscd: Natural person certificates with
QSCD \\
\texttt{0.4.0.194112.1.4} & QCP-l-qscd: Legal person certificates with
QSCD \\
\texttt{0.4.0.194112.1.0} & QCP-w: Website authentication \\
\end{longtable}

The \texttt{-qscd} variants indicate that the CA has verified that the
subscriber's private key is on a QSCD. This is a stronger assertion than
the QcSSCD statement alone --- the policy OID says "the CA has verified
the key is on a QSCD according to the procedures defined in ETSI EN 319
411-2."

\subsection{QSCD Requirements}\label{_qscd_requirements}

A Qualified Signature Creation Device (or Qualified Seal Creation
Device --- QSCD covers both) must meet the requirements of Regulation
(EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) Annex II:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  The private key must be generated on the device and never leave it
\item
  The signing operation must occur on the device itself
\item
  Access to the private key must require authentication of the
  legitimate holder (PIN, biometric, etc.)
\item
  The private key cannot be derived from signatures or the public key
  with reasonable assurance
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Certification path:} Common Criteria evaluation (ISO/IEC 15408)
at EAL4+ against Protection Profiles defined in EN 419 211 (for smart
cards and tokens) or EN 419 221 (for HSMs and remote signing). A
national certification body (BSI in Germany, ANSSI in France) performs
the evaluation.

\textbf{Hardware categories in practice:}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Smart cards} (Thales IDPrime MD, Atos CardOS): private key
  generated on chip, never exported, signing performed on-card
\item
  \textbf{USB tokens} (SafeNet eToken 5110, YubiKey 5 FIPS): same
  security model as smart cards, USB form factor
\item
  \textbf{HSMs} (Thales Luna, Utimaco SecurityServer): rack-mounted
  devices for server-side signing; basis for remote signing (Chapter 22)
\end{itemize}

Not every HSM is a certified QSCD. An HSM may be FIPS 140-2 Level 3 or
Common Criteria EAL4+ certified for \emph{general cryptographic
operations} without being certified as a QSCD under the specific EN 419
211/221 Protection Profiles. The QSCD certification must reference the
eIDAS-specific Protection Profiles.

\section{Real-World QTSPs}\label{_real_world_qtsps}

When you are debugging why a particular qualified certificate does or
does not validate, knowing who issued it and where they are listed is
invaluable. Here are the QTSPs you are most likely to encounter:

\textbf{D-Trust GmbH} (Germany) --- a subsidiary of the Bundesdruckerei
(the organization that prints German passports). Listed in the German
TSL, supervised by the Bundesnetzagentur. Dominant in German government
and enterprise contexts.

\textbf{DigiCert / QuoVadis} (Netherlands, Belgium) --- DigiCert
acquired QuoVadis in 2019, inheriting QTSP status in multiple EU
countries. One of the few providers with both AATL and eIDAS qualified
status --- useful when documents must validate in both Adobe Acrobat and
European government systems.

\textbf{GlobalSign} (Belgium) --- listed as a QTSP in Belgium,
supervised by the FPS Economy. Strong enterprise presence. AATL
membership plus EUTL listing gives broad compatibility.

\textbf{Swisscom Trust Services} (Austria, Switzerland) --- the Swiss
telecoms giant operates a QTSP listed in the \emph{Austrian} TSL (not
Swiss --- because Switzerland is not in the EU) plus ZertES
qualification in Switzerland. Their remote signing service is popular
for high-volume enterprise signing.

\textbf{InfoCert} (Italy) --- one of the largest European QTSPs by
volume. Listed in the Italian TSL, supervised by AgID. Part of the
Tinexta Group. Deeply embedded in Italian public administration.

\textbf{Asseco Data Systems / Certum} (Poland) --- the dominant QTSP in
Central/Eastern Europe. If you encounter qualified certificates from
Polish organizations, Certum is the likely issuer.

This list is not exhaustive --- there are over 200 qualified trust
services listed across the EUTL. The EU maintains a browsable version at
\texttt{https://eidas.ec.europa.eu/efda/tl-browser/} where you can
filter by country, service type, and status.

\section{Java Code: Parsing the EUTL}\label{_java_code_parsing_the_eutl}

\subsection{Downloading and Parsing the
LOTL}\label{_downloading_and_parsing_the_lotl}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{InputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{net}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{URI}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{List}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{javax}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{xml}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{parsers}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{DocumentBuilderFactory}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{w3c}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dom}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Document}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{w3c}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dom}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Element}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{w3c}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dom}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{NodeList}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{lombok}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Data}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{lombok}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{extern}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{slf4j}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Slf4j}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Parses the EU List of Trusted Lists }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{LOTL}\CommentTok{)}\NormalTok{ to extract}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ pointers to each national Trusted Service Status List}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\AttributeTok{@Slf4j}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ EutlLotlParser }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ LOTL\_URL }\OperatorTok{=}
            \StringTok{"https://ec.europa.eu/tools/lotl/eu{-}lotl.xml"}\OperatorTok{;}
    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ TSL\_NS }\OperatorTok{=}
            \StringTok{"http://uri.etsi.org/02231/v2\#"}\OperatorTok{;}

    \AttributeTok{@Data}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ TslPointer }\OperatorTok{\{}
        \KeywordTok{private} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ countryCode}\OperatorTok{;}
        \KeywordTok{private} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ tslUrl}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \KeywordTok{public} \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{TslPointer}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{parseLotl}\OperatorTok{()} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Downloading LOTL from: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ LOTL\_URL}\OperatorTok{);}

        \BuiltInTok{DocumentBuilderFactory}\NormalTok{ factory }\OperatorTok{=}
                \BuiltInTok{DocumentBuilderFactory}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{newInstance}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        factory}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setNamespaceAware}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}
        \CommentTok{// Prevent XXE attacks}
\NormalTok{        factory}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFeature}\OperatorTok{(}
                \StringTok{"http://apache.org/xml/features/"}
                \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"disallow{-}doctype{-}decl"}\OperatorTok{,} \KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}

        \BuiltInTok{Document}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{;}
        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{InputStream}\NormalTok{ in }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{URI}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{create}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{LOTL\_URL}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{toURL}\OperatorTok{()}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{openStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ factory}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{newDocumentBuilder}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{parse}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{in}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{TslPointer}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ pointers }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}();}
        \BuiltInTok{NodeList}\NormalTok{ nodes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getElementsByTagNameNS}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                TSL\_NS}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"OtherTSLPointer"}\OperatorTok{);}

        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ i }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{;}\NormalTok{ i }\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{ nodes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLength}\OperatorTok{();}\NormalTok{ i}\OperatorTok{++)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \BuiltInTok{Element}\NormalTok{ pointer }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Element}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ nodes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{item}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{i}\OperatorTok{);}

            \BuiltInTok{NodeList}\NormalTok{ locations }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ pointer}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getElementsByTagNameNS}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    TSL\_NS}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"TSLLocation"}\OperatorTok{);}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{locations}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLength}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{==} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{continue}\OperatorTok{;}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ url }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ locations}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{item}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{getTextContent}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{trim}\OperatorTok{();}

            \BuiltInTok{NodeList}\NormalTok{ territories }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ pointer}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getElementsByTagNameNS}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    TSL\_NS}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"SchemeTerritory"}\OperatorTok{);}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ country }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ territories}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLength}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{0}
                    \OperatorTok{?}\NormalTok{ territories}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{item}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{getTextContent}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{trim}\OperatorTok{()}
                    \OperatorTok{:} \StringTok{"UNKNOWN"}\OperatorTok{;}

\NormalTok{            TslPointer p }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{TslPointer}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{            p}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCountryCode}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{country}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{            p}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setTslUrl}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{url}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{            pointers}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{p}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{            log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{debug}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Found TSL: country=\{\}, url=\{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ country}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ url}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Parsed LOTL: \{\} national TSL pointers"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                pointers}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{());}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ pointers}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}

\subsection{Matching Certificates Against the
EUTL}\label{_matching_certificates_against_the_eutl}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ByteArrayInputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{InputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{net}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{URI}\OperatorTok{;}
