\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{List}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{javax}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{xml}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{parsers}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{DocumentBuilderFactory}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{w3c}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dom}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Document}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{w3c}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dom}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Element}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{w3c}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dom}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{NodeList}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{lombok}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Data}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{lombok}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{extern}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{slf4j}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Slf4j}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Parses a national TSL and matches signer certificates}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ against qualified CA services listed in the EUTL}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\AttributeTok{@Slf4j}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ EutlCertificateMatcher }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ TSL\_NS }\OperatorTok{=}
            \StringTok{"http://uri.etsi.org/02231/v2\#"}\OperatorTok{;}

    \AttributeTok{@Data}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ QualifiedCaEntry }\OperatorTok{\{}
        \KeywordTok{private} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ providerName}\OperatorTok{;}
        \KeywordTok{private} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ status}\OperatorTok{;}
        \KeywordTok{private} \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ caCertificate}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{/**}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Downloads a national TSL and extracts all CA}\CommentTok{/}\NormalTok{QC services}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ with }\CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{granted}\CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{ status}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*/}
    \KeywordTok{public} \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{QualifiedCaEntry}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{parseNationalTsl}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ tslUrl}\OperatorTok{)}
            \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Parsing national TSL: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ tslUrl}\OperatorTok{);}

        \BuiltInTok{DocumentBuilderFactory}\NormalTok{ factory }\OperatorTok{=}
                \BuiltInTok{DocumentBuilderFactory}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{newInstance}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        factory}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setNamespaceAware}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        factory}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFeature}\OperatorTok{(}
                \StringTok{"http://apache.org/xml/features/"}
                \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"disallow{-}doctype{-}decl"}\OperatorTok{,} \KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}

        \BuiltInTok{Document}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{;}
        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{InputStream}\NormalTok{ in }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{URI}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{create}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tslUrl}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{toURL}\OperatorTok{()}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{openStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ factory}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{newDocumentBuilder}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{parse}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{in}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{QualifiedCaEntry}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ entries }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}();}
        \BuiltInTok{NodeList}\NormalTok{ services }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getElementsByTagNameNS}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                TSL\_NS}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"TSPService"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{CertificateFactory}\NormalTok{ cf }\OperatorTok{=}
                \BuiltInTok{CertificateFactory}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"X.509"}\OperatorTok{);}

        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ i }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{;}\NormalTok{ i }\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{ services}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLength}\OperatorTok{();}\NormalTok{ i}\OperatorTok{++)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \BuiltInTok{Element}\NormalTok{ svc }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Element}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ services}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{item}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{i}\OperatorTok{);}

            \CommentTok{// Check service type}
            \BuiltInTok{NodeList}\NormalTok{ typeNodes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ svc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getElementsByTagNameNS}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    TSL\_NS}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"ServiceTypeIdentifier"}\OperatorTok{);}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{typeNodes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLength}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{==} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{continue}\OperatorTok{;}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ type }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ typeNodes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{item}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)}
                    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTextContent}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{trim}\OperatorTok{();}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(!}\NormalTok{type}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{contains}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"CA/QC"}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{continue}\OperatorTok{;}
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \CommentTok{// Check status}
            \BuiltInTok{NodeList}\NormalTok{ statusNodes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ svc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getElementsByTagNameNS}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    TSL\_NS}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"ServiceStatus"}\OperatorTok{);}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{statusNodes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLength}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{==} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{continue}\OperatorTok{;}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ status }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ statusNodes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{item}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)}
                    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTextContent}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{trim}\OperatorTok{();}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(!}\NormalTok{status}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{contains}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"granted"}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{continue}\OperatorTok{;}
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \CommentTok{// Extract CA certificate}
            \BuiltInTok{NodeList}\NormalTok{ certNodes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ svc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getElementsByTagNameNS}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    TSL\_NS}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"X509Certificate"}\OperatorTok{);}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certNodes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getLength}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{==} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \ControlFlowTok{continue}\OperatorTok{;}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ b64 }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ certNodes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{item}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{getTextContent}\OperatorTok{()}
                    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{replaceAll}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{s"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{""}\OperatorTok{);}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ certBytes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Base64}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDecoder}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{decode}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{b64}\OperatorTok{);}
            \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{)}
\NormalTok{                    cf}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generateCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayInputStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certBytes}\OperatorTok{));}

\NormalTok{            QualifiedCaEntry entry }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{QualifiedCaEntry}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{            entry}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setStatus}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{status}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{            entry}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCaCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cert}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{            entries}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{entry}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{            log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Found qualified CA: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                    cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSubjectX500Principal}\OperatorTok{());}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ entries}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{/**}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Checks whether the given signer certificate was issued}
     \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ by a CA listed in the EUTL with }\CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{granted}\CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{ status}\CommentTok{.}
     \CommentTok{*/}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{boolean} \FunctionTok{isIssuedByQualifiedCa}\OperatorTok{(}
            \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ signerCert}\OperatorTok{,}
            \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ issuerCert}\OperatorTok{,}
            \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{QualifiedCaEntry}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ eutlEntries}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ issuerEncoded}\OperatorTok{;}
        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            issuerEncoded }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ issuerCert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{();}
        \OperatorTok{\}} \ControlFlowTok{catch} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Exception}\NormalTok{ e}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{error}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Failed to encode issuer cert"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ e}\OperatorTok{);}
            \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{;}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{QualifiedCaEntry entry }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ eutlEntries}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ eutlEncoded }\OperatorTok{=}
\NormalTok{                        entry}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCaCertificate}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{();}
                \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Arrays}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{equals}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{issuerEncoded}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ eutlEncoded}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{                    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Issuer matched EUTL entry: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                            entry}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCaCertificate}\OperatorTok{()}
                                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSubjectX500Principal}\OperatorTok{());}
                    \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{;}
                \OperatorTok{\}}
            \OperatorTok{\}} \ControlFlowTok{catch} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Exception}\NormalTok{ e}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{                log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{warn}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Failed to encode EUTL cert"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ e}\OperatorTok{);}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The code above is educational --- it demonstrates the mechanics of EUTL
parsing and certificate matching. A production implementation must also:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Verify the XML signatures} on the LOTL and every national TSL
\item
  \textbf{Handle the LOTL pivot mechanism} when the signing certificate
  rotates
\item
  \textbf{Parse service history} to support time-based qualification
  checks
\item
  \textbf{Cache and refresh} the hierarchy on a schedule (not on every
  validation)
\item
  \textbf{Parse QCStatements} from the signer's certificate to determine
  the qualification type
\end{itemize}

For production use, the \textbf{EU DSS library} (Chapter 23) provides
\texttt{TrustedListsCertificateSource}, which handles all of this
correctly. Do not reimplement this from scratch unless you have a very
specific reason.

\section{Key Takeaways}\label{_key_takeaways_8}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  A \textbf{QTSP} is not a self-declared status --- it requires
  conformity assessment, supervisory body approval, and listing in the
  EUTL; the process takes 18-36 months and significant investment
\item
  The \textbf{EUTL} is a hierarchy: the LOTL (Commission) points to
  national TSLs (supervisory bodies); every document is
  cryptographically signed with XAdES
\item
  A certificate is qualified when: its issuer is in the EUTL with
  \texttt{granted} status for \texttt{CA/QC}, \emph{and} the certificate
  contains the \texttt{QcCompliance} QCStatement
\item
  \textbf{QCStatements} carry machine-readable qualification data:
  QcCompliance, QcSSCD, QcType (esign/eseal/web)
\item
  \textbf{Certificate Policy OIDs} from ETSI EN 319 411-2 encode the
  qualification policy: QCP-n, QCP-l, with \texttt{-qscd} variants
\item
  \textbf{QSCDs} require Common Criteria EAL4+ certification against EN
  419 211/221 Protection Profiles --- not every HSM qualifies
\item
  Check \textbf{service history} when validating: a certificate issued
  during a \texttt{granted} period is qualified even if the service is
  now \texttt{withdrawn}
\item
  Real-world QTSPs include D-Trust (DE), DigiCert/QuoVadis (NL/BE),
  GlobalSign (BE), Swisscom (AT/CH), InfoCert (IT), Certum (PL), and
  over 200 others
\item
  For production EUTL validation, use the EU DSS framework's
  \texttt{TrustedListsCertificateSource} --- do not reimplement the
  parsing, pivot handling, and signature verification from scratch
\end{itemize}

\chapter{Qualified Signatures vs
Seals}\label{_qualified_signatures_vs_seals}

Here is a question that trips up almost every developer the first time
they encounter it: if an electronic signature and an electronic seal use
exactly the same cryptographic mechanism --- the same RSA or ECDSA
algorithm, the same CMS SignedData structure, the same PAdES profile,
the same qualified certificate from the same QTSP, stored on the same
model of QSCD --- what, exactly, is the difference?

The answer is not technical. It is legal. A signature is created by a
\emph{natural person} (a human being) and expresses that person's
intent. A seal is created by a \emph{legal person} (a company,
government agency, association) and proves the origin and integrity of
data. Same bytes, same math, different legal meaning --- and getting it
wrong can mean the difference between a legally binding contract and an
authenticated invoice.

\section{The Fundamental
Distinction}\label{_the_fundamental_distinction}

\subsection{Signatures: Natural
Persons}\label{_signatures_natural_persons}

An electronic signature under Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) is
always attributed to a \textbf{natural person}. Article 3(10) defines an
electronic signature as "data in electronic form which is attached to or
logically associated with other data in electronic form and which is
used by the signatory to sign." Article 3(9) defines "signatory" as "a
natural person who creates an electronic signature."

A company cannot sign. A department cannot sign. A server cannot sign.
Only a human can sign, and a signature expresses that human's
\textbf{intent} --- consent, approval, acknowledgment, or whatever the
signing context requires.

\subsection{Seals: Legal Persons}\label{_seals_legal_persons}

An electronic seal is attributed to a \textbf{legal person} --- a
company, government agency, or any entity with legal personality.
Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) Article 3(25) defines an electronic
seal as "data in electronic form, which is attached to or logically
associated with other data in electronic form to ensure the latter's
origin and integrity."

Note the careful wording. A seal does not express \emph{intent}. It
proves \textbf{origin} (the data came from this organization) and
\textbf{integrity} (the data has not been tampered with). When Acme Corp
seals an invoice, the seal says: "This invoice was produced by Acme
Corp, and it has not been modified since." It says nothing about which
employee generated it or whether anyone reviewed it.

\section{eIDAS Articles 25 vs 35}\label{_eidas_articles_25_vs_35}

\subsection{Article 25: Signatures}\label{_article_25_signatures}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{25(1)}: Non-discrimination --- an electronic signature shall
  not be denied legal effect solely because it is electronic
\item
  \textbf{25(2)}: A qualified electronic signature has the
  \textbf{equivalent legal effect of a handwritten signature}
\item
  \textbf{25(3)}: A QES from one member state is recognized in all
  member states
\end{itemize}

The critical phrase in 25(2) is "equivalent legal effect." This is the
strongest possible legal recognition --- a QES \emph{is} a handwritten
signature for all legal purposes.

\subsection{Article 35: Seals}\label{_article_35_seals}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{35(1)}: Non-discrimination (same principle as 25(1))
\item
  \textbf{35(2)}: A qualified electronic seal enjoys a
  \textbf{presumption of integrity of the data and of correctness of the
  origin}
\item
  \textbf{35(3)}: Mutual recognition across member states
\item
  \textbf{Article 36}: Advanced seal requirements (same four properties
  as Art. 26, adapted for legal persons)
\item
  \textbf{Article 38}: Qualified certificates for seals must meet Annex
  III requirements
\item
  \textbf{Article 40}: Member states may define additional legal effects
  beyond the presumption
\end{itemize}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\caption{Legal effects comparison}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Aspect
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Qualified Signature (QES)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Qualified Seal (QESeal)
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Aspect
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Qualified Signature (QES)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Qualified Seal (QESeal)
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Created by & Natural person (human) & Legal person (organization) \\
Legal effect & Equivalent to handwritten signature (Art. 25(2)) &
Presumption of integrity and origin (Art. 35(2)) \\
Expresses intent? & Yes --- the signer intends to be bound &
No --- proves origin and integrity only \\
Cross-border & Mandatory recognition (Art. 25(3)) & Mandatory
recognition (Art. 35(3)) \\
\end{longtable}

The difference between "legal equivalence" and "presumption of integrity
and origin" is not academic. A QES provides \textbf{non-repudiation of
commitment} --- the signer personally committed to the content. A QESeal
provides \textbf{non-repudiation of origin} --- the data came from the
stated organization.

If a contract requires a signature, a seal will not suffice --- even a
qualified seal. A seal on a contract proves only that the document came
from the company, not that any authorized representative agreed to its
terms. Conversely, for automated invoices, a seal is the \emph{correct}
choice --- attributing personal intent to a batch process would be
legally inappropriate.

\section{Same Code, Different
Certificate}\label{_same_code_different_certificate}

\subsection{What Differs in the
Certificate}\label{_what_differs_in_the_certificate}

From a cryptographic perspective, there is zero difference. Both are
X.509 certificates, both use the same algorithms, both can be issued by
the same QTSP. The differences are in the metadata:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\caption{Certificate differences: signature vs seal}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Field
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Signature Certificate
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Seal Certificate
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Field
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Signature Certificate
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Seal Certificate
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Subject CN & Natural person's name ("Maria Schmidt") & Organization name
("Acme GmbH") \\
Certificate Policy OID & \texttt{0.4.0.194112.1.2} (QCP-n) or
\texttt{…\hspace{0pt}1.1} (QCP-n-qscd) & \texttt{0.4.0.194112.1.3}
(QCP-l) or \texttt{…\hspace{0pt}1.4} (QCP-l-qscd) \\
QCStatements: QcType & \texttt{0.4.0.1862.1.6.1} (esign) &
\texttt{0.4.0.1862.1.6.2} (eseal) \\
eIDAS Annex & Annex I & Annex III \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{Creating a Seal vs a Signature in
Java}\label{_creating_a_seal_vs_a_signature_in_java}

The code is \textbf{identical}. The only difference is which certificate
and private key you load.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PrivateKey}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Loader}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDDocument}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{interactive}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{digitalsignature}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{lombok}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{RequiredArgsConstructor}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{lombok}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{extern}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{slf4j}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Slf4j}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Creates either a qualified signature or a qualified seal on a}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ PDF document}\CommentTok{. }\NormalTok{The cryptographic operation is identical }\CommentTok{{-}{-}}\NormalTok{ the}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ distinction is entirely in the certificate used}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Signature}\CommentTok{:}\NormalTok{ cert has QcType esign }\CommentTok{(0.4.0.1862.1.6.1),}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{            subject }\CommentTok{=} \CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{CN}\CommentTok{=}\NormalTok{Alice Smith}\CommentTok{"}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Seal}\CommentTok{:}\NormalTok{      cert has QcType eseal }\CommentTok{(0.4.0.1862.1.6.2),}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{            subject }\CommentTok{=} \CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{O}\CommentTok{=}\NormalTok{Acme Corp}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ C}\CommentTok{=}\NormalTok{DE}\CommentTok{"}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\AttributeTok{@Slf4j}
\AttributeTok{@RequiredArgsConstructor}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ PdfSignOrSealService }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{PrivateKey}\NormalTok{ privateKey}\OperatorTok{;}
    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ certificateChain}\OperatorTok{;}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{signOrSeal}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ reason}\OperatorTok{,}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ location}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

\NormalTok{        PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{        PDSignature sig }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFilter}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FILTER\_ADOBE\_PPKLITE}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSubFilter}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                PDSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SUBFILTER\_ETSI\_CADES\_DETACHED}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setReason}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{reason}\OperatorTok{);}    
\NormalTok{        sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setLocation}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{location}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{        CmsSignatureCreator signer }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CmsSignatureCreator}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                privateKey}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ certificateChain}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{        doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addSignature}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sig}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signer}\OperatorTok{);}

        \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\NormalTok{ baos }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{saveIncremental}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{baos}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{close}\OperatorTok{();}

\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"PDF signed/sealed: cert=\{\}, reason=\{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                certificateChain}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{].}\FunctionTok{getSubjectX500Principal}\OperatorTok{(),}
\NormalTok{                reason}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ baos}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toByteArray}\OperatorTok{();}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The reason field typically differs: "Contract approval by Alice Smith"
  for a signature vs "Authenticated by Acme Corp" for a seal
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Detecting Signature vs Seal During
Validation}\label{_detecting_signature_vs_seal_during_validation}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ASN1Encodable}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ASN1ObjectIdentifier}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ASN1OctetString}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{asn1}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ASN1Sequence}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{lombok}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{extern}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{slf4j}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Slf4j}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Determines whether a certificate is for electronic signatures}
 \CommentTok{*} \CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{natural person}\CommentTok{)}\NormalTok{ or electronic seals }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{legal person}\CommentTok{)}\NormalTok{ by}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ examining the QcType statement in QCStatements}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\AttributeTok{@Slf4j}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ SignatureOrSealDetector }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ QC\_STATEMENTS\_OID }\OperatorTok{=}
            \StringTok{"1.3.6.1.5.5.7.1.3"}\OperatorTok{;}
    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ QC\_TYPE\_OID }\OperatorTok{=} \StringTok{"0.4.0.1862.1.6"}\OperatorTok{;}
    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ ESIGN\_OID }\OperatorTok{=} \StringTok{"0.4.0.1862.1.6.1"}\OperatorTok{;}
    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ ESEAL\_OID }\OperatorTok{=} \StringTok{"0.4.0.1862.1.6.2"}\OperatorTok{;}

    \KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{enum}\NormalTok{ CertType }\OperatorTok{\{}\NormalTok{ SIGNATURE}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ SEAL}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{UNKNOWN} \OperatorTok{\}}

    \KeywordTok{public}\NormalTok{ CertType }\FunctionTok{detect}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ extValue }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getExtensionValue}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{QC\_STATEMENTS\_OID}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{extValue }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ CertType}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{UNKNOWN}\OperatorTok{;}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ octets }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ASN1OctetString}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{extValue}\OperatorTok{)}
                    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getOctets}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{            ASN1Sequence statements }\OperatorTok{=}
\NormalTok{                    ASN1Sequence}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{octets}\OperatorTok{);}

            \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ASN1Encodable statement }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ statements}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{                ASN1Sequence seq }\OperatorTok{=}
\NormalTok{                        ASN1Sequence}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{statement}\OperatorTok{);}
                \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ oid }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ASN1ObjectIdentifier}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                        seq}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getObjectAt}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)).}\FunctionTok{getId}\OperatorTok{();}

                \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(!}\NormalTok{QC\_TYPE\_OID}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{equals}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{oid}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{||}\NormalTok{ seq}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{\textless{}} \DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                    \ControlFlowTok{continue}\OperatorTok{;}
                \OperatorTok{\}}

\NormalTok{                ASN1Sequence typeSeq }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ASN1Sequence}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                        seq}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getObjectAt}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{));}
                \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ASN1Encodable entry }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ typeSeq}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ typeOid }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ ASN1ObjectIdentifier}
                            \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{entry}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{getId}\OperatorTok{();}
                    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ESIGN\_OID}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{equals}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{typeOid}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{                        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Certificate type: SIGNATURE"}\OperatorTok{);}
                        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ CertType}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SIGNATURE}\OperatorTok{;}
                    \OperatorTok{\}}
                    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ESEAL\_OID}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{equals}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{typeOid}\OperatorTok{))} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{                        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Certificate type: SEAL"}\OperatorTok{);}
                        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ CertType}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SEAL}\OperatorTok{;}
                    \OperatorTok{\}}
                \OperatorTok{\}}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
        \OperatorTok{\}} \ControlFlowTok{catch} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Exception}\NormalTok{ e}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{warn}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Failed to parse QCStatements"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ e}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ CertType}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{UNKNOWN}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

If the QcType statement is absent (some older certificates predate ETSI
EN 319 412-5), fall back to the certificate policy OID:
\texttt{0.4.0.194112.1.2} / \texttt{…\hspace{0pt}1.1} indicate signature
certificates, \texttt{0.4.0.194112.1.3} / \texttt{…\hspace{0pt}1.4}
indicate seal certificates. As a last resort, check the subject DN --- a
certificate with only an organization name (no personal CN) is likely a
seal, though this heuristic is not definitive.

\section{Use Cases: When to Sign, When to
Seal}\label{_use_cases_when_to_sign_when_to_seal}

\subsection{Contracts and Agreements:
Signature}\label{_contracts_and_agreements_signature}

Any document requiring personal commitment --- contracts, NDAs, medical
prescriptions, regulatory filings, court submissions --- needs a
\textbf{signature}. The signer is the specific human being who agrees.
Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) Article 27 requires member states
that mandate handwritten signatures for public services to accept QES.

\subsection{Invoices and Automated Documents:
Seal}\label{_invoices_and_automated_documents_seal}

Documents needing organizational authentication without personal
approval --- invoices, bank statements, payslips, automated
reports --- are candidates for \textbf{seals}. EU VAT Directive
2006/112/EC (Article 233) recognizes electronic seals as meeting invoice
authenticity and integrity requirements.

\subsection{Hybrid Workflows}\label{_hybrid_workflows}

Many real-world workflows combine both:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  An organization \textbf{seals} a document template (e.g., a bank seals
  a loan agreement)
\item
  A natural person \textbf{signs} to express personal agreement (e.g.,
  the customer signs the loan)
\item
  The organization's representative \textbf{signs} to accept (a natural
  person acting as agent)
\end{enumerate}

The seal and signatures coexist as separate signature dictionaries in
the same PDF, each with its own byte range and CMS structure. Validators
should display both, clearly distinguishing "Sealed by Acme Corp" from
"Signed by Alice Smith."

\section{Server-Side Seal Creation and Batch
Sealing}\label{_server_side_seal_creation_and_batch_sealing}

Because seals represent organizations rather than individuals, they are
ideal for \textbf{automated, server-side creation}. No human needs to be
present at the moment of sealing.

\subsection{The Architecture}\label{_the_architecture}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  The organization holds a \textbf{qualified seal certificate} with its
  private key on an \textbf{HSM} (certified as a Qualified Seal Creation
  Device)
\item
  An application server generates documents (invoices, reports,
  certificates)
\item
  The server calls the HSM to sign each document --- no human
  interaction per document
\item
  The HSM authenticates the application server (mutual TLS, API key),
  not a person
\end{enumerate}

This is the critical difference from signatures: for QES, the signatory
must authenticate \emph{per signing operation} (sole control). For
seals, the organization's security policies ensure only authorized
systems can trigger sealing. This makes batch processing feasible.

At high volumes, watch for two bottlenecks:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{HSM throughput}: a single signing operation takes 1-10ms
  (RSA-2048 is slower than ECDSA P-256). At 50,000 documents per minute,
  you may need multiple HSM partitions or load balancing.
\item
  \textbf{TSA throughput}: if adding qualified timestamps (PAdES-B-T),
  the TSA becomes the constraint. Most commercial TSAs handle hundreds
  of requests per second, but verify against your volume requirements.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Cost Implications}\label{_cost_implications}

\textbf{Signature certificates} are per-person, per-year: 50-300 EUR per
natural person. For 500 employees, the annual cost is substantial.

\textbf{Seal certificates} are per-organization, per-year. A single seal
certificate can produce millions of seals at a fixed cost. The
per-document cost approaches zero at scale.

This asymmetry drives the architectural decision: seals for volume,
signatures for commitment.

\section{Common Pitfalls}\label{_common_pitfalls}

\subsection{Displaying Seals as
Signatures}\label{_displaying_seals_as_signatures}

If your validation UI says "Signed by Acme Corp" when it encounters a
seal, you are misleading the user. The correct display is "Sealed by" or
"Authenticated by" --- language that communicates origin without
implying personal intent.

\subsection{Using Signature Certificates for
Automation}\label{_using_signature_certificates_for_automation}

If your batch pipeline signs 10,000 documents per hour with Alice's
personal qualified certificate, you have a problem. Alice is not
exercising sole control over each operation, and may not even be aware
of most of them. Use a seal certificate for automation and reserve
signature certificates for workflows requiring individual human
approval.

\subsection{Ignoring the Certificate Type in
Code}\label{_ignoring_the_certificate_type_in_code}

Because the signing code is identical, it is easy to accidentally use a
seal certificate for a signature workflow or vice versa. Your
application must validate the certificate \emph{before} signing: check
QcType, reject mismatches, and never rely on the user to "use the right
certificate."

Some older qualified certificates lack a QcType statement entirely. For
these, you must fall back to the EUTL service entry's qualifiers or the
certificate policy OID. This is yet another reason to use the EU DSS
library (Chapter 23), which handles these edge cases.

\section{Key Takeaways}\label{_key_takeaways_9}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Signatures} are for natural persons and express
  \textbf{intent} --- Article 25(2) gives QES the legal equivalence of a
  handwritten signature
\item
  \textbf{Seals} are for legal persons and prove \textbf{origin and
  integrity} --- Article 35(2) gives qualified seals a presumption of
  integrity and correct origin, not equivalence to a signature
\item
  The cryptographic mechanism is \textbf{identical} --- same CMS, same
  PAdES, same QSCD; the only difference is the certificate (QcType esign
  vs eseal, certificate policy QCP-n vs QCP-l)
\item
  Use \textbf{signatures} for contracts, agreements,
  approvals --- anything requiring personal human commitment
\item
  Use \textbf{seals} for invoices, automated reports, official
  publications --- organizational authentication without personal
  approval
\item
  \textbf{Server-side batch sealing} is the dominant seal use case:
  HSM-backed, no per-operation human auth, millions of documents at
  fixed cost
\item
  \textbf{Enforce the certificate type in code} --- do not allow seal
  certs for signature workflows or vice versa
\item
  Display seals correctly: "Sealed by" not "Signed by" --- the legal
  distinction matters
\item
  Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0) focuses on personal QES via the
  EUDI wallet; seals continue on HSM infrastructure, with rising volume
  from QEAAs
\end{itemize}

\chapter{Remote Signing}\label{_remote_signing}

For the first two decades of digital signatures, the assumption was
simple: the private key lives with the signer. A smart card in a wallet,
a PKCS\#12 file on a laptop, a USB token plugged into a desktop. The
signer has physical custody of their key material, they authenticate
locally (enter a PIN, type a password), and the signing happens on their
device.

That model is dead --- or at least, it is dying.

In the remote signing model, the private key never leaves a server-side
Hardware Security Module (HSM). The signer authenticates remotely ---
via OAuth 2.0, via SMS OTP, via biometric verification on their phone
--- and the server performs the cryptographic signing operation on their
behalf. The signer never touches the key, never downloads it, never even
knows the key's technical parameters. They authorize a signing
operation, and the server produces the signature.

This chapter covers the architecture of remote signing: why it exists,
how the CSC API standardizes it, how HSMs and Signature Activation
Modules work together, what the SCAL levels mean for sole control, and
how to integrate with remote signing services in practice. If your
application needs to produce qualified electronic signatures (QES) at
scale --- for thousands of users who do not have smart cards and do not
want USB tokens --- remote signing is how you get there.

\section{Why Remote Signing Won}\label{_why_remote_signing_won}

\subsection{The Smart Card Problem}\label{_the_smart_card_problem}

The traditional QES model required every signer to have:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  A qualified certificate issued by a QTSP
\item
  A Qualified Signature Creation Device (QSCD) --- a certified smart
  card or USB token
\item
  A card reader connected to their computer
\item
  Driver software for the card reader and the QSCD
\item
  Middleware that bridges the QSCD to the signing application (typically
  PKCS\#11 or Microsoft CNG)
\end{enumerate}

This worked in tightly controlled enterprise environments --- banks,
government agencies, regulated industries where IT departments could
provision hardware to every employee. It failed completely for consumer
scenarios, mobile scenarios, and any context where "plug in a USB device
and install a driver" is not feasible.

The practical failure modes were numerous:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Driver hell}: Smart card drivers are notoriously
  platform-dependent. A card that works on Windows 10 may not work on
  Windows 11 without a driver update. macOS support is inconsistent.
  Linux support depends on the specific combination of card, reader, and
  PKCS\#11 module.
\item
  \textbf{Mobile impossibility}: Smartphones have no USB-A ports, no
  smart card readers, and limited support for external cryptographic
  hardware. The NFC-based smart card reading that some phones support is
  slow and unreliable.
\item
  \textbf{Certificate lifecycle}: Qualified certificates typically
  expire after 1-3 years. Renewal requires physically visiting a
  Registration Authority (for identity verification) and provisioning a
  new certificate onto the QSCD. Users forget, certificates expire,
  signing fails.
\item
  \textbf{Cost}: A qualified certificate costs 50-300 EUR per year. A
  smart card costs 20-50 EUR. A card reader costs 15-40 EUR. For an
  organization with 10,000 employees, the hardware cost alone is
  significant --- before you account for help desk tickets from users
  who cannot get their cards to work.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{The Remote Alternative}\label{_the_remote_alternative}

Remote signing eliminates every one of these problems:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{No hardware}: The signer needs only a web browser or a mobile
  app. No drivers, no readers, no tokens.
\item
  \textbf{Mobile-native}: Authentication happens through the phone
  itself --- push notifications, biometric confirmation, SMS OTP. The
  signing experience on a smartphone is identical to the desktop
  experience.
\item
  \textbf{Managed lifecycle}: The QTSP manages the key lifecycle
  entirely. Certificate issuance, renewal, and revocation happen
  server-side. The signer never handles key material.
\item
  \textbf{Lower per-user cost}: While the QTSP charges per signature or
  per subscription, the elimination of hardware provisioning and support
  often makes remote signing cheaper at scale.
\end{itemize}

Remote signing does not lower the \emph{security} bar --- it moves the
security boundary. Instead of trusting the signer's device to protect
the key (which, for a password-protected PKCS\#12 on a laptop, is a
dubious proposition), you trust the QTSP's HSM infrastructure and
authentication system. For most threat models, a professionally managed
HSM with multi-factor authentication provides stronger key protection
than a smart card with a four-digit PIN that the user wrote on a sticky
note.

\subsection{eIDAS and Remote QSCDs}\label{_eidas_and_remote_qscds}

The Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) does not require physical
possession of a QSCD --- it requires sole control (Article 26(c)) and
that the device meet the requirements of Annex II. An HSM operated by a
QTSP, where the signer's key partition is accessible only through strong
multi-factor authentication tied to the specific signing operation,
satisfies both requirements.

The European Committee for Standardization (CEN) formalized this in
\textbf{EN 419 241}, which defines requirements for trustworthy systems
supporting server signing:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{EN 419 241-1}: General requirements for server signing
\item
  \textbf{EN 419 241-2}: Protection profile for QSCDs for server signing
\end{itemize}

These standards, combined with the SCAL framework (discussed below),
provide the certification path for remote QSCDs. By 2024, remote signing
had become the dominant model for QES in practice --- more qualified
signatures are produced via remote HSMs than via local smart cards.

\section{The Hash-Signing Model}\label{_the_hash_signing_model}

\subsection{Why Send the Hash, Not the
Document}\label{_why_send_the_hash_not_the_document}

Remote signing uses a \textbf{hash-signing model}: the client computes
the hash of the data to be signed and sends only the hash to the server.
The server applies the private key to the hash (RSA-PSS, ECDSA, etc.)
and returns the raw signature value. The client then embeds the
signature value into the CMS structure and the PDF.

This design has three critical advantages:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Privacy}: The document content never leaves the client. The
  server sees only a 32-byte (SHA-256) or 64-byte (SHA-512) hash. It
  cannot reconstruct the document from the hash. For sensitive documents
  --- medical records, legal contracts, financial statements --- this is
  essential.
\item
  \textbf{Bandwidth}: Sending a 32-byte hash is dramatically faster than
  sending a 50 MB PDF over a network connection. For mobile clients on
  cellular networks, the difference matters.
\item
  \textbf{Separation of concerns}: The client handles PDF parsing, byte
  range computation, CMS structure building, and timestamp embedding.
  The server handles key management, access control, and the raw
  cryptographic operation. Neither needs to understand the other's
  domain.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{The Flow Step by Step}\label{_the_flow_step_by_step}

\begin{figure}
\centering
\pandocbounded{\includegraphics[keepaspectratio,alt={seq remote signing}]{diagrams/pdf/seq-remote-signing.pdf}}
\caption{Remote signing flow --- client and server responsibilities}
\end{figure}

Steps 1-3 and 11-14 happen entirely on the client. Only steps 4, 6, and
the server-side steps 5-10 involve the remote server. The document never
crosses the network.

The "hash" sent to the server in step 6 is not the hash of the document
bytes. It is the hash of the \textbf{DER-encoded signed attributes} of
the CMS SignerInfo. The signed attributes include the
\texttt{messageDigest} attribute, which itself contains the hash of the
document bytes. This two-level hashing is standard CMS behavior (defined
in RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) Section 5.4) and is
easy to get wrong.

If you hash the document bytes and send that to the remote server, the
resulting signature will not verify --- because CMS validators expect
the signature to be over the signed attributes, not the raw data.

\section{The CSC API (Cloud Signature
Consortium)}\label{_the_csc_api_cloud_signature_consortium}

\subsection{What the CSC Is}\label{_what_the_csc_is}

The \textbf{Cloud Signature Consortium (CSC)} is an industry body that
developed an open standard API for remote signing. Before the CSC API,
every remote signing provider had its own proprietary API --- different
authentication flows, different request/response formats, different
error codes. Integrating with Swisscom AIS required completely different
code than integrating with DigiCert, which required completely different
code than integrating with InfoCert.

The CSC API v2.0 (the current version, published in 2019 and updated
since) standardizes:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Authentication}: OAuth 2.0 with specific grant types
\item
  \textbf{Credential discovery}: listing available signing credentials
  for a user
\item
  \textbf{Signature activation}: the mechanism by which the signer
  authorizes a specific signing operation
\item
  \textbf{Hash signing}: the actual cryptographic operation
\end{itemize}

The API is RESTful, uses JSON for request and response bodies, and is
designed to be consumed by web and mobile clients.

\subsection{API Endpoints}\label{_api_endpoints}

The CSC API defines the following core endpoints:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}@{}}
\caption{CSC API v2.0 Core Endpoints}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Endpoint
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Method
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Purpose
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Endpoint
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Method
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Purpose
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{/auth/login} & POST & Authenticate the user with the remote
signing service. Returns an OAuth 2.0 access token. \\
\texttt{/auth/revoke} & POST & Revoke an active access token. \\
\texttt{/credentials/list} & POST & List the signing credentials
available to the authenticated user. Returns credential IDs, certificate
chains, key algorithms, and SCAL levels. \\
\texttt{/credentials/info} & POST & Get detailed information about a
specific credential, including the certificate chain and supported
algorithms. \\
\texttt{/credentials/authorize} & POST & Authorize a specific credential
for signing. For SCAL2 credentials, this triggers explicit user
authentication (OTP, biometric) and returns a Signature Activation Data
(SAD) token. \\
\texttt{/credentials/sendOTP} & POST & Send a one-time password to the
user (SMS, email) as part of SCAL2 authorization. \\
\texttt{/signatures/signHash} & POST & Sign one or more hashes using a
previously authorized credential. Returns the raw signature values. \\
\texttt{/signatures/timestamp} & POST & Request an RFC 3161 timestamp.
Some providers expose timestamping through the CSC API as well. \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{Authentication: OAuth 2.0}\label{_authentication_oauth_2_0}

The CSC API uses OAuth 2.0 for authentication. The specific grant type
depends on the deployment scenario:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Authorization Code Grant}: For web applications where the user
  authenticates in a browser. The signing application redirects the user
  to the QTSP's authorization server, the user logs in, and the
  application receives an authorization code that it exchanges for an
  access token.
\item
  \textbf{Client Credentials Grant}: For server-to-server scenarios
  where a system (not a human) needs to sign. The application
  authenticates with its own client ID and secret.
\item
  \textbf{Resource Owner Password Grant}: For scenarios where the
  application collects the user's credentials directly. Discouraged by
  OAuth 2.0 best practices but still supported by some providers.
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Authenticates with a CSC}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{compliant remote signing service}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Returns an OAuth }\CommentTok{2.0}\NormalTok{ access token for subsequent API calls}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \BuiltInTok{String} \FunctionTok{authenticate}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ cscBaseUrl}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ username}\OperatorTok{,}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ password}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{IOException} \OperatorTok{\{}

    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ loginUrl }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cscBaseUrl }\OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"/auth/login"}\OperatorTok{;}
    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ requestBody }\OperatorTok{=} \StringTok{"""}
\StringTok{            \{}
\StringTok{                "remoteUser": "\%s",}
\StringTok{                "remoteSecret": "\%s"}
\StringTok{            \}}
\StringTok{            """}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{formatted}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{username}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ password}\OperatorTok{);}

    \BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\NormalTok{ conn }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\OperatorTok{)}
            \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{URL}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{loginUrl}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{openConnection}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDoOutput}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestMethod}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"POST"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestProperty}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Content{-}Type"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"application/json"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setConnectTimeout}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{10000}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setReadTimeout}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{30000}\OperatorTok{);}

    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{OutputStream}\NormalTok{ out }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getOutputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{requestBody}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getBytes}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{UTF\_8}\OperatorTok{));}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getResponseCode}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{!=} \DecValTok{200}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \ControlFlowTok{throw} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{IOException}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"CSC auth/login failed: HTTP "} \OperatorTok{+}
\NormalTok{                conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getResponseCode}\OperatorTok{());}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{// Parse JSON response — extract access\_token}
    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ responseJson}\OperatorTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{InputStream}\NormalTok{ in }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        responseJson }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{in}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{readAllBytes}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{UTF\_8}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{// In production, use a proper JSON parser (Jackson, Gson)}
    \CommentTok{// This is simplified for illustration}
\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"CSC authentication successful for user: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ username}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{return} \FunctionTok{extractJsonField}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{responseJson}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"access\_token"}\OperatorTok{);}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Credential Discovery}\label{_credential_discovery}

Once authenticated, the client can discover what signing credentials are
available:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Lists the signing credentials available to the authenticated user}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Returns credential IDs that can be used for signing}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{CscCredential}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{listCredentials}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ cscBaseUrl}\OperatorTok{,}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ accessToken}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{IOException} \OperatorTok{\{}

    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ url }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cscBaseUrl }\OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"/credentials/list"}\OperatorTok{;}
    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ requestBody }\OperatorTok{=} \StringTok{"""}
\StringTok{            \{}
\StringTok{                "maxResults": 100}
\StringTok{            \}}
\StringTok{            """}\OperatorTok{;}

    \BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\NormalTok{ conn }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\OperatorTok{)}
            \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{URL}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{url}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{openConnection}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDoOutput}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestMethod}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"POST"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestProperty}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Content{-}Type"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"application/json"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestProperty}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Authorization"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"Bearer "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ accessToken}\OperatorTok{);}

    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{OutputStream}\NormalTok{ out }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getOutputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{requestBody}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getBytes}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{UTF\_8}\OperatorTok{));}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ responseJson}\OperatorTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{InputStream}\NormalTok{ in }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        responseJson }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{in}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{readAllBytes}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{UTF\_8}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{// Response contains:}
    \CommentTok{// \{}
    \CommentTok{//   "credentialIDs": ["cred{-}001", "cred{-}002"],}
    \CommentTok{//   "credentialInfos": [}
    \CommentTok{//     \{}
    \CommentTok{//       "credentialID": "cred{-}001",}
    \CommentTok{//       "key": \{ "algo": ["1.2.840.113549.1.1.11"], "len": 2048 \},}
    \CommentTok{//       "cert": \{ "certificates": ["MIIC...", "MIID..."] \},}
    \CommentTok{//       "SCAL": "2",}
    \CommentTok{//       "description": "Qualified certificate for John Doe"}
    \CommentTok{//     \}}
    \CommentTok{//   ]}
    \CommentTok{// \}}

\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"CSC credentials/list returned credentials for user"}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{return} \FunctionTok{parseCscCredentials}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{responseJson}\OperatorTok{);}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The credential response includes the full certificate chain
(Base64-encoded), which the client needs to build the CMS structure
\emph{before} requesting the hash signature. This is a critical detail:
the client must know the signer's certificate at CMS construction time
(for the ESS-signing-certificate-v2 attribute), not just at signing
time.

\subsection{Credential Authorization and
SAD}\label{_credential_authorization_and_sad}

For SCAL2 credentials (see below), the client must explicitly authorize
the credential before signing. This triggers an additional
authentication step --- the QTSP sends an OTP to the signer, or the
signer confirms via a mobile app, or biometric verification occurs.

The result of successful authorization is a \textbf{Signature Activation
Data (SAD)} token --- an opaque string that the client presents with the
signing request to prove that the signer authorized this specific
operation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Authorizes a credential for signing }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{SCAL2}\CommentTok{).}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Triggers user authentication }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{OTP}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ biometric}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ etc}\CommentTok{.).}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Returns a SAD token valid for a limited time and number of signatures}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \BuiltInTok{String} \FunctionTok{authorizeCredential}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ cscBaseUrl}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ accessToken}\OperatorTok{,}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ credentialId}\OperatorTok{,} \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ numSignatures}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{IOException} \OperatorTok{\{}

    \CommentTok{// Step 1: Trigger OTP delivery}
    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ sendOtpUrl }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cscBaseUrl }\OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"/credentials/sendOTP"}\OperatorTok{;}
    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ otpRequestBody }\OperatorTok{=} \StringTok{"""}
\StringTok{            \{}
\StringTok{                "credentialID": "\%s"}
\StringTok{            \}}
\StringTok{            """}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{formatted}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{credentialId}\OperatorTok{);}

    \BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\NormalTok{ otpConn }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\OperatorTok{)}
            \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{URL}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sendOtpUrl}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{openConnection}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    otpConn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDoOutput}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    otpConn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestMethod}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"POST"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    otpConn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestProperty}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Content{-}Type"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"application/json"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    otpConn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestProperty}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Authorization"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"Bearer "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ accessToken}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{OutputStream}\NormalTok{ out }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ otpConn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getOutputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{otpRequestBody}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getBytes}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{UTF\_8}\OperatorTok{));}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"OTP sent for credential: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ credentialId}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// Step 2: Collect OTP from user (your UI must handle this)}
    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ otp }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{collectOtpFromUser}\OperatorTok{();}

    \CommentTok{// Step 3: Authorize the credential with the OTP}
    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ authUrl }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cscBaseUrl }\OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"/credentials/authorize"}\OperatorTok{;}
    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ authRequestBody }\OperatorTok{=} \StringTok{"""}
\StringTok{            \{}
\StringTok{                "credentialID": "\%s",}
\StringTok{                "numSignatures": \%d,}
\StringTok{                "OTP": "\%s"}
\StringTok{            \}}
\StringTok{            """}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{formatted}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{credentialId}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ numSignatures}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ otp}\OperatorTok{);}

    \BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\NormalTok{ authConn }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\OperatorTok{)}
            \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{URL}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{authUrl}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{openConnection}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    authConn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDoOutput}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    authConn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestMethod}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"POST"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    authConn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestProperty}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Content{-}Type"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"application/json"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    authConn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestProperty}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Authorization"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"Bearer "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ accessToken}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{OutputStream}\NormalTok{ out }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ authConn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getOutputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{authRequestBody}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getBytes}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{UTF\_8}\OperatorTok{));}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ responseJson}\OperatorTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{InputStream}\NormalTok{ in }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ authConn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        responseJson }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{in}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{readAllBytes}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{UTF\_8}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{// Response: \{ "SAD": "eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIs..." \}}
    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ sad }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{extractJsonField}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{responseJson}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"SAD"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Credential authorized, SAD obtained for \{\} signatures"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            numSignatures}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ sad}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The SAD token has a limited lifetime (typically 5-10 minutes) and a
limited number of uses (specified in \texttt{numSignatures}). If you
request authorization for 1 signature, the SAD can only be used once. If
you need to sign multiple documents in a batch, request a higher
\texttt{numSignatures} value --- but be aware that some QTSPs limit the
maximum.

If the SAD expires before you use it, you must re-authorize. Design your
signing flow so that SAD acquisition happens immediately before hash
signing, not minutes before.

\subsection{Hash Signing}\label{_hash_signing}

The core operation --- sending a hash and receiving a signature:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Signs one or more hashes using a CSC}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{compliant remote signing service}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Returns raw signature values }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{one per hash}\CommentTok{).}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{signHashes}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ cscBaseUrl}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ accessToken}\OperatorTok{,}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ credentialId}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ sad}\OperatorTok{,}
        \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ hashes}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{IOException} \OperatorTok{\{}

    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ signUrl }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cscBaseUrl }\OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"/signatures/signHash"}\OperatorTok{;}

    \CommentTok{// Convert hashes to Base64 for JSON transport}
    \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ hashesBase64 }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}();}
    \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ hash }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ hashes}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        hashesBase64}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Base64}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getEncoder}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{encodeToString}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{hash}\OperatorTok{));}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{// Build JSON array of hashes}
    \BuiltInTok{StringBuilder}\NormalTok{ hashArray }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{StringBuilder}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"["}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ i }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{;}\NormalTok{ i }\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{ hashesBase64}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{();}\NormalTok{ i}\OperatorTok{++)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{i }\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ hashArray}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{","}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        hashArray}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}"}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{hashesBase64}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{i}\OperatorTok{)).}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}"}\StringTok{"}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\NormalTok{    hashArray}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{append}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"]"}\OperatorTok{);}

    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ requestBody }\OperatorTok{=} \StringTok{"""}
\StringTok{            \{}
\StringTok{                "credentialID": "\%s",}
\StringTok{                "SAD": "\%s",}
\StringTok{                "hashes": \%s,}
\StringTok{                "hashAlgo": "2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.1",}
\StringTok{                "signAlgo": "1.2.840.113549.1.1.11"}
\StringTok{            \}}
\StringTok{            """}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{formatted}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{credentialId}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sad}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ hashArray}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toString}\OperatorTok{());}

    \BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\NormalTok{ conn }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{HttpURLConnection}\OperatorTok{)}
            \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{URL}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signUrl}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{openConnection}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDoOutput}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestMethod}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"POST"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestProperty}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Content{-}Type"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"application/json"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRequestProperty}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Authorization"}\OperatorTok{,} \StringTok{"Bearer "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ accessToken}\OperatorTok{);}

    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{OutputStream}\NormalTok{ out }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getOutputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{requestBody}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getBytes}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{UTF\_8}\OperatorTok{));}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ responseJson}\OperatorTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{InputStream}\NormalTok{ in }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ conn}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInputStream}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        responseJson }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{in}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{readAllBytes}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ StandardCharsets}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{UTF\_8}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{// Response: \{ "signatures": ["MEUCIQD...", "MEQCIBD..."] \}}
    \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ signatures }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{parseSignatureValues}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{responseJson}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"CSC signHash returned \{\} signature values"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signatures}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{());}
    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ signatures}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The OIDs in the request body specify the algorithms:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\caption{Common algorithm OIDs for CSC signHash}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Algorithm
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Notes
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
OID
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Algorithm
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Notes
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
