\endlastfoot
\texttt{2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.1} & SHA-256 & Hash algorithm. The hash you
send must use this algorithm. \\
\texttt{2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.2} & SHA-384 & Stronger hash
alternative. \\
\texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.11} & SHA256withRSA (PKCS\#1 v1.5) & RSA
signature with SHA-256. The server applies PKCS\#1 v1.5 padding. \\
\texttt{1.2.840.113549.1.1.10} & RSASSA-PSS & RSA-PSS signature.
Preferred over PKCS\#1 v1.5 for new deployments. \\
\texttt{1.2.840.10045.4.3.2} & SHA256withECDSA & ECDSA signature with
SHA-256. Shorter signatures, equivalent security. \\
\end{longtable}

\section{SCAL: Sole Control Assurance
Levels}\label{_scal_sole_control_assurance_levels}

\subsection{The Problem SCAL Solves}\label{_the_problem_scal_solves}

The Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) Article 26(c) requires that an
advanced electronic signature be "created using electronic signature
creation data that the signatory can, with a high level of confidence,
use under his sole control."

For local signing with a smart card, sole control is straightforward:
the signer physically possesses the card and knows the PIN. For remote
signing, sole control is more nuanced. The key lives in a server-side
HSM. Multiple users\textquotesingle{} keys may reside in the same
physical HSM. The QTSP's system administrators have physical access to
the HSM. How do you prove that the signer --- and only the signer ---
authorized a specific signing operation?

The \textbf{SCAL} (Sole Control Assurance Level) framework, defined in
CEN EN 419 241-1, provides two levels of assurance:

\subsection{SCAL1: Server-Side Sole
Control}\label{_scal1_server_side_sole_control}

In SCAL1, the remote signing server ensures sole control through its own
authentication and authorization mechanisms, but there is no explicit
per-signature authorization from the user beyond the initial session
authentication.

Characteristics: - The signer authenticates to the service (username +
password, possibly with MFA) - Once authenticated, the service can sign
on the signer's behalf for the duration of the session - The service's
access control mechanisms prevent unauthorized use - There is no
additional user interaction for each signing operation

SCAL1 is suitable for: - \textbf{Advanced electronic signatures (AdES)}:
The sole control requirement of Article 26(c) can be met if the
authentication is sufficiently strong - \textbf{Automated signing
workflows}: Where a human authorizes a batch of signatures upfront -
\textbf{Internal enterprise signing}: Where the trust model accepts
session-level authorization

SCAL1 is \emph{not} suitable for QES, because the sole control assurance
is not strong enough --- the server could theoretically sign without the
user's knowledge during an active session.

\subsection{SCAL2: Explicit Per-Signature
Authorization}\label{_scal2_explicit_per_signature_authorization}

In SCAL2, every signing operation requires explicit authorization from
the signer, independent of session authentication. The authorization is
linked to the specific data being signed.

Characteristics: - The signer authenticates to the service
(session-level) - For each signing operation, the signer must provide
additional authorization: OTP, biometric confirmation, mobile app push
approval - The authorization produces a SAD (Signature Activation Data)
token that is bound to the specific credential and limited in scope
(time, number of signatures) - The HSM will not perform the signing
operation without a valid SAD

SCAL2 is required for: - \textbf{Qualified electronic signatures (QES)}:
The per-signature authorization satisfies Article 26(c) with a high
level of confidence - \textbf{Any scenario where you need to prove that
the signer actively authorized each specific signature}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\caption{SCAL1 vs. SCAL2 Comparison}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Aspect
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
SCAL1
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
SCAL2
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Aspect
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
SCAL1
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
SCAL2
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Authentication & Session-level (login once) & Session-level +
per-signature authorization \\
User interaction per signature & None after login & Required (OTP,
biometric, push) \\
SAD token & Not required & Required for each signing operation \\
Suitable for QES & No & Yes \\
Suitable for AdES & Yes & Yes \\
Batch signing & Easy (sign N documents in a loop) & Possible but
requires batch SAD (N signatures authorized at once) \\
eIDAS Article 26(c) assurance & Moderate & High \\
\end{longtable}

The distinction between SCAL1 and SCAL2 is not merely theoretical --- it
determines whether the resulting signature can be a QES. If your
application needs to produce qualified electronic signatures via remote
signing, you \emph{must} implement a SCAL2 flow. A SCAL1 flow, no matter
how strong the session authentication, will not produce a QES because it
lacks per-signature authorization.

This is the single most common mistake in remote signing integrations:
assuming that a strong login (username + password + TOTP) is sufficient
for QES. It is not. The authentication must be linked to the signing
operation, not just the session.

\section{HSM and SAM Architecture}\label{_hsm_and_sam_architecture}

\subsection{The HSM (Hardware Security
Module)}\label{_the_hsm_hardware_security_module}

The HSM is the physical device that stores private keys and performs
cryptographic operations. In a remote signing deployment, the HSM is
typically:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{FIPS 140-2 Level 3} or \textbf{Level 4} certified (US
  standard)
\item
  \textbf{Common Criteria EAL4+} certified (international standard)
\item
  Deployed in a physically secured data center with access logging,
  video surveillance, and multi-person access controls
\item
  Connected to the signing server via a high-speed internal network
  (never directly exposed to the internet)
\end{itemize}

Popular HSMs for remote signing: - \textbf{Thales Luna Network HSM}:
Widely used in European QTSPs. Supports PKCS\#11, JCE (Java Cryptography
Extension), and Microsoft CNG. Available in various throughput tiers. -
\textbf{Utimaco SecurityServer}: German manufacturer, popular with
European banks and government agencies. Strong PKCS\#11 support. -
\textbf{Entrust nShield Connect}: Used by large enterprises and some
QTSPs. Offers a proprietary "Security World" key management architecture
alongside standard PKCS\#11. - \textbf{AWS CloudHSM / Azure Dedicated
HSM}: Cloud-hosted HSMs. Convenient for cloud-native architectures, but
may not meet all QSCD certification requirements (check the specific
certification status).

\subsection{The SAM (Signature Activation
Module)}\label{_the_sam_signature_activation_module}

The SAM sits between the remote signing API and the HSM. Its job is to
enforce sole control:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Receive the signing request} with the signer's credentials and
  SAD
\item
  \textbf{Verify the SAD} --- check that it is valid, not expired, bound
  to the correct credential, and that the number of allowed signatures
  has not been exhausted
\item
  \textbf{Authorize the HSM operation} --- unlock the signer's key
  partition in the HSM for exactly one (or N) signing operations
\item
  \textbf{Return the signature value} to the API layer
\end{enumerate}

The SAM is the component that makes SCAL2 possible. Without a SAM, the
signing server could theoretically use any key in the HSM at any time.
The SAM ensures that keys are only activated when the legitimate signer
has provided explicit, verifiable authorization.

\begin{verbatim}
Architecture of a Remote Signing Service

                         ┌─────────────────────┐
    Internet             │   Load Balancer /    │
    ─────────────────────│   API Gateway        │
                         └──────────┬───────────┘
                                    │
                         ┌──────────┴───────────┐
                         │   CSC API Server      │
                         │   (REST endpoints)    │
                         │   OAuth 2.0 token     │
                         │   validation          │
                         └──────────┬───────────┘
                                    │
                         ┌──────────┴───────────┐
                         │   Signature           │
                         │   Activation Module   │
                         │   (SAM)               │
                         │   • SAD validation    │
                         │   • SCAL enforcement  │
                         │   • Audit logging     │
                         └──────────┬───────────┘
                                    │ PKCS#11
                         ┌──────────┴───────────┐
                         │   Hardware Security   │
                         │   Module (HSM)        │
                         │   • Key storage       │
                         │   • Crypto operations │
                         │   • Tamper-evident    │
                         └──────────────────────┘
\end{verbatim}

\subsection{PKCS\#11: The HSM
Interface}\label{_pkcs11_the_hsm_interface}

PKCS\#11 (also called Cryptoki) is the standard API for communicating
with HSMs and smart cards. It is a C-based API with Java bindings
available through the JCE \texttt{SunPKCS11} provider or through
dedicated libraries.

In a remote signing server, the PKCS\#11 flow for signing looks like
this:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Signs a hash using an HSM via PKCS}\CommentTok{\#11.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ This runs on the remote signing server}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ NOT on the client}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{signWithHsm}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ hash}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ keyAlias}\OperatorTok{,}
        \DataTypeTok{char}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ keyPin}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

    \CommentTok{// 1. Load the PKCS\#11 provider}
    \CommentTok{//    (configured once at server startup, not per request)}
    \BuiltInTok{Provider}\NormalTok{ pkcs11Provider }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{Security}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"SunPKCS11"}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// 2. Load the keystore from the HSM}
    \BuiltInTok{KeyStore}\NormalTok{ ks }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{KeyStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"PKCS11"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ pkcs11Provider}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    ks}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{load}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ keyPin}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// 3. Retrieve the private key}
    \BuiltInTok{PrivateKey}\NormalTok{ privateKey }\OperatorTok{=} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{PrivateKey}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ ks}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getKey}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{keyAlias}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ keyPin}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{privateKey }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \ControlFlowTok{throw} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{IllegalStateException}\OperatorTok{(}
                \StringTok{"Key not found in HSM: "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ keyAlias}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \CommentTok{// 4. Sign the hash}
    \CommentTok{//    Note: for RSA, Signature.sign() expects the raw data, not a}
    \CommentTok{//    pre{-}computed hash. Use "NONEwithRSA" to sign a raw hash, or}
    \CommentTok{//    use "SHA256withRSA" and pass the original data.}
    \BuiltInTok{Signature}\NormalTok{ sig }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{Signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"SHA256withRSA"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ pkcs11Provider}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{initSign}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{privateKey}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{update}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{hash}\OperatorTok{);}
    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ signatureValue }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{sign}\OperatorTok{();}

\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"HSM signing completed: key=\{\}, sigLen=\{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            keyAlias}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signatureValue}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ signatureValue}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The JCE \texttt{SunPKCS11} provider requires a configuration that
specifies the PKCS\#11 library path, slot number, and other HSM-specific
parameters. This configuration is typically loaded from a file or a
dynamic \texttt{InputStream}:

\begin{verbatim}
# pkcs11.cfg
name = MyHSM
library = /opt/hsm/lib/libCryptoki2_64.so
slot = 0
\end{verbatim}

Different HSM vendors provide different PKCS\#11 shared libraries
(\texttt{.so} on Linux, \texttt{.dll} on Windows). The library path is
the most common source of "HSM not found" errors in deployments.

\subsection{Key Lifecycle in an HSM}\label{_key_lifecycle_in_an_hsm}

Private keys in an HSM follow a strict lifecycle:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Generation}: The key pair is generated \emph{inside} the HSM.
  The private key never exists outside the HSM's tamper-resistant
  boundary. This is a fundamental requirement for QSCD certification.
\item
  \textbf{Activation}: The key is associated with a certificate (issued
  by the QTSP's CA) and made available for signing. For SCAL2,
  activation for each signing operation requires a valid SAD.
\item
  \textbf{Use}: The HSM performs signing operations using the key. Each
  use is logged for audit purposes.
\item
  \textbf{Suspension}: The key can be temporarily suspended (e.g., if
  the signer reports a compromised authentication factor). The
  certificate is not revoked, but signing is disabled.
\item
  \textbf{Revocation}: The key is permanently disabled, and the
  associated certificate is revoked. The key material may be destroyed
  or archived depending on the QTSP's policy.
\end{enumerate}

For QSCD certification under EN 419 241-2, the HSM must guarantee that
private keys are generated internally and can never be extracted --- not
even by the QTSP's own administrators. This is what "the key never
leaves the HSM" means in practice. The HSM must be designed so that
there is no API, no debug mode, no firmware backdoor that could extract
key material. Physical attacks (opening the HSM casing) must trigger key
destruction.

\section{Vendor Landscape}\label{_vendor_landscape}

\subsection{Major Remote Signing
Providers}\label{_major_remote_signing_providers}

The remote signing market is dominated by a handful of European QTSPs,
each offering CSC-compliant APIs with varying degrees of completeness:

Swisscom AIS & Switzerland & v2.0 & SCAL1, SCAL2 & Integrated with Swiss
law (ZertES). Strong mobile authentication via Mobile ID. High
throughput for batch signing. \\
DigiCert + QuoVadis & US / Switzerland & v2.0 & SCAL1, SCAL2 & Global
presence. QuoVadis (acquired by DigiCert) provides the EU-qualified
certificates. AATL-listed roots. \\
InfoCert & Italy & v2.0 & SCAL1, SCAL2 & Largest QTSP in Europe by
volume. SPID integration for Italian digital identity. Strong in Italian
government sector. \\
Namirial & Italy & v2.0 & SCAL1, SCAL2 & Focus on document workflow
integration. Offers both remote signing and a full document management
platform. \\
ZealID & Lithuania & v2.0 & SCAL2 & Mobile-first approach. Signing via
smartphone app with biometric authentication. Popular in Baltic
states. \\
D-Trust (Bundesdruckerei) & Germany & v2.0 & SCAL1, SCAL2 & German
Federal Printing Office subsidiary. Strong in German government and
healthcare sectors. \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{The Ascertia/ADSS Pattern}\label{_the_ascertiaadss_pattern}

A notable infrastructure pattern in the CSC ecosystem is the widespread
use of \textbf{Ascertia ADSS Server} as the underlying platform. Many
QTSPs --- especially regional and government-affiliated providers --- do
not build their own CSC API implementation. Instead, they deploy
Ascertia's ADSS (Automated Digital Signature Server), which exposes the
CSC API through a consistent base path:

\begin{verbatim}
https://<provider-domain>/adss/service/ras/csc/v1/
\end{verbatim}

This creates a recognizable fingerprint. If you encounter a CSC endpoint
with \texttt{/adss/service/ras/} in the URL, you are almost certainly
talking to an Ascertia deployment, regardless of the provider's
branding. The practical consequence: the CSC behavior, error formats,
and quirks will be identical across all Ascertia-based providers, even
though they are operated by different organizations in different
countries.

Common variations include:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{https://signinghub.ascertia.com/adss/service/ras/csc/v1/}
  (Ascertia's own hosted service)
\item
  \texttt{https://ras.\textless{}provider\textgreater{}.com/adss/service/ras/csc/v1/}
  (white-labeled deployments)
\item
  \texttt{https://sign.\textless{}provider\textgreater{}.com/adss/service/ras/csc/v1/}
  (government integrations)
\end{itemize}

This pattern matters for integration testing: if you have tested against
one Ascertia-based provider, you have effectively tested against all of
them. Conversely, if you encounter an Ascertia-specific bug, expect it
to appear at every provider using the same platform.

\subsection{Sandbox and Test
Endpoints}\label{_sandbox_and_test_endpoints}

Most CSC providers offer sandbox environments for development and
integration testing. These sandboxes are invaluable --- they let you
develop and test the entire signing flow without purchasing production
credentials or risking real certificates.

SSL.com eSigner & \texttt{https://cs-try.ssl.com/csc/v0} & Free sandbox
with demo credentials. Publicly documented test accounts available. Good
starting point for CSC integration. \\
DigiCert DTM &
\texttt{https://demo.one.digicert.com/documentmanager/csc/v1} & Demo
environment for Document Trust Manager. Requires DigiCert partnership
account. \\
ZealiD & \texttt{https://hermes-dev.zealid.com/api/v3.0/csc/v1} &
Development endpoint. Mobile-first --- requires ZealiD test app. \\
itsme & \texttt{https://sign.e2e.itsme.services/csc/v1} & End-to-end
test environment. Belgian Mobile ID integration testing. \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{Start with SSL.com's sandbox.} It is the most accessible CSC
test environment --- no partnership agreements, no special hardware,
publicly documented demo credentials. Build your integration against
their sandbox first, then adapt for your production provider. The CSC
API surface is standardized enough that switching providers later
requires only URL and credential changes, not code changes.

\subsection{National eID and CSC
Integration}\label{_national_eid_and_csc_integration}

Several European countries have integrated their national electronic
identity systems with CSC-compatible remote signing. This means citizens
can use their existing government-issued digital identity to create
qualified electronic signatures without obtaining separate signing
credentials.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}@{}}
\caption{National eID systems with CSC-compatible remote
signing}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Country
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
eID System
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
CSC Integration
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Country
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
eID System
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
CSC Integration
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Italy & SPID (Public Digital Identity System) & InfoCert and Namirial
both offer SPID-authenticated remote signing. Citizens use their
existing SPID identity to access signing credentials hosted by the
QTSP. \\
Norway & BankID & BankID provides remote signing through bank-operated
infrastructure. High adoption rate (\textasciitilde4.5 million active
users). \\
Austria & Handy-Signatur / ID Austria & PrimeSign (Cryptas)
infrastructure. Mobile phone-based signing via the national eID
system. \\
Estonia & eID Easy & Integrates with the Estonian national ID card and
Mobile-ID systems for remote signing. \\
Switzerland & SwissID / Mobile ID & Swisscom Trust Services. Qualified
under Swiss law (ZertES) rather than eIDAS, but cross-recognized. \\
Spain & DNIe / FNMT & FNMT (Fábrica Nacional de Moneda y Timbre)
provides remote signing tied to the national certificate
infrastructure. \\
\end{longtable}

For integrators, the key implication is authentication: when the CSC
provider is backed by a national eID system, the OAuth 2.0 flow
typically redirects through the national identity provider (OpenID
Connect federation). Your application's authentication UI must support
this redirect --- it is not a simple username/password flow.

\subsection{CSC Endpoint Discovery}\label{_csc_endpoint_discovery}

Because the CSC specification requires a public \texttt{/info} endpoint
that responds without authentication, CSC servers can be discovered by
probing for known URL patterns:

\begin{verbatim}
GET /csc/v2/info   → Most CSC v2 providers
GET /csc/v1/info   → Legacy CSC v1 providers
GET /adss/service/ras/csc/v1/info   → Ascertia ADSS deployments
\end{verbatim}

A successful response returns the service capabilities:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{\{}
  \DataTypeTok{"specs"}\FunctionTok{:} \StringTok{"CSC API v2"}\FunctionTok{,}
  \DataTypeTok{"name"}\FunctionTok{:} \StringTok{"Remote Signing Service"}\FunctionTok{,}
  \DataTypeTok{"authType"}\FunctionTok{:} \OtherTok{[}\StringTok{"oauth2"}\OtherTok{]}\FunctionTok{,}
  \DataTypeTok{"methods"}\FunctionTok{:} \OtherTok{[}\StringTok{"credentials/list"}\OtherTok{,} \StringTok{"credentials/info"}\OtherTok{,}
              \StringTok{"credentials/authorize"}\OtherTok{,} \StringTok{"signatures/signHash"}\OtherTok{]}
\FunctionTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This discovery mechanism is useful for integration testing (verifying
that a provider's endpoint is reachable and responsive) and for building
provider registries. The \texttt{/info} endpoint also reveals which CSC
API methods the provider supports --- not all providers implement the
full specification.

\subsection{Choosing a Provider}\label{_choosing_a_provider}

When selecting a remote signing provider, consider:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Trusted list presence}: The provider must be a QTSP listed in
  the EUTL of the member state where they are supervised. Verify this at
  the EU's official Trusted List Browser.
\item
  \textbf{AATL listing}: If your signed PDFs will be opened in Adobe
  Acrobat, the provider's root certificate should be in the Adobe
  Approved Trust List (AATL). Without AATL listing, Acrobat will show a
  warning even for EU-qualified signatures.
\item
  \textbf{Geographic coverage}: Some providers issue certificates
  qualified in one member state; others have qualified status in
  multiple states. For cross-border use, mutual recognition under eIDAS
  Article 25(3) applies, but AATL listing is a separate concern.
\item
  \textbf{API completeness}: Not all providers implement the full CSC
  API. Some support only SCAL1, some have proprietary extensions, some
  are still on CSC v1.x. Request the provider's API documentation and
  test against their sandbox environment.
\item
  \textbf{Authentication options}: SCAL2 requires per-signature
  authorization. Providers offer different mechanisms: SMS OTP, mobile
  app push, biometric (fingerprint/face), hardware token. Choose based
  on your users\textquotesingle{} capabilities.
\item
  \textbf{Pricing model}: Per-signature pricing (typically 0.10-2.00 EUR
  per signature), per-certificate pricing (annual fee per signer), or
  volume-based tiers. Calculate your total cost based on expected
  signing volume.
\item
  \textbf{SLA and availability}: For production signing, you need a
  provider with a published SLA. Remote signing is a synchronous
  operation in most workflows --- if the provider is down, your users
  cannot sign.
\end{enumerate}

Do not assume that all CSC API implementations are interchangeable. The
CSC specification leaves room for provider-specific behavior in areas
like:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  OTP delivery mechanisms (some providers trigger OTP automatically,
  others require an explicit \texttt{/credentials/sendOTP} call)
\item
  SAD token format and lifetime
\item
  Batch signing limits (how many hashes can be signed with a single SAD)
\item
  Error response formats and error codes
\end{itemize}

Always develop and test against the specific provider's sandbox before
going to production. The CSC specification is a standard, but
implementations have edges.

\section{Complete Integration
Example}\label{_complete_integration_example}

The following example shows a complete remote signing flow --- from PDF
preparation through CSC API calls to final PDF assembly:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Signs a PDF using a CSC}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{compliant remote signing service}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Produces a PAdES}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{B}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{T compliant signature}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{signPdfRemotely}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ cscBaseUrl}\OperatorTok{,}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ accessToken}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ credentialId}\OperatorTok{,}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ tsaUrl}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

    \CommentTok{// {-}{-}{-} Phase 1: Prepare the PDF and CMS structure {-}{-}{-}}

    \CommentTok{// 1a. Get the signer\textquotesingle{}s certificate from the CSC service}
\NormalTok{    CscCredentialInfo credInfo }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{getCredentialInfo}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            cscBaseUrl}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ accessToken}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ credentialId}\OperatorTok{);}
    \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ signerCert }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ credInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificateChain}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{);}
    \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ certChain }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ credInfo}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificateChain}\OperatorTok{();}

    \CommentTok{// 1b. Load the PDF and prepare the signature dictionary}
\NormalTok{    PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    PDSignature pdSignature }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    pdSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFilter}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FILTER\_ADOBE\_PPKLITE}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    pdSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSubFilter}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"ETSI.CAdES.detached"}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{    pdSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSignDate}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Calendar}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{());}

    \CommentTok{// 1c. Register our SignatureInterface}
    \CommentTok{// (PDFBox calls this to get the CMS bytes)}
\NormalTok{    RemoteSignatureInterface sigInterface }\OperatorTok{=}
            \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{RemoteSignatureInterface}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    cscBaseUrl}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ accessToken}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ credentialId}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                    signerCert}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ certChain}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ tsaUrl}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{    doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addSignature}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdSignature}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sigInterface}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// 1d. Save incrementally — this triggers sigInterface.sign()}
    \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\NormalTok{ baos }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{saveIncremental}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{baos}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{close}\OperatorTok{();}

\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"PDF signed remotely: resultSize=\{\} bytes"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ baos}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{());}
    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ baos}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toByteArray}\OperatorTok{();}
\OperatorTok{\}}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ PDFBox SignatureInterface implementation for remote signing}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Called by PDFBox during saveIncremental}\CommentTok{()}\NormalTok{ with the byte range content}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ RemoteSignatureInterface}
        \KeywordTok{implements}\NormalTok{ SignatureInterface }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ cscBaseUrl}\OperatorTok{;}
    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ accessToken}\OperatorTok{;}
    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ credentialId}\OperatorTok{;}
    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ signerCert}\OperatorTok{;}
    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ certChain}\OperatorTok{;}
    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{final} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ tsaUrl}\OperatorTok{;}

    \CommentTok{// Constructor omitted for brevity}

    \AttributeTok{@Override}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{sign}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{InputStream}\NormalTok{ content}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{IOException} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \CommentTok{// 2a. Read the byte range content (the data to be signed)}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ contentBytes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ content}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{readAllBytes}\OperatorTok{();}

            \CommentTok{// 2b. Build the CMS signed attributes}
\NormalTok{            CMSSignedDataGenerator gen }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CMSSignedDataGenerator}\OperatorTok{();}

            \CommentTok{// Add certificates to the CMS}
            \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{X509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ certHolders }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}();}
            \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ certChain}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{                certHolders}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaX509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cert}\OperatorTok{));}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
\NormalTok{            gen}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addCertificates}\OperatorTok{(}
                    \KeywordTok{new}\NormalTok{ CollectionStore}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}(}\NormalTok{certHolders}\OperatorTok{));}

            \CommentTok{// 2c. Compute the message digest of the content}
            \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\NormalTok{ md }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"SHA{-}256"}\OperatorTok{);}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ contentHash }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ md}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{digest}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{contentBytes}\OperatorTok{);}

            \CommentTok{// 2d. Build signed attributes manually}
\NormalTok{            ASN1EncodableVector signedAttrs }\OperatorTok{=}
                    \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{ASN1EncodableVector}\OperatorTok{();}

            \CommentTok{// content{-}type attribute}
\NormalTok{            signedAttrs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{Attribute}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    CMSAttributes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{contentType}\OperatorTok{,}
                    \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{DERSet}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{CMSObjectIdentifiers}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{data}\OperatorTok{)));}

            \CommentTok{// message{-}digest attribute}
\NormalTok{            signedAttrs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{Attribute}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    CMSAttributes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{messageDigest}\OperatorTok{,}
                    \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{DERSet}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{DEROctetString}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{contentHash}\OperatorTok{))));}

            \CommentTok{// ESS{-}signing{-}certificate{-}v2 attribute}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ certHash }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"SHA{-}256"}\OperatorTok{)}
                    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{digest}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signerCert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{            ESSCertIDv2 essCertId }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{ESSCertIDv2}\OperatorTok{(}
                    \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{AlgorithmIdentifier}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                            NISTObjectIdentifiers}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{id\_sha256}\OperatorTok{),}
\NormalTok{                    certHash}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{            SigningCertificateV2 sigCertV2 }\OperatorTok{=}
                    \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{SigningCertificateV2}\OperatorTok{(}
                            \KeywordTok{new}\NormalTok{ ESSCertIDv2}\OperatorTok{[]\{}\NormalTok{essCertId}\OperatorTok{\});}
\NormalTok{            signedAttrs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{Attribute}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    PKCSObjectIdentifiers}
                            \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{id\_aa\_signingCertificateV2}\OperatorTok{,}
                    \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{DERSet}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sigCertV2}\OperatorTok{)));}

            \CommentTok{// 2e. DER{-}encode the signed attributes}
\NormalTok{            DERSet signedAttrSet }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{DERSet}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedAttrs}\OperatorTok{);}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ signedAttrBytes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signedAttrSet}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{();}

            \CommentTok{// 2f. Hash the signed attributes — THIS is what we}
            \CommentTok{//     send to the remote signing server}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ signedAttrHash }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{MessageDigest}
                    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"SHA{-}256"}\OperatorTok{)}
                    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{digest}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedAttrBytes}\OperatorTok{);}

            \CommentTok{// {-}{-}{-} Phase 2: Remote signing via CSC API {-}{-}{-}}

            \CommentTok{// 3a. Authorize the credential (SCAL2)}
            \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ sad }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{authorizeCredential}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    cscBaseUrl}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ accessToken}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ credentialId}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{);}

            \CommentTok{// 3b. Sign the hash remotely}
            \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ signatures }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{signHashes}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    cscBaseUrl}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ accessToken}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ credentialId}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sad}\OperatorTok{,}
                    \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{of}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedAttrHash}\OperatorTok{));}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ signatureValue }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signatures}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{);}

            \CommentTok{// {-}{-}{-} Phase 3: Assemble the CMS and add timestamp {-}{-}{-}}

            \CommentTok{// 4a. Build the complete CMS SignedData manually}
            \CommentTok{//     (embedding the remote signature value)}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ cmsBytes }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{assembleCmsSignedData}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    signedAttrSet}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signatureValue}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                    certChain}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signerCert}\OperatorTok{);}

            \CommentTok{// 4b. Request a timestamp (for PAdES{-}B{-}T)}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsaUrl }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{                cmsBytes }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{addSignatureTimestamp}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                        cmsBytes}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signatureValue}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ tsaUrl}\OperatorTok{);}
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ cmsBytes}\OperatorTok{;}

        \OperatorTok{\}} \ControlFlowTok{catch} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Exception}\NormalTok{ e}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \ControlFlowTok{throw} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{IOException}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Remote signing failed"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ e}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The code above is a simplified illustration of the remote signing flow.
A production implementation must also handle:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Error recovery}: What happens if the CSC API call fails after
  SAD authorization? The SAD may be consumed. You may need to
  re-authorize.
\item
  \textbf{Timeout handling}: Network calls to the CSC API and TSA can
  fail. Implement timeouts and retries.
\item
  \textbf{Content size estimation}: PDFBox's \texttt{addSignature}
  requires you to pre-estimate the size of the \texttt{/Contents} value.
  If you underestimate, the CMS will not fit. Set
  \texttt{SignatureOptions.setPreferredSignatureSize()} generously
  (e.g., 16384 bytes).
\item
  \textbf{Thread safety}: If multiple users sign concurrently, your CSC
  API client must handle concurrent requests correctly. Use separate
  HTTP connections per request.
\end{itemize}

For production remote signing with 3.0.0, consider using the EU DSS
framework (Chapter 23), which provides a complete, tested implementation
of the remote signing flow with CSC API support.

\section{Security Considerations}\label{_security_considerations}

\subsection{Transport Security}\label{_transport_security}

All communication with the CSC API must use TLS 1.2 or later. The access
token is a bearer token --- anyone who intercepts it can use it to sign.
In production:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Pin the CSC API server's TLS certificate (or at least its root CA) to
  prevent man-in-the-middle attacks
\item
  Use short-lived access tokens (minutes, not hours)
\item
  Transmit tokens only in the \texttt{Authorization} header, never in
  query parameters or request bodies
\item
  Log all API calls for audit purposes, but never log access tokens or
  SAD values
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Hash Manipulation}\label{_hash_manipulation}

The remote signing server signs whatever hash you send it. It does not
--- and cannot --- verify that the hash corresponds to any particular
document. This means: if an attacker compromises the client application,
they can send arbitrary hashes to the server and obtain valid signatures
over arbitrary data.

Mitigations: - \textbf{SCAL2 authorization}: The signer must actively
authorize each signing operation, limiting the window for automated
abuse - \textbf{Audit logging}: Every signing operation is logged on the
server, creating a forensic trail - \textbf{Rate limiting}: Unusual
signing patterns (hundreds of signatures per minute from a single user)
should trigger alerts - \textbf{Document description in SAD}: Some CSC
implementations allow including a document description in the
authorization request, which the signer can verify before approving

\subsection{Key Compromise}\label{_key_compromise}

If an HSM is compromised, all keys stored in it are compromised. This is
the catastrophic failure mode of remote signing --- a single breach
affects all users.

HSM-level mitigations: - Physical security (data center access controls,
tamper detection) - Multi-person key ceremony for HSM initialization -
Regular firmware updates - Separate HSMs for different security levels
(production vs. test) - Geographic redundancy with key replication

Operational mitigations: - Certificate revocation within minutes of
detection - Automatic alerts for anomalous signing patterns - Regular
penetration testing of the signing infrastructure - Insurance against
breach liability (some QTSPs carry specific cyber insurance)

\section{Key Takeaways}\label{_key_takeaways_10}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Remote signing} moves the private key from the signer's device
  to a server-side HSM operated by a QTSP --- the signer authenticates
  remotely, the server signs
\item
  The \textbf{hash-signing model} preserves document confidentiality:
  only a 32-byte hash crosses the network, never the document content
\item
  The \textbf{CSC API v2.0} standardizes remote signing: OAuth 2.0
  authentication, credential discovery, signature activation, hash
  signing
\item
  \textbf{SCAL1} provides session-level sole control (suitable for
  AdES); \textbf{SCAL2} provides per-signature authorization (required
  for QES)
\item
  The \textbf{SAD token} is the proof that the signer authorized a
  specific signing operation --- it has limited lifetime and use count
\item
  \textbf{HSMs} store keys and perform cryptographic operations;
  \textbf{SAMs} enforce sole control and verify SAD tokens
\item
  PKCS\#11 is the standard interface between the signing server and the
  HSM --- vendor-specific libraries are the most common deployment issue
\item
  Major providers (Swisscom AIS, DigiCert/QuoVadis, InfoCert, Namirial,
  ZealID, D-Trust) all offer CSC-compliant APIs with varying degrees of
  completeness
\item
  \textbf{Always test against the specific provider's sandbox} --- CSC
  API implementations have provider-specific behaviors
\item
  Many providers run \textbf{Ascertia ADSS} as their CSC platform --- if
  you've tested one ADSS deployment, the behavior will be identical
  across all of them
\item
  \textbf{National eID integration} (SPID, BankID, SwissID) routes
  authentication through the national identity provider via OpenID
  Connect --- not a simple username/password flow
\item
  The CSC \texttt{/info} endpoint enables \textbf{endpoint discovery}
  --- it responds without authentication and reveals the provider's
  capabilities and supported methods
\item
  For production PDF signing via remote services, consider the EU DSS
  framework (Chapter 23), which provides tested CSC API integration
\end{itemize}

\chapter{The EU DSS Framework}\label{_the_eu_dss_framework}

Throughout this book, we have been building PDF signing and validation
code from the ground up --- constructing CMS structures by hand with
BouncyCastle, computing byte ranges manually, assembling DSS
dictionaries one stream at a time. This is the right approach for
understanding what is happening, but it is the wrong approach for
production software.

The \textbf{European Digital Signature Services (EU DSS)} framework is
the European Commission's open-source reference implementation for
digital signature creation, extension, and validation. It is maintained
by the European Commission's ISA2 programme (now the Digital Europe
Programme), developed primarily by the Luxembourg company Nowina
Solutions, and used by dozens of EU member states in their national
validation services. It handles PAdES, CAdES, XAdES, and JAdES. It
validates against the EU Trusted List. It implements every PAdES profile
from B-B through B-LTA. It supports remote signing via the CSC API. And
it is free --- released under the LGPL 2.1 license.

If you are building production software that creates or validates
digital signatures in a European context, EU DSS is not just an option
--- it is the benchmark against which your implementation will be
measured. This chapter covers everything you need to use it:
architecture, core modules, the signing flow, validation, Spring Boot
integration, and the honest trade-offs between using EU DSS and rolling
your own.

\section{History and Governance}\label{_history_and_governance}

\subsection{Origins}\label{_origins}

The EU DSS project began in 2011 as part of the European Commission's
\textbf{ISA programme} (Interoperability Solutions for European Public
Administrations). The goal was to provide member states with a reusable
software library that correctly implemented the ETSI signature standards
--- so that each country would not have to write its own implementation
from scratch and risk incompatibilities.

The project was motivated by the same cross-border recognition failures
that led to eIDAS itself (Chapter 19). Even when countries agreed on the
legal framework, their software implementations often produced or
validated signatures differently. A common reference implementation was
the practical answer to "why doesn't my signature validate in your
system?"

\subsection{Development}\label{_development}

The framework has been developed primarily by \textbf{Nowina Solutions}
(formerly Nowina Technologies), a Luxembourg-based company specializing
in electronic signatures and trust services. Nowina won the European
Commission's procurement for DSS development and has been the principal
technical contributor since 2015.

The source code is hosted on the European Commission's \textbf{GitHub
organization} at \texttt{github.com/esig/dss} and on the Commission's
own code repository at
\texttt{ec.europa.eu/digital-building-blocks/code/projects/ESIG}.

\subsection{Version History}\label{_version_history}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.6000}}@{}}
\caption{EU DSS Major Version History}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Version
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Date
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key Changes
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Version
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Date
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Key Changes
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}

\endlastfoot
4.x & 2015-2017 & First widely adopted version. PAdES, CAdES, XAdES
support. Java 7/8. \\
5.0 & 2018 & Major rewrite. Modular architecture (separate Maven
modules). Java 8 minimum. Introduced pluggable token architecture. \\
5.6 & 2020 & JAdES (JSON Advanced Electronic Signatures) support. ETSI
EN 319 102-1 validation policy. Improved EU Trusted List parsing. \\
5.10 & 2022 & Java 11 minimum. CSC API support for remote signing.
Updated BouncyCastle dependency (1.70+). \\
5.13 & 2023 & Improved PKCS\#11 token support. PSD2 (Payment Services
Directive) certificate validation. Updated CRL/OCSP handling. \\
6.0 & 2024 & Java 17 minimum. Module system (JPMS) support. Restructured
Maven coordinates (groupId changed from
\texttt{eu.europa.ec.joinup.sd-dss} to \texttt{eu.europa.dss}). Updated
1.80 dependency. \\
6.2 & 2025 & Java 17+. Enhanced EUDI wallet integration. Improved TSL
(Trusted List) refresh mechanisms. This is the current stable release as
of early 2026. \\
\end{longtable}

The groupId change in version 6.0 is a common source of confusion. If
your project depends on \texttt{eu.europa.ec.joinup.sd-dss:dss-pades},
that is DSS 5.x. If it depends on \texttt{eu.europa.dss:dss-pades}, that
is DSS 6.x. The two groupIds are not interchangeable --- do not mix
modules from different major versions.

\section{Architecture}\label{_architecture_2}

\subsection{Design Principles}\label{_design_principles}

EU DSS is built around three core architectural principles:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Format independence}: The same signing and validation workflow
  works for PAdES (PDF), CAdES (CMS), XAdES (XML), and JAdES (JSON). The
  format-specific logic is encapsulated in format modules; the core
  signing flow is shared.
\item
  \textbf{Pluggable token architecture}: The private key can come from
  anywhere --- a PKCS\#12 file, a PKCS\#11 smart card, a Microsoft CNG
  keystore, a remote CSC API service, or a custom
  \texttt{SignatureTokenConnection} implementation. The signing flow
  does not need to know where the key is.
\item
  \textbf{Policy-based validation}: Validation is driven by a
  configurable policy (an XML file) that defines which checks to perform
  and what their outcomes mean. You can use the default ETSI validation
  policy, or you can define your own --- accepting certain
  non-conformities for your use case while rejecting others.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Module Overview}\label{_module_overview}

EU DSS is organized into Maven modules, each with a specific
responsibility. You do not need all of them --- include only what you
use.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.6667}}@{}}
\caption{Core EU DSS Modules}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Module
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Purpose
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Module
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Purpose
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{dss-spi} & Service Provider Interface. Core abstractions:
\texttt{DSSDocument}, \texttt{SignatureTokenConnection},
\texttt{CertificateToken}, \texttt{DSSPrivateKeyEntry}. All other
modules depend on this. \\
\texttt{dss-model} & Data model classes: \texttt{SignatureLevel},
\texttt{DigestAlgorithm}, \texttt{EncryptionAlgorithm},
\texttt{TimestampType}, etc. Enumerations and DTOs used across the
framework. \\
\texttt{dss-token} & Token implementations:
\texttt{Pkcs12SignatureToken}, \texttt{Pkcs11SignatureToken},
\texttt{MSCAPISignatureToken}, \texttt{KeyStoreSignatureToken}. These
connect to key material. \\
\texttt{dss-pades} & PAdES (PDF) signing and validation. Depends on
Apache PDFBox internally. Produces and validates PAdES-B-B through
B-LTA. \\
\texttt{dss-cades} & CAdES (CMS) signing and validation. Used internally
by \texttt{dss-pades} for the CMS structures inside PDF signatures. \\
\texttt{dss-xades} & XAdES (XML) signing and validation. Not needed for
PDF work unless you also handle XML signatures. \\
\texttt{dss-jades} & JAdES (JSON) signing and validation. The newest
format support. \\
\texttt{dss-tsl-validation} & EU Trusted List (TSL/EUTL) parsing,
validation, and caching. Downloads the trusted lists, verifies their
signatures, and builds a trust store that other modules use for
validation. \\
\texttt{dss-crl-parser} & CRL downloading and parsing. \\
\texttt{dss-ocsp-parser} & OCSP response fetching and parsing. \\
\texttt{dss-service} & Higher-level service classes:
\texttt{OnlineOCSPSource}, \texttt{OnlineCRLSource},
\texttt{OnlineTSPSource}, \texttt{CommonsDataLoader} (HTTP client).
Implements the data-fetching operations that validation requires. \\
\texttt{dss-document} & Document-level abstractions:
\texttt{SignatureParameters}, \texttt{ToBeSigned},
\texttt{SignatureValue}, \texttt{SignedDocumentValidator}. The signing
flow API lives here. \\
\texttt{dss-diagnostic-data-jaxb} & JAXB model for the diagnostic data
XML that validation produces. Useful for detailed validation reports. \\
\texttt{dss-detailed-report-jaxb} & JAXB model for the detailed
validation report. Human-readable validation results. \\
\texttt{dss-simple-report-jaxb} & JAXB model for the simple validation
report. Summary-level pass/fail results. \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{Maven Coordinates}\label{_maven_coordinates}

For a typical PAdES signing and validation project with EU DSS 6.2, you
need:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{properties}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}}\KeywordTok{dss.version}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}6.2\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{dss.version}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{properties}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}

\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{dependencies}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
    \CommentTok{\textless{}!{-}{-} Core signing and validation for PAdES {-}{-}\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}}\KeywordTok{dependency}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{        \textless{}}\KeywordTok{groupId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}eu.europa.dss\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{groupId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{        \textless{}}\KeywordTok{artifactId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}dss{-}pades\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{artifactId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{        \textless{}}\KeywordTok{version}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}$\{dss.version\}\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{version}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}/}\KeywordTok{dependency}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}

    \CommentTok{\textless{}!{-}{-} Token support (PKCS\#12, PKCS\#11) {-}{-}\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}}\KeywordTok{dependency}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{        \textless{}}\KeywordTok{groupId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}eu.europa.dss\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{groupId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{        \textless{}}\KeywordTok{artifactId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}dss{-}token\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{artifactId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{        \textless{}}\KeywordTok{version}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}$\{dss.version\}\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{version}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}/}\KeywordTok{dependency}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}

    \CommentTok{\textless{}!{-}{-} Online sources for OCSP, CRL, TSA {-}{-}\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}}\KeywordTok{dependency}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{        \textless{}}\KeywordTok{groupId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}eu.europa.dss\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{groupId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{        \textless{}}\KeywordTok{artifactId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}dss{-}service\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{artifactId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{        \textless{}}\KeywordTok{version}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}$\{dss.version\}\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{version}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}/}\KeywordTok{dependency}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}

    \CommentTok{\textless{}!{-}{-} EU Trusted List validation {-}{-}\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}}\KeywordTok{dependency}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{        \textless{}}\KeywordTok{groupId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}eu.europa.dss\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{groupId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{        \textless{}}\KeywordTok{artifactId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}dss{-}tsl{-}validation\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{artifactId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{        \textless{}}\KeywordTok{version}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}$\{dss.version\}\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{version}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}/}\KeywordTok{dependency}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{dependencies}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

EU DSS depends on 3.0.0 internally for PDF operations. If your project
also uses PDFBox directly, ensure version alignment --- having two
different PDFBox versions on the classpath causes unpredictable
behavior. Check the DSS BOM (Bill of Materials) for the exact PDFBox
version it expects.

\section{The Signing Flow: ToBeSigned /
SignatureValue}\label{_the_signing_flow_tobesigned_signaturevalue}

\subsection{The Two-Phase Signing
Model}\label{_the_two_phase_signing_model}

EU DSS uses a two-phase signing model that maps directly to the
hash-signing model described in Chapter 22. This separation is what
makes remote signing possible --- the private key operation can happen
anywhere, including on a remote HSM via the CSC API.

\textbf{Phase 1: Compute what needs to be signed}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// 1. Create the PAdES service}
\NormalTok{PAdESService service }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PAdESService}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// 2. Set the TSA source (for B{-}T and above)}
\NormalTok{service}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setTspSource}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{onlineTSPSource}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// 3. Configure signature parameters}
\NormalTok{PAdESSignatureParameters parameters }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PAdESSignatureParameters}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{parameters}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSignatureLevel}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{SignatureLevel}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{PAdES\_BASELINE\_T}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{parameters}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDigestAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{DigestAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SHA256}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{parameters}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSigningCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CertificateToken}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signingCert}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{parameters}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCertificateChain}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certificateChainTokens}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// 4. Get the data to be signed (the hash)}
\NormalTok{DSSDocument toSignDocument }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{InMemoryDocument}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{ToBeSigned dataToSign }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ service}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDataToSign}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{        toSignDocument}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ parameters}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The \texttt{ToBeSigned} object contains the bytes that must be signed
--- specifically, the DER-encoded signed attributes of the CMS
SignerInfo, exactly as described in Chapter 22's hash-signing flow. EU
DSS has already computed the message digest over the PDF byte range,
built the ESS-signing-certificate-v2 attribute, and assembled the signed
attributes.

\textbf{Phase 2: Sign the hash and finalize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// 5. Sign the hash (locally or remotely)}
\CommentTok{//    This is where the private key is used}
\NormalTok{SignatureValue signatureValue }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signingToken}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{sign}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{        dataToSign}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ parameters}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDigestAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{(),}
\NormalTok{        privateKeyEntry}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// 6. Finalize the signature — embed in PDF}
\NormalTok{DSSDocument signedDocument }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ service}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{signDocument}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{        toSignDocument}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ parameters}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signatureValue}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// 7. Write the result}
\NormalTok{signedDocument}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{writeTo}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{FileOutputStream}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"signed.pdf"}\OperatorTok{));}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The beauty of this design: step 5 is the \emph{only} step that touches
the private key. Everything else --- PDF parsing, CMS construction,
timestamp embedding, incremental save --- is handled by EU DSS. If you
want to use a remote signing service instead of a local key, you replace
step 5 with a CSC API call and everything else stays the same.

\subsection{SignatureTokenConnection
Implementations}\label{_signaturetokenconnection_implementations}

The \texttt{SignatureTokenConnection} interface abstracts access to the
private key. EU DSS ships with several implementations:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// PKCS\#12 file (local keystore)}
\NormalTok{Pkcs12SignatureToken token }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{Pkcs12SignatureToken}\OperatorTok{(}
        \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{FileInputStream}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"keystore.p12"}\OperatorTok{),}
        \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{KeyStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{PasswordProtection}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"password"}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toCharArray}\OperatorTok{()));}
\BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{DSSPrivateKeyEntry}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ keys }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ token}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getKeys}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{DSSPrivateKeyEntry privateKey }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ keys}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// PKCS\#11 smart card / HSM}
\NormalTok{Pkcs11SignatureToken pkcs11Token }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{Pkcs11SignatureToken}\OperatorTok{(}
        \StringTok{"/usr/lib/opensc{-}pkcs11.so"}\OperatorTok{,}  \CommentTok{// PKCS\#11 library path}
        \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{KeyStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{PasswordProtection}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"pin"}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toCharArray}\OperatorTok{()),}
        \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// slot index}
\BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{DSSPrivateKeyEntry}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ smartCardKeys }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ pkcs11Token}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getKeys}\OperatorTok{();}

\CommentTok{// Microsoft CNG (Windows certificate store)}
\NormalTok{MSCAPISignatureToken mscapiToken }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{MSCAPISignatureToken}\OperatorTok{();}
\BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{DSSPrivateKeyEntry}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ windowsKeys }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ mscapiToken}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getKeys}\OperatorTok{();}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

For remote signing via CSC API, you implement
\texttt{SignatureTokenConnection} with HTTP calls to the CSC endpoints.
The \texttt{sign()} method sends the hash to
\texttt{/signatures/signHash} and returns the signature value.

\section{Complete PAdES-B-T Signing
Example}\label{_complete_pades_b_t_signing_example}

Here is a complete, working example that signs a PDF with PAdES-B-T
using a PKCS\#12 keystore and a TSA:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cades}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CAdESService}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{enumerations}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{DigestAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{enumerations}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{SignatureLevel}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{model}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{DSSDocument}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{model}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{InMemoryDocument}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{model}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{SignatureValue}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{model}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ToBeSigned}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pades}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PAdESSignatureParameters}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pades}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PAdESService}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{service}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{http}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{commons}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CommonsDataLoader}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{service}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{http}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{commons}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{OCSPDataLoader}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{service}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ocsp}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{OnlineOCSPSource}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{service}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{crl}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{OnlineCRLSource}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{service}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{tsp}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{OnlineTSPSource}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{spi}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{x509}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CertificateSource}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{spi}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{x509}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CommonTrustedCertificateSource}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{token}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{DSSPrivateKeyEntry}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{token}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Pkcs12SignatureToken}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{eu}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{europa}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{dss}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{validation}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CommonCertificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Complete PAdES}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{B}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{T signing with EU DSS}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ This example uses a PKCS}\CommentTok{\#12}\NormalTok{ keystore and a public TSA}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{signWithEuDss}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ keystorePath}\OperatorTok{,}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ keystorePassword}\OperatorTok{,} \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ tsaUrl}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

    \CommentTok{// 1. Open the PKCS\#12 token}
\NormalTok{    Pkcs12SignatureToken signingToken }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{Pkcs12SignatureToken}\OperatorTok{(}
            \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{FileInputStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{keystorePath}\OperatorTok{),}
            \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{KeyStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{PasswordProtection}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                    keystorePassword}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toCharArray}\OperatorTok{()));}

    \CommentTok{// 2. Get the private key entry (first key in the keystore)}
    \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{DSSPrivateKeyEntry}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ keys }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signingToken}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getKeys}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    DSSPrivateKeyEntry privateKey }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ keys}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// 3. Configure the certificate verifier}
    \CommentTok{//    (used during signing to validate the chain)}
\NormalTok{    CommonCertificateVerifier certificateVerifier }\OperatorTok{=}
            \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CommonCertificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{();}

    \CommentTok{// OCSP source for online revocation checking}
\NormalTok{    OnlineOCSPSource ocspSource }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OnlineOCSPSource}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    ocspSource}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDataLoader}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OCSPDataLoader}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{    certificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setOcspSource}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ocspSource}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// CRL source as fallback}
\NormalTok{    OnlineCRLSource crlSource }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OnlineCRLSource}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    crlSource}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDataLoader}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CommonsDataLoader}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{    certificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCrlSource}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crlSource}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// 4. Configure the TSA source}
\NormalTok{    OnlineTSPSource tspSource }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OnlineTSPSource}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsaUrl}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    tspSource}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDataLoader}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CommonsDataLoader}\OperatorTok{());}

    \CommentTok{// 5. Create the PAdES service}
\NormalTok{    PAdESService padesService }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PAdESService}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    padesService}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setTspSource}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tspSource}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// 6. Configure signature parameters}
\NormalTok{    PAdESSignatureParameters parameters }\OperatorTok{=}
            \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PAdESSignatureParameters}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    parameters}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSignatureLevel}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{SignatureLevel}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{PAdES\_BASELINE\_T}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    parameters}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDigestAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{DigestAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SHA256}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    parameters}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSigningCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            privateKey}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificate}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{    parameters}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCertificateChain}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            privateKey}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificateChain}\OperatorTok{());}

    \CommentTok{// Optional: set visual signature parameters}
    \CommentTok{// parameters.setSignatureImageParameters(...);}

    \CommentTok{// 7. Phase 1: Get the data to be signed}
\NormalTok{    DSSDocument toSignDocument }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{InMemoryDocument}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    ToBeSigned dataToSign }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ padesService}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDataToSign}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            toSignDocument}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ parameters}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// 8. Phase 2: Sign the hash}
\NormalTok{    SignatureValue signatureValue }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signingToken}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{sign}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            dataToSign}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ parameters}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDigestAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{(),}
\NormalTok{            privateKey}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// 9. Finalize: embed signature in PDF}
\NormalTok{    DSSDocument signedDocument }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ padesService}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{signDocument}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            toSignDocument}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ parameters}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signatureValue}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// 10. Extract the result}
    \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\NormalTok{ result }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    signedDocument}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{writeTo}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{result}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"PAdES{-}B{-}T signature created: \{\} bytes"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            result}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{());}
    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ result}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toByteArray}\OperatorTok{();}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

That is roughly 60 lines of code --- including logging, comments, and
error handling setup --- to produce a fully compliant PAdES-B-T
signature. Compare this to the hundreds of lines required to build the
CMS structure manually with BouncyCastle (Chapter 12), and the value
proposition of EU DSS becomes clear.

\section{Extending Signatures}\label{_extending_signatures}

One of EU DSS's most powerful features is \textbf{signature extension}:
upgrading a signature's PAdES profile without re-signing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Extends a PAdES}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{B}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{B or B}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{T signature to PAdES}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{B}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{LTA}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Fetches revocation data}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ builds the DSS}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ adds an archival timestamp}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{extendToLta}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ signedPdfBytes}\OperatorTok{,}
        \BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ tsaUrl}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

    \CommentTok{// 1. Set up the certificate verifier with online sources}
\NormalTok{    CommonCertificateVerifier verifier }\OperatorTok{=}
            \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CommonCertificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    verifier}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setOcspSource}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OnlineOCSPSource}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{    verifier}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCrlSource}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OnlineCRLSource}\OperatorTok{());}

    \CommentTok{// 2. Add the EU Trusted List as a trust source}
    \CommentTok{//    (required for validating qualified certificates)}
    \CommentTok{// TrustedListsCertificateSource is populated by the}
    \CommentTok{// TSL loading job — see Spring Boot integration below}

    \CommentTok{// 3. Create the PAdES service with a TSA}
\NormalTok{    PAdESService service }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PAdESService}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{verifier}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    OnlineTSPSource tspSource }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OnlineTSPSource}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsaUrl}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    service}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setTspSource}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tspSource}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// 4. Configure extension parameters}
\NormalTok{    PAdESSignatureParameters extensionParams }\OperatorTok{=}
            \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PAdESSignatureParameters}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    extensionParams}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSignatureLevel}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            SignatureLevel}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{PAdES\_BASELINE\_LTA}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// 5. Extend the document}
\NormalTok{    DSSDocument signedDoc }\OperatorTok{=}
            \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{InMemoryDocument}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedPdfBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    DSSDocument extendedDoc }\OperatorTok{=}
\NormalTok{            service}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{extendDocument}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedDoc}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ extensionParams}\OperatorTok{);}

    \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\NormalTok{ result }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    extendedDoc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{writeTo}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{result}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Signature extended to PAdES{-}B{-}LTA: \{\} bytes"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            result}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{());}
    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ result}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toByteArray}\OperatorTok{();}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The \texttt{extendDocument} method handles all the complexity described
in Chapter 17 (PAdES Profiles) and Chapter 18 (Timestamps and LTV):

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
