\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{interactive}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{digitalsignature}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Demonstrates SWA detection}\CommentTok{:}\NormalTok{ verify that the }\CommentTok{/}\NormalTok{ByteRange covers the entire}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ file without gaps }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{except the }\CommentTok{/}\NormalTok{Contents gap}\CommentTok{)}\NormalTok{ and without overlaps}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ SwaDefenseCheck }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{checkByteRangeIntegrity}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{)}
            \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

\NormalTok{        PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{);}

        \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature sig }\OperatorTok{:}\NormalTok{ doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignatureDictionaries}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
            \DataTypeTok{int}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ byteRange }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ sig}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getByteRange}\OperatorTok{();}

            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{byteRange }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null} \OperatorTok{||}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length} \OperatorTok{!=} \DecValTok{4}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{err}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"FAIL: Missing or malformed /ByteRange"}\OperatorTok{);}
                \ControlFlowTok{continue}\OperatorTok{;}
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ offset1 }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{];} \CommentTok{// Should be 0}
            \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ length1 }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{];}
            \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ offset2 }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\OperatorTok{];}
            \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ length2 }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ byteRange}\OperatorTok{[}\DecValTok{3}\OperatorTok{];}
            \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ signedEnd }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ offset2 }\OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ length2}\OperatorTok{;}

            \CommentTok{// Check 1: First range must start at byte 0}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{offset1 }\OperatorTok{!=} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{err}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \StringTok{"FAIL: /ByteRange does not start at byte 0. "}
                        \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"Starts at: "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ offset1}\OperatorTok{);} 
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \CommentTok{// Check 2: The gap between ranges is the /Contents value}
            \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ gapStart }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ offset1 }\OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ length1}\OperatorTok{;}
            \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ gapEnd }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ offset2}\OperatorTok{;}
            \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ gapSize }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ gapEnd }\OperatorTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ gapStart}\OperatorTok{;}

            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{gapSize }\OperatorTok{\textless{}=} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{err}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \StringTok{"FAIL: /ByteRange gap is zero or negative. "}
                        \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"Possible SWA indicator"}\OperatorTok{);} 
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \CommentTok{// Check 3: Verify /Contents hex string is at the gap position}
            \CommentTok{// The gap should contain exactly the hex{-}encoded CMS signature}
            \CommentTok{// surrounded by angle brackets: \textless{}hex...\textgreater{}}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{[}\NormalTok{gapStart}\OperatorTok{]} \OperatorTok{!=} \CharTok{\textquotesingle{}\textless{}\textquotesingle{}}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{err}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \StringTok{"FAIL: Expected \textquotesingle{}\textless{}\textquotesingle{} at gap start (offset "}
                        \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ gapStart }\OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"), found: 0x"}
                        \OperatorTok{+} \BuiltInTok{Integer}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toHexString}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                                pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{[}\NormalTok{gapStart}\OperatorTok{]} \OperatorTok{\&} \BaseNTok{0xFF}\OperatorTok{));} 
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \CommentTok{// Check 4: The signed region should cover the entire file}
            \CommentTok{// (for a single{-}signature document with no subsequent updates)}
            \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedEnd }\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
                \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{err}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}
                        \StringTok{"WARNING: /ByteRange ends at byte "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ signedEnd}
                        \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" but file is "} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length}
                        \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{" bytes. Unsigned trailing content detected"}\OperatorTok{);}
            \OperatorTok{\}}

            \CommentTok{// Check 5: Cross{-}validate that the /ByteRange in the file}
            \CommentTok{// matches what we\textquotesingle{}d expect from the signed revision}
            \CommentTok{// This requires parsing the original revision\textquotesingle{}s xref}
            \BuiltInTok{System}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{out}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{println}\OperatorTok{(}
                    \StringTok{"ByteRange analysis: "}
                    \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"range1=["} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ offset1 }\OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{","} \OperatorTok{+} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{offset1 }\OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ length1}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{") "}
                    \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"gap=["} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ gapStart }\OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{","} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ gapEnd }\OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{") "}
                    \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"range2=["} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ offset2 }\OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{","} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ signedEnd }\OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{") "}
                    \OperatorTok{+} \StringTok{"fileSize="} \OperatorTok{+}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

\NormalTok{        doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{close}\OperatorTok{();}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The first byte range must start at offset 0. If it doesn't, there are
  unsigned bytes at the beginning of the file --- a clear SWA indicator
\item
  The gap between the two ranges must be positive and should correspond
  to the \texttt{/Contents} hex string. A zero or negative gap indicates
  structural manipulation
\item
  The gap should contain the hex-encoded CMS value. Checking that it
  starts with \texttt{\textless{}} and ends with \texttt{\textgreater{}}
  is a basic sanity check
\end{itemize}

SWA defense requires more than just checking \texttt{/ByteRange}
arithmetic. A sophisticated attacker can construct a valid-looking byte
range that passes arithmetic checks but points to carefully crafted
content. The complete defense is: (a) verify \texttt{/ByteRange} covers
the file correctly, (b) verify the CMS signature cryptographically (USF
defense), and (c) verify that the \texttt{/ByteRange} values come from
the signed revision's cross-reference, not from an appended revision.

\subsection{Attack Class 4: Shadow Attacks
(2021)}\label{_attack_class_4_shadow_attacks_2021}

\subsubsection{A Fundamentally Different Threat
Model}\label{_a_fundamentally_different_threat_model}

The three attacks from CCS 2019 (USF, ISA, SWA) all shared a common
assumption: the attacker modifies a PDF \emph{after} it has been
legitimately signed. The signed document is genuine; the attacker's
manipulation comes afterward.

Shadow Attacks, published at NDSS 2021, inverted the model. The attacker
prepares the PDF \emph{before} it is signed, embedding hidden malicious
content that the signer cannot see during the signing process. The
signer reviews the document, sees nothing suspicious, and signs it. The
signed PDF is then sent to the recipient, who opens it --- and sees
different content from what the signer saw.

The signature is cryptographically valid. The signer genuinely signed
those exact bytes. The bytes just don't mean what the signer thought
they meant.

This is not a cryptographic failure. It is a semantic failure: the gap
between what a human \emph{sees} when they review a document and what
the \emph{bytes} actually encode. And it is far harder to defend against
than USF, ISA, or SWA.

\subsubsection{Shadow Attack Variant 1:
Hide}\label{_shadow_attack_variant_1_hide}

The attacker creates a PDF with two layers of content --- one visible,
one hidden. The hidden content is placed \emph{behind} the visible
content using PDF features like:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Overlapping objects}: A white rectangle drawn on top of text,
  hiding it. The text is in the content stream; the rectangle covers it.
  After signing, the attacker removes the rectangle via an incremental
  update, revealing the hidden text.
\item
  \textbf{Optional Content Groups (OCG)}: PDF's layer mechanism. Content
  can be tagged with \texttt{/OC} (Optional Content) markers and made
  visible or invisible via the \texttt{/OCProperties} dictionary. The
  attacker sets a layer to invisible before signing, then sets it to
  visible after signing.
\item
  \textbf{Referenced XObjects}: Content referenced as
  \texttt{Form\ XObject} but not actually rendered on any page. After
  signing, an incremental update adds the XObject to a page's content
  stream.
\end{itemize}

The key constraint: \emph{all of the hidden content must be present in
the file at the time of signing}, so that it falls within the signed
byte range. The attacker only changes the \emph{visibility} via an
incremental update after signing --- which some viewers allowed without
warning.

\subsubsection{Shadow Attack Variant 2:
Replace}\label{_shadow_attack_variant_2_replace}

The attacker creates a PDF where the visible content can be
\emph{replaced} by different visible content without modifying any
objects in the signed byte range. This exploits \textbf{interactive form
features}:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  A text field with a default value ("Contract for \$1,000") and a
  different actual value ("Contract for \$1,000,000") --- the default is
  displayed initially, but changing which value is rendered requires
  only an annotation flag change
\item
  Font references that can be swapped --- the same Unicode code points
  render as different glyphs depending on which font is active
\item
  Images referenced by name from a page's Resources dictionary ---
  changing which image a name resolves to requires only modifying the
  Resources dictionary
\end{itemize}

The Replace variant is particularly dangerous because it exploits
features that are \emph{designed} to change document appearance (form
filling, annotation updates) --- the viewer must distinguish between a
legitimate form fill and an attacker swapping content.

\subsubsection{Shadow Attack Variant 3:
Hide-and-Replace}\label{_shadow_attack_variant_3_hide_and_replace}

The most powerful variant combines both techniques. The attacker creates
a PDF that:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Contains the content the signer will see (layer A)
\item
  Contains the content the recipient will see (layer B), hidden behind
  layer A
\item
  Is structured so that a single incremental update (changing a few
  bytes in an annotation dictionary or OCG configuration) switches from
  layer A to layer B
\end{enumerate}

After signing, the attacker appends the minimal incremental update
needed to switch layers. If the viewer allows annotation or OCG changes
after signing (as many did, because these are often considered
"permissible" changes), the attack succeeds.

\subsubsection{Demonstration: Shadow Attack (Hide Variant)
Preparation}\label{_demonstration_shadow_attack_hide_variant_preparation}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Loader}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDDocument}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDPage}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDPageContentStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{common}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{font}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{apache}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdfbox}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{pdmodel}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{font}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{;}

\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{io}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{File}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{nio}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{file}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Files}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Demonstrates the preparation phase of a Shadow Attack }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{Hide variant}\CommentTok{).}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ The attacker creates a PDF with hidden text behind a white overlay}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ BEFORE signing}\CommentTok{:}\NormalTok{ Signer sees }\CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{Payment}\CommentTok{:} \CommentTok{$1,000"}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ AFTER signing }\CommentTok{+}\NormalTok{ reveal}\CommentTok{:}\NormalTok{ Recipient sees }\CommentTok{"}\NormalTok{Payment}\CommentTok{:} \CommentTok{$1,000,000"}
 \CommentTok{*}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ The hidden text }\CommentTok{("}\NormalTok{Payment}\CommentTok{:} \CommentTok{$1,000,000")}\NormalTok{ is present in the signed bytes}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ The overlay }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{white rectangle}\CommentTok{)}\NormalTok{ hides it}\CommentTok{.}\NormalTok{ An incremental update that removes}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ the overlay reveals the hidden text }\CommentTok{—}\NormalTok{ and the signature remains valid}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \KeywordTok{class}\NormalTok{ ShadowAttackHideDemo }\OperatorTok{\{}

    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{static} \DataTypeTok{void} \FunctionTok{main}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{String}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ args}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDDocument}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        PDPage page }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDPage}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDRectangle}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{A4}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addPage}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{page}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{        PDType1Font font }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FontName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HELVETICA}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        PDType1Font fontBold }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{(}
\NormalTok{                Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FontName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HELVETICA\_BOLD}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{        PDPageContentStream cs }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDPageContentStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ page}\OperatorTok{);}

        \CommentTok{// Layer 1: The hidden content (written FIRST, so it\textquotesingle{}s behind)}
        \CommentTok{// This text will be present in the signed bytes}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{beginText}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFont}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{fontBold}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{16}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{newLineAtOffset}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{50}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{700}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{showText}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Payment: $1,000,000"}\OperatorTok{);} 
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{endText}\OperatorTok{();}

        \CommentTok{// Layer 2: The overlay that hides it}
        \CommentTok{// A white rectangle drawn ON TOP of the hidden text}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setNonStrokingColor}\OperatorTok{(}\FloatTok{1.0f}\OperatorTok{,} \FloatTok{1.0f}\OperatorTok{,} \FloatTok{1.0f}\OperatorTok{);} \CommentTok{// White}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addRect}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{45}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{695}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{250}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{25}\OperatorTok{);} \CommentTok{// Covers the text above}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{fill}\OperatorTok{();} 

        \CommentTok{// Layer 3: The visible content (what the signer sees)}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setNonStrokingColor}\OperatorTok{(}\FloatTok{0.0f}\OperatorTok{,} \FloatTok{0.0f}\OperatorTok{,} \FloatTok{0.0f}\OperatorTok{);} \CommentTok{// Black}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{beginText}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFont}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{fontBold}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{16}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{newLineAtOffset}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{50}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{700}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{showText}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Payment: $1,000"}\OperatorTok{);} 
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{endText}\OperatorTok{();}

        \CommentTok{// Legitimate{-}looking document body}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{beginText}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFont}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{font}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{12}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{newLineAtOffset}\OperatorTok{(}\DecValTok{50}\OperatorTok{,} \DecValTok{650}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{showText}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"This agreement confirms the payment amount above."}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{endText}\OperatorTok{();}

\NormalTok{        cs}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{close}\OperatorTok{();}

        \CommentTok{// Save — this PDF is now ready to be sent to the signer}
        \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\NormalTok{ baos }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{save}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{baos}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{        doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{close}\OperatorTok{();}

\NormalTok{        Files}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{write}\OperatorTok{(}
                \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{File}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"shadow{-}prepared.pdf"}\OperatorTok{).}\FunctionTok{toPath}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ baos}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toByteArray}\OperatorTok{());}

        \CommentTok{// The signer opens this PDF, sees "Payment: $1,000", and signs it.}
        \CommentTok{// The signed byte range includes ALL content: both the hidden}
        \CommentTok{// "$1,000,000" text and the white rectangle that covers it.}
        \CommentTok{//}
        \CommentTok{// After signing, the attacker creates an incremental update that}
        \CommentTok{// removes or makes transparent the white rectangle, revealing}
        \CommentTok{// "Payment: $1,000,000" — and the signature validates.}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The hidden text is written to the content stream first. In PDF
  rendering order, later operators paint \emph{over} earlier ones on the
  same area
\item
  A white filled rectangle covers the hidden text completely. The signer
  sees only white space at this position
\item
  The visible text is drawn on top of the white rectangle. This is what
  the signer reads when reviewing the document before signing
\end{itemize}

Shadow Attacks are fundamentally harder to prevent than USF, ISA, or SWA
because the malicious content is \emph{part of the signed data}. The
signature is cryptographically valid over the exact bytes the signer
intended to sign. The defense must go beyond byte-level verification
into \emph{semantic} analysis: does the document contain hidden content?
Are there overlapping objects? Are there unused Form XObjects? Are there
Optional Content Groups with suspicious visibility settings? This is an
open research problem with no complete solution.

\subsubsection{The Scope of Shadow Attack
Vulnerability}\label{_the_scope_of_shadow_attack_vulnerability}

The Bochum team tested 29 PDF viewers against the Shadow Attack
variants. The results were comprehensive:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}@{}}
\caption{Shadow Attack test results across PDF viewers (NDSS
2021)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Viewer
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Hide
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Replace
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Hide-and-Replace
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Viewer
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
