Adobe Acrobat Reader DC & Vulnerable & Vulnerable & Vulnerable \\
Foxit Reader & Vulnerable & Vulnerable & Vulnerable \\
LibreOffice Draw & Vulnerable & Partially vulnerable & Vulnerable \\
Nitro Pro & Vulnerable & Vulnerable & Vulnerable \\
PDF-XChange & Vulnerable & Not vulnerable & Vulnerable \\
Okular & Vulnerable & Vulnerable & Vulnerable \\
Evince & Vulnerable & Vulnerable & Vulnerable \\
Chrome PDF Viewer & No signature validation & --- & --- \\
macOS Preview & Vulnerable & Vulnerable & Vulnerable \\
\end{longtable}

This table is a representative subset. The full results covering 29
viewers are available in the NDSS 2021 paper and at pdf-insecurity.org.
Chrome's built-in PDF viewer does not validate signatures at all (it
displays them but does not indicate validity), so Shadow Attacks are not
applicable in the traditional sense --- but the user also receives no
warning that the document has been signed and potentially tampered with.

\subsection{Pre-Bochum Vulnerabilities: Earlier PDF Signature
Issues}\label{_pre_bochum_vulnerabilities_earlier_pdf_signature_issues}

The Bochum research was the most systematic analysis of PDF signature
security, but it was not the first time vulnerabilities were discovered.
Several earlier issues laid the groundwork --- and reveal that the
\emph{category} of bug (failing to verify what you claim to verify) has
been a persistent pattern in PDF signature implementations.

\subsubsection{CVE-2018-16042 --- Adobe Acrobat Signature Bypass
(2018)}\label{_cve_2018_16042_adobe_acrobat_signature_bypass_2018}

In 2018, Adobe patched \textbf{CVE-2018-16042} in Acrobat DC and Acrobat
Reader DC (versions before 2019.008.20081). The vulnerability allowed a
crafted PDF to display a valid signature indicator even when the
document had been modified.

The technical details were not fully disclosed (Adobe's advisory was
characteristically brief), but the vulnerability class --- signature
validation bypass via document manipulation --- is the same category as
the ISA attacks that Bochum would systematize a year later. This CVE was
one of several signals that the PDF signature validation code in major
viewers had not been subject to rigorous adversarial testing.

\textbf{Affected versions:} Acrobat DC/Reader DC before 2019.008.20081\\
\textbf{Impact:} Signature validation bypass --- modified documents
displayed as validly signed\\
\textbf{Fix:} Updated signature validation logic in the January 2019
security update

\subsubsection{CVE-2018-4993 --- Adobe Acrobat NTLM Credential Theft
(2018)}\label{_cve_2018_4993_adobe_acrobat_ntlm_credential_theft_2018}

While not strictly a signature bypass, \textbf{CVE-2018-4993}
demonstrated a related risk: a crafted PDF could trigger NTLM
authentication requests to an attacker-controlled server when opened in
Adobe Acrobat. Combined with a forged signature (via any of the bypass
techniques), this creates a compound attack: the victim opens a "validly
signed" PDF, and their Windows credentials are silently exfiltrated.

\textbf{Affected versions:} Acrobat DC/Reader DC before 2018.011.20040\\
\textbf{Impact:} NTLM hash theft via SMB relay when opening a crafted
PDF\\
\textbf{Fix:} Restricted external resource loading in the May 2018
security update

\subsubsection{SHAttered and the SHA-1 Deprecation
(2017)}\label{_shattered_and_the_sha_1_deprecation_2017}

In February 2017, researchers from Google and CWI Amsterdam published
\textbf{SHAttered} --- the first practical collision attack against
SHA-1. They produced two different PDF documents with the same SHA-1
hash, proving that SHA-1 could no longer be relied upon for collision
resistance.

For PDF signatures, this had immediate implications. The
\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.sha1} SubFilter --- which embeds a SHA-1 hash of the
entire document as the CMS content data --- was now theoretically
vulnerable to a collision attack: an attacker could prepare two
documents with the same SHA-1 hash, have the victim sign one, and then
substitute the other.

The practical impact was limited (the collision required approximately
2\textsuperscript{63} computations and the two colliding documents had
to be carefully constructed), but the theoretical break was sufficient
to trigger industry-wide deprecation:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  ISO 32000-2:2020 deprecated \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.sha1} entirely
\item
  Adobe Acrobat began showing warnings for SHA-1 signed documents
\item
  The CA/Browser Forum required SHA-256 as the minimum for all new
  certificates
\item
  PAdES profiles already mandated SHA-256 as the minimum digest
  algorithm
\end{itemize}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.8000}}@{}}
\caption{SHA-1 timeline for PDF signatures}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Year
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Event
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Year
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Event
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
2005 & Theoretical weakness in SHA-1 published (Wang et al.) \\
2011 & NIST deprecated SHA-1 for digital signatures in federal use \\
2013 & Microsoft announced SHA-1 certificate sunset for 2016 \\
2017 & SHAttered: first practical SHA-1 collision (Google/CWI). Two PDFs
with identical SHA-1 hashes publicly demonstrated \\
2017 & ISO 32000-2 deprecated \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.sha1} SubFilter \\
2020 & SHA-1 collisions reduced to 2\textsuperscript{63} operations
(Leurent and Peyrin, "SHA-1 is a Shambles"), making chosen-prefix
collisions feasible \\
\end{longtable}

If your code still generates signatures using SHA-1 (either via
\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.sha1} or using SHA-1 as the \texttt{digestAlgorithm}
in the CMS \texttt{SignerInfo}), stop immediately. SHA-1 is broken. Use
SHA-256 or SHA-384. There is no legitimate reason to use SHA-1 for any
new signature created after 2017.

\subsubsection{The Comodo Registration Authority Breach
(2011)}\label{_the_comodo_registration_authority_breach_2011}

In March 2011 --- the same month as DigiNotar, though discovered
slightly earlier --- an attacker compromised a \textbf{registration
authority (RA)} affiliated with Comodo, one of the world's largest
certificate authorities at the time.

The attacker used the compromised RA account to issue nine fraudulent
SSL certificates for high-profile domains: \texttt{login.live.com},
\texttt{mail.google.com}, \texttt{www.google.com},
\texttt{login.yahoo.com}, \texttt{login.skype.com},
\texttt{addons.mozilla.org}, and three others. The certificates were
issued by Comodo's legitimate intermediate CA, meaning they chained
correctly to a trusted root.

The breach was discovered quickly because the attacker tested one of the
certificates against a live server, and certificate pinning in Google
Chrome detected the mismatch. Without Chrome's certificate pinning, the
fraudulent certificates could have been used for undetectable
man-in-the-middle attacks.

\textbf{For PDF signatures:} The Comodo breach demonstrated that even
without compromising a root CA's private key, an attacker could obtain
legitimate-looking certificates through a \emph{registration authority}
compromise --- a weaker link in the chain. A document signed with one of
these fraudulent certificates would have validated correctly in any PDF
viewer that trusted Comodo's root. The signer would appear to be
"Google" or "Microsoft" or "Yahoo" --- entities that obviously did not
sign the document.

The incident led to:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Mandatory two-factor authentication for all RA accounts at major CAs
\item
  Domain control validation (DCV) requirements tightened by the
  CA/Browser Forum
\item
  Increased scrutiny of the RA model, eventually leading several CAs to
  eliminate external RAs entirely
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{The Symantec Distrust
(2017--2018)}\label{_the_symantec_distrust_20172018}

Between 2015 and 2017, Google's Chrome security team and the CA/Browser
Forum discovered that \textbf{Symantec} (which at that time owned the
Thawte, GeoTrust, VeriSign, and RapidSSL certificate brands) had
mis-issued thousands of certificates --- including certificates for
domains whose owners had not authorized them.

The problems were systemic:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Symantec had delegated certificate issuance to third-party partners
  (RAs) without adequate oversight
\item
  Test certificates for real domains were issued to unauthorized parties
\item
  Audit logs were incomplete or missing
\item
  Symantec initially denied the scope of the problem, then acknowledged
  it in stages
\end{itemize}

Google announced a graduated distrust of all Symantec-issued
certificates:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Chrome 66 (April 2018): distrusted certificates issued before June 1,
  2016
\item
  Chrome 70 (October 2018): distrusted all remaining Symantec
  certificates
\end{itemize}

Mozilla Firefox and Apple Safari followed with their own distrust
timelines. Symantec sold its CA business to DigiCert, which re-issued
certificates for affected customers under DigiCert's infrastructure.

\textbf{For PDF signatures:} Symantec was a major CA in the AATL (Adobe
Approved Trust List). Documents signed with Symantec-issued certificates
before the distrust cutoff dates would validate correctly when signed,
but might show warnings or failures in PDF viewers that updated their
trust stores to reflect the browser distrust. This created a
time-dependent validation problem: a PDF signed with a Symantec
certificate in 2015 was "valid" when signed, "suspect" in 2018, and ---
depending on the viewer's trust configuration --- might show as
"invalid" or "valid with warnings" today.

This is exactly the problem that PAdES-B-LTA (long-term validation with
archival timestamps) was designed to solve: by embedding the full
certificate chain and revocation data at the time of signing, and then
protecting it with periodic archival timestamps, the signature's
validity at the time of signing can be verified even after the issuing
CA is no longer trusted.

\subsection{Exploits in the Wild}\label{_exploits_in_the_wild}

A natural question: were any of these attacks exploited before or after
the Bochum disclosures?

The honest answer is: \textbf{we don't know for certain}.

PDF signatures are used in financial contracts, government documents,
legal filings, tax returns, insurance claims, and medical records. If an
attacker successfully forged a signature on a financial contract, the
victim might not discover it until a dispute arises --- and even then,
the forensic analysis might not identify the specific attack technique.

What we do know:

\subsubsection{Indicators of Real-World
Exploitation}\label{_indicators_of_real_world_exploitation}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{The "1 Trillion Dollar Refund" title was not hyperbole.} The
  paper's title referenced the fact that tax authorities in multiple
  countries accept signed PDFs as official submissions. The German tax
  system (ELSTER), for example, processes millions of electronically
  signed documents per year. If USF or ISA could forge signatures on tax
  submissions, the financial exposure was enormous. The Bochum team
  coordinated with CERT-Bund (Germany's national CERT) before
  publication.
\item
  \textbf{Adobe's response was unusually fast.} Adobe issued
  CVE-2020-9592 and CVE-2020-9596 and released patches within weeks of
  the responsible disclosure. For a company that typically operates on
  monthly security update cycles, the accelerated timeline suggests
  Adobe assessed the risk as immediate and severe --- consistent with
  either active exploitation or intelligence indicating exploitation was
  imminent.
\item
  \textbf{The EU Digital Signature Service (DSS) was vulnerable.} The
  EU's own reference implementation for signature validation --- the
  tool member states use to validate eIDAS-compliant signatures --- was
  vulnerable to ISA attacks. If the EU's own validator could be fooled,
  signatures submitted to government services across 27 member states
  could potentially have been forged without detection.
\item
  \textbf{Commercial signing services had no detection capability.}
  Before the Bochum research, no commercial PDF signing or validation
  service tested for these attack classes. There were no automated tools
  to detect ISA, SWA, or Shadow Attack patterns in submitted PDFs. Any
  exploitation that occurred before 2019 would have been invisible to
  the existing security infrastructure.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection{Post-Disclosure Exploitation
Risk}\label{_post_disclosure_exploitation_risk}

After the papers were published and patches released, the risk shifted:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Unpatched viewers remain vulnerable.} Not all users update
  their PDF viewers. Enterprise environments, government agencies with
  slow update cycles, and users in regions with limited internet access
  may still be running vulnerable versions of Acrobat, Foxit, or other
  viewers.
\item
  \textbf{The attack techniques are public.} The Bochum team published
  detailed descriptions and proof-of-concept PDFs. Any attacker with
  basic PDF knowledge can reproduce the attacks.
\item
  \textbf{Shadow Attacks remain partially unresolved.} While most
  viewers now detect the Hide and Replace variants, the fundamental
  problem --- that PDF semantics allow content to be visually hidden ---
  is inherent to the format. New variants of Shadow Attacks are likely
  still discoverable.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{The Patch Response: What
Changed}\label{_the_patch_response_what_changed}

\subsubsection{Adobe's Response}\label{_adobes_response}

Adobe's patches for CVE-2020-9592 and CVE-2020-9596 (released in the May
2020 Acrobat security update, version 2020.006.20042) implemented:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Cryptographic signature verification} --- closing the USF
  vulnerability by ensuring the CMS signature value is always verified
  against the document hash
\item
  \textbf{Byte range coverage validation} --- checking that the
  \texttt{/ByteRange} covers the entire file from offset 0 to the file's
  end (minus the \texttt{/Contents} gap), closing the SWA vulnerability
\item
  \textbf{Incremental update analysis} --- warning users when an
  incremental update modifies content beyond what the signature's
  permission level allows, closing the ISA vulnerability
\item
  \textbf{Shadow content detection} --- partial detection of Hidden
  content patterns (overlapping objects, OCGs with suspicious visibility
  settings)
\end{enumerate}

Acrobat's signature panel now displays more granular information:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  "Signed and all signatures are valid" --- no modifications detected
\item
  "Signed but the document has been modified since signing" ---
  incremental updates detected, with details about what changed
\item
  "At least one signature has problems" --- cryptographic or structural
  verification failed
\item
  "The document has been altered or corrupted since the signature was
  applied" --- clear indication of tampering
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Foxit's Response}\label{_foxits_response}

Foxit Reader patched its signature validation in version 10.1 (released
October 2020), implementing similar checks: CMS verification, byte range
validation, and incremental update analysis. Foxit was notably slower
than Adobe in responding --- roughly five months after Adobe's patch.

\subsubsection{LibreOffice's Response}\label{_libreoffices_response}

LibreOffice (which uses the NSS library for cryptographic operations)
patched its PDF signature validation in version 7.0. The fix was more
complex because LibreOffice's PDF import/export pipeline differs
significantly from dedicated PDF viewers --- it converts PDF to an
internal format for editing, which complicates byte-level signature
verification.

\subsubsection{The Broader Industry}\label{_the_broader_industry}

By mid-2021, most actively maintained PDF viewers had released patches
addressing the three original attack classes (USF, ISA, SWA). Shadow
Attack mitigations were slower to arrive and remain incomplete in some
viewers as of 2026.

The Bochum research also influenced the standards bodies:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{ISO TC 171} (the committee responsible for ISO 32000-2:2020)
  incorporated the attack findings into the errata and clarifications
  for the second edition of PDF 2.0
\item
  \textbf{ETSI} updated the PAdES standard (EN 319 142-1 V1.2.1,
  published 2022) to include explicit requirements for byte range
  coverage verification and incremental update validation
\item
  \textbf{The EU Digital Signature Service (DSS)} --- the open-source
  reference validator maintained by the European Commission --- was
  patched and now includes specific test cases for each attack class
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Building a Robust Validator: Lessons from the
Attacks}\label{_building_a_robust_validator_lessons_from_the_attacks}

Every attack in this chapter exploits a specific gap between what a
viewer \emph{should} check and what it \emph{actually} checks. Here is
the complete checklist, informed by every vulnerability discussed above.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\caption{PDF Signature Validation Checklist
(post-Bochum)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\#
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Check
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Defends Against
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\#
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Check
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Defends Against
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
1 & Parse the CMS \texttt{SignedData} structure and verify it is
well-formed (correct OIDs, consistent version numbers, all required
fields present) & USF Variant 2 \\
2 & Verify the \texttt{SignerInfo.signature} value cryptographically
against the signer's public key and the computed digest & USF Variant
1 \\
3 & Verify the message digest in the signed attributes matches the hash
of the byte range content & USF Variant 3, SWA \\
4 & Verify \texttt{/ByteRange} starts at offset 0 & SWA \\
5 & Verify \texttt{/ByteRange} gap contains the \texttt{/Contents} hex
string & SWA \\
6 & Verify \texttt{/ByteRange} covers the entire file (for the signed
revision) --- no unaccounted bytes before, between, or after the ranges
& SWA, ISA \\
7 & If the file extends beyond the signed byte range, parse the
incremental update and determine what changed & ISA \\
8 & Verify that any changes in the incremental update are permissible
under the signature's DocMDP permission level (P=1: no changes; P=2:
form fills + signatures; P=3: form fills + signatures + annotations) &
ISA Variant 3 \\
9 & Verify the certificate chain from the signer's certificate to a
trusted root, checking revocation (OCSP/CRL) and validity dates &
Certificate forgery, CA breaches \\
10 & If a timestamp is present, verify the TSA certificate chain
independently and check that the timestamp covers the correct data &
Backdated signatures \\
11 & Check for hidden content patterns: overlapping objects, Optional
Content Groups with invisible layers, unreferenced Form XObjects, form
fields with discrepant default/actual values & Shadow Attacks (Hide,
Replace) \\
12 & Ensure the digest algorithm is SHA-256 or stronger --- reject SHA-1
& SHAttered, hash collision attacks \\
\end{longtable}

Checks 1--6 are non-negotiable --- any validator that skips them is
vulnerable to at least one of USF, ISA, or SWA. Checks 7--10 are
necessary for real-world security but require more complex
implementation. Checks 11--12 are defense-in-depth against attacks that
remain partially unresolved.

No checklist is complete. The Bochum team found these attack classes by
thinking adversarially about the gap between what the spec
\emph{requires} and what implementations \emph{do}. There will be more
attacks. The defense is not just implementing these twelve checks --- it
is building a culture of adversarial testing into your validation code.

\subsection{A Note on Responsible
Disclosure}\label{_a_note_on_responsible_disclosure}

The Bochum team followed responsible disclosure for both papers. They
notified affected vendors months before publication and provided
proof-of-concept PDFs for testing.

For the CCS 2019 paper, the disclosure timeline was approximately:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{March--April 2019}: Initial vendor notifications
\item
  \textbf{May--August 2019}: Back-and-forth with vendors; some requested
  extended timelines; some did not respond
\item
  \textbf{November 2019}: Paper presented at CCS; public disclosure
\item
  \textbf{May 2020}: Adobe patches released (CVE-2020-9592,
  CVE-2020-9596)
\item
  \textbf{October 2020}: Foxit patches released
\end{itemize}

The six-month gap between public disclosure and Adobe's patch --- and
the eleven-month gap before Foxit's patch --- is notable. The Bochum
team publicly criticized the slow response, particularly from viewers
that took more than six months to patch after detailed proof-of-concept
material was shared privately.

For the NDSS 2021 paper (Shadow Attacks), the disclosure process was
similar but vendors responded faster, having already been through the
CCS 2019 cycle.

The research materials --- including proof-of-concept PDFs, the
vulnerability matrix, and detailed attack descriptions --- remain
available at \textbf{pdf-insecurity.org}. The Hackmanit GmbH \textbf{PDF
Detector} tool (available as a web service and standalone library) can
check PDFs for known attack patterns.

\subsection{Summary}\label{_summary_10}

PDF signature security before 2019 was, to put it bluntly, an illusion.
The cryptographic primitives were sound --- RSA, ECDSA, SHA-256, CMS are
all well-designed and well-analyzed. The failure was entirely in how PDF
viewers \emph{used} those primitives. They verified certificates
meticulously and signatures not at all.

The Bochum research (CCS 2019, NDSS 2021) exposed four attack classes:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{USF}: The viewer doesn't check the cryptographic signature
  value --- forge anything
\item
  \textbf{ISA}: The viewer doesn't check for modifications after signing
  --- append anything
\item
  \textbf{SWA}: The viewer doesn't validate the byte range --- redirect
  it to anything
\item
  \textbf{Shadow Attacks}: The viewer doesn't analyze what the signed
  content \emph{means} --- hide anything
\end{itemize}

Every major PDF viewer was vulnerable. The patches took months to years.
Some Shadow Attack variants remain partially unresolved.

Earlier events --- the DigiNotar breach (2011), the Comodo RA compromise
(2011), the Symantec distrust (2017--2018), and the SHAttered SHA-1
collision (2017) --- had already weakened the trust infrastructure
underneath PDF signatures. The Bochum attacks showed that even if you
trusted the infrastructure, the validators themselves couldn't be
trusted.

The lesson for implementers is clear: \textbf{verify everything, trust
nothing, and test adversarially}. The validation checklist in this
chapter is the minimum. The next attack class is already being
researched.

\chapter{Certificate Authority
Breaches}\label{_certificate_authority_breaches}

Every PDF digital signature carries an implicit assertion: "I trust the
certificate authority that issued this certificate." That assertion is
not a mathematical fact --- it is an organizational, operational, and
political judgment. And like all judgments, it can turn out to be
catastrophically wrong.

This chapter chronicles the most consequential certificate authority
failures of the past two decades. Not because they are historical
curiosities, but because each one contains lessons that directly affect
how you should build, validate, and maintain PDF digital signatures
today. A CA breach does not just compromise web traffic. It
retroactively undermines every document signed with certificates issued
by that CA --- contracts, tax filings, court submissions, medical
records. The signatures do not become invalid in a cryptographic sense;
the math still checks out. They become \emph{untrustworthy}, which in
the real world is worse.

\section{Why CA Breaches Matter for PDF
Signatures}\label{_why_ca_breaches_matter_for_pdf_signatures}

When you sign a PDF, the recipient validates your signature by chaining
your signing certificate back to a trusted root. That chain passes
through one or more intermediate CAs, each vouching for the next, until
it reaches a root certificate that the validator explicitly trusts ---
either because it is in Adobe's AATL, in the EU Trusted List, in the
operating system's trust store, or in a custom trust configuration.

The entire chain is only as strong as its weakest link. If any CA in the
chain is compromised, an attacker can issue fraudulent certificates ---
certificates that look legitimate but are controlled by the attacker.
Documents signed with those fraudulent certificates will pass validation
in any viewer that trusts the compromised CA.

Worse, when a compromised CA is discovered and revoked, the collateral
damage extends to \emph{every} legitimate certificate that CA ever
issued. If you signed a contract last year with a certificate from CA-X,
and CA-X is discovered to be compromised this year, your signature's
trustworthiness is now in question --- not because your key was
compromised, but because the entity vouching for your identity can no
longer be trusted.

This is the fundamental fragility of the PKI trust model: trust is
transitive, delegated, and revocable. You cannot sign a document and
walk away confident that the signature will still be trusted in five
years, because you cannot predict whether the CA that issued your
certificate will still be trusted in five years.

The only mitigation for CA compromise is \textbf{embedding revocation
evidence at signing time}. A PAdES-B-LTA signature includes OCSP
responses and CRLs captured when the signature was created. If the CA is
later revoked, the embedded evidence proves that the certificate was
valid \emph{at the time of signing} --- before the breach was
discovered. Without LTV data, a validator checking the signature after
the CA revocation has no way to distinguish between a legitimate
pre-breach signature and a fraudulent one.

\section{DigiNotar (2011): The Breach That Broke a
Country}\label{_diginotar_2011_the_breach_that_broke_a_country}

\subsection{Background: A Small CA With a Big
Responsibility}\label{_background_a_small_ca_with_a_big_responsibility}

DigiNotar was a Dutch certificate authority founded in 1998,
headquartered in Beverwijk, a small town near Amsterdam. The company was
originally an offshoot of the Dutch notarial profession --- the name is
a portmanteau of "Digital Notary" --- and it carved out a niche issuing
certificates for Dutch government services, notaries, and businesses.

In 2010, VASCO Data Security International (now OneSpan) acquired
DigiNotar for approximately 10 million euros. At the time, DigiNotar was
one of the root CAs trusted by every major browser and operating system.
Its certificates were embedded in the trust stores of Windows, macOS,
Firefox, Chrome, and by extension, in the trust stores used by PDF
viewers.

DigiNotar also operated \textbf{PKIoverheid} (PKI for Government) ---
the Dutch government's public key infrastructure. PKIoverheid
certificates were used across Dutch government digital services,
including the DigiD electronic identity system, tax filing, and ---
critically for our purposes --- official government document signing.

\subsection{The Attack}\label{_the_attack}

In June 2011, an attacker gained access to DigiNotar's certificate
issuance infrastructure. The attack was later attributed to a
state-sponsored actor operating from Iran, though the attacker also
posted messages claiming to be a lone Iranian hacker named
"Comodohacker" --- the same handle used in the Comodo breach earlier
that year.

The attacker exploited multiple vulnerabilities in DigiNotar's
infrastructure:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The CA's servers ran outdated, unpatched software
\item
  Network segmentation between the public-facing servers and the
  certificate issuance systems was inadequate
\item
  The HSMs (Hardware Security Modules) that should have required
  physical authorization for certificate issuance were configured to
  allow automated remote issuance
\item
  Audit logs were incomplete and inconsistent --- DigiNotar did not have
  reliable records of which certificates had been issued
\end{itemize}

Once inside, the attacker issued over \textbf{531 fraudulent
certificates} for high-value domains. The confirmed targets included:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}@{}}
\caption{Known fraudulently issued DigiNotar certificates (partial
list)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Domain
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Significance
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Domain
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Significance
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
google.com, www.google.com & Gmail, Google Search --- primary
communication tool for millions \\
login.yahoo.com & Yahoo Mail \\
login.live.com & Microsoft Hotmail/Outlook \\
addons.mozilla.org & Firefox extension repository \\
login.skype.com & Skype communications \\
global.sts.microsoft.com & Microsoft federation services \\
www.sis.gov.uk & UK Secret Intelligence Service (MI6) \\
www.cia.gov & US Central Intelligence Agency \\
mossad.gov.il & Israeli intelligence service \\
azadegi.com & Iranian opposition news website \\
Several wildcard certs & Covering entire TLDs and subdomains \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{The Use: Surveillance of Iranian
Citizens}\label{_the_use_surveillance_of_iranian_citizens}

The fraudulent certificates were not issued for financial fraud. They
were used for \textbf{mass surveillance}.

In July and August 2011, the fraudulent \texttt{google.com} certificate
was deployed in man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks targeting Iranian
internet users. The Iranian government (or an actor with access to
Iranian internet infrastructure) intercepted HTTPS connections to Google
services --- primarily Gmail --- by presenting the fraudulent DigiNotar
certificate. Because DigiNotar was a trusted root CA, browsers accepted
the certificate without warning.

The victims were Iranian citizens communicating via Gmail during a
period of significant political tension. The Arab Spring protests had
swept through Tunisia, Egypt, Libya, and Syria. Iran's Green Movement,
which had erupted after the disputed 2009 presidential election, was
still active. The surveillance targeted people who thought their
communications were private.

The scale of the interception was significant. Fox-IT, the Dutch
forensics firm that later investigated the breach, estimated that
approximately \textbf{300,000 unique IP addresses} --- almost all
Iranian --- had been subjected to MITM attacks using the fraudulent
Google certificate.

\subsection{Discovery: How Certificate Pinning Saved the
Day}\label{_discovery_how_certificate_pinning_saved_the_day}

The attack was discovered on August 28, 2011, by an Iranian user of
Google Chrome.

Chrome has a feature called \textbf{certificate pinning} (also known as
HPKP, HTTP Public Key Pinning, or in Chrome's case, a hardcoded pin
set). For a small number of high-value domains --- including google.com
--- Chrome maintains a built-in list of expected certificate public
keys. If the presented certificate's public key does not match any of
the pinned keys, Chrome refuses the connection and displays a prominent
error, even if the certificate otherwise validates correctly against the
trust store.

When the Iranian Chrome user attempted to connect to Google, Chrome
detected that the presented certificate (signed by DigiNotar) did not
match Google's pinned public keys. Chrome displayed an error. The user
reported the error to Google.

Certificate pinning was not designed as a comprehensive defense against
CA compromise. It only worked for the handful of domains that Google had
pre-configured pins for. If the attacker had only issued certificates
for domains that Chrome did not pin --- the intelligence agency
websites, the Iranian opposition news site, the Yahoo and Skype domains
--- the attack might have continued undetected for months.

The lesson is humbling: the discovery of one of the most significant CA
compromises in internet history was essentially an accident --- it
depended on one user, in one country, using one specific browser,
connecting to one specific domain that happened to have certificate
pinning enabled.

\subsection{The Response: Death of a
CA}\label{_the_response_death_of_a_ca}

Once Google confirmed the fraudulent certificate, events moved rapidly:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{August 29, 2011}: Google published a Chrome update blocking
  the specific DigiNotar intermediate certificate used to sign the
  fraudulent Google certificate
\item
  \textbf{August 30, 2011}: Mozilla pushed a Firefox update removing
  DigiNotar's root from the trust store entirely
\item
  \textbf{September 2, 2011}: The Dutch government's GovCERT (Government
  Computer Emergency Response Team) issued an advisory acknowledging the
  breach
\item
  \textbf{September 3, 2011}: Google, Microsoft, and Apple all pushed
  updates removing DigiNotar from their trust stores
\item
  \textbf{September 5, 2011}: The Dutch government formally revoked
  DigiNotar's authorization to issue PKIoverheid certificates
\item
  \textbf{September 20, 2011}: DigiNotar's parent company VASCO filed
  for DigiNotar's bankruptcy in Dutch court
\end{itemize}

The entire collapse --- from discovery to bankruptcy --- took less than
four weeks.

Fox-IT's forensic investigation, published in their report "DigiNotar
Certificate Authority breach --- \textquotesingle Operation Black
Tulip\textquotesingle" (named with Dutch irony), found that the breach
was far more extensive than initially reported. The attacker had been
inside DigiNotar's systems since at least June 17, 2011, had compromised
multiple CA servers, and had access to issuing infrastructure for both
DigiNotar's commercial certificates and the PKIoverheid government
certificates.

\subsection{Impact on PDF Signatures}\label{_impact_on_pdf_signatures}

The immediate impact on PDF signatures was severe and irreversible.

Any PDF document signed with a DigiNotar-issued certificate became
\textbf{unverifiable} the moment browsers and operating systems removed
DigiNotar from their trust stores. Adobe Acrobat, which uses the Windows
certificate store (on Windows) or its own AATL (on macOS), stopped
trusting DigiNotar certificates. A signed PDF that showed a green
checkmark on September 2 showed a warning on September 4.

The documents themselves had not changed. The signatures had not been
tampered with. The signing keys had not been compromised (in most cases
--- the legitimate certificate holders were not the ones attacked). But
the trust anchor for those certificates no longer existed.

For Dutch government agencies, this was particularly painful.
PKIoverheid certificates were used to sign official government
documents, notarial deeds, and legal filings. The Dutch government had
to issue emergency replacement certificates through other CAs and
re-sign critical documents --- a process that took months.

This is the nightmare scenario for long-lived signed documents. A
contract signed in 2010 with a DigiNotar certificate and stored in an
archive had its trust chain severed in 2011. If the signature did not
include LTV data (OCSP responses and CRLs embedded at signing time, as
in PAdES-B-LTA), there was \emph{no way} to prove after the fact that
the certificate was valid when the signature was created. The document
was, for all practical purposes, unsigned.

This is why PAdES-B-LTA exists. This is why you embed revocation data.
This is not a theoretical concern --- it happened.

\subsection{Lessons from DigiNotar}\label{_lessons_from_diginotar}

The DigiNotar breach taught the PKI community several painful lessons:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{A single CA compromise can retroactively invalidate thousands
  of signatures.} Trust is not permanent. It is not even stable. It can
  be revoked at any time, with retrospective effect.
\item
  \textbf{CA security is only as strong as the weakest operational
  practice.} DigiNotar had HSMs, but they were misconfigured. They had
  audit logs, but they were incomplete. They had network segmentation,
  but it was insufficient. The technology existed to prevent the breach;
  the organization failed to deploy it correctly.
\item
  \textbf{Certificate pinning is a band-aid, not a solution.} It helped
  detect \emph{this} breach, but it does not scale. You cannot pin every
  domain's certificate in every application.
\item
  \textbf{Government PKI is not immune.} DigiNotar operated critical
  Dutch government infrastructure. Government CAs are not inherently
  more trustworthy than commercial ones --- they are subject to the same
  operational failures.
\item
  \textbf{Bankruptcy is the final revocation.} When a CA goes bankrupt,
  its certificates do not gracefully expire. They are abruptly
  distrusted. There is no migration path, no deprecation period, no
  gentle sunset. One day the certificates work; the next day they do
  not.
\end{enumerate}

\section{Comodo (2011): The Parallel
Breach}\label{_comodo_2011_the_parallel_breach}

\subsection{The Attack}\label{_the_attack_2}

Just five months before DigiNotar's collapse, in March 2011, a similar
attack targeted Comodo --- at the time, one of the world's largest
commercial CAs.

The attacker compromised \textbf{registration authority (RA) accounts}
at Comodo's reseller partners --- specifically, the Italian firm
GlobalTrust.it and the Argentine firm InstantSSL.it. Registration
authorities are entities authorized to perform identity verification on
behalf of a CA; they can request certificate issuance without having
direct access to the CA's root key.

Using the compromised RA credentials, the attacker obtained \textbf{nine
fraudulent certificates} for high-value domains:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  login.live.com (Microsoft)
\item
  mail.google.com (Google)
\item
  www.google.com (Google)
\item
  login.yahoo.com (Yahoo, three separate certificates)
\item
  login.skype.com (Skype)
\item
  addons.mozilla.org (Mozilla)
\item
  An additional "global trustee" certificate
\end{itemize}

The target list was remarkably similar to the DigiNotar targets ---
because the same attacker was behind both breaches.

\subsection{The Response: How Comodo Got It
Right}\label{_the_response_how_comodo_got_it_right}

Comodo's response was dramatically faster and more effective than
DigiNotar's would be five months later.

Comodo detected the fraudulent issuance \textbf{within hours} --- their
monitoring systems flagged the unusual certificate requests from the
compromised RA accounts. The nine certificates were revoked within the
same day. Comodo published a detailed incident report, contacted browser
vendors, and cooperated fully with the investigation.

The key differences between Comodo's response and DigiNotar's later
failure:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\caption{Comodo vs DigiNotar incident response
comparison}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Factor
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Comodo
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
DigiNotar
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Factor
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Comodo
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
DigiNotar
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Detection time & Hours & Weeks (possibly months) \\
Number of fraudulent certs & 9 & 531+ \\
Disclosure & Immediate and transparent & Delayed and incomplete \\
Certificate revocation & Same day & Gradual, over weeks \\
Root trust impact & Root preserved (reseller revoked) & Entire root
removed \\
Business outcome & Continued operating & Bankruptcy \\
CA audit logs & Complete and useful & Incomplete and unreliable \\
\end{longtable}

Comodo survived because it detected the breach quickly, responded
transparently, and demonstrated that the compromise was limited to
reseller accounts --- not the root CA infrastructure. The attackers got
nine certificates; at DigiNotar, they got the keys to the kingdom.

\subsection{The Attacker's Manifesto}\label{_the_attackers_manifesto}

The attacker posted a manifesto online claiming to be a 21-year-old
Iranian, acting alone, motivated by anger at Western
governments\textquotesingle{} treatment of Iran. The manifesto, written
in broken English, contained enough technical detail about both the
Comodo and DigiNotar breaches to be credible. Whether the attacker was
truly a lone wolf or a front for state-sponsored activity remains
debated. The operational sophistication of the MITM infrastructure
deployed against Iranian citizens (requiring cooperation with or access
to Iranian ISP infrastructure) suggests state involvement, regardless of
who wrote the code.

From a PDF signing perspective, the Comodo incident reinforces a
critical point: \textbf{your signing certificate's trustworthiness
depends not only on your CA, but on every reseller, registration
authority, and partner in the CA's issuance chain}. When selecting a CA
for document signing certificates, ask about their RA model. How many
resellers can request certificate issuance? What monitoring exists for
anomalous issuance patterns? How quickly can fraudulent certificates be
detected and revoked?

\section{Symantec Distrust (2017-2018): The Slow-Motion
Collapse}\label{_symantec_distrust_2017_2018_the_slow_motion_collapse}

\subsection{Background: The World's Largest
CA}\label{_background_the_worlds_largest_ca}

If DigiNotar was a small CA that died quickly, Symantec was a giant that
died slowly.

Through a series of acquisitions --- \textbf{VeriSign} (2010),
\textbf{Thawte} (via VeriSign), \textbf{GeoTrust} (via VeriSign), and
\textbf{RapidSSL} (via GeoTrust) --- Symantec had become the world's
largest certificate authority by 2015. At its peak, Symantec and its
subsidiary brands had issued approximately \textbf{30\% of all TLS
certificates on the public internet}.

That market share made Symantec's CA operation systemically important.
It also made it a tempting target for scrutiny.

\subsection{The Mis-Issuance Scandal}\label{_the_mis_issuance_scandal}

In September 2015, Google's Certificate Transparency (CT) monitoring
detected that Symantec had issued several test certificates for
google.com domains --- without Google's authorization. Symantec claimed
these were test certificates issued by employees during internal
testing, and that they had been revoked quickly.

Google investigated further and discovered that the problem was much
larger:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Symantec had issued \textbf{at least 164 certificates} for domains
  without proper authorization
\item
  Some were issued by Symantec employees, others by RA partners
  (including CrossCert, a Korean RA)
\item
  The unauthorized issuance spanned multiple years
\item
  Symantec's internal controls had failed to detect the mis-issuances
\item
  When confronted, Symantec's initial disclosures underreported the
  scope of the problem
\end{itemize}

In March 2017, Google engineers Ryan Sleevi and Devon O'Brien published
a detailed analysis on the Chromium bug tracker, proposing a phased
distrust of all Symantec-issued certificates. The proposal was
unprecedented: no one had ever suggested distrusting certificates from
the world's largest CA.

\subsection{The Distrust Timeline}\label{_the_distrust_timeline}

What followed was a two-year negotiation between Google, Mozilla,
Symantec, and the broader CA/Browser Forum. The timeline:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{March 2017}: Google proposes phased Symantec distrust in
  Chrome
\item
  \textbf{April 2017}: Symantec contests the proposal, arguing the
  issues were limited
\item
  \textbf{July 2017}: Google, Mozilla, and Symantec reach a compromise
  plan: Symantec must transition to a new infrastructure operated by a
  different CA
\item
  \textbf{August 2017}: DigiCert announces acquisition of Symantec's CA
  business for approximately \$950 million
\item
  \textbf{December 2017}: DigiCert completes the acquisition and begins
  re-issuing certificates under DigiCert roots
\item
  \textbf{April 2018}: Chrome 66 distrusts Symantec certificates issued
  before June 1, 2016
\item
  \textbf{October 2018}: Chrome 70 distrusts ALL remaining Symantec
  certificates
\item
  \textbf{October 2018}: Firefox follows with similar distrust in
  Firefox 63
\end{itemize}

The distrust was progressive: older certificates were distrusted first,
giving website operators time to obtain replacement certificates. But
the endpoint was absolute: after October 2018, no Symantec-issued
certificate was trusted by Chrome or Firefox.

\subsection{Impact on PDF Signatures}\label{_impact_on_pdf_signatures_2}

The Symantec distrust had a less dramatic but more insidious impact on
PDF signatures than the DigiNotar breach.

DigiNotar's collapse was sudden --- trust was revoked in days, and the
relatively small number of affected certificates limited the blast
radius. Symantec's distrust was gradual, but the scale was enormous.

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Thousands of document signing certificates} had been issued
  under Symantec, VeriSign, Thawte, and GeoTrust brands
\item
  Many of these certificates were in Adobe's AATL (Adobe Approved Trust
  List)
\item
  As Symantec certificates were distrusted, PDF signatures chaining to
  those certificates showed warnings in validators that followed
  Chrome/Firefox trust decisions
\item
  Adobe's trust store (AATL) moved independently from browsers --- Adobe
  did not automatically distrust Symantec certificates just because
  Chrome did
\item
  This created a confusing situation where a signed PDF might validate
  in Acrobat (using AATL) but fail in a web-based validator (using the
  browser trust store)
\end{itemize}

The Symantec case illustrates an important distinction: \textbf{PDF
trust stores and web browser trust stores are not the same thing}. Adobe
Acrobat maintains its own trust list (AATL), which is updated on its own
schedule, with its own criteria. A certificate can be distrusted by
Chrome but still trusted by Acrobat --- or vice versa. This is both a
feature (PDF signatures are not automatically affected by browser trust
decisions) and a bug (trust status becomes inconsistent across different
validation contexts).

The transition to DigiCert mitigated some of the damage --- DigiCert
re-issued certificates under its own roots, and existing Symantec
customers could obtain replacement certificates. But for documents
already signed with Symantec-chain certificates, the question of
long-term trust remained unresolved.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Checks whether a signing certificate chains to a currently trusted root}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ This is the kind of check you need to perform when validating old signatures}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ whose CAs may have been distrusted since signing time}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public}\NormalTok{ CertificateChainStatus }\FunctionTok{validateChainTrust}\OperatorTok{(}
        \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ signerCert}\OperatorTok{,}
        \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ chain}\OperatorTok{,}
        \BuiltInTok{Set}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{TrustAnchor}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ currentTrustAnchors}\OperatorTok{,}
        \BuiltInTok{Date}\NormalTok{ signingTime}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

    \CommentTok{// Build the certificate path}
    \BuiltInTok{CertificateFactory}\NormalTok{ cf }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{CertificateFactory}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"X.509"}\OperatorTok{);}
    \BuiltInTok{CertPath}\NormalTok{ certPath }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ cf}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{generateCertPath}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{chain}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// Configure the path validator with the signing time}
    \BuiltInTok{PKIXParameters}\NormalTok{ params }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{PKIXParameters}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{currentTrustAnchors}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    params}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signingTime}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// Validate as of signing time, not now}
\NormalTok{    params}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setRevocationEnabled}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{);}  \CommentTok{// We check revocation separately}

    \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{CertPathValidator}\NormalTok{ validator }\OperatorTok{=} \BuiltInTok{CertPathValidator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"PKIX"}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{PKIXCertPathValidatorResult}\NormalTok{ result }\OperatorTok{=}
                \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{PKIXCertPathValidatorResult}\OperatorTok{)}\NormalTok{ validator}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{validate}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certPath}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ params}\OperatorTok{);}

        \BuiltInTok{TrustAnchor}\NormalTok{ anchor }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ result}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTrustAnchor}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Certificate chain valid at signing time. Trust anchor: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                anchor}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTrustedCert}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getSubjectX500Principal}\OperatorTok{());}

        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ CertificateChainStatus}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{builder}\OperatorTok{()}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{valid}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{true}\OperatorTok{)}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{trustAnchor}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{anchor}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getTrustedCert}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getSubjectX500Principal}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getName}\OperatorTok{())}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{validationTime}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signingTime}\OperatorTok{)}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{();}

    \OperatorTok{\}} \ControlFlowTok{catch} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{CertPathValidatorException}\NormalTok{ e}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{        log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{warn}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Certificate chain validation failed at index \{\}: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                e}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getIndex}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ e}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getMessage}\OperatorTok{());}

        \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ CertificateChainStatus}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{builder}\OperatorTok{()}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{valid}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{false}\OperatorTok{)}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{failureReason}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{e}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getMessage}\OperatorTok{())}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{failedCertificateIndex}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{e}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getIndex}\OperatorTok{())}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{validationTime}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signingTime}\OperatorTok{)}
                \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{();}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Let's Encrypt: The Free CA's Growing
Pains}\label{_lets_encrypt_the_free_cas_growing_pains}

\subsection{The 2020 Mass Revocation}\label{_the_2020_mass_revocation}

Let's Encrypt, the free, automated CA that has issued over three billion
certificates since its launch in 2015, does not issue document signing
certificates. Its certificates are exclusively for TLS (web server) use.
So why does a Let's Encrypt incident appear in a book about PDF
signatures?

Because the 2020 mass revocation event demonstrated something important
about the fragility of CA infrastructure at scale --- a fragility that
applies equally to every CA in the PDF signing ecosystem.

On March 3, 2020, Let's Encrypt announced that it had discovered a bug
in its \textbf{CAA (Certificate Authority Authorization) checking code}.
CAA is a DNS-based mechanism that allows domain owners to specify which
CAs are authorized to issue certificates for their domain. Before
issuing a certificate, a CA must check the domain's CAA records and
refuse issuance if the domain specifies a different CA.

The bug: Let's Encrypt's validation system checked CAA records when a
domain was first validated, but did not re-check them when issuing a
certificate if the validation result was cached. This meant that if a
domain's CAA record changed after the initial validation (for example,
to explicitly prohibit Let's Encrypt from issuing certificates), Let's
Encrypt would still issue the certificate based on the stale cached
result.

This violated the CA/Browser Forum's Baseline Requirements, which
mandate that CAA records be checked within 8 hours of certificate
issuance.

\subsection{The Chaos}\label{_the_chaos}

Let's Encrypt identified approximately \textbf{3,048,289 certificates}
that had been issued without a compliant CAA check at issuance time. The
Baseline Requirements required these certificates to be revoked within 5
days.

Let's Encrypt gave certificate holders \textbf{24 hours} to renew their
certificates before revocation. For the ACME-based automated renewal
ecosystem that Let's Encrypt had built, this should have been manageable
--- the whole point of Let's Encrypt is automated certificate
management.

In practice, it was chaos. Not every certificate holder had functional
automated renewal. Not every server was properly configured for ACME.
Some certificates were on devices that could not easily be updated. The
announcement came on a Tuesday evening (US time), giving operators one
business day to respond.

\subsection{Relevance to PDF Signing}\label{_relevance_to_pdf_signing}

The Let's Encrypt incident did not directly affect any PDF signatures.
But it demonstrated three principles that every PDF signing architect
should internalize:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Scale amplifies errors.} A single bug in a CA's issuance
  pipeline can affect millions of certificates. The larger the CA, the
  larger the blast radius. When choosing a CA for document signing,
  understand that the CA's operational quality affects every certificate
  they issue --- including yours.
\item
  \textbf{Revocation at scale is operationally brutal.} Even with 24
  hours notice and automated tooling, millions of certificates could not
  be renewed in time. Now imagine the same scenario with document
  signing certificates, where "renewal" means re-signing every document
  that was signed with the revoked certificate. There is no automated
  tool for that.
\item
  \textbf{Compliance requirements drive revocation timelines.} The
  Baseline Requirements gave Let's Encrypt no discretion --- the
  certificates \emph{had} to be revoked within 5 days, regardless of the
  impact. EU regulations impose similar requirements on Qualified Trust
  Service Providers. If a QTSP discovers a compliance issue, it may be
  required to revoke certificates on a timeline that does not allow for
  graceful migration.
\end{enumerate}

If your QTSP discovers a compliance issue and is required to revoke your
signing certificate, every PDF you signed with that certificate becomes
suspect --- unless you embedded LTV data at signing time. This is not a
far-fetched scenario. It has happened to web certificates. It will
happen to document signing certificates. Plan accordingly.

\section{Certificate Transparency: The Response to CA
Failures}\label{_certificate_transparency_the_response_to_ca_failures}

\subsection{What Certificate Transparency
Is}\label{_what_certificate_transparency_is}

Certificate Transparency (CT) was invented by Google engineers
\textbf{Ben Laurie} and \textbf{Adam Langley} in 2012 (published as RFC
5280 --- Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile companion
document RFC 6962, and later updated in RFC 9162), directly in response
to the DigiNotar and Comodo breaches.

The core insight: if CAs cannot be trusted to only issue authorized
certificates, we need a public audit mechanism that makes \emph{every}
issued certificate visible to domain owners and the broader community.

CT works as follows:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  When a CA issues a certificate, it submits the certificate to one or
  more \textbf{CT logs} --- append-only, cryptographically verifiable
  public databases
\item
  The CT log returns a \textbf{Signed Certificate Timestamp (SCT)} --- a
  promise that the certificate will be included in the log within a
  specified time (the "Maximum Merge Delay," typically 24 hours)
\item
  The SCT is embedded in the certificate (as an X.509 extension), in the
  TLS handshake (as a TLS extension), or in the OCSP response (as an
  OCSP extension)
\item
  Browsers verify that certificates come with valid SCTs --- if a
  certificate has no SCT, Chrome treats it as untrusted (since April
  2018)
\item
  Domain owners (and anyone else) can monitor CT logs for certificates
  issued for their domains --- if an unauthorized certificate appears,
  they can demand revocation
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{How CT Detects Rogue
Certificates}\label{_how_ct_detects_rogue_certificates}

CT does not \emph{prevent} a compromised CA from issuing rogue
certificates. It makes rogue certificates \emph{detectable}.

If the DigiNotar attack had happened in a CT world, the fraudulent
\texttt{google.com} certificate would have appeared in public CT logs
within 24 hours of issuance. Google's CT monitoring infrastructure would
have detected it immediately --- not because a user in Iran happened to
be running Chrome, but because Google continuously monitors every CT log
for certificates mentioning google.com.

The detection would have been systematic, automatic, and fast.

\subsection{CT and PDF: Limited but
Growing}\label{_ct_and_pdf_limited_but_growing}

Certificate Transparency was designed for the web PKI ecosystem. Its
adoption in the document signing world has been slower:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Document signing certificates are not required to have SCTs.}
  The CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements mandate CT for TLS
  certificates, but no equivalent mandate exists for document signing
  certificates.
\item
  \textbf{Adobe's AATL does not require CT.} CAs in the AATL are not
  required to log their document signing certificates in CT logs.
\item
  \textbf{The EU Trusted List does not reference CT.} The eIDAS
  regulatory framework does not mention Certificate Transparency.
\end{itemize}

However, CT is increasingly relevant for PDF signatures:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Some CAs voluntarily log their document signing certificates in CT
  logs
\item
  Organizations building their own signature validation can incorporate
  CT log monitoring as an additional trust signal
\item
  The EUDI Wallet infrastructure (Chapter 27) may incorporate CT-like
  transparency mechanisms for wallet-issued certificates
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Fetches and embeds revocation data }\CommentTok{(}\NormalTok{OCSP and CRL}\CommentTok{)}\NormalTok{ at signing time}\CommentTok{,}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ creating the foundation for long}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{term validation that survives CA revocation}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ This is the essential defense against CA breach impact}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public}\NormalTok{ LtvData }\FunctionTok{collectRevocationData}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ chain}\OperatorTok{)}
        \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

    \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ ocspResponses }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}();}
    \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ crls }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ArrayList}\OperatorTok{\textless{}\textgreater{}();}

    \CommentTok{// For each certificate in the chain (except the root),}
    \CommentTok{// fetch OCSP response and/or CRL}
    \ControlFlowTok{for} \OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ i }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{0}\OperatorTok{;}\NormalTok{ i }\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{ chain}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{;}\NormalTok{ i}\OperatorTok{++)} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ cert }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ chain}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{i}\OperatorTok{);}
        \BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\NormalTok{ issuer }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ chain}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{get}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{i }\OperatorTok{+} \DecValTok{1}\OperatorTok{);}

        \CommentTok{// Try OCSP first (preferred — smaller, more specific)}
        \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ ocspResponse }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{fetchOcspResponse}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cert}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ issuer}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ocspResponse }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"OCSP response obtained for: \{\}"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                    cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSubjectX500Principal}\OperatorTok{());}
\NormalTok{            ocspResponses}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ocspResponse}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \CommentTok{// Also fetch CRL as fallback}
        \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ crl }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{fetchCrl}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cert}\OperatorTok{);}
        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crl }\OperatorTok{!=} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"CRL obtained for: \{\} (\{\} bytes)"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                    cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSubjectX500Principal}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ crl}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{            crls}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crl}\OperatorTok{);}
        \OperatorTok{\}}

        \ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ocspResponse }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null} \OperatorTok{\&\&}\NormalTok{ crl }\OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
\NormalTok{            log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{warn}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"No revocation data available for: \{\}. "} \OperatorTok{+}
                    \StringTok{"LTV validation may fail for this certificate."}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{                    cert}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSubjectX500Principal}\OperatorTok{());}
        \OperatorTok{\}}
    \OperatorTok{\}}

    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ LtvData}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{builder}\OperatorTok{()}
            \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{certificates}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{chain}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{stream}\OperatorTok{()}
                    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{map}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{c }\OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}} \OperatorTok{\{}
                        \ControlFlowTok{try} \OperatorTok{\{} \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ c}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getEncoded}\OperatorTok{();} \OperatorTok{\}}
                        \ControlFlowTok{catch} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Exception}\NormalTok{ e}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{} \ControlFlowTok{throw} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{RuntimeException}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{e}\OperatorTok{);} \OperatorTok{\}}
                    \OperatorTok{\})}
                    \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{collect}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Collectors}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toList}\OperatorTok{()))}
            \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{ocspResponses}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{ocspResponses}\OperatorTok{)}
            \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{crls}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{crls}\OperatorTok{)}
            \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{collectionTime}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{Date}\OperatorTok{())}
            \OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{build}\OperatorTok{();}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}

\section{What These Incidents Taught
Us}\label{_what_these_incidents_taught_us}

\subsection{Trust Is Fragile and
Revocable}\label{_trust_is_fragile_and_revocable}

The PKI model assumes that root CAs are trustworthy. DigiNotar proved
that assumption can fail catastrophically. Symantec proved it can fail
gradually. Comodo proved it can fail and be recovered from --- but only
if the CA responds competently.

For PDF signature architects, the lesson is: \textbf{design for CA
failure}. Assume that any CA in your signing certificate's chain might
be distrusted at some point in the future. Your architecture should
handle that scenario gracefully.

\subsection{Long-Term Validity Requires Embedded
Evidence}\label{_long_term_validity_requires_embedded_evidence}

The single most important defense against CA breach impact is
\textbf{embedding revocation data at signing time}.

A PAdES-B-LTA signature includes:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The complete certificate chain (signer, intermediates, root)
\item
  OCSP responses for every certificate in the chain, obtained at signing
  time
\item
  CRLs as fallback revocation evidence
\item
  A signature timestamp proving when the signature was created
\item
  A document timestamp proving when the validation data was embedded
\end{itemize}

With all this data embedded, a validator can determine that the signing
certificate was valid \emph{at the time of signing} --- even if the CA
is later revoked. The embedded OCSP response from 2024 proves the
certificate was not revoked in 2024, regardless of what happens to the
CA in 2025.

Without this data, the validator is left with an impossible question:
was this certificate valid when the signature was created? If the CA is
gone, there is no one to ask.

\subsection{You Cannot Predict CA
Longevity}\label{_you_cannot_predict_ca_longevity}

DigiNotar: bankrupt in 4 weeks. Symantec CA: sold and sunset in 18
months. Let's Encrypt: a compliance bug affecting 3 million
certificates.

When you select a CA for your document signing certificates, you are
making a bet on that CA's continued trustworthiness. History suggests
this bet is riskier than most organizations realize.

Practical mitigations:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Use PAdES-B-LTA for anything that matters.} The 5 seconds of
  extra processing time to fetch and embed revocation data is negligible
  compared to the cost of re-signing thousands of documents after a CA
  revocation.
\item
  \textbf{Have a certificate migration plan.} Know how you will
  transition to a new CA if your current CA is compromised, acquired, or
  distrusted. This includes having backup certificates from a second CA.
\item
  \textbf{Monitor your CA's compliance status.} Subscribe to CA/Browser
  Forum mailing lists, Google's security blog, and Mozilla's CA program
  communications. You want to know about problems with your CA before
  your customers do.
\item
  \textbf{Re-timestamp archived documents periodically.} A PAdES-B-LTA
  archival timestamp can be refreshed by adding a new document timestamp
  --- extending the signature's verifiability beyond the original TSA's
  certificate lifetime.
\end{enumerate}

\section{Timeline: Major CA Incidents
(2001-2024)}\label{_timeline_major_ca_incidents_2001_2024}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1429}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5714}}@{}}
\caption{Major CA security incidents and their impact on digital
signatures}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Year
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Incident
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Impact and Outcome
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Year
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Incident
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Impact and Outcome
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
2001 & VeriSign issues fraudulent Microsoft code-signing certs & Two
certificates issued to an impersonator claiming to be Microsoft. Revoked
quickly, but demonstrated that even the largest CA could be socially
engineered. Led to improved identity verification procedures. \\
2008 & Debian weak key vulnerability (CVE-2008-0166) & A Debian-specific
OpenSSL bug caused all keys generated on Debian/Ubuntu systems for 18
months to be predictable. Affected SSL certificates, SSH keys, and
potentially signing keys. Not a CA breach per se, but CAs had to revoke
certificates whose keys were generated on affected systems. \\
2011 (Mar) & Comodo reseller compromise & 9 fraudulent certificates
issued via compromised RA accounts. Revoked within hours. CA survived.
Attacker claimed Iranian motivation. \\
2011 (Jul-Sep) & DigiNotar breach & 531+ fraudulent certificates. Used
for mass MITM surveillance of Iranian citizens. CA bankrupt within 4
weeks. Dutch government PKI severely disrupted. All DigiNotar-chained
PDF signatures became unverifiable. \\
2012 & Trustwave subordinate CA for DLP & Trustwave issued a subordinate
CA certificate to a private company for use in DLP (Data Loss
Prevention) traffic inspection. Technically allowed the company to issue
certificates for any domain. Caused industry debate about acceptable CA
practices. \\
2013 & ANSSI/Google France intermediate cert & A French government
intermediate CA (operated by ANSSI) issued unauthorized certificates for
Google domains. Google detected via CT/pinning. Limited scope but raised
concerns about government CAs. \\
2014 & India CCA/NIC breach & India's National Informatics Centre (NIC),
operating under the Indian Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA),
issued unauthorized certificates for Google and Yahoo domains. Google
detected via CT. Indian CCA root was constrained in trust stores. \\
2015 & CNNIC/MCS Holdings & China Internet Network Information Center
(CNNIC) delegated certificate issuance to MCS Holdings (an Egyptian
company), which issued unauthorized certificates for Google domains via
a MITM proxy. Google and Mozilla distrusted CNNIC's root
certificates. \\
2015-2018 & Symantec mis-issuance and distrust & Thousands of
certificates issued without proper authorization. Progressive distrust
by Chrome and Firefox over 2 years. Symantec CA sold to DigiCert for
\$950M. Massive impact on document signing certificates issued under
Symantec, VeriSign, Thawte, and GeoTrust brands. \\
2017 & WoSign/StartCom distrust & Chinese CA WoSign acquired StartCom
(Israeli CA) without disclosure, backdated SHA-1 certificates to avoid
browser deadlines, and made false statements to browser vendors. Both
CAs distrusted by all major browsers. \\
2020 & Let's Encrypt CAA bug revocation & 3 million certificates revoked
due to CAA checking non-compliance. 24-hour notice to certificate
holders. Demonstrated operational fragility of CA infrastructure at
scale. \\
2022 & TrustCor allegations & Investigative reporting linked TrustCor (a
Panamanian CA) to a company involved in distributing spyware. Mozilla,
Google, and Microsoft removed TrustCor from their trust stores. No
evidence of fraudulent certificate issuance, but trust was revoked on
the basis of the CA operator's associations. \\
2023 & e-Tugra suspended from CCADB & Turkish CA e-Tugra suspended from
the Common CA Database (CCADB) after security researchers discovered
critical vulnerabilities in its infrastructure, including exposed admin
panels and improperly secured HSMs. \\
\end{longtable}

This timeline only includes publicly known incidents. The actual number
of CA compromises is certainly higher --- not every breach is detected,
and not every detected breach is publicly disclosed. The incidents we
know about are the ones where detection mechanisms worked. The ones we
should worry about are the ones where they did not.

\section{Impact on PDF Signature Validation: What Happens in
Practice}\label{_impact_on_pdf_signature_validation_what_happens_in_practice}

When a CA is revoked, the practical impact on PDF signature validation
depends on several factors:

\subsection{The Validator's Trust
Store}\label{_the_validators_trust_store}

Different validators use different trust stores:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Adobe Acrobat (Windows)}: Uses AATL plus optionally the
  Windows certificate store
\item
  \textbf{Adobe Acrobat (macOS)}: Uses AATL plus optionally the macOS
  Keychain
\item
  \textbf{Foxit Reader}: Uses the operating system's trust store
\item
  \textbf{LibreOffice}: Uses the operating system's trust store (via NSS
  on Linux)
\item
  \textbf{EU DSS validation service}: Uses the EU Trusted List (EUTL)
\item
  \textbf{Custom Java applications}: Use the JVM's \texttt{cacerts}
  trust store or a custom \texttt{TrustStore}
\end{itemize}

When a CA is revoked, the timing and scope of the revocation varies
across these stores. AATL updates are published by Adobe on their own
schedule. OS trust store updates come via system updates. The EUTL is
updated when a member state's supervisory body modifies its trusted
list.

This means a signed PDF can show different validation results in
different viewers during the transition period --- "valid" in one that
has not yet received the revocation update, "invalid" in one that has.

\subsection{Whether LTV Data Is
Present}\label{_whether_ltv_data_is_present}

If the signature includes embedded revocation data (PAdES-B-LT or
B-LTA), a competent validator can perform \textbf{historical
validation}: it checks the embedded OCSP response or CRL to determine
the certificate's status at the time of signing, rather than its status
now.

If the signature does not include LTV data, the validator must check the
certificate's current status --- and if the CA has been revoked, the
chain cannot be validated at all.

\subsection{The Certificate's Position in the
Chain}\label{_the_certificates_position_in_the_chain}

If only an intermediate CA is revoked (as in the Comodo case), the
impact is limited to certificates issued through that specific
intermediate. Other intermediates under the same root may be unaffected.

If the root CA is revoked (as in DigiNotar's case), \emph{every}
certificate in the chain is affected --- there is no way to validate the
chain without a trusted root.

\section{Key Takeaways}\label{_key_takeaways_12}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{CA breaches retroactively undermine PDF signatures} --- the
  math does not change, but the trust does
\item
  \textbf{DigiNotar (2011)} showed that a small CA's failure can have
  outsized impact --- 531 rogue certs, mass surveillance, bankruptcy in
  4 weeks
\item
  \textbf{Comodo (2011)} showed that fast, transparent response can save
  a CA --- 9 rogue certs, revoked in hours, CA survived
\item
  \textbf{Symantec (2015-2018)} showed that even the largest CA is not
  too big to distrust --- 30\% of TLS certificates, distrusted over 2
  years
\item
  \textbf{Let's Encrypt (2020)} showed that compliance bugs at scale
  force brutal revocation timelines --- 3 million certs, 24-hour notice
\item
  \textbf{Certificate Transparency} makes rogue certificates detectable
  but does not prevent them
\item
  \textbf{PAdES-B-LTA is the only reliable defense} --- embedded
  revocation data at signing time proves certificate validity regardless
  of what happens to the CA later
\item
  \textbf{Always have a CA migration plan} --- your CA's trustworthiness
  is not guaranteed for the lifetime of your signed documents
\item
  \textbf{Monitor your CA's status} --- you want to know about problems
  before your validators do
\item
  \textbf{Re-timestamp archived documents} periodically to extend their
  verifiability beyond any single CA's lifetime
\end{itemize}

\chapter{Oddities \& Annoyances}\label{_oddities_annoyances}

Every technology has its dark corners --- the design decisions that made
sense in 1999 but haunt us today, the interoperability failures that no
spec can fix, the political arrangements that masquerade as technical
standards. PDF digital signatures have more dark corners than most.

This chapter is a tour of the things that will make you mutter
unprintable words during implementation. Not bugs, exactly --- bugs get
fixed. These are \emph{structural annoyances}: the problems baked into
the specifications, the ecosystem, and the economics of PDF signing.
Each one has wasted thousands of engineering hours across the industry.
Understanding them will not make them go away, but it will stop you from
wasting your own hours rediscovering what everyone else already knows
and nobody bothered to write down.

\section{The Adobe Approved Trust List
Monopoly}\label{_the_adobe_approved_trust_list_monopoly}

\subsection{What the AATL Is}\label{_what_the_aatl_is}

Adobe Acrobat is the world's dominant PDF viewer. When a user opens a
