signed PDF in Acrobat, the first thing they see is a blue or green
banner at the top of the document. Green means "Signed and all
signatures are valid." Blue means "At least one signature has problems."
Yellow means "Signed, but the signature cannot be verified."

The difference between green and yellow, in the vast majority of cases,
is not whether the signature is cryptographically correct. It is whether
the signing certificate chains to a root in the \textbf{Adobe Approved
Trust List (AATL)}.

The AATL is a curated list of root certificates that Adobe trusts for
document signing. It is published at
\texttt{https://helpx.adobe.com/acrobat/kb/approved-trust-list2.html}
and ships embedded in Acrobat. If your signing certificate chains to an
AATL root, you get the green checkmark. If it does not --- even if the
certificate is perfectly valid, issued by a reputable CA, chains to a
trust anchor in the operating system's trust store, and was created with
impeccable cryptographic rigor --- you get yellow.

Yellow, in the real world, means "the customer calls support."

\subsection{The Economics of Green}\label{_the_economics_of_green}

Getting your root certificate into the AATL requires meeting Adobe's
technical requirements (documented in the "AATL Technical Requirements"
PDF, which is itself signed with an AATL certificate --- recursion
intended) and paying a fee. The requirements include:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The root must be at least 4096-bit RSA or equivalent
\item
  The CA must carry an annual WebTrust for CAs audit or equivalent (ETSI
  EN 319 411)
\item
  The CA must use HSMs for root key protection
\item
  Document signing certificates must include specific key usage
  extensions
\item
  The CA must have a published CPS (Certification Practice Statement)
\end{itemize}

These are reasonable requirements for a public trust anchor. The problem
is not the technical bar --- it is the gatekeeping.

Adobe is a private company. The AATL is Adobe's private trust list.
Adobe decides who gets in, Adobe decides who stays in, and Adobe decides
the criteria. There is no appeals process, no regulatory oversight, and
no obligation to accept any particular CA.

For CAs, inclusion in the AATL is not optional --- it is a commercial
necessity. Enterprise customers demand the green checkmark. Government
procurement specifications require it. A CA that is not in the AATL
effectively cannot sell document signing certificates at competitive
prices, because the certificates will produce yellow warnings in the
software that 80\% of users use to view PDFs.

The AATL creates a two-tier market for document signing certificates.
AATL-listed certificates cost significantly more than non-AATL
certificates --- typically \$200-\$500 per year for an individual
signing certificate, versus \$30-\$50 for a certificate from a non-AATL
CA. The price premium has nothing to do with cryptographic quality and
everything to do with the color of a banner in one company's software.

If you are building a signing service, the single most important
commercial decision you will make is whether your use case requires the
green checkmark. If your recipients will open signed PDFs in Acrobat
(and they almost certainly will), you need AATL. If your recipients use
a custom validation workflow that checks against the EU Trusted List or
your own trust store, you do not.

\subsection{The EUTL Alternative}\label{_the_eutl_alternative}

The EU Trusted List (Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS), Article 22) is
the European counterpart to the AATL. Every Qualified Trust Service
Provider in the EU is listed on the EUTL, and a certificate chaining to
a QTSP on the EUTL qualifies for the highest eIDAS tier regardless of
whether Adobe has heard of the CA.

But here is the catch: Acrobat does not validate against the EUTL by
default. Acrobat uses the AATL. There is an option to enable EUTL
validation in Acrobat (Edit \textgreater{} Preferences \textgreater{}
Signatures \textgreater{} Verification \textgreater{} select "European
Union Trusted Lists"), but it is off by default, and the vast majority
of users never touch it.

This means a signature with a qualified certificate from a QTSP that is
\emph{not} in the AATL will show a yellow warning in Acrobat --- even
though it is legally a qualified electronic signature with the same
standing as a handwritten signature under EU law.

The legal framework says: this is a QES, as valid as ink. The software
says: "The validity of the document certification is UNKNOWN."

The user believes the software.

If you are distributing signed PDFs to recipients who use Acrobat, and
your QTSP is on the EUTL but not the AATL, include instructions for
enabling EUTL validation in Acrobat. Better yet, embed the full
certificate chain and LTV data so that validation depends on the trust
store configuration rather than online lookups. Best of all: choose a
QTSP that is on \emph{both} the AATL and the EUTL. Most major European
QTSPs have pursued AATL membership precisely because of this problem.

\subsection{The Political Dimension}\label{_the_political_dimension}

The AATL gives Adobe significant influence over the digital signing
ecosystem. Adobe can, at its discretion, adjust requirements, add fees,
or remove CAs. This is not a theoretical concern --- Adobe has adjusted
AATL requirements multiple times, each adjustment requiring CAs to
re-certify and sometimes change their infrastructure.

Some in the industry argue that this is appropriate: someone needs to
curate trust, and Adobe has a legitimate interest in ensuring that
signatures validated by its software are trustworthy. Others argue that
a single company should not be the gatekeeper for a technology used in
legal, governmental, and financial transactions across the globe.

The emergence of the EUDI wallet (Chapter 27) may shift this dynamic. If
QES becomes a consumer technology available to every EU citizen, the
volume of qualified signatures will dwarf the current market --- and the
EUTL, not the AATL, will be the authoritative trust anchor for those
signatures. Whether Adobe adjusts its defaults accordingly remains to be
seen.

\section{Broken Validation Across
Viewers}\label{_broken_validation_across_viewers}

\subsection{The Problem}\label{_the_problem_3}

Take a signed PDF --- any signed PDF --- and open it in five different
viewers. You will get five different answers about whether the signature
is valid.

This is not an exaggeration. It is the daily reality of PDF signature
interoperability.

The fundamental problem is that ISO 32000-2:2020 defines the
\emph{structure} of a PDF signature but leaves enormous latitude in how
viewers should \emph{validate} that structure. The specification says
what a \texttt{/Sig} dictionary must contain, what the
\texttt{/ByteRange} must cover, and how the CMS \texttt{SignedData} must
be embedded. It does not say, with sufficient precision, what a viewer
should do when the trust chain is incomplete, when the timestamp is
missing, when the revocation data is stale, or when the certificate uses
an algorithm the viewer does not support.

ETSI EN 319 142 (PAdES) adds more precision, but it constrains
\emph{signing} more than it constrains \emph{validation}. A PAdES-B-LTA
signature is well-defined in terms of what it must contain. What a
validator must do with each piece of that content --- and what it should
report to the user when something is missing or ambiguous --- is still
underspecified.

\subsection{The Comparison}\label{_the_comparison}

The following table shows how five common validators handle the same
edge cases. These are real behaviors observed in testing, not
theoretical predictions.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2308}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1538}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1538}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1538}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1538}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 10\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1538}}@{}}
\caption{Signature validation behavior across viewers (observed, not
specified)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Scenario
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Acrobat DC
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Foxit Reader
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
LibreOffice
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
macOS Preview
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
PDFBox
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Scenario
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Acrobat DC
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Foxit Reader
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
LibreOffice
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
macOS Preview
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
PDFBox
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Valid PAdES-B-LTA, AATL cert & Green checkmark & Valid & Valid & Valid &
Valid (programmatic) \\
Valid CMS, self-signed cert & Yellow warning & Unknown signer & Valid
(if cert trusted) & Invalid & Valid (if cert in trust store) \\
Valid CMS, expired cert, no timestamp & Invalid (expired) & Invalid &
Valid at signing time & Invalid & Depends on validation date \\
Valid CMS, EUTL cert, not in AATL & Yellow (unknown signer) & Valid (if
EUTL enabled) & Valid & Unknown & Depends on trust store \\
SHA-1 digest algorithm & Warning (weak hash) & Valid (no warning) &
Valid (no warning) & Valid & Valid (no warning) \\
Missing OCSP/CRL data & Warning (revocation unknown) & Valid (ignores) &
Valid (ignores) & Valid (ignores) & No revocation check by default \\
Incremental update after signing & Modified after signing & Modified &
May not detect & Does not detect & Detects (if coded) \\
SubFilter \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached} & Full validation & Full
validation & Full validation & Partial & Full (if coded) \\
SubFilter \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} & Full validation & Full
validation & Full validation & Full validation & Full (if coded) \\
SubFilter \texttt{adbe.x509.rsa\_sha1} & Legacy support & May fail & May
fail & Fails & Not supported \\
\end{longtable}

The most dangerous row in this table is "Incremental update after
signing." This is exactly the scenario that the Bochum Incremental
Saving Attack (Chapter 24) exploits. If a viewer does not detect that
the file was modified after signing, it will show a valid signature on a
document whose content has been changed. As of 2026, most major viewers
have patched this --- but "most" is not "all," and not every installed
version has been updated.

\subsection{Why Viewers Disagree}\label{_why_viewers_disagree}

The disagreements stem from several root causes:

\textbf{Different trust stores.} Acrobat uses AATL. Foxit uses the OS
trust store. LibreOffice uses NSS (on Linux) or the OS store (on
Windows/macOS). macOS Preview uses the Keychain. A certificate trusted
by one store may not be trusted by another.

\textbf{Different algorithm support.} Some viewers still accept SHA-1;
others warn or reject. Some support ECDSA; others are RSA-only. Some
parse CAdES extensions; others only understand basic CMS.

\textbf{Different revocation policies.} Acrobat actively checks
revocation status (OCSP and CRL) if the information is available. Foxit
and LibreOffice are more lenient --- they may skip revocation checks if
the data is not embedded. macOS Preview performs minimal revocation
checking.

\textbf{Different byte range verification.} Acrobat carefully verifies
that the byte range covers the entire file except the \texttt{/Contents}
gap. Some other viewers are less rigorous --- they check that the CMS
signature verifies over the byte range data, but do not verify that the
byte range actually covers the whole file.

\textbf{Different handling of incremental updates.} This is the critical
one. A proper validator must compare the signed byte range to the total
file size and, if there are bytes after the signed range, analyze the
incremental update to determine whether it contains permitted changes
(adding signatures, annotations) or impermissible modifications
(changing page content). Not all viewers perform this analysis with
equal rigor.

\subsection{What You Can Do About It}\label{_what_you_can_do_about_it}

If you are a signature \emph{creator}, your goal is to produce
signatures that validate consistently across all major viewers:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Use PAdES-B-LTA.} It is the most broadly supported profile.
\item
  \textbf{Use SubFilter \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached}.} All modern
  viewers support it, and it enables PAdES validation.
\item
  \textbf{Use SHA-256 or SHA-384.} Never SHA-1. Avoid SHA-512 only
  because some older viewers have edge-case issues with it.
\item
  \textbf{Embed the full certificate chain.} Do not rely on the viewer
  fetching missing intermediates --- some viewers will not.
\item
  \textbf{Embed OCSP responses and CRLs.} If the revocation data is in
  the DSS, viewers that check revocation will find it; viewers that do
  not check will simply ignore it.
\item
  \textbf{Allocate sufficient space for \texttt{/Contents}.} We discuss
  this in detail later in this chapter.
\item
  \textbf{Test in at least three viewers.} Acrobat, Foxit/LibreOffice,
  and one programmatic validator (PDFBox or EU DSS). If it validates in
  all three, it will validate almost everywhere.
\end{enumerate}

If you are a signature \emph{validator}, your goal is to implement the
most rigorous checks and report the most precise results:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Verify the byte range covers the entire file minus the
  \texttt{/Contents} gap.} This catches the Signature Wrapping Attack
  (Chapter 24).
\item
  \textbf{Detect incremental updates after signing.} Compare the signed
  byte range to the file size. If there is trailing data, parse it and
  determine whether it represents permitted or impermissible changes.
\item
  \textbf{Check revocation status.} Use embedded OCSP/CRL data if
  available; fall back to online checks if not; report clearly when
  revocation data is unavailable.
\item
  \textbf{Report the PAdES profile level.} Tell the user whether the
  signature is B-B, B-T, B-LT, or B-LTA --- this is much more
  informative than a binary "valid/invalid."
\end{enumerate}

\section{SubFilter Confusion}\label{_subfilter_confusion}

\subsection{The Five SubFilter Values}\label{_the_five_subfilter_values}

The \texttt{/SubFilter} key in a signature dictionary tells the viewer
what format the signature value (in \texttt{/Contents}) uses. Over the
history of PDF, five SubFilter values have been defined:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2222}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4444}}@{}}
\caption{SubFilter values defined across PDF versions and
PAdES}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
SubFilter
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Defined In
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Status and Usage
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
SubFilter
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Defined In
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Status and Usage
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} & PDF 1.3 (1999) & The original. CMS
\texttt{SignedData} where the signed content is the byte range data (not
included in the CMS structure). Still widely used. Supported by every
viewer. Not PAdES-compliant on its own. \\
\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.sha1} & PDF 1.4 (2001) & The SHA-1 hash of the entire
document is embedded as the CMS \texttt{EncapsulatedContentInfo}.
Deprecated in ISO 32000-2:2020. SHA-1 is broken for collision resistance
since the SHAttered attack (2017). Do not use. \\
\texttt{adbe.x509.rsa\_sha1} & PDF 1.3 (1999) & Raw RSA signature over
SHA-1 hash. The certificate is in a separate \texttt{/Cert} key, not
embedded in CMS. Extremely legacy. Most modern viewers cannot validate
it. Do not use. \\
\texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached} & PAdES (ETSI TS 102 778, 2009) & The PAdES
SubFilter. CMS \texttt{SignedData} with CAdES extensions
(ESS-signing-certificate-v2, optionally timestamp attributes). Required
for all PAdES profiles. The recommended choice for any new
implementation. \\
\texttt{ETSI.RFC3161} & ISO 32000-2:2020 (2017) & Document-level
timestamp. The \texttt{/Contents} contains an RFC 3161
\texttt{TimeStampToken} instead of a regular CMS signature. Used for
archive timestamps in PAdES-B-LTA. Not a "signature" in the user-facing
sense --- it proves the document existed at a specific time. \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{Which One Should You Use?}\label{_which_one_should_you_use}

If you are implementing a new signing feature in 2026, the answer is
simple: \textbf{\texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached}} for signatures and
\textbf{\texttt{ETSI.RFC3161}} for document-level timestamps.

There is no reason to use \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} in new
implementations unless you have a specific compatibility requirement
with a legacy system that cannot parse CAdES extensions. The practical
difference between \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} and
\texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached} is the presence of the
\texttt{ESS-signing-certificate-v2} signed attribute (Chapter 17) ---
adding it makes the signature PAdES-B-B compliant at zero additional
cost.

\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.sha1} is deprecated and cryptographically broken. Do
not use it. If you encounter it in a legacy document, validate it with
the understanding that SHA-1 collision resistance has been publicly
demonstrated to be insufficient since February 2017 (the SHAttered
attack by Stevens et al.).

\texttt{adbe.x509.rsa\_sha1} is a relic of PDF 1.3. It separates the
certificate from the signature in a way that no modern library handles
well. If you encounter it, you are looking at a document from the early
2000s.

\subsection{What Happens with Unrecognized SubFilter
Values}\label{_what_happens_with_unrecognized_subfilter_values}

ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8.1 states that if a viewer encounters a SubFilter
value it does not recognize, it should not attempt to validate the
signature. In practice, viewers handle this inconsistently:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Acrobat}: displays "The signature format is not supported" ---
  a clear and honest message
\item
  \textbf{Foxit}: may attempt validation anyway and produce confusing
  results
\item
  \textbf{LibreOffice}: typically shows "Signature could not be
  verified" --- accurate but unhelpful
\item
  \textbf{macOS Preview}: ignores the signature entirely --- the
  document appears unsigned
\end{itemize}

If you are implementing a validator and encounter an unknown SubFilter,
the correct behavior is to report that the signature format is not
recognized. Do not attempt to guess the format. Do not silently skip the
signature. Report it.

A common source of SubFilter confusion in the wild: some signing tools
produce a SubFilter of \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} but include CAdES
extensions (ESS-signing-certificate-v2) in the CMS signed attributes.
Technically, this is a valid \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} signature that
\emph{also} happens to be PAdES-compliant --- because PAdES compliance
is about the content of the CMS, not just the SubFilter label. But some
validators treat the SubFilter as a strict indicator: if it says
\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached}, they skip CAdES-specific checks. The
result: a signature that is PAdES-compliant by content but not
recognized as such by label.

For maximum compatibility, use the SubFilter that matches your intent.
If you are producing PAdES signatures, use \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached}.

\section{PAdES vs ISO 32000-2:2020: The
Divergence}\label{_pades_vs_iso_32000_22020_the_divergence}

\subsection{Two Standards, One
Problem}\label{_two_standards_one_problem}

ISO 32000-2:2020 defines how to embed a digital signature in a PDF. ETSI
EN 319 142 (PAdES) defines additional requirements for that signature to
be legally meaningful in Europe.

These two standards \emph{should} be perfectly aligned. They are not.

The divergence is not dramatic --- PAdES is a strict subset of ISO
32000-2:2020, so a PAdES-compliant signature is always a valid ISO
32000-2:2020 signature. But the two standards were developed by
different organizations (ISO 32000-2:2020 by ISO TC 171, PAdES by ETSI
ESI), on different timelines, with different constituencies. The result
is a set of subtle mismatches that catch developers in the gap.

\subsection{Where They Disagree}\label{_where_they_disagree}

\textbf{Signed attributes.} ISO 32000-2:2020 requires the CMS
\texttt{SignedData} to include \texttt{content-type} and
\texttt{message-digest} signed attributes (RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic
Message Syntax (CMS) §11.1). PAdES \emph{additionally} requires
\texttt{ESS-signing-certificate-v2} (ETSI EN 319 122-1 §5.7.3). A
signature that satisfies ISO 32000-2:2020 but omits
\texttt{ESS-signing-certificate-v2} is a valid PDF signature but
\emph{not} a valid PAdES signature. This is the single most common PAdES
compliance failure.

\textbf{SubFilter semantics.} ISO 32000-2:2020 treats SubFilter as
informational --- it tells the viewer what format to expect, but the
viewer is expected to parse the CMS and determine the format from the
content. PAdES treats \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached} as a
\emph{requirement} --- a signature claiming PAdES compliance must use
this SubFilter. A technically identical CMS blob wrapped in
\texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} is not PAdES-compliant by label, even if
the CMS content is identical.

\textbf{Timestamp handling.} ISO 32000-2:2020 defines document-level
timestamps (SubFilter \texttt{ETSI.RFC3161}) and signature timestamps
(unsigned attribute \texttt{id-aa-signatureTimeStampToken} in the CMS).
PAdES defines the same mechanisms but adds requirements about timestamp
token format, the relationship between signature timestamps and document
timestamps, and the order in which they must be embedded in PAdES-B-LTA.
The PAdES requirements are stricter --- a timestamp arrangement that is
valid under ISO 32000-2:2020 may not satisfy PAdES-B-LTA's requirements
for archive timestamps.

\textbf{DSS dictionary.} The Document Security Store (DSS) dictionary
--- where certificates, OCSP responses, and CRLs are stored for LTV
validation --- is defined in ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8.4.3 and also
referenced in PAdES. The specifications agree on the structure but
differ slightly on when DSS data is mandatory. ISO 32000-2:2020 treats
DSS as optional enrichment. PAdES-B-LT and B-LTA \emph{require} DSS data
--- without it, the signature cannot claim the LT or LTA profile level.

\subsection{The Practical Consequence}\label{_the_practical_consequence}

If you are building a signing service that needs to produce signatures
valid in both the ISO 32000-2:2020 sense and the PAdES sense (and you
almost certainly are), always target PAdES. PAdES is the stricter
standard. A PAdES-B-LTA signature satisfies both standards
simultaneously. A signature that targets only ISO 32000-2:2020 without
PAdES awareness will fail PAdES validation --- and PAdES validation is
what matters for EU compliance, for AATL-listed CAs, and for most
enterprise validation workflows.

The EU DSS validation framework (Chapter 23) validates signatures
against PAdES profiles, not against raw ISO 32000-2:2020. If your
signatures validate in EU DSS, they satisfy both standards. If you are
building your own validator, validate against PAdES --- it is the
superset of requirements that covers both.

\section{The Timestamp Chicken-and-Egg
Problem}\label{_the_timestamp_chicken_and_egg_problem}

\subsection{The Problem}\label{_the_problem_4}

A PAdES-B-T signature requires a signature timestamp --- an RFC 3161
\texttt{TimeStampToken} embedded as an unsigned CMS attribute. The
timestamp proves when the signature was created.

To obtain the timestamp, you must:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Create the CMS \texttt{SignedData} (including the signed attributes
  and the signature value)
\item
  Hash the signature value
\item
  Send the hash to a Time Stamp Authority (TSA)
\item
  Receive the \texttt{TimeStampToken} from the TSA
\item
  Embed the token as an unsigned attribute in the CMS
  \texttt{SignedData}
\end{enumerate}

Here is the problem: the CMS \texttt{SignedData} is the
\texttt{/Contents} value in the signature dictionary. The
\texttt{/Contents} value is a hex string with a \emph{fixed} length ---
the space for it was allocated when the signature dictionary was written
into the PDF, before the CMS was created.

So the size of \texttt{/Contents} must be determined \emph{before} you
know how large the CMS will be. And the size of the CMS depends on
whether you include a timestamp --- and how large that timestamp is. And
you cannot obtain the timestamp until you have the CMS signature value.
And you cannot have the signature value until you have the byte range.
And the byte range depends on the size of \texttt{/Contents}.

This is the chicken-and-egg.

\subsection{The Standard Solution}\label{_the_standard_solution}

The standard solution, used by every major signing library, is to
\textbf{pre-allocate a fixed-size \texttt{/Contents} field}, create the
CMS signature, obtain the timestamp, embed the timestamp in the CMS, and
then pad the CMS to fit the pre-allocated space.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Pre{-}allocate /Contents with estimated size}
\CommentTok{// The hex encoding doubles the byte count, so allocate 2x the expected CMS size}
\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ estimatedCmsSize }\OperatorTok{=} \DecValTok{16384}\OperatorTok{;}  \CommentTok{// 16 KB — usually sufficient for B{-}T}
\NormalTok{SignatureOptions sigOptions }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{SignatureOptions}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{sigOptions}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setPreferredSignatureSize}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{estimatedCmsSize}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// The PDF is written with a /Contents placeholder of 2 * estimatedCmsSize hex chars}
\CommentTok{// After signing, the actual CMS (with timestamp) is padded with zeros to fill the space}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The padding is simple: after the CMS \texttt{SignedData} DER encoding,
the remaining bytes in \texttt{/Contents} are filled with \texttt{0x00}.
The hex encoding of these zero bytes produces \texttt{00} characters.
The viewer knows to ignore trailing zeros after the DER-encoded CMS ---
the DER encoding is self-delimiting, so the parser can determine where
the CMS ends and the padding begins.

\subsection{The 32 KB Problem}\label{_the_32_kb_problem}

How much space should you pre-allocate? This is a more consequential
question than it appears.

The CMS \texttt{SignedData} contains:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The DER-encoded \texttt{SignedData} structure itself
  (\textasciitilde100-300 bytes of overhead)
\item
  The signer's certificate (\textasciitilde1-2 KB)
\item
  Any intermediate certificates (\textasciitilde1-2 KB each, typically
  1-3 intermediates)
\item
  The signature value (\textasciitilde256-512 bytes for RSA-2048/4096,
  \textasciitilde64-72 bytes for ECDSA)
\item
  Signed attributes (\textasciitilde200-500 bytes)
\item
  The signature timestamp token (\textasciitilde3-8 KB, depending on the
  TSA)
\end{itemize}

A typical PAdES-B-T signature with one intermediate certificate and a
timestamp requires approximately 8-12 KB. A PAdES-B-LTA signature with
embedded OCSP responses and CRLs in the CMS (rather than in the DSS) can
require 15-30 KB.

Most implementations pre-allocate \textbf{32 KB} (32,768 bytes,
producing 65,536 hex characters in \texttt{/Contents}). This is
sufficient for the vast majority of signatures.

But not all.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3750}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.6250}}@{}}
\caption{Scenarios that exceed 32 KB}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Scenario
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Why It Is Large
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Scenario
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Why It Is Large
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Long certificate chain & 4-5 intermediates, each 2 KB. Happens with some
government CAs. \\
Large timestamp token & Some TSAs include their own certificate chain in
the TimeStampToken. If the TSA has 3 intermediates, the token alone can
be 10-15 KB. \\
Embedded CRLs & If a CRL is embedded directly in the CMS (as an unsigned
attribute), it can be 50-200 KB. This is unusual for new implementations
(CRLs go in the DSS instead), but some legacy signing tools do this. \\
Cross-certified chains & Government PKIs that cross-certify with
multiple roots can produce chains with redundant certificates, inflating
the CMS. \\
Multiple timestamps & Some workflows add both a signature timestamp and
a content timestamp, doubling the timestamp overhead. \\
\end{longtable}

If the CMS exceeds the pre-allocated \texttt{/Contents} size, the
signing operation fails. The failure mode depends on the library:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{PDFBox}: throws an \texttt{IOException} with a message about
  the signature being too large for the placeholder
\item
  \textbf{iText}: throws a similar exception
\item
  \textbf{OpenPDF}: throws an exception
\end{itemize}

The fix is simple: increase the pre-allocated size. But the fix means
you allocated \emph{more} space that is filled with zero padding ---
wasted bytes in every PDF you sign. There is a tension between
allocating enough space to handle edge cases and not bloating every PDF
with unused padding.

A practical compromise: pre-allocate 32 KB for PAdES-B-T signatures and
64 KB for PAdES-B-LTA signatures. If you know your signing chain is
unusually long (government PKIs, cross-certified CAs), increase to 96 KB
or 128 KB. The wasted space is insignificant compared to the risk of a
signing failure in production.

If you want to be precise, you can estimate the CMS size before
allocating:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Build a "dry run" CMS with a dummy signature value and a dummy
  timestamp of the expected size
\item
  Measure its DER-encoded length
\item
  Add a 50\% safety margin
\item
  Use that as your pre-allocation size
\end{enumerate}

This is more complex but avoids both undersizing and excessive
oversizing.

\section{Silent Signing Failures}\label{_silent_signing_failures}

\subsection{The Taxonomy of Silence}\label{_the_taxonomy_of_silence}

PDF signing can fail in ways that produce a valid-looking PDF with an
invalid signature. The document opens. The pages render. But the
signature is broken --- and the failure may not be obvious until someone
tries to validate it.

These silent failures are particularly dangerous because they often are
not detected at signing time. The signing code runs without exceptions.
The PDF is saved. It is emailed, archived, or submitted to a government
portal. And then, days or weeks later, someone discovers that the
signature is invalid.

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{Empty
\texttt{/Contents}}{Empty /Contents}}\label{_empty_contents}

The most egregious silent failure: the \texttt{/Contents} value is
present but contains only zero bytes. No CMS structure at all.

This happens when the signing pipeline fails to inject the CMS into the
\texttt{/Contents} placeholder after the byte range is computed and the
CMS is generated. The PDF has the correct structure --- the signature
dictionary exists, the byte range is set, the \texttt{/Contents} key is
there --- but the value is all zeros.

Some signing libraries have had bugs where exceptions during CMS
generation were swallowed (caught and not re-thrown), leaving the
\texttt{/Contents} empty. The resulting PDF looks signed --- it has a
signature field, possibly even a visual appearance --- but the signature
is cryptographically void.

After every signing operation, \emph{always} verify the result. Open the
output PDF, extract the \texttt{/Contents} value, and confirm it
contains a valid DER-encoded CMS \texttt{SignedData}. This adds a few
milliseconds to your signing pipeline and catches the most catastrophic
failure mode.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Post{-}signing verification — always do this}
\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ contents }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{extractContentsFromSignedPdf}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedPdfBytes}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{CMSSignedData cms }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CMSSignedData}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{contents}\OperatorTok{);}
\ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{cms}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignerInfos}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getSigners}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{isEmpty}\OperatorTok{())} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \ControlFlowTok{throw} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{IllegalStateException}\OperatorTok{(}
            \StringTok{"Signed PDF has no signers in CMS — signing failed silently"}\OperatorTok{);}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\NormalTok{log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Post{-}signing verification passed: \{\} signer(s) in CMS"}\OperatorTok{,}
\NormalTok{        cms}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getSignerInfos}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{getSigners}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{size}\OperatorTok{());}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Bad Byte Range}\label{_bad_byte_range}

The \texttt{/ByteRange} array must specify two ranges that together
cover the entire file except the \texttt{/Contents} hex string. A bad
byte range --- one that does not start at 0, does not end at the file's
last byte, or has gaps other than the \texttt{/Contents} region ---
produces a signature that covers only part of the file.

This can happen when:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The signing library miscalculates the offset of the \texttt{/Contents}
  value in the file
\item
  The file is modified (even trivially --- whitespace changes, metadata
  updates) between byte range calculation and signature injection
\item
  A streaming writer flushes partial content, causing offset shifts
\end{itemize}

The PDF opens fine. The signature exists. But a proper validator will
detect that the byte range does not cover the entire file and report the
signature as invalid or the document as modified.

\subsection{Missing Certificate Chain}\label{_missing_certificate_chain}

A CMS \texttt{SignedData} should include the signer's certificate and
all intermediate certificates needed to build a chain to a trusted root.
If the intermediates are missing, the validator must fetch them --- and
may not be able to.

Missing intermediates are common in testing (developers use self-signed
certificates and forget to include the chain) but also happen in
production when:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The signing code includes only the signer's certificate, not the
  intermediates
\item
  The PKCS\#12 keystore contains only the signer's certificate (the
  intermediates were not imported)
\item
  A library upgrade changed the default behavior for chain inclusion
\end{itemize}

The signature validates if the validator can fetch or already has the
missing intermediates. It fails if it cannot. This is a Heisenbug ---
the signature works on the developer's machine (where the intermediates
are in the trust store) but fails on the customer's machine (where they
are not).

\subsection{Expired Timestamp}\label{_expired_timestamp}

A signature timestamp proves when the signature was created. But the
timestamp itself is signed by the TSA's certificate --- and that
certificate can expire.

If the TSA certificate expires and the timestamp token does not include
sufficient chain data for historical validation, validators may reject
the timestamp. The signature itself may still verify, but the timestamp
--- which was the proof of when the signature was created --- becomes
unverifiable.

For PAdES-B-LTA, this is handled by the archive timestamp mechanism: a
document-level timestamp covers the entire file (including the DSS with
revocation data for the signature timestamp). As long as the archive
timestamp chain is valid, the signature timestamp can be verified
historically.

For PAdES-B-T signatures without LTV data, an expired TSA certificate
means the timestamp can no longer be verified. The signature degrades
from B-T to effectively B-B --- you lose the proof of when it was
created.

Monitor the expiration dates of the TSAs you use. TSA certificates
typically have 10-15 year validity periods, so this is not an urgent
problem --- but it \emph{is} a problem that will arrive eventually. When
it does, any PAdES-B-T signature without LTV data that used the expired
TSA will lose its timestamp verifiability.

This is another reason to prefer PAdES-B-LTA: the archive timestamp
mechanism provides a renewable chain that survives individual
certificate expirations.

\section{Reader-Enabled PDFs vs Signing
Conflicts}\label{_reader_enabled_pdfs_vs_signing_conflicts}

\subsection{What Reader-Enabling Is}\label{_what_reader_enabling_is}

Adobe Reader (the free version) historically could not save filled-in
forms. To allow Reader users to fill in and save a form, the PDF author
had to "enable" the PDF for Reader using Adobe Acrobat Pro. This was
done by applying a special \textbf{usage rights signature} --- a
certification-level signature with a specific set of usage rights
extensions that told Reader which features to unlock.

The usage rights signature is stored in the document's permissions
dictionary (\texttt{/Perms}) and uses a special Adobe-issued
certificate. When Reader opens a document with a valid usage rights
signature, it enables the specified features (fill-in, save, comment,
etc.).

\subsection{The Conflict with Document
Signing}\label{_the_conflict_with_document_signing}

The problem arises when a Reader-enabled PDF needs to be signed.

The usage rights signature is a certification signature --- it was the
first signature on the document, and it claims \texttt{/DocMDP}
permissions. Adding an approval signature after the certification
signature requires an incremental update, which is permitted under the
DocMDP permissions level set by the usage rights signature.

But here is the catch: some PDF editors, when they save the document
after adding a signature, perform operations that invalidate the usage
rights signature. This happens because:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  The editor modifies objects that the usage rights signature's byte
  range covers
\item
  The editor performs a full save instead of an incremental save,
  destroying the incremental structure that the usage rights signature
  depends on
\item
  The editor adds or modifies AcroForm entries in a way that the usage
  rights signature's \texttt{/FieldMDP} transform does not permit
\end{enumerate}

When the usage rights signature is invalidated, Reader reverts to its
restricted mode --- the user can no longer save the form. And if the
document's approval signature depends on the AcroForm structure (which
it does, since signature fields \emph{are} form fields), the interplay
between the invalidated usage rights and the new signature can produce
validation errors in Acrobat.

\subsection{The Historical Context}\label{_the_historical_context}

Reader-enabling was a significant source of revenue for Adobe: Acrobat
Pro was the only tool that could create the usage rights signature
(because it used an Adobe-controlled certificate). Third-party libraries
could not Reader-enable PDFs.

ISO 32000-2:2020 deprecated the \texttt{/Perms} usage rights mechanism.
PDF 2.0 assumes that all viewers can save forms without special
enablement. But the real world is not PDF 2.0 --- millions of
Reader-enabled PDF 1.7 documents are still in circulation, and
enterprises still generate new ones because their workflows depend on
the Reader experience.

If your signing service processes documents that may be Reader-enabled,
test the signing workflow with Reader-enabled PDFs specifically. Ensure
your signing code performs an incremental save (not a full save) and
does not modify objects covered by the usage rights signature. PDFBox's
\texttt{PDDocument.saveIncremental()} is designed for this --- but you
must be careful not to load and re-save objects that the usage rights
signature covers.

\subsection{The Modern Alternative}\label{_the_modern_alternative}

The practical alternative to Reader-enabling is to stop caring about it.
Adobe Acrobat Reader DC (the current free version) can save filled-in
forms without usage rights. The limitation existed in older versions of
Reader (pre-DC) and was removed as Adobe shifted to a subscription model
for Acrobat.

If your user base has upgraded to Reader DC (and by 2026, most have),
Reader-enabling is no longer necessary. If you must support legacy
Reader versions, the only safe approach is to Reader-enable the PDF
\emph{after} all signatures have been applied --- which is usually not
possible, since the usage rights signature must be the first signature.

The real answer is: test your specific workflow, document the behavior,
and warn your users if they are operating in a scenario where
Reader-enabling and signing conflict.

\section{\texorpdfstring{Trailing Bytes and Garbage After
\texttt{\%\%EOF}}{Trailing Bytes and Garbage After \%\%EOF}}\label{_trailing_bytes_and_garbage_after_eof}

\subsection{The Spec Says}\label{_the_spec_says}

ISO 32000-2:2020 §7.5.5 states that a PDF file must end with
\texttt{\%\%EOF}. Anything after the last \texttt{\%\%EOF} marker is not
part of the PDF.

In an incrementally saved PDF (which every signed PDF is --- signing
requires an incremental save), each revision ends with its own
\texttt{\%\%EOF}. The last \texttt{\%\%EOF} in the file marks the end of
the last revision.

\subsection{The Reality}\label{_the_reality}

In practice, many tools append data after \texttt{\%\%EOF}:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Some PDF generators append a newline or carriage return after
  \texttt{\%\%EOF} (harmless but technically non-conformant)
\item
  Some content management systems append metadata or tracking
  information
\item
  Some signing tools, due to buffer sizing bugs, write a few extra bytes
  after the CMS injection
\item
  Some email systems append MIME boundary markers when the PDF is
  transmitted as an attachment
\end{itemize}

These trailing bytes create a problem for signature validation: the
signed byte range (as specified by \texttt{/ByteRange}) covers the file
up to and including the last byte \emph{as calculated at signing time}.
If additional bytes appear after that, the file is longer than the
signed byte range. A strict validator will flag this as "the document
was modified after signing" --- because, technically, it was, even if
the modification was just a trailing newline.

When your validator detects bytes beyond the signed byte range, examine
what those bytes actually are before reporting "modified after signing."
A few bytes of whitespace or a line break after \texttt{\%\%EOF} is
cosmetically messy but not a security concern. A complete incremental
update after the signed range is a genuine modification that needs
careful analysis.

Distinguish between "there are trailing bytes that are not an
incremental update" (cosmetic) and "there is an incremental update that
was not covered by any signature" (potentially malicious).

\section{Key Takeaways}\label{_key_takeaways_13}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{The AATL is a private trust list} controlled by Adobe that
  determines whether Acrobat shows a green or yellow banner --- this
  gatekeeping creates a two-tier market for signing certificates
\item
  \textbf{Validation results differ across viewers} --- the same PDF can
  show valid, invalid, or unknown depending on trust stores, algorithm
  support, revocation policies, and byte range verification rigor
\item
  \textbf{Use \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached}} for all new signatures ---
  it is PAdES-compliant and universally supported
\item
  \textbf{PAdES is stricter than ISO 32000-2:2020} --- always target
  PAdES, because a PAdES signature satisfies both standards
\item
  \textbf{Pre-allocate 32-64 KB for \texttt{/Contents}} --- undersizing
  causes signing failures; oversizing wastes a few KB of padding
\item
  \textbf{Always verify the result after signing} --- check that
  \texttt{/Contents} contains valid CMS, the byte range is correct, and
  the chain is complete
\item
  \textbf{Monitor TSA certificate expiration} --- expired TSA certs
  degrade B-T signatures to effectively B-B
\item
  \textbf{Reader-enabled PDFs conflict with signing} --- test
  specifically if your workflow involves Reader-enabled documents
\item
  \textbf{Trailing bytes after \texttt{\%\%EOF}} are common and usually
  harmless, but your validator should distinguish cosmetic trailing data
  from actual post-signature modifications
\end{itemize}

\chapter{EUDI Wallet \& Digital
Identity}\label{_eudi_wallet_digital_identity}

For three decades, qualified electronic signatures have been a niche
technology --- expensive, complicated, and reserved for enterprises,
notaries, and government agencies. In 2024, the European Union decided
to change that. The revised eIDAS regulation puts a qualified signature
creation capability in the pocket of every EU citizen, on a device they
already own: their smartphone.

This is not incremental improvement. This is a category shift. If it
works --- and the "if" is substantial --- QES moves from a tool that a
few thousand specialized users understand to a feature that 450 million
Europeans can use as casually as they use contactless payment.

This chapter covers the regulatory, architectural, and technical
dimensions of the EUDI wallet and its impact on PDF digital signatures.
We start with the political context (because regulation drives
everything in this space), move through the architecture, and end with
what you --- the developer building signing or validation software ---
need to prepare.

\section{eIDAS 2.0: What Changed and
Why}\label{_eidas_2_0_what_changed_and_why}

\subsection{The Regulatory History}\label{_the_regulatory_history}

The original eIDAS regulation (Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS),
Chapter 19) entered into force in 2014 and became fully effective in
2016. It was a success in the narrow sense: it created a functioning
framework for qualified trust services, established the EU Trusted List,
and enabled cross-border recognition of QES.

It was a failure in the broad sense: almost nobody used it.

The adoption numbers tell the story. By 2022, eight years after eIDAS
entered into force, fewer than 14\% of EU citizens had ever used a
qualified electronic signature. The number who used QES regularly was a
fraction of that. In some member states, the number of active QES users
was in the low thousands --- in countries with populations of tens of
millions.

The reasons for low adoption were structural:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Cost.} A qualified signing certificate costs 50-300 EUR per
  year. Most citizens have no reason to pay that.
\item
  \textbf{Complexity.} Creating a QES required a smart card or USB
  token, driver software, and middleware that worked reliably on exactly
  zero consumer devices.
\item
  \textbf{No killer application.} There was no consumer use case
  compelling enough to justify the cost and complexity. Tax filing, the
  most common candidate, was solved by other means in most member
  states.
\item
  \textbf{eID fragmentation.} Each member state had its own electronic
  identity scheme, with its own technology stack, its own enrollment
  process, and its own level of adoption. Cross-border recognition of
  eIDs was technically possible under eIDAS but practically rare.
\end{enumerate}

The European Commission recognized by 2020 that voluntary adoption was
not going to achieve the digital single market it wanted. Something more
forceful was needed.

\subsection{Regulation (EU) 2024/1183}\label{_regulation_eu_20241183}

On April 11, 2024, the revised eIDAS regulation --- Regulation (EU)
2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0), commonly called eIDAS 2.0 --- entered into force.
The revision keeps the trust services framework from eIDAS 1.0 largely
intact (the three-tier signature model, QTSPs, the EU Trusted List ---
all unchanged) but adds a transformative new component.

The headline change: \textbf{every EU member state must provide a
European Digital Identity Wallet to its citizens and residents}.

The timeline:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.7143}}@{}}
\caption{eIDAS 2.0 key dates}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Date
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Milestone
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Date
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Milestone
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
April 11, 2024 & eIDAS 2.0 enters into force \\
By November 2024 & European Commission publishes the first set of
implementing acts defining technical specifications \\
2024-2025 & Large-Scale Pilots (LSPs) across the EU test wallet
implementations in real-world scenarios \\
By end of 2026 & Member states must make the EUDI wallet available to
all citizens and residents \\
2027 onwards & Mandatory acceptance of the wallet by public services
and, for certain use cases, private sector reliance parties \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{The Political Context}\label{_the_political_context}

eIDAS 2.0 did not emerge from a vacuum. It was shaped by several
converging political forces:

\textbf{COVID-19 and the Digital COVID Certificate.} The EU Digital
COVID Certificate (2021) demonstrated that a pan-European digital
credential system could be built and deployed at scale in months. Over
1.8 billion certificates were issued across 46 countries. The technical
infrastructure --- QR codes, digital signatures, trust frameworks ---
proved that citizens would adopt digital credentials if the use case was
compelling enough. The political lesson was clear: mandates work,
voluntary adoption does not.

\textbf{Strategic autonomy.} The European Commission increasingly frames
digital identity as a matter of strategic autonomy. European citizens
authenticate to online services using Google, Apple, and Facebook
identity providers --- American companies, subject to American law,
storing European identity data on American infrastructure. The EUDI
wallet is, in part, an assertion that European identity should be
managed by European institutions.

\textbf{The digital single market.} The EU's single market depends on
cross-border transactions. Those transactions require identity
verification. If every member state has its own identity system,
cross-border identity verification remains fragmented --- exactly the
problem eIDAS 1.0 was supposed to solve and did not.

\textbf{Article 45 and the browser wars.} The original eIDAS 2.0
proposal included a controversial provision (Article 45) that would have
required web browsers to trust Qualified Website Authentication
Certificates (QWACs) issued by QTSPs, without the ability to apply
additional security requirements. Browser vendors --- particularly
Mozilla and Google --- objected strenuously, arguing that this would
undermine browser security by forcing them to trust certificates that
did not meet their own security standards. The final text was amended to
address some of these concerns, but the episode illustrates the tension
between regulatory mandates and technical security communities.

\section{The EUDI Wallet Concept}\label{_the_eudi_wallet_concept}

\subsection{What the Wallet Is}\label{_what_the_wallet_is}

The EUDI wallet is a smartphone application --- provided by or on behalf
of a member state --- that allows citizens and residents to:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Store government-issued identity data (PID):} name, date of
  birth, nationality, and a unique persistent identifier --- the digital
  equivalent of a national ID card or passport
\item
  \textbf{Store qualified electronic attestations of attributes
  (QEAAs):} digitally signed attestations of specific attributes ---
  university degrees, professional qualifications, driving licenses, age
  verification, right to work
\item
  \textbf{Present credentials selectively:} share only the specific
  attributes required for a transaction (e.g., "I am over 18" without
  revealing date of birth or name)
\item
  \textbf{Create qualified electronic signatures:} sign documents with
  QES, using a remote QSCD accessed through the wallet
\end{enumerate}

The wallet is \emph{not} a single application developed by the European
Commission. Each member state is responsible for providing its own
wallet implementation. The Commission defines the technical
specifications and ensures interoperability; member states build or
procure the implementations.

\subsection{What the Wallet Is Not}\label{_what_the_wallet_is_not}

The EUDI wallet is not a blockchain. It is not decentralized in the
cryptocurrency sense. It is not self-sovereign identity (SSI), although
it borrows some concepts from that space.

The wallet is a \emph{government-issued} credential system. The identity
data comes from authoritative government sources (civil registries,
passport offices). The trust model is top-down: you trust the wallet
because you trust the member state that issued it, and other member
states trust it because the EU framework mandates mutual recognition.

The wallet is also not mandatory for citizens. eIDAS 2.0 requires member
states to \emph{offer} the wallet; it does not require citizens to
\emph{use} it. However, Article 5b requires that a wide range of public
and private sector entities accept the wallet --- which creates strong
incentives for adoption even without an explicit mandate.

The voluntary nature of the wallet is politically important. A mandatory
government identity app on every citizen's phone would face significant
political resistance, particularly in countries with strong privacy
traditions (Germany, Austria, the Netherlands). By making the wallet
optional for citizens but mandatory for reliance parties, eIDAS 2.0
creates adoption through demand-side pressure rather than supply-side
compulsion.

\section{Architecture Reference Framework
(ARF)}\label{_architecture_reference_framework_arf}

\subsection{Overview}\label{_overview}

The Architecture Reference Framework (ARF) is the technical blueprint
for the EUDI wallet ecosystem. Published and maintained by the European
Commission, the ARF defines the architecture, protocols, data models,
and trust framework for the wallet.

As of early 2026, the ARF is at version 1.4 and still evolving. The core
architecture is stable, but specific protocol choices and data formats
are being refined based on pilot feedback.

The ARF defines four main roles:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.7143}}@{}}
\caption{EUDI wallet ecosystem roles}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Role
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Role
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\textbf{Wallet User} & The citizen or resident who holds the wallet on
their device \\
\textbf{Wallet Provider} & The member state (or entity acting on its
behalf) that issues and manages the wallet application \\
\textbf{PID Provider / Attestation Provider} & The authoritative source
that issues identity data (PID) or attribute attestations (QEAAs) into
the wallet \\
\textbf{Relying Party} & The entity (public or private sector) that
requests and validates credentials from the wallet \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{The Trust Model}\label{_the_trust_model}

The EUDI wallet trust model has multiple layers:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Device trust.} The wallet runs on the user's smartphone. The
  device's secure element or Trusted Execution Environment (TEE)
  provides hardware-backed key storage. The wallet checks that the
  device meets minimum security requirements (OS version, no
  root/jailbreak).
\item
  \textbf{Wallet trust.} Each wallet implementation is certified by the
  member state that issues it. The certification confirms that the
  wallet meets the ARF's security and functional requirements. Wallet
  instances are registered with the member state's infrastructure.
\item
  \textbf{PID/QEAA trust.} PID and QEAAs are digitally signed by the
  issuing authority (the PID Provider for identity data, the Attestation
  Provider for attributes). The signature uses a key that chains to the
  member state's entry in the EU Trusted List. Relying parties validate
  the attestation signatures against the EUTL, just as PDF signature
  validators check the signer's certificate against a trust store.
\item
  \textbf{Relying party trust.} Relying parties that request credentials
  from the wallet must be registered and authenticated. The wallet
  verifies the relying party's identity before releasing any data ---
  the user knows \emph{who} is requesting their data, and the wallet
  ensures the request is legitimate.
\end{enumerate}

The trust model for the EUDI wallet is fundamentally the same trust
model as PDF digital signatures: X.509 certificates, certificate chains,
trusted lists, and CMS/COSE signatures. If you understand how PDF
signature validation works (Chapters 16-17), you understand the core of
the wallet's trust model. The wallet adds selective disclosure and user
consent on top, but the cryptographic foundations are identical.

\section{PID and QEAA}\label{_pid_and_qeaa}

\subsection{Person Identification Data
(PID)}\label{_person_identification_data_pid}

The PID is the wallet's core credential --- the digital equivalent of a
national ID card. It is issued by the member state's PID Provider
(typically the civil registry or passport authority) and contains a
minimum set of identity attributes.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4286}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5714}}@{}}
\caption{Mandatory PID attributes (ARF v1.4)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Attribute
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Attribute
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{family\_name} & Current family name(s) \\
\texttt{given\_name} & Current given name(s) \\
\texttt{birth\_date} & Date of birth \\
\texttt{unique\_id} & A persistent, unique identifier assigned by the
member state \\
\texttt{age\_over\_18} & Boolean: is the holder over 18? (Derived
attribute for age verification without revealing birth date) \\
\texttt{issuance\_date} & When the PID was issued \\
\texttt{expiry\_date} & When the PID expires \\
\texttt{issuing\_authority} & The authority that issued the PID \\
\texttt{issuing\_country} & The member state that issued the PID (ISO
3166-1 alpha-2) \\
\end{longtable}

The PID is signed by the PID Provider using a key that chains to the
member state's trust infrastructure. The signature format is currently
specified as either \textbf{ISO/IEC 18013-5 mdoc} (the same format used
for mobile driving licenses) or \textbf{SD-JWT} (Selective Disclosure
JSON Web Token, IETF draft). The ARF supports both formats, with ongoing
discussion about whether to mandate one or allow both.

\subsection{Qualified Electronic Attestations of Attributes
(QEAA)}\label{_qualified_electronic_attestations_of_attributes_qeaa}

QEAAs are the wallet's extensibility mechanism. While the PID provides
core identity, QEAAs attest to specific attributes beyond identity:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Educational credentials:} university degrees, professional
  certifications
\item
  \textbf{Professional qualifications:} medical licenses, legal bar
  admissions, engineering certifications
\item
  \textbf{Organizational identity:} employment status, company role,
  powers of representation
\item
  \textbf{Financial attributes:} bank account verification,
  creditworthiness attestations
\item
  \textbf{Travel documents:} digital driving licenses, digital travel
  credentials
\end{itemize}

A QEAA is issued by a Qualified Trust Service Provider (QTSP) or a
government authority. When issued by a QTSP, it carries the same legal
weight as the paper credential it replaces --- a QEAA attesting a
university degree, issued by a QTSP after verification with the
university, is legally equivalent to the paper diploma.

The "qualified" designation is critical. An Electronic Attestation of
Attributes (EAA) without the "qualified" prefix does not enjoy the same
legal presumptions. The distinction mirrors the QES/AdES distinction:
qualified attestations carry a legal presumption of correctness;
non-qualified attestations are evidence to be evaluated on their merits.

\section{Selective Disclosure and
SD-JWT}\label{_selective_disclosure_and_sd_jwt}

\subsection{The Privacy Problem}\label{_the_privacy_problem}

Traditional digital credentials have a privacy problem: they are
all-or-nothing. If your credential contains your name, date of birth,
address, and nationality, presenting it to verify your age requires
revealing all four attributes --- even though the relying party only
needs to know that you are over 18.

The EUDI wallet solves this through \textbf{selective disclosure} ---
the ability to present only a subset of the attributes in a credential,
without invalidating the cryptographic signature.

\subsection{How SD-JWT Works}\label{_how_sd_jwt_works}

The ARF specifies \textbf{SD-JWT (Selective Disclosure for JWTs)} as one
of the credential formats for selective disclosure. SD-JWT is an IETF
specification (currently in the RFC track) that extends JSON Web Tokens
with selective disclosure capability.

The mechanism works as follows:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Issuance.} The issuer creates a JWT containing all attributes,
  but each disclosable attribute is replaced with a hash of the
  attribute value combined with a random salt. The original attribute
  values and their salts are provided to the wallet holder as
  "disclosures" --- separate, individually presentable data items.
\item
  \textbf{Presentation.} When the wallet presents the credential to a
  relying party, it includes the JWT (with the hashed placeholders) plus
  only the disclosures for the attributes the relying party needs. The
  relying party can verify that each disclosed attribute matches its
  hash in the JWT, confirming it was part of the original signed
  credential --- but attributes whose disclosures are withheld remain
  hidden.
\item
  \textbf{Verification.} The relying party verifies the JWT signature
  (confirming it was issued by a trusted authority), then verifies each
  disclosed attribute against its hash in the JWT. Attributes that were
  not disclosed cannot be reconstructed from the hashes (the random
  salts ensure this).
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ErrorTok{//} \ErrorTok{Simplified} \ErrorTok{SD{-}JWT} \ErrorTok{structure} \ErrorTok{(conceptual,} \ErrorTok{not} \ErrorTok{a} \ErrorTok{real} \ErrorTok{token)}
\FunctionTok{\{}
  \DataTypeTok{"iss"}\FunctionTok{:} \StringTok{"https://pid.example{-}country.eu"}\FunctionTok{,}
  \DataTypeTok{"iat"}\FunctionTok{:} \DecValTok{1709913600}\FunctionTok{,}
  \DataTypeTok{"exp"}\FunctionTok{:} \DecValTok{1741449600}\FunctionTok{,}
  \DataTypeTok{"cnf"}\FunctionTok{:} \FunctionTok{\{} \DataTypeTok{"jwk"}\FunctionTok{:} \FunctionTok{\{} \ErrorTok{...} \FunctionTok{\}} \FunctionTok{\},}  \ErrorTok{//} \ErrorTok{Holder} \ErrorTok{binding} \ErrorTok{key}

  \ErrorTok{//} \ErrorTok{Selectively} \ErrorTok{disclosable} \ErrorTok{claims} \ErrorTok{(hashed} \ErrorTok{placeholders)}
  \DataTypeTok{"\_sd"}\FunctionTok{:} \OtherTok{[}
    \StringTok{"H3JQXF...base64url\_hash\_of\_family\_name\_disclosure"}\OtherTok{,}
    \StringTok{"K9PLWT...base64url\_hash\_of\_given\_name\_disclosure"}\OtherTok{,}
    \StringTok{"M2VRZN...base64url\_hash\_of\_birth\_date\_disclosure"}
  \OtherTok{]}\FunctionTok{,}

  \ErrorTok{//} \ErrorTok{Non{-}disclosable} \ErrorTok{claims} \ErrorTok{(always} \ErrorTok{visible)}
  \DataTypeTok{"age\_over\_18"}\FunctionTok{:} \KeywordTok{true}\FunctionTok{,}
  \DataTypeTok{"issuing\_country"}\FunctionTok{:} \StringTok{"DE"}
\FunctionTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{The ISO/IEC 18013-5 mdoc
Alternative}\label{_the_isoiec_18013_5_mdoc_alternative}

The ARF also supports the \textbf{mdoc} format defined in ISO/IEC
18013-5 (originally for mobile driving licenses). mdoc uses CBOR
(Concise Binary Object Representation) instead of JSON and COSE (CBOR
Object Signing and Encryption) instead of JOSE.

Both formats support selective disclosure. The choice between them is
still under discussion in the ARF, with mdoc favored for proximity
(NFC/BLE) use cases and SD-JWT favored for remote (online) use cases.

For PDF signing purposes, the credential format does not directly matter
--- what matters is that the wallet can authenticate the user and
authorize a signing operation. The credential format affects how the
wallet proves the user's identity to the signing service, not how the
PDF signature itself is structured.

If you are building a service that accepts signed PDFs and also needs to
verify the signer's attributes (e.g., a medical prescription service
that needs to confirm the signer is a licensed doctor), you will need to
handle QEAA validation \emph{in addition to} PDF signature validation.
The QEAA is presented separately from the PDF --- it is not embedded in
the signature's CMS. Your validation workflow will have two parallel
tracks: verify the PDF signature, and verify the QEAA.

\section{Signing with the Wallet}\label{_signing_with_the_wallet}

\subsection{The Signing Flow}\label{_the_signing_flow}

The most consequential feature of the EUDI wallet for this book's
audience is its ability to create qualified electronic signatures. eIDAS
2.0 Article 5a(4)(d) requires that the wallet support QES creation.
Article 45e goes further: member states must ensure that QES creation
through the wallet is offered to natural persons free of charge for
non-professional purposes.

Free QES for every EU citizen. That sentence alone justifies this
chapter.

The signing flow works as follows:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{The user initiates a signing request.} This could be from
  within the wallet app itself, or from an external application (a web
  portal, a desktop application, a government service) that redirects to
  the wallet via a protocol defined in the ARF (OpenID4VP --- OpenID for
  Verifiable Presentations).
\item
  \textbf{The wallet authenticates the user.} The user confirms their
  identity using the wallet's authentication mechanism --- typically
  biometric (fingerprint or face recognition) or device PIN. This is the
  "sole control" requirement from eIDAS Article 26(c) and Article 29.
\item
  \textbf{The wallet presents the document hash to a remote QSCD.} The
  wallet does \emph{not} contain the private signing key on the device
  itself. Instead, it communicates with a \textbf{remote QSCD} operated
  by a QTSP. The wallet sends a hash of the document to be signed (not
  the document itself --- the hash is small and privacy-preserving).
\item
  \textbf{The remote QSCD creates the signature.} The QSCD --- an HSM in
  the QTSP's data center, certified as a Qualified Signature Creation
  Device --- signs the hash with the user's private key. The private key
  was generated on and never leaves the QSCD.
\item
  \textbf{The signature is returned to the requesting application.} The
  CMS \texttt{SignedData} (or the raw signature value, depending on the
  integration pattern) is returned to the application that initiated the
  signing request. The application embeds the signature in the PDF.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1667}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.8333}}@{}}
\caption{EUDI wallet signing flow}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Step
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Step
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Description
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
1 & Application presents document to user and requests signature (via
OpenID4VP) \\
2 & Wallet displays document summary and requests user authentication
(biometric/PIN) \\
3 & Wallet sends document hash to remote QSCD at QTSP (over mTLS,
authenticated by wallet instance credentials) \\
4 & QSCD signs the hash with user's private key; returns CMS
\texttt{SignedData} with qualified certificate and signature
timestamp \\
5 & Application receives CMS, embeds it in PDF \texttt{/Contents}, adds
DSS data for LTV, and saves the signed PDF \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{Remote QSCD: Why the Key Is Not on the
Phone}\label{_remote_qscd_why_the_key_is_not_on_the_phone}

A natural question: why not put the signing key on the phone itself?
Modern smartphones have secure elements and TEEs that can generate and
store cryptographic keys.

The answer is QSCD certification.

A QSCD must be certified under Common Criteria EAL4+ (or equivalent)
against specific Protection Profiles (EN 419 211 or EN 419 221).
Smartphone secure elements are certified for payment (EMVCo) and some
for FIDO authentication, but as of 2026, no smartphone secure element
has been certified as a QSCD under the eIDAS Protection Profiles.

The certification gap is not primarily technical --- modern secure
elements are arguably as secure as certified smart cards. The gap is
procedural: QSCD certification requires extensive evaluation against
specific attack models, documentation of the manufacturing process, and
ongoing compliance monitoring. Smartphone vendors have not pursued this
certification because, until eIDAS 2.0, there was no market demand for
it on consumer devices.

This may change. The ARF explicitly contemplates the possibility of
"local QSCD" on a device --- a future scenario where smartphone secure
elements achieve QSCD certification and the signing key can reside on
the phone. But for the initial wallet deployments, the architecture is
remote QSCD.

Remote QSCD is not a compromise --- it is the dominant model for QES
today. Even before the EUDI wallet, most QES in the EU were created
using remote QSCDs. Services like DocuSign (through its European
qualified signing infrastructure), Swisscom, InfoCert, and D-Trust all
operate remote QSCDs. The wallet extends this existing model to a new
client (the smartphone) but does not fundamentally change the
architecture.

The Cloud Signature Consortium (CSC) API --- the industry-standard REST
API for remote signing --- is being adapted for wallet integration. If
your application already supports CSC-based remote signing (Chapter 22),
adapting it for wallet-initiated signing is a relatively small change.

\subsection{The Sole Control Assurance
Problem}\label{_the_sole_control_assurance_problem}

eIDAS requires that the signing key be under the signatory's "sole
control" (Article 26(c)). When the key is on a smart card in the
signer's wallet, sole control is straightforward: the signer possesses
the card and knows the PIN.

When the key is in a remote QSCD, sole control is more complex. The
signer does not physically possess the key. The key is in an HSM in a
data center, managed by a QTSP. Sole control must be established through
authentication mechanisms that ensure only the authorized signer can
trigger a signing operation with that key.

The SCAL (Sole Control Assurance Level) framework, defined in CEN EN 419
241-1, distinguishes two levels:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{SCAL1}: The QTSP relies on an external authentication
  mechanism (e.g., the wallet app's biometric authentication) to
  authorize signing. The QTSP trusts the authentication assertion from
  the wallet.
\item
  \textbf{SCAL2}: The QTSP independently verifies the signer's
  authorization --- the signer must authenticate directly to the QTSP's
  infrastructure (e.g., through a separate OTP sent by the QTSP), in
  addition to any wallet-level authentication.
\end{itemize}

SCAL2 is required for QES. This means the remote QSCD must have its own
authentication of the signer, not just trust the wallet's claim that the
user authenticated.

In practice, this is implemented through a combination of: -
Wallet-level biometric authentication (proves the user is holding the
phone) - A cryptographic challenge from the QSCD that the wallet's
secure element signs (proves the specific wallet instance is authorized)
- An additional PIN or biometric confirmation presented to the QSCD's
authentication service (proves the user explicitly authorized \emph{this
specific signing operation})

The user experience goal is to make this feel like a single
authentication step (touch fingerprint, confirm signing), while the
underlying protocol satisfies SCAL2's multi-factor requirement.

\section{Impact on PDF Signing}\label{_impact_on_pdf_signing}

\subsection{What Changes}\label{_what_changes}

The EUDI wallet does not change the PDF signature format. A PDF signed
through the wallet contains the same structures as any other PAdES
signature: a \texttt{/Sig} dictionary with \texttt{/SubFilter}
\texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached}, a \texttt{/ByteRange} covering the file, a
\texttt{/Contents} value containing CMS \texttt{SignedData}, and (for
PAdES-B-LTA) a DSS dictionary with certificates and revocation data.

What changes is the \emph{volume}, the \emph{source}, and the
\emph{validation path}.

\textbf{Volume.} Free QES for 450 million EU citizens will produce
orders of magnitude more qualified signatures than the current market.
Your validation code must handle this volume. If your validation
involves synchronous EUTL lookups for every signature, you need caching.
If your trust store is updated manually, you need automation.

\textbf{Source.} Wallet-generated signatures will come from certificates
issued by QTSPs participating in the wallet ecosystem. These QTSPs will
be on the EUTL. They may or may not be on Adobe's AATL. If they are not
on the AATL, Acrobat users will see yellow warnings on legitimate QES
--- which will create pressure on Adobe to either auto-consume the EUTL
or expand the AATL.

\textbf{Validation path.} Wallet-generated signatures chain through a
trust path that includes: the signer's qualified certificate (issued by
a QTSP) → the QTSP's intermediate/root certificate → the EUTL entry for
the QTSP. This is the same chain as any other QES. The novelty is that
the signer is a consumer on a smartphone, not an enterprise user with a
signing portal.

\subsection{PAdES-B-LTA from Mobile}\label{_pades_b_lta_from_mobile}

The wallet ecosystem targets PAdES-B-LTA as the default profile for
wallet-generated signatures. This is the correct engineering decision:
wallet-generated signatures will be used for contracts, government
submissions, and official documents that require long-term
verifiability.

Producing a PAdES-B-LTA signature from a mobile device requires:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Signature creation} (CMS \texttt{SignedData} with
  \texttt{ESS-signing-certificate-v2}, via remote QSCD)
\item
  \textbf{Signature timestamp} (RFC 3161 \texttt{TimeStampToken} from a
  TSA, embedded as unsigned CMS attribute)
\item
  \textbf{DSS population} (certificates, OCSP responses, CRLs for the
  entire chain)
\item
  \textbf{Document timestamp} (a separate signature dictionary with
  SubFilter \texttt{ETSI.RFC3161} covering the entire file including
  DSS)
\end{enumerate}

Steps 1-2 are performed by the QSCD/QTSP infrastructure. Steps 3-4 are
performed by the application that embeds the signature in the PDF ---
which may be a server-side service, not the mobile device itself.

The typical architecture:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  The mobile app sends the document hash to the QTSP via the wallet
\item
  The QTSP signs the hash on the QSCD and obtains a signature timestamp
  from its TSA
\item
  The CMS (with timestamp) is returned to the application server
\item
  The server embeds the CMS in the PDF, populates the DSS, and adds a
  document timestamp
\item
  The signed PDF is returned to the user or submitted to the relying
  party
\end{enumerate}

The mobile device never needs to perform the PDF manipulation itself ---
that is handled by the server. This is a practical necessity: PDF
signing with LTV data is computationally modest but requires PDF parsing
libraries that are not available on all mobile platforms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{/**}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ Server}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{side method that completes a wallet}\CommentTok{{-}}\NormalTok{initiated signing operation}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ The wallet has obtained a CMS SignedData from the remote QSCD}\CommentTok{;}
 \CommentTok{*}\NormalTok{ this method embeds it in the PDF and adds LTV data}\CommentTok{.}
 \CommentTok{*/}
\KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{completeWalletSignature}\OperatorTok{(}\DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{,}
                                       \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ cmsSignedData}\OperatorTok{,}
