                                       \BuiltInTok{List}\OperatorTok{\textless{}}\BuiltInTok{X509Certificate}\OperatorTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ chain}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{Exception} \OperatorTok{\{}

\NormalTok{    PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{pdfBytes}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// Create signature and set CMS from wallet/QSCD}
\NormalTok{    PDSignature signature }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFilter}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FILTER\_ADOBE\_PPKLITE}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSubFilter}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SUBFILTER\_ETSI\_CADES\_DETACHED}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{    signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSignDate}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Calendar}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{());}

    \CommentTok{// Add signature with pre{-}built CMS}
\NormalTok{    doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addSignature}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ content }\OperatorTok{{-}\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ cmsSignedData}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// Save incrementally}
    \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\NormalTok{ baos }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{ByteArrayOutputStream}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{saveIncremental}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{baos}\OperatorTok{);}
    \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]}\NormalTok{ signedPdf }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ baos}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{toByteArray}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{    doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{close}\OperatorTok{();}

    \CommentTok{// Add DSS with LTV data (separate incremental save)}
\NormalTok{    signedPdf }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{addDssWithLtvData}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedPdf}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ chain}\OperatorTok{);}

    \CommentTok{// Add document timestamp (another incremental save)}
\NormalTok{    signedPdf }\OperatorTok{=} \FunctionTok{addDocumentTimestamp}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signedPdf}\OperatorTok{);}

\NormalTok{    log}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{info}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Wallet signature completed: PAdES{-}B{-}LTA, \{\} bytes"}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signedPdf}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{length}\OperatorTok{);}
    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ signedPdf}\OperatorTok{;}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The code above is simplified for illustration. A production
implementation must handle: pre-allocating the \texttt{/Contents}
placeholder, coordinating the byte range computation with the CMS
injection, error handling for TSA timeouts, retry logic for remote QSCD
communication failures, and verification of the completed signature.
Chapter 29 covers the full PDFBox signing pipeline.

\section{Pilot Programs}\label{_pilot_programs}

\subsection{The Large-Scale Pilots}\label{_the_large_scale_pilots}

The European Commission is funding four Large-Scale Pilots (LSPs) that
test the EUDI wallet in real-world scenarios across multiple member
states. Each pilot covers different use cases and involves different
member states.

\textbf{POTENTIAL} & France, Germany, Italy, Spain & Government services

verification, age verification, organizational identity \\

France, Germany, Czech Republic, Croatia, Italy, Austria, Greece,
Romania & Travel (digital travel credentials), education (diploma
attestation), digital payments, organizational wallet use cases \\

(Professional Qualifications, Portable Document A1) \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{What the Pilots Are
Testing}\label{_what_the_pilots_are_testing}

The signing-relevant findings from the pilots (as of early 2026, with
pilots still running):

\textbf{QES from mobile works.} The core signing flow --- user
authentication, hash submission to remote QSCD, CMS return, PDF
embedding --- has been demonstrated successfully across multiple
implementations. The user experience typically involves a single
biometric confirmation (fingerprint or face recognition) with a clear
"sign this document" prompt.

\textbf{Cross-border validation is the challenge.} Creating the
signature is (relatively) straightforward. Validating it across borders
--- ensuring that a signature created by a French citizen's wallet
validates correctly in a German government portal --- requires all
parties to consume the EUTL, support the same PAdES profiles, and handle
certificate chains from unfamiliar QTSPs. This is exactly the same
interoperability problem discussed earlier in this chapter, amplified by
scale.

\textbf{TSA integration needs standardization.} Different wallet
implementations use different TSAs, with different timestamp token
sizes, different certificate chains, and different response times. The
pilots have exposed edge cases where timestamp tokens from one TSA cause
validation issues in validators that are not prepared for the TSA's
specific certificate chain length.

\textbf{User comprehension is limited.} In pilot user testing, most
participants understood "sign this document" but did not understand what
a qualified electronic signature \emph{is}, how it differs from a basic
electronic signature, or why it has legal equivalence to a handwritten
signature. This is not a technical problem, but it has technical
consequences: if users do not understand the significance of QES, they
may not treat the signing authentication with appropriate care.

\section{What Developers Need to
Prepare}\label{_what_developers_need_to_prepare}

\subsection{If You Build Signing
Software}\label{_if_you_build_signing_software}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Support wallet-initiated signing flows.} The wallet
  communicates with your application via OpenID4VP (OpenID for
  Verifiable Presentations). You will need to implement the RP (Relying
  Party) side of the OpenID4VP protocol to receive signing requests from
  the wallet.
\item
  \textbf{Handle remote QSCD signatures.} Your application will receive
  a CMS \texttt{SignedData} blob from the QTSP, not generate it locally.
  Your signing pipeline must support "external CMS" injection ---
  computing the byte range, sending the hash for external signing,
  receiving the CMS, and embedding it in the PDF.
\item
  \textbf{Pre-allocate generously.} Wallet-generated CMS may include
  longer certificate chains than you are accustomed to (government PKI
  chains are often deeper than commercial CA chains). Pre-allocate at
  least 64 KB for PAdES-B-LTA.
\item
  \textbf{Add DSS and document timestamps server-side.} The mobile
  device will not populate the DSS or add document timestamps. Your
  server must do this, which means your server needs access to OCSP
  responders and CRL distribution points for the signer's certificate
  chain, plus a TSA for the document timestamp.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{If You Build Validation
Software}\label{_if_you_build_validation_software}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Consume the EUTL programmatically.} Wallet-generated
  signatures chain to QTSPs on the EUTL. If your validator uses a static
  trust store, it will reject these signatures. Implement automated EUTL
  consumption --- fetch the LOT (List of Trusted Lists), parse each
  member state's trusted list, extract the trust anchors, and update
  your trust store on a regular schedule (daily is typical).
\item
  \textbf{Cache the EUTL.} With millions of wallet-generated signatures
  to validate, you cannot fetch the EUTL for every validation. Cache the
  parsed trust anchors with a TTL (time-to-live) and refresh
  periodically.
\item
  \textbf{Support deep certificate chains.} Government PKI chains can be
  4-5 certificates deep (signer → intermediate 1 → intermediate 2 →
  root). Ensure your chain-building code handles this without
  performance degradation.
\item
  \textbf{Report the PAdES profile level.} Wallet-generated signatures
  should be PAdES-B-LTA. If a wallet-generated signature is missing LTV
  data (B-T or B-B instead of B-LTA), this indicates a problem in the
  signing pipeline. Report the profile level to help the signer or their
  application diagnose the issue.
\item
  \textbf{Prepare for QEAA validation.} If your application needs to
  verify not just \emph{who signed} but \emph{what qualifications the
  signer has} (e.g., a medical prescription signed by a doctor), you
  will need to validate QEAAs in addition to the PDF signature. This is
  a separate validation path: the QEAA is a signed credential (SD-JWT or
  mdoc) that you verify against the issuing QTSP's certificate on the
  EUTL.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{If You Build Enterprise
Systems}\label{_if_you_build_enterprise_systems}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Update your trust store management.} If your enterprise trust
  store is managed manually (a Java \texttt{cacerts} file, a Windows
  certificate store), automate it. Wallet-generated signatures will come
  from QTSPs that may not be in your current trust store.
\item
  \textbf{Budget for EUTL integration.} Consuming the EUTL is not
  trivial: the LOT and individual trusted lists are XML documents signed
  with specific certificates, using a specific schema (ETSI TS 119 612).
  The EU DSS framework (Chapter 23) provides a reference implementation,
  but integrating it into your architecture requires engineering effort.
\item
  \textbf{Plan for volume.} If your system currently validates hundreds
  of qualified signatures per day, prepare for thousands or tens of
  thousands. The wallet will democratize QES, and your systems will see
  the increase.
\item
  \textbf{Test with pilot certificates.} The EUDI wallet pilots issue
  test certificates that can be used for integration testing. Engage
  with the pilot programs relevant to your member state and obtain test
  credentials for your validation pipeline.
\end{enumerate}

\section{The Open Questions}\label{_the_open_questions}

The EUDI wallet is ambitious, unprecedented, and not yet fully defined.
Several significant questions remain open as of early 2026:

\textbf{Will users actually adopt it?} Mandating reliance parties to
accept the wallet creates demand, but the wallet still needs a
compelling user experience. If the signing flow feels cumbersome ---
multiple authentication steps, slow QSCD response times, confusing
consent screens --- users will avoid it.

\textbf{Will Apple and Google cooperate?} The wallet runs on
smartphones, which means it needs integration with iOS and Android. Both
platforms control access to NFC, secure elements, and biometric APIs.
The EU Digital Markets Act (DMA) requires gatekeepers to allow
third-party wallet apps to access device hardware, but the
implementation details are being negotiated, and the mobile platforms
have commercial interests in their own wallet ecosystems (Apple Pay,
Google Pay).

\textbf{What happens with AATL?} If hundreds of millions of qualified
signatures are created through the wallet but the signing certificates
are not in Adobe's AATL, Acrobat users will see yellow warnings on
legitimate QES. This creates political pressure on Adobe to either
accept the EUTL as a trust anchor by default or face regulatory action.
The outcome will shape the PDF signature ecosystem for years.

\textbf{Will the credential format converge?} The current ARF supports
both SD-JWT and mdoc. Supporting two formats doubles the implementation
effort for relying parties. The industry is watching to see if one
format emerges as dominant or if the dual-format approach persists.

\textbf{How will cross-border QES work in practice?} The technical
framework is defined. The legal framework mandates mutual recognition.
But whether a German government portal will actually accept a Romanian
wallet-generated QES without friction --- that depends on whether all
the implementation details are correct across two different member state
implementations, two different QTSP certificate chains, and two
different validation stacks.

The answers to these questions will determine whether the EUDI wallet
becomes the most significant development in digital signing since eIDAS
itself --- or another ambitious European digital project that works in
theory and disappoints in practice.

The EUDI wallet is a moving target. The ARF is being updated,
implementing acts are being published, and pilots are generating
findings that feed back into the specifications. If you are reading this
in 2027 or later, some of the specifics in this chapter may have
changed. The architectural principles --- remote QSCD, selective
disclosure, EUTL-based trust, PAdES-B-LTA as the target profile --- are
stable. The protocol details and credential format choices may have
evolved.

\section{Key Takeaways}\label{_key_takeaways_14}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{eIDAS 2.0 (Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0))} mandates
  that every EU member state offer a digital identity wallet to citizens
  by end of 2026, with QES creation capability included
\item
  \textbf{The EUDI wallet} is a smartphone application that stores
  government-issued identity data (PID) and qualified attribute
  attestations (QEAAs) and can create qualified electronic signatures
\item
  \textbf{Remote QSCD} is the signing architecture: the private key
  lives in a certified HSM operated by a QTSP, not on the phone itself
  --- the wallet provides authentication and sole control
\item
  \textbf{Selective disclosure} (via SD-JWT or mdoc) allows users to
  share only the attributes a relying party needs, without revealing
  their full identity
\item
  \textbf{Free QES for citizens} (Article 45e) will dramatically
  increase the volume of qualified signatures --- plan your
  infrastructure for orders-of-magnitude growth
\item
  \textbf{PAdES-B-LTA is the target profile} for wallet-generated
  signatures --- server-side DSS population and document timestamping
  are required
\item
  \textbf{EUTL consumption is essential} --- wallet-generated signatures
  chain to QTSPs that may not be in your current trust store; automate
  EUTL updates
\item
  \textbf{Four Large-Scale Pilots} (POTENTIAL, NOBID, EWC, DC4EU) are
  testing the wallet across sectors and member states; engage with them
  for test credentials
\item
  \textbf{Open questions remain} about user adoption, platform
  cooperation (Apple/Google), AATL integration, credential format
  convergence, and real-world cross-border validation
\item
  \textbf{The architectural principles are PDF-native} --- if you
  understand PAdES, certificate chains, and the EUTL, you understand the
  cryptographic foundation of the EUDI wallet
\end{itemize}

\chapter{Code, Tools \&
Implementation}\label{_code_tools_implementation}

\chapter{The Java PDF Library
Landscape}\label{_the_java_pdf_library_landscape}

Before we write a single line of signing code, we need to talk about the
tools we're using --- where they came from, why they exist, and what
baggage they carry. If you've ever wondered why there are three
different "iText" libraries on Maven Central, why PDFBox's API changed
so dramatically between version 2 and version 3, or why a library from
the year 2000 with known bugs is still running in Fortune 500 production
systems --- this chapter is for you.

The Java PDF ecosystem is not just a collection of libraries. It is a
story of open-source idealism, licensing betrayal (depending on who you
ask), corporate acquisitions, community forks, and the kind of technical
debt that accumulates when enterprises build critical infrastructure on
a dependency and then the rules change. Understanding this history is
not optional background reading --- it directly affects the code you
write, the signatures you produce, and the compatibility problems you
will encounter.

\section{The iText Saga: From PhD Project to Corporate Licensing
Drama}\label{_the_itext_saga_from_phd_project_to_corporate_licensing_drama}

\subsection{Bruno Lowagie and the Birth of iText
(2000--2009)}\label{_bruno_lowagie_and_the_birth_of_itext_20002009}

The story of Java PDF processing begins, for practical purposes, with
Bruno Lowagie. In 2000, Lowagie --- a Belgian mathematician working at a
printing company --- needed to generate PDF documents from Java. The
options at the time were essentially nonexistent. Adobe had published
the PDF specification, but the only tools that could create PDFs were
Adobe's own products (Acrobat, Distiller) and a handful of expensive
commercial libraries. There was nothing usable in the open-source Java
world.

So Lowagie wrote his own library. He called it \textbf{iText}, released
it under the \textbf{Mozilla Public License (MPL)} and later the
\textbf{GNU Lesser General Public License (LGPL)}, and published it on
SourceForge --- the GitHub of its era. The LGPL license was critically
important: it allowed companies to use iText in proprietary software
without open-sourcing their own code, as long as they didn't modify the
iText library itself. This is the license model that made iText
ubiquitous.

The timing was perfect. Java was exploding in enterprise environments.
J2EE application servers were the dominant architecture. Companies
needed to generate invoices, reports, contracts, and forms as PDFs ---
millions of them per day, in automated workflows. iText was free, it
worked, and its API was straightforward:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// iText 2.x — the classic API that a generation of Java developers learned}
\BuiltInTok{Document}\NormalTok{ document }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{Document}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{PdfWriter}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{document}\OperatorTok{,} \KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{FileOutputStream}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"output.pdf"}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{document}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{open}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{document}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{add}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{Paragraph}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Hello, PDF"}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{document}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{close}\OperatorTok{();}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This simplicity was revolutionary. You didn't need to understand PDF
internals. You didn't need to manipulate dictionaries or worry about
cross-reference tables. You told iText what you wanted on the page, and
it produced a valid PDF. The library handled fonts, images, tables,
forms, encryption, and --- crucially for our purposes ---
\textbf{digital signatures}.

By the mid-2000s, iText was everywhere. It was embedded in JBoss,
Alfresco, Jasper Reports, BIRT, and hundreds of other Java projects.
Large banks, insurance companies, government agencies, and healthcare
organizations had built their document generation pipelines on iText.
The Maven coordinates \texttt{com.lowagie:itext} were among the most
downloaded artifacts in the Java ecosystem.

\subsection{The LGPL-to-AGPL Switch: The Day the Rules Changed
(2009)}\label{_the_lgpl_to_agpl_switch_the_day_the_rules_changed_2009}

On December 1, 2009, Bruno Lowagie changed the license of iText from
\textbf{LGPL} to \textbf{AGPL} (GNU Affero General Public License),
starting with version \textbf{5.0.0}.

This was, depending on your perspective, either a reasonable business
decision or an act of betrayal against the community that had made iText
successful. Both perspectives have merit, and understanding the
controversy matters because its consequences are still playing out in
production systems today.

\textbf{What AGPL means in practice:} The AGPL is like the GPL, but with
one additional requirement: if you use an AGPL-licensed library in
software that users interact with over a network (which includes web
applications, SaaS products, and API services), you must release the
\emph{entire source code} of your application under the AGPL. Not just
your modifications to iText --- your entire application.

For the thousands of companies using iText in their proprietary Java
backends --- generating invoices, processing insurance claims, signing
contracts --- this was devastating. They had exactly three options:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Open-source their entire application} under AGPL ---
  unthinkable for most enterprises
\item
  \textbf{Buy a commercial license} from iText Group NV (the company
  Lowagie founded to monetize iText) --- expensive, typically
  €5,000--€50,000+ per year depending on deployment scale
\item
  \textbf{Stay on iText 2.1.7} --- the last LGPL-licensed version,
  released in 2009 --- forever
\end{enumerate}

Most chose option 3.

\textbf{Why Lowagie made the switch:} Lowagie's stated reasons were
straightforward: he had spent nearly a decade maintaining a complex
library for free, while companies worth billions used it to generate
revenue without contributing back. He wanted to build a business. The
AGPL created a forcing function: use iText for free in open-source
projects, or pay for a commercial license. This is a perfectly
legitimate business model --- MongoDB, Elasticsearch, and Redis would
later make similar moves (though with different licenses).

\textbf{Why the community was angry:} The anger wasn't about the
business model itself --- it was about the trust violation. Enterprise
architects had chosen iText \emph{because} of its LGPL license. They had
written it into procurement documents, gotten legal approval, and built
systems that depended on it. The license change felt like a
bait-and-switch: attract an enormous user base with a permissive
license, then change the terms once everyone is locked in.

The fact that existing versions remained LGPL was cold comfort. iText
2.1.7 was already showing its age in 2009. By 2012, it had known bugs in
font handling, incomplete PDF 1.7 support, and no support for the newer
CMS structures that PAdES was beginning to require. But upgrading to
iText 5.x meant either paying or going AGPL.

If you encounter a PDF signed by a Java application that uses
\texttt{com.lowagie.text.pdf.PdfSignatureAppearance} in its imports ---
that's iText 2.x. The class was renamed to
\texttt{com.itextpdf.text.pdf.PdfSignatureAppearance} in iText 5.x (note
the package change from \texttt{com.lowagie} to \texttt{com.itextpdf}).
The package name tells you which era of iText produced the signature.

\subsection{The CMS Structure Problem: Why Old iText Signatures Look
Different}\label{_the_cms_structure_problem_why_old_itext_signatures_look_different}

Here's where the licensing drama directly impacts signature
compatibility.

iText 2.x (the LGPL version that much of the world is still using)
produces CMS \texttt{SignedData} structures that are technically valid
per RFC 5652 --- Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS), but differ from
what modern validators expect in several ways:

\textbf{Encapsulated vs. Detached content:} Early iText versions could
produce CMS signatures with \emph{encapsulated} content --- meaning the
signed hash was embedded inside the \texttt{SignedData} structure's
\texttt{encapContentInfo} field rather than being referenced externally
via the PDF byte range. This was not wrong per the CMS spec, but it
created problems:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Modern PAdES validators expect detached signatures (SubFilter
  \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} or \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached}), where
  the signed content is the byte range in the PDF itself --- not a copy
  inside the CMS
\item
  Some validators would see encapsulated content and assume the
  signature was standalone (not bound to the PDF), failing validation
  even though the cryptographic math was correct
\item
  The double-hash problem: if the signed bytes in the CMS didn't match
  what a byte-range-aware validator computed from the PDF file, the
  signature appeared invalid
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Missing signed attributes:} iText 2.x did not always include the
\texttt{ESS-signing-certificate-v2} attribute that PAdES requires.
Without this attribute, the signature is valid per ISO 32000-2 but
\emph{not} PAdES-compliant --- which means it fails validation in any
system that enforces PAdES profiles (including EU government validation
services and Adobe Acrobat in its stricter validation modes).

\textbf{SHA-1 default:} iText 2.1.7 defaulted to SHA-1 for the message
digest. SHA-1 was already theoretically weakened by 2009 (Wang and Yu's
collision attacks were published in 2005), and it was definitively
broken in 2017 when the SHAttered attack produced actual colliding
files. PDFs signed with iText 2.x before SHA-256 became the norm carry
SHA-1 digests, which modern validators increasingly flag or reject.

If your organization has a corpus of PDFs signed between 2005 and 2015
by Java applications, there is a significant chance they were signed
with iText 2.x using SHA-1 and potentially using encapsulated (not
detached) CMS content. These signatures may fail validation in modern
tools.

To verify: extract the CMS SignedData from the \texttt{/Contents} entry
and check:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Is \texttt{encapContentInfo.eContentType} set with actual content?
  (encapsulated --- old style)
\item
  Is the digest algorithm SHA-1 (\texttt{OID\ 1.3.14.3.2.26})?
  (deprecated)
\item
  Is the \texttt{ESS-signing-certificate-v2} attribute present in
  \texttt{signedAttrs}? (missing = not PAdES-compliant)
\end{enumerate}

Chapter 31 provides code to perform all three checks.

\subsection{iText 5.x, 7.x, and 8.x: The Commercial
Evolution}\label{_itext_5_x_7_x_and_8_x_the_commercial_evolution}

After the AGPL switch, iText evolved rapidly --- but behind a paywall
for commercial use.

\textbf{iText 5.x (2009--2016):} Maintained the original API design but
added PDF 2.0 features, improved CMS handling, and better PAdES support.
The package name changed from \texttt{com.lowagie} to
\texttt{com.itextpdf}. If you see both package prefixes in the same
project's classpath --- and this happens more often than you'd think ---
you're looking at a project that tried to use both the old LGPL version
and the new AGPL version simultaneously, which is a recipe for
classloading nightmares.

\textbf{iText 7.x (2016--2023):} A complete rewrite with a modular
architecture. The API was entirely different from iText 5.x ---
migration was not trivial. iText 7 introduced separate modules for core
PDF operations, forms, signing, and other features. The signing module
(\texttt{itext7-sign}) included comprehensive PAdES support, including
B-LTA profiles with embedded timestamps and revocation data. The
commercial license became the primary offering, with AGPL remaining for
open-source projects.

\textbf{iText 8.x (2023--present):} The current generation, continuing
the modular architecture with added support for PDF 2.0 features,
improved accessibility tagging, and enhanced signing capabilities. iText
Group NV (now iText Software, headquartered in Ghent, Belgium, with
offices in the US and Singapore) is a substantial commercial entity with
paying enterprise customers worldwide.

The commercial iText is, by all accounts, a high-quality library. Its
signing implementation is mature, well-tested, and PAdES-compliant. But
at commercial license prices that can reach tens of thousands of euros
per year for enterprise deployments, it is not an option for every
project --- which is why the open-source alternatives exist.

\subsection{The IBM Connection}\label{_the_ibm_connection}

IBM's involvement with iText is a lesser-known but important part of the
story. In the early 2000s, IBM was one of the largest users of iText ---
embedding it in several of their enterprise products, including
\textbf{IBM Content Manager}, \textbf{IBM Filenet}, and various
WebSphere-based document processing systems.

IBM contributed patches and improvements back to the project during the
LGPL era, which was entirely within the LGPL's collaborative spirit.
However, when the license changed to AGPL, IBM --- like most large
enterprises --- was unwilling to either open-source their proprietary
products or pay iText's commercial licensing fees for a dependency that
had previously been free.

This created an awkward situation: IBM's products contained iText 2.x
code, their customers expected continued PDF functionality, and the
upgrade path required either legal procurement (commercial iText
license) or engineering investment (migrate to a different library).
IBM's response, like most enterprises in this position, was a
combination of staying on old versions and gradually migrating new
development to Apache PDFBox --- which was, by then, becoming mature
enough for production use.

The IBM story is representative of what happened across the industry:
hundreds of enterprises had iText deeply embedded in their systems, and
the license change created a slow-motion migration that, in many cases,
is still incomplete in 2026.

\section{OpenPDF: The Community Fork That Refuses to
Die}\label{_openpdf_the_community_fork_that_refuses_to_die}

\subsection{The Fork (2016)}\label{_the_fork_2016}

When it became clear that iText 5.x was permanently AGPL for commercial
use, and that iText 2.1.7 was accruing unfixed bugs and missing modern
features, a group of developers forked iText 2.1.7 under the name
\textbf{OpenPDF} (initially called \textbf{LibrePDF}).

The fork was hosted on GitHub under the \texttt{LibrePDF/OpenPDF}
repository, and it was explicitly positioned as the LGPL-licensed
continuation of the original iText codebase. The initial stated goals
were modest: fix bugs, add basic modern features (SHA-256 support,
better font handling), and keep the library usable for projects that
needed LGPL licensing.

\subsection{What OpenPDF Is (and
Isn't)}\label{_what_openpdf_is_and_isnt}

OpenPDF today (version 2.0.x, as of 2026) is a capable PDF generation
library that maintains API compatibility with iText 2.x while adding
features that the original 2.1.7 never had:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{SHA-256/384/512 message digests} for signing (critical ---
  SHA-1 is no longer acceptable)
\item
  \textbf{AES-256 encryption} support
\item
  \textbf{Improved font handling}, including better CJK support and
  TrueType subsetting
\item
  \textbf{Bug fixes} for dozens of issues that were never patched in
  iText 2.1.7
\end{itemize}

However, OpenPDF also carries significant limitations:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  It is built on the iText 2.x architecture, which was designed for PDF
  1.7 and earlier
\item
  \textbf{No PDF 2.0 support} --- cross-reference streams, tagged PDF
  improvements, and other 2.0 features are absent
\item
  \textbf{Limited PAdES support} --- basic PAdES-B-B signatures are
  possible, but B-LT and B-LTA profiles (which require DSS dictionary
  construction and archival timestamps) are not natively supported
\item
  \textbf{No built-in LTV (Long-Term Validation)} --- you'll need to
  construct the DSS dictionary yourself
\item
  The contributor community is small --- a handful of maintainers, not
  the large team that iText Group NV employs
\item
  \textbf{Documentation is sparse} --- the iText 2.x book (Lowagie's
  "iText in Action") is the closest thing to comprehensive
  documentation, and it describes an API from 2008
\end{itemize}

OpenPDF lives at \texttt{com.github.librepdf:openpdf} on Maven Central.
Do not confuse it with \texttt{com.lowagie:itext} (the original iText
2.1.7) or \texttt{com.itextpdf:itextpdf} (the AGPL iText 5.x). All three
exist on Maven Central. All three have different licenses, different
capabilities, and different bugs. Getting the wrong one in your
dependency tree is a common source of confusion --- and potential legal
liability.

\subsection{Should You Use OpenPDF?}\label{_should_you_use_openpdf}

For \textbf{PDF generation} (creating invoices, reports, forms) where
you need an LGPL-licensed library and don't require PDF 2.0 features:
OpenPDF is a reasonable choice.

For \textbf{digital signatures}: OpenPDF can produce basic CMS
signatures, but for PAdES compliance, long-term validation, and the kind
of robust signing infrastructure this book teaches, \textbf{PDFBox is
the better choice}. PDFBox's signing API is more actively maintained,
better tested against the Bochum attack scenarios, and has a larger
community contributing security fixes.

That said --- if you have an existing codebase built on iText 2.x that
you cannot easily migrate, OpenPDF is the path of least resistance for
getting off the unmaintained 2.1.7 version while keeping your existing
API calls intact.

\section{Apache PDFBox: The Open-Source
Standard}\label{_apache_pdfbox_the_open_source_standard}

\subsection{Origin Story: Ben Litchfield and the Apache
Incubation}\label{_origin_story_ben_litchfield_and_the_apache_incubation}

Apache PDFBox started as \textbf{PDFBox} --- a standalone project
created by \textbf{Ben Litchfield} in 2002. Litchfield, working as a
Java developer, needed to \emph{read} existing PDF documents --- not
just create them (which was iText's primary strength at the time). He
needed to extract text, parse structures, and manipulate the low-level
PDF object model. iText was designed as a PDF \emph{writer}; PDFBox was
designed as a PDF \emph{reader} that could also write.

This fundamental design difference --- reader-first vs. writer-first ---
is still visible in the APIs today:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{iText/OpenPDF} excels at high-level document construction:
  "add a paragraph here, put a table there, generate the PDF"
\item
  \textbf{PDFBox} excels at low-level document manipulation: "give me
  the COS dictionary for object 42, let me modify the annotation
  rectangle, show me the raw font encoding"
\end{itemize}

For digital signature work, PDFBox's reader-first architecture is a
significant advantage. Signing a PDF requires understanding the existing
document structure, computing byte ranges, parsing existing signatures,
and manipulating specific dictionaries --- all tasks where low-level
access is essential.

In 2008, PDFBox entered the \textbf{Apache Software Foundation}
incubator process, and in 2009 it graduated as a top-level Apache
project under the \textbf{Apache License 2.0} --- one of the most
permissive open-source licenses available. Unlike iText's LGPL→AGPL
journey, PDFBox has been Apache-licensed since inception and will remain
so. This licensing certainty is one of the reasons enterprises migrated
to PDFBox.

\subsection{PDFBox 2.x: The Workhorse
(2016--2023)}\label{_pdfbox_2_x_the_workhorse_20162023}

PDFBox 2.0 was the version that brought PDFBox into mainstream
production use. It featured:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  A complete COS (Carousel Object System) model for low-level PDF
  manipulation
\item
  Text extraction with position information
\item
  PDF rendering (via \texttt{PDFRenderer})
\item
  AcroForm support (creating, filling, and flattening forms)
\item
  Digital signature support via \texttt{PDDocument.addSignature()}
\item
  Font handling (Type1, TrueType, CID fonts, subsetting)
\end{itemize}

The signing API in PDFBox 2.x was functional but had rough edges. The
\texttt{SignatureInterface} required you to provide a
\texttt{sign(InputStream)} method that received the bytes to sign and
returned the CMS \texttt{SignedData} as a byte array. The actual CMS
construction was your responsibility --- typically using BouncyCastle
(we'll discuss this later). PDFBox handled the PDF-specific parts:
creating the signature dictionary, computing the byte range, reserving
space for \texttt{/Contents}, and performing the incremental save.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// PDFBox 2.x signing pattern}
\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ PDDocument}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{load}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{inputFile}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{PDSignature signature }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFilter}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FILTER\_ADOBE\_PPKLITE}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSubFilter}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SUBFILTER\_ADBE\_PKCS7\_DETACHED}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Signer Name"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSignDate}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Calendar}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{());}

\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addSignature}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{,} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{SignatureInterface}\OperatorTok{()} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \AttributeTok{@Override}
    \KeywordTok{public} \DataTypeTok{byte}\OperatorTok{[]} \FunctionTok{sign}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{InputStream}\NormalTok{ content}\OperatorTok{)} \KeywordTok{throws} \BuiltInTok{IOException} \OperatorTok{\{}
        \CommentTok{// Your BouncyCastle CMS construction goes here}
        \ControlFlowTok{return} \FunctionTok{createCMSSignature}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{content}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ certificate}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ privateKey}\OperatorTok{);}
    \OperatorTok{\}}
\OperatorTok{\});}

\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{saveIncremental}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{outputStream}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{close}\OperatorTok{();}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This separation of concerns --- PDFBox handles the PDF, you handle the
cryptography --- is elegant in theory but demanding in practice. Getting
the CMS structure right for PAdES compliance requires deep knowledge of
signed attributes, certificate embedding, and timestamp handling.
Chapters 29 and 30 provide the complete implementation.

\subsection{PDFBox 3.x: The Breaking Change
(2024)}\label{_pdfbox_3_x_the_breaking_change_2024}

PDFBox 3.0 was released in 2024 as a major version bump with
\textbf{breaking API changes}. The migration was significant enough to
catch many projects off guard:

PDFBox 3.x is \textbf{not} a drop-in replacement for 2.x. If you're
upgrading, budget significant development time. The changes are
pervasive and affect every area of the API.

The most impactful changes for signing:

\textbf{Loading documents:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// PDFBox 2.x}
\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ PDDocument}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{load}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{File}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"document.pdf"}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ PDDocument}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{load}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{byteArray}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// PDFBox 3.x — PDDocument.load() is GONE}
\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \BuiltInTok{File}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"document.pdf"}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{PDDocument doc }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ Loader}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{loadPDF}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{byteArray}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Font construction:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// PDFBox 2.x}
\NormalTok{PDType1Font font }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HELVETICA}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{// PDFBox 3.x — static font constants are GONE}
\NormalTok{PDType1Font font }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDType1Font}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{Standard14Fonts}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FontName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{HELVETICA}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{AcroForm API:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// PDFBox 2.x}
\DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ needsAppearances }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ acroForm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{isNeedAppearances}\OperatorTok{();}

\CommentTok{// PDFBox 3.x — method renamed}
\DataTypeTok{boolean}\NormalTok{ needsAppearances }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ acroForm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getNeedAppearances}\OperatorTok{();}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Font encoding access:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// PDFBox 2.x}
\BuiltInTok{Encoding}\NormalTok{ encoding }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getEncoding}\OperatorTok{();} \CommentTok{// Worked for most font types}

\CommentTok{// PDFBox 3.x — getEncoding() removed from PDFont interface}
\CommentTok{// Must access encoding through COS dictionary directly}
\NormalTok{COSDictionary fontDict }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ font}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getCOSObject}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{COSBase encoding }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ fontDict}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDictionaryObject}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{ENCODING}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Content streams:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// PDFBox 2.x — could write to form XObjects with PDPageContentStream}
\NormalTok{PDPageContentStream cs }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDPageContentStream}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{doc}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ formXObject}\OperatorTok{,} \KeywordTok{...}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// PDFBox 3.x — PDPageContentStream is for pages ONLY}
\CommentTok{// For form XObjects, write raw PDF operators via the stream\textquotesingle{}s output}
\BuiltInTok{OutputStream}\NormalTok{ os }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ formXObject}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getStream}\OperatorTok{().}\FunctionTok{createOutputStream}\OperatorTok{();}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The signing API itself (\texttt{PDDocument.addSignature()},
\texttt{SignatureInterface}, \texttt{PDSignature}) remained structurally
similar, but the surrounding changes --- especially in document loading,
font handling, and content stream creation --- mean that any signing
code that also creates visual signature appearances needs substantial
rework.

\textbf{Migration strategy:} Don't try to upgrade PDFBox in-place.
Create a new branch, update the dependency, let the compiler tell you
every break, and fix them systematically. The PDFBox team maintains a
\href{https://pdfbox.apache.org/3.0/migration.html}{migration guide}
that covers the major changes, but the edge cases (especially around
font encoding and content stream APIs) will require reading the 3.x
Javadoc carefully.

\subsection{The PDFBox Signing Infrastructure: What's Under the
Hood}\label{_the_pdfbox_signing_infrastructure_whats_under_the_hood}

PDFBox's signing implementation is one of the most impressive pieces of
open-source PDF engineering. It's worth understanding the high-level
architecture, because when something goes wrong during signing --- and
it will --- you need to know where to look.

The signing process in PDFBox involves these internal steps:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Signature dictionary creation}: PDFBox creates the
  \texttt{/Sig} dictionary with the properties you set
  (\texttt{/Filter}, \texttt{/SubFilter}, \texttt{/Name}, \texttt{/M},
  etc.)
\item
  \textbf{Space estimation}: PDFBox needs to know how much space to
  reserve for the \texttt{/Contents} hex string \emph{before} the
  signature is computed --- because the signature covers the bytes
  outside the \texttt{/Contents} gap, and the file must be written
  before the signature can be calculated. The default is 16KB (32,768
  hex characters), but PAdES-B-LTA signatures with embedded timestamps
  and revocation data can easily exceed this.
\item
  \textbf{Byte range computation}: PDFBox calculates the
  \texttt{/ByteRange} array --- the two regions of the file that the
  signature covers (everything before and after the \texttt{/Contents}
  value)
\item
  \textbf{Incremental save}: The modified PDF is written as an
  incremental update --- appended to the original file, preserving
  previous revisions
\item
  \textbf{Signing callback}: PDFBox passes the byte range data (as an
  \texttt{InputStream}) to your \texttt{SignatureInterface.sign()}
  implementation
\item
  \textbf{Signature injection}: The returned CMS bytes are hex-encoded
  and written into the reserved \texttt{/Contents} space
\end{enumerate}

If the CMS signature is larger than the reserved space, the signing
fails. If it's smaller (which is normal), the remaining space is filled
with zeros --- the zero-padding that you see in the hex dump of any
signed PDF's \texttt{/Contents} entry.

This architecture means that \textbf{PDFBox does not construct the CMS
signature itself}. It provides the bytes to sign, and you provide the
signature. This is both a strength (maximum flexibility --- you can use
any CMS library, any algorithm, any certificate) and a burden (you must
get the CMS right yourself). That's why the next chapter is dedicated
entirely to BouncyCastle CMS construction.

\section{The Legion of the Bouncy
Castle}\label{_the_legion_of_the_bouncy_castle}

\subsection{Why BouncyCastle Exists}\label{_why_bouncycastle_exists_2}

Java has a built-in cryptographic provider --- the \texttt{SunJSSE} and
\texttt{SunJCE} providers that ship with every JDK. So why does almost
every Java PDF signing project use BouncyCastle instead?

Three reasons:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{CMS support}: The JDK's built-in providers do not include a
  CMS (\texttt{SignedData}) implementation. They provide the raw
  cryptographic primitives (RSA, ECDSA, SHA-256, etc.), but not the
  higher-level CMS container format that PDF signatures require.
  BouncyCastle's \texttt{bcpkix} module provides
  \texttt{CMSSignedDataGenerator}, \texttt{CMSSignedData}, and all the
  ASN.1 infrastructure needed to construct and parse CMS structures.
\item
  \textbf{Algorithm breadth}: BouncyCastle supports algorithms that the
  JDK providers don't --- including EdDSA (Ed25519, Ed448), GOST, SM2,
  and various other national or emerging algorithms. While PDF
  signatures currently use RSA and ECDSA almost exclusively, the ability
  to work with less common algorithms is valuable for government and
  specialized applications.
\item
  \textbf{ASN.1 library}: BouncyCastle's ASN.1 library (\texttt{bcutil}
  and \texttt{bcprov}) is the de facto standard for Java ASN.1
  encoding/decoding. When you need to parse a certificate, construct a
  signed attribute, or decode an OCSP response, BouncyCastle's ASN.1
  classes are what you use.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{The BouncyCastle
Ecosystem}\label{_the_bouncycastle_ecosystem}

BouncyCastle is not a single JAR --- it's a family of related libraries:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1250}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.6250}}@{}}
\caption{BouncyCastle modules relevant to PDF signing}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Module
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Maven Artifact
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Purpose
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Module
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Maven Artifact
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Purpose
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\textbf{Provider} & \texttt{bcprov-jdk18on} & Core cryptographic
provider: symmetric/asymmetric ciphers, hashes, key generation, random
number generation. Register via
\texttt{Security.addProvider(new\ BouncyCastleProvider())} \\
\textbf{PKIX/CMS/EAC} & \texttt{bcpkix-jdk18on} & CMS
\texttt{SignedData} construction/parsing, PKCS\#10 certificate requests,
OCSP, timestamp protocol (RFC 3161), certificate path validation.
\textbf{This is the module you need for PDF signing.} \\
\textbf{Utilities} & \texttt{bcutil-jdk18on} & PEM reader/writer, ASN.1
dump utilities, base64 encoding. Pulled in as a transitive
dependency. \\
\end{longtable}

You must explicitly register the BouncyCastle provider before using it.
If you forget this step, you'll get cryptic
\texttt{NoSuchAlgorithmException} errors at runtime.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{org}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{bouncycastle}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{jce}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{provider}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{;}
\KeywordTok{import} \ImportTok{java}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{security}\OperatorTok{.}\ImportTok{Security}\OperatorTok{;}

\CommentTok{// Do this once at application startup}
\ControlFlowTok{if} \OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Security}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"BC"}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{==} \KeywordTok{null}\OperatorTok{)} \OperatorTok{\{}
    \BuiltInTok{Security}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{addProvider}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{BouncyCastleProvider}\OperatorTok{());}
\OperatorTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

In Spring Boot applications, put this in a \texttt{@Configuration} class
with a \texttt{@PostConstruct} method. In standalone applications, put
it in your \texttt{main()} method. Forgetting this registration is the
\#1 cause of "it works in my IDE but fails in production" signing
issues.

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{BouncyCastle Version History and the
\texttt{-jdk18on}
Suffix}{BouncyCastle Version History and the -jdk18on Suffix}}\label{_bouncycastle_version_history_and_the_jdk18on_suffix}

BouncyCastle's artifact naming has changed over the years, which causes
dependency confusion:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{bcprov-jdk15} --- for JDK 1.5 (ancient, do not use)
\item
  \texttt{bcprov-jdk15on} --- for JDK 1.5 and later (the long-lived
  artifact ID)
\item
  \texttt{bcprov-jdk18on} --- for JDK 1.8 (Java 8) and later (current,
  use this)
\end{itemize}

The \texttt{jdk18on} variants (introduced with BouncyCastle 1.71)
dropped support for Java versions before 8 and added Java module system
(\texttt{module-info.java}) support. If you're on Java 11 or later (and
for PDF signing with PDFBox 3.x, you should be on Java 17+), use the
\texttt{jdk18on} artifacts exclusively.

Do not mix \texttt{jdk15on} and \texttt{jdk18on} artifacts in the same
classpath. BouncyCastle uses a provider registration model, and having
two versions of the provider registered simultaneously causes
unpredictable failures --- the wrong version may handle a given
algorithm, producing incompatible ASN.1 structures.

\section{EU DSS: The European Commission's Signing
Framework}\label{_eu_dss_the_european_commissions_signing_framework}

\subsection{What EU DSS Is}\label{_what_eu_dss_is}

There is one more library that deserves its own section --- not because
it replaces PDFBox or BouncyCastle, but because it \emph{builds on top
of them} and adds a layer that would take months to implement yourself.

\textbf{EU DSS} (Digital Signature Services) is an open-source Java
framework developed and maintained by \textbf{Nowina Solutions} under
contract from the \textbf{European Commission}. It is the reference
implementation for creating and validating digital signatures that
comply with the European eIDAS regulation --- which means it implements
the full PAdES, CAdES, XAdES, and JAdES signature profiles, including
the complex long-term validation (LTV) workflows that individual
libraries like PDFBox cannot do alone.

The project lives at \texttt{github.com/esig/dss} and is released under
the \textbf{LGPL 2.1} license. The Maven coordinates follow the pattern
\texttt{eu.europa.ec.joinup.sd-dss:dss-*}.

\subsection{Why EU DSS Matters for PDF
Signatures}\label{_why_eu_dss_matters_for_pdf_signatures}

If your application needs to produce \textbf{PAdES-B-LTA} signatures ---
the gold standard for long-term archival signatures that remain
verifiable for decades --- you have two choices:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Build it yourself}: Use PDFBox for the PDF layer, BouncyCastle
  for CMS construction, write your own timestamp client (RFC 3161),
  implement OCSP and CRL fetching, construct the DSS (Document Security
  Store) dictionary, embed the VRI (Validation Related Information), and
  manage archival timestamp chains. This is what Chapters 29--31 of this
  book teach. It works. It is also hundreds of hours of development and
  testing.
\item
  \textbf{Use EU DSS}: Import the \texttt{dss-pades} module, configure a
  \texttt{PAdESSignatureParameters} object, provide a
  \texttt{SignatureTokenConnection} (which wraps your certificate and
  private key), and let the framework handle everything --- CMS
  construction, signed attribute inclusion, timestamp requests,
  revocation data embedding, DSS dictionary creation, and incremental
  save.
\end{enumerate}

Here's what a PAdES-B-LTA signature looks like with EU DSS:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// EU DSS — PAdES{-}B{-}LTA signing (simplified)}
\NormalTok{PAdESSignatureParameters params }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PAdESSignatureParameters}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{params}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSignatureLevel}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{SignatureLevel}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{PAdES\_BASELINE\_LTA}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{params}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setDigestAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{DigestAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SHA256}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{params}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSigningCertificate}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{CertificateToken}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{certificate}\OperatorTok{));}
\NormalTok{params}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setCertificateChain}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Arrays}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{asList}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{intermediateCerts}\OperatorTok{));}

\CommentTok{// TSA (Timestamp Authority) for the signature timestamp}
\NormalTok{OnlineTSPSource tspSource }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{OnlineTSPSource}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"http://tsa.example.com/timestamp"}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{params}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setContentTimestampParameters}\OperatorTok{(}\KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{TimestampParameters}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{DigestAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{SHA256}\OperatorTok{));}

\NormalTok{PAdESService service }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PAdESService}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{commonCertificateVerifier}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{service}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setTspSource}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{tspSource}\OperatorTok{);}

\CommentTok{// Two{-}step signing: digest, then sign}
\NormalTok{ToBeSigned dataToSign }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ service}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDataToSign}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{documentToSign}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ params}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{SignatureValue signatureValue }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ signingToken}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{sign}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{dataToSign}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ params}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getDigestAlgorithm}\OperatorTok{(),}\NormalTok{ privateKey}\OperatorTok{);}
\NormalTok{DSSDocument signedDocument }\OperatorTok{=}\NormalTok{ service}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{signDocument}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{documentToSign}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ params}\OperatorTok{,}\NormalTok{ signatureValue}\OperatorTok{);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Compare this to implementing the same B-LTA profile manually with PDFBox
+ BouncyCastle --- the EU DSS version handles the timestamp embedding,
OCSP/CRL fetching, DSS dictionary construction, and VRI population
automatically.

\subsection{EU DSS Architecture}\label{_eu_dss_architecture}

EU DSS is modular. You don't need to import the entire framework ---
pick the modules relevant to your signature format:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2857}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.7143}}@{}}
\caption{EU DSS modules relevant to PDF signing}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Module
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Purpose
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Module
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Purpose
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{dss-pades} & PAdES signature creation and extension (B-B → B-T →
B-LT → B-LTA). Uses PDFBox internally for PDF manipulation. \\
\texttt{dss-cades} & CAdES signature creation. The PAdES module
delegates CMS construction to this. \\
\texttt{dss-document} & Core document model (\texttt{DSSDocument},
\texttt{InMemoryDocument}, \texttt{FileDocument}) and service
interfaces. \\
\texttt{dss-service} & Online data loaders --- HTTP clients for TSA,
OCSP, and CRL fetching. \\
\texttt{dss-tsl-validation} & EU Trusted List (EUTL) parsing and
validation. Essential for determining whether a signing certificate is
qualified under eIDAS. \\
\texttt{dss-validation-report} & Generates detailed validation reports
following ETSI TS 119 102-2 --- the same validation report format that
EU government validation services produce. \\
\texttt{dss-token} & \texttt{SignatureTokenConnection} implementations:
PKCS\#12 file, PKCS\#11 (HSM), JKS keystore, MS-CAPI (Windows
certificate store). \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{EU DSS vs. Rolling Your
Own}\label{_eu_dss_vs_rolling_your_own_2}

The honest comparison:

\textbf{Advantages of EU DSS:}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Compliance out of the box}: EU DSS is the European
  Commission's reference implementation. If you use it correctly, your
  signatures will pass validation on the EU's official validation
  service (DSS demonstration webapp) and on member-state validation
  services. This is not a guarantee you get from hand-rolling with
  PDFBox + BouncyCastle.
\item
  \textbf{LTV complexity hidden}: Long-term validation requires fetching
  OCSP responses, downloading CRLs, embedding them in the PDF's DSS
  dictionary, creating VRI entries, and adding archival timestamps. EU
  DSS does all of this. Implementing it yourself is a significant
  effort.
\item
  \textbf{Trusted List integration}: The \texttt{dss-tsl-validation}
  module can load and parse the EU Trusted List, letting you
  programmatically determine whether a signing certificate is qualified.
  Building this yourself means implementing ETSI TS 119 612 XML parsing
  and certificate matching --- nontrivial.
\item
  \textbf{Validation reports}: EU DSS generates ETSI-compliant
  validation reports that are accepted by auditors and compliance
  officers.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Advantages of rolling your own (PDFBox + BouncyCastle):}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Full control}: You understand every byte. When something
  fails, you know exactly where to look because you wrote every layer.
\item
  \textbf{No framework weight}: EU DSS pulls in a substantial dependency
  tree. If you only need PAdES-B-B (baseline signature without
  timestamps or LTV), you can do it with PDFBox + BouncyCastle in about
  200 lines of code --- EU DSS adds a megabytes of framework for
  something achievable with direct library calls.
\item
  \textbf{Learning}: Building it yourself teaches you the spec. Using EU
  DSS teaches you the framework's API. For a book like this one,
  understanding comes from building.
\item
  \textbf{Customization}: EU DSS makes assumptions about how signatures
  should be constructed. If your use case deviates (unusual SubFilter
  values, custom signed attributes, non-standard timestamp
  configurations), you may fight the framework rather than benefit from
  it.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Our recommendation}: Learn to build signatures with PDFBox +
BouncyCastle first (Chapters 29--31). Then, when you need PAdES-B-LTA or
EU Trusted List integration in production, use EU DSS --- you'll
understand what it's doing under the hood, and you'll be able to debug
it when it behaves unexpectedly.

Chapter 23 provides a complete walkthrough of EU DSS for PAdES signing
and validation.

\subsection{EU DSS Version History}\label{_eu_dss_version_history}

EU DSS follows a rapid release cycle, with major versions roughly
annually:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{DSS 5.x} (2017--2022): The long-lived stable series. Most
  documentation and blog posts you'll find online reference 5.x APIs.
\item
  \textbf{DSS 6.x} (2023--present): The current generation, with Java
  17+ as the minimum, updated dependencies (including PDFBox 3.x for the
  PAdES module), and improved validation reporting. The API changed
  significantly between 5.x and 6.x --- if you're following tutorials,
  make sure they match your version.
\end{itemize}

EU DSS 6.x uses PDFBox 3.x internally. If your project also uses PDFBox
directly (for example, for document manipulation before signing), you
must ensure version alignment. Having DSS pull in PDFBox 3.0.0 while
your code uses PDFBox 3.0.1 can cause subtle \texttt{NoSuchMethodError}
exceptions at runtime. Use Maven's
\texttt{\textless{}dependencyManagement\textgreater{}} to lock the
PDFBox version across your entire project.

\section{Analysis and Debugging
Tools}\label{_analysis_and_debugging_tools}

A signing implementation is only as good as your ability to inspect what
it produces. The following tools are indispensable for PDF signature
development.

\subsection{RUPS: The PDF Object
Explorer}\label{_rups_the_pdf_object_explorer}

\textbf{RUPS} (Reading and Updating PDF Syntax) is a Java Swing
application that lets you browse the internal object structure of a PDF
file. It was originally developed by Bruno Lowagie as a companion to
iText, and it shows every COS object, dictionary key, stream, and
cross-reference entry in the file.

For signature work, RUPS is invaluable because it lets you:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Inspect the \texttt{/Sig} dictionary and all its keys
  (\texttt{/ByteRange}, \texttt{/Contents}, \texttt{/SubFilter},
  \texttt{/Filter})
\item
  Navigate the field tree to see how signature fields are structured
\item
  View the \texttt{/AcroForm} dictionary and its \texttt{/SigFlags}
\item
  Examine \texttt{/Lock} and \texttt{/SV} (Seed Value) dictionaries
\item
  Browse incremental updates to see what changed between revisions
\item
  Inspect the raw byte content of the \texttt{/Contents} hex string
\end{itemize}

RUPS is available on GitHub at \texttt{LibrePDF/OpenPDF} (it's bundled
with the OpenPDF project). It's also available as a standalone download.
The interface is spartan --- it's a developer tool, not a user-facing
product --- but once you learn to navigate the tree view, it becomes the
fastest way to understand what's actually inside a PDF.

When debugging a signature failure, the first thing you should do is
open the PDF in RUPS and check:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Does the \texttt{/Sig} dictionary exist? (Navigate: Root → AcroForm →
  Fields → {[}signature field{]} → V)
\item
  Is the \texttt{/ByteRange} array plausible? (Four integers, the first
  should be 0, the last should be close to the file size)
\item
  Is \texttt{/Contents} non-empty? (Should be a long hex string, not all
  zeros)
\item
  What is the \texttt{/SubFilter}? (Should be
  \texttt{adbe.pkcs7.detached} or \texttt{ETSI.CAdES.detached})
\end{enumerate}

If any of these are wrong, you've found your problem before even looking
at the CMS.

\subsection{veraPDF: The Compliance
Validator}\label{_verapdf_the_compliance_validator}

\textbf{veraPDF} is the industry-standard PDF/A validation tool,
developed by the Open Preservation Foundation and the PDF Association.
While its primary mission is PDF/A compliance checking, it includes PDF
analysis capabilities that are useful for signature work:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Policy-based validation} --- you can define custom validation
  profiles that check signature-specific requirements
\item
  \textbf{Feature extraction} --- enumerate all signatures,
  certificates, and cryptographic properties in a PDF
\item
  \textbf{Structural analysis} --- verify that the PDF structure
  conforms to the specification
\end{itemize}

veraPDF is available as a CLI tool, a Java library (which you can embed
in your validation pipeline), and a GUI application. It's the validator
that national archives and government agencies use to verify PDF/A
compliance --- if your signed PDFs also need to be PDF/A compliant (as
many government document workflows require), veraPDF is essential.

\subsection{QPDF: The Swiss Army
Knife}\label{_qpdf_the_swiss_army_knife}

\textbf{QPDF} is a C++ command-line tool (created by Jay Berkenbilt)
that can inspect, transform, and repair PDF files at the structural
level. It's not Java, but every Java PDF developer should have it
installed.

Key capabilities for signature work:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{qpdf\ -\/-show-xref} --- displays the cross-reference table,
  showing where every object lives in the file. Critical for
  understanding byte range issues.
\item
  \texttt{qpdf\ -\/-show-object=N} --- displays a specific object by
  number. Faster than navigating a GUI when you know the object number
  from an error message.
\item
  \texttt{qpdf\ -\/-check} --- validates the PDF's structural integrity.
  If this reports problems, your signing code has a bug.
\item
  \texttt{qpdf\ -\/-qdf} --- produces a "QDF" (QPDF data format) output
  where the PDF is restructured for human readability, with objects in
  order and comments showing object numbers. Invaluable for
  understanding what incremental saves changed.
\item
  \texttt{qpdf\ -\/-linearize} --- linearizes the PDF for fast web
  viewing. \textbf{Do not linearize after signing} --- it destroys the
  signature by reorganizing the file.
\end{itemize}

Several QPDF operations will destroy existing signatures:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{-\/-linearize} --- reorganizes the entire file
\item
  \texttt{-\/-normalize-content=y} --- reprocesses content streams
\item
  \texttt{-\/-object-streams=generate} --- restructures object storage
\item
  Any operation that produces a non-incremental output
\end{itemize}

Always use \texttt{-\/-copy-encryption=source.pdf} and check whether the
operation preserves incremental structure before running it on a signed
PDF.

\subsection{MuTool (from MuPDF)}\label{_mutool_from_mupdf}

\textbf{MuTool} is the command-line companion to MuPDF (Artifex's
open-source PDF renderer). Its \texttt{mutool\ show} command provides
quick access to PDF objects:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ExtensionTok{mutool}\NormalTok{ show document.pdf trailer          }\CommentTok{\# Show trailer dictionary}
\ExtensionTok{mutool}\NormalTok{ show document.pdf xref             }\CommentTok{\# Show cross{-}reference table}
\ExtensionTok{mutool}\NormalTok{ show document.pdf 42 0             }\CommentTok{\# Show object 42, generation 0}
\ExtensionTok{mutool}\NormalTok{ show document.pdf outline          }\CommentTok{\# Show document outline/bookmarks}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

MuTool is lightweight and fast --- useful for quick inspections when you
don't want to launch a GUI tool.

\subsection{OpenSSL: CMS Verification Outside the PDF
Context}\label{_openssl_cms_verification_outside_the_pdf_context}

For verifying the CMS signature independently of the PDF wrapper,
OpenSSL is the standard tool:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Extract the raw signature bytes from /Contents (after hex{-}decoding)}
\CommentTok{\# and save as signature.der}

\CommentTok{\# Verify the CMS structure}
\ExtensionTok{openssl}\NormalTok{ cms }\AttributeTok{{-}inform}\NormalTok{ DER }\AttributeTok{{-}in}\NormalTok{ signature.der }\AttributeTok{{-}noout} \AttributeTok{{-}cmsout} \AttributeTok{{-}print}

\CommentTok{\# Verify the signature against the signed data}
\ExtensionTok{openssl}\NormalTok{ cms }\AttributeTok{{-}inform}\NormalTok{ DER }\AttributeTok{{-}in}\NormalTok{ signature.der }\AttributeTok{{-}verify} \DataTypeTok{\textbackslash{}}
    \AttributeTok{{-}content}\NormalTok{ signed{-}data.bin }\DataTypeTok{\textbackslash{}}
    \AttributeTok{{-}CAfile}\NormalTok{ ca{-}bundle.pem }\DataTypeTok{\textbackslash{}}
    \AttributeTok{{-}out}\NormalTok{ /dev/null}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This separates the CMS verification from the PDF byte-range logic --- if
OpenSSL says the CMS is valid but your PDF validator says the signature
is invalid, the problem is in the byte range handling, not the
cryptography.

\subsection{ASN.1 Dump Tools}\label{_asn_1_dump_tools}

When debugging CMS issues, being able to read the raw ASN.1 structure is
essential. Several tools help:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{\texttt{openssl\ asn1parse}} --- parses DER-encoded ASN.1,
  showing the tag-length-value tree
\item
  \textbf{BouncyCastle's \texttt{ASN1Dump}} ---
  \texttt{org.bouncycastle.asn1.util.ASN1Dump.dumpAsString(obj)} ---
  prints any ASN.1 object as a readable tree from Java code
\item
  \textbf{\texttt{dumpasn1}} (Peter Gutmann) --- the original ASN.1
  dumper, still the most detailed output
\item
  \textbf{Web-based tools} --- several websites parse ASN.1/DER online;
  useful for quick checks but do not upload production signatures to
  third-party websites
\end{itemize}

\section{Choosing the Right Library for Your
Project}\label{_choosing_the_right_library_for_your_project}

After all this history, here is the practical guidance:

\textbf{License} & Apache 2.0 & LGPL 2.1 & LGPL 2.1 & AGPL 3.0 &
Proprietary \\
\textbf{Cost} & Free & Free & Free & Free (if AGPL-compatible) & €€€€ \\
\textbf{PAdES-B-B} & Yes (manual CMS) & Yes (built-in) & Basic & Yes
(built-in) & Yes (built-in) \\
\textbf{PAdES-B-LTA} & Yes (manual DSS) & Yes (built-in) & No & Yes
(built-in) & Yes (built-in) \\

Partial & Yes \\
\textbf{PDF 2.0} & Yes & Yes (via PDFBox) & No & Yes & Yes \\

N/A (AGPL) & Vendor-supported \\

High \\
\textbf{Low-level PDF access} & Excellent & Limited (framework) &
Limited & Good & Good \\
\textbf{Our recommendation} & \textbf{Learn + custom} &
\textbf{Production PAdES} & Legacy migration & Open-source projects &
Budget permits \\
\end{longtable}

\subsection{Our Recommendation}\label{_our_recommendation}

For the projects this book targets --- production signing services,
validation pipelines, signature analysis tools --- \textbf{Apache PDFBox
3.x with BouncyCastle} is the default recommendation:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Apache 2.0 license}: No surprises, no AGPL obligations, no
  license fees. This will not change.
\item
  \textbf{Low-level access}: Signing requires understanding and
  manipulating the raw PDF structure. PDFBox's COS model gives you
  direct access. iText and OpenPDF abstract this away, which is
  convenient until you need to debug a byte-range computation or inspect
  a malformed signature dictionary.
\item
  \textbf{Active maintenance}: PDFBox has a dedicated Apache community,
  regular releases, and responsive handling of security issues. The
  Bochum attacks were patched quickly.
\item
  \textbf{Separation of concerns}: PDFBox handles the PDF, BouncyCastle
  handles the crypto. You understand both layers because you built both
  layers. When something breaks, you know exactly where to look.
\end{enumerate}

The trade-off is that PDFBox makes you build more yourself ---
especially the CMS layer. That's what Chapters 29--31 are for.

\textbf{The best way to learn is to build it yourself.} Using a library
that does everything for you (like commercial iText) gets you to
production faster, but leaves you helpless when something goes wrong.
With PDFBox + BouncyCastle, you understand every byte of the signature
because you put it there. When a customer reports that their signature
"shows invalid in Adobe but valid in Foxit," you'll know exactly where
to look --- because you wrote the code that constructed the CMS,
computed the byte range, and injected the signature.

To close this chapter, here is a summary of every tool and library we've
discussed, organized by function:

of iText 2.x. Good for legacy migration. \\

Feature-rich but expensive or license-restrictive. \\

De facto standard for Java CMS. Register provider explicitly. \\

reference implementation. PAdES-B-B through B-LTA, validation reports,
EU Trusted List integration. \\
PDF structure inspection & RUPS & LGPL & GUI object browser. First tool
to reach for. \\
PDF/A compliance & veraPDF & Open source (MPL 2.0) & Industry-standard
validator. Also useful for general PDF analysis. \\

\texttt{-\/-show-xref}, \texttt{-\/-qdf}, \texttt{-\/-check}. \\

Quick object inspection. \\

of PDF context. \\
ASN.1 inspection & BouncyCastle \texttt{ASN1Dump} & MIT & Java-based
ASN.1 tree dump. \\
ASN.1 inspection & \texttt{dumpasn1} (Gutmann) & Public domain & The
classic ASN.1 dumper. \\
\end{longtable}

Every tool in this table will appear at some point in the remaining
chapters. Install them now --- when you're debugging a signature failure
at 2 AM, you don't want to be downloading tools.

\section{Key Takeaways}\label{_key_takeaways_15}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{iText's license change} (LGPL → AGPL in 2009) split the Java
  PDF world in two. The consequences --- old versions in production,
  community forks, enterprise migrations --- are still visible in 2026.
\item
  \textbf{OpenPDF} is the LGPL continuation of iText 2.x. Useful for
  legacy migration but limited for modern PAdES signing.
\item
  \textbf{PDFBox + BouncyCastle} is the recommended stack for new
  signing projects. Apache-licensed, actively maintained, gives you
  low-level control.
\item
  \textbf{PDFBox 3.x} broke API compatibility with 2.x. Budget migration
  time. Key changes: \texttt{Loader.loadPDF()}, new font constructors,
  content stream API changes.
\item
  \textbf{BouncyCastle} provides the CMS layer that PDFBox doesn't.
  Always register the provider explicitly. Use \texttt{jdk18on}
  artifacts.
\item
  \textbf{RUPS, QPDF, veraPDF, OpenSSL} --- know your tools. PDF
  signature debugging is 50\% knowing what to look at and 50\% having
  the right tool to look at it.
\item
  \textbf{The library you choose is a 10-year decision.} Choose based on
  license stability, community health, and technical fit --- not just
  today's feature checklist.
\end{itemize}

\chapter{PDFBox Signing --- Complete
Walkthrough}\label{_pdfbox_signingcomplete_walkthrough}

Chapter 28 gave us the lay of the land. Now we write code. This chapter
is a complete, production-quality walkthrough of PDF signing with Apache
PDFBox 3.0.0 and BouncyCastle 1.80 on Java 21. Every code listing
compiles, every class is self-contained, and every pitfall we describe
is one we have hit in production --- or watched someone else hit at 2 AM
on a Friday.

We start with the core PDFBox signing
API --- \texttt{PDDocument.addSignature()}, \texttt{SignatureInterface},
and \texttt{SignatureOptions} --- then build outward: the
\texttt{PDSignature} dictionary and its fields, visible and invisible
signature appearances, multi-signature workflows, external/HSM signing,
certification signatures with DocMDP, timestamp embedding, and the
migration traps waiting for anyone upgrading from PDFBox 2.x to 3.x.

By the end of this chapter, you will have a complete, production-ready
signing class that produces PAdES-B-B and PAdES-B-T compliant
signatures. Chapter 30 goes deeper into the BouncyCastle CMS
construction that powers the actual cryptography.

\section{The PDFBox Signing API: Three Moving
Parts}\label{_the_pdfbox_signing_api_three_moving_parts}

PDFBox signing revolves around three objects that you wire together
before calling \texttt{saveIncremental()}. Understanding what each one
does --- and what happens when you get the order wrong --- is essential.

\subsection{PDSignature: The Signature
Dictionary}\label{_pdsignature_the_signature_dictionary}

\texttt{PDSignature} is PDFBox's Java representation of the PDF
\texttt{/Sig} dictionary (ISO 32000-2:2020 S12.8.1). When you create a
\texttt{PDSignature} and set its properties, you are building the
dictionary that will be written into the PDF file.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{PDSignature signature }\OperatorTok{=} \KeywordTok{new} \FunctionTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{();}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setFilter}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{PDSignature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{FILTER\_ADOBE\_PPKLITE}\OperatorTok{);}       \CommentTok{// /Filter}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSubFilter}\OperatorTok{(}\NormalTok{COSName}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getPDFName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"ETSI.CAdES.detached"}\OperatorTok{));} \CommentTok{// /SubFilter}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setSignDate}\OperatorTok{(}\BuiltInTok{Calendar}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{getInstance}\OperatorTok{());}                \CommentTok{// /M}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setName}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Alice Smith"}\OperatorTok{);}                             \CommentTok{// /Name}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setReason}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"I approve this document"}\OperatorTok{);}               \CommentTok{// /Reason}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setLocation}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"Berlin, Germany"}\OperatorTok{);}                     \CommentTok{// /Location}
\NormalTok{signature}\OperatorTok{.}\FunctionTok{setContactInfo}\OperatorTok{(}\StringTok{"alice@example.com"}\OperatorTok{);}                \CommentTok{// /ContactInfo}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Every setter maps directly to a key in the \texttt{/Sig} dictionary. The
following table lists all the fields you can set, what they map to, and
when you should care.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2308}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1538}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1538}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.4615}}@{}}
\caption{PDSignature Fields --- ISO 32000-2:2020 S12.8.1, Table
255}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Java Method
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
PDF Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Required
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What It Does
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Java Method
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
PDF Key
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Required
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What It Does
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{setFilter(COSName)} & \texttt{/Filter} & Yes & The signature
handler. Almost always \texttt{Adobe.PPKLite}. PDFBox provides
\texttt{FILTER\_ADOBE\_PPKLITE} as a constant. Other values
(\texttt{Entrust.PPKEF}, \texttt{CICI.SignIt}, \texttt{VeriSign.PPKVS})
exist in the spec but are rarely seen in the wild. \\
